Method of Learning

Distinctive Features of This Edition
Editor enumerates five features of this edition.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the five editorial features that distinguish this edition of Al-Ibanah, including critical text comparison, hadith verification, commentary on key theological attributions, and indices.

Core Idea
This edition combines scholarly rigor with humility, aiming for correction and utility.

Before diving into the theological content of Al-Ibanah, it is helpful to understand how this edition was prepared. The editor, Bashir Muhammad Uyun, outlines five key features that set this edition apart. These features highlight the methodology of careful comparison, comprehensive hadith sourcing, critical commentary, and user-friendly indices. This introduction will walk through each feature so you appreciate the scholarly effort behind the text you are about to study.

Guided Path
1
Read the list of features
Read the Arabic passage carefully, noting the five numbered points. Then read the translation to ensure you understand each feature.
Pay attention to the theological clarification in point 3.
2
Identify the editorial method
Notice how the editor compares multiple earlier editions and scholarly works. This shows the importance of textual criticism in Islamic scholarship.
The edition uses footnotes for variant readings except clear errors.
3
Understand the theological clarification
Point 3 addresses a specific historical claim about Abu Hanifa and the created-Quran issue. Understand why the editor corrects this attribution.
The editor points to Hammad ibn Abi Hanifa as the actual source.
4
Note the helpful additions
The preface by Sheikh Hammad al-Ansari and the three indices are added to aid the reader. These are important for academic study.
5
Reflect on the conclusion
Read the editor's closing remarks about perfection and reform. This reveals the scholarly humility and intention behind the work.
The editor hopes to approach perfection but acknowledges only Allah is perfect.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم تأتي طبعتنا هذه تمتاز عما سبقها بالآتي
١ - عدنا فقابلنا الكتاب على الطبعة الهندية والمنيرية وعلى طبعة الدكتور فوقية محمود ، وكتاب " تبيين كذب المفتري " لابن عساكر ، واخترنا ما رأيناه أنه أصح وأشرنا إلى اختلاف النسخ في الحواشي إلا ما كان خطأ بيناً لا خفاء فيه فلم نشر إليه
٢ - استوفينا تخريج الأحاديث كاملة مفصلة
٣ - علقنا على بعض المواضع التي تحتاج إلى تعليق وخاصة ما نسبه الأشعري إلى أبي حنيفة رحمهما الله تعالى من القول بخلق القرآن وأنه تاب من ذلك ، وبيان أن القائل بذلك هو حماد بن أبي حنيفة ، وأنه نسب قوله إلى أبيه ظلماً وبهتاناً . وأحلنا القارئ في كثير من المواضع إلى «شرح العقيدة الطحاوية» للعلامة ابن أبي العز ليستكمل أطراف الكلام عن مواضيع الكتاب
٤ - صدرنا هذه الطبعة بمقدمة الشيخ حماد الأنصاري رحمه الله تعالى شاكرين له فضله وعلمه، والله يتولى جزاءه خير الجزاء .
٥ - وضعنا للكتاب فهارس لأطراف الحديث ، وآخر للأعلام ، وثالث للمواضيع .
-
ونرجو أن نكون بعملنا قد اقتربنا من الكمال - والكمال لله وحده ـ وما أريد إلا الإصلاح ما استطعت والله يتولى الصالحين ، والحمد لله رب العالمين .
دمشق ١٤١٠/١١/١٣
الراجي من الله العون
بشیر محمد عیون
Then this edition of ours is distinguished from what preceded it by the following: 1 - We returned and compared the book with the Indian edition, the Muniriyya edition, and the edition of Dr. Fawqiya Mahmoud, and the book "Tabyin Kadhib al-Muftari" by Ibn Asakir, and we chose what we saw as most correct, and we indicated the differences of copies in the footnotes except for what was a clear mistake with no ambiguity, so we did not refer to it. 2 - We completed the verification of hadiths fully and in detail. 3 - We commented on some passages that need commentary, especially what al-Ash'ari attributed to Abu Hanifa (may Allah have mercy on them both) of the statement of the creation of the Quran and that he repented from that, and the clarification that the one who said that is Hammad ibn Abi Hanifa, and that he attributed his statement to his father unjustly and slanderously. And we referred the reader in many places to the "Sharh al-Aqida al-Tahawiyya" by the scholar Ibn Abi al-Izz to complete the discussion on the topics of the book. 4 - We prefaced this edition with an introduction by Sheikh Hammad al-Ansari (may Allah have mercy on him), thanking him for his favor and knowledge, and Allah is the one who will reward him with the best reward. 5 - We prepared for the book indices: one for the hadiths, one for the notable persons, and a third for topics. - And we hope that by our work we have approached perfection—and perfection is for Allah alone—and I desire nothing but reform as much as I am able, and Allah takes care of the righteous. Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds. Damascus, 13/11/1410. The one hoping for Allah's help: Bashir Muhammad Uyun.
Source pages p. 4

Commentary
This passage is an editorial note listing the features of the current edition of Al-Ibanah. It is a methodology passage that explains the editorial choices. The editor first mentions comparing the text to three earlier editions (Indian, Muniriyya, and Fawqiya Mahmoud) and the book 'Tabyin Kadhib al-Muftari', choosing the most correct readings and noting variants in footnotes only when not obviously erroneous. Second, he states that all hadiths have been fully sourced and verified. Third, he highlights critical commentary on specific points, especially the claim that Ash'ari attributed the created-Quran view to Abu Hanifa and later repented; the editor clarifies that the actual proponent was Hammad ibn Abi Hanifa, and that the attribution to Abu Hanifa was unjust. He also directs readers to 'Sharh al-Aqida al-Tahawiyya' for further discussion. Fourth, he thanks Sheikh Hammad al-Ansari for providing a preface. Fifth, he lists three indices: for hadiths, notable persons, and topics. He concludes with a hope of approaching perfection, acknowledging that perfection belongs only to Allah, and expresses his intention solely for reform.

Key Terms
تبيين كذب المفتري
الطبعة الهندية
المنيرية
خلق القرآن
شرح العقيدة الطحاوية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The editor claims his edition is perfect.
Correction: The editor states he hopes to have approached perfection, but clearly says 'perfection is for Allah alone' and that he only intends reform.

Checkpoint
What did the editor clarify regarding the attribution of the created-Quran statement to Abu Hanifa?
A) That Abu Hanifa repented from it.
B) That the actual proponent was Hammad ibn Abi Hanifa, and it was wrongly attributed to Abu Hanifa.
C) That al-Ash'ari himself believed in it.
D) That the statement is authentic from Abu Hanifa.

Key Takeaway
This edition of Al-Ibanah is distinguished by careful textual comparison, complete hadith verification, critical clarification of a theological misattribution, a scholarly preface, and useful indices.

Method of Learning

Proving the Authenticity of al-Ibanah
States the purpose of the second research: proving the ascription of al-Ibanah to al-Ash'ari.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the purpose of the second research: to prove that al-Ash'ari's book al-Ibanah is authentically attributed to him and to refute the claim that it was forged.

Core Idea
Al-Ibanah is authentically by al-Ash'ari, not a forgery.

Having established al-Ash'ari's return to the Ahl al-Sunnah, the author now addresses doubts about the genuineness of his book al-Ibanah. Some critics alleged it was forged onto him. This lesson explains the purpose of proving its correct attribution and refuting that false claim.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then read the translation to understand the author's statement.
Notice the phrase 'هذا بيت القصيد' (this is the heart of the matter).
2
Identify the main claim being refuted
The passage mentions that some ignorant ones (الأغمار) claimed the book was forged (مدسوسة). Identify this as the claim the author will refute.
3
Understand the significance
The authenticity of al-Ibanah is important because it is a key text of al-Ash'ari's creed after his repentance. Proving its attribution supports the reliability of the book.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وبعد إتمام هذا الحديث حول رجوعه عن الاعتزال نقر بحثاً ثانياً في صحة نسبة ( الإبانة عن أصول الديانة ( إليه رداً على بعض الأغمار الذين زعموا أنها مدسوسة عليه - وهذا بيت القصيد فنقول : وبالله نستعين : ثبوت نسبة الإبانة إلى أبي الحسن الأشعري والرد على من أنكر ذلك وزعم إنها مدسوسة عليه .
And after completing this discussion about his turning away from Mu'tazilism, we conduct a second research concerning the correctness of the ascripion of al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah to him, in refutation of some ignorant ones who claimed that it was forged upon him – and this is the heart of the matter. So we say: and with Allah we seek help: The proof of the ascripion of al-Ibanah to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari and the refutation of those who deny that and claim that it is forged upon him.
Source pages p. 12

Commentary
This passage introduces the second main topic of the discussion. After having established al-Ash'ari's return to the Salafi creed, the author now turns to defend the authenticity of al-Ash'ari's book al-Ibanah. Some ignorant people alleged that this book was falsely attributed to al-Ash'ari. The author declares that he will prove its correct ascripion and refute those who claim it is a forgery.

Key Terms
صحة النسبة
مدسوسة
الأغمار

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people claim that the book al-Ibanah was forged and falsely attributed to al-Ash'ari.
Correction: The author will prove that al-Ibanah is authentically by al-Ash'ari and refute the forgery claim.

Checkpoint
What is the purpose of the second research mentioned?
A) To prove al-Ash'ari was Mu'tazili
B) To prove al-Ibanah is authentically by al-Ash'ari
C) To list all of al-Ash'ari's works
D) To criticize al-Ash'ari

Key Takeaway
The second research aims to prove that al-Ibanah is authentically by al-Ash'ari, refuting the forgery claim.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The author calls those who claim al-Ibanah is a forgery 'al-aghmar' (ignorant ones). When you hear claims that question the authenticity of Islamic texts, do you automatically assume the claimants are ignorant, or do you investigate? How does this passage challenge you to both defend the truth and seek true knowledge?
?
This passage identifies proving the authenticity of al-Ibanah as the 'heart of the matter'. What is the 'heart of the matter' in your own relationship with the creed of Ahl al-Sunnah? What would you be willing to research and defend, even against popular doubts?

Method of Learning
beginner
5 min
Upholding Authentic Narrations, Love for the Salaf, and the Caliphate of Abu Bakr
Ash'ari affirms following authentic hadith, love for the Salaf, and the caliphate of Abu Bakr.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three core affirmations of Ash'ari's creed regarding hadith, the Salaf, and the caliphate of Abu Bakr.

Core Idea
Sunni creed is built on authentic hadith, love for the Companions, and the rightful caliphate of Abu Bakr.

In this foundational passage from Al-Ibanah, Imam al-Ash'ari outlines three key tenets of Ahl al-Sunna: the acceptance of only authentic hadith transmitted by reliable narrators, the obligation to love and honor the Salaf (the early Muslims, especially the Companions), and the affirmation that Abu Bakr al-Siddiq is the most excellent leader after the Prophet. These principles distinguish Sunni Islam from various sectarian deviations.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once to get the flow. Then read the translation to understand the meaning.
Notice how Ash'ari uses 'we submit', 'we hold as religion', 'we say' to express creedal statements.
2
Identify the three affirmations
The passage contains three clear affirmations: about hadith, about the Salaf, and about Abu Bakr. Underline each one.
3
Understand the reasoning for Abu Bakr
Ash'ari gives reasons: Allah strengthened the religion through him, he fought apostates, and the Muslims agreed on his caliphate following the Prophet's precedence in prayer.
This shows the Sunni argument for Abu Bakr's superiority is based on both divine support and communal consensus.
4
Reflect on the significance
Think about why these three points are essential in defining Sunni identity against groups like the Shia (who reject some hadith and criticize the Salaf) or the Khawarij (who may reject the caliphate).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونسلم بالروايات الصحيحة عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم التي رواها الثقات عدل عن عدل حتى ينتهي إلى رسول الله . وندين بحب السلف الذين اختارهم الله عز وجل لصحبة نبيه عليه السلام ونثني عليهم بما أثنى الله به عليهم ونتولاهم أجمعين ونقول : إن الإمام الفاضل بعد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم أبو بكر الصديق رضوان الله عليه . وأن الله أعز به الدين وأظهره على المرتدين ، وقدمه المسلمون بالإمامة كما قدمه رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم للصلاة ، وسموه جميعهم خليفة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم
And we submit to the authentic narrations from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, which were narrated by trustworthy narrators, trustworthy from trustworthy, until it reaches the Messenger of Allah. And we hold as religion love for the predecessors (al-salaf) whom Allah, Mighty and Majestic, chose for the companionship of His Prophet, peace be upon him; and we praise them with what Allah praised them, and we are loyal to them all. And we say: the excellent leader after the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, is Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, may Allah be pleased with him. And that Allah strengthened the religion through him and made it prevail over the apostates, and the Muslims put him forward for the caliphate just as the Messenger of Allah put him forward for prayer, and they all called him the Caliph of the Messenger of Allah.
Source pages p. 24

Commentary
This passage lays out the Sunni methodology regarding hadith and the status of the companions. First, the Ash'aris accept authentic hadith that are transmitted by reliable, trustworthy narrators in an unbroken chain back to the Prophet. Second, they affirm love for the Salaf (the early generations, especially the Companions) whom Allah chose, praising them as Allah praised them and showing allegiance to all of them. Finally, they declare that the best and most excellent leader after the Prophet is Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. The justification is given: Allah strengthened Islam through him, he fought the apostates, and the Muslims unanimously chose him as caliph, following the precedent set by the Prophet when he appointed Abu Bakr to lead the prayer. Thus, his caliphate is legitimate and based on both divine support and communal consensus.

Key Terms
روايات صحيحة
ثقات
عدل
سلف
صحابة
خلافة
إمامة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some sects deny the superiority of Abu Bakr or reject the Sunni criterion for accepting hadith.
Correction: Ash'ari defends both by grounding them in the Qur'an and Sunnah and the consensus of the Muslims.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what is the basis for Abu Bakr's caliphate?
A) He was the wealthiest companion.
B) The Prophet put him forward to lead the prayer.
C) He was the oldest.
D) There was no one else.

Key Takeaway
Ash'ari's creed affirms the authority of authentic hadith, love for all the Salaf, and the rightful caliphate of Abu Bakr as the best leader after the Prophet.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You say you love the Companions, but do you find yourself criticizing any of them in your heart? Al-Ash'ari declares, 'we are loyal to them all.' Examine your heart: is there any grudge or reservation? If so, repent and actively seek to love each one as Allah praised them.
?
When you remember that the Prophet put Abu Bakr forward to lead the prayer, does it settle your heart about his caliphacy? The Muslims followed that precedent. In moments of doubt, will you consciously affirm, 'the excellent leader after the Prophet is Abu Bakr al-Siddiq'? Make a firm intention to defend this belief.
?
You accept narrations daily, but are they authentic? The Ash'ari says 'we submit to authentic narrations.' Reflect: have you ever acted upon a weak hadith because it suited your desires? From today, commit to seeking the authenticity of narrations you follow, especially in matters of creed.

Method of Learning
beginner
5 min
Quotes from the Salaf on Following the Predecessors
A collection of five quotes from early scholars urging adherence to the salaf.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the emphasis placed by early scholars on adhering to the way of the salaf and avoiding innovation and personal opinion.

Core Idea
Early scholars unanimously urged following transmitted knowledge over personal opinion.

The following passage collects sayings from five prominent early Muslims, spanning from the Companion Ibn Mas'ud to the renowned jurist al-Shafi'i. Each quote reinforces the foundational principle of sticking to the path of the predecessors (salaf) and warns against innovation and individual reasoning. As you read, notice how each scholar expresses this core idea in slightly different terms.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation. Let the voices of the scholars come through before interpreting.
The passage ends abruptly; it may be a fragment.
2
Identify each scholar's main instruction
Go through each quote and note what each scholar commands or advises. Look for keywords like 'follow', 'stop', 'adhere', 'believe', 'stick'.
Each quote has a slightly different emphasis.
3
Compare warnings against innovation
Notice how Ibn Mas'ud says 'do not innovate' and al-Awza'i warns against 'opinions of men'. Compare these with the others.
Al-Shafi'i stresses belief according to Allah's and the Messenger's intent.
4
Reflect on the significance
Ask yourself why these early scholars placed such heavy emphasis on following the predecessors. How does this guide the study of Islamic theology?
This connects to the broader methodology of Ahl al-Sunnah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال عبد الله بن مسعود رضي الله عنه : اتبعوا ولا تبتدعوا . وقال عمر بن عبد العزيز رحمه الله : قف حيث وقف القوم فإنهم عن علم وقفوا ، وببصر كفوا . وقال الإمام الأوزاعي رحمه الله : عليك بآثار من سلف وإن رفضك الناس، وإياك وآراء الرجال وإن زخرفوه لك بالقول . وقال الإمام الشافعي رحمه الله : آمنت بالله وبما جاء عن الله على مراد الله تعالى ، وآمنت برسول الله ، وبما جاء عن رسول الله على مراد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم . وقال الفضيل بن عياض رحمه الله : الزم طريق الهدى ولا يضرك قلة السالكين ، وإياك وطرق الضلالة ، ولا تغتر بكثرة الهالكين . والله تبارك وتعالى
Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, may Allah be pleased with him, said: 'Follow and do not innovate.' Umar ibn 'Abd al-'Aziz, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'Stop where the people stopped, for they stopped based on knowledge, and they refrained with insight.' Imam al-Awza'i, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'You must adhere to the traditions of those who preceded, even if people reject you; and beware of the opinions of men, even if they embellish them for you with speech.' Imam al-Shafi'i, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'I believe in Allah and in what came from Allah according to the intent of Allah Almighty; and I believe in the Messenger of Allah and in what came from the Messenger of Allah according to the intent of the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him.' Al-Fudayl ibn 'Iyad, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'Stick to the path of guidance, and the scarcity of those who tread it will not harm you; and beware of the paths of misguidance, and do not be deceived by the multitude of the perishing.' And Allah, Blessed and Most High...
Source pages p. 30

Commentary
This passage presents a series of authoritative sayings from early Islamic scholars, all emphasizing the importance of following the methodology of the salaf (predecessors) and avoiding innovation and personal opinion. Ibn Mas'ud commands to follow and not innovate. Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz advises to stop where the early community stopped, as they had knowledge and insight. Al-Awza'i stresses adherence to the transmitted reports even if people reject you, and warns against personal opinions no matter how attractive. Al-Shafi'i expresses his faith in Allah and His Messenger exactly as intended by them, without distortion. Al-Fudayl ibn Iyad urges holding fast to the path of guidance regardless of how few follow it, and warns against being misled by the majority on misguidance. The passage ends abruptly with 'And Allah, Blessed and Most High,' likely continuing the warning.

Key Terms
اتباع
ابتداع
الوقوف عند السلف
آثار السلف
آراء الرجال
طريق الهدى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think following the salaf means blind imitation without understanding.
Correction: These quotes emphasize informed adherence based on transmitted knowledge and insight, not blind following. For example, Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz says they stopped 'based on knowledge' and 'with insight'.

Checkpoint
According to Imam al-Awza'i, what should one adhere to?
A) The opinions of scholars
B) The traditions (athar) of those who preceded
C) The consensus of the majority
D) Rational argument

Key Takeaway
The early scholars consistently taught that true guidance lies in following the transmitted reports of the salaf, not in personal opinion or innovation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The scholars said 'Follow and do not innovate.' In which specific area of your daily worship have you been following your own desires rather than the transmitted guidance? What is one innovation you can abandon today to align with the salaf?
?
Al-Fudayl warns: 'Do not be deceived by the multitude of the perishing.' Think of a popular practice in your community that contradicts the way of the salaf. Whose approval are you seeking more: Allah's or the people's? Will you stand for the truth even if you stand alone?
?
Imam al-Shafi'i said he believes according to the intent of Allah and His Messenger, not his own interpretation. Where have you twisted a clear text to fit your own comfort? Commit to reading the Quran and Sunnah to understand what Allah and His Messenger actually intended, even if it challenges your lifestyle.

Method of Learning
beginner
5 min
Sayings of the Salaf on Following the Salaf
Five sayings from the Salaf urging adherence to the early generation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Salaf themselves commanded adherence to their path and warned against innovation and personal opinion.

Core Idea
The Salaf themselves commanded following their path.

This passage presents five authoritative sayings from the early Muslims—Ibn Mas'ud, Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz, al-Awza'i, al-Shafi'i, and al-Fudayl—each emphasizing the necessity of adhering to the way of the predecessors and rejecting innovation. These quotes collectively establish the methodological foundation of relying solely on transmitted creed and avoiding speculative interpretation.

Guided Path
1
Read each saying carefully
Read the Arabic and translation of each of the five sayings one by one. Notice how each scholar uses different language but conveys the same core message.
Ibn Mas'ud's saying is the most concise: 'Follow and do not innovate.'
2
Identify the common theme
After reading all five, identify the central command that unites them. Each saying urges the listener to stick to the path of the early generations and avoid deviation.
The theme is summed up in Ibn Mas'ud's phrase: اتبعوا ولا تبتدعوا.
3
Reflect on the warnings
Each saying contains a warning: against innovation (Ibn Mas'ud), against moving beyond where the Salaf stopped (Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz), against personal opinions (al-Awza'i), against interpreting scripture by one's own desire (al-Shafi'i), and against being deceived by large numbers (al-Fudayl).
Al-Awza'i's warning is especially strong: 'Beware of the opinions of men, even if they embellish them with speech.'
4
Connect to modern practice
Consider how these sayings apply today. They establish that following the Salaf is not optional but obligatory, and that innovation (bid'ah) in religion is strictly prohibited.
This lesson grounds the methodology of the Salaf in the very words of the Salaf themselves.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال عبد الله بن مسعود رضي الله عنه : اتبعوا ولا تبتدعوا . وقال عمر بن عبد العزيز رحمه الله : قف حيث وقف القوم فإنهم عن علم وقفوا ، وببصر كفوا . وقال الإمام الأوزاعي رحمه الله : عليك بآثار من سلف وإن رفضك الناس، وإياك وآراء الرجال وإن زخرفوه لك بالقول . وقال الإمام الشافعي رحمه الله : آمنت بالله وبما جاء عن الله على مراد الله تعالى ، وآمنت برسول الله ، وبما جاء عن رسول الله على مراد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم . وقال الفضيل بن عياض رحمه الله : الزم طريق الهدى ولا يضرك قلة السالكين ، وإياك وطرق الضلالة ، ولا تغتر بكثرة الهالكين .
'Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, may Allah be pleased with him, said: 'Follow (the path) and do not innovate.' 'Umar ibn 'Abd al-'Aziz, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'Stop where the people (i.e., the early generations) stopped, for they stopped based on knowledge and refrained based on insight.' Imam al-Awza'i, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'Cling to the traces of those who preceded, even if the people reject you, and beware of the opinions of men, even if they embellish them for you with speech.' Imam al-Shafi'i, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'I believe in Allah and in whatever came from Allah according to the intended meaning of Allah Almighty, and I believe in the Messenger of Allah and in whatever came from the Messenger of Allah according to the intended meaning of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.' Al-Fudayl ibn 'Iyad, may Allah have mercy on him, said: 'Adhere to the path of guidance and let not the small number of followers harm you, and beware of the paths of misguidance and let not the large number of those who perish deceive you.'
Source pages p. 30

Commentary
This passage presents a series of five authoritative sayings from the Salaf themselves, commanding adherence to the way of the predecessors and warning against innovation and personal opinion. Ibn Mas'ud commands to follow and not innovate. Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz tells us to stop at the same point the early generations stopped, because they had knowledge and insight. Al-Awza'i urges clinging to the traditions (athar) of the Salaf even if people reject you, and avoiding people's opinions even if they are embellished. Al-Shafi'i states his faith in Allah and the Prophet, accepting what came from them as they intended without alteration. Finally, al-Fudayl commands sticking to the path of guidance despite few followers, and avoiding paths of misguidance despite many followers. Together, these quotes build a strong case for the methodology of relying solely on the transmitted creed of the Salaf and rejecting speculative interpretation.

Key Terms
اتبعوا ولا تبتدعوا
قف حيث وقف القوم
آثار من سلف
مراد الله
مراد رسول الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Following the Salaf is optional or a matter of personal preference.
Correction: These sayings show that following the Salaf is an obligation commanded by the Salaf themselves, and innovation is strictly prohibited.

Checkpoint
What is the central message of the sayings quoted from the Salaf?
A) The importance of personal reasoning
B) The necessity of following the early generations and avoiding innovation
C) The need to reinterpret scripture metaphorically
D) The superiority of later scholars over early ones

Key Takeaway
The Salaf themselves commanded us to follow their path, stop where they stopped, and avoid innovation and personal opinion.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Ibn Mas'ud said 'Follow and do not innovate.' Where in your life have you been tempted to add something new to your faith, thinking it would improve it? Will you commit today to abandoning that innovation and strictly following the Salaf?
?
Al-Fudayl said: 'Adhere to the path of guidance and let not the small number of followers harm you.' Are you willing to stand alone on truth, even if your family or friends mock you for being 'too strict' or 'narrow-minded'? What concrete step will you take this week to strengthen your resolve?
?
Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz explained that the Salaf stopped based on knowledge. How much of your current belief and practice is based on actual knowledge from the Salaf versus cultural habit or personal opinion? What is one specific belief or practice you will verify with the teachings of the Salaf starting today?

Allah Preserves His Religion Through Upright Scholars
Allah preserves Shari'ah, citing Quran and hadith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Allah preserves His religion through both the Quranic text and the continuous tradition of upright scholars who defend it from distortion.

Core Idea
Allah guards His religion through trustworthy scholars in every generation.

This lesson presents a foundational principle of Sunni theology: Allah has taken it upon Himself to preserve the Shari'ah. He does so through His Book and through a chain of upright scholars who refute misinterpretations. The passage combines a Quranic verse of assurance with a prophetic hadith detailing the scholars' protective role.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation, noting how the author links the Quranic promise to the hadith tradition.
Notice the three specific threats mentioned in the hadith.
2
Observe the Quranic proof
The verse from Surah al-Hijr (15:9) declares that Allah Himself preserves the 'Reminder' (the Quran). Ask: does preservation mean only the text, or also its correct understanding?
3
Learn the three types of corruption
The hadith lists three dangers: distortion by extremists, false claims by falsifiers, and figurative interpretation by the ignorant. Identify each term in Arabic.
These three categories cover major threats to religious understanding.
4
Understand the role of scholars
The hadith implies that in every generation, upright scholars ('udul) will arise to carry and defend true knowledge. Reflect on why this is necessary.
5
Apply the lesson
Consider how this principle assures believers that the true religion remains accessible and protected despite challenges.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والله تبارك وتعالى تكفل بحفظ شريعته في كتابه المبين ، فقال عز وجل : ﴿ إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَفِظُونَ ﴾ . ورسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم قال في حديثه المروي من طرق : ( يحمل هذا العلم من كل خلف عدوله ، ينفون عنه تحريف الغالين ، وانتحال المبطلين ، وتأويل الجاهلين )
And Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has guaranteed the preservation of His Shari'ah in His clear Book. He, Mighty and Majestic, said: {Indeed, We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will be its guardians.} And the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, said in a hadith transmitted through multiple chains: 'Upright individuals from every generation will carry this knowledge. They will ward off from it the distortions of the extremists, the false claims of the falsifiers, and the (figurative) interpretations of the ignorant.'
Source pages p. 30

Commentary
This passage explains that Allah Himself has taken responsibility to protect His law (Shari'ah) in the Quran. It quotes the Quranic verse confirming that Allah will preserve the Quran (the Reminder). Then it cites a hadith stating that at every generation, trustworthy scholars ('udul) will carry the knowledge of this religion and defend it against three types of corruption: the distortions (tahreef) of extremists (ghali), the false claims (intihal) of falsifiers (mubtil), and the (figurative) interpretations (ta'wil) of the ignorant (jahil). This hadith underscores the vital role of orthodox scholars in safeguarding the correct understanding of revelation.

Key Terms
شريعته
الذكر
حفظ
عدوله
تحريف الغالين
انتحال المبطلين
تأويل الجاهلين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that preservation applies only to the Quranic text, not to its understanding or to the broader Shari'ah.
Correction: This passage shows that preservation encompasses both the text (Quran) and the correct understanding of religion, upheld by upright scholars in every age.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith, what are the three types of corruption that the upright scholars will ward off?
A) Forgetfulness, mistakes, and evil
B) Distortion by extremists, false claims by falsifiers, and interpretation by the ignorant
C) Loss, theft, and destruction
D) Misquotation, misunderstanding, and neglect

Key Takeaway
Allah preserves His religion through both His Book and a continuous line of upright scholars who defend it from distortion, falsehood, and ignorant interpretation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you actively seek knowledge from upright, qualified scholars, or do you rely on your own opinions or popular figures? What would it take for you to verify whom you learn from?
?
Think of a time you encountered a distortion or false claim about Islam. Did you speak up or remain silent? What does this lesson demand of you?
?
Are you contributing to the preservation of this religion by learning it correctly and rejecting ignorant interpretations, or are you part of the problem by spreading what you don't fully understand?

Seeking Guidance and Success from Allah
He seeks God's guidance and success to avoid ruin.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning and significance of seeking guidance and success from Allah as a fundamental act of reliance on Him.

Core Idea
Guidance is sought from Allah using the guidance He already gave.

In this brief but profound supplication, Imam al-Ash'ari encapsulates the believer's complete dependence on Allah for both direction and protection. He teaches us that the very guidance we use to seek God is itself a gift from God. This lesson explores the meaning of 'seeking guidance by guidance' and the importance of asking for success in avoiding error.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic supplication once, then read its literal translation. Notice its brevity and depth.
2
Analyze the phrase 'نستهدي الله بالهدى'
This phrase means 'we seek guidance from Allah through guidance'. Consider what 'through guidance' implies about the source of guidance.
It means using the Quran and Sunnah as a means to ask for more guidance.
3
Understand 'التوفيق لمجانبة الردى'
This asks for success (tawfiq) in avoiding ruin (radā). Ruin refers to error and destruction in this life and the next.
4
Reflect on the deeper meaning
Reflect on how even our ability to seek guidance is from Allah. This teaches humility and reliance.
This is a core Ash'ari principle.
5
Connect to the broader methodology
This supplication sets the tone for Imam al-Ash'ari's work: beginning with reliance on Allah. Consider how this shapes theological inquiry.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونستهدي الله بالهدى، ونسأله التوفيق لمجانبة الردى.
And we seek guidance from Allah through guidance, and we ask Him for success in avoiding ruin.
Source pages p. 34

Commentary
This short passage is a request for guidance and protection from error. The author emphasizes seeking God's guidance by means of the guidance He has already provided (the Quran and Sunnah) and asking for success to avoid deviation.

Key Terms
الهدى
التوفيق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Seeking guidance *with* guidance is redundant or unnecessary.
Correction: It means using the revealed guidance (Quran and Sunnah) as a means to ask for more guidance, acknowledging that even our ability to seek guidance is from Allah.

Checkpoint
What do the author and his followers ask of Allah?
A) Wealth and power.
B) Guidance and success to avoid error.
C) Victory over enemies.
D) Long life.

Key Takeaway
Seeking guidance from Allah through the guidance He has given is a recognition that all direction and success come from Him alone.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever tried to make a decision without first turning to the Quran or Sunnah for direction, relying only on your own opinion? What would it look like this week to seek guidance *through* the guidance Allah has already given you?
?
Think of a specific area of your life where you are prone to 'ruin'—a bad habit or sin. Have you sincerely asked Allah for success in avoiding it, or do you just rely on your willpower? Will you make this dua daily from now on?
?
Do you truly believe that every bit of success in staying on the straight path comes from Allah alone? If so, why do you still feel proud of your own efforts? How can you shift your heart to attribute all tawfiq to Him, starting today?

Method of Learning
beginner
5 min
The Prophetic Sunnah as Divine Revelation
Al-Ash'ari cites Quranic verses to prove the Prophet's speech is revelation and to condemn those who reject the Sunnah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Prophet's speech is divinely revealed and that obedience to him is as obligatory as obedience to Allah, based on Quranic proofs.

Core Idea
The Prophet's Sunnah is revelation from Allah, not personal opinion.

Imam al-Ash'ari opens this section with powerful Quranic evidence that the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) does not speak from his own desire but from divine revelation. He then shows that Allah commands believers to obey the Messenger just as they obey Him, establishing the binding authority of the Sunnah. However, many people have turned away from the Sunnah, following their own predecessors in blind imitation. This lesson lays the foundation for the obligation to adhere to the Prophet's guidance.

Guided Path
1
Read the Quranic proofs
Read the three Quranic verses cited by al-Ash'ari that establish the divine origin of the Prophet's speech and the command to obey him.
These verses are from Surah an-Najm, Yunus, and an-Nur.
2
Understand the obligation to obey
Notice that al-Ash'ari emphasizes that Allah commands obedience to the Messenger just as He commands obedience to Himself, using Surah an-Nisa.
3
Reflect on the condemnation
Read how al-Ash'ari harshly criticizes those who abandon the Sunnah and follow their own predecessors, calling them misguided.
This shows the gravity of rejecting the Sunnah.
4
Connect to your own practice
Ask yourself: Do you treat the Prophet's Sunnah as a binding source of guidance, or do you sometimes prioritize personal opinion or tradition?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال: ﴿ وَمَا يَنطِقُ عَنِ المَوَى * إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْيٌ يُوحَى ﴾ [النجم: ٣-٤]، وقال: ﴿ قُل مَا يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أُبَدِّلَهُ مِن تِلْقَايِي نَفْسِي إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَى إِلَى ﴾ [يونس: ١٥]. وقال: ﴿ إِنَّمَا كَانَ قَوْلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذَا دُعُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ لِيَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن يَقُولُوا سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ﴾ [النور: ٥١]، فأمرهم أن يسمعوا قوله، ويطيعوا أمره، ويحذروا مخالفته. وقال: ﴿ أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ ﴾ [النساء: ٥٩]، فأمرهم بطاعةِ رسولِهِ صلى الله عليه وسلم كما أمرهم بطاعته، ودعاهم إلى التمسك بسنة نبيه، كما أمرهم بالعمل بكتابه.

٥ - فَنَبَذَ كثير ممن غلبت عليهم شقوتهم، واستحوذ عليهم الشيطان سُنَنَ نبي الله وراء ظهورهم ومالوا إلى أسلاف لهم قلدوهم بدينهم، ودانوا بديانتهم، وأبطلوا سُنَنَ رسول الله، ورفضوها وأنكروها وجحدوها، افتراء منهم على الله، قَدْ ضَلُّوا وَمَا كَانُوا مُهْتَدِينَ [الأنعام: ١٤٠].

أوصيكم عباد الله بتقوى الله، وأحذركم الدنيا، فإنها حلوة خضرَةٌ، تغرُّ أَهْلَها، وَتَخْدَعُ سكانها، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَاضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلَ الْحَيَوة الدُّنْيَا كَمَاء أَنزَلَتَهُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَبَاتُ الْأَرْضِ فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا تَذْرُوهُ الرِّيحُ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُّقْتَدِرًا ﴾ [الكهف: ٤٥]. من كان فيها في حَبْرَةٍ أعقبته بعدها عبرة، ومن أعطته من سرائها بطناً أعقبته من ضراتها ظهراً، غرارة، غرور ما فيها، فانية، فان ما عليها، كما حكم عليها ربها بقوله تعالى: ﴿ كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ ﴾ [الرحمن: ٢٦].

فاعملوا رحمكم الله للحياة الدائمة، ولخلود الأبد، فإن الدنيا تنقضي عن أهلها، وتبقى الأعمال قلائد في رقاب أهلها.

واعلموا أنكم ميتون، ثم إنكم من بعد موتكم إلى ربكم راجعون، وَلِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا بِمَا عَمِلُوا وَيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا بِالْحُسْنَى [النجم: ٣١]. فكونوا بطاعة ربكم عاملين، وعما نهاكم منتهين.
And He said: 'And he does not speak from desire. It is but a revelation revealed' [Najm 53:3-4], and He said: 'Say, It is not for me to change it on my own accord. I only follow that which is revealed to me' [Yunus 10:15]. And He said: 'The only statement of the believers when they are called to Allah and His Messenger to judge between them is that they say, We hear and we obey' [Nur 24:51]. So He commanded them to hear His statement and obey His command and beware of opposing it. And He said: 'Obey Allah and obey the Messenger' [Nisa 4:59]. So He commanded them to obey His Messenger, peace be upon him, just as He commanded them to obey Him, and He called them to cling to the Sunnah of His Prophet, just as He commanded them to act by His Book.

5. So many of those whose misery overcame them and Satan took possession of them cast the traditions (sunan) of the Prophet of Allah behind their backs and inclined to predecessors of theirs whom they blindly followed in their religion, and they adopted their religion, and they nullified the traditions (sunan) of the Messenger of Allah, and rejected them, denied them, and disavowed them, slandering Allah. They have gone astray and were not guided [An'am 6:140].

I advise you, servants of Allah, to fear Allah, and I warn you of the world, for it is sweet and green, it deceives its people and tricks its inhabitants. Allah the Exalted said: 'And present to them the example of the life of this world, like water which We send down from the sky, and the vegetation of the earth mingles with it and becomes dry debris that the winds scatter. And Allah is over all things competent' [Kahf 18:45]. Whoever is in it in comfort, it leaves him afterward with grief, and whoever it gives of its pleasures a bellyful, it leaves him from its harms a back heavy. It is deceitful, deluding what is in it; it is perishing, perishing is what is upon it, as its Lord decreed for it by His statement the Exalted: 'Everyone upon it is perishing' [Rahman 55:26].

So work, may Allah have mercy on you, for the everlasting life and eternal immortality, for the world passes away from its people, and deeds remain as necklaces on the necks of their owners.

And know that you will die, then after your death you will return to your Lord, 'That He may reward those who did evil for what they did and reward those who did good with the best' [Najm 53:31]. So be acting in obedience to your Lord and refraining from what He has forbidden you.
Source pages p. 36

Commentary
This passage is an exhortation by Imam al-Ash'ari, likely from an introductory section of al-Ibanah. It opens with Quranic proofs that the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) does not speak from his own desire but by revelation (wahy). The verses cited establish the divine origin of the Prophet's speech and the obligation to obey him. Al-Ash'ari then notes that many people, overcome by misery and Satan, have abandoned the Prophet's sunnah and instead followed their own predecessors in blind imitation (taqlid), thereby nullifying and rejecting the sunnah. He declares that such people have gone astray. The passage then shifts to a direct admonition to the audience: fear Allah, and beware of the world (dunya), which is described as sweet and green but deceitful, using a Quranic metaphor from Surah al-Kahf to illustrate its fleeting nature. He emphasizes that the world is perishing and that deeds remain. He concludes by reminding the audience of death and the return to Allah for judgment, urging them to act in obedience and refrain from what is forbidden.

Key Terms
وحي
سنة
طاعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that following the Prophet's Sunnah is optional or less important than following the Quran.
Correction: The passage shows that the Prophet's speech is revelation and that Allah commands obedience to the Messenger just as He commands obedience to Himself, making the Sunnah equally binding.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, what is the evidence that the Prophet's speech is revelation and not from his own desire?
A) The verse 'Obey Allah and obey the Messenger'
B) The verse 'And he does not speak from desire; it is but a revelation revealed'
C) The verse 'Say, I do not ask you for any reward'
D) The verse 'We have not sent you except as a mercy to the worlds'

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's Sunnah is not human opinion but divine revelation, and obedience to it is as obligatory as obedience to Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In your daily life, do you treat the Prophet's sunnah as binding revelation, or do you sometimes prioritize your own desires or cultural practices over it? What specific area have you been neglecting, and what step will you take today to revive it?
?
When a clear command from the Prophet contradicts your comfort or convenience, does your heart immediately say 'We hear and obey,' or does it hesitate? What is one sunnah you have knowingly abandoned, and what is stopping you from resuming it starting today?
?
The lesson warns that those who reject the Sunnah are 'overcome by misery' and led by Satan. Are there any sunnah practices you have dismissed as insignificant or outdated? How will you change your attitude towards the Sunnah from this moment forward?

Method of Learning
beginner
5 min
The Reality of the World and the Call to Piety
Al-Ash'ari delivers a direct admonition to fear Allah, warns of the world's deceit, and urges preparation for the afterlife.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the fleeting nature of this world and the importance of fearing Allah, working for the afterlife, and obeying His commands.

Core Idea
This world is deceitful and perishing; only deeds remain for the afterlife.

After establishing the authority of the Sunnah, Imam al-Ash'ari turns to a direct exhortation for his audience. He advises them to fear Allah and warns them against the deception of this worldly life. Using a vivid Quranic metaphor, he describes how the world is like rain that produces vegetation which then dries up and scatters. He reminds us that everyone and everything on earth will perish, and that our deeds will remain as necklaces around our necks. The lesson ends with a powerful call to obedience and a warning of the return to Allah for judgment.

Guided Path
1
Read the admonition to taqwa
Start by reading al-Ash'ari's opening advice: 'I advise you, servants of Allah, to fear Allah.' Understand that taqwa is the foundation.
2
Reflect on the warning about the world
Read the Quranic metaphor from Surah al-Kahf and al-Ash'ari's commentary on how the world deceives its people.
The world is described as sweet and green but ultimately perishing.
3
Consider the lasting nature of deeds
Notice the powerful image: deeds remain as necklaces on the necks of their owners. This emphasizes accountability.
4
Internalize the call to action
Read the final sentences urging obedience and avoidance of sin, remembering that death and return to Allah are certain.

⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال: ﴿ وَمَا يَنطِقُ عَنِ المَوَى * إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْيٌ يُوحَى ﴾ [النجم: ٣-٤]، وقال: ﴿ قُل مَا يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أُبَدِّلَهُ مِن تِلْقَايِي نَفْسِي إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا مَا يُوحَى إِلَى ﴾ [يونس: ١٥]. وقال: ﴿ إِنَّمَا كَانَ قَوْلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذَا دُعُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ لِيَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن يَقُولُوا سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ﴾ [النور: ٥١]، فأمرهم أن يسمعوا قوله، ويطيعوا أمره، ويحذروا مخالفته. وقال: ﴿ أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ ﴾ [النساء: ٥٩]، فأمرهم بطاعةِ رسولِهِ صلى الله عليه وسلم كما أمرهم بطاعته، ودعاهم إلى التمسك بسنة نبيه، كما أمرهم بالعمل بكتابه.

٥ - فَنَبَذَ كثير ممن غلبت عليهم شقوتهم، واستحوذ عليهم الشيطان سُنَنَ نبي الله وراء ظهورهم ومالوا إلى أسلاف لهم قلدوهم بدينهم، ودانوا بديانتهم، وأبطلوا سُنَنَ رسول الله، ورفضوها وأنكروها وجحدوها، افتراء منهم على الله، قَدْ ضَلُّوا وَمَا كَانُوا مُهْتَدِينَ [الأنعام: ١٤٠].

أوصيكم عباد الله بتقوى الله، وأحذركم الدنيا، فإنها حلوة خضرَةٌ، تغرُّ أَهْلَها، وَتَخْدَعُ سكانها، قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَاضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلَ الْحَيَوة الدُّنْيَا كَمَاء أَنزَلَتَهُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَبَاتُ الْأَرْضِ فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا تَذْرُوهُ الرِّيحُ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُّقْتَدِرًا ﴾ [الكهف: ٤٥]. من كان فيها في حَبْرَةٍ أعقبته بعدها عبرة، ومن أعطته من سرائها بطناً أعقبته من ضراتها ظهراً، غرارة، غرور ما فيها، فانية، فان ما عليها، كما حكم عليها ربها بقوله تعالى: ﴿ كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ ﴾ [الرحمن: ٢٦].

فاعملوا رحمكم الله للحياة الدائمة، ولخلود الأبد، فإن الدنيا تنقضي عن أهلها، وتبقى الأعمال قلائد في رقاب أهلها.

واعلموا أنكم ميتون، ثم إنكم من بعد موتكم إلى ربكم راجعون، وَلِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا بِمَا عَمِلُوا وَيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا بِالْحُسْنَى [النجم: ٣١]. فكونوا بطاعة ربكم عاملين، وعما نهاكم منتهين.
And He said: 'And he does not speak from desire. It is but a revelation revealed' [Najm 53:3-4], and He said: 'Say, It is not for me to change it on my own accord. I only follow that which is revealed to me' [Yunus 10:15]. And He said: 'The only statement of the believers when they are called to Allah and His Messenger to judge between them is that they say, We hear and we obey' [Nur 24:51]. So He commanded them to hear His statement and obey His command and beware of opposing it. And He said: 'Obey Allah and obey the Messenger' [Nisa 4:59]. So He commanded them to obey His Messenger, peace be upon him, just as He commanded them to obey Him, and He called them to cling to the Sunnah of His Prophet, just as He commanded them to act by His Book.

5. So many of those whose misery overcame them and Satan took possession of them cast the traditions (sunan) of the Prophet of Allah behind their backs and inclined to predecessors of theirs whom they blindly followed in their religion, and they adopted their religion, and they nullified the traditions (sunan) of the Messenger of Allah, and rejected them, denied them, and disavowed them, slandering Allah. They have gone astray and were not guided [An'am 6:140].

I advise you, servants of Allah, to fear Allah, and I warn you of the world, for it is sweet and green, it deceives its people and tricks its inhabitants. Allah the Exalted said: 'And present to them the example of the life of this world, like water which We send down from the sky, and the vegetation of the earth mingles with it and becomes dry debris that the winds scatter. And Allah is over all things competent' [Kahf 18:45]. Whoever is in it in comfort, it leaves him afterward with grief, and whoever it gives of its pleasures a bellyful, it leaves him from its harms a back heavy. It is deceitful, deluding what is in it; it is perishing, perishing is what is upon it, as its Lord decreed for it by His statement the Exalted: 'Everyone upon it is perishing' [Rahman 55:26].

So work, may Allah have mercy on you, for the everlasting life and eternal immortality, for the world passes away from its people, and deeds remain as necklaces on the necks of their owners.

And know that you will die, then after your death you will return to your Lord, 'That He may reward those who did evil for what they did and reward those who did good with the best' [Najm 53:31]. So be acting in obedience to your Lord and refraining from what He has forbidden you.
Source pages p. 36

Commentary
This passage is an exhortation by Imam al-Ash'ari, likely from an introductory section of al-Ibanah. It opens with Quranic proofs that the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) does not speak from his own desire but by revelation (wahy). The verses cited establish the divine origin of the Prophet's speech and the obligation to obey him. Al-Ash'ari then notes that many people, overcome by misery and Satan, have abandoned the Prophet's sunnah and instead followed their own predecessors in blind imitation (taqlid), thereby nullifying and rejecting the sunnah. He declares that such people have gone astray. The passage then shifts to a direct admonition to the audience: fear Allah, and beware of the world (dunya), which is described as sweet and green but deceitful, using a Quranic metaphor from Surah al-Kahf to illustrate its fleeting nature. He emphasizes that the world is perishing and that deeds remain. He concludes by reminding the audience of death and the return to Allah for judgment, urging them to act in obedience and refrain from what is forbidden.

Key Terms
تقوى
الدنيا
فناء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people become engrossed in worldly pleasures and forget that this life is temporary and that they will be held accountable.
Correction: The passage emphasizes that the world is perishing and deceitful, and only deeds done for the afterlife are lasting. It calls to action for the eternal life.

Checkpoint
What does al-Ash'ari say happens to deeds after a person dies?
A) They disappear with the person
B) They remain as necklaces on the necks of their owners
C) They are turned into dust
D) They are forgotten by everyone

Key Takeaway
The world is temporary and deceptive; true success lies in fearing Allah, obeying Him, and working for the eternal life to come.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are your daily choices building necklaces of deeds that will adorn you on the Day of Judgment, or are you chasing the sweet and green that will turn to dry debris? What one thing can you change today to ensure your work is for the everlasting life?
?
Al-Ash'ari warns that whoever enjoys comfort will eventually grieve. Which comfort in your life are you most attached to that you know will lead to regret? How can you detach your heart from it right now?
?
You know you will die and return to your Lord. If your life ended tonight, would you be among those who acted in obedience and refrained from sin? What is the sin you are most struggling to leave and what step will you take to leave it today?

The Creed of the People of Truth and Sunnah
The author responds to a question by stating his creedal method.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the foundational sources of the Ash'ari creed as articulated by Imam al-Ash'ari: the Quran, Sunnah, and the way of Ahmad ibn Hanbal.

Core Idea
The true creed is grounded in revelation, not rational speculation alone.

This passage is a pivotal moment in Al-Ibanah where Imam al-Ash'ari directly states his creed in response to a question. After rejecting various deviant sects, he clarifies that his beliefs are based on the Quran, the Sunnah, and the transmitted teachings of the Salaf, especially Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal. This sets the methodological foundation for the entire work.

Guided Path
1
Read the question posed
Notice how the questioner asks about the author's own creed after hearing his rejections of other sects.
The sects listed are Mu'tazila, Qadariyya, Jahmiyya, Haruriyya, Rafida, and Murji'a.
2
Identify the deviant sects
List the sects mentioned and consider why they are considered deviant in the author's view.
3
Examine the answer's structure
The author begins his answer by stating his adherence to the Quran, Sunnah, and the reports from the Salaf.
He emphasizes holding fast to these sources.
4
Note the special mention of Ahmad ibn Hanbal
Imam al-Ash'ari explicitly follows the way of Ahmad ibn Hanbal and praises him as the one who clarified the truth.
This shows the authority of Imam Ahmad in the author's methodology.
5
Connect to the book's purpose
Reflect on how this passage sets the tone for the rest of Al-Ibanah as a defense of Sunni creed based on revelation and the Salaf.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الباب الثاني
في إِبَانَةِ قَوْلِ أَهْلِ الحَقِ وَالسُّنَّةِ
٢٣ - فإن قال لنا قائل : قد أنكرتم قول المعتزلة والقدرية والجهمية والحرورية والرافضة والمرجئة، فعرفونا قولكم الذي به تقولون، وديانتكم التي بها تدينون .
٢٤ قيل له قولنا الذي نقول به وديانتنا التي ندين بها : التمسك بكتاب الله ربنا عز وجل، وبسنة نبينا ، وما روي عن السادة الصحابة والتابعين وأئمة الحديث، ونحن بذلك معتصمون، وبما كان يقول به (١) أبــو عبد الله أحمد بن محمد بن حنبل نضر الله وجهه، ورفع درجته، وأجـزل مثوبته، قائلون، ولمن خالف قوله مجانبون؛ لأنه الإمام الفاضل، والرئيس الكامل، الذي أبان الله به الحق عند ظهور الضلال، وأوضح به المنهاج، وقمع به بدع المبتدعين، وزيغ الزائغين، وشك الشاكين، فرحمة الله عليه مــن إمام مقدَّم، وجليل معظم، وكبير مفخّم، وعلى جميع أئمة المسلمين .
Chapter Two: On Elucidating the Statement of the People of Truth and the Sunnah. 23 - If a questioner says to us: You have rejected the statement of the Mu'tazila, Qadariyya, Jahmiyya, Haruriyya, Rafida, and Murji'a, so inform us of your statement with which you speak, and your religion which you practice. 24 - It is said to him: Our statement with which we speak and our religion which we practice is: adhering to the Book of Allah, our Lord, Mighty and Majestic, and to the Sunnah of our Prophet ﷺ, and what has been narrated from the noble Companions, the Followers, and the Imams of Hadith; to these we hold fast. And we are followers of what Abu 'Abdillah Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Hanbal (may Allah brighten his face, raise his rank, and increase his reward) used to say, and we avoid those who oppose his statement, because he is the virtuous Imam, the complete leader, through whom Allah manifested the truth at the appearance of misguidance, made clear the path, and suppressed the innovations of the innovators, the deviations of the deviators, and the doubts of the doubters. May the mercy of Allah be upon him, a foremost Imam, a revered noble one, a dignified great one, and upon all the Imams of the Muslims.
Source pages p. 43

Commentary
This passage is a question-and-answer introduction. The author begins by stating a hypothetical question from someone who notes that they have rejected various deviant sects (Mu'tazila, etc.), and asks for their own creed. The response affirms that their creed is based strictly on the Quran, the Sunnah, and the transmitted reports from the Companions, Followers, and Hadith scholars. Specifically, they adhere to the way of Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, praising him as the one through whom Allah cleared the truth. This establishes the methodological foundation: the creed is not based on rational speculation alone but on scripture and the consensus of the early Salaf, especially Imam Ahmad.

Key Terms
أهل الحق والسنة
التمسك بكتاب الله
بسنة نبينا
أئمة الحديث
أحمد بن حنبل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That this passage is just a preface without doctrinal commitment.
Correction: The author explicitly commits to the way of Ahmad ibn Hanbal, making it a substantive methodological statement.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what is the basis of the author's creed?
A) Rational theology alone
B) The Quran, Sunnah, and the way of Ahmad ibn Hanbal
C) The opinions of the Mu'tazila
D) The consensus of all Muslims

Key Takeaway
The Ash'ari creed, as presented here, is rooted in the Quran, Sunnah, and the Salaf, especially Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You claim to adhere to the Quran and Sunnah, but when a ruling (like hijab or interest) goes against your desires, do you still submit? Take one area today where you will choose revelation over your own opinion.
?
The author says they avoid those who oppose the way of Imam Ahmad. Is there a friend, scholar, or influencer whose teachings have subtly led you away from the Salaf? Will you distance yourself from them this week?
?
You say you follow the Salaf, yet how often do you actually consult the sayings of the Companions and Hadith scholars before forming an opinion? Commit to checking a Salafi source for one matter this week.

Reliance on Allah and Acknowledging Need
Al-Ash'ari declares reliance and neediness before Allah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Ash'ari creed emphasizes total reliance on Allah and constant recognition of one's need and poverty before Him.

Core Idea
True faith is absolute dependence on Allah at every moment.

This brief but profound statement from Imam al-Ash'ari captures the heart of Islamic reliance on Allah. Before we explore the attributes of Allah, we must first ground ourselves in our own utter neediness. This passage sets the tone for the believer's posture before the Creator.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic phrase
Read the Arabic carefully. Notice the key verbs 'nalja'' (seek refuge) and 'nuthbitu' (affirm). Also note the nouns 'hajah' (need) and 'faqr' (poverty).
2
Understand the meaning of 'faqr'
'Faqr' here is not material poverty but spiritual and existential poverty before Allah. Reflect on what it means to be in constant need of Allah.
3
Connect to tawakkul
This passage is a declaration of tawakkul (reliance). Consider how reliance on Allah does not negate using means, but places ultimate trust in Him alone.
Avoid the misunderstanding that this means passive inaction.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٤٠ - وأنا نَلْجَأَ في أمورنا إلى الله ، ونثبت الحاجة والفقر في كل وقت إليه .
40. And we seek refuge in Allah in our affairs, and we affirm need and poverty to Him at all times.
Source pages p. 47

Commentary
This short passage is a declaration of tawakkul (reliance on Allah) and the recognition of one's absolute dependency on Him. Al-Ash'ari states that believers seek refuge in Allah (i.e., turn to Him) in all their matters, and they constantly affirm their neediness and poverty before Allah. The term 'poverty' (faqr) here means spiritual and existential need of the Creator. This reflects the Ash'ari emphasis that humans have no independent power or sufficiency and are always in need of Allah's grace.

Key Terms
لجأ
حاجة
فقر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: This passage implies that believers should not use worldly means or strive, but only rely on Allah.
Correction: The passage does not negate using means; it affirms that ultimate reliance is on Allah alone. Using means is part of reliance, as long as the heart is attached to Allah, not the means.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what do believers affirm about themselves in relation to Allah?
A) They are independent and self-sufficient
B) They have no need of Allah
C) They are in constant need and poverty before Allah
D) They only need Allah in times of difficulty

Key Takeaway
Believers constantly rely on Allah and acknowledge their absolute need and poverty before Him at all times.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly feel your poverty before Allah in every moment, or do you often act as if you are self-sufficient? What would change in your daily routine if you genuinely internalized that you need Allah for everything, even your next breath?
?
When you face a difficulty, do your first thoughts turn to Allah or to other means? Examine your heart: is your reliance on Allah or on your own plans?
?
How would you live differently today if you genuinely believed that you are in need of Allah's mercy at every instant? Write one concrete action you will take to remind yourself of this dependence.

On the Sources of Religion and Avoiding Innovation
Imam Ash'ari states the primary sources of authority and forbids innovation in religion.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three primary sources of Islamic theology according to Imam Ash'ari and the prohibition against religious innovation.

Core Idea
True reliance in religion rests on the Quran, Sunnah, and Consensus, not on invented innovations.

Imam Ash'ari lays down the epistemological foundation of his school: in matters of disagreement, we turn to authoritative sources. This passage identifies the Quran, the Sunnah, and the consensus of the Muslim community as the sole arbiters, while warning against introducing innovations. This concise statement sets the methodological tone for the entire treatise.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without pausing. Then read the English translation. Let the author's concise style impress upon you the gravity of his claims.
Notice how the passage moves from positive sources to negative prohibitions.
2
Identify the three sources
The passage lists three explicit sources of authority. Find each one: Kitab Allah, Sunnat al-Nabi, and Ijma' al-Muslimin. Note the phrase 'what is in its meaning' which implicitly includes analogical reasoning.
The phrase 'ما كان في معناه' is crucial for understanding the acceptance of qiyas.
3
Understand the prohibitions
The passage forbids two things: innovation (bid'a) and speaking about Allah without knowledge. Reflect on why these prohibitions are placed together.
Innovation often stems from speaking without knowledge.
4
Reflect on the implication
Ask yourself: How does this passage guide the way we resolve disagreements? Why are these three sources sufficient?
This establishes a clear methodology for theology and jurisprudence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونعول فيما اختلفنا فيه على كتاب ربنا عز وجل، وسنة نبينا، وإجماع المسلمين، وما كان في معناه، ولا نبتدع في دين الله بدعة لم يأذن الله بها، ولا نقول على الله ما لا نعلم.
And we rely, in what we differ about, upon the Book of our Lord (Almighty and Majestic), and the Sunnah of our Prophet, and the consensus of the Muslims, and what is in its meaning. And we do not innovate in the religion of Allah an innovation that Allah has not permitted, and we do not say about Allah what we do not know.
Source pages p. 51

Commentary
This passage defines the epistemological framework of the author's school. In matters of disagreement, the sources of authority are, in order: the Quran, the Sunnah, the consensus of the Muslim community (إجماع), and anything that falls within their meaning (traditionally understood as analogical reasoning, قياس). The passage then prohibits two things: inventing innovations (بدعة) not sanctioned by Allah, and speaking about Allah without knowledge.

Key Terms
كتاب الله (Book of Allah)
سنة النبي (Sunnah of the Prophet)
إجماع المسلمين (consensus of the Muslims)
بدعة (innovation)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: This passage rejects all forms of reasoning, including analogy.
Correction: The phrase 'and what is in its meaning' (وما كان في معناه) implicitly accepts analogical reasoning (qiyas), which was a key tool of the Ash'ari school. The passage does not reject reasoning per se, but only innovation and speech without knowledge.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is explicitly mentioned as a source of authority in this passage?
A) Reason alone
B) The consensus of the Muslims
C) The opinions of the founders of the madhabs
D) The practices of the caliphs

Key Takeaway
Imam Ash'ari establishes the Quran, Sunnah, and Ijma' as the foundational sources, and warns against innovation and speaking without knowledge—a framework that guards against theological deviation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever followed a practice in your religion simply because it was popular or from your culture, without checking if it comes from the Quran, Sunnah, or consensus? What is one such practice you will now investigate or abandon?
?
The author warns against speaking about Allah without knowledge. When was the last time you made a statement about Allah or His religion without being certain of its evidence? How will you ensure this does not happen again?
?
This lesson teaches that innovation (bid'ah) is forbidden. Is there any action you currently do or defend that you know has no clear basis in the revealed sources? What will you do about it starting today?

Method of Learning
beginner
5 min
Reliance on Quran, Sunnah, and Ijma'
Imam Ash'ari declares the sources of religious authority for resolving disputes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three primary sources of authority in Sunni Islam according to Imam Ash'ari and how they are used to resolve disagreements.

Core Idea
In disputes, Sunni scholars rely on Quran, Sunnah, and Ijma'.

Imam Ash'ari sets out the foundational methodology of Ahl al-Sunnah. When disagreements arise, we do not rely on personal opinion or speculation. Instead, we turn to three authoritative sources revealed or sanctioned by Allah. Let's see how he lays this out.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice the order of sources mentioned.
2
Identify the three sources
Imam Ash'ari lists three explicit sources: the Book of Allah, the Sunnah of the Prophet, and the consensus of the Muslims. What is the fourth phrase 'and what is in its meaning' referring to?
Scholars interpret this as analogical reasoning (qiyas) that aligns with these sources.
3
Reflect on the purpose
Why does Imam Ash'ari start with 'in what we disagree about'? This shows that these sources are the arbiter for disputes among Muslims.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونعول فيما اختلفنا فيه على كتاب ربنا عز وجل، وسنة نبينا، وإجماع المسلمين، وما كان في معناه، ولا نبتدع في دين الله بدعة لم يأذن الله بها، ولا نقول على الله مالا نعلم.
And we rely, in what we disagree about, upon the Book of our Lord, Mighty and Majestic, and the Sunnah of our Prophet, and the consensus (ijma') of the Muslims, and what is in its meaning, and we do not innovate in the religion of Allah an innovation which Allah has not permitted, and we do not say about Allah what we do not know.
Source pages p. 51

Commentary
This passage is a methodological statement about the sources of religious authority. Imam Ash'ari declares that in matters of dispute (ikhtilaf), the Sunni position is to rely (naw'alu) on three primary sources: (1) the Book of Allah (the Quran), (2) the Sunnah of the Prophet (peace be upon him), and (3) the consensus (ijma') of the Muslims. The phrase 'and what is in its meaning' likely refers to analogical reasoning (qiyas) that is consistent with these sources. Then he adds two negative principles: (a) they do not introduce innovations (bida') into religion that Allah has not permitted, and (b) they do not say about Allah what they do not know (i.e., they speak only based on revelation). This firmly establishes the Sunni methodology of textual authority and prohibition of innovation.

Key Terms
اعتماد
كتاب
سنة
إجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some claim that 'what is in its meaning' allows unrestricted personal opinion.
Correction: Imam Ash'ari means valid analogical reasoning (qiyas) that is grounded in the primary sources, not arbitrary opinion.

Checkpoint
How many primary sources does Imam Ash'ari explicitly list for resolving disagreements?
A) Two
B) Three
C) Four
D) Five

Key Takeaway
Sunni methodology resolves disputes by prioritizing Quran, Sunnah, and Ijma', with analogy as a tool consistent with them.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hold a religious opinion, do you check whether it is grounded in the Quran and Sunnah, or do you rely on what 'everyone' says? Will you commit to verifying your views with revelation?
?
If your local community practices something that has no basis in the Quran or Sunnah, would you have the courage to follow the sources instead of the crowd? How does this passage challenge you to prioritize divine authority over social conformity?
?
What is one area of your faith where you have relied on your own logic rather than the Quran and Sunnah? What specific step can you take today to align your understanding with these primary sources?

Prohibition of Innovation and Speaking Without Knowledge
Imam Ash'ari prohibits innovation and speaking about Allah without knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why innovations in religion are forbidden and why speaking about Allah without knowledge is prohibited.

Core Idea
Innovation and speaking without knowledge are forbidden in religious matters.

Having established the positive sources, Imam Ash'ari now sets boundaries. True following of the Salaf requires avoiding two pitfalls: introducing new practices into the religion and making statements about Allah without divine authorization. Let's examine these prohibitions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation. Note the two prohibitions: innovation (bid'ah) and speaking without knowledge.
2
Understand 'innovation not permitted by Allah'
Imam Ash'ari specifies that innovations are only forbidden if Allah has not permitted them. This implies that some matters may be permissible as long as they do not introduce new religious practices.
The key is that innovations in worship and creed are rejected.
3
Reflect on speaking without knowledge
The second prohibition is against saying about Allah what one does not know. This emphasizes that all theological statements must be based on revelation and reason derived from it.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونعول فيما اختلفنا فيه على كتاب ربنا عز وجل، وسنة نبينا، وإجماع المسلمين، وما كان في معناه، ولا نبتدع في دين الله بدعة لم يأذن الله بها، ولا نقول على الله مالا نعلم.
And we rely, in what we disagree about, upon the Book of our Lord, Mighty and Majestic, and the Sunnah of our Prophet, and the consensus (ijma') of the Muslims, and what is in its meaning, and we do not innovate in the religion of Allah an innovation which Allah has not permitted, and we do not say about Allah what we do not know.
Source pages p. 51

Commentary
This passage is a methodological statement about the sources of religious authority. Imam Ash'ari declares that in matters of dispute (ikhtilaf), the Sunni position is to rely (naw'alu) on three primary sources: (1) the Book of Allah (the Quran), (2) the Sunnah of the Prophet (peace be upon him), and (3) the consensus (ijma') of the Muslims. The phrase 'and what is in its meaning' likely refers to analogical reasoning (qiyas) that is consistent with these sources. Then he adds two negative principles: (a) they do not introduce innovations (bida') into religion that Allah has not permitted, and (b) they do not say about Allah what they do not know (i.e., they speak only based on revelation). This firmly establishes the Sunni methodology of textual authority and prohibition of innovation.

Key Terms
بدعة
قول بغير علم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What does Imam Ash'ari say about innovations in religion?
A) Some innovations are good.
B) They are not permitted if not authorized by Allah.
C) Only if they contradict the Quran.
D) They are acceptable if meant for good.

Key Takeaway
To preserve pure religion, one must avoid innovation and only speak about Allah with knowledge from revelation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In what area of your religious practice have you introduced something new without clear permission from Allah, and what is stopping you from removing it today?
?
When was the last time you spoke about Allah's attributes or rulings without verifying your knowledge from revelation? How will you change that habit starting now?
?
Do you value the authority of the Quran and Sunnah over your own reasoning when it comes to religious innovations? Be honest: where have you justified an innovation because it felt good or seemed beneficial?
