Obedience, Advice, and Avoiding Innovation
Two creedal articles on communal duties and methodological note.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the communal duties of obedience to legitimate leaders, sincere advice to Muslims, and the imperative to disassociate from callers to innovation as foundational principles of Ahl al-Sunnah.

Core Idea
Obedience to leaders and separation from innovators are twin pillars of communal unity.

In this brief passage, Imam al-Ash'ari states two core communal obligations: obedience to the imams and sincere advice to fellow Muslims, and the imperative to distance oneself from every caller to innovation and people of heretical desires. These principles define the boundaries of the Muslim community and its relationship with authority and deviation. The passage also signals the author's intention to provide scriptural arguments for each article, chapter by chapter.

Guided Path
1
Read the two articles
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the translation. Notice the concise, creedal style.
Each article begins with 'و' (and) indicating a continuation of the list of beliefs.
2
Understand the obligations
Reflect on what 'obedience to the leaders' and 'sincere advice to the Muslims' entail. Obedience is to legitimate rulers and scholars; advice covers all forms of good counsel.
These are duties toward authority and community.
3
Understand the disassociation
Grasp the command to disassociate from every caller to innovation and avoid the people of desires. This sets a clear boundary against deviant groups.
Avoidance does not mean hostility but separation to protect one's faith.
4
Note the methodology
Observe the promise to provide evidence for each belief. This shows the Sunnī commitment to proof and structured argumentation.
The phrase 'chapter by chapter' indicates an organized treatise.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٧٠ - وبطاعة الأئمة ونصيحة المسلمين. ٧١ - ونرى مفارقة كل داعية إلى بدعة، ومجانبة أهل الأهواء وسنحتج لما ذكرناه من قولنا وما بقي منه مما لم نذكره باباً باباً.
70 - And (we enjoin) obedience to the leaders and sincere advice to the Muslims. 71 - And we see disassociation from every caller to innovation, and avoidance of the people of desires, and we will present evidence for what we have mentioned of our saying and what remains of it that we have not mentioned, chapter by chapter.
Source pages p. 56

Commentary
Article 70 commands obedience to political/religious leaders (a'imma) and advising Muslims sincerely (nasiha). Article 71 instructs believers to disassociate from anyone who calls to innovation (bid'a) and to avoid people of heretical desires (ahl al-ahwa'). The author also promises to provide scriptural evidence (ihTijaj) for all the beliefs stated in this work, organized chapter by chapter.

Key Terms
الأئمة
نصيحة
بدعة
أهل الأهواء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Obedience to leaders is absolute regardless of their justice.
Correction: In the Sunni tradition, obedience is due only to legitimate rulers who do not command sin; absolute obedience is not required.

Checkpoint

A) We should engage with them politely
B) We should disassociate from them
C) We should debate them
D) We should follow them

Key Takeaway
This passage establishes that obedience to legitimate leaders, sincere advice, and separation from innovators are essential communal duties, all grounded in evidence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

?
The passage calls for sincere advice (nasiha) to Muslims. Who is one person you know who is struggling spiritually or morally, and you have remained silent out of fear of awkwardness? Will you commit to speaking with them this week, and how will you do it with wisdom?

Objection and Answer: Specification of General Terms
Imam Ashari answers an objection about specification of general terms using Quranic examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses Quranic examples to defend the principle that a general term requires specific evidence to be narrowed, and that the Quran itself remains uncreated because it falls under 'al-amr' (command) not 'al-khalq' (creation).

Core Idea
General terms need specific evidence to be narrowed; the Quran is a command, not creation.

In this passage, an objector questions the consistency of interpreting general terms in the Quran. Citing a verse where angels include Jibril and Mikal separately, the objector wonders why the term 'creation' cannot be similarly specified to exclude the Quran. Imam al-Ash'ari responds by distinguishing between specification backed by evidence and unsupported generalization. He shows that the separate mention of Jibril and Mikal actually serves as an implicit specification ('except'), but in the verse about creation and command, no such specification exists. This lesson unpacks this argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
Identify the objector's quote and the assumed parallelism between the two verses. Notice how the objector expects that if 'angels' can be specified, so can 'creation'.
The objector is using Quran 2:98 as a precedent.
2
Understand Ashari's opening principle
Imam Ashari states: 'We specify the Quran by consensus and by evidence.' This is key — specification is not arbitrary but requires textual or consensus-based evidence.
3
Analyze the angel example
Ashari explains that the separate mention of Jibril and Mikal after the general term 'angels' functions like an exception ('except'), thus narrowing the general term. This shows that specification can occur when there is evidence.
This is the evidence that justifies specification in that verse.
4
Apply to the creation verse
Contrast that with the verse 'أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ'. Ashari notes that the word 'al-khalq' (creation) is left general with no specifying evidence. The word 'al-amr' (command) is separate, not a specification. Therefore, the Quran, being 'al-amr', is not created.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإن قال قائل : أليس قد قال الله تعالى في كتابه: ﴿ مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِلَّهِ وَمَلَائِكَتِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَجِبْرِيلَ وَمِيكَالَ ﴾ [البقرة :۹۸] . قيل له: نحن نخص القرآن بالإجماع ،وبالدليل، فلما ذكر الله عز وجل نفسه وملائكته ولم يدخل في ذكر الملائكة جبريل وميكال إن كانا من الملائكة، ثم ذكرهما بعد ذلك، كأنه قال: الملائكة إلا جبريل وميكال، ثم ذكرهمـا بعـد ذكر الملائكة فقال : وجبريل وميكال ولما قال : ( أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ ولم يخص قوله : ( الخلق ) ،دليل كان قوله: ﴿أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ في
If someone says: Did not Allah, Exalted, say in His book: (Whoever is an enemy to Allah, His angels, His messengers, and Gabriel and Michael) [al-Baqarah: 98]? It is said to him: We specify the Quran by consensus and by evidence. When Allah, Mighty and Majestic, mentioned Himself and His angels, and did not include Gabriel and Michael in the mention of the angels if they are among the angels, then He mentioned them afterwards, as if He said: the angels except Gabriel and Michael. Then He mentioned them after the mention of the angels, saying: and Gabriel and Michael. And when He said: (Unquestionably, His is the creation and the command) and He did not specify His saying: (the creation), it indicated that His saying: (Unquestionably, His is the creation) is in... [text cut off]
Source pages p. 72

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer regarding the interpretation of general terms. The objector points to the verse in al-Baqarah where Allah mentions angels in general, then separately names Jibril and Mikal. This shows that a general term can be specified (takhsis) by later mention, indicating that the general term 'angels' does not include them in that context. The objector implies that similarly, the general term 'al-khalq' (creation) in the verse 'أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ' might be specified to exclude some created things, potentially including the Quran. Al-Ash'ari responds by distinguishing the two cases. In the verse about angels, the specification is done by the same text, by consensus (ijma') and evidence (dalil). In contrast, in the verse 'أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ', the word 'al-khalq' is not specified by any evidence; it remains general. The word 'al-amr' is mentioned separately, not as a specification of 'al-khalq' but as something distinct. The text cuts off, but the argument is that since no specification exists for 'al-khalq', it retains its general meaning of all created things, and 'al-amr' is separate, hence uncreated.

Key Terms
تخصيص
العموم
الإجماع
الدليل
الاستثناء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The separate mention of Jibril and Mikal proves that the general term 'angels' excludes them; al-Ash'ari uses this to show specification is possible, but not applicable to 'al-khalq' in the other verse.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari argues that the separate mention serves as evidence for specification, not that the general term automatically excludes them. He then shows that no such evidence exists for 'al-khalq' in the other verse, so it remains general.

Checkpoint
How does Imam al-Ash'ari respond to the objection that the term 'al-khalq' might be specified like 'angels' in verse 2:98?
A) He says the verse 2:98 is abrogated.
B) He says specification requires evidence, and none exists for 'al-khalq' in the other verse.
C) He agrees that 'al-khalq' includes the Quran.
D) He says 'al-amr' is part of 'al-khalq'.

Key Takeaway
Specification of a general term requires specific textual evidence; without it, the general meaning holds.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read the verse 'His is the creation and the command,' do you instinctively consider the Quran as part of creation or command? How does this passage challenge you to refine your understanding based on specific evidence?
?
Imam al-Ash'ari states that specification requires textual evidence. In your own understanding of Allah's attributes, are you relying on clear textual evidence or your own reasoning? Where might you be tempted to specify God's general attributes without proof?
?
The separate mention of Jibril and Mikal after 'angels' shows that specification can happen. What does this teach you about the importance of examining every part of a verse in context? Are you willing to re-evaluate a belief you hold based on a more careful reading of the Quran?

Al-Ash'ari's Opening Supplication for Guidance
Al-Ash'ari invokes God's guidance and help before new topics.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the significance of Al-Ash'ari's opening supplication and its role in setting a tone of reliance on divine guidance in theological discourse.

Core Idea
Every theological inquiry begins with seeking Allah's guidance, not human intellect alone.

Al-Ash'ari opens his work with a profound supplication that establishes the spiritual foundation for the theological discussions to follow. This passage reminds the reader that all guidance, power, and sufficiency come from Allah alone. Before delving into complex debates, the author humbly invokes divine assistance, setting an example for students of theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the translation to grasp the meaning.
Notice the reverent tone from the start.
2
Identify key phrases and their significance
Locate the words نستهدي, نستكفي, حول وقوة, and مستعان. Understand that each expresses a different aspect of dependence on Allah.
These are common in Islamic supplications.
3
Reflect on the transition phrase
Consider the function of 'أما بعد' as a marker to move from invocation to the main content. Why might Al-Ash'ari begin this way?
This reflects traditional Arabic rhetorical style.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال الشيخ أبو الحسن علي بن إسماعيل الأشعري رحمه الله ورضي عنه : بالله نستهدي وإياه نستكفي ولا حول ولا قوة إلا بالله العلي العظيم، وهو المستعان أما بعد :
Shaykh Abu al-Hasan 'Ali ibn Isma'il al-Ash'ari — may Allah have mercy on him and be pleased with him — said: By Allah we seek guidance, and from Him we seek sufficiency, and there is no power nor strength except by Allah, the Most High, the Almighty. And He is the One whose help is sought. To proceed:
Source pages p. 105

Commentary
This passage is an introductory formula where Al-Ash'ari, the author, begins a new section with a supplication. He states that we seek guidance from Allah, we seek sufficiency from Him, and that all power and strength come from Allah alone. He then affirms that Allah is the One whose help is sought, followed by the phrase 'أما بعد' (to proceed), which is a traditional transition to the main content. This reflects the author's reliance on divine assistance before delving into theological discussions.

Key Terms
نستهدي
نستكفي
حول وقوة
مستعان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint

A) بسم الله
B) أما بعد
C) الحمد لله
D) والله أعلم

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's opening supplication teaches that theological inquiry must begin with humble reliance on Allah for guidance, power, and sufficiency.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sit to study your faith, do you consciously and sincerely beg Allah for guidance, or do you rely on your intelligence and memory? Al-Ash'ari's first words are 'By Allah we seek guidance.' What would change in your learning if you made this your own practice starting today?
?
Think of a time you felt spiritually confused. Where did you turn first—to books, scholars, or to Allah in supplication? The lesson shows that genuine guidance begins with acknowledging 'there is no power nor strength except by Allah.' Are you willing to humble yourself and start there the next time you are lost?
?
The phrase 'لا حول ولا قوة إلا بالله' means that every ability you have—including the ability to understand—is a gift from Allah. Can you point to one area of your life where you act as if your success is your own doing? What concrete step will you take today to attribute that area fully to Allah's power?

Life of Imam al-Ashari

The Need to Know Imam al-Ash'ari's True Biography
Preface highlights the gap between claimed affiliation and actual knowledge of al-Ash'ari.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the author of Al-Ibanah begins by stressing the importance of knowing the actual biography and settled creed of Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari.

Core Idea
Many claim to follow al-Ash'ari, but few know who he truly was.

This passage opens the preface to Al-Ibanah with a striking observation: most people who claim to follow the Ash'ari school of theology are largely ignorant of the imam's own life and final creed. The author, Sheikh Hammad al-Ansari, sets out to remedy this by presenting a reliable biography drawn from authentic sources. As you read, notice how the preface immediately establishes the importance of knowing the imam beyond mere name.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and its translation
Read the Arabic passage slowly. Then read the English translation. Notice the formal opening with bismillah and the author's declaration of purpose.
The passage is from the preface of the second edition.
2
Identify the author's main concern
The author states that 'most people in the Islamic regions attribute their creed to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, yet they know nothing about him.' Ask yourself: why is this a problem?
The author emphasizes the need for accurate knowledge over mere attribution.
3
Reflect on the role of biography in theology
Think about why understanding the imam's own creed and biography matters for those who follow his school. The lineage that begins here (Ali ibn Isma'il...) roots him in a known chain.
The lineage connects him to the Companion Abu Musa al-Ash'ari.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مقدمة الطبعة الثانية
تقدیم
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم
لفضيلة الشيخ حماد بن محمد الأنصاري
الحمد لله الذي أرسل رسوله بالهدى ودين الحق ليظهره على الدين كله ولو كره المشركون .
والصلاة والسلام على نبينا محمد وعلى آله وصحبه ومن تبعهم بإحسان إلى يوم الدين .
وبعد : لما كان أكثر الناس في الأقطار الإسلامية ينتسب عقيدة إلى أبي الحسن الأشعري ، ومع ذلك لا يعرف شيئاً عن أبي الحسن الأشعري ، ولا عن عقيدته التي استقر عليها أمره أخيراً ، واستحق بها أن يكون من الأئمة المقتدى . أحببنا أن نفيد أولئك عن حقائق هذا الإمام المجهول عند كثير ممن ينتسب إليه وينتحل عقيدته ، حسب ما تتبعنا من المراجع المعتبرة . وقبل كل شيء أتحف القارئ بنبذة قليلة من ترجمة الأشعري فأقول وبالله أستعين :
التعريف بالإمام أبو الحسن الأشعري :
هو علي بن إسماعيل بن إسحاق بن إسماعيل بن عبد الله بن موسى بن أبي
Preface of the Second Edition
Presentation
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful
From the virtuous Sheikh Hammad ibn Muhammad al-Ansari
Praise be to Allah who sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth to make it prevail over all religions, even though the polytheists dislike it.
And peace and blessings be upon our Prophet Muhammad, his family, his companions, and those who follow them in righteousness until the Day of Judgment.
To proceed: Since most people in the Islamic regions attribute their creed to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, yet they know nothing about Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, nor about his creed upon which his matter finally settled, and by which he deserved to be among the imams followed. We wanted to inform those people about the realities of this imam, who is unknown to many of those who attribute themselves to him and adopt his creed, according to what we have traced from reliable references. And before everything else, I present to the reader a brief excerpt from the biography of al-Ash'ari, so I say, and with Allah I seek help:
Identification of Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari:
He is Ali ibn Isma'il ibn Ishaq ibn Isma'il ibn Abdullah ibn Musa ibn Abi
Source pages p. 5

Commentary
This passage is the beginning of a preface written by Sheikh Hammad al-Ansari for the second edition of Al-Ibanah. It sets the context by noting that many people who claim to follow Ash'ari's creed are actually ignorant of his true biography and final settled creed. The author expresses his desire to provide accurate information based on reliable sources. As a starting point, he offers a brief biography, beginning with the full name and lineage of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari: Ali ibn Isma'il ibn Ishaq ibn Isma'il ibn Abdullah ibn Musa ibn Abi... (the lineage is cut off here). The lineage traces back to the Companion Abu Musa al-Ash'ari (known from other sources). The passage emphasizes the importance of understanding the imam's true teachings rather than relying on assumptions.

Key Terms
أبو الحسن الأشعري
علي بن إسماعيل
ترجمة
الأئمة المقتدى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Many people assume that merely attributing their creed to al-Ash'ari is enough; they do not need to know his actual teachings.
Correction: The author points out that most people who claim to follow al-Ash'ari know nothing about him or his settled creed, so he aims to provide true knowledge from reliable sources.

Checkpoint
Why does the author begin this preface?
A) To praise al-Ash'ari as a scholar.
B) Because many people claim to follow al-Ash'ari but know nothing about him or his creed.
C) To advertise the book Al-Ibanah.
D) To refute opposing sects.

Key Takeaway
The preface establishes that true adherence to al-Ash'ari requires knowing his actual life and final creed, not just claiming his name.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The author states that many attribute themselves to al-Ash'ari without knowing his actual biography or creed. Is there a scholar or teacher you claim to follow? How much do you truly know about their life and final teachings, and what will you do to ensure your claim is not empty?
?
If someone asked you to explain the creed of the imam you claim to follow, could you articulate it from authentic sources? What step will you take this week to learn it properly?

Life of Imam al-Ashari

Biography of Imam al-Ash'ari and His Conversion
Al-Ansari introduces Imam al-Ash'ari and recounts his conversion from Mu'tazilism.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the biography of Imam al-Ash'ari and the story of his conversion from Mu'tazilism to the Sunnah through a prophetic dream.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's conversion from Mu'tazilism was guided by a dream of the Prophet.

This lesson introduces Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, the founder of the Ash'ari school. We learn about his lineage, his teachers in hadith and kalam, and the pivotal dream that led him to abandon Mu'tazilism and adhere to the Sunnah. The passage is from the introduction to his book al-Ibanah.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage from the introduction once, then the literal translation. This will give you the overall flow of the biography and conversion story.
Pay attention to the dream narrative as the turning point.
2
Identify key biographical details
From the passage, locate al-Ash'ari's full name, birth year, teachers (both hadith and kalam), and the scholars who recorded his biography. Note how his early training in Mu'tazilism came from his stepfather al-Jubba'i.
The conversion story is the highlight.
3
Reflect on the role of the dream
Consider the dream of the Prophet (ﷺ) as the catalyst. The passage says he prayed, slept, saw the Prophet, was told to adhere to the Sunnah, and then compared kalam with Quran and hadith, casting aside what differed.
This illustrates a divine intervention guiding his theological shift.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مقدمة الطبعة الثانية
تقديم
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم
لفضيلة الشيخ حماد بن محمد الأنصاري
الحمد لله الذي أرسل رسوله بالهدى ودين الحق ليظهره على الدين كله ولو كره المشركون .
والصلاة والسلام على نبينا محمد وعلى آله وصحبه ومن تبعهم بإحسان إلى يوم الدين .
وبعد : لما كان أكثر الناس في الأقطار الإسلامية ينتسب عقيدة إلى أبي الحسن الأشعري ، ومع ذلك لا يعرف شيئاً عن أبي الحسن الأشعري ، ولا عن عقيدته التي استقر عليها أمره أخيراً ، واستحق بها أن يكون من الأئمة المقتدى . أحببنا أن نفيد أولئك عن حقائق هذا الإمام المجهول عند كثير ممن ينتسب إليه وينتحل عقيدته ، حسب ما تتبعنا من المراجع المعتبرة . وقبل كل شيء أتحف القارئ بنبذة قليلة من ترجمة الأشعري فأقول وبالله أستعين :
التعريف بالإمام أبو الحسن الأشعري :
هو علي بن إسماعيل بن إسحاق بن إسماعيل بن عبد الله بن موسى بن أبي بردة بن أبي موسى الأشعري ، ولد سنة ستين ومئتين من الهجرة النبوية ، ترجم له أبو القاسم علي بن الحسن بن هبة الله بن عساكر الدمشقي في كتابه « تبيين كذب المفتري فيما نسب إلى أبي الحسن الأشعري » ، والخطيب البغدادي في " تاريخ الإسلام " وابن خلكان في « وفيات الأعيان » ، والذهبي في " تاريخ الإسلام " ، وابن كثير في « البداية والنهاية » وطبقات الشافعية » والتاج السبكي في طبقات الشافعية الكبرى » وابن فرحون المالكي في « الديباج المذهب في أعيان المذهب » ومرتضى الزبيدي في إتحاف السادة المتقين بشرح أسرار إحياء علوم الدين ) ، وابن العماد الحنبلي في ( شذرات الذهب في أعيان من ذهب ) وغيرهم .
دخل هذا الإمام بغداد وأخذ الحديث عن زكريا بن يحيى الساجي ، أحد أئمة الحديث والفقه ، وعن أبي خليفة الجمحي وسهل بن سرح ومحمد بن يعقوب المقري وعبد الرحمن بن خلف البصريين ، وروى عنهم كثيراً في تفسيره المختزن » ، وأخذ علم الكلام عن شيخه زوج أمه أبي علي الجبائي شيخ المعتزلة .
ولما تبحر في كلام الاعتزال وبلغ فيه الغاية كان يورد الأسئلة على أستاذه في الدرس ولا يجد فيها جواباً شافياً فتحير في ذلك . فحكى عنه أنه قال : وقع في صدري في بعض الليالي شيء مما كنت فيه من العقائد فقمت وصليت ركعتين وسألت الله تعالى أن يهديني الطريق المستقيم ونمت فرأيت رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم في المنام فشكوت إليه بعض ما بي من الأمر ، فقال لي رسول الله : « عليك بسنتي » فانتبهت !! وعارضت مسائل الكلام بما وجدت في القرآن والأخبار ، فأثبته ونبذت ما سواه ورائي ظهرياً .
قال أبو بكر أحمد بن علي بن ثابت المعروف بالخطيب البغدادي المتوفى سنة ٤٦٣ هـ في الجزء الحادي عشر من تاريخه المشهور صفحة ٣٤٦ : « أبو
Introduction to the Second Edition
Presentation
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful
By His Eminence Sheikh Hammad bin Muhammad al-Ansari
Praise be to Allah who sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth to make it prevail over all religions, even if the polytheists dislike it.
And prayers and peace be upon our Prophet Muhammad, his family, his Companions, and those who follow them in goodness until the Day of Judgment.
To proceed: Since most people in the Islamic regions attribute (their) creed to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, yet they do not know anything about Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, nor about his creed which he finally settled upon, and by which he deserved to be among the imams followed. We desired to inform those people about the realities of this imam, who is unknown to many who attribute themselves to him and adopt his creed, according to what we have traced from reliable references. And before everything, I present to the reader a short excerpt from the biography of al-Ash'ari, so I say, and with Allah I seek help:
Introduction to Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari:
He is Ali bin Ismail bin Ishaq bin Ismail bin Abdullah bin Musa bin Abi Burda bin Abi Musa al-Ash'ari. He was born in the year 260 AH. His biography was recorded by Abu al-Qasim Ali bin al-Hasan bin Hibat Allah bin Asakir al-Dimashqi in his book "Tabyin Kadhib al-Muftari fima Nusiba ila Abi al-Hasan al-Ash'ari," and al-Khatib al-Baghdadi in "Tarikh al-Islam," and Ibn Khalikan in "Wafayat al-A'yan," and al-Dhahabi in "Tarikh al-Islam," and Ibn Kathir in "al-Bidaya wa al-Nihaya" and "Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya," and al-Taj al-Subki in "Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya al-Kubra," and Ibn Farhun al-Maliki in "al-Dibaj al-Mudhhab fi A'yan al-Madhhab," and Murtada al-Zabidi in "Ithaf al-Sada al-Muttaqin bi-Sharh Asrar Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din," and Ibn al-'Imad al-Hanbali in "Shadharat al-Dhahab fi A'yan man Dhahab," and others.
This Imam entered Baghdad and took hadith from Zakariyya bin Yahya al-Saji, one of the imams of hadith and fiqh, and from Abu Khalifa al-Jumahi, Sahl bin Sarh, Muhammad bin Ya'qub al-Muqri', and Abd al-Rahman bin Khalaf al-Basriyyin, and he narrated much from them in his tafsir "al-Mukhtazan." He took kalam from his teacher, the husband of his mother, Abu Ali al-Jubba'i, the leader of the Mu'tazila.
When he became deeply versed in the speech of I'tizal and reached its utmost, he would pose questions to his teacher in the lesson and would not find a satisfactory answer, so he was perplexed about that. It is narrated from him that he said: "Something of what I was in of (the) beliefs fell into my chest one night, so I stood and prayed two rak'as and asked Allah Almighty to guide me to the straight path, and I slept. Then I saw the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, in a dream, and I complained to him about some of the matter that was with me. The Messenger of Allah said to me: 'You must adhere to my Sunnah.' So I woke up!! And I contrasted the issues of kalam with what I found in the Qur'an and the reports, so I affirmed it and cast aside what was other than it behind my back."
Abu Bakr Ahmad bin Ali bin Thabit, known as al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, who died in 463 AH, said in part eleven of his famous Tarikh, page 346: "Abu
Source pages p. 5

Commentary
This passage is a biographical introduction to Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, written by Sheikh Hammad al-Ansari as a preface to the second edition of al-Ash'ari's work "al-Ibanah." The author begins with the basmalah and hamdalah, then states his purpose: many people claim to follow al-Ash'ari's creed but know nothing about the imam himself or his final settled creed. He aims to provide information about this imam who is unknown to many of his followers, based on reliable sources.

He then gives a brief biography: al-Ash'ari's full lineage is traced back to Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, the famous Companion. He was born in 260 AH. His biography has been recorded by many prominent scholars: Ibn Asakir, al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, Ibn Khalikan, al-Dhahabi, Ibn Kathir, al-Subki, Ibn Farhun, Murtada al-Zabidi, Ibn al-'Imad, and others.

Al-Ash'ari studied hadith in Baghdad from several renowned scholars, such as Zakariyya al-Saji and others, and narrated from them in his commentary "al-Mukhtazan." However, he also studied kalam (theological speculation) under his stepfather, Abu Ali al-Jubba'i, who was the head of the Mu'tazila. This is the early phase of al-Ash'ari's life when he was a Mu'tazili.

The narrative then describes his famous conversion. Al-Ash'ari became deeply skilled in Mu'tazili kalam, but he began to ask his teacher questions that remained unanswered. This caused him doubt. One night, he felt turmoil in his chest regarding his beliefs. He prayed two rak'as, asked Allah for guidance, and then slept. In a dream, he saw the Prophet Muhammad (ﷺ) and complained to him. The Prophet instructed him to adhere to the Sunnah. Upon waking, he examined the issues of kalam against the Qur'an and prophetic reports (hadith). He affirmed what agreed with them and cast aside all else—meaning he abandoned Mu'tazili doctrines and adopted the Sunni path.

The passage ends with a citation from al-Khatib al-Baghdadi's Tarikh, which likely continues to confirm this story, but the OCR cuts off. The lesson thus covers the biographical background and the key conversion story that shaped al-Ash'ari's final school.

Key Terms
أبو الحسن الأشعري
الاعتزال
سنة
الكلام
أبو علي الجبائي
المنام

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think al-Ash'ari remained a Mu'tazili throughout his life.
Correction: The passage shows he had a clear conversion: after a dream of the Prophet, he contrasted kalam with Quran and hadith and abandoned Mu'tazilism, adhering to the Sunnah.

Checkpoint
What prompted Imam al-Ash'ari to abandon Mu'tazilism and adhere to the Sunnah?
A) A debate with his teacher al-Jubba'i that he won
B) A dream in which the Prophet (ﷺ) told him to adhere to the Sunnah
C) A letter from a Sunni scholar
D) Reading a book on hadith

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Ash'ari's definitive break from Mu'tazilism came after a dream of the Prophet (ﷺ) instructed him to adhere to the Sunnah.

Life of Imam al-Ashari
beginner
5 min
The Early Life and Teachers of Imam al-Ash'ari
Biographical details of al-Ash'ari's birth, biographers, and teachers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the scholarly background of Imam al-Ash'ari, including his birth, the historians who documented his life, and the two dimensions of his education in hadith and kalam.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's education combined hadith and kalam, preparing him to defend Sunni creed.

Before we can understand al-Ash'ari's theological contributions, we must first appreciate his formation as a scholar. He was born in 260 AH and studied under the leading traditionists and Mu'tazili theologians of his time. This biographical passage introduces the man behind the school.

Guided Path
1
Note the birth year and biographers
Observe that al-Ash'ari was born in 260 AH and that many famous historians and biographers wrote about him, showing his importance.
2
Identify his teachers in hadith
Find the list of hadith scholars he studied with in Baghdad and Basra. Notice they were authorities in both hadith and fiqh.
This shows he was rooted in transmitted sciences.
3
Identify his teacher in kalam
See that he studied kalam under Abu Ali al-Jubba'i, the leader of the Mu'tazila. This explains his early training in rational theology.
This dual training is crucial for his later transformation.

بردة بن أبي موسى الأشعري ، ولد سنة ستين ومئتين من الهجرة النبوية ، ترجم له أبو القاسم علي بن الحسن بن هبة الله بن عساكر الدمشقي في كتابه « تبيين كذب المفتري فيما نسب إلى أبي الحسن الأشعري » ، والخطيب البغدادي في " تاريخ الإسلام » وابن خلكان في « وفيات الأعيان » ، والذهبي في " تاريخ الإسلام » ، وابن كثير في « البداية والنهاية » وطبقات الشافعية » والتاج السبكي في طبقات الشافعية الكبرى » وابن فرحون المالكي في « الديباج المذهب في أعيان المذهب » ومرتضى الزبيدي في إتحاف السادة المتقين بشرح أسرار إحياء علوم الدين ) ، وابن العماد الحنبلي في ( شذرات الذهب في أعيان من ذهب » وغيرهم . دخل هذا الإمام بغداد وأخذ الحديث عن زكريا بن يحيى الساجي ، أحد أئمة الحديث والفقه ، وعن أبي خليفة الجمحي وسهل بن سرح ومحمد بن يعقوب المقري وعبد الرحمن بن خلف البصريين ، وروى عنهم كثيراً في تفسيره المختزن » ، وأخذ علم الكلام عن شيخه زوج أمه أبي علي الجبائي شيخ المعتزلة .
Braida ibn Abi Musa al-Ash'ari, born in the year 260 AH. He was biographized by Abu al-Qasim Ali ibn al-Hasan ibn Hibat Allah ibn Asakir al-Dimashqi in his book 'Tabyin Kadhib al-Muftari fi ma Nusiba ila Abi al-Hasan al-Ash'ari', and al-Khatib al-Baghdadi in 'Tarikh al-Islam', and Ibn Khallikan in 'Wafayat al-A'yan', and al-Dhahabi in 'Tarikh al-Islam', and Ibn Kathir in 'al-Bidaya wa al-Nihaya' and 'Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya', and al-Taj al-Subki in 'Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya al-Kubra', and Ibn Farhun al-Maliki in 'al-Dibaj al-Mudhhab fi A'yan al-Madhhab', and Murtada al-Zabidi in 'Ithaf al-Sada al-Muttaqin bi Sharh Asrar Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din', and Ibn al-Imad al-Hanbali in 'Shadharat al-Dhahab fi A'yan min Dhahab', and others. This imam entered Baghdad and took hadith from Zakariya ibn Yahya al-Saji, one of the imams of hadith and jurisprudence, and from Abu Khalifa al-Jumahi, Sahl ibn Sarh, Muhammad ibn Ya'qub al-Muqri, and Abd al-Rahman ibn Khalaf al-Basriyyin, and narrated from them extensively in his Tafsir al-Mukhtazan. And he took kalam from his teacher, the husband of his mother, Abu Ali al-Jubba'i, the shaykh of the Mu'tazila.
Source pages p. 6

Commentary
This passage is a biographical introduction to Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari. It first lists his birth year and the numerous scholars who compiled his biography, establishing his scholarly standing. Then it details his education: he studied hadith under notable traditionists in Baghdad and Basra, and kalam under his teacher (and stepfather) Abu Ali al-Jubba'i, the leading Mu'tazili of his time. The passage presents al-Ash'ari as a scholar trained in both transmitted and rational sciences, which sets the stage for his later transformation and defense of Sunni creed.

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think al-Ash'ari only studied kalam and was not trained in hadith.
Correction: This biography clearly shows he studied extensively under hadith scholars, establishing his credentials in both fields.

Checkpoint
Who was al-Ash'ari's teacher in kalam?
A) al-Khatib al-Baghdadi
B) Abu Ali al-Jubba'i
C) Zakariya al-Saji
D) Ibn Khallikan

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Ash'ari was trained in both hadith and kalam, which equipped him to later refute Mu'tazili positions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You may have teachers with whom you disagree on important matters. How can you learn from them without compromising your principles, as al-Ash'ari learned from al-Jubba'i before refuting him? What step will you take this week to engage with a differing perspective while grounding yourself in the truth?
?
Al-Ash'ari studied both hadith and kalam. Which area of Islamic knowledge are you neglecting because it feels uncomfortable or difficult? Will you commit to studying it, even for 10 minutes a day, starting today?
?
The source lists many biographers who preserved al-Ash'ari's legacy. Who is documenting your spiritual journey? Are you surrounding yourself with people who will testify to your commitment to truth, or are you content to be forgotten?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
beginner
4 min
Al-Ash'ari's Conversion Dream
Narrative of al-Ash'ari's dream and his turn to Sunnah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari's personal crisis and prophetic dream led him to abandon Mu'tazilism and commit to the Sunnah.

Core Idea
A prophetic dream turned al-Ash'ari from rationalist confusion to Sunnah-based certainty.

Al-Ash'ari was a leading Mu'tazili scholar, but he became dissatisfied with his teacher's answers. One night, he prayed for guidance and saw the Prophet in a dream, who commanded him to follow the Sunnah. This dream marked a turning point in his life, leading him to evaluate kalam issues against the Qur'an and hadith.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to understand the narrative flow.
Notice al-Ash'ari's emotional state: 'perplexed' and seeking guidance.
2
Identify the before and after
Contrast al-Ash'ari's state before the dream (deep Mu'tazili knowledge but confusion) with after (following the Sunnah).
3
Trace the sequence of events
Follow the steps: prayer, dream, complaint, command from the Prophet, awakening, and his subsequent action of comparing kalam to Qur'an and hadith.
This sequence shows the process of his conversion.
4
Reflect on the methodological shift
Notice that al-Ash'ari did not abandon kalam entirely; he compared it to revelation. This is key to understanding his later theology.
His approach became 'revelation-first' while still engaging with rational theology.
5
Consider the role of dreams in Islam
Think about how this dream is presented as a divine guidance. What does it tell us about the legitimacy of dreams as a source of guidance?
The common misunderstanding warns that the dream itself is not a proof, but a personal experience.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولما تبحر في كلام الاعتزال وبلغ فيه الغاية كان یورد الأسئلة على أستاذه في الدرس ولا يجد فيها جواباً شافياً فتحير في ذلك . فحكى عنه أنه قال : وقع في صدري في بعض الليالي شيء مما كنت فيه العقائد فقمت وصليت ركعتين وسألت الله تعالى أن يهديني الطريق المستقيم ونمت فرأيت رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم في المنام فشكوت إليه بعض ما بي من الأمر ، فقال لي رسول الله : « عليك بسنتي ) فانتبهت !! وعارضت مسائل الكلام بما وجدت في القرآن والأخبار ، فأثبته ونبذت ما سواه ورائي ظهراً .
And when he became deeply versed in the speech of the Mu'tazila and reached the utmost point in it, he used to pose questions to his teacher in the lesson and could not find a satisfactory answer for them, so he became perplexed about that. It is related from him that he said: 'One night, something came into my heart regarding the beliefs I was in. So I got up, prayed two rak'as, and asked Allah the Exalted to guide me to the straight path. Then I slept and saw the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, in a dream. I complained to him of some of my condition. The Messenger of Allah said to me: "Follow my Sunnah." So I awoke! Then I compared the issues of kalam with what I found in the Qur'an and the reports, so I affirmed it and cast aside everything else behind my back.'
Source pages p. 6

Commentary
This passage narrates al-Ash'ari's spiritual crisis. He was deeply learned in Mu'tazili theology but found his teacher's answers insufficient. In a dream, the Prophet (sallallahu alayhi wa sallam) commanded him to adhere to the Sunnah. After waking, he tested the issues of kalam against the Qur'an and hadith, accepted what conformed, and rejected the rest. This illustrates his methodological shift from pure rationalism to a revealed-based approach.

Key Terms

منام
رؤيا
هداية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that al-Ash'ari's dream alone proves the truth of Sunni Islam or that dreams are a source of theological authority.
Correction: The passage shows that after the dream, he did not rely solely on it; he compared the issues of kalam with the Qur'an and hadith. The dream was a personal catalyst, not a public proof.

Checkpoint
What did the Prophet tell al-Ash'ari in the dream?
A) Continue with the Mu'tazila
B) Follow my Sunnah
C) Write more books
D) Debate your teacher

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's dream of the Prophet guided him to abandon Mu'tazilism and embrace the Sunnah as his source of theology.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When has your own knowledge or study left you feeling more confused rather than certain about your faith? Did you turn to Allah with the same urgency that al-Ash'ari did—praying, begging for guidance—or did you simply push the doubts aside?
?
Al-Ash'ari did not let his spiritual experience become the final authority; he tested it against the Qur'an and Sunnah. In your own life, have you ever treated an emotional or spiritual experience as if it overrides what revelation clearly teaches? Are you willing to subject every experience to that test?
?
This passage shows a man who was both deeply intellectual and deeply spiritual. Which of these two sides is underdeveloped in your own relationship with Allah, and what one step will you take this week to strengthen it?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
beginner
4 min
Praise of al-Ash'ari by al-Khatib and Ibn Farhun
Two scholars praise al-Ash'ari's refutation of heretical groups.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari was esteemed by later Sunni scholars as a defender of orthodoxy against various heretical groups.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari was hailed as a champion who subdued the Mu'tazila and other innovators.

This lesson presents two historical commendations of al-Ash'ari from authoritative Sunni scholars. Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi and Ibn Farhun both testify to his role in refuting heretical sects and defending orthodox theology. These quotes demonstrate that al-Ash'ari was not a marginal figure but was widely respected among the Sunni mainstream.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then its English translation. Notice the names and titles used for al-Ash'ari and the groups he refuted.
The passage contains two distinct testimonies.
2
Identify the scholars and their credentials
Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi was a famous historian and hadith scholar; Ibn Farhun was a Maliki jurist. Their praise carries weight.
3
Examine the metaphor used by al-Khatib
Al-Khatib says the Mu'tazila were 'confined in sesame pods.' Reflect on what this vivid image conveys about their defeat.
Sesame pods are tiny; the metaphor suggests total humiliation.
4
Understand the significance of Ibn Farhun's quote
Ibn Farhun mentions that al-Ash'ari was praised by Imam Ibn Abi Zayd al-Qayrawani, a key Maliki figure, showing cross-madhhab acceptance.
5
Reflect on the doctrinal context
Think about why Sunni scholars felt the need to highlight al-Ash'ari's defense against Mu'tazila, Rafida, Jahmiyya, etc.
This connects to the broader Sunni-Ash'ari identity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال أبو بكر أحمد بن علي بن ثابت المعرف بالخطيب البغدادي المتوفى سنة ٤٦٣ هـ في الجزء الحادي عشر من تاريخه المشهور صفحة ٣٤٦ : « أبو الحسن الأشعري المتكلم صاحب الكتب والتصانيف في الرد على الملحدة وغيرهم من المعتزلة والرافضة والجهمية والخوارج وسائر أصناف المبتدعة . إلى أن قال : وكانت المعتزلة قد رفعوا رؤوسهم حتى أظهر الله تعالى الأشعري فحجزهم في أقماع السمسم ) قال ابن فرحون في الديباج : ( أثنى على أبي الحسن الأشعري أبو محمد بن أبي زيد القيراوني وغيره من أئمة المسلمين ) .
Abu Bakr Ahmad ibn Ali ibn Thabit, known as al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, died 463 AH, said in the eleventh part of his famous history, page 346: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, the mutakallim, author of books and works in refutation of the atheists and other Mu'tazila, Rafida, Jahmiyya, Khawarij, and all types of innovators.' He also said: 'The Mu'tazila had raised their heads until Allah the Exalted manifested al-Ash'ari, and He confined them in the pods of sesame.' Ibn Farhun said in al-Dibaj: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was praised by Abu Muhammad ibn Abi Zayd al-Qayrawani and other imams of the Muslims.'
Source pages p. 7

Commentary
This passage presents two commendations of al-Ash'ari from prominent later scholars. Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi first describes him as a mutakallim and author of books refuting various heretical groups, including Mu'tazila, Rafida, Jahmiyya, and Khawarij. He then emphasizes that al-Ash'ari was raised by God to halt the dominance of the Mu'tazila, using the striking metaphor of confining them in sesame pods—indicating their abasement. Ibn Farhun adds that al-Ash'ari was praised by the Maliki imam Abu Muhammad ibn Abi Zayd al-Qayrawani and other leading scholars, further solidifying his acceptance within Sunni orthodoxy.

Key Terms
الملحدة
المعتزلة
الرافضة
الجهمية
الخوارج
المبتدعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think al-Ash'ari was a controversial figure rejected by mainstream Sunni scholars.
Correction: These quotes from al-Khatib al-Baghdadi and Ibn Farhun show that al-Ash'ari was praised by prominent Sunni authorities, including a Maliki imam, and was recognized as a defender of orthodoxy.

Checkpoint
According to al-Khatib, what was the state of the Mu'tazila before al-Ash'ari appeared?
A) They were in decline
B) They had raised their heads
C) They were ignored
D) They were persecuted

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari was widely praised by Sunni scholars as the one who defeated heretical innovations, particularly the Mu'tazila.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you know what the Mu'tazila, Rafida, and Jahmiyya actually believed, or have you only repeated labels? This lesson shows al-Ash'ari was raised by Allah to subdue them. Have you ever thanked Allah for preserving the Sunnah through such scholars, or have you taken orthodoxy for granted? Ask yourself: if the Mu'tazila had prevailed, would your faith look the same? And what are you doing today to ensure you are not aligning with any form of innovation?
?
Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, a Hanbali, praised al-Ash'ari. This challenges the narrative that Hanbalis and Ash'aris are irreconcilable. Is there a person or group you have avoided or dismissed because of a label? Are you willing to recognize truth and virtue in those you disagree with, as these scholars did? What would it cost you to do so?

Life of Imam al-Ashari

The First Testimony: Al-Ash'ari Humiliates the Mu'tazila
First testimony: al-Ash'ari confines Mu'tazila to cones of sesame.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how early scholars portrayed al-Ash'ari as a divine instrument who crushed the Mu'tazila, symbolized by 'cones of sesame.'

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari confined the Mu'tazila in cones of sesame—a metaphor for total defeat.

The first testimony in this passage uses a striking agricultural metaphor to describe al-Ash'ari's impact. It portrays him as the one who cut the Mu'tazila down to size after they had grown arrogant. This sets the stage for appreciating his role as a defender of orthodoxy.

Guided Path
1
Read the testimony
Read the Arabic and translation of the first part: 'Al-Hasan al-Ash'ari... until he confined them in cones of sesame.'
Notice the metaphor 'cones of sesame'—a symbol of extreme smallness.
2
Understand the context
The Mu'tazila had become powerful; al-Ash'ari's emergence was seen as divine intervention to humble them.
3
Reflect on the metaphor
Ask yourself: Why would 'cones of sesame' be chosen? What does it convey about the scale of al-Ash'ari's victory?
This metaphor emphasizes total humiliation and insignificance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الحسن الأشعري المتكلم صاحب الكتب والتصانيف في الرد على الملحدة وغيرهم من المعتزلة والرافضة والجهمية والخوارج وسائر أصناف المبتدعة . إلى أن قال : وكانت المعتزلة قد رفعوا رؤوسهم حتى أظهر الله تعالى الأشعري فحجزهم في أقماع السمسم ) قال ابن فرحون في الديباج : ( أثنى على أبي الحسن الأشعري أبو محمد بن أبي زيد القيراوني وغيره من أئمة المسلمين ) . اهـ . وقال ابن العماد الحنبلي في ( الشذرات ( الجزء الثاني صفحة ٣٠٣ ( ومما بيض به أبو الحسن الأشعري وجوه أهل السنة النبوية وسود به رايات أهل الاعتزال والجهمية ، فأبان به وجه الحق الأبلج ، ولصدور أهل الإيمان والعرفان أثلج ، مناظرته مع شيخه الجبائي التي قصم ظهر كل مبتدع مرائي وهي أعني المناظرة كما قال ابن خلكان :
Al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, the theologian, author of books and compilations in refutation of the atheists and others from the Mu'tazila, Rafida, Jahmiyya, Khawarij, and all classes of innovators. To the extent that he said: 'The Mu'tazila had raised their heads until Allah the Exalted manifested al-Ash'ari, and he confined them in the cones of sesame.' Ibn Farhun said in al-Dibaj: 'Abu Muhammad ibn Abi Zayd al-Qayrawani and other imams of the Muslims praised Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari.' End. And Ibn al-'Imad al-Hanbali said in al-Shadharat (part 2, p. 303): 'Among the things by which Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari whitened the faces of the people of the Prophetic Sunnah and blackened the banners of the people of I'tizal and Jahmiyya, thereby making the white face of truth manifest and cooling the hearts of the people of faith and knowledge, is his debate with his teacher al-Jubba'i which broke the back of every deceitful innovator. That is the debate as Ibn Khallikan said:'
Source pages p. 7

Commentary
This passage is a collection of testimonies praising Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari's role in defending orthodox Sunni Islam. The first testimony likens him to a divine instrument that confined the Mu'tazila to 'cones of sesame,' a metaphor for utter humiliation. Ibn Farhun then quotes Ibn Abi Zayd al-Qayrawani and other imams praising al-Ash'ari. Finally, Ibn al-'Imad al-Hanbali highlights the famous debate with al-Jubba'i as a defining moment that 'whitened the faces of Ahl al-Sunna' and 'blackened the banners' of the Mu'tazila and Jahmiyya, introducing the debate as a proof of al-Ash'ari's vindication. The passage serves to establish his scholarly authority before presenting his arguments.

Key Terms
الملحدة
المعتزلة
الرافضة
الجهمية
الخوارج

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the first testimony, what did Allah do through al-Ash'ari regarding the Mu'tazila?
A) He elevated their status.
B) He confined them in 'cones of sesame.'
C) He made them rulers.
D) He converted them to Sunni Islam.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's emergence was seen as a divine act that humiliated the Mu'tazila, symbolized by confining them in cones of sesame.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt proud in your understanding of religion, looking down on those who differ? The Mu'tazila 'raised their heads' until Allah humbled them. Where in your life do you need to lower your head in humility before the truth? What step will you take today to abandon arrogance in your knowledge?
?
Al-Ash'ari was raised by Allah to defeat falsehood. When you see false ideologies spreading, do you remain silent hoping someone else will defend the truth? What personal responsibility do you have in your circle to speak up for correct belief, even if it costs you comfort? Commit to one concrete action this week.

The Second Testimony: Praise from Ibn Abi Zayd and Other Imams
Second testimony: Ibn Farhun records praise from Ibn Abi Zayd and others.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will recognize that al-Ash'ari was praised by major Sunni imams, as recorded by Ibn Farhun in al-Dibaj.

Core Idea
Sunni imams like Ibn Abi Zayd al-Qayrawani praised al-Ash'ari.

The second testimony comes from a biographical work, al-Dibaj, by Ibn Farhun. It provides a direct quote from the famous Maliki scholar Ibn Abi Zayd al-Qayrawani, along with other unnamed imams, praising al-Ash'ari. This shows that al-Ash'ari's orthodoxy was recognized by leading figures of his time.

Guided Path
1
Read the testimony
Read the Arabic and translation of: 'Ibn Farhun said in al-Dibaj: "Abu Muhammad ibn Abi Zayd al-Qayrawani and other imams of the Muslims praised Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari."'
Ibn Farhun is a later scholar quoting earlier authorities.
2
Identify the source
Note that the testimony is transmitted through two layers: Ibn Farhun quotes Ibn Abi Zayd and others. This strengthens the credibility.
3
Recognize the significance
Ibn Abi Zayd al-Qayrawani is a highly respected Maliki scholar. His praise is a strong endorsement of al-Ash'ari's orthodoxy.
This counters any claim that al-Ash'ari was rejected by Sunni scholars.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

The Third Testimony: The Debate That Whitened Sunna's Faces
Third testimony: Ibn al-'Imad praises al-Ash'ari's debate with al-Jubba'i.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari's debate with al-Jubba'i was seen as a pivotal victory that defended Sunni orthodoxy and exposed innovation.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's debate with al-Jubba'i 'whitened the faces of Ahl al-Sunna' and 'blackened the banners of the innovators.'

The final testimony is from Ibn al-'Imad al-Hanbali, who emphasizes the famous debate between al-Ash'ari and his former teacher al-Jubba'i. This debate is portrayed as a turning point that visibly distinguished truth from falsehood. The vivid imagery of whitening and blackening underscores its impact.

Guided Path
1
Read the testimony
Read the Arabic and translation: 'Among the things by which Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari whitened the faces of the people of the Prophetic Sunnah... is his debate with his teacher al-Jubba'i.'
The debate is highlighted as the key event.
2
Understand the metaphor
'Whitened faces' symbolizes honor and triumph; 'blackened banners' symbolizes defeat and disgrace for the opponents.
This is a common Arabic rhetorical device.
3
Recognize the historical context
Al-Jubba'i was a leading Mu'tazili scholar and al-Ash'ari's teacher. Defeating him in debate was a major blow to Mu'tazilism.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
beginner
5 min
Al-Ash'ari's Sunni Creed and Madhhab Dispute
Subki affirms al-Ash'ari's Sunni creed and the madhhab dispute.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that al-Ash'ari's creed was identical to Imam Ahmad's, and that while there is dispute over his fiqh madhhab, his theological legacy is universally claimed by Sunni schools.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's creed is Ahmad's, but his madhhab is debated—his legacy unites Sunni schools.

This lesson establishes al-Ash'ari's place as the leader of Ahl al-Sunna after Imam Ahmad, drawing from the authoritative testimony of Taj al-Din al-Subki. It also clarifies the confusion over his fiqh school, showing that his impact transcends madhhab boundaries.

Guided Path
1
Read al-Subki's testimony
Carefully read the Arabic passage where al-Subki affirms al-Ash'ari's creed as that of Imam Ahmad. Note the emphatic language 'لا شك في ذلك ولا ارتياب'.
Al-Ash'ari himself repeatedly stated this in his writings.
2
Understand the madhhab dispute
Identify the three schools that claim al-Ash'ari: Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanafi. Reflect on Ibn 'Asakir's report that he was actually Maliki, but his works made him a symbol of Sunni theology.
This dispute shows his wide influence.
3
Note the death date
Ibn Furak states he died in 324 AH. Consider how his life's work continued after his repentance.
This date is important for historical context.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال تاج الدين السبكي في ( طبقات الشافعية الكبرى » : أبو الحسن الأشعري كبير أهل السنة بعد الإمام أحمد بن حنبل وعقيدته عقيدة الإمام أحمد رحمه الله واحدة لا شك في ذلك ولا ارتياب ، وبه صرح الأشعري في تصانيفه وذكره غير ما مرة من أن عقيدتي هي عقيدة الإمام المبجل أحمد بن حنبل ، هذه عبارة الشيخ أبي الحسن في غير موضع من كلامه » . اهـ وفضائل أبي الحسن الأشعري ومناقبه أكثر من أن يمكن حصرها في هذه العجالة ، ومن وقف على تواليفه بعد توبته من الاعتزال رأى أن الله تعالى قد أمده بمواد توفيقه ، وأقامه لنصرة الحق والذب عن طريقه . وقد تنازع فيه أهل المذاهب ، فالمالكي يدعي أنه مالكي ، والشافعي يزعم أنه شافعي ، والحنفي كذلك . قال ابن عساكر : لقيت الشيخ الفاضل رافعاً الحمال الفقيه ، فذكر لي عن شيوخه أن أبا الحسن الأشعري كان مالكياً فنسب من تعلق اليوم بمذهب أهل السنة وتفقه في معرفة أصول الدين من سائر المذاهب إلى الأشعري لكثرة تواليفه وكثرة قراءة الناس لها . قال ابن فورك: توفي أبو الحسن الأشعري سنة ٣٢٤ هـ . اهـ وبعد ذكر هذه النتفة من ترجمة هذا الإمام نذكر ما يلي إثبات رجوعه عن الاعتزال ، وإثبات نسبة الإبانة إليه ، ننقل ذلك من المراجع الموثوق بها ، فنقول وبالله التوفيق . رجوع أبي الحسن الأشعري عن الاعتزال إلى عقيدة السلف قال الحافظ مؤرخ الشام أبو القاسم علي بن الحسن بن هبة الله بن الدمشقي المتوفى سنة ٥٧١هـ في كتابه ( التبيين ( قال أبو بكر إسماعيل بن أبي محمد بن إسحاق الأزدي القيرواني المعروف بابن عزرة : إن أبا الحسن الأشعري كان معتزلياً وأنه أقام على مذهب الاعتزال أربعين سنة ، وكان لهم
And Taj al-Din al-Subki said in 'The Greater Classes of Shafi'is': 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is the great authority of Ahl al-Sunna after Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, and his creed is the creed of Imam Ahmad, may Allah have mercy on him, without doubt or misgiving. Al-Ash'ari stated this in his books and mentioned it more than once: that my creed is the creed of the venerated Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal. This is the expression of Shaykh Abu al-Hasan in more than one place in his speech.' End. And the virtues and merits of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari are too many to be encompassed in this brief account. Whoever looks at his writings after his repentance from Mu'tazilism sees that Allah the Exalted supplied him with materials of His success and raised him to defend the truth and protect His path. The people of the schools disputed over him: the Maliki claims he is Maliki, the Shafi'i claims he is Shafi'i, and the Hanafi likewise. Ibn 'Asakir said: 'I met the virtuous shaykh Rafi' al-Hammal al-Faqih, and he told me from his shaykhs that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was Maliki, so whoever today adheres to the Madhhab of Ahl al-Sunna and studies the science of the principles of religion from all schools is attributed to al-Ash'ari because of his many writings and the many people's study of them.' Ibn Furak said: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari died in the year 324 AH.' End. After mentioning this selection from the biography of this Imam, we mention the following: establishing his return from Mu'tazilism, and establishing the attribution of al-Ibana to him. We transmit that from reliable references, so we say, and with Allah is success. The return of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari from Mu'tazilism to the creed of the Salaf. Al-Hafiz, the historian of Syria, Abu al-Qasim 'Ali ibn al-Hasan ibn Hibat Allah al-Dimashqi, who died in 571 AH, said in his book al-Tabyin: 'Abu Bakr Isma'il ibn Abi Muhammad ibn Ishaq al-Azdi al-Qayrawani, known as Ibn 'Uzra, said: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili and he adhered to the Mu'tazili school for forty years, and he had...'
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is a biographical summary that establishes al-Ash'ari's orthodox Sunni position and begins the narrative of his repentance from Mu'tazilism. Taj al-Din al-Subki asserts that al-Ash'ari is the head of Ahl al-Sunna after Imam Ahmad, and that his creed is identical to Ahmad's, a point al-Ash'ari himself repeatedly affirmed in his writings. The author then notes that al-Ash'ari's virtues are vast, and that his post-repentance works show divine support. A dispute over his madhhab is recorded: Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanafi each claim him; Ibn 'Asakir's report suggests he was a Maliki who became the eponym of Sunni theological method due to his many works. His death in 324 AH is given via Ibn Furak. Finally, the author states the purpose of the coming section: to prove al-Ash'ari's return from Mu'tazilism and the attribution of the book al-Ibana to him. The passage ends with a citation from al-Tabyin, quoting Ibn 'Uzra that al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili for forty years.

Key Terms
طبقات الشافعية
أبو الحسن الأشعري
ابن عساكر
ابن فورك

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think al-Ash'ari was a Shafi'i in fiqh because his theology is closely associated with Shafi'ism.
Correction: The passage shows that al-Ash'ari was actually Maliki according to Ibn 'Asakir's report, and that all schools claim him, indicating his broad influence.

Checkpoint
According to Ibn Furak, in what year did al-Ash'ari die?
A) 320 AH
B) 324 AH
C) 330 AH
D) 340 AH

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's creed is the Sunni creed of Imam Ahmad, and his theological works are foundational for all Sunni schools, regardless of his personal fiqh affiliation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear about debates between Ash'aris and Hanbalis today, does this passage's explicit statement that al-Ash'ari's creed is exactly the same as Ahmad's humble your pride in your own group? Will you let that humility change how you speak about others who follow different scholarly traditions?
?
Al-Ash'ari repented from Mu'tazilism and then devoted his entire scholarly output to defending the truth. Is there a false idea, a sin, or a comfortable lifestyle you have been unwilling to fully repent from? What would it look like for you to turn from it completely and spend your energy on truth?
?
The passage reports that each madhhab claimed al-Ash'ari as its own. Do you ever find yourself wishing a great scholar belonged to your specific group so you could feel more validated? How can you instead rejoice that Allah raises defenders of truth from all communities and that the truth itself is what matters, not the group affiliation?

The Beginning of Al-Ash'ari's Repentance from Mu'tazilism
Author states purpose and quotes report of al-Ash'ari's Mu'tazili past.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the historical testimony that al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili for forty years before returning to Sunni Islam, and how this repentance is documented in reliable sources.

Core Idea
Before leading Sunni theology, al-Ash'ari spent 40 years as a Mu'tazili.

This lesson begins the narrative of al-Ash'ari's transformation from Mu'tazilism to orthodox Sunni belief. It introduces the key historical report from Ibn 'Uzra, preserved by the historian Ibn 'Asakir, which forms the basis for understanding his conversion.

Guided Path
1
Read the author's stated purpose
Notice that the author announces he will prove two things: al-Ash'ari's return from Mu'tazilism and the attribution of al-Ibana to him. This sets the agenda.
This frames the coming evidence.
2
Examine the chain of transmission
Identify the reporter: Ibn 'Uzra, via al-Dimashqi (Ibn 'Asakir). Understand that this is a reliable historical chain.
Al-Dimashqi is a famous historian of Syria.
3
Absorb the key fact
The report states clearly that al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili and adhered to that school for forty years. Ponder the significance of this long period before his repentance.
This shows a profound transformation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال تاج الدين السبكي في ( طبقات الشافعية الكبرى » : أبو الحسن الأشعري كبير أهل السنة بعد الإمام أحمد بن حنبل وعقيدته عقيدة الإمام أحمد رحمه الله واحدة لا شك في ذلك ولا ارتياب ، وبه صرح الأشعري في تصانيفه وذكره غير ما مرة من أن عقيدتي هي عقيدة الإمام المبجل أحمد بن حنبل ، هذه عبارة الشيخ أبي الحسن في غير موضع من كلامه » . اهـ وفضائل أبي الحسن الأشعري ومناقبه أكثر من أن يمكن حصرها في هذه العجالة ، ومن وقف على تواليفه بعد توبته من الاعتزال رأى أن الله تعالى قد أمده بمواد توفيقه ، وأقامه لنصرة الحق والذب عن طريقه . وقد تنازع فيه أهل المذاهب ، فالمالكي يدعي أنه مالكي ، والشافعي يزعم أنه شافعي ، والحنفي كذلك . قال ابن عساكر : لقيت الشيخ الفاضل رافعاً الحمال الفقيه ، فذكر لي عن شيوخه أن أبا الحسن الأشعري كان مالكياً فنسب من تعلق اليوم بمذهب أهل السنة وتفقه في معرفة أصول الدين من سائر المذاهب إلى الأشعري لكثرة تواليفه وكثرة قراءة الناس لها . قال ابن فورك: توفي أبو الحسن الأشعري سنة ٣٢٤ هـ . اهـ وبعد ذكر هذه النتفة من ترجمة هذا الإمام نذكر ما يلي إثبات رجوعه عن الاعتزال ، وإثبات نسبة الإبانة إليه ، ننقل ذلك من المراجع الموثوق بها ، فنقول وبالله التوفيق . رجوع أبي الحسن الأشعري عن الاعتزال إلى عقيدة السلف قال الحافظ مؤرخ الشام أبو القاسم علي بن الحسن بن هبة الله بن الدمشقي المتوفى سنة ٥٧١هـ في كتابه ( التبيين ( قال أبو بكر إسماعيل بن أبي محمد بن إسحاق الأزدي القيرواني المعروف بابن عزرة : إن أبا الحسن الأشعري كان معتزلياً وأنه أقام على مذهب الاعتزال أربعين سنة ، وكان لهم
And Taj al-Din al-Subki said in 'The Greater Classes of Shafi'is': 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is the great authority of Ahl al-Sunna after Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, and his creed is the creed of Imam Ahmad, may Allah have mercy on him, without doubt or misgiving. Al-Ash'ari stated this in his books and mentioned it more than once: that my creed is the creed of the venerated Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal. This is the expression of Shaykh Abu al-Hasan in more than one place in his speech.' End. And the virtues and merits of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari are too many to be encompassed in this brief account. Whoever looks at his writings after his repentance from Mu'tazilism sees that Allah the Exalted supplied him with materials of His success and raised him to defend the truth and protect His path. The people of the schools disputed over him: the Maliki claims he is Maliki, the Shafi'i claims he is Shafi'i, and the Hanafi likewise. Ibn 'Asakir said: 'I met the virtuous shaykh Rafi' al-Hammal al-Faqih, and he told me from his shaykhs that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was Maliki, so whoever today adheres to the Madhhab of Ahl al-Sunna and studies the science of the principles of religion from all schools is attributed to al-Ash'ari because of his many writings and the many people's study of them.' Ibn Furak said: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari died in the year 324 AH.' End. After mentioning this selection from the biography of this Imam, we mention the following: establishing his return from Mu'tazilism, and establishing the attribution of al-Ibana to him. We transmit that from reliable references, so we say, and with Allah is success. The return of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari from Mu'tazilism to the creed of the Salaf. Al-Hafiz, the historian of Syria, Abu al-Qasim 'Ali ibn al-Hasan ibn Hibat Allah al-Dimashqi, who died in 571 AH, said in his book al-Tabyin: 'Abu Bakr Isma'il ibn Abi Muhammad ibn Ishaq al-Azdi al-Qayrawani, known as Ibn 'Uzra, said: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili and he adhered to the Mu'tazili school for forty years, and he had...'
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is a biographical summary that establishes al-Ash'ari's orthodox Sunni position and begins the narrative of his repentance from Mu'tazilism. Taj al-Din al-Subki asserts that al-Ash'ari is the head of Ahl al-Sunna after Imam Ahmad, and that his creed is identical to Ahmad's, a point al-Ash'ari himself repeatedly affirmed in his writings. The author then notes that al-Ash'ari's virtues are vast, and that his post-repentance works show divine support. A dispute over his madhhab is recorded: Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanafi each claim him; Ibn 'Asakir's report suggests he was a Maliki who became the eponym of Sunni theological method due to his many works. His death in 324 AH is given via Ibn Furak. Finally, the author states the purpose of the coming section: to prove al-Ash'ari's return from Mu'tazilism and the attribution of the book al-Ibana to him. The passage ends with a citation from al-Tabyin, quoting Ibn 'Uzra that al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili for forty years.

?

?

Does your own understanding of Islamic theology rely on such careful verification, or have you accepted ideas without checking their sources?

Al-Subki Testifies to Creedal Unity
Al-Subki asserts the creedal identity of Ash'ari and Ahmad.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Taj al-Din al-Subki explicitly confirms the doctrinal unity between Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari and Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, and how Ash'ari himself asserted this identity.

Core Idea
Al-Subki and al-Ash'ari both affirm: the creed is one and the same.

Taj al-Din al-Subki, a major Shafi'i scholar, provides a historical testimony that the founder of the Ash'ari school adhered to the same creed as Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal. This passage from his Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya al-Kubra serves as a primary source defending the orthodoxy of Ash'ari theology. Let's read Subki's words and see how he supports his claim.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice Subki's emphatic language ('one and the same, no doubt').
The passage is a short but powerful testimony.
2
Identify the key claim
Subki asserts that Ash'ari's creed is identical to that of Imam Ahmad. He states there is no doubt or uncertainty.
3
Recognize Ash'ari's own statement
Subki reports that Ash'ari himself said this multiple times. This strengthens the claim.
Ash'ari's own words: 'My creed is the creed of the revered Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal.'

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال تاج الدين السبكي في ( طبقات الشافعية الكبرى ) : أبو الحسن الأشعري كبير أهل السنة بعد الإمام أحمد بن حنبل وعقيدته عقيدة الإمام أحمد رحمه الله واحدة لا شك في ذلك ولا ارتياب ، وبه صرح الأشعري في تصانيفه وذكره غير ما مرة من أن عقيدتي هي عقيدة الإمام المبجل أحمد بن حنبل ، هذه عبارة الشيخ أبي الحسن في غير موضع من كلامه . اهـ
Taj al-Din al-Subki said in (Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya al-Kubra): Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is the great one of Ahl al-Sunna after Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, and his creed (aqida) is the creed of Imam Ahmad, may Allah have mercy on him, one and the same, there is no doubt about that nor any uncertainty. Ash'ari explicitly stated this in his works and mentioned more than once that 'my creed is the creed of the revered Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal.' This is the expression of the Shaykh Abu al-Hasan in more than one place in his speech. End.
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is a quotation from Taj al-Din al-Subki's Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya al-Kubra. Subki declares that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is the greatest of Ahl al-Sunna after Ahmad ibn Hanbal, and his creed (aqida) is one and the same as Ahmad's, without any doubt or uncertainty. He adds that Ash'ari himself explicitly stated this in his works multiple times: 'My creed is the creed of the revered Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal.' Subki notes that this is Ash'ari's own wording in more than one place.

Key Terms
أبو الحسن الأشعري
عقيدة
أهل السنة
أحمد بن حنبل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Ash'ari's creed differs from Ahmad ibn Hanbal's.
Correction: Al-Subki explicitly states: 'his creed is the creed of Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, one and the same, no doubt.' He also cites Ash'ari's own repeated assertion of this identity.

Checkpoint
What does al-Subki say about Ash'ari's creed?
A) It is different from Ahmad ibn Hanbal's.
B) It is identical to Ahmad ibn Hanbal's.
C) It is unclear.
D) It is a new school.

Key Takeaway
Al-Subki's testimony confirms that Ash'ari's creed is identical to that of Imam Ahmad, a key point in defending Ash'ari orthodoxy.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are you quick to label others as deviant when they follow a different madhab, or do you seek to understand the historical unity of creed as al-Subki testifies? Will you investigate before judging?
?
Al-Ash'ari explicitly stated his creed is that of Imam Ahmad. Do you have the same courage to declare your allegiance to the creed of the Salaf, even when others accuse you of innovation? How would you respond if your beliefs were questioned?
?
This passage shows a scholar defending another's orthodoxy. Have you ever defended a fellow Muslim's sincerity when they were misunderstood? Are you silent when injustice is done with words? Will you speak up for truth when it costs you?

Ash'ari's Virtues and the Dispute over His Legal Madhhab
The author praises Ash'ari and quotes Ibn 'Asakir and Ibn Furak on his madhhab and death.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the profound impact of Imam al-Ash'ari's works after his repentance from Mu'tazila, and the historical debate about his affiliation with a legal school.

Core Idea
Imam al-Ash'ari's legacy transcends legal schools, uniting Ahl al-Sunna.

This passage highlights the immense virtues of Imam al-Ash'ari and the fact that his writings influenced generations. It also addresses a historical curiosity: scholars of different madhabs claimed him as their own. Ibn 'Asakir provides a key report resolving this dispute, while Ibn Furak records his death year. As you read, note how the author uses these details to underscore Ash'ari's central role in Sunni theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to understand the flow.
Notice the praise and the claims of different madhabs.
2
Analyze Ibn 'Asakir's report
Identify what Ibn 'Asakir reports about Ash'ari's madhhab. Why does he say people attribute themselves to Ash'ari?
The key is the abundance of his writings.
3
Reflect on the significance
Consider why this dispute is mentioned. How does it show Ash'ari's unifying role in Sunni theology?
4
Memorize the death year
Ibn Furak gives the death year of 324 AH. Commit this to memory as a historical milestone.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وفضائل أبي الحسن الأشعري ومناقبه أكثر من أن يمكن حصرها في هذه العجالة ، ومن وقف على تواليفه بعد توبته من الاعتزال رأى أن الله تعالى قد أمده بمواد توفيقه ، وأقامه لنصرة الحق والذب عن طريقه . وقد تنازع فيه أهل المذاهب ، فالمالكي يدعي أنه مالكي ، والشافعي يزعم أنه شافعي ، والحنفي كذلك . قال ابن عساكر : لقيت الشيخ الفاضل رافعاً الحمال الفقيه ، فذكر لي عن شيوخه أن أبا الحسن الأشعري كان مالكياً فنسب من تعلق اليوم بمذهب أهل السنة وتفقه في معرفة أصول الدين من سائر المذاهب إلى الأشعري لكثرة تواليفه وكثرة قراءة الناس لها . قال ابن فورك: توفي أبو الحسن الأشعري سنة 324 هـ . اهـ
The virtues of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari and his merits are too many to be enumerated in this brief account. Whoever looks at his writings after his repentance from Mu'tazila sees that Allah Almighty supplied him with the materials of His success and raised him to support the truth and defend His path. The people of the madhhabs differed concerning him: the Maliki claims he was Maliki, the Shafi'i claims he was Shafi'i, and the Hanafi likewise. Ibn 'Asakir said: I met the virtuous shaykh Rafi' al-Hammal al-Faqih, and he mentioned to me from his teachers that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was Maliki, so whoever today adheres to the way of Ahl al-Sunna and gains knowledge of usul al-din from the various madhhabs is attributed to al-Ash'ari because of the abundance of his writings and the frequency of people reading them. Ibn Furak said: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari died in the year 324 AH. End.
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage first praises Ash'ari's virtues as too many to count. Whoever studies his works after his repentance from Mu'tazila sees that Allah aided him and raised him to defend the truth. Then it mentions that scholars of different madhahib claim him: Malikis claim he was Maliki, Shafi'is claim he was Shafi'i, and Hanafis likewise. Ibn 'Asakir reports that he met a scholar named Rafi' al-Hammal who told him from his teachers that Ash'ari was Maliki; therefore, anyone today who adheres to the way of Ahl al-Sunna and studies usul al-din from any madhhab is attributed to Ash'ari because of his many writings and people's frequent study of them. Finally, Ibn Furak states that Ash'ari died in 324 AH.

Key Terms
تواليف
مذهب
ابن عساكر
ابن فورك

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the dispute over Ash'ari's madhhab means his Sunni creed is uncertain.
Correction: The dispute only concerns his legal school, not his theology. His allegiance to Ahl al-Sunna is clear from his works.

Checkpoint
According to Ibn 'Asakir, what was Ash'ari's legal madhhab?
A) Shafi'i.
B) Hanafi.
C) Maliki.
D) Hanbali.

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Ash'ari's writings solidified Sunni theology, and his death in 324 AH marked the end of an era of repentance and defense of truth.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about scholars like al-Ash'ari, do you view them through the lens of affiliation (madhhab) or through their contribution to truth? Which lens dominates your heart, and what would it take to shift it?
?
Al-Ash'ari's repentance from Mu'tazila was a turning point. Is there a false belief or habit in your own life that you need to turn from completely—not just adjust, but repent? What is it, and what step will you take today toward that change?
?
The passage says those who look at his writings 'see that Allah Almighty supplied him with His success.' Have you ever examined a scholar's work with the intention of seeing Allah's aid? If not, what is stopping you from beginning that practice this week?
