Life of Imam al-Ashari
beginner
4 min
Ash'ari's Public Renunciation of Mu'tazilism
Ibn 'Uzra narrates Ash'ari's seclusion, public sermon, and distribution of his new books.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the dramatic event of Ash'ari's conversion from Mu'tazilism to Sunni Islam, including his seclusion, his public sermon, and the distribution of his books.

Core Idea
Ash'ari publicly shed his Mu'tazili past like a garment, marking a turning point in Islamic theology.

This lesson recounts the pivotal moment when Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, after forty years as a leading Mu'tazili, publicly renounced his former beliefs in the mosque of Basra. His dramatic act of removing his garment and distributing his new books symbolizes his complete break with I'tizal and his embrace of Sunni orthodoxy. This story is foundational for understanding the Ash'ari school's emergence.

Guided Path
1
Read the narrative
Read the Arabic passage and translation carefully to understand the sequence of events. Note the key details: forty years as Mu'tazili, fifteen-day seclusion, Friday sermon, shedding of garment, distribution of books.
The passage is from Ibn 'Asakir's Al-Tabyin, quoting Ibn 'Uzra.
2
Reflect on the symbolism
Consider the symbolic act of shedding the garment. How does this emphasize the totality of his break with Mu'tazilism? What does it teach about sincerity in changing one's creed?
3
Consider the impact
Think about why the people of hadith and fiqh from Ahl al-Sunna accepted his books and took him as an imam. How did this event shape the formation of the Ash'ari school?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال الحافظ مؤرخ الشام أبو القاسم علي بن الحسن بن هبة الله بن الدمشقي المتوفى سنة 571هـ في كتابه ( التبيين ) قال أبو بكر إسماعيل بن أبي محمد بن إسحاق الأزدي القيرواني المعروف بابن عزرة : إن أبا الحسن الأشعري كان معتزلياً وأنه أقام على مذهب الاعتزال أربعين سنة ، وكان لهم إماماً ثم غاب عن الناس في بيته خمسة عشر يوماً ، فبعد ذلك خرج إلى الجامع بالبصرة فصعد المنبر بعد صلاة الجمعة ، وقال : معاشر الناس إني تغيبت عنكم في هذه المدة لأني نظرت فتكافأت عندي الأدلة ولم يترجح عندي حق على باطل ولا باطل على حق ، فاستهديت الله تبارك وتعالى فهداني إلى ما أودعته في كتبي هذه ، وانخلعت من جميع ما كنت اعتقده ، كما انخلعت من ثوبي هذا ، وانخلع من ثوب كان عليه ورمى به ودفع الكتب إلى الناس ، فمنها كتاب اللمع وغيره من تواليفه الآتي ذكر بعضها قريباً إن شاء الله : فلما قرأ تلك الكتب أهل الحديث والفقه من أهل السنة والجماعة أخذوا بما فيها وانتحلوه واعتقدوا تقدمه واتخذوه إماماً حتى نسب مذهبهم إليه
The hafiz, historian of Sham, Abu al-Qasim Ali ibn al-Hasan ibn Hibat Allah al-Dimashqi, who died in 571 AH, said in his book Al-Tabyin: Abu Bakr Isma'il ibn Abi Muhammad ibn Ishaq al-Azdi al-Qayrawani, known as Ibn 'Uzra, said: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili, and he remained on the madhhab of I'tizal for forty years, and he was an imam for them. Then he disappeared from the people in his house for fifteen days. After that, he came out to the mosque in Basra, ascended the pulpit after the Friday prayer, and said: 'O people, I have been absent from you during this period because I examined the proofs and they became balanced before me, and no truth over falsehood nor falsehood over truth outweighed with me. So I sought guidance from Allah, Blessed and Exalted, and He guided me to what I have deposited in these books of mine. I have shed all that I used to believe, just as I shed this garment of mine.' And he shed a garment that was on him and threw it away, and he gave the books to the people. Among them was the book Al-Luma' and others of his writings, some of which will be mentioned soon, God willing. When the people of hadith and fiqh from Ahl al-Sunna wal-Jama'a read those books, they took what was in them, adopted them, believed in his precedence, and took him as an imam, until their madhhab was attributed to him.
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is a biographical narrative from Ibn 'Asakir's Al-Tabyin, quoting Abu Bakr Isma'il ibn Abi Muhammad al-Azdi al-Qayrawani, known as Ibn 'Uzra. He says that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili and remained on that path for forty years, even being their imam. Then he disappeared from people in his house for fifteen days. After that, he came out to the mosque in Basra and ascended the pulpit after Friday prayer. He addressed the people saying: 'O people, I have been absent from you during this period because I examined the proofs and they became balanced before me, and no truth was distinguished from falsehood. So I sought guidance from Allah, and He guided me to what I have written in these books. I have shed all that I used to believe, just as I shed this garment.' He then took off his garment and threw it away, and distributed the books to the people, among them the book Al-Luma' and others. When the people of hadith and fiqh from Ahl al-Sunna read those books, they accepted them, adopted them, believed in his precedence, and took him as an imam, to the point that their madhhab was attributed to him.

Key Terms
معتزلي
الجامع بالبصرة
اللمع
التوبة
انخلع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Ash'ari's change was gradual or private, not a dramatic public event.
Correction: The passage shows he made a public break, complete with a sermon, symbolic shedding of his garment, and distribution of books.

Checkpoint
What did Ash'ari do after his fifteen-day seclusion?
A) Wrote a new book in private.
B) Gave a sermon and renounced Mu'tazilism publicly.
C) Disappeared permanently.
D) Joined the Mu'tazila again.

Key Takeaway
Ash'ari's public renunciation of Mu'tazilism and distribution of his books marked the birth of the Ash'ari school, which became the theological foundation for Sunni Islam.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Ash'ari spent 40 years as a respected Mu'tazili imam, then secluded himself for 15 days to re-examine the proofs. When was the last time you set aside dedicated time to critically examine your own beliefs with the sole intention of seeking Allah's guidance, even if it meant abandoning what you once held dear?
?
Ash'ari publicly shed his garment as a symbol of shedding his past beliefs. Is there a belief, habit, or attachment in your life that you know is contrary to the truth but you continue to hold because of pride, comfort, or fear of what others might think? What would a public act of shedding look like for you?
?
After his conversion, Ash'ari wrote books that became the foundation for Sunni theology. If you were to die today, what legacy of truth would you leave behind? Are you actively working to correct your own falsehoods and contribute to the spread of sound belief, or are you content to remain where you are?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
beginner

Why Ash'ari's Mu'tazili Past Strengthened His Refutation
Defense of Ash'ari's Mu'tazili past with analogy and story.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari's former adherence to Mu'tazilism actually equipped him to refute it more effectively, and the analogy of Harun al-A'war that illustrates this principle.

Core Idea
A convert's past makes them the most powerful refuter of their former belief.

This passage defends al-Ash'ari against the criticism that his long Mu'tazili past discredits him. It uses the striking analogy of a Jewish convert who becomes a formidable debater against his former religion. The author argues that familiarity with a false doctrine from within gives one unparalleled insight into its flaws and ability to refute it. This lesson unpacks that argument and the supporting story of Harun al-A'war.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read both the Arabic original and the English translation to grasp the flow of the argument.
Notice the key comparison: 'like a convert from Judaism who exposes the flaws of what he left'.
2
Identify the main claim
The author claims that Ash'ari's long Mu'tazili period does not degrade him but elevates him in knowledge and ability. Find where this claim is stated.
It begins at 'وليس طول مقام...'.
3
Understand the Harun al-A'war story
Read the story of Harun, a Jewish convert. See how his opponent's insult backfires.
Harun's response 'فبئس ما صنعت' turns the insult into a compliment.
4
Connect to the consensus of hadith scholars
The passage ends with the consensus that Ash'ari was an imam of Ahl al-Hadith. Consider how this affirms his orthodoxy.
This consensus counters any lingering doubt about his standing.
5
Reflect on the misconception
Many think Ash'ari's past discredits him. This passage shows the opposite.
Identify your own preconceptions and how this passage challenges them.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصار عند المعتزلة ككتابي أسلم وأظهر عوار ما تركه فهو أعدى إلى الخلق أهل الذمة وكذلك أبو الحسن الأشعري أعدى الخلق إلى المعتزلة فهم يشنعون عليه وينسبون إليه الأباطيل وليس طول مقام أبي الحسن الأشعري على مذهب المعتزلة مما يفضي به إلى انحطاط المنزلة بل يقضي له في معرفة الأصول بعلو المرتبة ويدل عند ذوي البصائر له على سمو المنقبة لأن من رجع عن مذهب كان بعواره أخير وعلى رد شبه أهله وكشف تمويهاتهم أقدر وبتبيين ما يلبسون به لمن يهتدي باستبصاره أبصر فاستراحة من يعيره بذلك كاستراحة مناظر هارون بن موسى الأعور وقصته أن هارون الأعور كان يهودياً فأسلم وحسن إسلامه وحفظ القرآن وضبطه وحفظ النحو وناظر إنسان يوماً في مسألة فغلبه هارون فلم يدر المغلوب ما يصنع فقال له أنت كنت يهودياً فأسلمت فقال له هارون فبئس ما صنعت فغلبه هارون في هذا واتفق أصحاب الحديث أن أبا الحسن الأشعري كان إماماً من أئمة أصحاب الحديث ومذهبه مذهب أصحاب الحديث تكلم في أصول الديانات على طريقة أهل السنة ورد على المخالفين من أهل الزيغ والبدعة وكان على المعتزلة والروافض والمبتدعين
Thus he became with the Mu'tazila like a convert from Judaism who exposes the flaws of what he left, so he is the most hostile of people to the people of dhimma. Likewise, Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is the most hostile of creation to the Mu'tazila. They defame him and attribute falsehoods to him. But the long stay of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari on the madhhab of the Mu'tazila does not lead to degradation of his rank; rather, it decrees for him in knowledge of usul a high status and indicates to those with insight his exalted merit, because one who returns from a madhhab is more aware of its flaws, more capable of refuting the doubts of its people and uncovering their deceptions, and more discerning in clarifying what they obscure to those who seek guidance through his insight. So the one who reproaches him for that is like the one who reproached the debater Harun ibn Musa al-A'war. His story is that Harun al-A'war was a Jew, then he converted to Islam and his Islam was good, he memorized the Quran and mastered it, and learned grammar. He debated a person one day over a matter and Harun overcame him. The one defeated did not know what to do, so he said to him: 'You were a Jew and then you converted to Islam.' Harun said to him: 'Then how evil is what I have done?' So Harun overcame him in this. And the companions of hadith agreed that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was an imam from the imams of the companions of hadith, his madhhab is the madhhab of the companions of hadith, he spoke on the principles of religions (usul al-diyanat) according to the way of Ahl al-Sunna, refuted the opponents from the people of deviation and innovation, and he was against the Mu'tazila, Rawafid, and innovators.
Source pages p. 9

Commentary
This passage is an argument defending Ash'ari's long Mu'tazili period. It begins with a comparison: just as a convert from Judaism who knows its flaws becomes the most hostile to the people of dhimma, so Ash'ari became the most hostile to the Mu'tazila. They slander him and attribute falsehoods to him. But his long adherence to Mu'tazila does not degrade his rank; rather, it elevates him in knowledge of usul and shows his high merit, because one who abandons a sect is more aware of its flaws, better able to refute its doubts and expose its deceptions, and more insightful in clarifying what they obscure. The author then provides the story of Harun ibn Musa al-A'war, who was a Jew and converted to Islam, learned the Quran and grammar. When his opponent rebuked him for his former Judaism, Harun cleverly replied: 'Then how evil is what I have done?' (implying that his conversion is a good deed, so the insult is actually a compliment). This illustrates that Ash'ari's past is not a flaw but a strength. The passage concludes with the consensus of the hadith scholars that Ash'ari was an imam of the hadith scholars, his madhhab was that of the hadith scholars, he spoke on usul al-din according to the way of Ahl al-Sunna, refuted the deviants and innovators, and stood against the Mu'tazila, Rawafid, and innovators.

Key Terms
أعدى
المعتزلة
هارون الأعور
تمويه
أصحاب الحديث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Ash'ari's past in Mu'tazilism makes his later Sunni position less credible.
Correction: The passage argues that his past made him more aware of Mu'tazili flaws and more capable of refuting them, thereby strengthening his authority.

Checkpoint
What analogy does the author use to defend Ash'ari's Mu'tazili past?
A) A doctor who was once sick.
B) A judge who was once a criminal.
C) A convert from Judaism who becomes a strong Muslim debater.
D) A student who changes teachers.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's long association with Mu'tazilism, rather than a liability, was a strength that made him the most effective refuter of its errors.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you secretly look down on someone who was once misguided and is now practicing, perhaps thinking their past disqualifies them? How does the story of Harun al-A'war force you to confront that attitude, and what will you change today about how you view such people?
?
If you have a past sin or a period of ignorance, do you use it as an excuse to feel inferior, or do you see it as a tool to help others avoid the same mistake? Will you now embrace your past as Ash'ari did, or continue to let it shame you?
?
When you hear a brother or sister share their journey from a false belief or sinful lifestyle, do you respect the unique insight they have, or do you subtly hold their past against them? Ask yourself honestly: are you like the one who reproached Harun? If so, what is your repentance?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
beginner
5 min
Al-Ash'ari's Public Repentance and Declaration
Narrates al-Ash'ari's seclusion and dramatic public repentance.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the dramatic public event of Imam al-Ash'ari's repentance from Mu'tazilism and how he symbolically broke with his past.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's repentance was a public, symbolic act that defined his legacy.

Imam al-Ash'ari did not leave Mu'tazilism quietly. After fifteen days of seclusion, he mounted the pulpit in Basra, made a public confession of his former error, and dramatically cast off a garment as a symbol of discarding his old beliefs. This powerful scene set the stage for his lifelong defense of Sunni orthodoxy.

Guided Path
1
Read the account of the seclusion
Read how al-Ash'ari disappeared for fifteen days to examine the evidence. This shows his sincerity in seeking truth.
Notice the description of his inner turmoil: 'the evidences became equal before me'.
2
Witness the public declaration
After Friday prayer, he addressed the congregation, announcing that Allah guided him to write his books. Pay attention to his words 'I divest myself of all that I used to believe'.
He literally removed his garment to emphasize the break.
3
Reflect on the symbolism
The removal of the garment and giving away his books symbolized a complete rejection of his Mu'tazili past. Consider how this act strengthened the faith of the Sunni community.
This event is often cited to demonstrate his sincerity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إماماً ثم غاب عن الناس في بيته خمسة عشر يوماً ، فبعد ذلك خرج إلى الجامع بالبصرة فصعد المنبر بعد صلاة الجمعة ، وقال : معاشر الناس إني تغيبت عنكم في هذه المدة لأني نظرت فتكأفات عندي الأدلة ولم يترجح عندي حق على باطل ولا باطل على حق ، فاستهديت الله تبارك وتعالى فهداني إلى ما أودعته في كتبي هذه ، وانخلعت من جميع ما كنت اعتقده ، كما انخلعت من ثوبي هذا ، وانخلع من ثوب كان عليه ورمى به ودفع الكتب إلى الناس ، فمنها كتاب اللمع » وغيره من تواليفه الآتي ذكر بعضها قريباً إن شاء الله : فلما قرأ تلك الكتب أهل الحديث والفقه من أهل السنة والجماعة أخذوا بما فيها وانتحلوه واعتقدوا تقدمه واتخذوه إماماً حتى نسب مذهبهم إليه فصار عند المعتزلة ككتابي أسلم وأظهر عوار ما تركه فهو أعدى إلى الخلق أهل الذمة وكذلك أبو الحسن الأشعري أعدى الخلق إلى المعتزلة ، فهم يشنعون عليه وينسبون إليه الأباطيل ، وليس طول مقام أبي الحسن الأشعري على مذهب المعتزلة ، مما يفضي به إلى انحطاط المنزلة ، بل يقضي له في معرفة الأصول بعلو المرتبة ، ويدل عند ذوي البصائر له على سمو المنقبة ، لأن من رجع عن مذهب كان بعواره أخير ، وعلى رد شبه أهله وكشف تمويهاتم أقدر ، وبتبيين ما يلبسون به لمن يهتدي باستبصاره أبصر ، فاستراحة من يعيره بذلك كاستراحة مناظر هارون بن موسى الأعور وقصته أن هارون الأعور كان يهودياً فأسلم وحسن إسلامه وحفظ القرآن وضبطه وحفظ النحو ، وناظر إنسان يوماً في مسألة فغلبه هارون فلم يدر المغلوب ما يصنع ، فقال له : أنت كنت يهودياً فأسلمت ، فقال له هارون : فبئس ما صنعت ، فغلبه هارون في هذا . وأتفق أصحاب الحديث أن : أبا الحسن الأشعري كان إماماً من أئمة أصحاب الحديث ، ومذهبه مذهب أصحاب الحديث ، تكلم في أصول الديانات على طريقة أهل السنة ، ورد على المخالفين من أهل الزيغ والبدعة ، وكان على المعتزلة والروافض والمبتدعين من أهل القبلة والخارجين عن الملة سيفاً مسلولاً ومن طعن فيه أو سبه فقد بسط لسان السوء في جميع أهل السنة ، ولم يكن أبو الحسن الأشعري أول متكلم بلسان أهل السنة ، وإنما جرى على سنن غيره وعلى نصرة مذهب معروف ، فزاده حجة وبياناً ، ولم يبتدع مقالة اخترعها ولا مذهباً انفرد به وليس له في المذاهب أكثر من بسطه وشرحه كغيره من الأئمة وقال أبو بكر بن فورك : رجع أبو الحسن الأشعري عن الاعتزال إلى مذهب أهل السنة سنة ٣٠٠ هـ قال و ممن الدين أحمد من العلماء برجوع الأشعري عن الاعتزال أبو العباس شمس بن محمد بن أبي بكر بن خلكان الشافعي المتوفى سنة ٦٨١ هـ ، قال : في ) وفيات الأعيان ( الجزء الثاني صفحة ٤٦٦ : كان أبو الحسن الأشعري معتزلياً ثم تاب ومنهم عماد الدين أبو الفداء إسماعيل بن عمر بن كثير القرشي الدمشقي الشافعي المتوفى سنة ٧٧٤ هـ . قال في البداية والنهاية » : الجزء الحادي عشر صفحة ۱۸۷ : « كان أبو الحسن الأشعري معتزلياً فتاب منه بالبصرة فوق المنبر ، ثم أظهر فضائح المعتزلة وقبائحهم » . ومنهم شمس الدين محمد بن أحمد بن عثمان الدمشقي الشافعي الشهير بالذهبي المتوفى سنة ٧٤٨ هـ : قال في كتابه « العلو للعلي الغفار » : « كان أبو الحسن أولاً معتزلياً أخذ عن أبي علي الجبائي ثم نابذه ورد عليه وصار متكلماً للسنة ، ووافق أئمة الحديث ، فلو انتهى أصحابنا المتكلمون إلى مقالة أبي الحسن ولزموها لأحسنوا ولكنهم خاضوا كخوض حكماء الأوائل في الأشياء ومشوا خلف المنطق فلا قوة إلا بالله » .
An imam, then he disappeared from the people in his house for fifteen days. After that, he went out to the mosque in Basra and ascended the pulpit after Friday prayer and said: 'O people, I was absent from you during this period because I examined [matters] and the evidences became equal before me, and no truth was preponderant over falsehood nor falsehood over truth. So I sought guidance from Allah, Blessed and Exalted, and He guided me to what I have deposited in these my books. And I have divested myself of all that I used to believe, just as I divest myself of this garment of mine.' And he removed a garment he was wearing and threw it away, and gave the books to the people. Among them was the book 'Al-Luma'' and others of his works, some of which will be mentioned shortly, God willing. When the people of hadith and fiqh from Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah read those books, they took what was in them, adopted them, believed in his precedence, and took him as an imam until their school was attributed to him. So he became for the Mu'tazila like a book that is most harmful [؟] and exposes the defects of what he abandoned, so he is most hostile to the people of dhimmah [؟]. Likewise, Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is the most hostile of people to the Mu'tazila, so they malign him and attribute falsehoods to him. And the lengthy stay of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari upon the school of the Mu'tazila does not lead to degradation of his rank; rather, it testifies for him, in knowledge of principles (usul), to high station, and indicates to those with insight his lofty merit. Because one who returns from a school is better acquainted with its defects, more capable of refuting its doubts and uncovering its dissimulations, and more discerning in exposing what they confuse for those who seek guidance with insight. So the comfort of one who reproaches him for that is like the comfort of the debater of Harun ibn Musa al-A'war. His story: Harun al-A'war was a Jew then embraced Islam, and his Islam was good. He memorized the Quran and mastered it, and memorized grammar. One day he debated someone on an issue and Harun overcame him, so the defeated one did not know what to do. He said to him: 'You were a Jew and then became Muslim.' Harun said to him: 'How bad is what I did!' So Harun overcame him in this. And the hadith scholars agreed that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was an imam among the imams of the hadith scholars, and his school was the school of the hadith scholars. He spoke on the principles of religions (usul al-diyanat) according to the way of Ahl al-Sunnah, and refuted the opponents from among the people of deviation and innovation. And he was a drawn sword against the Mu'tazila, the Rawafid, and the innovators from among the people of the qibla and those who deviated from the religion. Whoever attacks him or insults him has extended an evil tongue against all Ahl al-Sunnah. And Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was not the first to speak in the tongue of Ahl al-Sunnah; rather, he followed the path of others and supported a known school, so he increased it in proof and clarification. He did not initiate an article he invented nor a school he alone held, and he had nothing in the schools beyond expanding and explaining it, like other imams. Abu Bakr ibn Furak said: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari returned from I'tizal to the school of Ahl al-Sunnah in the year 300 AH.' He said, 'And among those who [testified] to al-Ash'ari's return from I'tizal is Abu al-Abbas Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr ibn Khallikan al-Shafi'i, died 681 AH. He said in 'Wafayat al-A'yan', part 2, p. 466: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili then repented.' And among them is 'Imad al-Din Abu al-Fida' Isma'il ibn 'Umar ibn Kathir al-Qurashi al-Dimashqi al-Shafi'i, died 774 AH. He said in 'Al-Bidaya wa al-Nihaya', part 11, p. 187: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili, then he repented from it in Basra on the pulpit, then he exposed the scandals and ugly deeds of the Mu'tazila.' And among them is Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn 'Uthman al-Dimashqi al-Shafi'i, known as al-Dhahabi, died 748 AH. He said in his book 'Al-'Uluww li al-'Ali al-Ghaffar': 'Abu al-Hasan was first a Mu'tazili, he studied under Abu 'Ali al-Jubba'i, then he opposed him and refuted him, and became a mutakallim for the Sunnah, and agreed with the imams of hadith. If our fellow mutakallimun had stopped at the article of Abu al-Hasan and adhered to it, they would have done well; but they plunged like the ancient philosophers into things and followed logic. There is no power except with Allah.'
Source pages p. 9

Commentary
This passage is a biographical account of Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari's repentance from Mu'tazilism and his subsequent acceptance by Ahl al-Sunnah. It also includes testimonies from later scholars confirming this event. The passage begins by describing his seclusion of fifteen days, after which he publicly declared on the pulpit in Basra that he had been uncertain and then guided by Allah to the truth, which he compiled in his books. He dramatically removed a garment and gave away his books, symbolizing his complete break from his previous beliefs. The people of hadith and fiqh accepted his works and elevated him to the rank of imam, so much so that his school became identified with them. The passage then argues that al-Ash'ari's former Mu'tazilism actually enhanced his ability to refute them, using the analogy of a person who leaves a religion being better equipped to expose its flaws. This is illustrated with the story of Harun al-A'war, a Jewish convert to Islam who successfully debated an adversary who tried to discredit him for converting. Similarly, al-Ash'ari's background made him a formidable opponent of the Mu'tazila. The hadith scholars unanimously regarded him as one of their own, and he is described as a drawn sword against all heretics. He did not innovate a new doctrine but rather defended and clarified the established Sunni position. The passage closes with quotations from Ibn Furak, Ibn Khallikan, Ibn Kathir, and al-Dhahabi, all confirming his repentance and praising his role. The key theological point is that al-Ash'ari's return was a return to the orthodox Sunni creed, not the invention of a new one.

Key Terms
أبو الحسن الأشعري
المعتزلة
أهل السنة
اللمع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What did al-Ash'ari do to symbolize his break from Mu'tazilism during his public declaration?
A) He burned his old books
B) He removed his garment and threw it away
C) He gave a lecture against the Mu'tazila
D) He wrote a letter to the caliph

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's public repentance was a bold, symbolic act that marked his complete rejection of Mu'tazilism and his embrace of Sunni orthodoxy.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever held a belief you later realized was wrong, but you kept quiet about it? What would it cost you to publicly correct it, as al-Ash'ari did?
?
Al-Ash'ari spent fifteen days in seclusion, wrestling with doubts and seeking Allah's guidance. When was the last time you set aside that kind of dedicated, uncomfortable time to test your own convictions?
?
His act of removing his garment symbolized stripping away his old self. Is there a habit, a relationship, or an identity you cling to that contradicts the truth you know? Will you have the courage to shed it publicly?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
intermediate
6 min
The Strength Gained from Leaving a Sect
Argues that leaving a sect enhances one's ability to refute it, with Harun's story as example.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Imam al-Ash'ari's former adherence to Mu'tazilism was considered an asset rather than a liability in defending Sunni orthodoxy.

Core Idea
Those who leave a falsehood know its weaknesses best.

Critics might think al-Ash'ari's long stay with the Mu'tazila discredits him. However, the passage argues that his intimate knowledge of their arguments made him uniquely capable of refuting them. An analogy from Jewish convert Harun al-A'war reinforces this point.

Guided Path
1
Read the argument about his past
Read how the authors assert that his lengthy Mu'tazilism actually elevates his rank because he knows its defects.
The Arabic phrase 'من رجع عن مذهب كان بعواره أخير' is key.
2
Study the story of Harun al-A'war
Read the story of Harun, a Jewish convert to Islam, who outdebated an opponent who tried to shame him for converting. Note how the analogy applies to al-Ash'ari.
Harun's response was: 'فبئس ما صنعت' — he used the opponent's own logic against him.
3
Connect the analogy to al-Ash'ari
Just as Harun's conversion gave him insight into Jewish arguments, al-Ash'ari's Mu'tazili background gave him deep insight into their errors.
This is a powerful rhetorical tool against those who deride former associates.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إماماً ثم غاب عن الناس في بيته خمسة عشر يوماً ، فبعد ذلك خرج إلى الجامع بالبصرة فصعد المنبر بعد صلاة الجمعة ، وقال : معاشر الناس إني تغيبت عنكم في هذه المدة لأني نظرت فتكأفات عندي الأدلة ولم يترجح عندي حق على باطل ولا باطل على حق ، فاستهديت الله تبارك وتعالى فهداني إلى ما أودعته في كتبي هذه ، وانخلعت من جميع ما كنت اعتقده ، كما انخلعت من ثوبي هذا ، وانخلع من ثوب كان عليه ورمى به ودفع الكتب إلى الناس ، فمنها كتاب اللمع » وغيره من تواليفه الآتي ذكر بعضها قريباً إن شاء الله : فلما قرأ تلك الكتب أهل الحديث والفقه من أهل السنة والجماعة أخذوا بما فيها وانتحلوه واعتقدوا تقدمه واتخذوه إماماً حتى نسب مذهبهم إليه فصار عند المعتزلة ككتابي أسلم وأظهر عوار ما تركه فهو أعدى إلى الخلق أهل الذمة وكذلك أبو الحسن الأشعري أعدى الخلق إلى المعتزلة ، فهم يشنعون عليه وينسبون إليه الأباطيل ، وليس طول مقام أبي الحسن الأشعري على مذهب المعتزلة ، مما يفضي به إلى انحطاط المنزلة ، بل يقضي له في معرفة الأصول بعلو المرتبة ، ويدل عند ذوي البصائر له على سمو المنقبة ، لأن من رجع عن مذهب كان بعواره أخير ، وعلى رد شبه أهله وكشف تمويهاتم أقدر ، وبتبيين ما يلبسون به لمن يهتدي باستبصاره أبصر ، فاستراحة من يعيره بذلك كاستراحة مناظر هارون بن موسى الأعور وقصته أن هارون الأعور كان يهودياً فأسلم وحسن إسلامه وحفظ القرآن وضبطه وحفظ النحو ، وناظر إنسان يوماً في مسألة فغلبه هارون فلم يدر المغلوب ما يصنع ، فقال له : أنت كنت يهودياً فأسلمت ، فقال له هارون : فبئس ما صنعت ، فغلبه هارون في هذا . وأتفق أصحاب الحديث أن : أبا الحسن الأشعري كان إماماً من أئمة أصحاب الحديث ، ومذهبه مذهب أصحاب الحديث ، تكلم في أصول الديانات على طريقة أهل السنة ، ورد على المخالفين من أهل الزيغ والبدعة ، وكان على المعتزلة والروافض والمبتدعين من أهل القبلة والخارجين عن الملة سيفاً مسلولاً ومن طعن فيه أو سبه فقد بسط لسان السوء في جميع أهل السنة ، ولم يكن أبو الحسن الأشعري أول متكلم بلسان أهل السنة ، وإنما جرى على سنن غيره وعلى نصرة مذهب معروف ، فزاده حجة وبياناً ، ولم يبتدع مقالة اخترعها ولا مذهباً انفرد به وليس له في المذاهب أكثر من بسطه وشرحه كغيره من الأئمة وقال أبو بكر بن فورك : رجع أبو الحسن الأشعري عن الاعتزال إلى مذهب أهل السنة سنة ٣٠٠ هـ قال و ممن الدين أحمد من العلماء برجوع الأشعري عن الاعتزال أبو العباس شمس بن محمد بن أبي بكر بن خلكان الشافعي المتوفى سنة ٦٨١ هـ ، قال : في ) وفيات الأعيان ( الجزء الثاني صفحة ٤٦٦ : كان أبو الحسن الأشعري معتزلياً ثم تاب ومنهم عماد الدين أبو الفداء إسماعيل بن عمر بن كثير القرشي الدمشقي الشافعي المتوفى سنة ٧٧٤ هـ . قال في البداية والنهاية » : الجزء الحادي عشر صفحة ۱۸۷ : « كان أبو الحسن الأشعري معتزلياً فتاب منه بالبصرة فوق المنبر ، ثم أظهر فضائح المعتزلة وقبائحهم » . ومنهم شمس الدين محمد بن أحمد بن عثمان الدمشقي الشافعي الشهير بالذهبي المتوفى سنة ٧٤٨ هـ : قال في كتابه « العلو للعلي الغفار » : « كان أبو الحسن أولاً معتزلياً أخذ عن أبي علي الجبائي ثم نابذه ورد عليه وصار متكلماً للسنة ، ووافق أئمة الحديث ، فلو انتهى أصحابنا المتكلمون إلى مقالة أبي الحسن ولزموها لأحسنوا ولكنهم خاضوا كخوض حكماء الأوائل في الأشياء ومشوا خلف المنطق فلا قوة إلا بالله » .
An imam, then he disappeared from the people in his house for fifteen days. After that, he went out to the mosque in Basra and ascended the pulpit after Friday prayer and said: 'O people, I was absent from you during this period because I examined [matters] and the evidences became equal before me, and no truth was preponderant over falsehood nor falsehood over truth. So I sought guidance from Allah, Blessed and Exalted, and He guided me to what I have deposited in these my books. And I have divested myself of all that I used to believe, just as I divest myself of this garment of mine.' And he removed a garment he was wearing and threw it away, and gave the books to the people. Among them was the book 'Al-Luma'' and others of his works, some of which will be mentioned shortly, God willing. When the people of hadith and fiqh from Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah read those books, they took what was in them, adopted them, believed in his precedence, and took him as an imam until their school was attributed to him. So he became for the Mu'tazila like a book that is most harmful [؟] and exposes the defects of what he abandoned, so he is most hostile to the people of dhimmah [؟]. Likewise, Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is the most hostile of people to the Mu'tazila, so they malign him and attribute falsehoods to him. And the lengthy stay of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari upon the school of the Mu'tazila does not lead to degradation of his rank; rather, it testifies for him, in knowledge of principles (usul), to high station, and indicates to those with insight his lofty merit. Because one who returns from a school is better acquainted with its defects, more capable of refuting its doubts and uncovering its dissimulations, and more discerning in exposing what they confuse for those who seek guidance with insight. So the comfort of one who reproaches him for that is like the comfort of the debater of Harun ibn Musa al-A'war. His story: Harun al-A'war was a Jew then embraced Islam, and his Islam was good. He memorized the Quran and mastered it, and memorized grammar. One day he debated someone on an issue and Harun overcame him, so the defeated one did not know what to do. He said to him: 'You were a Jew and then became Muslim.' Harun said to him: 'How bad is what I did!' So Harun overcame him in this. And the hadith scholars agreed that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was an imam among the imams of the hadith scholars, and his school was the school of the hadith scholars. He spoke on the principles of religions (usul al-diyanat) according to the way of Ahl al-Sunnah, and refuted the opponents from among the people of deviation and innovation. And he was a drawn sword against the Mu'tazila, the Rawafid, and the innovators from among the people of the qibla and those who deviated from the religion. Whoever attacks him or insults him has extended an evil tongue against all Ahl al-Sunnah. And Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was not the first to speak in the tongue of Ahl al-Sunnah; rather, he followed the path of others and supported a known school, so he increased it in proof and clarification. He did not initiate an article he invented nor a school he alone held, and he had nothing in the schools beyond expanding and explaining it, like other imams. Abu Bakr ibn Furak said: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari returned from I'tizal to the school of Ahl al-Sunnah in the year 300 AH.' He said, 'And among those who [testified] to al-Ash'ari's return from I'tizal is Abu al-Abbas Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr ibn Khallikan al-Shafi'i, died 681 AH. He said in 'Wafayat al-A'yan', part 2, p. 466: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili then repented.' And among them is 'Imad al-Din Abu al-Fida' Isma'il ibn 'Umar ibn Kathir al-Qurashi al-Dimashqi al-Shafi'i, died 774 AH. He said in 'Al-Bidaya wa al-Nihaya', part 11, p. 187: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili, then he repented from it in Basra on the pulpit, then he exposed the scandals and ugly deeds of the Mu'tazila.' And among them is Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn 'Uthman al-Dimashqi al-Shafi'i, known as al-Dhahabi, died 748 AH. He said in his book 'Al-'Uluww li al-'Ali al-Ghaffar': 'Abu al-Hasan was first a Mu'tazili, he studied under Abu 'Ali al-Jubba'i, then he opposed him and refuted him, and became a mutakallim for the Sunnah, and agreed with the imams of hadith. If our fellow mutakallimun had stopped at the article of Abu al-Hasan and adhered to it, they would have done well; but they plunged like the ancient philosophers into things and followed logic. There is no power except with Allah.'
Source pages p. 9

Commentary
This passage is a biographical account of Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari's repentance from Mu'tazilism and his subsequent acceptance by Ahl al-Sunnah. It also includes testimonies from later scholars confirming this event. The passage begins by describing his seclusion of fifteen days, after which he publicly declared on the pulpit in Basra that he had been uncertain and then guided by Allah to the truth, which he compiled in his books. He dramatically removed a garment and gave away his books, symbolizing his complete break from his previous beliefs. The people of hadith and fiqh accepted his works and elevated him to the rank of imam, so much so that his school became identified with them. The passage then argues that al-Ash'ari's former Mu'tazilism actually enhanced his ability to refute them, using the analogy of a person who leaves a religion being better equipped to expose its flaws. This is illustrated with the story of Harun al-A'war, a Jewish convert to Islam who successfully debated an adversary who tried to discredit him for converting. Similarly, al-Ash'ari's background made him a formidable opponent of the Mu'tazila. The hadith scholars unanimously regarded him as one of their own, and he is described as a drawn sword against all heretics. He did not innovate a new doctrine but rather defended and clarified the established Sunni position. The passage closes with quotations from Ibn Furak, Ibn Khallikan, Ibn Kathir, and al-Dhahabi, all confirming his repentance and praising his role. The key theological point is that al-Ash'ari's return was a return to the orthodox Sunni creed, not the invention of a new one.

Key Terms
هارون بن موسى الأعور
المعتزلة
أبو الحسن الأشعري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That al-Ash'ari's former Mu'tazilism discredits his Sunni arguments.
Correction: On the contrary, his background made him more knowledgeable of Mu'tazili flaws and more effective in refuting them.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why does al-Ash'ari's lengthy stay with the Mu'tazila not degrade his rank?
A) Because he secretly remained a Mu'tazili
B) Because those who leave a school know its defects best
C) Because he was forced to stay with them
D) Because the Mu'tazila were not a real school

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's past in Mu'tazilism equipped him with unique insight to refute their errors, turning a potential weakness into a strength.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever clung to a false belief for fear of being called inconsistent if you changed? How does the story of Harun al-A'war challenge that fear and call you to embrace the truth even if it means admitting past error?
?
Think of someone you know who left a misguided group or lifestyle. Do you secretly look down on them for their past, or do you see their experience as a strength? What would it take for you to view them as al-Ash'ari is viewed in this passage?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
intermediate
7 min
Scholarly Consensus on al-Ash'ari's Sunni Orthodoxy
Presents the agreement of hadith scholars and testimonies from later authorities.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how later Sunni scholars (Ibn Furak, Ibn Khallikan, Ibn Kathir, al-Dhahabi) affirmed Imam al-Ash'ari's orthodoxy and his role as a defender of Ahl al-Sunnah.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari is unanimously recognized as an imam of Ahl al-Sunnah, not an innovator.

After recounting al-Ash'ari's repentance and his effective refutation, the passage presents the unanimous agreement of hadith scholars and later authorities that he was an imam of Ahl al-Sunnah. Quotations from Ibn Furak, Ibn Khallikan, Ibn Kathir, and al-Dhahabi solidify his legacy.

Guided Path
1
Read the consensus of hadith scholars
Read the statement that all hadith scholars agreed he was one of their imams and his school was theirs.
The phrase 'أتفق أصحاب الحديث' indicates universal agreement.
2
Study al-Ash'ari's role as defender
He is described as a drawn sword against deviants. Note the list: Mu'tazila, Rawafid, innovators.
He is not accused of being the first; he followed the path of earlier Sunni scholars.
3
Examine the testimonies of later scholars
Read the quotes from Ibn Furak (year 300 AH), Ibn Khallikan, Ibn Kathir, and al-Dhahabi. Each confirms his repentance and contributions.
Al-Dhahabi's quote is particularly interesting: he wished later mutakallimun had stuck to al-Ash'ari's position.
4
Reflect on the significance
These testimonies show that al-Ash'ari is not an isolated figure but part of a continuous tradition.
The passage ends with a warning against attacking him.

عندي حق على باطل ولا باطل على حق ، فاستهديت الله تبارك وتعالى فهداني إلى ما أودعته في كتبي هذه ، وانخلعت من جميع ما كنت اعتقده ، كما انخلعت من ثوبي هذا ، وانخلع من ثوب كان عليه ورمى به ودفع الكتب إلى وضبطه وحفظ النحو ، وناظر إنسان يوماً في مسألة فغلبه هارون فلم يدر المغلوب ما يصنع ، فقال له : أنت كنت يهودياً فأسلمت ، فقال له هارون : فبئس ما صنعت ، فغلبه هارون في هذا . وأتفق أصحاب الحديث أن : أبا الحسن الأشعري كان إماماً من أئمة أصحاب الحديث ، ومذهبه مذهب أصحاب الحديث ، تكلم في أصول الديانات على طريقة أهل السنة ، ورد على المخالفين من أهل الزيغ والبدعة ، وكان على المعتزلة والروافض والمبتدعين من أهل القبلة والخارجين عن الملة سيفاً مسلولاً ومن طعن فيه أو سبه فقد بسط لسان السوء في جميع أهل السنة ، ولم يكن أبو الحسن الأشعري أول متكلم بلسان أهل السنة ، وإنما جرى على سنن غيره وعلى نصرة مذهب معروف ، فزاده حجة وبياناً ، ولم يبتدع مقالة اخترعها ولا مذهباً انفرد به وليس له في المذاهب أكثر من بسطه وشرحه كغيره من الأئمة وقال أبو بكر بن فورك : رجع أبو الحسن الأشعري عن الاعتزال إلى مذهب أهل السنة سنة ٣٠٠ هـ قال و ممن الدين أحمد من العلماء برجوع الأشعري عن الاعتزال أبو العباس شمس بن محمد بن أبي بكر بن خلكان الشافعي المتوفى سنة ٦٨١ هـ ، قال : في ) وفيات الأعيان ( الجزء الثاني صفحة ٤٦٦ : كان أبو الحسن الأشعري معتزلياً ثم تاب ومنهم عماد الدين أبو الفداء إسماعيل بن عمر بن كثير القرشي الدمشقي الشافعي المتوفى سنة ٧٧٤ هـ . قال في البداية والنهاية » : الجزء الحادي عشر صفحة ۱۸۷ : « كان أبو الحسن الأشعري معتزلياً فتاب منه بالبصرة فوق المنبر ، ثم أظهر فضائح المعتزلة وقبائحهم » . ومنهم شمس الدين محمد بن أحمد بن عثمان الدمشقي الشافعي الشهير بالذهبي المتوفى سنة ٧٤٨ هـ : قال في كتابه « العلو للعلي الغفار » : « كان أبو الحسن أولاً معتزلياً أخذ عن أبي علي الجبائي ثم نابذه ورد عليه وصار متكلماً للسنة ، ووافق أئمة الحديث ، فلو انتهى أصحابنا المتكلمون إلى مقالة أبي الحسن ولزموها لأحسنوا ولكنهم خاضوا كخوض حكماء الأوائل في الأشياء ومشوا خلف المنطق فلا قوة إلا بالله » .
An imam, then he disappeared from the people in his house for fifteen days. After that, he went out to the mosque in Basra and ascended the pulpit after Friday prayer and said: 'O people, I was absent from you during this period because I examined [matters] and the evidences became equal before me, and no truth was preponderant over falsehood nor falsehood over truth. So I sought guidance from Allah, Blessed and Exalted, and He guided me to what I have deposited in these my books. And I have divested myself of all that I used to believe, just as I divest myself of this garment of mine.' And he removed a garment he was wearing and threw it away, and gave the books to the people. Among them was the book 'Al-Luma'' and others of his works, some of which will be mentioned shortly, God willing. When the people of hadith and fiqh from Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah read those books, they took what was in them, adopted them, believed in his precedence, and took him as an imam until their school was attributed to him. So he became for the Mu'tazila like a book that is most harmful [؟] and exposes the defects of what he abandoned, so he is most hostile to the people of dhimmah [؟]. Likewise, Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is the most hostile of people to the Mu'tazila, so they malign him and attribute falsehoods to him. And the lengthy stay of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari upon the school of the Mu'tazila does not lead to degradation of his rank; rather, it testifies for him, in knowledge of principles (usul), to high station, and indicates to those with insight his lofty merit. Because one who returns from a school is better acquainted with its defects, more capable of refuting its doubts and uncovering its dissimulations, and more discerning in exposing what they confuse for those who seek guidance with insight. So the comfort of one who reproaches him for that is like the comfort of the debater of Harun ibn Musa al-A'war. His story: Harun al-A'war was a Jew then embraced Islam, and his Islam was good. He memorized the Quran and mastered it, and memorized grammar. One day he debated someone on an issue and Harun overcame him, so the defeated one did not know what to do. He said to him: 'You were a Jew and then became Muslim.' Harun said to him: 'How bad is what I did!' So Harun overcame him in this. And the hadith scholars agreed that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was an imam among the imams of the hadith scholars, and his school was the school of the hadith scholars. He spoke on the principles of religions (usul al-diyanat) according to the way of Ahl al-Sunnah, and refuted the opponents from among the people of deviation and innovation. And he was a drawn sword against the Mu'tazila, the Rawafid, and the innovators from among the people of the qibla and those who deviated from the religion. Whoever attacks him or insults him has extended an evil tongue against all Ahl al-Sunnah. And Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was not the first to speak in the tongue of Ahl al-Sunnah; rather, he followed the path of others and supported a known school, so he increased it in proof and clarification. He did not initiate an article he invented nor a school he alone held, and he had nothing in the schools beyond expanding and explaining it, like other imams. Abu Bakr ibn Furak said: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari returned from I'tizal to the school of Ahl al-Sunnah in the year 300 AH.' He said, 'And among those who [testified] to al-Ash'ari's return from I'tizal is Abu al-Abbas Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr ibn Khallikan al-Shafi'i, died 681 AH. He said in 'Wafayat al-A'yan', part 2, p. 466: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili then repented.' And among them is 'Imad al-Din Abu al-Fida' Isma'il ibn 'Umar ibn Kathir al-Qurashi al-Dimashqi al-Shafi'i, died 774 AH. He said in 'Al-Bidaya wa al-Nihaya', part 11, p. 187: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari was a Mu'tazili, then he repented from it in Basra on the pulpit, then he exposed the scandals and ugly deeds of the Mu'tazila.' And among them is Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn 'Uthman al-Dimashqi al-Shafi'i, known as al-Dhahabi, died 748 AH. He said in his book 'Al-'Uluww li al-'Ali al-Ghaffar': 'Abu al-Hasan was first a Mu'tazili, he studied under Abu 'Ali al-Jubba'i, then he opposed him and refuted him, and became a mutakallim for the Sunnah, and agreed with the imams of hadith. If our fellow mutakallimun had stopped at the article of Abu al-Hasan and adhered to it, they would have done well; but they plunged like the ancient philosophers into things and followed logic. There is no power except with Allah.'
Source pages p. 9

Commentary
This passage is a biographical account of Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari's repentance from Mu'tazilism and his subsequent acceptance by Ahl al-Sunnah. It also includes testimonies from later scholars confirming this event. The passage begins by describing his seclusion of fifteen days, after which he publicly declared on the pulpit in Basra that he had been uncertain and then guided by Allah to the truth, which he compiled in his books. He dramatically removed a garment and gave away his books, symbolizing his complete break from his previous beliefs. The people of hadith and fiqh accepted his works and elevated him to the rank of imam, so much so that his school became identified with them. The passage then argues that al-Ash'ari's former Mu'tazilism actually enhanced his ability to refute them, using the analogy of a person who leaves a religion being better equipped to expose its flaws. This is illustrated with the story of Harun al-A'war, a Jewish convert to Islam who successfully debated an adversary who tried to discredit him for converting. Similarly, al-Ash'ari's background made him a formidable opponent of the Mu'tazila. The hadith scholars unanimously regarded him as one of their own, and he is described as a drawn sword against all heretics. He did not innovate a new doctrine but rather defended and clarified the established Sunni position. The passage closes with quotations from Ibn Furak, Ibn Khallikan, Ibn Kathir, and al-Dhahabi, all confirming his repentance and praising his role. The key theological point is that al-Ash'ari's return was a return to the orthodox Sunni creed, not the invention of a new one.

Key Terms
أئمة أصحاب الحديث
أبو بكر بن فورك
ابن خلكان
ابن كثير
الذهبي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That al-Ash'ari founded a new sect or innovation within Sunni Islam.
Correction: The passage explicitly states he did not initiate an article or school he alone held; he only expanded and explained the existing Sunni school.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what was al-Ash'ari's relationship with the existing Sunni school?
A) He founded a completely new school
B) He followed and supported a known school, adding more proof and explanation
C) He disagreed with all previous scholars
D) He only wrote about logic

Key Takeaway
Later Sunni scholars unanimously affirmed al-Ash'ari's orthodoxy, viewing him as a defender and clarifier of the Sunni tradition, not an innovator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear that someone has repented from a deviant belief, do you truly accept their return, or do you hold their past against them? The scholars unanimously accepted al-Ash'ari. Is your heart as quick to forgive?
?
The passage emphasizes that al-Ash'ari did not innovate but clarified an existing path. Are you content to follow the way of the Salaf, or do you secretly desire to be known as an originator of something new in religion?
?
Al-Dhahabi praised al-Ash'ari for not exceeding the approach of the hadith imams. In your own study of theology, do you check yourself against the methodology of the early scholars, or do you venture beyond their limits?

Al-Ash'ari's Final Creed
States al-Ash'ari's final adherence to Salafi creed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari ultimately adopted the creed of the Salaf, as established by reliable narrations from prominent scholars.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's final creed was the Salafi creed, not Mu'tazilism.

Historical accounts tell us that Imam al-Ash'ari passed through a Mu'tazili phase before returning to the creed of the early Muslims. This lesson examines a concise scholarly assertion that his final position was firmly rooted in the Quran and Sunnah, as transmitted by leading authorities.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage aloud
Read the Arabic sentence carefully, then read the English translation. Notice the strong language used to affirm certainty.
2
Identify the key claim
The passage states that al-Ash'ari's final settled creed was that of the Salaf. Underline the phrase 'استقر أمره' and note its significance.
This is the central historical claim.
3
Understand the evidence
The passage says this is proven by 'these narrations from these eminent figures'. Connect this to the preceding context (not shown) where those narrations were cited.
The author relies on transmitted reports (naql) from known scholars.
4
Reflect on the lesson
Consider why it matters that al-Ash'ari ended on Salafi creed. This shows his alignment with orthodox Sunni theology despite earlier Mu'tazili associations.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وبهذه النقول عن هؤلاء الأعلام ثبت ثبوتاً لا شك فيه ولا مرية أن أبا الحسن الأشعري استقر أمره بعد أن كان معتزلياً على عقيدة السلف التي جاء بها القرآن الكريم وسنة النبي عليه أزكى الصلاة وأتم التسليم
And by these narrations from these eminent figures, it is established with certainty, without any doubt or uncertainty, that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, after having been a Mu'tazili, settled upon the creed of the Salaf which was brought by the Noble Quran and the Sunnah of the Prophet (upon him be the purest prayers and the most complete salutations).
Source pages p. 12

Commentary
This passage states a historical claim backed by evidence from previous narrations: that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, after his earlier Mu'tazili phase, finally adopted the creed of the Salaf (the early pious Muslims). This creed is defined as that which comes from the Quran and the Sunnah of the Prophet. The passage asserts that this is proven beyond doubt by the transmissions from leading scholars.

Key Terms
النقول
الأعلام
استقر أمره
عقيدة السلف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What was the final position of al-Ash'ari according to this passage?
A) He remained a Mu'tazili
B) He adopted the creed of the Salaf
C) He became a philosopher
D) He abandoned theology

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's final creed was the Salafi creed, confirmed by authoritative narrations.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever held a belief sincerely, only to later realize it was not the truth? What did it take for you to let go of it? Are you willing to follow evidence even if it means abandoning a comfortable long-held view?
?
The passage says al-Ash'ari settled on the creed of the Salaf, which comes from Quran and Sunnah. How settled is your own creed? Do you actively seek to ground your beliefs on revelation, or are you influenced by cultural or intellectual trends?
?
If you had to defend your current understanding of Islamic creed with the same certainty as al-Ash'ari's final position, could you cite the narrations and evidences? What steps will you take this week to solidify your knowledge of Salafi creed?

Al-Ash'ari's Major Works After His Repentance
Enumerates several major works authored by al-Ash'ari after his repentance.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will be familiar with several major works authored by Imam al-Ash'ari after his departure from Mu'tazilism.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari authored dozens of works defending Sunni orthodoxy.

This passage lists some of the key books written by al-Ash'ari after his famous repentance. It shows the breadth and depth of his scholarly output, including a massive Qur'an commentary and works refuting various heretical groups.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice how it begins by citing Ibn 'Asakir and Ibn Hazm to establish the number of works.
2
Identify the listed works
Find the titles of the books mentioned: al-Luma', Kashf al-Asrar, Tafsir al-Mukhtazan, al-Fusul, Maqalat al-Muslimin, and al-Ibanah.
The passage also mentions that the compiler has seen three of these works.
3
Reflect on the significance
Consider why the list is important: it demonstrates al-Ash'ari's immense scholarly production and his dedication to refuting falsehood after his repentance.
Note that Tafsir al-Mukhtazan consisted of 500 volumes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقبل البحث في صحة نسبة هذا الكتاب إلى أبي الحسن نذكر نبذة قليلة من تواليفه التي ألفها بعد توبته من الاعتزال . فنقول : قال الحافظ ابن عساكر في كتابه " تبيين كذب المفتري » : ذكر ابن حزم الظاهري أن لأبي الحسن الأشعري خمسة وخمسين تصنيفاً ، ثم قال : ترك ابن حزم من عدد مصنفاته أكثر من مقدار النصف ، وبعد ذلك سردها فقال : منها كتاب اللمع » ، وكتاب أظهر فيه عوار المعتزلة سماه بكتاب « كشف « الأسرار وهتك الأستار » . ومنها تفسير المختزن ، وهو خمسمائة مجلد لم يترك فيه آية تعلق بها بدعي إلا أبطل تعلقه بها ، وجعلها حجة لأهل الحق ، وبين المجمل وشرح المشكل ونقض فيه ما حرفه الجبائي والبلخي في تفسيريهما . ومنها « الفصول في الرد على الملحدين والخارجين على الملة ) كالفلاسفة والطبائعيين والدهريين وأهل التشبيه . ومنها « مقالات المسلمين » استوعب فيه جميع اختلافهم ومقالاتهم وذكرها الحافظ ابن عساكر بأسمائها وموضوعاتها في كتابه « التبيين ) من صفحة ١٢٨ إلى صفحة ١٣٦ ، وقد أطلعت أنا الجامع لهذه الرسالة على ثلاثة من المقالات و ( الإبانة » الكتب المذكورة ، وهي مطبوعة : ( اللمع ) الإسلامية » .
And before researching the correctness of the ascripion of this book to Abu al-Hasan, we mention a small portion of his works that he authored after his repentance from Mu'tazilism. So we say: al-Hafiz Ibn 'Asakir said in his book 'Tabyin Kadhib al-Muftari': Ibn Hazm al-Zahiri mentioned that Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari had fifty-five works. Then he said: Ibn Hazm omitted more than half of the number of his works. After that he listed them and said: Among them is the book 'al-Luma', and a book in which he exposed the flaws of the Mu'tazila, which he called 'Kashf al-Asrar wa Hatk al-Astar'. And among them is 'Tafsir al-Mukhtazan', which consists of five hundred volumes – he did not leave a single verse that any innovator clung to except he invalidated their clinging to it, and made it an argument for the people of truth, and explained the ambiguous and clarified the problematic, and refuted therein what al-Jubba'i and al-Balkhi had distorted in their two commentaries. And among them is 'al-Fusul fi al-Radd 'ala al-Mulhidin wa al-Kharijin 'ala al-Milla', such as the philosophers, naturalists, materialists, and anthropomorphists. And among them is 'Maqalat al-Muslimin', in which he covered all their differences and sayings, and al-Hafiz Ibn 'Asakir mentioned them with their names and topics in his book 'al-Tabyin' from page 128 to page 136. And I, the compiler of this epistle, have seen three of the mentioned books: 'al-Maqalat', and 'al-Ibanah', and they are printed: 'al-Luma' and 'al-Islamiyyah'.
Source pages p. 12

Commentary
This passage provides a list of some of al-Ash'ari's major works after his repentance. It begins by citing Ibn 'Asakir, who in turn cites Ibn Hazm's count of 55 works, but notes that Ibn Hazm omitted over half. Then it lists specific works: (1) al-Luma', (2) a book exposing Mu'tazili flaws called Kashf al-Asrar, (3) Tafsir al-Mukhtazan in 500 volumes that refuted innovated interpretations of verses, (4) al-Fusul against heretics and deviants, (5) Maqalat al-Muslimin covering all Muslim sects. The compiler adds that he has seen three of these works: al-Maqalat, al-Ibanah, and they are printed along with al-Luma'.

اللمع
كشف الأسرار
تفسير المختزن
الفصول
مقالات المسلمين

Misconception: Some might think al-Ash'ari wrote only a few books or that his works were limited to theology.
Correction: This passage shows he authored many works, including a 500-volume tafsir and works against various groups, indicating a vast and diverse output.

Key Takeaway

?

?
After his repentance, al-Ash'ari dedicated his life to writing books defending the truth. If you were to die tomorrow, what written legacy would you leave behind for Islam? What one book will you read or write this year to strengthen your faith?
?
The passage mentions that Ibn Hazm omitted more than half of al-Ash'ari's works. How often do you overlook the full scope of Islamic scholarship and settle for a partial picture? What steps will you take this week to learn about a scholarly work you previously ignored?

Ibn 'Asakir's Praise of al-Ash'ari's Works and al-Ibanah
Ibn 'Asakir's testimony on al-Ash'ari's works and the importance of al-Ibanah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Ibn 'Asakir’s testimony establishes the credibility of al-Ash'ari’s works, especially al-Ibanah, and the importance of accurately transmitting his creed.

Core Idea
Ibn 'Asakir confirms that al-Ash'ari's books are widely acclaimed and that al-Ibanah reveals his high status in knowledge and piety.

Ibn 'Asakir, a renowned historian and hadith scholar, provides valuable external testimony about al-Ash'ari's works. In this passage, he declares that al-Ash'ari's books are famous and respected, and that reading al-Ibanah reveals his deep knowledge and piety. This serves as a foundation for why we must accurately report al-Ash'ari's creed from his own writings.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Note that the poem at the end is corrupted but the main prose is clear.
The poem has OCR issues; focus on the prose sections.
2
Identify the two key quotations
Ibn 'Asakir first states that al-Ash'ari's works are well-known and praised, and that al-Ibanah shows his status. He then argues that because al-Ash'ari's creed is sound, we must report it accurately without alteration.
3
Reflect on the purpose
Consider why Ibn 'Asakir includes this testimony. It reinforces the reliability of al-Ash'ari's works and justifies using al-Ibanah as a primary source for his creed.
This connects to the earlier lessons on al-Ash'ari's methodology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال ابن عساكر في صفحة ۲۸ من ( التبيين ) : وتصانيف أبي الحسن الأشعري بين أهل العلم مشهورة معروفة ؛ وبالإجادة والإصابة للتحقيق عند المحققين موصوفة ومن وقف على كتابه المسمى ( الإبانة » عرف موضعه من العلم والديانة . ثم قال في صفحة ١٥٢ : فإذا كان أبو الحسن كما ذكر عنه من حسن الإعتقاد ، مستصوب المذهب عند أهل المعرفة بالعلم والانقياد يوافقه في أكثر ما يذهب إليه أكابر العباد ، ولا يقدح في معتقده غير أهل الجهل والعناد ، فلا بد أن نحكي عنه معتقده على وجهه بالأمانة ، ونتجنب أن نزيد فيه أو ننقص منه تركاً للخيانة ، لتعلم حقيقة حاله في صحة عقيدته في أصول الديانة ، فاسمع ما ذكره في أول كتابه الذي سماه بالإبانة ونذكر ما يأتي في آخر الرسالة إن شاء الله تعالى : ثم قال في صفحة ۱۷۱ في جملة أبيات نسبها لبعض المعاصرين له : لولم صنف عمر لكفى فكيف وقد تفن مجموعة تربى على المئت لم يــــــــــأل في صنيفها فهـ ــدی بهـ بها المسترشدي ير الإبانة واللمـ ن في العلــــوم بـمـــــا ين د صنع أخذاً بأحسن ما استمع ن ومن تصفحها انتفع
And Ibn 'Asakir said on page 28 of 'al-Tabyin': 'And the works of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari are famous and well-known among the people of knowledge; they are described by the researchers as having excellence and accuracy. And whoever comes across his book called 'al-Ibanah' knows his status in knowledge and piety.' Then he said on page 152: 'Since Abu al-Hasan is, as is reported about him, of good creed, his school is considered correct by those knowledgeable in learning and submission, and the greatest servants agree with him in most of his views, and none disparage his creed except the people of ignorance and obstinacy, then it is necessary for us to report his creed precisely with trustworthiness, and avoid adding or subtracting from it, leaving treachery aside, so that the reality of his situation regarding the soundness of his creed in the fundamentals of religion may be known. So listen to what he mentioned at the beginning of his book which he named al-Ibanah, and we will mention what comes at the end of the epistle, Allah willing.' Then he said on page 171 in a set of verses he attributed to some of his contemporaries: 'Had he not authored anything but 'Umar would have sufficed, so how [when he has excelled?] a collection that exceeds a hundred? He did not spare in its authorship, so [؟] the seeker of guidance with it. [؟] al-Ibanah and al-Luma' [؟] in the sciences with what [؟] he made, taking the best of what he heard, and whoever examined it benefited.'
Source pages p. 13

Commentary
This passage contains three quotations from Ibn 'Asakir's book 'Tabyin Kadhib al-Muftari'. First, he declares that al-Ash'ari's works are well-known and acclaimed for their excellence, and that anyone who reads al-Ibanah recognizes his high level of knowledge and piety. Second, Ibn 'Asakir states that because al-Ash'ari is of good creed and his school is accepted by scholars, we must accurately report his creed from his own book al-Ibanah without alteration. Third, he cites a poem from a contemporary praising al-Ash'ari for his many works, specifically mentioning al-Ibanah and al-Luma'. The poem is somewhat corrupted in the OCR, but the general praise is evident.

مشهورة معروفة
موضعه من العلم والديانة
التبيين

Key Terms from Source Passage

A) His Mu'tazili past
B) His status in knowledge and piety

Key Takeaway
Ibn 'Asakir's testimony confirms that al-Ash'ari's works are authoritative and that al-Ibanah is a key source for his creed.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

Will you commit today to accurately transmit what you learn, even if it contradicts your biases?
?
The contemporary poet said that whoever examines al-Ash'ari's works benefits. When was the last time you examined a primary Islamic text with the genuine intention to understand its author—not to confirm your own views? If it has been a while, what is one book you can open today and read with that intention?

Ibn Asakir's Testimony on al-Ash'ari's Works and Creed
Lists al-Ash'ari's major works and cites Ibn Asakir's praise with a poem.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the historical testimony of Ibn 'Asakir regarding the authenticity and acceptance of al-Ash'ari's works, particularly al-Ibanah.

Core Idea
Ibn Asakir confirms al-Ash'ari's creed through his written works.

Ibn Asakir, a renowned historian and hadith scholar, provides crucial testimony about al-Ash'ari's writings. His endorsement in al-Tabyin affirms that al-Ash'ari's books, especially al-Ibanah, are widely respected and reflect sound creed. This lesson presents that testimony and the poetic praise supporting it.

Guided Path
1
Identify the listed works
Notice the three works mentioned: al-Fusul fi al-Radd 'ala al-Mulhidin, Maqalat al-Muslimin, and the three printed books al-Luma', al-Ibanah, and Maqalat al-Islamiyyin.
The compiler has personally seen three of these works.
2
Read Ibn Asakir's testimony
Focus on the quote from al-Tabyin page 28, where Ibn Asakir praises al-Ash'ari's writings and specifically says whoever reads al-Ibanah knows his standing in knowledge and religion.
3
Understand the context of the poem
The poem, attributed to a contemporary, praises al-Ash'ari's works as surpassing a hundred in number, guiding seekers, and refuting opponents.
Some words are garbled due to OCR, but the overall meaning is clear.
4
Reflect on the significance
Consider why such testimony from a scholar like Ibn Asakir is important for establishing al-Ash'ari's orthodoxy and the authenticity of his works.
This guards against doubts about al-Ash'ari's creed.

ومنها « الفصول في الرد على الملحدين والخارجين على الملة » كالفلاسفة والطبائعيين والدهريين وأهل التشبيه . ومنها « مقالات المسلمين » استوعب فيه جميع اختلافهم ومقالاتهم وذكرها الحافظ ابن عساكر بأسمائها وموضوعاتها في كتابه « التبيين » من صفحة ١٢٨ إلى صفحة ١٣٦ ، وقد أطلعت أنا الجامع لهذه الرسالة على ثلاثة من الكتب المذكورة ، وهي مطبوعة : ( اللمع ) و ( الإبانة ) و ( مقالات الإسلاميين ) . وقال ابن عساكر في صفحة ٢٨ من ( التبيين ) : وتصانيف أبي الحسن الأشعري بين أهل العلم مشهورة معروفة ؛ وبالإجادة والإصابة للتحقيق عند المحققين موصوفة ومن وقف على كتابه المسمى ( الإبانة ) عرف موضعه من العلم والديانة . ثم قال في صفحة ١٥٢ : فإذا كان أبو الحسن كما ذكر عنه من حسن الإعتقاد ، مستصوب المذهب عند أهل المعرفة بالعلم والانقياد يوافقه في أكثر ما يذهب إليه أكابر العباد ، ولا يقدح في معتقده غير أهل الجهل والعناد ، فلا بد أن نحكي عنه معتقده على وجهه بالأمانة ، ونتجنب أن نزيد فيه أو ننقص منه تركاً للخيانة ، لتعلم حقيقة حاله في صحة عقيدته في أصول الديانة ، فاسمع ما ذكره في أول كتابه الذي سماه بالإبانة ونذكر ما يأتي في آخر الرسالة إن شاء الله تعالى : ثم قال في صفحة ١٧١ في جملة أبيات نسبها لبعض المعاصرين له : لولم صنف عمر لكفى فكيف وقد تفنن مجموعة تربى على المئت لم يــأل في صنيفها فهدى به المسترشدي ير الإبانة واللمـ ن في العلـوم بما ين د صنع أخذاً بأحسن ما استمع ن ومن تصفحها انتفع تتــلى معـاني كتبـه فوق المنابر في الجمع افحامــه أهل الكنائس والبيع ويخاف منه فهو الشجا في حلق من ترك المحجة وابتدع
Among them are 'Al-Fusul fi al-Radd 'ala al-Mulhidin wa al-Kharijin 'ala al-Millah' (The Chapters on Refuting the Atheists and Those Who Deviate from the Religion), such as the philosophers, naturalists, materialists, and anthropomorphists. And among them are 'Maqalat al-Muslimin' (The Discourses of the Muslims), in which he encompassed all their differences and discourses, and al-Hafiz Ibn 'Asakir mentioned them by their names and subjects in his book 'Al-Tabyin' from page 128 to page 136. I, the compiler of this treatise, have examined three of the mentioned books, which are printed: 'Al-Luma'', 'Al-Ibanah', and 'Maqalat al-Islamiyyin'. Ibn 'Asakir said on page 28 of 'Al-Tabyin': The writings of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari are famous and well-known among the people of knowledge; they are described by the verifiers as excelling and hitting the mark in verification. Whoever comes across his book called 'Al-Ibanah' knows his standing in knowledge and religion. Then he said on page 152: Since Abu al-Hasan, as mentioned about him, is of good creed, his madhhab is deemed correct by those knowledgeable in learning and submission, and the great worshippers agree with him in most of his views, and none disparage his creed except the ignorant and obstinate, we must relate his creed faithfully, avoiding addition or omission to avoid betrayal, so that you may know the reality of his sound creed in the foundations of religion. So listen to what he mentioned at the beginning of his book which he named 'Al-Ibanah', and we will mention what comes at the end of the treatise, God willing. Then he said on page 171, in a set of verses he attributed to one of his contemporaries: 'Had he not authored, 'Umar would have sufficed, but how [can one] when he has innovated a collection surpassing the hundred? He did not fail in their composition, so the seeker of guidance is guided by them. He saw al-Ibanah and al-Luma' in the sciences with what he made, taking the best of what he heard. Whoever peruses them benefits. The meanings of his books are recited on the pulpits in the gatherings; his refutation of the people of churches and synagogues is feared. He is a thorn in the throat of whoever abandons the path and innovates.'
Source pages p. 13

Commentary
This passage is a biographical and testimonial section from the compiler of the treatise. It first lists several works of Imam al-Ash'ari: 'Al-Fusul fi al-Radd 'ala al-Mulhidin' (a refutation of atheists, materialists, and anthropomorphists), 'Maqalat al-Muslimin' (an exhaustive work on the doctrines of Muslims), and mentions that Ibn 'Asakir catalogued these works with titles and subjects in his book 'Al-Tabyin'. The compiler notes that he has personally seen three of al-Ash'ari's printed books: 'Al-Luma'', 'Al-Ibanah', and 'Maqalat al-Islamiyyin'. Then the passage quotes Ibn 'Asakir's testimony from 'Al-Tabyin' (p. 28) praising al-Ash'ari's writings as famous and accurate, and specifically states that anyone who reads 'Al-Ibanah' recognizes al-Ash'ari's high standing in knowledge and religion. On p. 152, Ibn 'Asakir affirms al-Ash'ari's good creed and the agreement of scholars with him, and emphasizes the need to transmit his creed faithfully. Finally, Ibn 'Asakir cites a contemporary poem praising al-Ash'ari's works, their wide recitation, and their effectiveness against opponents. The poem, though partially garbled in the OCR, conveys that al-Ash'ari's books are recited on pulpits and feared by non-Muslims.

Key Terms
الإبانة
مقالات المسلمين
التبيين
ابن عساكر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may doubt the authenticity of al-Ash'ari's works like al-Ibanah.
Correction: This passage provides early testimonial evidence from Ibn Asakir, a reputable scholar, confirming the existence and acceptance of these works.

Checkpoint
What does Ibn 'Asakir say about anyone who reads al-Ash'ari's book al-Ibanah?
A) He will be considered a heretic.
B) He will know al-Ash'ari's standing in knowledge and religion.
C) He will become a philosopher.
D) He will reject all previous scholarship.

Key Takeaway
Ibn Asakir's testimony confirms that al-Ash'ari's books, especially al-Ibanah, were widely accepted and his creed regarded as sound by the scholarly community.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Ibn 'Asakir stressed transmitting al-Ash'ari's creed 'without addition or omission.' Is there any aspect of your own belief about Allah that you have adopted from hearsay without verifying it from the original sources? What specific step will you take this week to check that belief?
?
The poem describes al-Ash'ari's books as 'a thorn in the throat of whoever abandons the path and innovates.' Does your understanding of Islamic creed have such clarity that it would expose and oppose falsehood? What are you actively doing to equip yourself with sound knowledge so that you can stand firm against deviant ideas?
?
Ibn 'Asakir mentioned that those who disparage al-Ash'ari's creed are 'the ignorant and obstinate.' Have you ever dismissed a scholar's teaching without personally examining their works? What would it take for you to be like the 'verifiers' who test and confirm truth before accepting or rejecting?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
intermediate
4 min
External Testimonies to the Authenticity of Al-Ibanah
Multiple scholars attest that Al-Ibanah is Ash'ari's final authoritative work.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that multiple classical scholars have attested that Al-Ibanah is the authentic final work of Imam al-Ash'ari, establishing its authority for the Ash'ari school.

Core Idea
Scholarly consensus confirms Al-Ibanah as Ash'ari's final creed.

This passage gathers testimonies from renowned scholars like al-Dhahabi, Ibn Kathir, and Ibn Darbas, all confirming that Al-Ibanah is indeed the work of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari and represents his ultimate theological position. By examining these external proofs, we see how the book's authenticity is firmly established.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the full translation to grasp the flow of testimonies.
Notice the repeated phrase 'عزاها لأبي الحسن الأشعري' indicating attribution.
2
Identify the scholars quoted
List the scholars mentioned: al-Dhahabi, Ibn Farhun, Ibn al-Imad, al-Zabidi, Ibn Kathir, and Ibn Darbas. Notice their diverse backgrounds (Shafi'i, Maliki, Hanbali).
Each scholar provides a different angle: al-Dhahabi cites the book's content, Ibn al-Imad calls it his last book, etc.
3
Understand the key claim about Al-Ibanah
The passage repeatedly asserts that Al-Ibanah is Ash'ari's last book and his final doctrinal position. Ibn Darbas argues it is impossible for him to have retracted from it.
Focus on Ibn Darbas's strong rhetorical question at the end.
4
Reflect on the significance
These testimonies establish Al-Ibanah as the definitive source for Ash'ari's creed, silencing claims that he changed his views later.
This is crucial for the Ash'ari school's self-understanding.
