وممن ذكر الإبانة وعزاها لأبي الحسن الأشعري الحافظ المعروف بالذهبي، في كتابه « العلو للعلي الغفار » صفحة ١٦٠ :
قال الأشعري في كتاب الإبانة عن أصول الديانة (١) ، له في باب الإستواء
فإن قال قائل : ما تقولون في الإستواء
قيل نقول : إن الله مستو على عرشه كما قال : ﴿ الرَّحْمَنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوَى ) إلى آخر ما في الإبانة
ثم قال : وكتاب الإبانة من أشهر تصانيف أبي الحسن الأشعري شهره الحافظ ابن عساكر واعتمد عليه ونسخه الإمام محيي الدين النووي .
الأشعري
وذكر الذهبي عن الحافظ أبي العباس أحمد بن ثابت الطرقي : أنه قال : قرأت في كتاب أبي الحسن الأشعري الموسوم بالإبانة أدلة على إثبات الإستواء . ونقل عن أبي علي الدقاق أنه سمع زاهر بن أحمد الفقيه يقول : مات رحمه الله ورأسه في حجري فكان يقول شيئاً في حال نزعه . لعن الله المعتزلة موهوا ومخرقوا . اهـ . كلام البيهقي وممن نسبها إلى أبي الحسن الأشعري ابن فرحون المالكي قال في كتابه الديباج » صفحة ۱۹۳ : ولأبي الحسن الأشعري كتب منها كتاب اللمع الكبير، واللمع الصغير ، وكتاب الإبانة عن أصول الديانة ا.هـ • وممن عزاها لأبي الحسن الأشعري أبو الفلاح عبد الحي ابن العماد الحنبلي المتوفى سنة ١٠٩٨ هـ .
قال في الجزء الثاني من كتابه «شذرات في أعيان من ذهب » صفحة ٣٠٣
قال أبو الحسن الأشعري في كتابه ( الإبانة عن أصول الديانة ، وهو آخر كتاب صنفه وعليه يعتمد أصحابه في الذب عنه عند من يطعن عليه ، ثم ذكر فصلاً كاملاً من الإبانة
وممن عزاها لأبي الحسن الأشعري السيد مرتضى الزبيدي
قال في « إتحاف السادة المتقين بشرح أسرار إحياء علوم الدين » في الجزء الثاني صفحة ٢ ، قال : صنف أبو الحسن الأشعري بعد رجوعه من الاعتزال الموجز، وهو في ثلاث مجلدات ، كتاب مفيد في الرد على الجهمية والمعتزلة، ومقالات الإسلاميين ، وكتاب الإبانة .
• وقد تقدم حكاية عن ابن كثير : أن الإبانة هي آخر كتاب صنفه أبو الحسن الأشعري . وممن ذكر أن الإبانة تأليف أبي الحسن الأشعري أبو القاسم عبد الملك بن عیسی بن درباس الشافعي ، قال في رسالته « الذب عن أبي الحسن الأشعري ) : اعلموا معشر الإخوان أن كتاب الإبانة عن أصول الديانة ، الذي ألفه الإمام أبو الحسن علي بن إسماعيل الأشعري هو الذي استقر عليه أمره فيما يعتقده وبه كان يدين الله سبحانه وتعالى بعد رجوعه من الاعتزال بمن الله ولطفه ، الله ولطفه ، وكل مقالة تنسب إليه الآن مما يخالف ما فيه فقد رجع عنها وتبرأ إلى الله سبحانه منها ، وكيف وقد نص فيه على أنه ديانته التي يدين الله سبحانه بها . وروى وأثبت أنه ديانة الصحابة والتابعين وأئمة الحديث الماضين وقول أحمد بن حنبل رضي الله عنهم أجمعين . وأن ما فيه هو الذي يدل عليه كتاب الله وسنة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم فهل يسوغ أن يقال : إنه رجع عن هذا إلى غيره فإلى ماذا أنراه يرجع عن كتاب الله وسنة يرجع نبي الله خلاف ما كان عليه الصحابة والتابعون وأئمة الحديث المرضيون وقد علم أنه مذهبهم ، ورواه عنهم ؟؟!!
Among those who mentioned Al-Ibanah and attributed it to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is the memorizer known as al-Dhahabi, in his book 'Al-Uluw li al-Ali al-Ghaffar' page 160: He said: Al-Ash'ari said in the book Al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah (1), in the chapter on al-Istiwa': If a questioner says: What do you say about al-Istiwa'? It is said: We say: Indeed, Allah is established over His Throne as He said: 'The Most Merciful established Himself over the Throne' to the rest of what is in Al-Ibanah. Then he said: And the book Al-Ibanah is among the most famous compositions of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari—al-Hafiz Ibn Asakir made it famous, relied upon it, and Imam Muhyi al-Din al-Nawawi copied it. Al-Ash'ari. And al-Dhahabi mentioned from al-Hafiz Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn Thabit al-Turuqi that he said: I read in the book of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari titled Al-Ibanah proofs for establishing al-Istiwa'. And he narrated from Abu Ali al-Daqqaq that he heard Zahir ibn Ahmad al-Faqih say: He died—may Allah have mercy on him—with his head in my lap, and he was saying something during his death throes: May Allah curse the Mu'tazila, they dissimulated and deceived. End quote. Words of al-Bayhaqi. And among those who attributed it to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is Ibn Farhun al-Maliki; he said in his book 'Al-Dibaj' page 193: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari has books including Al-Luma' al-Kabir, Al-Luma' al-Saghir, and the book Al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah. End quote. • And among those who attributed it to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is Abu al-Falah Abd al-Hayy ibn al-Imad al-Hanbali, died 1098 AH. He said in the second volume of his book 'Shadharat fi A'yan min Dhahab' page 303: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari said in his book Al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah—and it is the last book he authored, and upon it his followers rely in defending him against those who attack him—then he quoted an entire chapter from Al-Ibanah. And among those who attributed it to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is Sayyid Murtada al-Zabidi. He said in 'Ithaf al-Sada al-Muttaqin bi Sharh Asrar Ihya Ulum al-Din' in the second volume page 2: He said: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari authored after his return from Mu'tazilism: Al-Mujaz, which is in three volumes, a useful book in refutation of the Jahmiyya and Mu'tazila, and Maqalat al-Islamiyyin, and the book Al-Ibanah. • And the report from Ibn Kathir has preceded: that Al-Ibanah is the last book composed by Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari. And among those who mentioned that Al-Ibanah is the composition of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is Abu al-Qasim Abd al-Malik ibn Isa ibn Darbas al-Shafi'i; he said in his treatise 'Al-Dhabb 'an Abi al-Hasan al-Ash'ari': Know, O community of brothers, that the book Al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah, which the Imam Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Isma'il al-Ash'ari composed, is that upon which his affair settled regarding what he believes, and by it he was professing faith in Allah, Exalted and Majestic, after his return from Mu'tazilism, by the grace and kindness of Allah, Allah and His kindness. And every statement attributed to him now that contradicts what is in it, he has retracted from it and disavowed to Allah, Exalted, from it. And how so, since he stated in it that it is his religion by which he professes faith in Allah, Exalted? And he narrated and affirmed that it is the religion of the Companions, the Successors, and the past Imams of Hadith, and the saying of Ahmad ibn Hanbal—may Allah be pleased with them all. And that what is in it is what is indicated by the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of His Messenger, peace and blessings be upon him. So is it permissible to say that he retracted from this to something else? Then to what do we see him retracting from the Book of Allah and the Sunnah—the peace of the Prophet of Allah—contrary to what was the way of the Companions, the Successors, and the approved Imams of Hadith, when it is known that it is their doctrine, and he narrated it from them?!!
Source pages p. 15

Commentary
This passage is a compilation of external testimonies from multiple scholars—al-Dhahabi, Ibn Farhun, Ibn al-Imad, al-Zabidi, Ibn Kathir, and Ibn Darbas—all attesting that the book 'Al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah' is indeed the work of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari and is his final, authoritative statement of creed. The passage begins by quoting al-Dhahabi's citation of al-Ash'ari's own words from the chapter on al-Istiwa' (Allah's establishedness over the Throne), showing that al-Ash'ari affirms the verse 'The Most Merciful established Himself over the Throne' without further qualification. Al-Dhahabi also notes the fame of the book, relying on Ibn Asakir and al-Nawawi. Then al-Dhahabi quotes al-Turuqi as reading proofs for istiwa' in the book, and a deathbed story where Ash'ari cursed the Mu'tazila. The passage then lists Ibn Farhun's mention of the book among Ash'ari's works, Ibn al-Imad's statement that it was his last book and relied upon by his followers, and al-Zabidi's listing of Ash'ari's works after his conversion, calling al-Ibanah a book of refutation. Finally, the longest testimony is from Ibn Darbas, who argues passionately that al-Ibanah contains Ash'ari's final beliefs, that he never retracted from it, and that its content is identical to the Quran, Sunnah, and the creed of the Salaf and Ahmad ibn Hanbal. Ibn Darbas uses rhetorical questions to assert that it is impossible for Ash'ari to have abandoned the clear truth of the Book and Sunnah. Overall, the passage functions as a historical and doctrinal authentication of the book, establishing its authority for the Ash'ari school.

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think al-Ash'ari abandoned the creed of Al-Ibanah later in life.
Correction: This passage shows multiple scholars affirming that Al-Ibanah is his final book and that he never retracted from its contents.

Checkpoint
Which of the following scholars is NOT mentioned in this passage as attesting to the authorship of Al-Ibanah?
A) Al-Dhahabi
B) Ibn Kathir
C) Al-Ghazali
D) Ibn Darbas

Key Takeaway
The combined testimony of multiple scholars establishes that Al-Ibanah is the authentic, final, and unchanged creed of Imam al-Ash'ari.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face doubts about your own theological commitments, do you have a definitive reference—like Al-Ibanah for Ash'ari—that settles your heart and mind? Are you willing to defend that reference with the same certainty and passion as Ibn Darbas defended Al-Ibanah against detractors?
?
Ibn Darbas argued that Al-Ibanah is identical to the Quran, Sunnah, and the creed of the Salaf. Have you ever hidden behind a scholar's name while secretly doubting that his position truly aligns with those primary sources? What would it take for you to be as absolutely convinced as these scholars that the book you rely on is the unadulterated truth?
?
The scholars in this passage risked their reputations by publicly attesting to a book that was controversial. Is there a truth you know but are afraid to publicly stand by? What would change in your life if you treated that truth as non-negotiable, just as these scholars treated Al-Ibanah as Ash'ari's final, unwavering creed?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
intermediate
8 min
Evidence That al-Ibanah Is al-Ash'ari's Final Creed
Compilation of testimonies affirming al-Ibanah as al-Ash'ari's final creedal work.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the historical and scholarly evidence that establishes al-Ash'ari's book al-Ibanah as his final settled creed after his departure from Mu'tazilism.

Core Idea
Al-Ibanah is al-Ash'ari's final book and represents his definitive Sunni creed.

This passage assembles testimonies from multiple authoritative scholars confirming that al-Ash'ari's final theological work is al-Ibanah, written after his departure from Mu'tazilism. The testimonies emphasize that the book reflects his settled creed, that he never recanted it, and that it aligns with the Salaf's methodology. This compilation serves as a conclusive refutation of claims that al-Ash'ari held conflicting views later in life.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the main claim
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Note that the central thesis is that al-Ibanah is al-Ash'ari's last and authoritative book.
2
List the scholars citing al-Ibanah
As you read, list each scholar mentioned and note what they say about the book. This will help you see the weight of the evidence.
Focus on Murtada al-Zabidi, Ibn Kathir, Ibn Darbas, al-Tarqi, al-Sabuni, al-Fasi, al-Maqdisi, and al-Mujalli.
3
Analyze Ibn Darbas's detailed argument
Ibn Darbas provides the longest testimony. Read carefully his logical argument that al-Ash'ari could not have recanted from a book based on Quran and Sunnah.
Pay attention to his rhetorical questions and strong refutation.
4
Understand the significance of 'final' work
Reflect on why the idea of a 'final' creed is important: it shows al-Ash'ari's matured position and settles debates about any contradictions in his writings.
Consider how this passage is used to defend al-Ash'ari from accusations of later deviation.

قال أبو الحسن الأشعري في كتابه ( الإبانة عن أصول الديانة )، وهو آخر كتاب صنفه وعليه يعتمد أصحابه في الذب عنه عند من يطعن عليه، ثم ذكر فصلاً كاملاً من الإبانة. وممن عزاها لأبي الحسن الأشعري السيد مرتضى الزبيدي قال في « إتحاف السادة المتقين بشرح أسرار إحياء علوم الدين » في الجزء الثاني صفحة ٢، قال : صنف أبو الحسن الأشعري بعد رجوعه من الاعتزال الموجز، وهو في ثلاث مجلدات، كتاب مفيد في الرد على الجهمية والمعتزلة، ومقالات الإسلاميين، وكتاب الإبانة. وقد تقدم حكاية عن ابن كثير : أن الإبانة هي آخر كتاب صنفه أبو الحسن الأشعري. وممن ذكر أن الإبانة تأليف أبي الحسن الأشعري أبو القاسم عبد الملك بن عیسی بن درباس الشافعي، قال في رسالته « الذب عن أبي الحسن الأشعري ) : اعلموا معشر الإخوان أن كتاب الإبانة عن أصول الديانة، الذي ألفه الإمام أبو الحسن علي بن إسماعيل الأشعري هو الذي استقر عليه أمره فيما يعتقده وبه كان يدين الله سبحانه وتعالى بعد رجوعه من الاعتزال بمن الله ولطفه، وكل مقالة تنسب إليه الآن مما يخالف ما فيه فقد رجع عنها وتبرأ إلى الله سبحانه منها، وكيف وقد نص فيه على أنه ديانته التي يدين الله سبحانه بها. وروى وأثبت أنه ديانة الصحابة والتابعين وأئمة الحديث الماضين وقول أحمد بن حنبل رضي الله عنهم أجمعين. وأن ما فيه هو الذي يدل عليه كتاب الله وسنة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم فهل يسوغ أن يقال : إنه رجع عن هذا إلى غيره فإلى ماذا أنراه يرجع عن كتاب الله وسنة نبي الله خلاف ما كان عليه الصحابة والتابعون وأئمة الحديث المرضيون وقد علم أنه مذهبهم، ورواه عنهم ؟؟!! هذا العمري! ما لا يليق نسبته إلى عوام المسلمين وكيف بأئمة الدين، أو هل يقال : إنه جهل فيما نقله عن السلف الماضيين مع إفنائه جل عمره في استقراء المذاهب وتعرف الديانات هذا مما لا يتوهمه منصف، ولا يزعمه إلا مكابر مسرف، وقد ذكر الإبانة واعتمد عليها وأثبتها عن الإمام أبي الحسن الأشعري وأثنى عليه بما ذكره فيها وبرأه من كل بدعة نسبت إليه، ونقل منها تصنيفه جماعة من الأئمة الأعلام من فقهاء الإسلام وأئمة القراء وحفاظ الحديث وغيرهم. وذكر ابن درباس طائفة من الذين قدمنا ذكرهم وزاد الحافظ أبو العباس بن ثابت الطرقي، وذكر عنه أنه قال في بيان مسألة الاستواء من تأليفه : رأيت هؤلاء الجهمية ينتمون في نفي علو الله على العرش وتأويل الإستواء إلى أبي الحسن الأشعري، وما هذا بأول باطل أدعوه وكذب تعاطوه، فقد قرأت في كتابه الموسوم بالإبانة عن أصول الديانة أدلة من جملة ما ذكرته على إثبات الإستواء، ومنهم الإمام الأستاذ الحافظ أبو عثمان إسماعيل بن عبد الرحمن بن أحمد الصابوني ذكره عنه أنه ما كان يخرج إلا مجلس درسه إلا بيده كتاب الإبانة لأبي الحسن الأشعري ويظهر الإعجاب بها، ويقول : ما الذي ينكر على من هذا الكتاب شرح مذهبه ( هذا قول الإمام أبي عثمان وهو من أعيان أهل الأثر بخراسان ). ومنهم إمام القراء أبو علي الحسن بن علي بن إبراهيم الفاسي ذكر الإمام أبا الحسن الأشعري رحمه الله تعالى فقال : وله كتاب في السنة سماه كتاب الإبانة صنفه ببغداد لما دخلها، وذكر ابن درباس أنه وجد كتاب الإبانة في كتب أبي الفتح نصر المقدسي رحمه الله تعالى ببيت المقدس وقال : رأيت في بعض تأليفه في الأصول فصولاً منها بخطه. ومنهم الفقيه أبو المعالي مجلي صاحب كتاب ( الذخائر في الفقه )، قال:
Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari said in his book 'Al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah', which is the last book he authored and upon which his followers rely in defending him against those who attack him, then he mentioned a complete chapter from al-Ibanah. Among those who attributed it to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is Sayyid Murtada al-Zabidi, who said in 'Ithaf al-Sadah al-Muttaqin bi-Sharh Asrar Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din', part two, page 2: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari authored after his return from I'tizal: al-Mujaz, which is in three volumes, a beneficial book refuting the Jahmiyyah and Mu'tazilah, Maqalat al-Islamiyyin, and the book al-Ibanah. And the narration from Ibn Kathir has preceded: that al-Ibanah is the last book authored by Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari. Among those who mentioned that al-Ibanah is the composition of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari is Abu al-Qasim 'Abd al-Malik ibn 'Isa ibn Darbas al-Shafi'i, who said in his epistle 'al-Dhabb 'an Abi al-Hasan al-Ash'ari': Know, O brethren, that the book al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah, which the Imam Abu al-Hasan 'Ali ibn Isma'il al-Ash'ari authored, is that upon which his affair settled regarding what he believed and with which he worshiped God Almighty after his return from I'tizal by God's grace and kindness. And every statement now attributed to him that contradicts what is in it, he has retracted from it and disavowed himself to God Almighty from it. How so, when he stated in it that it is his religion with which he worships God Almighty? And he narrated and affirmed that it is the religion of the Companions, the Followers, and the past Imams of Hadith, and the saying of Ahmad ibn Hanbal, may God be pleased with them all. And that what is in it is that which the Book of God and the Sunnah of the Messenger of God, peace be upon him, indicate. So is it permissible to say that he retracted from this to something else? Then to what do we see him retract from the Book of God and the Sunnah of the Prophet of God to something contrary to what the Companions, Followers, and approved Imams of Hadith were upon, when it is known that it was their doctrine, and he narrated it from them?!! This is [something]! That which is not fitting to be attributed to the common Muslims, how about the Imams of religion? Or is it said that he was ignorant in what he transmitted from the pious predecessors, despite spending most of his life in studying the schools of thought and learning the religions? This is something that no fair-minded person would imagine, nor would anyone claim it except a stubborn and extreme person. And he mentioned al-Ibanah and relied upon it, and affirmed it from Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, and praised him for what he mentioned in it, and absolved him of every innovation attributed to him. And a group of prominent imams from the jurists of Islam, imams of recitation, hadith memorizers, and others transmitted his book from it. And Ibn Darbas mentioned a group of those we have previously mentioned, and added al-Hafiz Abu al-'Abbas ibn Thabit al-Tarqi, and mentioned from him that he said in his treatise on the issue of al-Istiwa': I saw these Jahmiyyah claiming, in denying the elevation of God over the Throne and interpreting al-Istiwa', to Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari. And this is not the first falsehood I have denounced and lie they have perpetrated. For I have read in his book titled al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah proofs from what I mentioned affirming al-Istiwa'. Among them is the Imam, the teacher, al-Hafiz Abu 'Uthman Isma'il ibn 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Ahmad al-Sabuni, who related from him that he would not go out except to his study circle without having in his hand the book al-Ibanah of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, showing admiration for it, and saying: What can be objected to regarding one whose book explains his doctrine? (This is the saying of Imam Abu 'Uthman, and he is among the authorities of the people of tradition in Khurasan.) Among them is the Imam of the Reciters, Abu 'Ali al-Hasan ibn 'Ali ibn Ibrahim al-Fasi, who mentioned Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, may God Almighty have mercy on him, and said: He has a book on the Sunnah called the book al-Ibanah, which he authored in Baghdad when he entered it. And Ibn Darbas mentioned that he found the book al-Ibanah among the books of Abu al-Fath Nasr al-Maqdisi, may God Almighty have mercy on him, in Jerusalem, and said: I saw in some of his works on principles chapters from it in his handwriting. Among them is the faqih Abu al-Ma'ali Mujalli, author of the book 'al-Dhakha'ir fi al-Fiqh', who said:
Source pages p. 16

Commentary
This passage is an editorial introduction that compiles testimonies from numerous scholars to establish that al-Ibanah is the final authoritative book of Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari and represents his settled creed after his repentance from Mu'tazilism. The passage proceeds as follows: First, it identifies al-Ibanah as al-Ash'ari's last work upon which his followers rely for defense. Then it cites Murtada al-Zabidi, who lists al-Ash'ari's post-I'tizal works including al-Ibanah. Next, it references Ibn Kathir's statement that al-Ibanah is the last book. The long quotation from Ibn Darbas argues forcefully that al-Ibanah is al-Ash'ari's final doctrine, that any contradictory statements attributed to him are retracted, and that the book represents the creed of the Salaf and Ahmad ibn Hanbal. Ibn Darbas rejects the notion that al-Ash'ari later recanted from al-Ibanah, calling such a claim absurd. The passage then lists other scholars who confirmed the authenticity of al-Ibanah: al-Tarqi, al-Sabuni (who carried the book and admired it), al-Fasi (who said it was written in Baghdad), al-Maqdisi (who found it in his library), and al-Mujalli. Each testimony reinforces the same point: al-Ibanah is a reliable and celebrated work that reflects al-Ash'ari's final creed.

Key Terms
الإبانة
أصول الديانة
الذب عن أبي الحسن الأشعري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some claim that al-Ash'ari recanted from al-Ibanah and adopted different views later in life.
Correction: The passage shows multiple scholars affirming that al-Ibanah is his final book and that any contradictory statements are retracted. Ibn Darbas explicitly argues that it is impossible for him to have recanted from a book based on Quran and Sunnah.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the significance of al-Ibanah in relation to al-Ash'ari's theological positions?
A) It was his first work written before joining the Mu'tazila.
B) It is a summary of the Mu'tazili position that he later abandoned.
C) It is his last book and represents his final settled creed after leaving Mu'tazilism.
D) It was written by a student of al-Ash'ari, not by him personally.

Key Takeaway
Multiple testimonies confirm that al-Ibanah is al-Ash'ari's final and authoritative creed, representing his settled Sunni theology after departing from Mu'tazilism.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read multiple testimonies affirming al-Ibanah as al-Ash'ari's final creed. What is the one theological or spiritual conviction that you hold as final and unchangeable, and would you be willing to defend it with the same rigor as Ibn Darbas defended al-Ash'ari?
?
Al-Ash'ari was accused of various positions throughout his life. When others misunderstand or misrepresent your faith, do you rely on a settled source like al-Ibanah, or are you swayed by accusations? What would it take for you to confidently say 'this is my religion and I will not retract'?
?
Ibn Darbas says any statement contradicting al-Ibanah has been retracted. Is there any belief or practice in your life that you have not fully committed to, leaving room for retraction? What would it mean to settle 'your affair' as al-Ash'ari did?

Life of Imam al-Ashari

The Editor's Endorsement of the Ash'ari Creed
Editorial praise of the Ash'ari creed's clarity and orthodoxy.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the significance of the editor's praise for Imam al-Ash'ari's creed and its alignment with Sunni orthodoxy.

Core Idea
The editor's praise highlights the clarity, orthodoxy, and unity of the Ash'ari creed with the Sunni tradition.

The editor of the present work concludes his presentation of Imam al-Ash'ari's creed with heartfelt praise. In this passage, he emphasizes the creed's clarity and its harmony with the teachings of Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, affirming that the Ash'ari school is firmly within Sunni Islam. This lesson unpacks the editor's remarks and what they reveal about the reception of Ash'ari's theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the editor's concluding remarks
Read the Arabic passage and its translation carefully to grasp the tone and content of the editor's praise.
The passage is not part of the original creed but a later editorial addition.
2
Identify the key claims
Notice the editor states this creed was Ash'ari's final position after forty years of Mu'tazilism, and that it aligns with Ahmad ibn Hanbal.
This shows the creed's orthodox credentials.
3
Reflect on the editor's exhortation
The editor urges the reader to embrace this creed and warns against deviation. Consider why such a strong endorsement is made.
The editor wants to assert the creed's correctness and discourage innovation.
4
Connect to broader context
Understand that this editorial is a later voice validating the Ash'ari school as mainstream Sunni.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
هذا مجمل عقيدة الإمام أبي الحسن الأشعري التي استقر أمره عليها بعد أن أقام على مذهب الاعتزال أربعين عاماً . ذكره في أول كتابه الإبانة وفصله باباً باباً فراجعها إن شئت تجد ما يشفي ويكفي . فتأمل أيها الأخ المنصف هذه العقيدة ما أوضحها وأبينها واعترف بفضل هذا الإمام العالم الذي شرحها وبينها ، وانظر سهولة لفظها فما أفصحه وأحسنه . وكن ممن قال الله فيهم : ﴿ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ) وتبين فضل أبي الحسن الأشعري واعرف إنصافه واسمع وصفه للإمام أحمد بن حنبل بالفضل لتعلم أنهما كانا في الاعتقاد متفقين وفي أصول الدين ومذهب السنة غير مفترقين . ولعمري أن هذه العقيدة ينبغي لكل مسلم أن يعتقدها ولا يخرج عن شيء منها إلا من في قلبه غش ونكد نسأل الله تعالى الثبات عليها ونستودعها عند من لا تضيع عنده وديعة والحمد لله الذي بنعمته تتم الصالحات وصلى الله وسلم على نبينا محمد معلم الخيرات وعلى آله وأصحابه ومن تبعهم بإحسان إلى يوم تجزى فيه الحسنات حماد الأنصاري
This is the summary of the creed of Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari upon which his position settled after he had remained upon the doctrine of Mu'tazilism for forty years. He mentioned it in the beginning of his book al-Ibanah and detailed it chapter by chapter, so refer to it if you wish, you will find what suffices and satisfies. So contemplate, O fair-minded brother, this creed—how clear and evident it is! And acknowledge the virtue of this imam, the scholar who explained and elucidated it. And look at its ease of expression—how eloquent and beautiful it is! And be among those of whom God said: “Those who listen to the word and follow the best of it.” And realize the virtue of Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, and know his fairness, and hear his description of Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal with virtue, so that you may know that they were in agreement in belief and in the principles of religion and the way of the Sunna, not differing. By my life, this creed should be believed by every Muslim, and none deviates from any part of it except one in whose heart there is deception and trouble. We ask God Almighty for steadfastness upon it, and we entrust it to Him with Whom no trust is lost. Praise be to God by whose grace good deeds are completed. And may God bless and grant peace to our Prophet Muhammad, teacher of good things, and to his family and companions and those who follow them in righteousness until the Day when good deeds are recompensed. Hammad al-Ansari.
Source pages p. 27

Commentary
This passage is the editor's concluding remarks after presenting the creed of Imam al-Ash'ari. It begins by describing that this creed represents Ash'ari's final position after forty years on the Mu'tazilite school, indicating his return to Sunni orthodoxy. The editor notes that Ash'ari presented this creed in his book al-Ibanah, detailing it chapter by chapter, and encourages the reader to consult it. He then calls upon the fair-minded reader to reflect on the clarity and evident nature of this creed, and to acknowledge the virtue of Ash'ari for explaining and making it clear. The editor praises the ease and eloquence of its expression. He exhorts the reader to be among those who listen to speech and follow the best of it, quoting Quran 39:18. He then highlights Ash'ari's fairness and his praise of Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, establishing that both imams were united in belief, principles of religion, and the Sunni way, dispelling any notion of division. The editor emphatically states that this creed ought to be believed by every Muslim, and that only those with deceit and trouble in their hearts would deviate from it. He concludes with a prayer for steadfastness upon this creed, entrusting it to God, and standard Islamic blessings upon the Prophet and his followers. The passage ends with the editor's name, Hammad al-Ansari.

أصول الدين
مذهب السنة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that al-Ash'ari followed Ahmad ibn Hanbal in every detail of jurisprudence or theology.
Correction: The editor states they were in agreement in belief (faith) and the principles of religion, meaning on fundamentals, not necessarily all matters of detail. Their agreement is on the core creed, not on every subsidiary issue.

Checkpoint
How long did Imam al-Ash'ari remain upon the Mu'tazilite doctrine before adopting this creed?
A) Twenty years
B) Thirty years
C) Forty years
D) Fifty years

Key Takeaway
The editor's praise confirms that the Ash'ari creed, as presented in al-Ibanah, is clear, orthodox, and fully aligned with Sunni principles as taught by Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The editor calls you to 'contemplate this creed—how clear and evident it is.' Have you truly examined your own beliefs to see if they align with this clarity, or have you accepted teachings that obscure the truth? What specific step will you take today to verify that your creed matches the clarity of the Ash'ari creed as described in this passage?
?
The editor says that only those with 'deception and trouble' in their hearts deviate from this creed. What hidden deceptions or worldly attachments might be causing you to compromise on any part of your belief in Allah's attributes or other principles? Ask Allah to purify your heart from such deception.
?
The editor praises the agreement between al-Ash'ari and Ahmad ibn Hanbal, showing unity in the principles of religion. Is there any theological disagreement you hold that actually divides the Sunni community? How can you seek unity based on the core principles outlined here?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
intermediate

Al-Ash'ari's Three Theological Stages
Al-Ash'ari's three theological stages: Mu'tazilism, partial affirmation, and Salafi method.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three theological stages of Imam al-Ash'ari, culminating in his final Salafi method of affirming all divine attributes without modality or anthropomorphism.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari’s journey from Mu'tazilism to Salafi orthodoxy shows the triumph of Sunni theology.

Imam al-Ash'ari (d. 324 AH) is one of the most influential figures in Sunni theology, but his thought underwent significant development. This passage from his later work al-Ibanah outlines the three stages of his theological evolution, from early Mu'tazilism to his final position that aligns with the Salaf. Understanding this progression is key to appreciating his mature creed.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic text and its translation to get an overview of the three stages. Pay attention to the terminology used for each stage.
The passage is dense; read it twice.
2
Identify each stage
The passage explicitly lists three stages. Note the key features of each: the first is Mu'tazilism, the second affirms seven rational attributes but interprets verbal attributes, and the third affirms all attributes without ta'wil, takyeef, or tashbeeh.
The second stage includes a list of seven attributes; note that 'knowledge' is missing in this text.
3
Understand the progression
Recognize the movement away from Mu'tazilite negation, through a middle stage that still used interpretation, to a final stage that accepts the apparent meaning of all attributes while rejecting modality and anthropomorphism.
This progression reflects al-Ash'ari's return to the way of the Salaf.
4
Connect to al-Ash'ari's work
The passage states that his method in al-Ibanah represents the third stage. Consider why this matters for understanding his mature theology.
Al-Ibanah is considered his final and most authoritative work.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد مرَّ الإمام الأشعري بثلاثة أحوال : الحال الأول : الاعتزال ، وقد تاب منه ورجع عنه ، والحال الثاني : إثبات الصفات العقلية السبعة ، وهي : الحياة، والقدرة ، والإرادة ، والسمع ، والبصر ، والكلام ، وتأويل الصفات الخبرية ، كالوجه ، واليدين ، والقدم ، والساق ، والحال الثالث : إثبات ذلك كله من غير تكييف ولا تشبيه جرياً على منوال السلف الصالح رضي الله عنهم ، وهي طريقته في ( الإبانة ) الذي ألفه أخيراً.
And Imam al-Ash'ari passed through three states: the first state: I'tizal (Mu'tazilism), and he repented from it and turned away from it; the second state: affirmation of the seven mental attributes, namely: life, power, will, hearing, sight, speech, and interpretation of the verbal attributes, such as face, hands, foot, and shin; and the third state: affirmation of all that without modality (takyeef) or anthropomorphism (tashbeeh), following the way of the righteous predecessors (Salaf), may Allah be pleased with them, and this is his method in al-Ibanah which he authored last.
Source pages p. 29

Commentary
This passage classifies the three theological stages that Imam al-Ash'ari went through during his life. The first stage was Mu'tazilism (i'tizal), which he later repented from. The second stage involved affirming seven rational attributes of Allah (life, power, will, hearing, sight, speech — knowledge is missing in the list) while interpreting (ta'wil) the so-called 'verbal attributes' (such as face, hands, foot, shin) away from their apparent meanings. The third and final stage was affirming all attributes — both rational and verbal — without insisting on how they are (takyeef) and without likening them to creation (tashbeeh), adhering strictly to the method of the Salaf. The passage states that his book al-Ibanah represents this final stage. This shows the evolution of al-Ash'ari's thought and his ultimate return to the way of the early Sunni community.

Key Terms
أحوال
الاعتزال
الصفات العقلية
الصفات الخبرية
تأويل
تكييف
تشبيه
السلف الصالح

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Al-Ash'ari remained a Mu'tazilite throughout his life.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that he repented from Mu'tazilism and passed through two subsequent stages, culminating in a Salafi method.

Checkpoint
What was al-Ash'ari's method in his final stage?
A) Affirming attributes with interpretation (ta'wil)
B) Complete negation of attributes
C) Affirming all attributes without modality or anthropomorphism
D) Following the philosophy of the Mu'tazila

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's theological journey moved from Mu'tazilism to affirming all divine attributes without modality or anthropomorphism, following the Salaf.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Al-Ash'ari moved from Mu'tazilism to full submission to the texts. Are you holding onto any interpretation about Allah that you know contradicts the way of the Salaf, yet you hesitate to let go? What is the root of that hesitation?
?
Stage two seems reasonable: affirm some attributes but interpret others. But the Salaf did not distinguish. Do you trust that Allah's attributes are exactly as He described, even if your mind cannot reconcile them? What would it mean for your worship if you truly affirmed them without takyeef?
?
Al-Ash'ari publicly repented from his first stage. Is there a belief you held in the past that you now know was wrong? Have you actually repented from it, or do you still defend it quietly? What step can you take today to align your heart completely with the truth?

Al-Ash'ari's Creed: Quran, Sunnah, and the Salaf
Al-Ash'ari declares his creed as adherence to Quran, Sunnah, and the way of Ahmad ibn Hanbal.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the foundational creed of Imam al-Ash'ari as expressed in al-Ibanah, and its significance in his break from Mu'tazilism.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's creed is total adherence to Quran, Sunnah, and the way of Ahmad ibn Hanbal.

This lesson presents the opening statement from al-Ash'ari's major work, al-Ibanah. After years of Mu'tazilite rationalism, al-Ash'ari publicly declared his return to the way of the Salaf. His creed is a powerful affirmation of textual authority over independent reason. Through this passage, we witness his explicit alignment with Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the confrontational context with the Mu'tazila.
2
Identify the three components of his creed
Al-Ash'ari lists adherence to the Quran, the Sunnah, and what is narrated from the Companions, Successors, and hadith imams. Identify each one and understand how they form the foundation of his theology.
He specifically mentions Ahmad ibn Hanbal as his reference point.
3
Understand the historical context
The passage explains that al-Ash'ari made this declaration after leaving the Mu'tazili school at around 40 years of age (304 AH). Reflect on why this timing is significant.
The Mu'tazila prioritized reason over revelation.
4
Recognize the scholarly witnesses
Several notable scholars are listed as testifying to his return from Mu'tazilism. Note their names and the fact that they document his return.
This counters any claims of ambiguity in his position.
5
Reflect on the passage's significance
Consider why this statement is a cornerstone of Ash'ari identity. How does it anchor the school in traditionalist methodology?
Al-Ash'ari directly aligns with Ahmad ibn Hanbal, a key traditionalist figure.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
عصره ، وما امتحن به من أجل عقيدة الإسلام من قبل المعتزلة الذين حاولوا أن يحكموا عقولهم ويقدموها على كتاب الله ، وسنة رسوله ، لذلك قال الأشعري في أول كتابه : « وقولنا الذي نقول به ، وديانتنا التي ندين بها : التمسك بكتاب ربنا عز وجل وسنة نبينا عليه السلام ، وما روي عن الصحابة والتابعين وأئمة الحديث ، ونحن بذلك معتصمون ، وبما كان يقول به أبو عبد الله أحمد بن حنبل ـ نضر الله وجهه ورفع درجته وأجزل مثوبته ـ قائلون » هذه عقيدة الإمام أبي الحسن الأشعري التي استقر أمره عليها بعد أن أقام على مذهب الاعتزال إلى ما بعد الأربعين من عمره ، حيث أيقظ عز وجل بصيرته ، وهو في بداية نضجه وذلك سنة ٣٠٤ هـ ، فأعلن رجوعه عن تعطيل المعتزلة ، وأظهر فضائحهم وقبائحهم ، ولذلك شنعوا عليه ونسبوا إليه الأباطيل قال برجوعه من مذهب الاعتزال : ابن خلكان في « وفيات الأعيان» والحافظ الذهبي في ( العلو للعلي الغفار ) ، وابن كثير في ( البداية والنهاية » ، والسبكي في ( طبقات الشافعية ) ، وابن فرحون المالكي في ( الديباج المذهب والمرتضى في ( شرح الإحياء » . والعلم وممن
His era, and what he was tested with for the sake of the creed of Islam by the Mu'tazila who tried to make their intellects rule and give them precedence over the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of His Messenger. Therefore al-Ash'ari said at the beginning of his book: 'Our statement which we hold and our religion which we practice is: adherence to the Book of our Lord, Mighty and Majestic, and the Sunnah of our Prophet, peace be upon him, and what is narrated from the Companions, the Successors, and the imams of hadith. We cling to that, and we say what Abu 'Abdillah Ahmad ibn Hanbal (may Allah brighten his face, raise his rank, and multiply his reward) used to say.' This is the creed of Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari upon which he settled after having followed the Mu'tazili school until after his fortieth year, when Allah, Mighty and Majestic, awakened his insight at the beginning of his maturity in the year 304 AH. He announced his return from the Mu'tazilite negation, exposed their scandals and reprehensible deeds, for which they denounced him and attributed falsehoods to him. Those who reported his return from the Mu'tazili school are: Ibn Khallikan in 'Wafayat al-A'yan', al-Hafiz al-Dhahabi in 'al-'Uluw li-l-'Ali al-Ghaffar', Ibn Kathir in 'al-Bidaya wa al-Nihaya', al-Subki in 'Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya', Ibn Farhun al-Maliki in 'al-Dibaj al-Mudhahhab', and al-Murtada in 'Sharh al-Ihya'. And the scholars and those who...
Source pages p. 29

Commentary
This passage introduces the context and central creedal statement from al-Ash'ari's al-Ibanah. It first describes the hostility he faced from the Mu'tazila for prioritizing reason over revelation. Then it quotes al-Ash'ari's own words summarizing his creed: total adherence to the Quran, Sunnah, and the transmitted reports from the Companions, Successors, and hadith imams, specifically aligning with Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal. The author explains that this statement represents al-Ash'ari's settled position after abandoning Mu'tazilism in his forties (around 304 AH). The passage then lists several scholars who documented his return, emphasizing its authenticity. The final phrase 'والعلم وممن' is unclear but likely intended to list further scholars.

?

Life of Imam al-Ashari

Al-Ash'ari's Three Theological Stages
Al-Ash'ari's three stages: Mu'tazilism, affirming seven attributes with metaphorical interpretation, and affirming all attributes without modality or likeness.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three distinct theological stages of Imam al-Ash'ari and how he ultimately adopted the position of affirming all divine attributes without modality or likeness, following the salaf.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's journey: from Mu'tazilism to affirming attributes without interpretation.

Imam al-Ash'ari, the founder of the Ash'ari school, underwent a significant theological evolution. This passage from al-Ibanah outlines his three stages, culminating in his final position of affirming all attributes without takif or tashbih, in line with the righteous predecessors. Understanding this development is key to appreciating Ash'ari theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that the passage uses a numbered list-like structure.
2
Identify the three stages
Find each stage mentioned: Mu'tazilism, the seven rational attributes with interpretation, and full affirmation without modality.
The stages are labeled al-hal al-awwal, al-thani, al-thalith.
3
Understand the first stage
The first stage is Mu'tazilism, from which al-Ash'ari repented. Reflect on what that means for his development.
Mu'tazilites emphasized reason and often interpreted attributes allegorically.
4
Compare the second and third stages
In the second stage, he affirmed seven rational attributes but interpreted reported ones. In the third, he affirmed all without interpretation. Note the difference.
The third stage aligns with the salaf methodology.
5
Reflect on the final stage's significance
The third stage is his final position, as seen in al-Ibanah. Understand why this matters for Ash'ari theology.
This shows his return to a more traditionalist stance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد مرَّ الإمام الأشعري بثلاثة أحوال : الحال الأول : الاعتزال ، وقد تاب منه ورجع عنه ، والحال الثاني : إثبات الصفات العقلية السبعة ، وهي : الحياة، ، والقدرة ، والإرادة ، والسمع ، والبصر ، والكلام ، وتأويل الصفات الخبرية ، كالوجه ، واليدين ، والقدم ، والساق ، والحال الثالث : إثبات ذلك كله من غير تكييف ولا تشبيه جرياً على منوال السلف الصالح رضي الله عنهم ، وهي طريقته في ( الإبانة » الذي ألفه أخيراً .
And Imam al-Ash'ari passed through three states: The first state: Mu'tazilism, from which he repented and returned. The second state: Affirming the seven rational attributes, which are: life, power, will, hearing, sight, speech, and interpreting the reported attributes (al-sifat al-khabariyya) such as the face, the hands, the foot, and the shin. The third state: Affirming all of that without modality (takif) or likeness (tashbih), following the way of the righteous predecessors (al-salaf al-salih), may Allah be pleased with them, and this is his method in al-Ibanah which he authored last.
Source pages p. 29

Commentary
This passage enumerates the three theological stages of Imam al-Ash'ari. The first stage is his early adherence to Mu'tazilism, which he later repented from. The second stage is a transitional position where he affirmed the seven 'rational attributes' (life, power, will, hearing, sight, speech, and speech? Actually it mentions al-kalam, so knowledge? The list includes life, power, will, hearing, sight, and speech — but he omits knowledge? Typically the seven attributes are life, knowledge, power, will, hearing, sight, speech. Here it says 'life, power, will, hearing, sight, speech' — that's six, but there is a comma after 'life' so maybe it's seven? Actually it says: 'الحياة، والقدرة، والإرادة، والسمع، والبصر، والكلام' That is six. Perhaps knowledge is missing or implied. The text may have omitted 'العلم'. However, in this stage, he affirmed these attributes but interpreted metaphorical attributes like face, hands, foot, shin. The third and final stage is affirming all attributes (including the reported ones) without takif (asking how) or tashbih (likening to creation), following the salaf. The passage explicitly states that this is the method of his book al-Ibanah, which he wrote last, indicating it was his final position.

Key Terms
الاعتزال
الصفات العقلية السبعة
الصفات الخبرية
التكييف
التشبيه
السلف الصالح
الحال الأول
الحال الثاني
الحال الثالث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think al-Ash'ari never returned to the full salaf position, and that his theology remained allegorical.
Correction: This passage shows that his third and final stage affirms all attributes without interpretation or allegory, following the salaf.

Checkpoint
What characterizes al-Ash'ari's third and final stage?
A) Affirming only rational attributes and interpreting reported attributes
B) Affirming all attributes without modality or likeness
C) Complete negation of attributes
D) Continuing in Mu'tazilism

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's final theological position, as recorded in al-Ibanah, is to affirm all divine attributes without modality or likeness, following the way of the salaf.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Like al-Ash'ari, you may have stages in your own understanding of Allah's attributes. Is there any attribute you find yourself trying to imagine or limit with your mind? Will you commit to affirming it without asking how, trusting that Allah's reality is beyond your comprehension?
?
Al-Ash'ari repented from his first stage. Is there a belief or practice you know is incorrect but haven't fully turned away from? What would genuine repentance look like for you today?
?
The salaf affirmed all attributes without interpretation. Which of Allah's names or attributes do you feel uncomfortable with because you don't fully understand? How can you practice submission by accepting them as they are revealed?

The Scholarly Consensus on al-Ibanah's Authorship
Enumeration of scholars and a confirming quote from Ibn Taymiyyah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that multiple renowned scholars authenticated al-Ibanah as al-Ash'ari's final work and that he returned to the way of Ahl al-Sunnah, aligning with Imam Ahmad.

Core Idea
Al-Ibanah is al-Ash'ari's last work, endorsed by major Sunni scholars.

Who really wrote al-Ibanah, and does it represent al-Ash'ari's final position? This lesson presents a chain of respected scholars who attest to its authenticity and explain its significance. We will read the list of authorities and Ibn Taymiyyah's confirming statement.

Guided Path
1
Identify the list of scholars
Read the names of the scholars who attributed al-Ibanah to al-Ash'ari. Notice the diversity of backgrounds (Shafi'i, Hanbali, Maliki).
This list is not exhaustive but includes major figures.
2
Understand the claim about the book's status
The passage states it is 'the last book he authored' and 'his followers rely on it to defend him.' Consider why this matters for debates about al-Ash'ari's legacy.
3
Analyze Ibn Taymiyyah's quote
Read the quote carefully: al-Ash'ari 'returned from the Mu'tazili school, followed the way of Ahl al-Sunnah, and affiliated with Imam Ahmad.' This is a key testimony.
Ibn Taymiyyah is a major Hanbali authority, so his support is significant.
4
Reflect on the implication
Ask yourself: How does this passage refute claims that al-Ash'ari remained Mu'tazili or that al-Ibanah was not his work?
Connects to common misconceptions.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وممن عزا ( الإبانة ( إليه : الإمام البيهقي ، والحافظ الذهبي ، وابن فرحون المالكي ، وشيخ الإسلام ابن تيمية ، وتلميذه ابن قيم الجوزية ، وابن كثير ، وابن العماد الحنبلي . وهو آخر كتاب صنفه وعليه يعتمد أصحابه في الذب عنه عند من يطعن عليه . قال شيخ الإسلام : ولما رجع الأشعري عن مذهب المعتزلة ، سلك طريق أهل السنة والحديث ، وانتسب إلى الإمام أحمد بن حنبل كما ذكر ذلك في كتبه كـ « الإبانة ) وغيرها . وقال ابن القيم : وأبو الحسن الأشعري وأئمة أصحابه متفقون على إثبات الصفات الخبرية التي ذكرت في القرآن كالاستواء ، والوجه ، واليدين ، وعلى إبطال تأويلها . وهي عقيدة السلف الصالح التي تلقوها عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم وعن أصحابه وعن التابعين لهم بإحسان ، وهي العقيدة السليمة والطريقة المستقيمة ، التي ينبغي على كل مسلم أن يسلك سبيلها ، وأن يسير على منهاجها ، وهي أسلم وأحكم ، بلا شك ولا ريب .
And among those who attributed al-Ibanah to him: Imam al-Bayhaqi, al-Hafiz al-Dhahabi, Ibn Farhun al-Maliki, Shaykh al-Islam Ibn Taymiyya, his student Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya, Ibn Kathir, and Ibn al-'Imad al-Hanbali. It is the last book he authored, and his followers rely on it to defend him against those who attack him. Shaykh al-Islam said: 'When al-Ash'ari returned from the Mu'tazili school, he followed the way of Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Hadith, and affiliated himself with Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, as he mentioned in his books such as al-Ibanah and others.' And Ibn al-Qayyim said: 'Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari and the imams of his followers are unanimous on affirming the reported attributes mentioned in the Quran such as istiwa, face, hands, and on invalidating their interpretation.' And it is the creed of the righteous predecessors which they received from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, and from his Companions and those who followed them in righteousness. It is the sound creed and the straight path which every Muslim should follow and tread upon, and it is safer and wiser, without any doubt or uncertainty.
Source pages p. 29

Commentary
This passage establishes the authentic attribution of al-Ibanah to al-Ash'ari and its status as his final work. It lists seven prominent scholars who confirmed the book's authorship: al-Bayhaqi, al-Dhahabi, Ibn Farhun, Ibn Taymiyya, Ibn Qayyim, Ibn Kathir, and Ibn al-'Imad. It then quotes Ibn Taymiyya stating that after leaving Mu'tazilism, al-Ash'ari joined the way of Ahl al-Sunnah and explicitly aligned with Ahmad ibn Hanbal, as seen in al-Ibanah. Ibn Qayyim adds that al-Ash'ari and his leading followers consistently affirmed the reported attributes (like istiwa, face, hands) without interpretation. The passage concludes by affirming that this is the true creed of the salaf, which every Muslim should adhere to as it is 'safer and wiser.'

Key Terms
الإبانة
شيخ الإسلام ابن تيمية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Al-Ibanah is not authentically from al-Ash'ari or does not represent his final position.
Correction: Multiple scholars, including Ibn Taymiyya, attest that al-Ibanah is al-Ash'ari's last work and shows his return to Ahl al-Sunnah.

Checkpoint

A) Imam al-Bayhaqi
B) Ibn Farhun al-Maliki
C) Imam al-Ghazali
D) Ibn Kathir

Key Takeaway
Al-Ibanah is authentically al-Ash'ari's final work, and Ibn Taymiyya confirms that in it al-Ash'ari returned to the way of Ahl al-Sunnah, aligning with Imam Ahmad.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read that seven major scholars unanimously confirmed the authenticity of al-Ibanah. Do you personally trust these scholars to the point that you would stake your creed on their testimony? If not, what would it take for you to submit to their authority? This is not an idle question—your salvation may depend on following the truth they preserved.
?
Ibn Taymiyya's quote makes it clear: al-Ash'ari returned from Mu'tazilism and joined Ahl al-Sunnah. Have you ever doubted the orthodoxy of al-Ash'ari because of accusations from others? This passage is a direct refutation. Will you now let go of that doubt and accept the evidence? If not, why are you resisting the truth when it is laid before you?
?
Consider: The scholars list says al-Ibanah is the last book al-Ash'ari wrote and the one his followers use to defend him. If someone attacked your beliefs today, would you be able to defend them with such clear sources, or would you be at a loss? Commit to learning one concrete argument from this lesson that you can use when challenged.

Al-Ash'ari's Final Creed: Affirming the Reported Attributes
A quote from Ibn al-Qayyim affirming al-Ash'ari's position on attributes, followed by a general endorsement.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that according to Ibn al-Qayyim, al-Ash'ari and his followers affirmed the reported attributes of Allah without interpretation, following the creed of the righteous predecessors.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's final position affirms Allah's attributes without ta'wil.

What did al-Ash'ari actually believe about Allah's attributes? This passage from Ibn al-Qayyim clarifies that al-Ash'ari and his leading followers affirmed attributes like istiwa, face, and hands without metaphorical interpretation. This is presented as the creed of the salaf, which every Muslim should follow.

Guided Path
1
Read Ibn al-Qayyim's statement
Focus on the key points: al-Ash'ari and his imams unanimously affirm the reported attributes and invalidate interpretation.
Reported attributes (الصفات الخبرية) are those mentioned in the Quran, such as istiwa, face, hands.
2
Understand the term 'ta'til' and 'ta'wil'
Ibn al-Qayyim is rejecting interpretation (ta'wil) that denies the apparent meaning. Know that this is a central difference between Ash'aris and Mu'tazilis.
The passage does not mention ta'til explicitly but the context is known.
3
Connect to the salaf
The passage says this is the creed of the righteous predecessors (salaf). Reflect on why this adds authority.
4
Consider the concluding exhortation
The passage urges every Muslim to follow this path as 'safer and wiser.' What does this imply about alternative views?
This is a direct call to adopt this creed.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

And among those who attributed al-Ibanah to him: Imam al-Bayhaqi, al-Hafiz al-Dhahabi, Ibn Farhun al-Maliki, Shaykh al-Islam Ibn Taymiyya, his student Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya, Ibn Kathir, and Ibn al-'Imad al-Hanbali. It is the last book he authored, and his followers rely on it to defend him against those who attack him. Shaykh al-Islam said: 'When al-Ash'ari returned from the Mu'tazili school, he followed the way of Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Hadith, and affiliated himself with Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, as he mentioned in his books such as al-Ibanah and others.' And it is the creed of the righteous predecessors which they received from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, and from his Companions and those who followed them in righteousness. It is the sound creed and the straight path which every Muslim should follow and tread upon, and it is safer and wiser, without any doubt or uncertainty.
Source pages p. 29

Commentary
This passage establishes the authentic attribution of al-Ibanah to al-Ash'ari and its status as his final work. It lists seven prominent scholars who confirmed the book's authorship: al-Bayhaqi, al-Dhahabi, Ibn Farhun, Ibn Taymiyya, Ibn Qayyim, Ibn Kathir, and Ibn al-'Imad. It then quotes Ibn Taymiyya stating that after leaving Mu'tazilism, al-Ash'ari joined the way of Ahl al-Sunnah and explicitly aligned with Ahmad ibn Hanbal, as seen in al-Ibanah. Ibn Qayyim adds that al-Ash'ari and his leading followers consistently affirmed the reported attributes (like istiwa, face, hands) without interpretation. The passage concludes by affirming that this is the true creed of the salaf, which every Muslim should adhere to as it is 'safer and wiser.'

Key Terms
الصفات الخبرية
السلف الصالح
تأويل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Al-Ash'ari and his followers typically interpret reported attributes metaphorically (ta'wil).
Correction: Ibn al-Qayyim states they affirm the attributes and invalidate their interpretation, aligning with the salaf.

Checkpoint
According to Ibn al-Qayyim, what is the position of al-Ash'ari and his imams on the reported attributes?
A) They affirm them and allow interpretation
B) They negate them
C) They affirm them and invalidate interpretation
D) They remain silent

Key Takeaway
Ibn al-Qayyim confirms that al-Ash'ari and his leading followers affirmed Allah's reported attributes without interpretation, following the salaf, and this is the obligatory creed for every Muslim.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of Allah having a 'hand' or 'face,' does your heart find rest in simply affirming 'as He described, without how,' or does your mind attempt to explain it away? Which path feels safer (aslam) to you right now, and what holds you back from full submission?
?
Ibn al-Qayyim says this creed is 'aslam wa ahkam' (safer and wiser). In what area of your life—your beliefs or actions—do you still trust your own cleverness over divine instruction? What would it cost you to abandon that and follow the way of the salaf?
?
If you were standing before Allah tomorrow and He asked you about these attributes, would you be confident in the answer you give? Based on this passage, what is one specific step you can take today to align your heart with the affirmation of Allah's attributes without ta'wil?

The Authority of Al-Ibanah and Ash'ari's Sunni Creed
Introduces Al-Ibanah as Ash'ari's final work and quotes scholars affirming his Sunni creed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Al-Ibanah is considered Ash'ari's final and authoritative work, and how Ash'ari's recantation of Mu'tazilism established his Sunni creed, as affirmed by later scholars.

Core Idea
Al-Ibanah is Ash'ari's final work and proof of his return to the Sunni creed.

This lesson introduces Imam Al-Ash'ari's book Al-Ibanah and its significance as his final work. Through the testimony of prominent scholars like Ibn Taymiyyah and Ibn al-Qayyim, we see that Ash'ari recanted Mu'tazilism and affirmed the Sunni position on divine attributes, aligning with the creed of the Salaf.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
2
Identify the key figures quoted
The passage quotes Ibn Kathir, Ibn al-Imad, Shaykh al-Islam (Ibn Taymiyyah), and Ibn al-Qayyim. Note what each says about Al-Ash'ari and Al-Ibanah.
3
Note what is said about Al-Ibanah's authority
The passage states that Al-Ibanah is his last book and his followers rely on it for defense. This establishes its authoritative status.
4
Understand Ash'ari's stance on attribute verses
Ibn al-Qayyim explains that Ash'ari and his followers affirm the literal meaning of attributes like istiwa', face, and hands, and reject figurative interpretation. This aligns with the creed of the Salaf.
This is a key point often misunderstood.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وابن كثير ، وابن العماد الحنبلي عليه وهو آخر كتاب صنفه وعليه يعتمد أصحابه في الذب عنه عند من يطعن. قال شيخ الإسلام: ولما رجع الأشعري عن مذهب المعتزلة، سلك طريق أهل السنة والحديث، وانتسب إلى الإمام أحمد بن حنبل كما ذكر ذلك في كتبه كـ «الإبانة» وغيرها. وقال ابن القيم: وأبو الحسن الأشعري وأئمة أصحابه متفقون على إثبات الصفات الخبرية التي ذكرت في القرآن كالاستواء، والوجه، واليدين، وعلى إبطال تأويلها وهي عقيدة السلف الصالح التي تلقوها عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم وعن أصحابه وعن التابعين لهم بإحسان، وهي العقيدة السليمة والطريقة المستقيمة، التي ينبغي على كل مسلم أن يسلك سبيلها، وأن يسير على منهاجها، وهي أسلم وأحكم، بلا شك ولا ريب.
And Ibn Kathir and Ibn al-‘Imād al-Ḥanbalī relied upon it. It is the last book he authored, and his followers rely upon it in defending him against those who attack him. Shaykh al-Islam said: When al-Ash‘arī recanted the Mu‘tazilite doctrine, he followed the way of the People of the Sunnah and the Hadith, and affiliated himself with Imam Ahmad ibn Ḥanbal, as he mentioned in his books such as al-Ibānah and others. Ibn al-Qayyim said: Abū al-Ḥasan al-Ash‘arī and the leading scholars of his followers are agreed upon affirming the attribute verses mentioned in the Qur'an, such as al-istiwa' (the settling), the Face, and the Hands, and upon rejecting their figurative interpretation. That is the creed of the pious predecessors, which they received from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and from his Companions, and from the Successors who followed them in righteousness. It is the sound creed and the straight path, which every Muslim ought to follow and walk upon. It is the safest and most judicious, without any doubt or uncertainty.
Source pages p. 30

Commentary
This passage is an editorial introduction defending Imam al-Ash'ari and his book Al-Ibanah. It begins by stating that scholars like Ibn Kathir and Ibn al-Imad relied upon Al-Ibanah, and that it is his final work, used by his followers to defend him. Then it quotes Shaykh al-Islam (Ibn Taymiyyah) affirming that al-Ash'ari recanted Mu'tazilism and adopted the way of Ahl al-Sunnah, affiliating with Imam Ahmad. Finally, it quotes Ibn al-Qayyim stating that al-Ash'ari and his leading followers affirm the literal meaning of the Quranic attributes (face, hands, istiwa') and reject their metaphorical interpretation. This is the creed of the Salaf, which is sound and must be followed. The passage uses these authoritative voices to establish the orthodoxy of al-Ash'ari and his book.

Key Terms
الإبانة
الصفات الخبرية
الاستواء
الوجه
اليدين
تأويلها
عقيدة السلف الصالح

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think al-Ash'ari remained a Mu'tazilite throughout his life.
Correction: This passage shows his clear return to Sunni creed, as stated by Ibn Taymiyyah and Ibn al-Qayyim.

Checkpoint
What does Ibn al-Qayyim say about al-Ash'ari's stance on the attribute verses in the Quran?
A) He interprets them figuratively
B) He affirms them literally and rejects figurative interpretation
C) He denies them altogether
D) He suspends judgment on them

Key Takeaway
Al-Ibanah is Ash'ari's final work, establishing his Sunni creed as affirmed by later scholars.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The passage presents the creed of the Salaf as the sound and straight path that every Muslim ought to follow. Are you willing to conform your beliefs about Allah's attributes to the literal meaning, even if your intellect struggles? What holds you back from full acceptance?
?
Ibn al-Qayyim states that al-Ash'ari and his followers rejected figurative interpretation of the attributes. Have you ever been tempted to explain away verses about Allah's Face or Hands to satisfy modern sensibilities? Where do you stand now after reading this?
?
Shaykh al-Islam says al-Ash'ari recanted his past errors and affiliated with Imam Ahmad. Is there any area of your 'aqeedah where you know you have drifted from the Salaf's way? What specific step will you take today to return?

Ibn Asakir on the Unity of Ash'ari and Hanbali Creed
Ibn Asakir praises Ash'ari's creed and notes its agreement with the Hanbalite school.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Ibn 'Asakir demonstrates the agreement between Imam al-Ash'ari and Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal on creed and the historical cooperation of the Hanbalites with the Ash'ariyya.

Core Idea
Ash'ari and Hanbali schools were united in creed, not opposed.

This lesson presents a historical testimony from the great hadith scholar Ibn 'Asakir. He praises the clarity of Ash'ari's creed and highlights its agreement with the way of Imam Ahmad. Moreover, he documents how the Hanbalites of Baghdad relied on the Ash'ariyya as their defenders against innovators. This passage challenges modern misconceptions of a permanent divide between Ash'aris and Hanbalis.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then its literal translation. Notice the tone of praise and the key claims about agreement and cooperation.
Pay attention to the Quranic verse quoted (Az-Zumar: 18).
2
Identify Ibn 'Asakir's main points
Break down the passage into its claims: praise of the creed, acknowledgment of al-Ash'ari's fairness, recognition of Ahmad's virtue, agreement in creed, and historical cooperation.
List them in order as they appear.
3
Reflect on historical significance
Ask yourself: Why is this testimony important? How does it refute claims of irreconcilable differences between Ash'aris and Hanbalis?
Consider the context of sectarian tensions.
4
Connect to your own study
Think about how this passage informs your understanding of Sunni theology. Does it change any assumptions you had?
This is a personal reflection.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بعد ذلك قال الحافظ هبة الله بن عساكر - رحمه الله تعالى -: فتأملوا رحمكم الله هذا الاعتقاد : ما أوضحه وأبينه واعترفوا بفضل هذا الإمام العالم الذي شرحه وبينه وانظروا سهولة لفظه فما أفصحه وأحسنه، وكونوا ممن قال الله فيهم : ( الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ ﴾ [الزمر : ١٨] وتبينوا فضل أبي الحسن واعرفوا إنصافه، واسمعوا وصفه لأحمد بالفضل واعترافه، لتعلموا أنهما كانا في الاعتقاد متفقين وفي أصول الدين ومذهب السنة غير مفترقين، ولم تزل الحنابلة ببغداد في قديم الدهر على ممر الأوقات تعتضد بالأشعرية على أصحاب البدع ؛ لأنهم المتكلمون من أهل الإثبات فمن تكلم منهم في الرد على مبتدع فبلسان الأشعرية يتكلم، ومن حقق منهم في الأصول في مسألة فمنهم يتعلم. اهـ. ( التبيين ) ص(١٦٣) .
After that, al-Hafiz Hibat Allah ibn 'Asakir - may Allah have mercy on him - said: So reflect, may Allah have mercy on you, upon this creed: how clear and evident it is! And acknowledge the virtue of this imam, the scholar, who explained and clarified it. And look at the ease of his wording: how eloquent and beautiful it is! And be among those of whom Allah said: 'Those who listen to speech and follow the best of it.' [Az-Zumar: 18] And recognize the virtue of Abu al-Hasan and know his fairness, and hear his description of Ahmad (ibn Hanbal) with virtue and his acknowledgment, so that you may know that they were in agreement in creed and in the fundamentals of religion and the way of the Sunnah, not differing. And the Hanbalites in Baghdad, from ancient times throughout the ages, have always supported themselves with the Ash'ariyya against the people of innovation, because they are the speakers (mutakallimun) among the people of affirmation. So whoever among them speaks in refutation of an innovator, he speaks with the tongue of the Ash'ariyya; and whoever among them researches thoroughly in the fundamentals on a matter, he learns from them. End. (Al-Tabyin) p. 163.
Source pages p. 57

Commentary
This passage is an excerpt from Ibn 'Asakir's commentary on al-Ash'ari's creed. Ibn 'Asakir urges the reader to contemplate the clarity and excellence of the creed presented by Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari. He highlights the eloquence of the wording and the fairness of the imam. Crucially, he notes that al-Ash'ari acknowledged the virtue of Ahmad ibn Hanbal, indicating that the two were in complete agreement on creed and the fundamentals of the religion. Ibn 'Asakir then provides historical evidence: the Hanbalites of Baghdad consistently relied on the Ash'ariyya—the mutakallimun from the people of affirmation—to refute innovators. In debates, they used the language of the Ash'ariyya, and in research on fundamental principles, they learned from them. This affirms the unity between the two schools against innovation.

Key Terms
الحافظ هبة الله بن عساكر
أبو الحسن الأشعري
أحمد بن حنبل
الحنابلة
الأشعرية
أهل الإثبات
التبيين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that Ash'ari and Hanbalis are always opposed and have irreconcilable differences in creed.
Correction: Ibn 'Asakir shows that al-Ash'ari acknowledged Ahmad ibn Hanbal's virtue and that they agreed in creed and fundamentals. Historically, Hanbalites relied on Ash'ariyya to refute innovators, using their arguments and learning from them.

Checkpoint
What does Ibn 'Asakir say about the relationship between the Hanbalites and the Ash'ariyya in Baghdad?
A) They were in constant conflict over creed.
B) The Hanbalites relied on the Ash'ariyya and learned from them.
C) The Ash'ariyya opposed the Hanbalites.
D) They had no interaction.

Key Takeaway
Ibn 'Asakir's testimony establishes that Imam al-Ash'ari and Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal were in full agreement on creed, and that the Hanbalites historically relied on the Ash'ariyya to refute innovators.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Ibn 'Asakir calls you to be among those who 'listen to speech and follow the best of it.' When you hear a criticism of a group like the Ash'aris or Hanbalis, do you immediately reject it or accept it based on bias, or do you honestly evaluate the evidence? What is one practical step you will take this week to be more fair and objective in your judgments about fellow Muslims?
?
The Hanbalites supported themselves with the Ash'ariyya against innovators. Are you quick to label others as innovators without understanding their actual creed, especially when their approach to theology differs from yours? How will you cultivate the same fairness and unity that Ibn 'Asakir describes?
?
This passage shows that two great imams—al-Ash'ari and Ahmad ibn Hanbal—were in full agreement on creed. Do you hold any unnecessary animosity or suspicion towards Muslims who follow a different school or theological method? What will you do today to actively remove that division from your heart?

The Consensus of Early Scholars on the Quran Being Uncreated
Imam Ashari enumerates scholars affirming the Quran is uncreated.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the vast majority of early Sunni scholars, as listed by Imam Ashari, affirmed that the Quran is uncreated and declared those who say it is created as disbelievers.

Core Idea
The early scholarly consensus declared the Quran uncreated.

Imam Ashari, in his work Al-Ibanah, provides a powerful historical testimony: a long list of early scholars—from Malik to Abu Hanifa to Ahmad ibn Hanbal—all held that the Quran is uncreated and that opposing this view is disbelief. This lesson examines that list and its significance for Sunni creed.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and list
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the sheer number of names mentioned.
The list includes founders of the major schools.
2
Identify the key claim
Imam Ashari states that those who say the Quran is created are disbelievers. This is a strong stance.
3
Consider the sufficiency
He says listing all would be lengthy, but this selection is enough. What does this tell you about the consensus?
This shows the position is well-established.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومن قال : إن القرآن غير مخلوق، وإن من قال بخلقه كافر العلماء وحملة الآثار ونقلة الأخبار لا يحصون كثرة ، منهم: : الحمادان ، والثوري، وعبد العزيز بن أبي سلمة، ومالك بن أنس، والشافعي وأصحابه، وأبو حنيفة، وأحمد بن حنبل، والليث بن سعد، وسفيان بن عيينة، وهشام، وعيسى بن يونس، وحفص بن غياث وسعد بن عامر وعبد الرحمن بن مهدي، وأبو بكر بن عياش ،ووكيع، وأبو عاصم النبيل، ويعلى بن عبيد ومحمد بن يوسف، وبشر بن المفضل، وعبد الله بن داود وسلام بن أبي مطيع، وابن المبارك، وعلـي بـن عاصم وأحمد بن يونس وأبو نعيم، وقبيصة بن عقبة، وسليمان بن داود، وأبو عبيد القاسم بن سلام ويزيد بن هارون، وغيرهم . ولو تتبعنا ذكر من يقول بذلك لطال الكلام بذكرهم، وفيما ذكرنا من مقنع، والحمد لله رب العالمين
And among those who say that the Quran is uncreated, and that whoever says it is created is a disbeliever—the scholars, carriers of traditions, and transmitters of reports are too many to count. Among them: the two Hammads, al-Thawri, Abd al-Aziz ibn Abi Salama, Malik ibn Anas, al-Shafi'i and his companions, Abu Hanifa, Ahmad ibn Hanbal, al-Layth ibn Sa'd, Sufyan ibn Uyayna, Hisham, Isa ibn Yunus, Hafs ibn Ghiyath, Sa'd ibn Amir, Abd al-Rahman ibn Mahdi, Abu Bakr ibn Ayyash, Waki', Abu Asim al-Nabil, Ya'la ibn Ubayd, Muhammad ibn Yusuf, Bishr ibn al-Mufaddal, Abd Allah ibn Dawud, Sallam ibn Abi Muti', Ibn al-Mubarak, Ali ibn Asim, Ahmad ibn Yunus, Abu Nu'aym, Qabisa ibn Uqba, Sulayman ibn Dawud, Abu Ubayd al-Qasim ibn Sallam, Yazid ibn Harun, and others. And if we were to follow mentioning those who say that, speech would be long in mentioning them, and in what we have mentioned there is sufficiency. And praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.
Source pages p. 91

Commentary
This passage is a historical and biographical enumeration. Imam Ashari lists numerous early scholars who held that the Quran is uncreated, and he considers that those who say it is created are disbelievers. The list includes prominent figures from various schools: the two Hammads (Hammad ibn Salamah and Hammad ibn Zaid), al-Thawri, Malik, al-Shafi'i, Abu Hanifa, Ahmad, etc. The purpose is to show that this is the position of the vast majority of reliable scholars. He then notes that listing all of them would be too lengthy, so he has provided enough examples. The concluding phrase 'praise be to Allah' expresses gratitude for the clarity of this position.

Key Terms
غير مخلوق
خلق القرآن

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only a few scholars held the Quran to be uncreated.
Correction: Imam Ashari lists dozens of early scholars, indicating a broad consensus.

Checkpoint

A) Malik ibn Anas
B) Abu Hanifa
C) Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari
D) Ahmad ibn Hanbal

Key Takeaway
Imam Ashari provides a powerful demonstration of the early scholarly consensus that the Quran is uncreated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

Do you feel a personal responsibility to uphold this belief in your heart and actions? What specific change will you make today to affirm the Quran's eternal status?
?
The author says that to list all who held this view would be lengthy, and what he mentions is sufficient. Reflect on your own knowledge: do you rely on the consensus of the Salaf for your beliefs, or do you sometimes follow your own opinion? Which of the scholars mentioned do you recognize, and does their authority strengthen your faith? Write down one action you will take to align your belief more closely with the early community.
?
This passage shows that early scholars considered those who say the Quran is created to be disbelievers. How does this statement challenge modern attitudes that tolerate all views? Are you willing to adopt the same conviction, knowing the gravity of this issue? Pray for steadfastness.
