The Emergence of Insincere Elements and Abdullah ibn Saba
Al-Khidr Husayn narrates the start of the fitna with the entry of insincere converts and Ibn Saba's activities.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how insincere converts and the figure of Abdullah ibn Saba contributed to the early stages of the fitna against Caliph Uthman.

Core Idea
The fitna was not spontaneous; it was ignited by covert propagandists like Ibn Saba.

Imam al-Khidr Husayn begins by painting a picture of Uthman's early popularity, then introduces the entry of insincere elements into Islam. This lesson focuses on the emergence of Abdullah ibn Saba and his secret propaganda, which sowed the seeds of discord.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice how the author describes Uthman's initial popularity before introducing the problem.
The contrast between unity and discord is key.
2
Identify Ibn Saba's role
Find the sentence introducing Abdullah ibn Saba. Note his background (Jewish) and his method: secret propaganda that Ali was the rightful caliph.
He is presented as the first instigator.
3
Trace his movements
List the cities Ibn Saba visited and note how he was expelled from each until he settled in Egypt and recruited followers.
This shows his determination and the spread of the fitna.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال الإمام الأكبر محمد الخضر حسين شيخ الأزهر تحت عنوان : كيف نشأت الفتنة؟ قضى عثمان معظم أيام خلافته والناس مجمعون على حبه والاغتباط بإمارته لشدة رأفته وواسع حلمه ولين جانبه، ولأن أيدي الناس امتلأت في عهده من المغانم، وصاروا يتمتعون بالعيش الطيب أكثر مما كانوا يتمتعون . كان بعد هذا أن دخل في الإسلام نفر غير مخلصين في إسلامهم، وإنما يريدون الكيد له، وتفريق كلمة أهله من هؤلاء عبد الله بن سبأ المعروف بابن السوداء. وهو من يهود حمير، نزل البصرة على حكيم بن جبلة العبدي، وابتدأ يبث في السر أن علياً أحق في الخلافة، ويدعو إلى نزع الخلافة من عثمان، فتعلق بدعوته نفر، وبلغ أمره عبد الله بن عامر البصرة، فطرده، وجاء الكوفة فأخرج منها ، ثم جاء الشام فأخرج منها كذلك، فأتى مصر واتصل به نفر من أوشاب كنانة الناس، منهم بن بشر وخالد بن ملجم، وأخذ يردد عليهم أن علياً وصي النبي ، وأن عثمان أخذ الخلافة بغير حق، وبعث من هؤلاء دعاة في البلاد التي كان قد طرد منها، واتفق أن كان لعثمان عمال من ذوي ،قرابته، وهناك أناس قد يلاقون من هؤلاء العمال ما لا يوافق أهواءهم، فيطعنون فيهم، ويتخذون من قرابتهم لعثمان وسيلة إلى الطعن في عثمان، فوجد فريقاً يوافقون أصحاب تلك الدعاية السرية في الطعن في عثمان نفسه، وإن كان الفريقان يختلفان في الباعث على هذا الطعن فأصحاب تلك الدعاية السرية يريدون تفريق كلمة المسلمين أو إفساد دينهم والفريق الثاني إنما يريد نقل الخلافة من عثمان إلى غيره، كراهة لبعض عماله أو رجاء أن يجد في خلافة غيره ولاية أو غنيمة وتكون من الفريقين جمع يتمضمضون بالطعن في عمال عثمان، ويتعدون بالطعن إلى عثمان نفسه، ينسبون إليه أشياء زوراً، ويذكرون بعض أعماله على غير وجهها الصحيح، وكان عثمان يقابل زعماء هذه الدعاية باللين ولا يزيد على أن يدفعهم بالحجة. كان جماعة من أهل الكوفة قد أظهروا العداء لعثمان فأبلغ سعيد بن العاص عثمان خبرهم =
The Greatest Imam Muhammad al-Khidr Husayn, Sheikh of al-Azhar, said under the title: How did the fitna arise? Uthman spent most of the days of his caliphate with the people united in love for him and delight in his governance, due to his extreme compassion, vast forbearance, and gentle disposition, and because people's hands were filled with spoils in his era, and they came to enjoy a good life more than they had enjoyed before. After this, some people entered Islam who were not sincere in their Islam, but rather wanted to plot against it and divide its people. Among them was Abdullah ibn Saba, known as Ibn al-Sawda. He was from the Jews of Himyar. He settled in Basra with Hakim ibn Jabalah al-Abdi and began secretly propagating that Ali was more entitled to the caliphate, and called for removing the caliphate from Uthman. A few people adhered to his call. Abdullah ibn Amir, the governor of Basra, heard of his affair and expelled him. He came to Kufa and was expelled from there. Then he came to Syria and was likewise expelled. So he went to Egypt and contacted some of the dregs of people, among them [?] ibn Bishr and Khalid ibn Muljam. He began repeating to them that Ali was the wasi (legatee) of the Prophet, and that Uthman had taken the caliphate without right. He sent from among these people callers to the lands from which he had been expelled. It happened that Uthman had governors from among his relatives, and there were people who would meet from these governors what did not suit their desires, so they would criticize them and use their relationship to Uthman as a means to criticize Uthman himself. So there came to be a faction that agreed with those behind the secret propaganda in criticizing Uthman himself, although the two factions differed in the motive for this criticism. Those behind the secret propaganda wanted to divide the word of the Muslims or corrupt their religion, while the second faction merely wanted to transfer the caliphate from Uthman to someone else, disliking some of his governors or hoping to find in the caliphate of another a position or booty. From the two factions there arose a group that would speak ill of Uthman's governors and then extend criticism to Uthman himself, attributing false things to him and mentioning some of his deeds in a distorted manner. Uthman would confront the leaders of this propaganda with leniency and would only respond with argument. A group from the people of Kufa had shown enmity to Uthman, so Sa'id ibn al-As informed Uthman of their news =
Source pages p. 171

Commentary
This passage is a historical narrative explaining how the fitna (sedition) against Caliph Uthman began. The author, Imam Muhammad al-Khidr Husayn, starts by describing Uthman's early reign as a time of unity and prosperity. He then identifies the root cause: insincere converts, particularly Abdullah ibn Saba, a Jew, who spread propaganda that Ali was the rightful caliph. Ibn Saba moved from city to city, gaining followers, and eventually settled in Egypt. He and his followers used the grievances of people against Uthman's governors (who were his relatives) to incite opposition to Uthman himself. Two groups emerged: one seeking to destroy Islam (the Sabaites) and another seeking political change. Both converged in spreading slander against Uthman. Uthman responded mildly, only with arguments. The narrative breaks off as it mentions the Kufan opposition being reported to Uthman.

Key Terms
الفتنة
عبد الله بن سبأ
السرية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The fitna was a natural result of Uthman's policies.
Correction: The author demonstrates that it was started by insincere converts like Ibn Saba who secretly plotted against Uthman.

Checkpoint
What was Abdullah ibn Saba's primary claim in his secret propaganda?
A) Ali was the rightful caliph.
B) Uthman was a tyrant.
C) The caliphate should be abolished.
D) The Muslims should revolt.

Key Takeaway
The fitna began with the covert activities of insincere converts, chief among them Abdullah ibn Saba, who spread propaganda to undermine Uthman's caliphate.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever hidden a belief or opinion from others for fear of rejection, while secretly wishing to influence them? What does this lesson teach you about the harm of insincerity in faith?
?
Ibn Saba used secret propaganda to divide the Muslim community. Are there any ways you might be contributing to division among Muslims—by spreading rumors, gossip, or partial truths—without realizing it? Will you commit to seeking reconciliation instead?
?
The passage says insincere converts entered Islam 'only wanting to plot against it'. Examine your own heart: when you engage with Islamic knowledge, is your intention truly to please Allah and benefit the community, or is there any trace of envy, pride, or hidden agenda you need to repent from?

Life of Imam al-Ashari
beginner
5 min
The Exploitation of Grievances and the Merging of Factions
Al-Khidr Husayn explains how grievances against governors were used to attack Uthman and how two factions converged.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the secret propaganda exploited grievances against Uthman's governors, leading to a coalition of critics and the spread of slander.

Core Idea
Real grievances were weaponized by secret propagandists to turn people against Uthman.

Building on the previous lesson, Al-Khidr Husayn now describes how Uthman's relatives as governors attracted criticism, and how the secret propagandists merged with those who had political or material motives. This lesson examines the deadly combination that led to widespread condemnation of Uthman.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on the two factions
Read the Arabic and translation focusing on the description of the two groups: the secret propagandists and those with personal grievances.
Note the difference in motives: one wants to destroy Islam, the other wants political change.
2
See how criticism escalates
Identify how criticism of Uthman's governors ('ummal) extends to Uthman himself, and how false accusations are made.
The phrase 'attributing false things to him' is key.
3
Observe Uthman's response
Note that Uthman responded with leniency and argument, not force.
This contrasts with the aggression of his opponents.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال الإمام الأكبر محمد الخضر حسين شيخ الأزهر تحت عنوان : كيف نشأت الفتنة؟ قضى عثمان معظم أيام خلافته والناس مجمعون على حبه والاغتباط بإمارته لشدة رأفته وواسع حلمه ولين جانبه، ولأن أيدي الناس امتلأت في عهده من المغانم، وصاروا يتمتعون بالعيش الطيب أكثر مما كانوا يتمتعون . كان بعد هذا أن دخل في الإسلام نفر غير مخلصين في إسلامهم، وإنما يريدون الكيد له، وتفريق كلمة أهله من هؤلاء عبد الله بن سبأ المعروف بابن السوداء. وهو من يهود حمير، نزل البصرة على حكيم بن جبلة العبدي، وابتدأ يبث في السر أن علياً أحق في الخلافة، ويدعو إلى نزع الخلافة من عثمان، فتعلق بدعوته نفر، وبلغ أمره عبد الله بن عامر البصرة، فطرده، وجاء الكوفة فأخرج منها ، ثم جاء الشام فأخرج منها كذلك، فأتى مصر واتصل به نفر من أوشاب كنانة الناس، منهم بن بشر وخالد بن ملجم، وأخذ يردد عليهم أن علياً وصي النبي ، وأن عثمان أخذ الخلافة بغير حق، وبعث من هؤلاء دعاة في البلاد التي كان قد طرد منها، واتفق أن كان لعثمان عمال من ذوي ،قرابته، وهناك أناس قد يلاقون من هؤلاء العمال ما لا يوافق أهواءهم، فيطعنون فيهم، ويتخذون من قرابتهم لعثمان وسيلة إلى الطعن في عثمان، فوجد فريقاً يوافقون أصحاب تلك الدعاية السرية في الطعن في عثمان نفسه، وإن كان الفريقان يختلفان في الباعث على هذا الطعن فأصحاب تلك الدعاية السرية يريدون تفريق كلمة المسلمين أو إفساد دينهم والفريق الثاني إنما يريد نقل الخلافة من عثمان إلى غيره، كراهة لبعض عماله أو رجاء أن يجد في خلافة غيره ولاية أو غنيمة وتكون من الفريقين جمع يتمضمضون بالطعن في عمال عثمان، ويتعدون بالطعن إلى عثمان نفسه، ينسبون إليه أشياء زوراً، ويذكرون بعض أعماله على غير وجهها الصحيح، وكان عثمان يقابل زعماء هذه الدعاية باللين ولا يزيد على أن يدفعهم بالحجة. كان جماعة من أهل الكوفة قد أظهروا العداء لعثمان فأبلغ سعيد بن العاص عثمان خبرهم =

Uthman spent most of the days of his caliphate with the people united in love for him and delight in his governance, due to his extreme compassion, vast forbearance, and gentle disposition, and because people's hands were filled with spoils in his era, and they came to enjoy a good life more than they had enjoyed before. Then he came to Syria and was likewise expelled. So he went to Egypt and contacted some of the dregs of people, among them [?] ibn Bishr and Khalid ibn Muljam. Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: All critics of Uthman were sincere reformers.
Correction: The passage shows two factions: one sought to destroy Islam, the other sought personal gain.

Checkpoint
What was the motive of the second faction in criticizing Uthman?
A) To divide the Muslims.
B) To transfer the caliphate to someone else for personal gain.
C) To restore justice.
D) To support Ali.

Key Takeaway
The fitna escalated when personal grievances against Uthman's governors were exploited by secret propagandists, leading to a united front of slander against Uthman.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear criticism of a leader, do you pause to examine the critic's motive and the truth of the claim, or do you let your own grievances and desires color your judgment? This lesson reveals how legitimate concerns were weaponized to destroy a righteous caliph. How will you protect yourself from being used in a similar way?
?
Consider the second faction: they had a legitimate dislike for some governors, but they went beyond that to slander Uthman himself, hoping for personal gain. Where in your life might you be exaggerating a flaw in someone to justify pursuing your own advantage? What step can you take today to correct that?
?
Uthman responded to severe provocation with leniency and argument, not force. If someone slanders you or uses your mistakes against you, what would it take for you to respond with similar restraint? Are you willing to entrust your reputation to Allah and avoid escalating conflict?

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
6 min
The Debate on the Three Brothers
Al-Ash'ari debates al-Jubba'i on the fate of three brothers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari used logical reasoning to expose a contradiction in the Mu'tazili doctrine of divine justice.

Core Idea
The Mu'tazili system collapses when asked why God spared the child but not the disbeliever.

This lesson presents one of the most famous debates in Islamic theological history. Al-Ash'ari, a former Mu'tazili student, challenges his teacher al-Jubba'i with a hypothetical scenario that exposes an inconsistency in the Mu'tazili understanding of God's justice. As you read the passage, pay close attention to each step of the argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Carefully read the Arabic passage and its literal English translation. Notice the conversational style of the debate and the key terms used.
The passage includes an introduction by Ibn al-Imad, the debate itself, and a concluding remark.
2
Identify the three brothers and their fates
Note the three brothers: a believer, a disbeliever, and a child. According to al-Jubba'i's initial answer, what happens to each?
The child is in a state of safety, neither rewarded nor punished.
3
Trace the logical progression of the debate
Follow al-Ash'ari's questions: first about the child wanting to join the believer, then about the child's excuse, and finally about the elder brother's counterargument. Observe how each response from al-Jubba'i leads to a contradiction.
Al-Ash'ari's final question forces al-Jubba'i into silence.
4
Reflect on why al-Jubba'i was silenced
Consider why al-Jubba'i could not answer. What is the logical inconsistency in the Mu'tazili position that al-Ash'ari reveals?
If God knows the child would have disobeyed, He could have known the same for the disbeliever and prevented his sin.
5
Consider the broader implication
Reflect on Ibn al-Imad's concluding remark: 'In this debate is evidence that Allah has singled out whom He wills for mercy and another for punishment.' How does this support the Ash'ari view of divine sovereignty?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال ابن العماد الحنبلي في ( الشذرات ( الجزء الثاني صفحة ٣٠٣ ( ومما بيض به أبو الحسن الأشعري وجوه أهل السنة النبوية وسود به رايات أهل الاعتزال والجهمية ، فأبان به وجه الحق الأبلج ، ولصدور أهل الإيمان والعرفان أثلج ، مناظرته مع شيخه الجبائي التي قصم ظهر كل مبتدع مرائي وهي أعني المناظرة كما قال ابن خلكان : « سأل أبو الحسن الأشعري أستاذه أبا علي الجبائي على ثلاثة أخوة ، كان أحدهم مؤمناً براً تقياً . والثاني كان كافراً فاسقاً شقياً ، والثالث كان صغيراً ، فماتوا فكيف حالهم ؟ فقال الجبائي : أما الزاهد ففي الدرجات ، وأما الكافر ففي الدركات ، وأما الصغير فمن أهل السلامة . فقال الأشعري : إن أراد الصغير أن يذهب إلى درجات الزاهد هل يؤذن له ؟ فقال الجبائي : لا !! لأنه يقال له : أخوك إنما وصل إلى هذه الدرجات بطاعاته الكثيرة وليس لك تلك الطاعات ، فقال الأشعري : فإن قال ذلك التقصير ليس مني ، فإنك ما أبقيتني ما أقدرتني على الطاعة ، الجبائي : يقول الباري جل وعلا : كنت أعلم لو بقيت لعصيت وصرت مستحقاً للعذاب الأليم فراعيت مصلحتك ، فقال الأشعري : فلو قال الأخ الأكبر يا إله العالمين كما علمت حاله فقد علمت حالي، فلم راعيت مصلحته دوني فانقطع الجبائي ! ! فقال ابن العماد : « وفي هذه المناظرة دلالة على أن الله تعالى خص من شاء برحمته واختص آخر بعذابه » .
And Ibn al-Imad al-Hanbali said in 'al-Shadharat' (part 2, page 303): 'Among the things by which Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari whitened the faces of the people of the Prophetic Sunnah and blackened the banners of the people of Mu'tazila and Jahmiyya, thus showing the bright face of truth and cooling the hearts of the people of faith and gnosis, was his debate with his teacher al-Jubba'i which broke the back of every deceitful innovator. And it is the debate as Ibn Khallikan said: "Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari asked his teacher Abu Ali al-Jubba'i about three brothers: one was a believing, pious, God-fearing man; the second was a disbelieving, immoral, wretched man; and the third was a child. They died. What is their state? Al-Jubba'i said: 'As for the ascetic, he is in the ranks; as for the disbeliever, he is in the depths; and as for the child, he is among the people of safety.' Al-Ash'ari said: 'If the child wants to go to the ranks of the ascetic, will he be allowed?' Al-Jubba'i said: 'No, because it will be said to him: Your brother only attained these ranks through his many acts of obedience, and you do not have those acts of obedience.' Al-Ash'ari said: 'Then he says: That shortcoming is not from me, for You did not keep me alive and did not enable me to obey.' Al-Jubba'i said: 'The Creator, Exalted and Glorious, says: I knew that if you had remained, you would have disobeyed and become deserving of painful punishment, so I protected your welfare.' Al-Ash'ari said: 'Then if the elder brother says: O God of the worlds, just as You knew his state, You knew my state, so why did You protect his welfare but not mine?' At that, al-Jubba'i was silenced!" Then Ibn al-Imad said: 'In this debate is evidence that Allah the Exalted has singled out whom He wills for mercy and singled out another for punishment.'
Source pages p. 6

Commentary
This passage presents the famous debate (manazara) between al-Ash'ari and his Mu'tazili teacher al-Jubba'i. Ibn al-Imad introduces it as a decisive refutation of the Mu'tazila and Jahmiyya. The debate revolves around the issue of divine justice and predestination. Al-Ash'ari poses a hypothetical scenario of three brothers: one believer who dies in obedience, one disbeliever who dies in sin, and a child who dies before reaching puberty. Al-Jubba'i, according to Mu'tazili theology, assigns the believer to Paradise, the disbeliever to Hell, and the child to a state of safety (neither rewarded nor punished). Al-Ash'ari then asks whether the child could request to join the believer in Paradise. Al-Jubba'i answers no, because the child did not earn those rewards. Al-Ash'ari points out that the child could argue that the inability to obey was due to God not granting him life and ability. Al-Jubba'i responds that God knew the child would have disobeyed if he had lived, so He protected him. Al-Ash'ari then asks why God did not similarly protect the disbeliever, who also would have disobeyed? This leads to a contradiction in the Mu'tazili position, forcing al-Jubba'i into silence. The lesson concludes with Ibn al-Imad's comment that this debate demonstrates God's absolute sovereignty and mercy, not constrained by human notions of justice.

Key Terms
مناظرة
الجبائي
ثلاثة أخوة
طفل
عدل
قدر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Mu'tazilism is fully logically consistent.
Correction: This debate shows that the Mu'tazili position leads to a contradiction: if God protects the child because He knows he would sin, He should also protect the disbeliever, but He does not.

Checkpoint
According to al-Jubba'i, why could the child not join the believer's rank in Paradise?
A) The child did not perform good deeds
B) The child was too young
C) God did not create the child for heaven
D) The child was sinful

Key Takeaway
This debate demonstrates that the Mu'tazili emphasis on human justice leads to logical contradictions that only the Ash'ari view of divine sovereignty can resolve.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were the disbeliever brother, would you question God's wisdom in not protecting you as He protected the child? Do you trust that God's judgment is always just, even when you cannot understand His reasons? What would it take for you to submit completely to His decree?
?
The child's salvation was based not on his deeds but on God's knowledge of what he would have done. Are you relying on your deeds or God's mercy? If your salvation depended on your own efforts, would you be confident? How does this realization change how you approach your relationship with Allah today?
?
In your hardest moments, when life feels unfair, do you ever feel like the elder brother, wondering why God didn't give you an easier path? This debate shows that God's ways are beyond human logic. Can you honestly say, 'I trust You even when I do not understand'? What is one specific area where you need to surrender your 'why' to His will?

Module 3 · Lesson 2

Divine Transcendence
beginner
4 min
God's Majesty in Creation
He describes God's infinite knowledge and power

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the natural world and all beings testify to God's sole power and oneness.

Core Idea
Creation itself declares God's absolute dominion and transcendence.

Imam Ash'ari uses vivid imagery and a cascade of natural signs to illustrate God's infinite knowledge and authority. This passage invites us to see the universe as a living proof of divine power. As you read, notice how each element—from the heavens to the seas—obeys His command, while doubters are left bewildered.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic once without referring to the translation. Let the rhythm and imagery settle in your mind.
Pay attention to the repeated pattern of verbs indicating establishment and movement.
2
Identify the opening claim
The passage begins with those who doubt. What happens to them? Then note how all of creation is brought into order.
The word 'mumtarun' refers to those who hesitate or doubt God's power.
3
List the signs of creation
Find each created thing mentioned: heavens, earth, mountains, winds, clouds, and seas. Reflect on how each is described as being established or moving by God's word.
These signs are not random; they form a coherent picture of cosmic order.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
The passage ends with the submission of all beings—mighty and humble alike. Ask yourself: what does this teach about God's oneness?
The phrase 'willingly and unwillingly' shows that submission is inevitable.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وانقطع دون الرسوخ في علمه الممترون، وذلت له الرقاب، وحارت في ملكوته فطن ذوي الألباب، وقامت بكلمته السماوات السبع، واستقرت الأرض المهاد، وثبتت الجبال الرواسي، وجرت الرياح اللواقع، وسار في جو السماء السحاب، وقامت على حدودها البحار، وهو الله الواحد القهار. يخضع له المتعززون، ويخشع له المترفعون، ويدين طوعا وكرها له العالمون.
And the doubters are cut off from reaching the depth of His knowledge, and necks are humbled before Him, and the intellects of those of understanding are bewildered in His dominion, and by His word the seven heavens are established, and the earth as a bed is settled, and the firm mountains are fixed, and the fertilizing winds blow, and clouds move in the sky, and the seas stand at their boundaries, and He is Allah, the One, the Subduer. The mighty submit to Him, the exalted humble themselves before Him, and the worlds obey Him willingly and unwillingly.
Source pages p. 34

Commentary
This passage is a description of God's majesty and power. The author lists signs of God's creation to illustrate His infinite knowledge and authority. He begins by stating that those who doubt cannot fathom His knowledge. Then he enumerates the heavenly bodies, earth, mountains, winds, clouds, and seas as subject to His command. He concludes by affirming that all beings, even the proud, submit to Him. The style is poetic and exhortative.

Key Terms
الرسوخ
المملكوت
القهر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: This passage implies that God is present within creation (pantheism) because He controls it directly.
Correction: The passage actually emphasizes God's transcendence: He establishes and commands creation from above. The phrase 'by His word' indicates His command is separate from the created order.

Key Takeaway
Every part of creation—from the heavens to the seas—bears witness to God's absolute power and oneness, and all beings ultimately submit to Him.

?

?
You read that the learned are bewildered in His dominion. Do you approach your knowledge of God with humility, or do you secretly think you have Him figured out? What is one mystery about God that you can admit you do not understand, and how can that admission deepen your worship?

The Beatific Vision of Allah in the Hereafter
Al-Ash'ari affirms the beatific vision for believers, veiling for disbelievers, and its impossibility in this world.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that believers will see Allah in Paradise with their eyes, that disbelievers are veiled from Him, and that no one can see Allah in this world.

Core Idea
Allah will be seen by believers in Paradise, but not in this life.

This passage from al-Ash'ari's classic creed, al-Ibānah, states the Sunni position on the vision of Allah in the afterlife. The author grounds this belief in both prophetic tradition and the Qur'an, while also clarifying that disbelievers are veiled from their Lord and that even Prophet Musa could not see Allah in this world. Let's unpack this foundational creedal statement.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation. Let the flow of the creedal assertion settle in your mind.
Notice the use of the moon analogy—a common sunni trope.
2
Identify the three sub-points
The passage makes three distinct theological claims: (1) believers will see Allah in the afterlife like seeing the full moon, (2) disbelievers are veiled from Him, (3) no one can see Allah in this world, as shown by Musa's request. Find each one in the text.
3
Reflect on the proof texts
The author quotes Qur'an 83:15 for the veiling of disbelievers and alludes to the story of Musa and the mountain (Qur'an 7:143) to show that vision in this world is impossible. Ask yourself: why does each proof support the claim?
These scriptural references are the backbone of the argument.
4
Address common misunderstandings
Some think that seeing Allah implies a physical form or that it happens in this life. The passage explicitly denies the latter and the Ash'ari school affirms the former is beyond modality (without 'how'). Check the 'common_misunderstanding' field for guidance.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وندين بأن الله تعالى يُرى في الآخرة بالأبصار، كما يُرى القمر ليلة البدر يراه المؤمنون كما جاءت الروايات عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم ونقول: إن الكافرين محجوبون عنه إذا رآه المؤمنون في الجنة، كما قال الله عز وجل: ﴿ كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَذٍ مَحْجُوبُونَ ﴾ [المطففين: ١٥]، وأن عليه الصلاة والسلام سأل الله عز وجل الرؤية في الدنيا، وأن الله سبحانه تجلى للجبل فجعله دكا، فأعلم بذلك موسى أنه لا يراه في الدنيا.
And we hold as religion that Allah, exalted, is seen in the afterlife with eyes, as the moon is seen on the night of the full moon; the believers see Him as has come in the narrations from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him. And we say: Indeed the disbelievers are veiled from Him when the believers see Him in Paradise, as Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'No, indeed they are veiled from their Lord that Day.' [Al-Mutaffifin: 15], and that upon him peace and blessings be upon him asked Allah, Mighty and Majestic, for the vision in the world, and Allah, glorified, manifested Himself to the mountain and made it crumble, and thus He informed Musa that he would not see Him in the world.
Source pages p. 47

Commentary
This passage is a creedal statement on the beatific vision (ru'ya). The author affirms that Allah will be seen in the afterlife by sight (bi'l-absar), comparing it to seeing the full moon. He grounds this in prophetic traditions. Then he introduces two corollaries: (1) The disbelievers will be veiled from seeing Allah, quoting Quran 83:15. (2) Prophet Musa (peace upon him) asked to see Allah in this world, but Allah manifested to the mountain, causing it to crumble, thereby showing Musa that vision in this world is impossible. The passage thereby affirms the Sunni doctrine that Allah will be seen in Paradise by believers, but not in this life.

Key Terms
رؤية
آخرة
أبصار
حجاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that seeing Allah implies He has a physical form or that He can be seen in this life.
Correction: The Ash'ari school affirms a real vision without modality (bilā kayf), and this passage clearly states that no one sees Allah in this world, as shown by Musa's experience.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what does the story of Musa and the mountain demonstrate?
A) That Musa saw Allah
B) That Allah cannot be seen at all
C) That Allah cannot be seen in this world
D) That the mountain was stronger than Musa

Key Takeaway
Allah will be seen by believers in Paradise in a real yet modality-free manner, while disbelievers are veiled from Him and no one sees Him in this world.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read that the believers will see Allah clearly like the full moon in Paradise. Do you truly believe that? If you do, why does the thought of that vision not dominate your daily decisions? What would change if you woke up every morning with that certainty?
?
The disbelievers are veiled from Allah on that Day. If you were to die right now, would you be among the veiled? What in your life right now might be a veil that keeps you from Allah—not just in the Hereafter, but even today? Commit to removing one such veil this week.
?
Prophet Musa, a mighty messenger, asked to see Allah in this world and was denied. Have you ever clung to a demand from Allah that He has not promised? How does knowing that even Musa was shown the impossibility of seeing Him in this world humble your expectations? Will you submit to Allah’s timing and promise, or will you insist on seeing His signs now?

The Vision of Allah for Believers in Paradise
Al-Ash'ari affirms ru'yat Allah for believers and veiling for disbelievers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari doctrine that believers will see Allah in Paradise while disbelievers are veiled, and the scriptural proofs for this.

Core Idea
Believers will see Allah in Paradise; disbelievers are veiled.

This passage from al-Ash'ari's al-Ibanah presents a central creedal point about the vision of Allah (ru'yat Allah) in the Hereafter. He affirms that believers will see their Lord in Paradise, while disbelievers are veiled from seeing Him. This lesson examines the two proofs al-Ash'ari offers: a Quranic verse and the story of Prophet Musa.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its literal English translation. Let the author's words speak for themselves before diving into analysis.
2
Identify the two proofs
Look for the two proofs al-Ash'ari provides: the Quranic verse from al-Mutaffifin and the story of Musa asking to see Allah. Notice how each proof supports the doctrine.
The first proof shows disbelievers are veiled, the second shows that vision is impossible in this world.
3
Understand the distinction between believers and disbelievers
Al-Ash'ari explicitly states that believers see Allah in Paradise while disbelievers are veiled. Consider the theological significance of this distinction.
This is a point of difference from some sects who deny vision entirely.
4
Reflect on why vision is limited to the Hereafter
The story of Musa teaches that no one can see Allah in this life. Reflect on the wisdom of reserving this vision for the next world.
5
Connect to broader Ash'ari theology
Affirming ru'yat Allah is a core Ash'ari belief. Consider how this fits with other doctrines like divine transcendence (tanzih).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونقول : إن الكافرين محجوبون عنه إذا رآه المؤمنون في الجنة، كما قال الله عزّ وجل: كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَذٍ مَحْجُوبُونَ [المطففين: ١٥]، وأن عليه الصلاة والسلام سأل الله عز وجل الرؤية في الدنيا، وأن الله سبحانه تجلى للجبل فجعله دكا، فأعلم بذلك موسى أنه لا يراه في الدنيا.
And we say: Indeed the disbelievers are veiled from Him when the believers see Him in Paradise, as Allah Almighty said: 'No! Indeed, from their Lord, that Day, they will be veiled' [Surah al-Mutaffifin: 15]; and that the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, asked Allah Almighty for the vision in the world, and that Allah subhanahu manifested Himself to the mountain and made it level, so He informed Musa thereby that he would not see Him in the world.
Source pages p. 48

Commentary
This passage affirms the doctrine of vision (ru'yat) of Allah for believers in the Hereafter, while disbelievers are veiled from seeing Him. Al-Ash'ari provides two proofs: the Quranic verse stating that disbelievers are veiled on that Day, and the story of Musa (Moses) who asked to see Allah in this world but was shown the mountain crumbling, teaching that no one can see Allah in the earthly life. Thus, vision is reserved for believers in Paradise.

Key Terms
الرؤية
المؤمنون
الكافرون
محجوبون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that Allah is absolutely invisible and cannot be seen by anyone at any time.
Correction: This passage clarifies that vision is possible for believers in Paradise, while disbelievers are veiled. Vision is not impossible, but it is reserved for the Hereafter.

Checkpoint

A) Only prophets
B) All believers
C) Only the righteous
D) Both believers and disbelievers

Key Takeaway
Allah will be seen by believers in Paradise, while disbelievers will be veiled from Him.

?

Refuting the Claim of Parallelism Between Two Verses
Imam Ashari refutes an objection about conjunction.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses dialectical questioning to refute an opponent's claim that grammatical conjunction forces identical scope between two verses.

Core Idea
Conjunction does not force identical meaning; context determines scope.

In this passage, Imam Ashari confronts an opponent who argued that because two verses are grammatically conjoined, they must have the same scope of generality. By getting the opponent to admit that the first verse negates all physical perception, and that the second does not imply physical perception, Ashari shows the argument's flaw. This lesson examines his logical technique.

Guided Path
1
Understand the opponent's claim
The opponent says the two verses are parallel because they are conjoined (عطف). What does that imply?
The opponent likely thought both verses refer to physical perception.
2
Imam Ashari's first question
He asks whether the eyes perceive Allah by any means. The opponent admits no. This establishes that 'La tudrikuhu' negates all perception, not just sight.
3
Second question and conclusion
He asks whether Allah perceives sights by physical means. The opponent says no. This shows that 'yudriku' does not mean physical perception. Thus, the conjunction doesn't force identical meaning.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٨٦- جواب : ويقال لهم : إذا كان قول الله عز وجل: لَا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ في العموم كقوله: ﴿وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الأَبْصَرَ ؛ لأن أحد الكلامين معطوف على الآخر، فخبرونا أليس الأبصار والعيون لا تدركه رؤية ولا لمساً ولا ذوقاً ولا على وجه من الوجوه؟ فمن قولهم : نعم، فيقال لهم : أخبرونا عن قوله عز وجل: ﴿وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الأَبْصَرَ أتزعمون أنه يدركها لمساً وذوقاً بأن يلمسها؟ فمن قولهم : لا ، فيقال لهم : فقد انتقض قولكم : إن قوله : ( وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصَرَ في العموم كقوله : ( لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ)
86- Response: And it is said to them: If the saying of Allah Almighty 'Sight does not perceive Him' is in generality like His saying 'And He perceives sights,' because one of the two statements is conjoined to the other, then inform us: do the sights and eyes not perceive Him by sight, nor by touch, nor by taste, nor in any manner? So from their statement: Yes. Then it is said to them: Inform us about the saying of Allah Almighty 'And He perceives sights'—do you claim that He perceives them by touch and taste, such that He touches them? So from their statement: No. Then it is said to them: Then your statement is refuted: that His saying 'And He perceives sights' is in generality like His saying 'Sight does not perceive Him'.
Source pages p. 70

Commentary
This passage is a response (جواب) to an opponent's objection. The opponent had claimed that the two verses 'Sight does not perceive Him' and 'And He perceives sights' are parallel in generality because they are conjoined (عطف). Imam Ashari uses their own logic against them. He first gets them to admit that sights and eyes do not perceive Allah by any means—not by seeing, touching, tasting, etc. Then he asks whether they claim that Allah perceives sights by touching or tasting. They say no. Thus, the parallel they claimed breaks down: if one verse negates all perception and the other affirms only knowledge (not physical perception), then the opponent's argument is invalid.

Key Terms
إدراك
بصر
رؤية
عطف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The opponent believed that because the two verses are conjoined (عطف), they must have the same scope of generality, meaning that if one negates perception, the other also negates it or that both refer to physical perception.
Correction: Conjunction does not require identical meanings; each verse retains its own signification. 'La tudrikuhu' negates all physical perception, while 'Wa huwa yudriku' affirms a non-physical perception (knowledge).

Checkpoint
What does the opponent admit regarding the eyes perceiving Allah?
A) They perceive Him by sight only.
B) They perceive Him by touch and taste.
C) They do not perceive Him by any means.
D) They perceive Him by knowledge only.

Key Takeaway
Imam Ashari refutes the opponent's claim of parallelism by showing that the first verse negates all physical perception while the second does not imply physical perception, thus breaking the alleged symmetry.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read verses like 'La tudrikuhu al-absar' and 'Wa huwa yudriku al-absar', do you ever subconsciously imagine Allah having eyes or hands? This passage exposes how even a subtle assumption about grammar can lead to anthropomorphism. What specific image or thought about Allah do you need to repent of and replace with pure tanzih?
?
The opponent in this passage was a learned person who made a logical error. When someone corrects your understanding of Allah's attributes, do you humbly accept or stubbornly defend your old view? Think of a recent conversation where you might have been defensive. How will you change your response next time?
?
Imam Ashari used logical reasoning to defend divine transcendence. Are you equipped to give a similar rational defense of your beliefs? Identify one logical fallacy you often encounter in discussions about Allah's attributes and commit to learning how to refute it this week.

Divine Transcendence

The Conjunction Argument: Refuting 'Yudrik' as Mere Knowledge
Imam Ashari refutes the interpretation of 'yudrik' as knowledge using conjunction.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses the principle of conjunction (عطف) to refute the opponent's interpretation of 'yudrik' as mere knowledge.

Core Idea
The conjunction between the two verses forces a contradiction in the opponent's logic.

Imam Ashari begins his response by examining the opponent's claim that 'yudrik' means 'knows'. By using the Arabic rhetorical device of conjunction (عطف), he shows that if one verse means 'knows', the other would mean 'does not know', which is not about vision at all. This passage demonstrates a classic kalam refutation by internal critique.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice how the argument progresses from the opponent's claim to the refutation.
Pay attention to the word 'عطف' (conjunction).
2
Identify the two interpretations
The opponent says 'yudrik' means 'knows'. Imam Ashari points out two possible consequences: either the other verse means 'does not know' (negation of knowledge) or it means a kind of seeing. Identify both paths.
3
Analyze the conjunction
Understand how the principle of conjunction (عطف) links the two verses. If one is interpreted a certain way, the other must follow logically.
This is a common kalam technique: using the opponent's own logic against them.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٨٨- جواب : ويقال لهم : حدثونا عن قول الله عز وجل: (وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الأَبْصَرَ) ما فإن قالوا : معنى يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصَرَ أنه يعلمها . قيل لهم : وإذا كان أحد الكلامين معطوفاً على الآخر، وكان قوله عز وجل : ( وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الأَبْصَرَ ) معناه يعلمها، فقد وجب أن يكون قوله تعالى: لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ ) لا تعلمه، وهذا نفي للعلم لا لرؤية الأبصار فإن قالوا : معنى قوله تعالى: ﴿وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصَرَ معناه يعلمها، فقد وجب أن يكون قوله: ﴿ لَا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ أنه يراها رؤية ليس معناها العلم. قيل لهم : فالأبصار التي في العيون يجوز أن ترى ؟ فإن قالوا نعم نقضوا قولهم إنا لا نرى بالبصر إلا من جنس ما نرى الساعة، فإن جاز أن يرى الله ما ليس من جنس المرئيات وهو الإبصار التي في العين، فلم لا يجوز أن يرى نفسه، وإن لم يكن من جنس المرئيات؟ ولم لا يجوز أن يرينا نفسه وإن لم يكن من جنس المرئيات ؟ ويقال لهم : حدثونا إذا رأينا شيئاً فيبصرنا نراه، أو إنما يراه الرائي دون البصر ؟ فمن قولهم: : إنه محال أن يرى البصر الذي في العين. فيقال لهم: الآية تنفي أن تراه الأبصار، ولا تنفي أن يراه المبصرون، وإنما قال الله عز وجل: لَا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ فهذا لا يدل على أن المبصرين لا يرونه على ظاهر الآية .
88- Response: And it is said to them: Inform us about the saying of Allah Almighty 'And He perceives sights'—what [is its meaning]? If they say: The meaning of 'He perceives sights' is that He knows them. It is said to them: If one of the two speech-acts is conjoined to the other, and the saying of Allah Almighty 'And He perceives sights' means He knows them, then it would necessitate that His saying 'Sight does not perceive Him' [means] does not know Him, and this is a negation of knowledge, not of the vision of sights. If they say: The meaning of His saying 'And He perceives sights' means He knows them, then it would necessitate that His saying 'Sight does not perceive Him' [means] He sees them with a vision whose meaning is not knowledge. It is said to them: Can the sights that are in the eyes see? If they say yes, they contradict their statement that we do not see with sight except what is of the same kind as what we see now. For if it is permissible that Allah sees what is not of the kind of visible things—namely the sights that are in the eye—then why is it not permissible that He sees Himself, even if He is not of the kind of visible things? And why is it not permissible that He shows us Himself, even if He is not of the kind of visible things? And it is said to them: Inform us: when we see something, is it our sight that sees it, or rather the seer who sees it without the sight? So from their statement: It is impossible that the sight which is in the eye sees. Then it is said to them: The verse negates that the sights (absar) see Him, but it does not negate that the seers (mubsirun) see Him. Allah Almighty only said 'Sights do not perceive Him' – this does not indicate that the seers do not see Him, according to the apparent meaning of the verse.
Source pages p. 70

Commentary
This passage is a response (جواب) to the opponent's claim that the verse 'Sight does not perceive Him' (La tudrikuhu al-absar) proves that Allah cannot be seen in the afterlife. Imam Ashari first addresses the opponent's interpretation of the related verse 'And He perceives sights' (wa huwa yudriku al-absar). The opponent had said 'yudriku' means 'knows' (ya'lamu). Using their own logic of conjunction (عطف), Imam Ashari points out that if one verse means 'knows', then the other would mean 'does not know' – which is not about vision. Alternatively, if they try to avoid that, they would have to say 'yudriku' means a kind of seeing that is not knowledge, which contradicts their original claim. Then he turns to a different angle: can the eyes (absar) themselves see? The opponent had argued that we only see things similar in kind to what we see now. Imam Ashari argues that if Allah can miraculously make the eyes see something unlike normal visible things (i.e., Himself), then why can't He be seen? Finally, he makes a key distinction: the verse says 'sights do not perceive Him' (la tudrikuhu al-absar), not that 'the seers do not see Him' (la yarahu al-mubsirun). Sight (basar) is the instrument; the verse negates perception by the instrument, but does not negate that the person (the seer) may see Allah directly. This paves the way for the Ash'ari position that the believers will see Allah in the afterlife with their eyes, even though the eyes cannot fully encompass or perceive Him in a comprehensive sense (idrak).

Key Terms
إدراك
علم
عطف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that 'yudrik' in the verse can only mean 'knows' without considering the conjunction and context.
Correction: Imam Ashari shows that the conjunction forces either a contradiction or an admission of a type of seeing.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what does the principle of conjunction (عطف) imply about the interpretation of 'yudrik'?
A) It proves that 'yudrik' means 'knows' without exception.
B) It forces either a contradiction or an admission of a type of seeing.
C) It shows that the verse is not about vision at all.
D) It confirms that Allah does not see anything.

Key Takeaway
The conjunction between the two verses reveals that interpreting 'yudrik' as mere knowledge leads to a logical contradiction or forces an admission of vision.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read the Quran, do you consciously consider the grammatical structure (like conjunctions) of the verses, or do you quickly settle on a meaning that fits your existing beliefs? How might this habit blind you to deeper truths?
?
Imam Ashari's argument forces a choice: either accept the logical implications of Allah's words or twist them. Where in your own understanding of Islam are you avoiding the logical consequences of clear texts?
?
If Allah's perception (idrāk) is direct and personal—He sees you—does your behaviour reflect that you are truly seen, or merely known? What would change if you lived with the certainty that every action is witnessed by His sight, not just His knowledge?

Distinguishing 'Absar' from 'Mubsirun': The Vision of Allah
Imam Ashari distinguishes between the instrument (absar) and the seer (mubsirun) in the vision verse.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the critical distinction between the instrument of sight (absar) and the seer (mubsirun) in the verse 'La tudrikuhu al-absar'.

Core Idea
The verse negates perception by the instrument, not the act of seeing by the believer.

Having dispensed with the conjunction argument, Imam Ashari now turns to the opponent's claim that we can only see things of the same kind. He then makes a crucial distinction: the verse says 'sights do not perceive Him', not 'the seers do not see Him'. This opens the door for the Ash'ari doctrine that believers will see Allah in the afterlife.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the series of questions Imam Ashari poses to the opponent.
The argument builds towards the final distinction.
2
Understand the opponent's stance
The opponent believes that we can only see things of the same kind as what we see now. Imam Ashari challenges this with the question: can the eyes themselves see?
3
Grasp the key distinction
The verse says 'La tudrikuhu al-absar' (sights do not perceive Him), not 'La yarahu al-mubsirun' (the seers do not see Him). This distinction is crucial for the Ash'ari position on the vision of Allah.
Absar = instruments; Mubsirun = persons who see.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٨٨- جواب : ويقال لهم : حدثونا عن قول الله عز وجل: (وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الأَبْصَرَ) ما فإن قالوا : معنى يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصَرَ أنه يعلمها . قيل لهم : وإذا كان أحد الكلامين معطوفاً على الآخر، وكان قوله عز وجل : ( وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الأَبْصَرَ ) معناه يعلمها، فقد وجب أن يكون قوله تعالى: لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ ) لا تعلمه، وهذا نفي للعلم لا لرؤية الأبصار فإن قالوا : معنى قوله تعالى: ﴿وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصَرَ معناه يعلمها، فقد وجب أن يكون قوله: ﴿ لَا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ أنه يراها رؤية ليس معناها العلم. قيل لهم : فالأبصار التي في العيون يجوز أن ترى ؟ فإن قالوا نعم نقضوا قولهم إنا لا نرى بالبصر إلا من جنس ما نرى الساعة، فإن جاز أن يرى الله ما ليس من جنس المرئيات وهو الإبصار التي في العين، فلم لا يجوز أن يرى نفسه، وإن لم يكن من جنس المرئيات؟ ولم لا يجوز أن يرينا نفسه وإن لم يكن من جنس المرئيات ؟ ويقال لهم : حدثونا إذا رأينا شيئاً فيبصرنا نراه، أو إنما يراه الرائي دون البصر ؟ فمن قولهم: : إنه محال أن يرى البصر الذي في العين. فيقال لهم: الآية تنفي أن تراه الأبصار، ولا تنفي أن يراه المبصرون، وإنما قال الله عز وجل: لَا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ فهذا لا يدل على أن المبصرين لا يرونه على ظاهر الآية .
88- Response: And it is said to them: Inform us about the saying of Allah Almighty 'And He perceives sights'—what [is its meaning]? If they say: The meaning of 'He perceives sights' is that He knows them. It is said to them: If one of the two speech-acts is conjoined to the other, and the saying of Allah Almighty 'And He perceives sights' means He knows them, then it would necessitate that His saying 'Sight does not perceive Him' [means] does not know Him, and this is a negation of knowledge, not of the vision of sights. If they say: The meaning of His saying 'And He perceives sights' means He knows them, then it would necessitate that His saying 'Sight does not perceive Him' [means] He sees them with a vision whose meaning is not knowledge. It is said to them: Can the sights that are in the eyes see? If they say yes, they contradict their statement that we do not see with sight except what is of the same kind as what we see now. For if it is permissible that Allah sees what is not of the kind of visible things—namely the sights that are in the eye—then why is it not permissible that He sees Himself, even if He is not of the kind of visible things? And why is it not permissible that He shows us Himself, even if He is not of the kind of visible things? And it is said to them: Inform us: when we see something, is it our sight that sees it, or rather the seer who sees it without the sight? So from their statement: It is impossible that the sight which is in the eye sees. Then it is said to them: The verse negates that the sights (absar) see Him, but it does not negate that the seers (mubsirun) see Him. Allah Almighty only said 'Sights do not perceive Him' – this does not indicate that the seers do not see Him, according to the apparent meaning of the verse.
Source pages p. 70

Commentary
This passage is a response (جواب) to the opponent's claim that the verse 'Sight does not perceive Him' (La tudrikuhu al-absar) proves that Allah cannot be seen in the afterlife. Imam Ashari first addresses the opponent's interpretation of the related verse 'And He perceives sights' (wa huwa yudriku al-absar). The opponent had said 'yudriku' means 'knows' (ya'lamu). Using their own logic of conjunction (عطف), Imam Ashari points out that if one verse means 'knows', then the other would mean 'does not know' – which is not about vision. Alternatively, if they try to avoid that, they would have to say 'yudriku' means a kind of seeing that is not knowledge, which contradicts their original claim. Then he turns to a different angle: can the eyes (absar) themselves see? The opponent had argued that we only see things similar in kind to what we see now. Imam Ashari argues that if Allah can miraculously make the eyes see something unlike normal visible things (i.e., Himself), then why can't He be seen? Finally, he makes a key distinction: the verse says 'sights do not perceive Him' (la tudrikuhu al-absar), not that 'the seers do not see Him' (la yarahu al-mubsirun). Sight (basar) is the instrument; the verse negates perception by the instrument, but does not negate that the person (the seer) may see Allah directly. This paves the way for the Ash'ari position that the believers will see Allah in the afterlife with their eyes, even though the eyes cannot fully encompass or perceive Him in a comprehensive sense (idrak).

Key Terms
بصر
رؤية
مبصرون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Many think 'la tudrikuhu al-absar' negates all vision of Allah.
Correction: It only negates perception by the instruments of sight, not the vision of the believers.

Checkpoint
According to Imam Ashari, what does the verse 'La tudrikuhu al-absar' negate?
A) That the seers (mubsirun) see Allah.
B) That the eyes (absar) perceive Allah.
C) That Allah sees anything.
D) That knowledge of Allah is possible.

Key Takeaway
The verse 'La tudrikuhu al-absar' negates perception by the instruments of sight, not the vision of the believers, allowing for the possibility of seeing Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite 'La tudrikuhu al-absar', do you subconsciously assume that Allah can never be seen, or do you pause to consider the precise wording? Commit today to reading every verse about Allah's attributes with the same care as Imam Ashari, not letting your assumptions override the text.
?
If the verse only negates perception by the physical eyes, what does that imply about your heart's deepest desire to see Allah? Are you willing to hope for that vision in the afterlife, even if your mind cannot fully grasp how? Let that hope transform your worship today.
?
Imam Ashari draws a line between the instrument and the seer. In your life, do you let your physical senses (what you see and feel) dictate what you believe about Allah, or do you allow the inner seer—your soul—to perceive beyond material limits? Which one guides your trust in Allah's promises?

The Meaning of 'Yudriku' in the Vision Verses
Imam Ashari argues that 'yudriku' means 'knows', negating knowledge not vision.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses the linguistic principle of conjunctive parallelism to argue that the verse 'Eyes perceive Him not' negates knowledge, not vision, thereby defending the possibility of seeing Allah in the afterlife.

Core Idea
The verse negates sensory perception by eyes, not the beatific vision of believers.

This passage presents a classic Ash'ari argument about the interpretation of Quranic verses on divine vision. Imam al-Ash'ari engages with opponents who claim that 'yudriku' means 'sees', which would imply that Allah cannot be seen. Using a linguistic principle of parallelism, he shows that the word actually means 'knows', and therefore the negation is of knowledge, not vision. He then refutes objections and clarifies that the verse only negates perception by physical eyes, not vision by the believers themselves.

Guided Path
1
Understand the linguistic principle
Notice that the two statements are conjoined (معطوف). Al-Ash'ari relies on the rule that when two similar phrases are joined, they share the same meaning for the key verb.
2
Identify the opponents' interpretation
The opponents claim that in 'Wa huwa yudriku al-absar', the verb 'yudriku' means 'knows them' (يعلمها). This is a concession they make.
Al-Ash'ari uses this concession to build his argument.
3
Apply the principle to the negation verse
If the conjoined verse has 'yudriku' meaning 'knows', then the parallel negation 'la tudrikuhu al-absar' must also mean 'they do not know Him', not 'they do not see Him'.
This negates knowledge, not vision.
4
Consider the opponents' possible objection
They might say that in the first verse 'yudriku' means 'sees' (يرى) not 'knows'. Al-Ash'ari then refutes this by questioning whether physical eyes can see at all, leading to a deeper discussion.
5
Distinguish between eyes and seers
Al-Ash'ari concludes that the verse only negates perception by the eyes (الأبصار), not the seers (المبصرون). Therefore, the believers can see Allah in the afterlife.
This is the final conclusion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قيل لهم : وإذا كان أحد الكلامين معطوفاً على الآخر، وكان قوله عز وجل : ( وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الأَبْصَرَ ) معناه يعلمها، فقد وجب أن يكون قوله تعالى: لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ ) لا تعلمه، وهذا نفي للعلم لا لرؤية الأبصار. فإن قالوا : معنى قوله تعالى: ﴿وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصَرَ معناه يعلمها، فقد وجب أن يكون قوله: ﴿ لَا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ أنه يراها رؤية ليس معناها العلم. قيل لهم : فالأبصار التي في العيون يجوز أن ترى ؟ فإن قالوا نعم نقضوا قولهم إنا لا نرى بالبصر إلا من جنس ما نرى الساعة، فإن جاز أن يرى الله ما ليس من جنس المرئيات وهو الإبصار التي في العين، فلم لا يجوز أن يرى نفسه، وإن لم يكن من جنس المرئيات؟ ولم لا يجوز أن يرينا نفسه وإن لم يكن من جنس المرئيات ؟ ويقال لهم : حدثونا إذا رأينا شيئاً فيبصرنا نراه، أو إنما يراه الرائي دون البصر ؟ فمن قولهم: : إنه محال أن يرى البصر الذي في العين. فيقال لهم: الآية تنفي أن تراه الأبصار، ولا تنفي أن يراه المبصرون، وإنما قال الله عز وجل: لَا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ فهذا لا يدل على أن المبصرين لا يرونه على ظاهر الآية.
It was said to them: Since one of the two statements is conjoined to the other, and His statement, Mighty and Majestic: (And He perceives the eyes) means He knows them, then it is necessary that His statement, Exalted: (Eyes perceive Him not) means they do not know Him, and this is a negation of knowledge, not of the vision of the eyes. If they say: The meaning of His statement, Exalted: (And He perceives the eyes) is that He knows them, then it is necessary that His statement: (Eyes perceive Him not) is that He sees them with a vision that does not have the meaning of knowledge. It is said to them: Is it permissible for the eyes that are in the heads to see? If they say yes, they contradict their statement that we do not see by the eye except what is of the same kind as what we see at the moment. For if it is permissible for Allah to see what is not of the kind of visible things, namely the sight that is in the eye, then why is it not permissible for Him to see Himself, even if He is not of the kind of visible things? And why is it not permissible for Him to show us Himself, even if He is not of the kind of visible things? And it is said to them: Inform us: When we see something, does it make us see it, or does the seer see it without the eye? According to their statement: It is impossible for the eye that is in the head to see. So it is said to them: The verse negates that the eyes see Him, but does not negate that the seers see Him. Allah, Mighty and Majestic, only said: (Eyes perceive Him not). So this does not indicate that the seers do not see Him, according to the apparent meaning of the verse.
Source pages p. 71

Commentary
This passage is a dialectical argument about the interpretation of the Quranic verses on divine vision. The author begins by establishing a linguistic principle: if two statements are conjoined (ma'tuf) and one is interpreted in a certain way, the other must follow consistently. The opponents (likely Mu'tazilites or Jahmites) claim that 'yudriku' in 'Wa huwa yudriku al-absar' (He perceives the eyes) means 'knows them' (ya'lamuha). Al-Ash'ari then argues that if that is so, then in the parallel verse 'La tudrikuhu al-absar' (Eyes perceive Him not), the verb must also mean 'know', resulting in 'they do not know Him'. This negates knowledge, not vision (ru'ya). The opponents might object that 'yudriku' in the first verse means 'sees' (yara), so the negation would be of vision. Al-Ash'ari refutes this by examining the nature of vision: Can the eyes themselves see? If they say yes, that contradicts the principle that physical eyes can only see objects of their kind (like colors and shapes). If they grant that Allah can make the eyes see what is not of their kind (like Himself), then why can He not see Himself? And why can He not show Himself to believers in the afterlife? The argument concludes by distinguishing between the eyes (al-absar) as organs and the seers (al-mubsirun) as persons. The verse only negates perception by the eyes, not vision by the believers. This supports the Ash'ari doctrine that Allah will be seen in the afterlife.

Key Terms
يدرك
إدراك
الأبصار
رؤية
المبصرون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The verse 'لا تدركه الأبصار' proves that Allah cannot be seen at all, by anyone.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari shows that the verse only negates sensory perception by the physical eyes, not the vision of the believers themselves.

Checkpoint
According to Imam al-Ash'ari in this passage, what does the verb 'yudriku' mean in the verse 'وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الأَبْصَرَ'?
A) He sees them
B) He knows them
C) He creates them
D) He encompasses them

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Ash'ari demonstrates that the Quranic negation of perception by eyes does not negate the believers' vision of Allah in the afterlife.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have learned that the verse 'لا تدركه الأبصار' does not negate the vision of Allah by believers in the afterlife. How does this hope of seeing your Creator affect the way you live today? What would you change if you truly believed you will see Him?
?
The argument hinges on distinguishing the physical eye from the seer. Are you more often controlled by your physical senses or by your inner vision of faith? What steps can you take to let your faith see beyond what your eyes perceive?
?
You have seen that al-Ash'ari uses precise linguistic analysis to defend a core belief about Allah. Examine your own understanding of Allah's attributes—do you have any doubts about what you claim to believe? What are you going to do about them starting today?

Quranic Evidence for Allah's Being Above
Al-Ash'ari cites Quranic verses proving Allah is above the heavens.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses Quranic verses to prove that Allah is above the heavens, established on His Throne, and that His istiwa' is free from anthropomorphism.

Core Idea
Quranic verses consistently affirm Allah's being above His creation, established on the Throne without modality.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a Quranic proof for Allah's attribute of being above ('uluw). By citing five distinct verses, he builds a cumulative argument that the heavens are the locus of Allah's istiwa' and that this does not imply physical indwelling. This lesson guides you through each verse and its theological implication.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the literal translation. Let the verses and their sequence speak for themselves.
Notice the pattern: each verse adds a piece of evidence.
2
Identify the five verses
Find each of the five Quranic citations. Note the specific aspect each verse highlights: direction (Nahl), ascension (Ma'arij), action towards heaven (Fussilat), and explicit istiwa' (Furqan, Sajda).
The verses are arranged from general to specific.
3
Understand the conclusion
Examine Al-Ash'ari's conclusion: all verses indicate that Allah is in the heaven, established on the Throne, and distinct from the earth. He emphasises that istiwa' is free from indwelling (hulul) and union (ittihad).
This conclusion refutes anthropomorphic interpretations.
4
Reflect on the common misunderstanding
Many assume 'above' implies physical location. Al-Ash'ari clarifies that istiwa' is an attribute befitting Allah without modality. Think about why this distinction matters in Islamic theology.
The Salaf affirmed istiwa' without asking 'how'.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
دليل آخر :
قال الله عز وجل : { يَخَافُونَ رَبَّهُم مِّن فَوقِهِمْ } [النحل:٥٠] وقال تعالى: { تَعْرُجُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ } [المعارج : ٤] وقال تعالى: { ثُمَّ اسْتَوَى إِلَى السَّمَاء وَهيَ دخان} [فصلت: ۱۱] وقال تعالى: { ثُمَّ اسْتَوَى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ الرَّحْمَنُ فَاسْأَلْ بِهِ خَبِيرًا } [الفرقان: ٥٩] وقال تعالى: { ثُمَّ اسْتَوَى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ مَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا شَفِيع } [السجدة: ٤] فكل ذلك يدل على أنه تعالى في السماء مستو على عرشه، والسماء بإجماع الناس ليست الأرض، فدل على أنه تعالى منفرد بوحدانيته، مستو على عرشه استواء منزهاً عن الحلول والاتحاد.
Another proof:
Allah Almighty said: {They fear their Lord above them} [an-Nahl: 50] and He said: {The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him} [al-Ma'arij: 4] and He said: {Then He turned to the heaven while it was smoke} [Fussilat: 11] and He said: {Then He established Himself on the Throne, the Most Merciful, so ask about Him one well informed} [al-Furqan: 59] and He said: {Then He established Himself on the Throne; you have not besides Him any protector or intercessor} [as-Sajda: 4]. All this indicates that He, exalted, is in the heaven, established on His Throne, and the heaven, by consensus of people, is not the earth. Thus it indicates that He, exalted, is unique in His unity, established on His Throne with an establishment free from indwelling and union.
Source pages p. 100

Commentary
This passage is a proof based on Quranic verses. Al-Ash'ari cites five verses that collectively demonstrate that Allah is above the heavens, specifically established on the Throne (istiwa'). The first verse (an-Nahl 50) mentions fearing Allah 'above them', indicating direction. The second (al-Ma'arij 4) speaks of angels ascending to Him, implying He is above. The third (Fussilat 11) says He turned to the heaven, showing His action towards it. The fourth and fifth (al-Furqan 59 and as-Sajda 4) explicitly state His istiwa' on the Throne. He concludes that since the heaven is not the earth, Allah is distinct from His creation, and His istiwa' is free from anthropomorphic concepts like indwelling (hulul) or union (ittihad).

Key Terms
استوى
العرش
فوق
السماء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Istiwa' means Allah sits on the Throne like a physical body.
Correction: Istiwa' is an attribute of Allah that befits His majesty, without modality, direction, or physical location, as understood by the Salaf.

Checkpoint
Which verse does al-Ash'ari cite to show that angels ascend to Allah, indicating He is above?
A) {They fear their Lord above them}
B) {The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him}
C) {Then He established Himself on the Throne}
D) {Then He turned to the heaven while it was smoke}

Key Takeaway
The passage proves that Allah is established above His creation on the Throne in a manner free from physical indwelling.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you pray, do you sense that Allah is above you, or does your heart slip into imagining a vague presence? What specific thought can you anchor yourself with to feel His transcendence in your next prayer?
?
If Allah is truly established on the Throne without any physical likeness, does that make Him distant or near to you? How can you cultivate intimacy with a God who is both above and closer than your jugular vein?
?
The passage warns against thinking of Allah in terms of union (ittihad) or indwelling (hulul). Have you ever, in a moment of spiritual emotion, unintentionally lowered His majesty by imagining Him as part of your surroundings? What will you do to guard against that?

Proof of Allah's Transcendence from Scripture and Consensus
Al-Ash'ari cites Qur'anic verses and consensus to prove Allah is above the heavens.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses Qur'anic verses and the consensus of the Muslim community to establish that Allah is exalted above His creation.

Core Idea
Allah’s transcendence is proven by both revelation and the living tradition of the Muslims.

Al-Ash'ari now presents a combined proof from divine speech and communal practice. He cites verses that describe Allah’s coming, descending, and raising, showing that He is above His creation. Then he points to the universal Muslim practice of addressing Allah as the 'Dweller of heaven' as a consensus that affirms this truth. This lesson walks through the verses and the consensus argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the translation. Notice the two types of evidence: Qur'anic verses and consensus.
The passage begins with 'Another proof'.
2
Examine the verses about Allah's coming and descending
Identify the verses from Al-Fajr, Al-Baqarah, and An-Najm. What do they imply about Allah's relationship to creation? They indicate that Allah can 'come' and 'descend' without being confined.
These verses are used to show that Allah is above His creation.
3
Examine the verses about Jesus' raising
Look at the verses from Al Imran and An-Nisa about Jesus being raised to Allah. How does that support the belief that Allah is in heaven? It shows a physical ascension to a place above.
4
Understand the consensus argument
Al-Ash'ari then cites the consensus of the ummah that Jesus was raised to the sky, and the common supplication practice. How does this serve as proof? It shows that the apparent meaning of the verses is accepted by all Muslims.
The supplication 'O Dweller of heaven' is key.
5
Reflect on the combined argument
Consider why al-Ash'ari uses both scripture and consensus. Together they provide a strong foundation for the belief in Allah's transcendence.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
دليل آخر : قال الله تعالى: وَجَاءَ رَبُّكَ وَالْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا ) [الفجر: ٢٢] وقال تعالى: هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلا أَن يَأْتِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ فِي ظُلَلٍ مِنَ الْغَمَامِ وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ ) [البقرة: ٢١٠] وقال : ثُمَّ دَنَا فَتَدَلَّى * فَكَانَ قَابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنَى * فَأَوْحَى إِلَى عَبْدِهِ مَا أَوْحَى مَا كَذَبَ الْفُؤَادُ مَا رَأَى * أَفَتُمَارُونَهُ عَلَى مَا يَرَى * وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرَى * عِندَ سِدْرَةِ الْمُنتَهَى * عِندَهَا جَنَّةُ الْمَأْوَى * إِذْ يَغْشَى السِّدْرَةَ مَا يَغْشَى * مَا زَاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَمَا طَغَى * لَقَدْ رَأَى مِنْ آيَاتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرَى ) [النجم: ٨-١٨] . وقال عز وجل لعيسى بن مريم عليه السلام إِنِّي مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَيَّ ) [آل عمران: ٥٥] وقال تعالى: وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًا * بَل رَفَعَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ ) [النساء: ١٥٧-١٥٨] وأجمعت الأمة على أن الله عز وجل رفع عيسى إلى السماء. ومن دعاء أهل الإسلام جميعاً إذا هم رغبوا إلى الله عز وجل في الأمر النازل بهم يقولون جميعاً: يا ساكن السماء، ومن خلفهم جميعاً: لا والذي احتجب بسبع سماوات.
Another proof: Allah Most High said: 'And your Lord comes, and the angels, rank upon rank' [Al-Fajr 22]. And He Most High said: 'Do they then await anything other than that Allah should come to them in the shadows of the clouds and the angels?' [Al-Baqarah 210]. And He said: 'Then he drew near and descended, and was at a distance of two bow-lengths or nearer, and He revealed to His servant what He revealed. The heart did not lie about what it saw. So do you dispute with him about what he saw? And he certainly saw him in another descent, at the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary, near which is the Garden of Refuge, when there covered the Lote Tree that which covered it. The sight did not swerve, nor did it transgress. He certainly saw of the greatest signs of his Lord.' [An-Najm 8-18]. And He, Mighty and Majestic, said to Jesus son of Mary, peace be upon him: 'Indeed, I will take you and raise you to Myself' [Al Imran 55]. And He Most High said: 'And they did not kill him, for certain. Rather, Allah raised him to Himself' [An-Nisa 157-158]. And the nation is unanimous that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, raised Jesus to the heaven. And from the supplication of all the people of Islam, when they beseech Allah, Mighty and Majestic, regarding a matter that has befallen them, they all say: 'O Dweller of heaven', and after them all: 'No, by the One who has veiled Himself with seven heavens.'
Source pages p. 101

Commentary
This passage presents a scriptural proof for Allah's transcendence and His being above the heavens. The author, al-Ash'ari, first cites several Qur'anic verses that use language of 'coming' (maji'), 'descending' (dunuw), and 'raising' (raf'), which indicate that Allah is not confined to His creation but is exalted above it. The verses about the coming of the Lord and His approach to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ show that Allah can be encountered without being embodied. The raising of Jesus to Allah is interpreted as a physical ascension to a place above, reinforcing the idea that Allah is in heaven. Then al-Ash'ari appeals to the consensus (ijma') of the Muslim community: both their collective belief that Jesus was raised to the sky and their common practice in supplication of addressing Allah as 'O Dweller of heaven' and swearing 'by the One who has veiled Himself with seven heavens'. This consensus is used as a proof that the apparent meaning of the verses is correct and that Allah is truly above His creation, not immanent in it.

Key Terms
علو الله
استواء
رفع عيسى
إجماع
دعاء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The proof is that Allah is physically located in a place within the heavens.
Correction: The proof affirms that Allah is exalted above His creation in a manner befitting His majesty, not that He is confined to a spatial location.

Checkpoint
What evidence from practice does al-Ash'ari use to support the claim that Allah is in heaven?
A) The consensus of scholars that Allah is everywhere.
B) The supplication of Muslims saying 'O Dweller of heaven'.
C) The verse 'There is nothing like unto Him'.
D) The story of Moses on Mount Sinai.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari demonstrates that the Qur'an and the consensus of Muslims both affirm Allah's exalted status above the heavens.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you make du'a, do you truly feel you are addressing the Most High, the One above the heavens, or do you merely repeat words? How can you cultivate genuine awe in your supplication?
?
The consensus of the ummah on the meaning of the verses regarding Jesus shows the unity of the early community. How deeply are you connected to that same consensus today? Do you feel part of that chain of belief?
?
The verses describe Allah's coming and the Prophet's nearness. What does it mean for your daily life that Allah is both transcendent and approachable? Are you living as if He is near, or as if He is distant?
