Divine Transcendence
intermediate
5 min
Allah is Not Within His Creation
Al-Ash'ari cites Qur'anic verses to prove Allah is not within creation and warns against excessive tanzih.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses Qur'anic verses about standing before Allah to prove that Allah is not within His creation, and why an extreme tanzih leads to ta'til.

Core Idea
Being returned to Allah implies that Allah is distinct from His creation.

Al-Ash'ari continues his argument against those who claim Allah is everywhere or within His creation. He presents a simple but powerful proof from the Qur'an: creatures are described as being 'returned to Allah' and 'standing before their Lord'. Such language only makes sense if Allah is not confined within His creation. This passage also warns against an overzealous tanzih that ends up denying Allah's attributes altogether.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without attempting analysis. Then read the literal translation to grasp the flow of the argument.
Pay attention to the four Qur'anic verses cited.
2
Identify the logical inference
Ask yourself: Why do these verses—'returned to Allah', 'stood before their Lord'—indicate that Allah is not in His creation? Think about what these expressions imply about spatial relationship.
Al-Ash'ari argues that if Allah were within creation, such language of coming to Him would be meaningless.
3
Understand the warning against extreme tanzih
Read the latter part where Al-Ash'ari criticizes those who claim transcendence (tanzih) but end up denying attributes (ta'til). Notice that he says such tanzih leads to negation.
This is a key point in Ash'ari theology: affirming attributes without modality (bila kayf) while avoiding both tashbih and ta'til.
4
Reflect on the balance
Consider how Al-Ash'ari maintains both transcendence and the reality of attributes. He affirms istiwa' without modality and without settling.
The passage ends with a prayer seeking refuge from a tanzih that necessitates negation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
دليل آخر : وقال عز وجل : ثُمَّ رُدُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ مَوْلَاهُمُ الْحَيِّ ) [الأنعام: ٦٢] وقال: وَلَوْ تَرَى إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلَى رَبِّهِمْ ) [الأنعام: ٣٠] وقال: وَلَوْ تَرَىٰ إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ نَاكِسُو رُءُوسِهِمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ) [السجدة: ١٢] وقال عز وجل : وَعُرِضُوا عَلَى رَبِّكَ صَفًّا ) [الكهف: ٤٨] . كُلُّ ذَلِكَ يَدُلُّ عَلَى أَنَّهُ لَيْسَ فِي خَلْقِهِ، وَلَا خَلْقُهُ فِيهِ، وَأَنَّهُ مُسْتَوٍ عَلَى عَرْشِهِ بِلَا كَيْفٍ وَلَا اسْتِقْرَارٍ، وَتَعَالَى عَمَّا يَقُولُ الظَّالِمُونَ وَالْجَاحِدُونَ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا، فَلَمْ يُثْبِتُوا لَهُ فِي وَصْفِهِمْ حَقِيقَةً، وَلَا أَوْجَبُوا لَهُ بِذِكْرِهِمْ إِيَّاهُ وَحْدَانِيَّةً؛ إِذْ كُلُّ كَلَامِهِمْ يَؤُولُ إِلَى التَّعْطِيلِ، وَجَمِيعُ أَوْصَافِهِمْ تَدُلُّ عَلَى النَّفْيِ، يُرِيدُونَ بِذَلِكَ التَّنْزِيهَ، وَنَفْيَ التَّشْبِيهِ، فَنَعُوذُ بِاللَّهِ مِنْ تَنْزِيهٍ يُوجِبُ النَّفْيَ وَالتَّعْطِيلَ.
Another proof: And He, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'Then they are returned to Allah, their true Lord.' [Al-An'am 62]. And He said: 'And if you could see when they are made to stand before their Lord.' [Al-An'am 30]. And He said: 'And if you could see when the criminals are hanging their heads before their Lord.' [As-Sajdah 12]. And He, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'And they are presented before your Lord in rows.' [Al-Kahf 48]. All of this indicates that He is not in His creation, nor is His creation in Him, and that He is established over His Throne without modality and without settling, and exalted above what the wrongdoers and deniers say with a great exaltation. For they have not affirmed for Him in their description any reality, nor have they established for Him by their mention of Him His oneness; since all their speech leads to denial (ta'til), and all their descriptions indicate negation, intending thereby a transcendence (tanzih) and a negation of anthropomorphism (tashbih). We seek refuge with Allah from a tanzih that necessitates negation and ta'til.
Source pages p. 101

Commentary
This passage is another proof using verses that describe creatures being 'returned to Allah', 'made to stand before their Lord', and 'presented before their Lord'. Al-Ash'ari argues that these phrases imply a direction and a separation between Allah and His creation: if Allah were within His creation, these expressions of coming to Him would be nonsensical. The author concludes that Allah is not in His creation nor is creation in Him, but He is established over His Throne (istiwa') in a manner without modality (bila kayf) and without settling (istiqrar) — meaning indivisible from spatial location as we understand it. Then al-Ash'ari criticizes those who claim transcendence (tanzih) but end up denying attributes altogether (ta'til). He warns that such an approach leads to negation of Allah's reality and oneness. The passage thus affirms both transcendence and the reality of attributes, avoiding both anthropomorphism and outright denial.

Key Terms
استواء
عرش
بلا كيف
تنزيه
تعطيل
تشبيه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Affirming that Allah is above His throne implies that He is physically located in a place like a body.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari says istiwa' is 'bila kayf wa la istiqrar'—without modality and without settling—meaning it is not a physical location or spatial relationship as we understand it.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, what is the danger of a tanzih (transcendence) that denies attributes?
A) It leads to anthropomorphism.
B) It leads to ta'til (negation) of Allah's attributes.
C) It leads to saying Allah is everywhere.
D) It leads to saying Allah is a body.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari proves that Allah is distinct from His creation using Qur'anic language, and warns that an excessive tanzih can lead to denying His attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In your own worship, do you ever feel that Allah is so distant and transcendent that He becomes abstract and impersonal—a 'force' rather than a Lord who hears and responds? How does this lesson's warning against excessive tanzih challenge that feeling, and what one concrete step can you take today to affirm both His majesty and His nearness?
?
Al-Ash'ari warns that a tanzih which leads to negation (ta'til) is actually a form of disbelief. Examine your own heart: have you ever hesitated to affirm an attribute of Allah (like His anger, His love, or His speaking) because you were afraid of falling into anthropomorphism? Where did that hesitation come from, and how will you correct it starting now?
?
The verses in this lesson all describe creatures being 'returned to' or 'presented before' their Lord. This implies that Allah is a distinct, personal being who will be encountered. Does the reality of standing before your Lord fill you with hope or fear—or both? How does this truth change how you live today, especially in moments when you feel invisible or forgotten?

Divine Transcendence
beginner
6 min
Proof from Hadith on Allah's Above-ness
Al-Ash'ari cites two hadiths affirming Allah is above the heavens.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses two hadiths to argue that Allah is above the heavens in a manner befitting His majesty, and why this does not imply spatial limitation.

Core Idea
Hadith proves Allah's above-ness without spatial confinement.

Al-Ash'ari now turns to transmitted evidence (samiyyat) to affirm Allah's attribute of 'fawqiyya'. He cites two hadiths: one about the servant standing before Allah, and the famous hadith of the slave-girl. These narratives ground the doctrine of divine transcendence in the words of the Prophet himself.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Note the two hadiths and the author's concluding statement.
The passage includes lengthy footnotes; focus on the core hadith texts and the author's comment.
2
Identify the first hadith
The first hadith speaks of the servant standing 'between the hands of Allah' on the Day of Judgment. What does this phrase imply about Allah's position relative to His creation?
The phrase 'bayna yaday Allah' is used to indicate standing before a sovereign, implying above-ness.
3
Analyze the second hadith
In the hadith of the slave-girl, the Prophet asks 'Where is Allah?' and she replies 'In heaven'. Observe how the Prophet affirms her faith based on this answer.
This hadith is often cited to refute those who deny Allah's above-ness.
4
Understand the author's conclusion
Al-Ash'ari concludes that these hadiths indicate Allah is above His throne, above the heaven, with a 'fawqiyya' that does not imply closeness to the throne.
Some manuscripts omit the clarification about closeness, so the nuance is important.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وروت العلماء عن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم أنه قال : « إن العبد لا تزول قدماه من بين يدي الله عز وجل حتى يسأله عن عمله» (۱). وروت العلماء أن رجلاً أتى النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم بأمة سوداء فقال: يا رسول الله ! إني أريد أن أعتقها في كفّارة، فهل يجوز عتقها؟ فقال لها النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم: «أيــن الله؟ قالت : في السماء ، قال : فمن أنا؟ قالت : أنت رسول الله ، فقـال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم : ( أعتقها فإنها مؤمنة» (۲). وهذا يدل على أن الله عز وجـل علـى عرشه فوق السماء، فوقية لا تزيده قرباً من العرش (۳) (۱) رواه الترمذي رقم (٢٤١٩) باب رقم (١) ، وقال الترمذي : هذا حديث حسن صحيح، وهو كما قال، والخطيب البغدادي في ( اقتضاء العلم ) رقم (۱) ، وفي الباب عن ابن مسعود عند الترمذي رقم (٢٤١٨)، وعن معاذ عند الخطيب رقم (۲)، وعن أبي سعيد عند البيهقي في كتاب ( البعث والنشور». قال الألباني: وأخرجه الدارمي. انظر ( اقتضاء العلم العمل ( ص (١٥٩-١٦٠) ، و ( صحيح الجامع) رقم (٧١٧٦-٧١٧٧). من حديث أبي برزة نضلة بن عبيد الأسلمي ، ولفظه : ( لا تزول قدما عبد يوم القيامة حتى يسأل عن عمره ، فيم أفناه، وعن علمه فيم ،فعل، وعن ماله من أين اكتسبه وفيم أنفقه، وعن جسمه فيم أبلاه ) . (۲) رواه الدارمي في ( الرد على الجهمية) ص (٩٥) ، وإسناده صحيح كما في ( مختصر العلو) ص(۸۱)، ومسلم رقم (٥٣٧ ) في الصلاة باب تحريم الكلام في الصلاة، من حديث معاوية بن الحكم السلمي قال: بينا أنا أصلي مع رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم إذ عطس رجل من القوم، فقلت: يرحمك الله، فرمـانـي القــوم بأبصارهم ، فقلت : والكل أمياه ما شأنكم تنظرون إلي؟ فجعلوا يضربون بأيديهم على أفخاذهم فلما رأيتهم يصمتونني، لكني سكت، فلما صلى رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم - فبأبي هو وأمي ! ما رأيت معلماً قبله ولا بعده أحسن تعليماً منه، فو الله ما كَهَرَني ولا ضربني ولا شتمني - قال : « إن هذه الصلاة لا يصلح فيها شيء من كلام الناس، إنما هو التسبيح والتكبير وقراءة القرآن ، أو كما قال رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم. قلت: يا رسول الله ! إني حديث عهد بجاهلية، وقد جاء الله بالإسلام وإن منا رجالاً يأتون الكهان قال : ( فلا تأتهم ، قال : ومنا رجال يتطيرون قال : ( ذاك شيء يجدونه في صدورهم فلا يصدنهم ) قال: قلت: ومنا رجال يخطون. قال: كان نبي من الأنبياء يخط، فمن وافق خطه فذاك ». قال: وكانت لي جارية ترعى غنماً لي قبل أحد والجوانية، فاطلعت ذات يوم فإذا الذئب قد ذهب بشاة مــن غنمها وأنا رجل من بني آدم آسف كما يأسفون، لكني صككتها صكة، فأتيت رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم فعظم ذلك علي، قلت: يا رسول الله أفلا أعتقها؟ قال : ( انتني بها ، فأتيته بها فقال لها : « أين الله...» الحديث . · (۳) قوله : فوقية لا تزيده قرباً من العرش، ساقطة من بعض النسخ .
And the scholars have narrated from the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, that he said: 'Indeed, the servant's two feet will not move from before Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, until He asks him about his deeds' (1). And the scholars have narrated that a man came to the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, with a black slave-girl and said: 'O Messenger of Allah! I want to set her free as an expiation, so is it permissible to free her?' The Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, said to her: 'Where is Allah?' She said: 'In heaven.' He said: 'Who am I?' She said: 'You are the Messenger of Allah.' So the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, said: 'Set her free, for she is a believer' (2). And this indicates that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is above His throne above the heaven, a 'fawqiyya' (above-ness) that does not increase His closeness to the throne (3). (1) Narrated by al-Tirmidhi no. 2419, chapter no. 1, and al-Tirmidhi said: This hadith is hasan sahih, and it is as he said, and al-Khatib al-Baghdadi in Iqtida' al-'Ilm no. 1, and in this chapter from Ibn Mas'ud at al-Tirmidhi no. 2418, and from Mu'adh at al-Khatib no. 2, and from Abu Sa'id at al-Bayhaqi in Kitab al-Ba'th wa al-Nushur. Al-Albani said: And al-Darimi also narrated it. See Iqtida' al-'Ilm al-'Amal pp. 159-160, and Sahih al-Jami' no. 7176-7177. From the hadith of Abu Barzah Nadlah ibn 'Ubayd al-Aslami, and its wording is: 'The feet of a servant will not move on the Day of Resurrection until he is asked about his life, in what he spent it; and about his knowledge, in what he did; and about his wealth, from where he earned it and on what he spent it; and about his body, in what he wore it out.' (2) Narrated by al-Darimi in al-Radd 'ala al-Jahmiyyah p. 95, and its chain is sahih as in Mukhtasar al-'Uluww p. 81, and Muslim no. 537 in the Book of Prayer, chapter on the prohibition of speech in prayer, from the hadith of Mu'awiyah ibn al-Hakam al-Sulami who said: While I was praying with the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, a man sneezed, so I said: 'May Allah have mercy on you.' The people glared at me, so I said: 'Woe is me, what is wrong with you that you look at me?' They started striking their thighs with their hands. When I saw they were silencing me, I kept quiet. When the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, finished the prayer—may my father and mother be sacrificed for him! I never saw a teacher before him or after him who taught better than him, by Allah he did not scold me, nor strike me, nor revile me—he said: 'Indeed, this prayer does not admit any of the speech of people; it is only glorification, magnification, and recitation of the Quran.' Or as the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, said. I said: 'O Messenger of Allah! I am only recently from Jahiliyyah, and Allah has brought Islam, and among us there are men who go to fortune-tellers.' He said: 'Do not go to them.' He said: 'And among us there are men who practice tiyarah (omens).' He said: 'That is something they find in their hearts; let it not deter them.' He said: 'And among us there are men who draw lines (for divination).' He said: 'There was a prophet among the prophets who drew lines; whoever's line coincides with that, then it is that.' He said: 'I had a slave-girl who tended my sheep near Uhud and al-Jawwaniyyah. One day I checked and found that a wolf had taken a sheep from her flock. I am a man from the sons of Adam who gets angry as they get angry, so I struck her a blow. I came to the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and that was grave to me. I said: O Messenger of Allah, shall I set her free? He said: 'Bring her to me.' So I brought her to him, and he said to her: 'Where is Allah...' the hadith. (3) His saying: 'a fawqiyya that does not increase His closeness to the throne' is omitted from some copies.
Source pages p. 103

Commentary
This passage presents two hadith-based proofs for the attribute of Allah’s 'fawqiyya' (above-ness) and His being above the heavens. First, the author cites a hadith about the servant being questioned 'between the hands of Allah' (bayna yaday Allah), implying Allah is above and the servant stands before Him. Second, the famous hadith of the black slave-girl, where the Prophet asks her 'Where is Allah?' and she replies 'In heaven,' and the Prophet confirms her faith. The author explicitly states that this indicates Allah is above His throne, above the heaven, with a 'fawqiyya' that does not imply proximity to the throne in a spatial sense. The long footnotes provide chains and evaluations; notably, the author’s own text is reinforced by these narrations. The final note (3) indicates that some manuscript copies omit the phrase 'that does not increase His closeness to the throne,' which protects against misinterpretation of spatial nearness.

Key Terms
فوقية
فوق السماء
عرش
بين يدي الله
أين الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some interpret the hadith 'Where is Allah?' with the answer 'In heaven' as meaning that Allah is spatially confined to heaven or that heaven contains Him.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari clarifies that it indicates a 'fawqiyya' (above-ness) that does not increase His closeness to the throne, meaning it is a rank of elevation, not spatial containment.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari's argument from the hadith of the slave-girl, what does her answer 'in heaven' indicate?
A) That Allah is confined to heaven.
B) That Allah is above the heaven in a manner befitting His majesty.
C) That Allah is everywhere.
D) That heaven contains Allah.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari uses hadith to affirm Allah's above-ness (fawqiyya) without spatial limitation, grounding divine transcendence in prophetic teaching.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The slave-girl answered with simple certainty: 'In heaven.' Do you have that same clarity about Allah's attributes, or do you overcomplicate? What would change if you affirmed Allah's above-ness with the same childlike faith? Will you commit to accepting the clear texts without intellectualizing them away?
?
The Prophet taught that you will stand 'between the hands of Allah' and be questioned about your deeds. Does this awareness shape how you live each day? Right now, is there an area of your life where you act as if this accountability is distant? What one concrete action will you take today to live in light of that standing?
?
Allah's above-ness is not spatial but a matter of rank and transcendence. Yet your heart often imagines Allah as physically distant or near. How does this refined understanding challenge your concept of closeness to Allah? Will you seek nearness through obedience and worship rather than imagining a location?

If God's Will Is Not Effective, Things He Dislikes Occur
A refutation showing the implication of denying God's effective will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why denying the effectiveness of God's will leads to an implication of weakness and poverty in God.

Core Idea
If God's will is not effective, He would be weak and poor—impossible for the Almighty.

This passage presents a logical refutation of those who claim that something can occur in God's dominion without His will. The argument shows that such a view forces one to admit that God dislikes or refuses something that still happens, which implies imperfection. The author then exalts God above such attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that the argument is concise and moves stepwise.
2
Trace the logical chain
Identify each step: start with the opponents' claim, then see the first implication (disliked), then the second (refused), then the consequence (disobedience occurs anyway), and finally the conclusion (weakness/poverty).
Ask yourself why each step follows from the previous.
3
Reflect on the refutation
Consider how this argument refutes the opponents: it shows their position entails an absurdity—attributing imperfection to God.
This connects to the core idea of God's transcendence.
4
Apply the lesson
Explain in your own words why the author says 'God is exalted above that by a great exaltation'.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۱۸۹ - مسألة : ويقال لهم : إذا قلتم إنه يكون في سلطانه تعالى ما لا يريد، فقد كان إذاً في سلطانه ما كرهه، فلا بد من نعم . فيقال لهم : فإذا كان في سلطانه ما يكرهه، فما أنكرتم أن يكون في سلطانه ما يأبى كونه، فإن أجابوا إلى ذلك قيل لهم : فقد كانت المعاصي شاء الله أم أبى، وهذه صفة الضعف والفقر، تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً .
189 - Issue: And it is said to them: If you say that in His dominion there occurs what He does not will, then there has occurred in His dominion what He dislikes. There is no escape from 'yes'. Then it is said to them: If there is in His dominion what He dislikes, then why do you deny that there is in His dominion what He refuses to be? If they answer that, it is said to them: Then disobedience occurs whether God wills or refuses? And this is the attribute of weakness and poverty. God is exalted above that by a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 125

Commentary
This passage is a short refutation (mas'ala) that draws out the implications of denying the effectiveness of God's will. The author argues: if you (the opponents) claim that something occurs in God's dominion that He does not will, then necessarily that thing is disliked (makra) by God. The author then presses further: if there is something in His dominion that He dislikes, why would you deny that there is something that God absolutely refuses to be? If they concede that, then it would follow that disobedience occurs regardless of God's will or refusal — which would imply weakness and poverty in God. The passage concludes by exalting God far above such imperfections.

Key Terms
السلطان
الكره
الضعف
الفقر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God can dislike something that still happens without any imperfection.
Correction: This passage shows that such a scenario would imply weakness and poverty in God, which is impossible.

Checkpoint
What consequence does the author draw from the claim that what God does not will occurs in His dominion?
A) It means God is weak.
B) It means God dislikes something that nevertheless happens.
C) It means God is ignorant.
D) It means God's power is limited.

Key Takeaway
The effective will of God is essential to His sovereignty; denying it leads to attributing weakness and poverty to Him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever live as if some events—especially your own mistakes—escape God's will? How does this lesson challenge that assumption, and what would it cost you to fully accept that nothing happens outside His will?
?
When you sin, do you think God dislikes it but is unable to stop you? If you truly believed His will is effective over all things, how would your repentance and reliance on Him change starting today?
?
If you have ever despaired because of trials, did you subconsciously doubt that God willed them for you? This lesson says that denying effective will implies weakness in God. How will you surrender to His will in your current hardship?

Quranic Proof that God Does What He Wills
Uses Quranic verse to prove God's effective will and refutes deniers by showing absurdities.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Quranic verse 'Doer of what He wills' establishes that God's will is perfectly effective, and why denying this leads to absurd implications of heedlessness or weakness in God.

Core Idea
Denying God's effective will forces an admission of divine heedlessness or weakness.

This lesson presents a concise Quranic argument from Imam al-Ash'ari. By citing the verse 'Doer of what He wills,' he shows that God's will is always effective. He then uses a reductio ad absurdum: if God did something He did not will, then He must have been either unaware or powerless—both impossible for Allah. The same logic applies to human actions within God's dominion.

Guided Path
1
Read the Quranic verse
Read the Arabic verse 'فعال لما يريد' and its translation. Notice the absolute phrase that attributes effective will to God.
The verse appears in Surah Hud 11:107 and Surah al-Buruj 85:16.
2
Identify the dilemma for God's acts
The passage argues that if someone claims God does what He does not will, then that act must have occurred while God was either heedless (sahw/ghaflah) or weak (da'f/taqsir). Recognize both as impossible for God.
3
Extend the same logic to human actions
The author applies the same reasoning: if any event in God's dominion occurs against His will, then again either heedlessness or weakness must be attributed to God. This shows that all events, including human actions, are under God's effective will.
This refutes the notion of independent human will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۱۹۱ - مسألة : ويقال لهم : أليس قد قال الله تعالى: (فعال لما يريد) [هود : ۱۰۷ والبروج : ١٦]؟ فلا بد من نعم . فيقال لهم : فمن زعم أن الله تعالى فعل ما لا يريد، وأراد أن يكون من فعله ما لا يكون لزمه أن يكون قد وقع ذلك وهو ساه غافل عنه، أو أن الضعف والتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده لحقه، فلا بد من نعم . فيقال لهم : فكذلك من زعم أنه يكون في سلطان الله عز وجل ما لا يريده من عبيده لزمه أحد أمرين : إما أن يزعم أن ذلك كان عن سهو وغفلة، أو أن يزعم أن الضعف والتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده لحقه .
191 - Issue: And it is said to them: Has not God Almighty said: 'Doer of what He wills' [Hud 11:107 and al-Buruj 85:16]? There is no escape from 'yes'. Then it is said to them: So whoever claims that God Almighty does what He does not will, and wills that from His act something does not occur, then it necessarily follows for him that that occurred while He was heedless and inattentive, or that weakness and shortcoming to attain what He wills befell Him. There is no escape from 'yes'. Then it is said to them: Likewise, whoever claims that in the dominion of God Almighty there occurs what He does not will from His servants, then he necessarily faces one of two things: either he claims that that occurred through heedlessness and inattention, or he claims that weakness and shortcoming to attain what He willed befell Him.
Source pages p. 125

Commentary
This passage is a final refutation (mas'ala) that cites a Quranic verse as authority: 'Doer of what He wills' (فعال لما يريد). The author then argues that anyone who claims God does what He does not will — meaning that some act of God occurs that He did not intend — must accept that such an occurrence would have happened while God was heedless (sāh) or inattentive (ghāfil), or that weakness (ḍa'f) and shortcoming (taqṣīr) prevented Him from achieving His will. Both implications are impossible for God. The author extends the same reasoning to the actions of servants: if someone claims that in God's dominion something occurs that God does not will from His servants, then that person must also accept that either God was unaware or lacked power. Thus, the passage establishes that God's will is perfectly effective over all events, including human actions.

Key Terms
فعال لما يريد
سهو
غفلة
ضعف
تقصير

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's will does not always extend to human actions; humans can act independently of God's will.
Correction: The verse and the argument show that if any action in God's dominion occurred against His will, it would imply heedlessness or weakness in God. Since both are impossible, all human actions are under God's effective will.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, if someone claims that God does what He does not will, what two possibilities must he accept?
A) Either God is able or unable.
B) Either God is knowledgeable or ignorant.
C) Either God was heedless or weak.
D) Either God is just or unjust.

Key Takeaway
The Quranic verse 'Doer of what He wills' proves that God's will is perfectly effective over all things, including human actions, and denying it leads to absurd implications about God's attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you commit a sin, do you truly believe it occurred only because God willed it, or do you harbor a secret sense that you acted outside His will? How does that affect your repentance and trust in His decree?
?
If God's will is effective over everything, including your choices, how does that change the way you view your failures and successes? Are you willing to surrender the illusion of independent control and accept His decree with contentment?
?
The verse 'Doer of what He wills' implies that nothing happens without His will. Is there an area of your life where you resist this truth—some outcome you wish had been different? How can you train your heart to see His will in all that occurs?

God Cannot Be Called Foolish for Willing Folly
Al-Ash'ari argues that God cannot be called foolish for willing folly because He is not under law.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why al-Ash'ari argues that attributing foolishness to God is invalid because the concept of foolishness depends on being under a higher authority.

Core Idea
Foolishness only applies to beings under law; God has no lawgiver above Him.

Al-Ash'ari presents a sophisticated argument defending God's transcendence. He clarifies that calling someone foolish requires that they have violated a superior's command. Since God has no superior, even if He wills what appears ugly, it cannot be called foolishness.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the literal translation to grasp the argument's flow.
Notice the contrast between human and God.
2
Understand the human analogy
Identify why a human is called foolish: because they intend folly while being under a superior's law that forbids it.
The key point is that foolishness requires a prohibition from a higher authority.
3
Apply to God and grasp the conclusion
Since God has no superior, no lawgiver, no one to permit or forbid, the basis for calling an action foolish is absent. Therefore, God cannot be called foolish even if He wills what is ugly.
This defends divine sovereignty and transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: السفيه منا إنما كان سفيها كما أراد السفه؛ لأنه نهي عن ذلك، ولأنه تحت شريعة من هو فوقه، ومن يحد له الحدود، ويرسم لـه الرسوم، فلما أتى ما نهي عنه كان سفيهاً، ورب العالمين جل ثناؤه وتقدست أسماؤه ليس تحت شريعة ولا فوقه من يحد له الحدود، ويرسم له الرسوم ولا فوقه مبيح ولا حاظر ولا آمر ولا زاجر ، فلم يجب إذا أراد أن يكون قبيحاً أن ينسب إلى السفه سبحانه وتعالى
And it is said to them: The foolish one among us is only foolish when he intends folly because he is forbidden from that, and because he is under the law of one above him, who sets limits for him and prescribes rules for him. So when he does what he was forbidden from, he becomes foolish. But the Lord of the worlds, majestic is His praise and sanctified are His names, is not under any law, nor is there above Him anyone who sets limits for Him or prescribes rules for Him, nor is there above Him any permitter or prohibiter, commander or restrainer. So it is not necessary, when He intends something that is ugly, that He be attributed with foolishness—glorified and exalted is He.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage presents an argument (hujja) from al-Ash'ari against those who accuse God of foolishness (safah) for willing evils. The argument uses analogy: a human becomes foolish only when he intends folly while being under a superior's law that forbids it. Humans have a legislator above them who sets boundaries; when they violate those boundaries, they are foolish. God, however, has no superior, no lawgiver above Him, no one to permit or forbid Him. Therefore, even if He intends something considered ugly or evil, it cannot be attributed to foolishness because the entire basis for calling an action foolish—being under a prohibition—does not apply to God. The argument defends divine sovereignty and transcendence (tanzih).

Key Terms
السفيه (al-safih)
السفه (al-safah)
الشريعة (al-shari'a)
الحدود (al-hudud)
الرسوم (al-rusum)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That God can be accused of foolishness when He wills evil.
Correction: The argument clarifies that the concept of foolishness presupposes a higher authority, which God lacks.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, why can God not be called foolish even if He wills what appears ugly?
A) Because God never wills what is ugly
B) Because foolishness only applies to beings under a superior's law
C) Because God's actions are always good by definition
D) Because humans cannot judge God's actions

Key Takeaway
God cannot be called foolish for any action because foolishness requires being under a superior's prohibition, and God has no superior.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin, do you ever feel that God is unfair or foolish for allowing that trial or test? How does this passage expose that thought as a failure to recognize His absolute sovereignty? Will you repent of that today and submit to His wisdom?
?
If you were to honestly examine your heart, do you place yourself above God as a judge of His actions? What does this lesson demand you change about how you think about God's commands in your life?
?
How often do you demand that God explain His actions according to your standards of justice? What would it look like to trust that He is never foolish, even when you don't understand, starting today?

The Master-Fornication Analogy
Al-Ash'ari uses an analogy to refute the claim that God is foolish.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses a reductio ad absurdum analogy to defend God from the charge of foolishness for permitting evil.

Core Idea
Permission with power does not equal foolishness.

In this passage, al-Ash'ari takes on a common objection: if God is all-powerful and allows evil, doesn't that make Him foolish? He answers with a simple analogy from everyday life—a master who leaves his slaves to fornicate. Through this analogy, he shows that the accusation of foolishness is based on a flawed comparison between human and divine actions. This lesson walks you through his argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
First, read al-Ash'ari's concise argument in Arabic and in the translation. Pay attention to the structure of the rhetorical question he poses.
Notice the repetition of the phrase 'يُقال لهم'.
2
Identify the analogy
The analogy compares a human master who leaves his slaves to commit fornication while able to stop them, and God who does the same. Identify the three elements: the master, the slaves, and the act of permission.
3
Understand the reductio
Al-Ash'ari uses a reductio ad absurdum: if the human master is called foolish, then God would also be foolish—which is impossible. Therefore, the premise that permission implies foolishness is false.
The argument works for both permission and intention.
4
Consider the implication
Think about why this argument matters for divine transcendence. It shows that God's actions are not subject to the same moral categories as human actions.
This reinforces that God is beyond human judgment.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: أليس من خلى بين عبيده وبين إمائـه منـا يـزنـي بعـضـهـم ببعض، وهو يقدر على التفريق بينهم يكون سفيهاً، ورب العالمين عز وجل قد خلى بين عبيده وإمائه يزني بعضهم ببعض، وهو يقدر على التفريق بينهم وليس سفيهاً، وكذلك من أراد السفه منا كان سفيهاً، ورب العالمين جل وعز يريد السفه، وليس سفيهاً .
And it is said to them: Is it not the case that one among us who leaves his male and female slaves free to commit fornication with one another, while he is able to separate them, would be called foolish? Yet the Lord of the worlds, mighty and majestic, has left His male and female slaves to commit fornication with one another, while He is able to separate them, and He is not foolish. Likewise, whoever among us intends folly becomes foolish, but the Lord of the worlds, majestic and mighty, intends folly and is not foolish.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage presents another objection (mas'ala) using a reductio ad absurdum. The opponents claim that God is foolish because He allows evil (e.g., fornication) despite being able to prevent it. Al-Ash'ari argues analogically: if a human master leaves his slaves to fornicate while able to stop them, we do not necessarily call him foolish—and if we do, then God would also be foolish. But since we affirm God is not foolish (by consensus), the analogy shows that the premise is false: mere permission with ability to prevent does not entail foolishness. The argument reinforces that God's actions are not subject to human moral judgments in the same way.

Key Terms
خلى (khalla)
عبيد وإماء (slaves)
السفه (al-safah)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That God is foolish for permitting evil while being able to prevent it.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari argues that permission with power does not entail foolishness, as shown by the analogy of the human master.

Checkpoint
What does al-Ash'ari use to argue that God is not foolish for permitting fornication?
A) God does not permit fornication
B) A human master who does the same is not called foolish
C) Fornication is not evil
D) God forces people to fornicate

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari defends God's transcendence by showing that the charge of foolishness relies on a false analogy.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You claim to believe in God's perfect wisdom, yet when you face suffering or witness injustice, do you not secretly harbor doubts about His judgment? How does Al-Ash'ari's argument directly challenge that hidden accusation? Will you commit to affirming that God is above being foolish, even when His ways seem inscrutable to you?
?
Reflect on a specific moment in your life when you thought, 'This doesn't make sense; if I were God, I would have done it differently.' Based on this lesson, what false analogy were you drawing? Are you willing to surrender your human standard of wisdom and accept that God's permission of evil does not make Him foolish?
?
If Al-Ash'ari's reductio proves that no human can call God foolish, then what does your own complaining about God's decree reveal about your heart? In what areas of obedience have you withheld trust because you think you know better? What concrete step will you take this week to submit to God's wisdom, even when you don't understand?

God Does Not Intend Injustice: A Precise Meaning
Exegetical refutation regarding God's intention of injustice

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the precise meaning of the Quranic verses that state Allah does not intend injustice, and how this refutes the misconception that God does not will human acts of injustice.

Core Idea
God’s non-intention of injustice means He Himself does no wrong, not that He prohibits all injustice.

This lesson examines a crucial Quranic argument from Imam al-Ash'ari's al-Ibanah. The verses that say Allah does not intend injustice for His servants are often misunderstood. Some mistakenly think they deny God's will entirely over human actions. Al-Ash'ari provides a precise linguistic and theological clarification that protects divine transcendence while affirming His will over all things.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic and English to understand the question and answer structure. Note the specific wording Allah uses.
The passage is short but dense.
2
Focus on the linguistic distinction
Notice how al-Ash'ari draws attention to what the verse says ('Allah does not intend wrongdoing for them') and what it does not say ('He does not intend some of them wronging others').
3
Grasp the theological implication
Understand why this distinction matters: it preserves Allah's transcendence and justice while affirming His will over all human actions, including injustice.
This is key to the Ash'ari position on qadar.
4
Reflect on the misconception
Consider the common error this passage guards against: thinking that God neither wills nor permits any injustice whatsoever.
The misconception is detailed in the lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
212 - مسألة: وإن سألوا عن قول الله عز وجل : (وَمَا اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِلْعِبَادِ) [غافر :31] وعن قوله : (وَمَا اللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِلْعَالَمِينَ) [آل عمران : 108] . قيل لهم : معنى ذلك أنه لا يريد أن يظلمهم؛ لأنه قال: وما الله يريد ظلماً لهم، ولم يقل : لا يريد ظلم بعضهم لبعض، فلم يرد أن يظلمهم وإن
Issue 212: And if they ask about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'And Allah does not intend injustice for the servants' (Quran 40:31) and about His saying: 'And Allah does not intend injustice for the worlds' (Quran 3:108). It is said to them: The meaning of that is that He does not intend to wrong them; because He said: 'Allah does not intend wrongdoing for them,' and He did not say: 'He does not intend some of them wronging others.' So He did not intend to wrong them, and...
Source pages p. 135

Commentary
This passage is a Quranic interpretation. It addresses verses that say God does not intend injustice for servants/worlds. The author explains that the meaning is that God Himself does not intend to wrong them. The evidence is the wording: 'Allah does not intend wrongdoing for them' – focusing on wrongdoing emanating from God, not from people to each other. The passage is cut off but the argument is clear: God's not intending injustice means He does not commit injustice.

Key Terms
ظلم
إرادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that 'God does not intend injustice' means God does not will any act of injustice to occur anywhere, including human acts.
Correction: The verse only negates God Himself intending to wrong anyone. It does not negate His will over human acts of injustice as part of His overall decree.

Checkpoint
According to the interpretation, what does 'God does not intend injustice for the servants' mean?
A) God does not will any act of injustice to occur
B) God does not will to wrong them Himself
C) God prevents all injustice among people
D) God does not decree actions

Key Takeaway
The Quranic phrase 'Allah does not intend injustice for the servants' means Allah Himself does not wrong them; it does not deny His will over human wrongdoing.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you suffer a wrong or see injustice in the world, do you truly believe that Allah Himself is not the one wronging you? Or does your faith waiver? How does this verse reshape your trust in Allah's justice?
?
The author distinguishes between Allah's non-intention of injustice and His permission of injustice among people. Is there an area in your life where you blame Allah for the wrongs done by others? How will you realign your thinking today based on this precise meaning?
?
If Allah does not intend injustice for you, then what does that mean for your response to those who wrong you? Does it free you to forgive, knowing that Allah's will over their actions does not make Him unjust? What step will you take to embody this truth?

Refuting the Claim that Disbelief is a Disparity
Al-Ash'ari exegetically refutes the claim that disbelief contradicts Quranic perfection.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses Quranic context to refute an objection about the existence of disbelief.

Core Idea
The Quranic verse about no disparity refers only to the heavens, not to human actions.

Al-Ash'ari's opponents argued that since God says there is no disparity in His creation, the existence of disbelief (a disparity) cannot be from God. In this passage, Al-Ash'ari shows that the verse's context is about the physical heavens, not moral evil. This lesson examines his precise exegetical argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
The opponents cite Quran 67:3 and argue that disbelief is a disparity, so how can it be from God?
2
Identify Al-Ash'ari's answer
He quotes verses 67:3-4 to show the context: the 'creation of the Most Merciful' refers to the seven heavens, not all creation.
3
Understand the refutation
Since the verse is about the heavens and mentions 'cracks' (futur), 'tafawut' means physical defects, not moral evil.
4
See the conclusion
Al-Ash'ari concludes that the objection is invalid because the verse does not address human actions.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مسألة : وإن سألوا عن قول الله تعالى: ﴿ مَا تَرَىٰ فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمَنِ مِن تَفَوُتِ [الملك: ٣] قالوا: والكفر متفاوت فكيف يكون من خلق الله ؟ والجواب عن ذلك أنه عز وجل قال : ( خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَوَاتٍ طِبَاقًا مَا تَرَىٰ فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمَنِ مِن تَفَوتِ فَارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرَى مِن فُطُورٍ * ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَتَيْنِ يَنقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ الْبَصَرُ خَاسِنا وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ ﴾ [الملك : ٣-٤] فإنما عنى ما ترى في السموات من فطور ؛ لأنه ذكــر خلق السموات ولم يذكر الكفر، وإذا كان هذا على ما قلناه بطل ما قالوه، والحمد لله رب العالمين .
An issue: And if they ask about the saying of God, Exalted: 'You do not see in the creation of the Most Merciful any disparity' (67:3). They said: And disbelief is a disparity, so how can it be from the creation of God? The answer to that is that He, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'He created seven heavens in layers. You do not see in the creation of the Most Merciful any disparity. So return your gaze, do you see any cracks? Then return your gaze twice, your gaze will return to you humbled and weary' (67:3-4). Thus He meant what you see in the heavens from cracks; because He mentioned the creation of the heavens and He did not mention disbelief. And if this is as we have said, their statement is invalidated, and praise be to God, Lord of the worlds.
Source pages p. 136

Commentary
This is a narrow exegetical argument. Opponents claimed that since disbelief is a disparity or flaw, and God says there is no disparity in His creation, disbelief cannot be from God. Al-Ash'ari points out that the verse is in the context of the heavens and their perfect order, mentioning cracks (futur), not human actions. The word 'tafawut' (disparity) here refers to physical defects, not moral evil. Thus the argument fails.

Key Terms
تفاوت
خلق
فطور

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Disbelief is a disparity and therefore cannot be from God because God created no disparity.
Correction: The 'tafawut' in the verse refers only to physical cracks in the heavens, not to moral qualities, so the objection is invalid.

Checkpoint
In the verse 'You do not see in the creation of the Most Merciful any disparity,' what is the referent of 'creation' according to al-Ash'ari?
A) All of creation
B) The heavens only
C) Human actions
D) The earth

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari refutes the objection by pointing out that the Quranic verse about no disparity is specifically about the heavens, not all of creation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever been tempted to use a single verse to support a position without checking its context, like the opponents did with 67:3? What does this lesson reveal about the danger of proof-texting? Are you willing to examine your own beliefs to see if any rest on verses taken out of context? Will you commit to studying verses in their full setting from now on?
?
When you hear an argument that challenges your faith, do you immediately look for a quick refutation, or do you seek to understand the opponent's reasoning from the Quran and Sunnah? This lesson shows the importance of precise interpretation. Think of a specific doubt you have encountered. How can you apply Al-Ash'ari's method of returning to the full text to answer it?
?
The opponents claimed that disbelief is a 'disparity' and thus cannot be from God. In your own heart, do you ever find it difficult to accept that God creates both belief and disbelief? How does this lesson help you trust God's wisdom even when you cannot fully comprehend why He creates evil? What would it mean for you to submit to His decree completely, without questioning His perfection?

Refuting the Objection: Disparity in Creation
Al-Ash'ari refutes the objection about disparity in creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari refutes the objection that disbelief is disparity in creation by contextualizing the Qur'anic verse to the heavens.

Core Idea
Context determines meaning: the verse about 'no disparity' refers to the heavens, not to disbelief.

The objection attempts to use a Qur'anic verse to argue that disbelief, being disparate, cannot be from Allah's creation. Al-Ash'ari responds by closely reading the verse in its original context. This lesson shows how theological reasoning relies on precise scriptural interpretation.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
Understand the opponents' claim: they link disparity in disbelief to the verse 'You see not in the creation of the Most Merciful any disparity' (al-Mulk 3), arguing that disbelief is varied and thus cannot be from Allah's creation.
Notice the objectors assume the verse applies to all creation.
2
Read the answer
Al-Ash'ari quotes the fuller verse (al-Mulk 3-4) and points out that it explicitly mentions 'the creation of the heavens' and 'fissures' (فطور). The context shows the verse is about celestial bodies, not disbelief.
Pay attention to the key term 'فطور' (fissures).
3
Identify the key move
Al-Ash'ari distinguishes between the verse's referent (the heavens) and the objectors' misapplication to disbelief. He argues that since the verse speaks of the heavens and does not mention disbelief, the objection is invalid.
This is a classic example of tafsir al-ayāt bi'l-siyāq (contextual interpretation).
4
Reflect on the method
Consider how al-Ash'ari uses textual context to defend the doctrine that all creation, including disbelief, is from Allah. This shows the importance of precise reading in Islamic theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإن سألوا عن قول الله تعالى: ﴿ مَا تَرَىٰ فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمَنِ مِن تَفَوُتِ [الملك: ٣] قالوا: والكفر متفاوت فكيف يكون من خلق الله ؟
والجواب عن ذلك أنه عز وجل قال : ( خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَوَاتٍ طِبَاقًا مَا تَرَىٰ فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمَنِ مِن تَفَوتِ فَارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرَى مِن فُطُورٍ * ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَتَيْنِ يَنقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ الْبَصَرُ خَاسِنا وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ ﴾ [الملك : ٣-٤] فإنما عنى ما ترى في السموات من فطور ؛ لأنه ذكــر خلق السموات ولم يذكر الكفر، وإذا كان هذا على ما قلناه بطل ما قالوه، والحمد لله رب العالمين .
And if they ask about the saying of Allah Most High: 'You see not in the creation of the Most Merciful any disparity' (al-Mulk 3), they say: And disbelief is disparate, so how can it be from the creation of Allah? And the answer to that is that He, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'He created seven heavens in layers. You see not in the creation of the Most Merciful any disparity. So return the vision, do you see any fissures? Then return the vision twice, the vision will return to you humbled and weary' (al-Mulk 3-4). Indeed He meant what you see in the heavens of fissures, because He mentioned the creation of the heavens and did not mention disbelief. And if this is as we have said, what they said is invalid. And praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.
Source pages p. 136

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer (مسألة وجواب). The objectors argue: Allah says there is no disparity (تَفَاوُت) in His creation, yet disbelief (كفر) is not uniform—some disbelief is worse than others, so how can disbelief be from Allah's creation? Al-Ash'ari answers by explaining the context of the verse. He quotes the full verse (al-Mulk 3-4) which speaks specifically about the heavens—their lack of fissures or flaws. The verse does not address disbelief at all; it is about the perfection of the celestial creation. Therefore the objection is invalid because it misapplies the verse. The answer follows the literal wording of the Qur'an and uses its immediate context to refute the misreading.

Key Terms
تفاوت
فطور
خلق السموات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the verse 'You see not in the creation of the Most Merciful any disparity' applies to all creation universally, including human actions and disbelief.
Correction: The verse specifically refers to the heavens and their lack of fissures, as clarified by the subsequent verse about 'fissures' and the context of celestial creation.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, what does the verse 'You see not in the creation of the Most Merciful any disparity' refer to?
A) All of creation including human actions
B) The heavens only, regarding fissures or flaws
C) The perfect equality of all creatures
D) The lack of disparity in disbelief

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari defends divine creation by showing that the verse about no disparity is about the heavens, not about disbelief.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you see inequality or disparity among people in faith or deeds, do you ever doubt the perfection of Allah's creation? How does this lesson correct that doubt?
?
The objectors used the Qur'an to argue against Allah's creation. In what ways might you unknowingly use the Qur'an to justify your own limited understanding rather than submitting to its intended meaning?
?
Al-Ash'ari answered by returning to the immediate context of the verse. When you read a verse that seems to contradict something you believe, do you rush to reject it or do you pause to consider its context and original meaning? What will you do differently now?

Children of Polytheists and Divine Justice
Imam Ash'ari argues that God may punish children of polytheists as an act of justice.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses a logical argument based on conceded premises to defend the possibility of divine justice in the afterlife for children of polytheists.

Core Idea
God's justice is consistent across this world and the Hereafter.

This passage presents a sharp dialectical argument against the Mu'tazila, who denied that God could punish children of polytheists in the afterlife. Imam Ash'ari builds on a premise they already accept—that God's causing pain to children in this world is just—and turns it against them. By showing that the same logic applies to the Hereafter, he challenges their consistency and supports his position with authentic hadith.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the English translation. Notice the rhetorical structure: a question-and-answer style.
The passage uses conditional phrasing: 'if they say... then it is said to them'.
2
Identify the opponent's conceded premise
The Mu'tazila concede that God's causing pain to children in this world is just because it serves as a lesson for fathers. Identify this premise in the text.
3
Trace the logical analogy
Follow how Imam Ash'ari argues from this premise to the afterlife: if it is just in this world for that purpose, then it is just in the Hereafter for the same purpose (to grieve fathers).
This is a qiyas (analogical reasoning) based on the principle of consistency.
4
Examine the scriptural evidence
Read the two hadiths cited. The first describes a test by fire; the second mentions hearing their cries. See how they support the possibility of such punishment.
These hadiths are not proof that all children will be punished, but that God may deal with them as He wills.
5
Reflect on the misconception
Consider the common misunderstanding that this passage is about the final fate of all polytheists' children. Instead, it is a refutation of Mu'tazilite inconsistency.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
به، وكان ذلك سائغاً جائزاً. فإذا قالوا: نعم . قيل لهم : فإذا كان هذا عدلاً فما أنكرتم أن يؤلمهم في الآخر ويكون ذلك منه عدلاً . فإن قالوا آلمهم في الدنيا ليعتبر بهم الآباء . عدلاً قيل لهم : فإذا فعل بهم ذلك في الدنيا ليعتبر بهم الآباء، وكان ذلك منه فلم لا يؤلم أطفال الكافرين في الآخرة ليغيظ بذلك آباءهم، ويكون ذلك منه عدلاً؟ وقد قيل في الخبر : ( إِنَّ أَطْفَالَ المُشْرِكِينَ تُؤَجَّجُ لَهُمْ نَارٌ يَوْمَ القِيامَة، ثُمَّ يُقَالُ لَهُمْ: اقْتَحِمُوهَا ، فَمَنْ اقْتَحَمَهَا أَدْخَلَهُ الجَنَّةَ، وَمَنْ لَمْ يَقْتَحِمْهَا أَدْخَلَهُ النَّارَ» (1) . وقد قيل في الأطفال، وروي عن النبي : ( إِن شئت أسْمَعْتُك صَغَاءَهُم في النَّارِ) (۲)
He said: and that was permissible and allowed. So if they say: Yes. It is said to them: If this is just, then why do you deny that He causes them pain in the Hereafter, and that is justice from Him? If they say: He caused them pain in this world so that fathers may take warning from them. It is said to them: If He did that to them in this world so that fathers may take warning from them, and that was justice from Him, then why does He not cause pain to the children of disbelievers in the Hereafter in order to grieve their fathers thereby, and that would be justice from Him? And it has been narrated: 'Indeed, the children of the polytheists will have a fire kindled for them on the Day of Resurrection, then it will be said to them: Jump into it. Whoever jumps into it will be admitted to Paradise, and whoever does not jump into it will be admitted to Hellfire.' And it has been said concerning children, and it is narrated from the Prophet: 'If you wish, I will make you hear their crying in the Fire.'
Source pages p. 139

Commentary
This passage is a dialectical argument against the Mu'tazila regarding the fate of children of polytheists. The author builds on a premise conceded by the opponent: that God's causing pain to children in this world is just because it serves as a lesson for fathers. The argument proceeds in the form of a dilemma: if they accept this premise, then they cannot logically deny that God may also cause pain to these children in the Hereafter for the purpose of grieving their fathers, which would likewise be just. The author then cites two hadiths to support the possibility of such punishment. The first hadith states that the children will be tested by a fire and their response determines their fate. The second hadith mentions the Prophet offering to make someone hear their wailing in the Fire. The argument thus shows that the Mu'tazila, who affirm justice in this-worldly suffering, are inconsistent if they reject otherworldly suffering for the same purpose.

Key Terms
عدل
أطفال المشركين
آخرة
الآباء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The passage proves that all children of polytheists will be punished in Hell.
Correction: The passage is a rebuttal of Mu'tazila's consistency on divine justice; it uses the possibility of punishment as a logical consequence of their own premise, not a definitive statement about all children.

Checkpoint
What is the main point of the argument about children of polytheists?
A) To prove that all children of polytheists go to Hell.
B) To show that God's justice is consistent in both this world and the Hereafter.
C) To establish that children are not accountable.
D) To refute the hadith about the fire.

Key Takeaway
Imam Ash'ari argues that if God's causing pain to children in this world is just as a lesson to fathers, then the same logic applies to the afterlife, supported by hadith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you see the suffering of innocent children in this world, do you truly believe God is just, or do you struggle with doubt? What would it take for you to lose that belief?
?
If God's justice allows for temporary suffering of children for a greater purpose (like warning fathers or grieving disbelievers), how does that shape your view of God's character? Are you willing to accept that His purposes may be beyond your understanding?
?
The hadith mentions a test where children must jump into fire on the Day of Resurrection. If you were in their place, would your trust in God's promise be strong enough to jump? Does your current faith prepare you for such a test?

The Divine Sealing of Hearts: A Proof of Allah's Creation of Disbelief
Imam al-Ash'ari argues from Quranic verses that Allah's sealing of hearts proves He creates disbelief.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses the Quranic concept of sealing of hearts to argue that Allah creates both guidance and misguidance, and that the inability to believe is itself a divine act.

Core Idea
Allah's sealing of hearts proves that He creates disbelief as an act of justice.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a powerful argument from the Quran: if Allah seals hearts and makes them tight, can He also claim to guide them? The contradiction forces us to acknowledge that Allah alone creates both faith and disbelief. This passage lays the foundation for the Ash'ari position on divine predestination and human accountability.

Guided Path
1
Read the Quranic verses cited
Start by reciting the verses about sealing (al-Baqarah 2:7) and guidance/misguidance (al-An'am 6:125). Reflect on what they seem to say about divine action and human response.
2
Identify the dilemma posed
Find where Imam al-Ash'ari asks the opponents whether those with sealed hearts are guided. Understand the logical contradiction if they answer 'yes'.
The key phrase: 'أتزعمون أنه هداهم وشرح للإسلام صدورهم وأضلهم؟'
3
Examine the objection and response
When opponents argue that sealing and expansion cannot coexist, see how Imam al-Ash'ari turns their logic around: guidance and misguidance also cannot coexist, so Allah must have sealed, not expanded, the disbelievers' hearts.
The reply: 'وكذلك الهدى لا يجتمع مع الضلال'
4
Draw the conclusion
Understand the final inference: since Allah created obstacles (coverings, locks) and then commanded faith, He commanded what they cannot do, proving that Allah creates their disbelief.
The phrase: 'وهذا يبين أن الله خلق كفرهم ومعاصيهم.'

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
يقال لهم : أليس قد قال الله عز وجل : خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَى قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَى سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلَى أَبْصَرِهِمْ غِشَوَةٌ ﴾ [البقرة : ۷] وقال عز وجل: فَمَن يُرِدِ اللهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ وَمَن يُرِدْ أَن يُضِلَهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقًا حَرَجًا) [الأنعام: ١٢٥] فخبرونا عن الذين ختم الله على قلوبهم وعلى سمعهم ، أتزعمون أنه هداهم وشرح للإسلام صدورهم وأضلهم؟ فإن قالوا: نعم، تناقض قولهم . وقيل لهم : كيف تكون الصدور مشروحة للإيمان وهي ضيقة حرجة مختوم عليها، وكيف يجمع القفل الذي قال الله عز وجل : أَمْ عَلَى قُلُوبٍ أَقْفَالُهَا ﴾ [محمد : ٢٤] مع الشرح، والضيق مع السعة، والهدى مع الضلال؟ إن كان هذا جائزاً، جاز أن يجتمع التوحيد والإلحاد الذي هو ضد التوحيد، والكفر والإيمان معاً في قلب واحد، وإن لم يجز هذا لم يجز ما قلتموه.
فإن قالوا: الختم والضيق والضلال لا يجوز أن يجتمع مع شرح الله الصدر
قيل لهم : وكذلك الهدى لا يجتمع مع الضلال، وإذا كان هكذا فما شرح الله صدور الكافرين للإيمان، بل ختم الله على قلوبهم، وأقفلهـا عـن الحق وشد عليها كما دعا نبي الله موسى عليه الصلاة والسلام على قومه فقال: ﴿رَبَّنَا أَطْمِس عَلَى أَمْوَالِهِمْ وَاشْدُدْ عَلَى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُوا حَتَّى يَرَوُا الْعَذَابَ الأليم ﴾ [يونس : ۸۸] ، وقال الله عز وجل: ﴿ قَدْ أُجِيبَت دَعْوَتُكُمَا ﴾ [يونس : ۸۹] وقال عز وجل يخبر عن الكافرين أنهم قالوا قُلُوبُنَا فِي أَكِنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَا إِلَيْهِ وَفِي عَاذَانِنَا وَقَرٌ وَمِنْ بَيْنِنَا وَبَيْنكَ حِجَابٌ [فصلت: ٥] فإذا خلق الله الأكنة في قلوبهم والقفل والزيغ والختم وضيق الصدر، ثم أمرهم بالإيمان الذي علم أنه لا يكون، فقد أمرهم بما لا يقدرون عليه، وإذا خلق الله في قلوبهم ما ذكرناه من الضيق عن الإيمان، فهل الضيق عن الإيمان إلا الكفر الذي في قلوبهم، وهذا يبين أن الله خلق كفرهم ومعاصيهم .
It is said to them: Has not Allah said: 'Allah has set a seal upon their hearts and upon their hearing, and over their vision is a veil' [al-Baqarah 2:7], and He said: 'So whoever Allah wants to guide, He expands his breast for Islam; and whoever He wants to misguide, He makes his breast tight and constricted' [al-An'am 6:125]. So inform us about those upon whom Allah has sealed their hearts and their hearing: do you claim that He guided them and expanded their breasts for Islam and misled them? If they say: Yes, their statement contradicts itself. And it is said to them: How can the breasts be expanded for faith while they are tight, constricted, and sealed, and how can the lock which Allah mentioned: 'Or are there locks upon their hearts?' [Muhammad 47:24] be combined with expansion, tightness with spaciousness, and guidance with misguidance? If this were permissible, it would be permissible for tawhid and ilhad (which is its opposite), and disbelief and faith to coexist in one heart. If this is not permissible, then what you have said is not permissible. If they say: The sealing, tightness, and misguidance cannot coexist with Allah's expansion of the breast. It is said to them: Likewise, guidance cannot coexist with misguidance. If it is so, then Allah did not expand the breasts of the disbelievers for faith; rather, He sealed their hearts, locked them against the truth, and tightened them, just as Allah's prophet Musa (peace be upon him) supplicated against his people: 'Our Lord, obliterate their wealth and tighten their hearts so that they will not believe until they see the painful punishment' [Yunus 10:88], and Allah said: 'Your supplication has been answered' [Yunus 10:89]. And Allah informs about the disbelievers that they said: 'Our hearts are in coverings from what you call us to, and in our ears is deafness, and between us and you is a veil' [Fussilat 41:5]. So if Allah created the coverings in their hearts, the lock, deviation, sealing, and tightness of the breast, then commanded them to faith which He knew would not happen, then He commanded them with what they are unable to do. And if Allah created in their hearts what we mentioned of tightness from faith, is tightness from faith anything other than the disbelief in their hearts? This shows that Allah created their disbelief and their acts of disobedience.
Source pages p. 141

Commentary
This passage is a sustained argument (mas'ala) regarding the divine sealing of hearts (al-khatm). It begins by citing Quranic verses about sealing and guidance/misguidance. The author poses a dilemma: do you claim that those whom Allah sealed were guided and expanded for Islam? If the opponents answer yes, they contradict themselves because sealing and guidance cannot coexist. The author then explains that combining sealing with expansion, tightness with spaciousness, and guidance with misguidance would imply that opposites like tawhid and ilhad, faith and disbelief can coexist in one heart, which is absurd. The opponents then object that sealing and misguidance cannot coexist with expansion. The author replies that guidance cannot coexist with misguidance either, and that Allah did not expand the breasts of disbelievers; rather, He sealed and locked them, citing the supplication of Musa and the verse about coverings. The conclusion is that Allah created the obstacles (coverings, locks, deviation, sealing, tightness) and then commanded them to faith, which they could not do, proving that Allah created their disbelief and sins.

Key Terms
ختم (khatm/sealing)
شرح الصدر (sharh al-sadr/expansion of breast)
ضيق (dayq/tightness)
قفل (qufl/lock)
أكنة (akinnah/coverings)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that Allah's sealing of hearts is purely metaphorical and that humans still have complete free will to believe or disbelieve.
Correction: Imam al-Ash'ari argues that the sealing is literal and creates an actual inability to believe, proving that Allah creates disbelief itself.

Checkpoint
What does Imam Ashari conclude from the fact that Allah created coverings and locks in the hearts of disbelievers then commanded them to faith?
A) That they have free will to overcome these obstacles.
B) That Allah commanded them to do something they are unable to do, proving He creates their disbelief.
C) That the coverings are only metaphorical and do not prevent faith.
D) That the disbelievers are not truly commanded to believe.

Key Takeaway
Allah's sealing of hearts is a real act that prevents belief, proving that He creates disbelief and that human actions are subject to His will.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that Allah seals hearts, do you sense any hardness in your own heart towards His commands? What will you do today to beg Him to expand your chest for faith?
?
If your faith is entirely from Allah's granting, does that humble you? How does knowing that He could have sealed your heart instead move you to gratitude and desperate prayer for steadfastness?
?
The tightness in the chest is itself disbelief. Where in your life do you feel resistance to obeying Allah? Will you ask Him to remove that tightness and replace it with expansion?

Refuting the Claim that Adalla Means to Name Misguided
Ashari refutes the claim that God's 'adalla' means calling misguided.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses Arabic verb form distinctions to refute the claim that Allah's 'adalla' merely means naming someone misguided.

Core Idea
The Arabic verb form 'adalla' (أضل) cannot mean 'to call misguided'—that is a different verb form.

Imam Ashari addresses an opponent who argues that when Allah 'adalla' someone, He only declares them misguided without creating misguidance. This lesson shows how Ashari turns to the Arabic language itself, which was the language of the Qur’an, to demonstrate that the verb 'adalla' does not have that meaning. By distinguishing between Arabic verb forms, Ashari invalidates the opponent's interpretation.

Guided Path
1
Acknowledge the Qur'an in Arabic
Notice that Ashari first gets the opponent to agree that the Qur'an was revealed in Arabic, establishing that Arabic usage is the criterion.
This is a common method in kalam: using agreed premises.
2
Examine the opponent's claim
The opponent claims that 'adalla' can mean 'to name someone misguided.' Look at how they try to support this by referencing 'dallala'.
They confuse Form II with Form IV.
3
See the linguistic distinction
Ashari shows that 'dallala' is used for naming, but 'adalla' is not. Therefore the Qur'anic use of 'yudillu' cannot mean naming.
This refutation relies purely on Arabic usage.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قيل لهم : أليس خاطب الله العرب بلغتهم فقال : بِلِسَانٍ عربي مبين ) [الشعراء: ١٩٥] وقال: ﴿ وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسَانِ قَوْمِهِ ) [إبراهيم: ٤]؟
فلا بد من نعم .
فيقال لهم : فإذا كان الله عز وجل أنزل القرآن بلسان العرب، فمن أين وجدتم في لغة العرب أن يقال: أضل فلان فلاناً، أي سماه ضالاً؟
فإن قالوا: وجدنا القائل يقول : إذا قال رجل لرجل: ضال: قد ضللته قيل لهم : قد وجدنا العرب يقولون : ضلل فلان فلاناً إذا سماه ضالاً، ولم نجدهم يقولون: أضل فلان فلاناً بهذا المعنى، فلما قال الله عز وجل: ﴿وَيُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الظَّالِمِينَ ) لم يجز أن يكون ذلك معنى الاسم والحكم إذا لم يجز في لغة العرب أن يقال : أضل فلان فلاناً، إذا سماه ضالاً، بطل تأويلكم إذا كان خلاف لسان العرب
It was said to them: Did Allah not address the Arabs in their tongue, saying: 'In a clear Arabic tongue' [Ash-Shu'ara': 195] and said: 'And We did not send any messenger except in the language of his people' [Ibrahim: 4]? So they must say yes. Then it is said to them: Since Allah, Mighty and Majestic, revealed the Qur'an in the tongue of the Arabs, from where did you find in the Arabic language that it is said: 'So-and-so adalla so-and-so,' meaning he named him misguided? If they say: We found the speaker saying: When a man says to a man: 'You are misguided,' he has 'dallalahu' [called him misguided]. It is said to them: We have found the Arabs saying: 'dallala so-and-so so-and-so' when he named him misguided, but we did not find them saying: 'adalla so-and-so so-and-so' with this meaning. So when Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'And Allah leads astray [yudillu] the wrongdoers' [Ibrahim: 27], it is not permissible that this be the meaning of the noun and the judgment, since it is not permissible in the Arabic language to say: 'adalla so-and-so so-and-so' meaning he named him misguided. Your interpretation is invalidated because it contradicts the language of the Arabs.
Source pages p. 150

Commentary
This passage is a linguistic refutation of an opposing view. The opponent claims that when Allah is said to 'adalla' someone, it means He merely named them 'misguided' (i.e., declared them to be so), without actually creating misguidance in them. The Ashari counters by first getting the opponent to admit that the Qur'an was revealed in the Arabic language. Then he asks: Where in Arabic usage do you find the verb 'adalla' (Form IV) used to mean 'to name someone misguided'? The opponent attempts to claim that if one says 'dallala' (Form II) with that meaning, it is synonymous. The Ashari points out that the Arabs do use 'dallala' (Form II) for 'naming someone misguided', but they do not use 'adalla' (Form IV) in that sense. Since Allah uses 'yudillu' (Form IV) in the Qur'an, it cannot carry the meaning of 'naming' because that would contradict Arabic usage. Therefore the opponent's interpretation is invalid.

Key Terms
أضل
ضلل
لسان العرب
تأويل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The verb 'adalla' (أضل) can be used in Arabic to mean 'to name someone misguided'.
Correction: The correct usage: The verb 'dallala' (ضلل) is used for naming, while 'adalla' is not. The Qur'an uses 'adalla' (يضل) which means to cause misguidance, not to name.

Checkpoint
What Arabic verb form does the opponent claim 'adalla' is equivalent to for the meaning 'to name someone misguided'?
A) Form IV (أفعل)
B) Form II (فعّل)
C) Form I (فعل)
D) Form III (فاعل)

Key Takeaway
In Arabic, verb forms carry distinct meanings; 'adalla' (Form IV) does not mean 'to name someone misguided'—that is expressed by 'dallala' (Form II).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read a verse like 'And Allah leads astray the wrongdoers,' do you instinctively try to soften its meaning to fit your own sense of fairness? Where in your heart do you resist the plain linguistic meaning that Allah creates misguidance?
?
This lesson shows how a small verb form difference crushes a whole theological argument. In what areas of your faith have you been careless with precision? Will you commit to studying Arabic terms more deeply before forming opinions about Allah's actions?
?
If you were personally debating someone who misinterpreted a Qur'anic verse due to ignorance of Arabic, would you have the courage to correct them? What holds you back from speaking truth when it challenges popular views?
