The Ocean of Ink: Proving the Eternity of God's Speech
Imam Ashari uses Quran 18:109 to argue that God's speech is inexhaustible and eternal.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Quran 18:109 is used by Imam Ashari to prove that Allah's speech is eternal and not subject to finitude.

Core Idea
God's speech cannot be exhausted, proving it is eternal and uncreated.

Imam Ashari turns to a powerful Quranic verse to demonstrate that Allah's speech is infinite. If all the seas were ink, they would run out before His words. This vivid imagery not only shows the vastness of divine speech but also its eternal nature, against the Jahmiyya who claimed it was created and finite.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and its argument
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice how the verse sets up a conditional scenario: if the sea were ink, it would be exhausted before the words of Allah.
The verse is from Surah Al-Kahf (18:109).
2
Understand the logical implication
Imam Ashari draws a logical conclusion: if the sea cannot exhaust Allah's words, then His speech is not subject to finitude. This implies that speech cannot end or perish.
Compare with divine knowledge which is also infinite.
3
Connect to divine attributes
If speech could be exhausted, it would imply a defect or silence in Allah, which is impossible. Therefore, Allah has always been speaking (eternal attribute).
The attribute of speech is like knowledge – neither can be finite.
4
Recognize the refutation of Jahmiyya
The Jahmiyya claimed that Allah's speech is created and finite. This verse refutes that by showing it is impossible for His words to be exhausted.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال الله عز وجل : ( قُل لَّوْ كَانَ الْبَحْرُ مِدَادًا لِكَلِمَتِ رَبِّي لَنَفِدَ الْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَن تَنقَدَ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّي ﴾ [الكهف : ١٠٩] فلو كانت البحار مداداً كتبت لنفدت البحار وتكسرت الأقلام، ولم يلحق الفناء كلمات ربي، كما لا يلحق الفناء علــم الله عز وجل، ومن فني كلامه لحقته الآفات وجرى عليه السكوت، فلما لم يجز ذلك على ربنا عز وجل صح أنه لم يزل متكلماً؛ لأنه لو لم يكن متكلماً، لوجب عليه السكوت والآفات تعالى ربنا عن قول الجهمية علواً كبيراً .
And Allah Almighty said: 'Say, if the sea were ink for the words of my Lord, the sea would be exhausted before the words of my Lord are exhausted.' [Al-Kahf:109] So if the seas were ink and written, the seas would be exhausted and the pens would break, and the words of my Lord would not be overtaken by finitude, just as the knowledge of Allah Almighty is not overtaken by finitude. And whoever's speech perishes, defects overtake him and silence occurs to him. So when that is not permissible for our Lord Almighty, it is correct that He has not ceased to be speaking; because if He were not speaking, silence and defects would be necessary for Him—exalted is our Lord above the saying of the Jahmiyya, a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 74

Commentary
This passage is a scriptural proof from Quran 18:109. Imam Ashari cites the verse where Allah says that if the ocean were ink for His words, the ocean would run out before the words run out. He then argues: if the seas were ink and all writing were done, the words of Allah would not be exhausted. This demonstrates that His speech is not subject to finitude or exhaustion. Exhaustion would imply that His speech could end, which would necessitate silence or defect—attributes impossible for Allah. Therefore, Allah has always been speaking (eternally). He concludes with a condemnation of the Jahmiyya who claim otherwise.

Key Terms
كلمات ربي (kalimat Rabbi)
الفناء (al-fana')
السكوت (al-sukut)
الجهمية (al-Jahmiyya)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The verse means that Allah's words are many but not necessarily eternal.
Correction: Imam Ashari uses the verse to prove that Allah's words are not subject to finitude, hence they are eternal and uncreated.

Checkpoint
What does Imam Ashari conclude from the verse about the ocean of ink?
A) That God's words are many but can be written
B) That God's words are uncreated and eternal
C) That God's speech is created and finite
D) That the ocean is a metaphor for creation

Key Takeaway
Allah's speech is eternal and not subject to finitude, as demonstrated by the inexhaustibility of His words.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God’s speech is eternal and inexhaustible, what does that mean for how you approach the Quran? Do you read it as a finite book with a fixed meaning, or as an infinite ocean that demands your constant humility and seeking? Will you today begin to read even one verse with the conviction that its depths can never be exhausted?
?
Imam Ashari says that if God were not always speaking, silence would be a defect. How does it change your awareness of God to know that He is always speaking? Does that thought make you more accountable to listen to His words? When you remain silent before His verses, do you realize you are meeting the Eternal Speaker? What will you do tomorrow morning to truly listen?
?
The Jahmiyya claimed God’s speech is created, but the verse proves otherwise. Have you ever internally limited God by thinking His words could be captured or finished? When you memorize a surah or finish a translation, do you feel you have 'done' your duty? Or do you feel the weight of the infinite call to engage with the Speaking Lord? What will you change so that your interaction with the Quran reflects its eternity?

Refuting the Jahmiyya: The Absurdity of a Speaking Tree
Imam Ashari refutes the Jahmiyya claim by reductio ad absurdum, comparing them to Christians and citing a Quranic verse.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses logical absurdity to refute the Jahmiyya claim that God's speech was created and dwelled in a tree, exposing the theological error of attributing divine speech to a creature.

Core Idea
If God's speech were in a tree, the tree would be claiming divinity—a clear absurdity.

Imam Ashari confronts a radical deviation of the Jahmiyya sect, who claimed that God's speech—the Quran—was created and temporarily lodged in a tree (the burning bush). In this passage, he exposes the logical consequence of their view: the tree itself would have to be the speaker of 'I am Allah,' reducing their claim to absurdity. He also contrasts them with Christians, showing they went even further.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation. Let the force of the argument come through.
Pay attention to the comparison with Christians at the beginning.
2
Identify the comparison with Christians
Notice how Ashari links the Jahmiyya to the Christians, then shows the Jahmiyya went further by claiming God's speech was created and entered a tree.
This comparison highlights the extreme nature of the Jahmiyya claim.
3
Spot the reductio ad absurdum
Find the logical consequence: if speech is created in a tree, then the tree itself would be the speaker of 'I am Allah.' This is an impossibility.
A created thing cannot claim to be the Creator.
4
Connect the Quranic verse
Read the verse from Surah As-Sajda (32:13) that Ashari cites. It affirms that Allah's word is from Him, not created in a creature.
The verse reinforces that divine speech is uncreated and eternal.
5
Reflect on the conclusion
Understand Ashari's final point: just as Allah's knowledge cannot be created in a creature, His speech cannot be created in a tree. This upholds the transcendence of Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل : وزعمت الجهمية كما زعمت النصارى ؛ لأن النصارى زعمت أن كلمة الله حواها بطن مريم وزادت الجهمية عليهم ؛ فزعمت أن كلام الله مخلوق حل في شجرة، وكانت الشجرة حاوية له؛ فلزمهم أن تكون الشجرة بذلك الكلام متكلمةً، ووجب عليهم أن مخلوقاً من المخلوقين كلّم موسى صلى الله عليه وسلم، وأن الشجرة قالت: يا موسى إنَّني أنا الله لا إله إلا أنا فاعبدني، فلو كان كلام الله مخلوقاً في شجرة، لكان المخلوق قال : يا موسى إنَّني أنا الله لا إله إلا أنا فاعبدني، وقد قال الله عز وجل: وَلَكِن حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِن لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ ﴾ [السجدة : ١٣] . وكلام الله من الله عز وجل لا يجوز أن يكون كلامه الذي هو منه مخلوقاً في شجرة مخلوقة، كما لا يجوز أن يكون علمه الذي هو منه مخلوقاً في غيره، تعالى الله ذلك علواً كبيراً .
Section: And the Jahmiyya claimed just as the Christians claimed; because the Christians claimed that the word of Allah was contained in the womb of Mary, and the Jahmiyya added to them: they claimed that the speech of Allah is created and settled in a tree, and the tree was containing it. So it necessarily follows for them that the tree, by that speech, is speaking, and it is necessary for them that a created thing among the created beings spoke to Moses (peace be upon him), and that the tree said: 'O Moses, indeed I am Allah, there is no god but Me, so worship Me.' For if the speech of Allah were created in a tree, then the created thing would have said: 'O Moses, indeed I am Allah, there is no god but Me, so worship Me.' And Allah Almighty said: 'But the word from Me has been justified: I will surely fill Hell with jinn and people all together.' [As-Sajda:13] And the speech of Allah is from Allah Almighty; it is not permissible that His speech, which is from Him, be created in a created tree, just as it is not permissible that His knowledge, which is from Him, be created in other than Him. Exalted is Allah above that, a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 74

Commentary
This passage is a refutation by reductio ad absurdum. Imam Ashari compares the Jahmiyya to Christians: Christians said the word of Allah (Jesus) was contained in Mary's womb; the Jahmiyya outdid them by claiming that Allah's speech was created and entered a tree (the burning bush from which Moses heard the voice). He then draws the necessary consequence: if the speech were created in the tree, then the tree itself would be speaking and claiming divinity: 'I am Allah.' This would mean a created object is declaring itself to be the Creator—extreme absurdity. Ashari also cites Quran 32:13 to assert that the word of Allah is from Him and cannot be created in a creature, just as His knowledge cannot be created in a creature.

Key Terms
الجهمية (al-Jahmiyya)
النصارى (al-Nasara)
حل (halla)
شجرة (shajara)
كلمة الله (kalimat Allah)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the voice Moses heard from the tree was a created speech attributed to Allah.
Correction: Ashari shows that if speech were created in the tree, the tree itself would be claiming divinity—an absurdity. Therefore, the speech must be uncreated and directly from Allah.

Checkpoint
What absurdity does Imam Ashari point out in the Jahmiyya claim that God's speech dwelled in a tree?
A) The tree would have to be holy
B) A created thing (the tree) would be claiming 'I am Allah'
C) Moses would have spoken to an angel
D) The tree would be eternal

Key Takeaway
Divine speech cannot be created in a creature, for that would lead to a creature claiming divinity—an impossibility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The Jahmiyya thought they were honoring Allah by saying His speech is created, but Ashari showed it leads to a creature claiming divinity. Where in your own thinking have you tried to 'protect' Allah's majesty with reasoning that actually diminishes it? What assumption do you need to repent from today?
?
If you heard a tree say 'I am Allah', would you believe it? How does this lesson train you to recognize false claims about Allah's attributes even when they sound reverent? What does your response reveal about your understanding of tawhid?
?
The lesson ends by declaring Allah exalted above such claims. Does your heart truly feel that exaltation, or do you still harbor thoughts that diminish His majesty? Write down one practical step you will take today to increase your reverence for Allah's speech.

Answer to Jahmiyya: God's Speech Cannot Be Created in a Creature
Ashari uses an analogy with God's will to argue against created speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God's speech cannot be created in a creature, using the parallel argument with God's will.

Core Idea
God's speech cannot be created in a creature because that would make the creature the speaker.

Imam Ashari refutes the Jahmiyya claim that God's speech was created in a tree by drawing a parallel with God's will. Just as God's will cannot be placed in a creature without that creature becoming the wielder of that will, God's speech cannot be placed in a creature without that creature becoming the speaker. This argument preserves God's transcendence and the uniqueness of His attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Pay attention to the structure: the first part presents the analogy with God's will, and the second part applies it to speech.
2
Identify the analogy
Notice that Ashari begins by stating a parallel: just as it is not permissible for Allah to create His will in a creature, so too it is not permissible to create His speech. The underlying principle is that attributes of Allah cannot become attributes of a creature.
The Jahmiyya claimed Allah's speech was created in a tree (as a sound), but Ashari argues this would make the tree the speaker.
3
Understand the reductio ad absurdum
Ashari then shows the consequence: if Allah created His will in a creature, that creature would be the one willing. Similarly, if Allah created His speech in a creature, that creature would be the one speaking. Both are impossible because it would attribute divine attributes to created beings.
This is a logical argument that shows the Jahmiyya position leads to an absurd conclusion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : كما لا يجوز أن يخلق الله عز وجل إرادته في بعض المخلوقات، كذلك لا يجوز أن يخلق كلامه في بعض المخلوقات، ولو كانت إرادة الله مخلوقة في بعض المخلوقات، لكان ذلك المخلوق هو المريد بها، وذلك يستحيل .
وكذلك يستحيل أن يخلق الله كلامه في مخلوق؛ لأن هذا يوجب أن ذلك المخلوق متكلم به ويستحيل أن يكون كلام الله عز وجل كلاماً للمخلوق .
And it is said to them: Just as it is not permissible for Allah Almighty to create His will in some created things, likewise it is not permissible for Him to create His speech in some created things. And if the will of Allah were created in some created things, then that created thing would be the one willing by it, and that is impossible. And likewise it is impossible for Allah to create His speech in a created thing; because this necessitates that that created thing is speaking by it, and it is impossible for the speech of Allah Almighty to be speech of a created thing.
Source pages p. 75

Commentary
This passage is an argument from parallel and reductio ad absurdum. Imam Ashari presents a rebuttal: just as it is impermissible for Allah to create His will in a creature (since that would make the creature the actual wielder of that will), it is equally impermissible to create His speech in a creature. If He did, the creature would become the speaker of that speech, meaning Allah's speech would become the speech of a creation—an impossibility. Therefore, the Jahmiyya claim that Allah's speech was created in the tree is false.

Key Terms
إرادة الله (iradat Allah)
مخلوق (makhluq)
متكلم (mutakallim)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God can create His attributes in creatures without transferring the attribute.
Correction: Ashari shows that if an attribute is created in a creature, the creature becomes the subject of that attribute, which is impossible for divine attributes.

Checkpoint
What analogy does Imam Ashari use to refute the claim that God's speech can be created in a creature?
A) God's knowledge cannot be created
B) God's will cannot be created in a creature
C) God's power cannot be created
D) God's mercy cannot be created

Key Takeaway
God's speech cannot be created in a creature because that would make the creature the speaker, which is impossible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Quran, do you truly believe that you are uttering Allah's uncreated speech, not a mere created thing? How does this belief affect the reverence and focus in your recitation? What will you do today to increase your awe of the Quran as Allah's very words?
?
The Jahmiyya tried to rationalize God's speech as created to fit their theology. Are there any attributes of Allah that you have been tempted to reduce or explain away to make them more 'understandable'? How can you instead submit to the reality of His perfect attributes without alteration?
?
If you ever catch yourself treating the Quran as just a text among many, or doubting its eternal nature, you are walking the path of the Jahmiyya. What concrete step will you take this week to remind yourself of the Quran's divine origin and uncreatedness?

Proof from Quran That Quran is Not Created
Scriptural proof by implication from Quran 74:25.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses a Quranic verse to argue that the Quran is not created by equating the claim of creation with the polytheists' claim that it is human speech.

Core Idea
Calling the Quran created equals calling it human speech—which Allah condemned.

Imam Ashari presents a concise scriptural proof. He cites the Quran where Allah reports the polytheists dismissing the Quran as 'only the speech of a human being.' Ashari then draws a logical implication: anyone who says the Quran is created is in fact agreeing with the polytheists, making the Quran human speech. Since Allah condemned that statement, the position that the Quran is created is invalid. Prepare to see how Ashari uses this simple yet powerful argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Ashari uses a very direct scriptural citation.
2
Identify the Quranic verse and its context
The verse is from Surah Al-Muddaththir (74:25). It reports what the polytheists said about the Quran. Understand that this is a statement Allah is condemning.
3
Understand Ashari's logical move
Ashari equates 'created' with 'human speech'. He argues that anyone who says the Quran is created is effectively saying it is human speech, because created things are not divine speech.
This is a key implication: creation implies being from humans, not from Allah.
4
Note the implication of divine condemnation
Since Allah condemned the polytheists for that statement, anyone who repeats it (by calling the Quran created) falls under the same condemnation. Therefore, the Quran cannot be created.
This is a reductio ad absurdum.
5
Reflect on the argument's strength
Consider why this scriptural proof is compelling. It ties the theological position directly to a Quranic verse and shows that the createdness claim contradicts Allah's own words.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومما يبطل قولهم أن الله عز وجل قال مخبراً عن المشركين أنهم قالوا: ) إِنْ هَذَا إِلَّا قَولُ الْبَشَرِ ﴾ [المدثر : ۲۵] يعني القرآن فمن زعم أن القرآن مخلوق فقد جعله قولاً للبشر، وهذا ما أنكره الله على المشركين .
And among what invalidates their statement is that Allah Almighty said, informing about the polytheists that they said: 'This is not but the speech of a human being' [Al-Muddaththir:25] meaning the Quran. So whoever claims that the Quran is created has made it the speech of humans, and this is what Allah denied upon the polytheists.
Source pages p. 75

Commentary
This passage is a scriptural proof by implication. Imam Ashari cites the Quran where Allah reports that the polytheists dismissed the Quran as mere human speech. He then argues that anyone who says the Quran is created is essentially agreeing with the polytheists and saying it is human speech. Since Allah condemned the polytheists for this statement, claiming the Quran is created is a false and blameworthy position. Therefore, the Quran is not created.

Key Terms
قول البشر (qawl al-bashar)
القرآن (al-Quran)
مخلوق (makhluq)
المشركين (al-mushrikeen)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that 'created' is different from 'human speech', so equating them is a false analogy.
Correction: Ashari's argument does not rely on a mere analogy; it uses the logical implication that if something is created, it is not divine and thus must be from a created source. The polytheists explicitly called the Quran 'human speech' and Allah condemned them. So claiming creation leads to the same condemnation.

Checkpoint
How does Imam Ashari use the disbelievers' statement to refute the claim that the Quran is created?
A) He says the disbelievers were correct
B) He says claiming the Quran is created is similar to claiming it is human speech, which Allah condemned
C) He says the disbelievers were ignorant
D) He says the Quran is both created and uncreated

Key Takeaway
Imam Ashari argues that claiming the Quran is created is equivalent to the polytheists' statement that it is human speech, which Allah condemned, so the Quran cannot be created.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear someone say the Quran is just a human book, does this lesson make you feel the weight of Allah's condemnation? How will you respond next time you encounter such a statement?
?
Do you ever treat the Quran as if it were created—something you can casually ignore or criticize? If you truly believe it is Allah's uncreated speech, what habit must you change starting today?

The Burning Bush Argument: Why God's Speech Cannot Be Created
Al-Ash'ari refutes creation of speech using the tree that spoke to Moses.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand al-Ash'ari's argument that God's speech cannot be created in a creature, using the example of the tree that spoke to Moses.

Core Idea
If God's speech were created in a tree, the tree would claim to be God—impossible.

In this passage, al-Ash'ari presents a powerful reductio ad absurdum against the Jahmiyya claim that God's speech is created. He uses the well-known story of the burning bush where a tree speaks to Moses. By examining what it would mean for God's speech to be 'in' that tree, he exposes a theological absurdity.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then its literal translation. Let the argument's flow become clear.
The Arabic may have an OCR issue at the start; the translation helps.
2
Identify the refuted claim
Notice that the passage argues against the idea that God's speech is created (makhluq) and can be placed in a creature.
3
Follow the logical steps
Trace the argument: if speech were created in the tree, then the tree would be the speaker of 'I am Allah'—an obvious absurdity since a tree cannot be divine.
This is a reductio ad absurdum.
4
Examine the Quranic support
Al-Ash'ari quotes Quran 32:13 to show that God's speech is 'from Him', not from a created thing.
This reinforces that speech is an attribute of God.
5
Connect to the conclusion
Understand that the passage concludes that God's speech cannot be created in another, just as His knowledge cannot be created in another.
This establishes speech as an eternal attribute.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
حل في شجرة، وكانت الشجرة حاوية له؛ فلزمهم أن تكون الشجرة بذلك الكلام متكلمةً، ووجب عليهم أن مخلوقاً من المخلوقين كلّم موسى صلى الله عليه وسلم، وأن الشجرة قالت: يا موسى إنَّني أنا الله لا إله إلا أنا فاعبدني، فلو كان كلام الله مخلوقاً في شجرة، لكان المخلوق قال : يا موسى إنَّني أنا الله لا إله إلا أنا فاعبدني، وقد قال الله عز وجل: وَلَكِن حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِن لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ ﴾ [السجدة : ١٣] . وكلام الله من الله عز وجل لا يجوز أن يكون كلامه الذي هو منـه مخلوقاً في شجرة مخلوقة، كما لا يجوز أن يكون علمه الذي هو منه مخلوقاً في غيره، تعالى الله ذلك علواً كبيراً .
It was placed in a tree, and the tree contained it; thus it is necessary for them that the tree by that speech was speaking, and it is obligatory for them that a created being among creation spoke to Moses, peace be upon him, and that the tree said: 'O Moses, indeed I am Allah, there is no god but Me, so worship Me.' So if the speech of Allah were created in a tree, then the created thing would have said: 'O Moses, indeed I am Allah, there is no god but Me, so worship Me.' And Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'But the word from Me was justified: I will surely fill Hell with jinn and people all together.' [Sajdah 32:13]. And the speech of Allah is from Allah, Mighty and Majestic; it is not permissible that His speech which is from Him be created in a created tree, just as it is not permissible that His knowledge which is from Him be created in another, exalted is Allah above that, a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 75

Commentary
This passage presents a proof by reductio ad absurdum against the Jahmiyya claim that God's speech is created in a creature. The author reasons: if God's speech were created in a tree (as in the story of Moses), then the tree would be the actual speaker of the divine words 'I am Allah' — an absurdity because a created tree cannot be the speaker of divinity. The author supports this with Quran 32:13, which affirms that God's word is from Him, not from a creature. The conclusion is that God's speech cannot be created in anything else, just as His knowledge cannot be created in another. Thus, speech is an eternal attribute of God, not a created accident in a substrate.

Key Terms
كلام الله
مخلوق
شجرة
متكلمة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that if God creates speech in a tree, the tree only conveys it without actually speaking—the speech remains God's.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari insists that if the speech is created in the tree, the tree is the subject of the speech act, making it the speaker of 'I am Allah', which is impossible for a creature.

Checkpoint
What is the core absurdity al-Ash'ari points out about the Jahmiyya view that God's speech was created in the tree that spoke to Moses?
A) The tree would have to be eternal.
B) The tree would be the speaker, claiming divinity.
C) The tree would become a prophet.
D) The tree would cease to exist.

Key Takeaway
God's speech cannot be created in a creature because that would make the creature the speaker of divine words, an impossibility; therefore, speech is an eternal attribute of Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If a mere tree could not claim divinity, then how can you give the status of 'Lord' to anything other than Allah in your heart—whether it be wealth, status, or a person? Will you repent today and realign your sole devotion to the One who alone deserves worship?
?
You recite the Quran daily, but do you truly believe it is the uncreated speech of Allah, or do you treat it as just another book? What would change in your reverence, attention, and obedience if you were fully convinced that every word originates directly from Allah?
?
Al-Ash'ari argues that if speech were created, the tree would be the speaker. Is there any area of your life where you let creation speak for God, adding or changing His commands to suit your desires? How will you ensure that only Allah's words—through His revelation—guide your actions today?

The Impossibility of Creating God's Speech in a Creature
Al-Ash'ari draws an analogy between God's will and speech to refute creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why al-Ash'ari argues that God's speech, like His will, cannot be created in a creature without attributing creaturehood to the divine attribute.

Core Idea
God's speech cannot be created in a creature because that would make the creature the actual speaker.

Al-Ash'ari continues his refutation of those who claim God's speech can be created in creatures. He draws a powerful analogy: just as it is impossible for God to place His will in a creature without that creature becoming the one who wills, it is likewise impossible for God to place His speech in a creature without that creature becoming the speaker. This brief but incisive argument preserves the transcendence of God's attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text slowly, noting the parallel structure between will and speech.
2
Identify the analogy
Notice how al-Ash'ari compares the impossibility of creating will with the impossibility of creating speech.
3
Understand the consequence
Grasp that if God's attribute were created in a creature, the creature would be the subject of that attribute, which is impossible.
4
Reflect on the implication
Think about why this argument safeguards God's unique attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : كما لا يجوز أن يخلق الله عز وجل إرادته في بعض المخلوقات، كذلك لا يجوز أن يخلق كلامه في بعض المخلوقات، ولو كانت إرادة الله مخلوقة في بعض المخلوقات، لكان ذلك المخلوق الله هو المريد بها، وذلك يستحيل . وكذلك يستحيل أن يخلق الله كلامه في مخلوق؛ لأن هذا يوجب أن ذلك المخلوق متكلم به ويستحيل أن يكون كلام الله عز وجل كلاماً للمخلوق .
And it is said to them: Just as it is not permissible that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, create His will in some created things, likewise it is not permissible that He create His speech in some created things. And if the will of Allah were created in some created things, then that created thing would be the one willing by it, and that is impossible. And likewise, it is impossible that Allah create His speech in a created thing, because this necessitates that that created thing is speaking with it, and it is impossible that the speech of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, be speech of a created thing.
Source pages p. 75

Commentary
This passage is a response (jawaab) that uses an analogical argument. The author compares speech to will: just as it is impossible for God to create His will in a creature (because then the creature would be the one who wills, making it like God), it is also impossible for God to create His speech in a creature. If God's speech were created in a creature, that creature would be the speaker of those words, and the speech would belong to the creature, not to God. This is impossible because God's speech is an attribute of His essence, not a created accident in another being.

Key Terms
إرادة
كلام
مخلوق
مريد
متكلم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that God's speech can be created in a creature as sounds are created in the air.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari argues that if speech were created in a creature, the creature would be the actual speaker, which is impossible for God's eternal attribute.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, what would happen if God created His will in a created thing?
A) The created thing would become good.
B) The created thing would be the one willing by it.
C) The will would become independent.
D) God would lose His will.

Key Takeaway
God's speech, like His will, is an eternal attribute that cannot be created in a creature without violating His transcendence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that the Quran you recite is the uncreated speech of Allah, or have you ever treated it as just another book? How would your reverence change if you harbored even a grain of doubt about its eternal nature?
?
If God's attributes cannot be transferred to any creature, what does that mean for how you view the Qur'an as a physical object (paper and ink)? Is your heart attached to the divine attribute or the mere creation?
?
When you hear verses about Allah's will and speech, do you humbly accept that they are beyond your full comprehension, or do you secretly reduce them to human categories? What repentance might that require?

The Quran Is Not Human Speech: Proof from Surah 74:25
Al-Ash'ari uses Quran 74:25 to prove the Quran is not created.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses the Quranic condemnation of polytheists to prove that the Quran is uncreated.

Core Idea
If the Quran were created, it would be human speech—and Allah condemned that.

In this brief but powerful argument, al-Ash'ari turns the accusation of the polytheists into a proof. The polytheists dismissed the Quran as mere human speech; Allah recorded their statement and condemned it. Al-Ash'ari argues that anyone who claims the Quran is created is making the same claim, thereby falling under the same condemnation. This shows that the Quran must be uncreated divine speech.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic and English carefully. Note that al-Ash'ari quotes the verse directly.
The verse is Surah al-Muddaththir 74:25.
2
Identify the logical structure
The argument is: (1) The polytheists said the Quran is human speech. (2) Allah condemned that statement. (3) Saying the Quran is created is equivalent to saying it is human speech. (4) Therefore, saying it is created is condemned.
This is a reductio ad absurdum.
3
Reflect on the implication
Understand why al-Ash'ari thinks 'created' equals 'human speech'. In his theology, only created beings produce created speech; divine speech must be uncreated.
This connects to the broader doctrine of the uncreated Quran.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومما يبطل قولهم أن الله عز وجل قال مخبراً عن المشركين أنهم قالوا: إِنْ هَذَا إِلَّا قَولُ الْبَشَرِ ﴾ [المدثر : ۲۵] يعني القرآن فمن زعم أن القرآن مخلوق فقد جعله قولاً للبشر، وهذا ما أنكره الله على المشركين .
And among what invalidates their saying is that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said informing about the polytheists that they said: 'This is nothing but the word of a human' [Muddaththir 74:25], meaning the Quran. So whoever claims that the Quran is created has made it the speech of humans, and this is what Allah denied upon the polytheists.
Source pages p. 75

Commentary
This is another proof (daleel aakhar) against the Jahmiyya. Al-Ash'ari cites Quran 74:25, where the polytheists accused the Quran of being mere human speech. He argues that by claiming the Quran is created, the Jahmiyya are effectively agreeing with the polytheists' accusation, which Allah condemned. Therefore, the Quran cannot be created; it is the uncreated speech of Allah.

Key Terms
قول البشر
مخلوق
قرآن
مشركين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that calling the Quran 'created' does not imply it is human speech, since Allah creates many things that are not human speech.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari argues that the Quran is speech (kalam), and if it is created, it must be the speech of a created being (human), because only created beings produce created speech. Divine speech is uncreated.

Checkpoint
What does al-Ash'ari say about those who claim the Quran is created?
A) They are correct because the Quran is in Arabic.
B) They are equating the Quran with human speech, which Allah denied.
C) They affirm the Quran is from Allah.
D) They are ignorant of grammar.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari proves the Quran's uncreatedness by showing that claiming it is created aligns with the polytheists' statement that Allah condemned.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Quran, do you truly believe you are hearing the uncreated speech of Allah, or do you sometimes treat it as just another book? What would change in your recitation if you were certain you were encountering Allah's eternal speech?
?
The polytheists denied the Quran's divine origin and attributed it to human invention. How often do you, by neglecting its guidance, effectively treat the Quran as if it were merely human words? What would it take for you to submit to its authority as divine speech?

The Logical Necessity of God's Eternal Speech
Al-Ash'ari argues that God must be eternally speaking for creation to be by His command.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God's speech must be eternal for creation to occur by His command.

Core Idea
God's eternal speech is necessary for creation to be by His command.

Al-Ash'ari presents a concise logical argument against the view that God's attribute of speech began at creation. Using the Qur'anic command 'Be' as evidence, he shows that if God were not always speaking, creation would have occurred without His word, contradicting scripture and the consensus of Ahl al-Sunnah.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that this is a conditional argument: if X, then Y.
2
Understand the conditional argument
Identify the condition (God not speaking before creation) and the consequence (things came to be without His command).
The argument's power lies in the logical contradiction with the Quran.
3
Identify the Qur'anic basis
Al-Ash'ari references the divine command 'Be' (kun) from the Quran. Consider why this command implies God's eternal speech.
Quran 36:82 is the implicit proof text.
4
See the consequence
If the consequence is false (i.e., it contradicts Quran and consensus), then the condition must be false. Conclude that God must be eternally speaking.
This is a reductio ad absurdum argument.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأيضاً فلو لم يكن الله متكلماً حتى خلق الخلق ثم تكلم بعد ذلك، لكانت الأشياء قد كانت لا عن أمره، ولا عن قوله، ولم يكن قائلاً لها: كوني »، وهذا رد للقرآن وخروج عما عليه جمهور أهل الإسلام .
And also, if Allah were not speaking until He created creation, then spoke after that, then things would have come into being not by His command, nor by His word, and He would not have said to them 'Be'. And this is a rejection of the Quran and a departure from what the majority of the people of Islam are upon.
Source pages p. 76

Commentary
This passage presents a logical consequence of the Jahmiyya view that God began speaking only after creation. If God were silent before creation, then the first act of creation would have occurred without His command 'Be' (kun). But the Quran states that what He wills is to say 'Be' and it is (Quran 36:82). Therefore, the Jahmiyya position contradicts the Quran and the consensus (jumhoor) of Ahl al-Sunnah.

Key Terms
متكلماً
أمره
قوله
كوني
جمهور أهل الإسلام

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's speech is a temporal event that began at creation.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari shows that if God's speech began at creation, then the first act of creation would have occurred without His command 'Be', which contradicts the Quran.

Checkpoint
What problem does al-Ash'ari identify if God only began speaking after creation?
A) God would be weak.
B) Creation would have occurred without God's word 'Be'.
C) Angels would have created the world.
D) The Quran would be unnecessary.

Key Takeaway
God's speech must be eternal because if it were not, creation would occur independently of His command, contradicting the Quran.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that every single thing that happens in your life happens by God's eternal command, or do you sometimes act as if things happen by chance? How does al-Ash'ari's argument challenge your tendency to ignore God's sovereignty?
?
The Quran says 'Be' and it is. Does your heart rest in the certainty that God's word is the ultimate cause of all existence, or do you find yourself attributing more power to secondary causes? What step can you take today to realign your trust?
?
Al-Ash'ari warns against rejecting the Quran. Are there any verses you have conveniently reinterpreted to avoid a difficult divine attribute? Ask yourself honestly: are you willing to accept God's eternal speech even if it challenges your intellect?

The Jahmiyya Position Implies God Is Like Dumb Idols
Al-Ash'ari uses Abraham's argument against idolatry to refute the Jahmiyya.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses Abraham's argument against idolatry to prove that God must be eternally speaking.

Core Idea
If God were not speaking, He would be inferior to mute idols—impossible.

Al-Ash'ari presents a powerful analogy from the story of Abraham. The Jahmiyya claim that God's speech is created implies that in pre-eternity, God was not speaking. But Abraham argued that mute idols cannot be gods. So how can the One for whom speech is impossible in pre-eternity be God? This lesson unpacks that argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Note the structure: a claim, an analogy from Abraham, and a conclusion.
2
Understand the analogy from Abraham
Examine how Abraham argued that idols are not gods because they cannot speak. Al-Ash'ari applies this to the Jahmiyya position.
Even idols could potentially be given speech, yet they are not gods.
3
Grasp the conclusion about divine speech
See why God cannot be silent in pre-eternity without being inferior to idols. Therefore, God must be eternally speaking.
This establishes speech as an essential attribute of God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واعلموا - رحمكم الله - أن قول الجهمية إن كلام الله مخلوق، يلزمهم به أن يكون الله عز وجل لم يزل كالأصنام التي لا تنطق ولا تتكلم لو كان لم يزل غير متكلم ؛ لأن الله عز وجل يخبر عن إبراهيم عليه الصلاة والسلام أنه قال لقومه لما قالوا له : أَنتَ فَعَلْتَ هَذَا بِنَا لِهَتِنَا يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ * قَالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُ كَبِيرُهُمْ هَذَا فَتَتَلُوهُمْ إن كَانُوا يَنطِقُونَ ﴾ [الأنبياء: ٦٢-٦٣] فاحتج عليهم بأن الأصنام إذا لم تكن ناطقة متكلمة لم تكن آلهة، وأن الإله لا يكون غير ناطق ولا متكلم، فلما كانت الأصنام التي لا يستحيل أن يحييها الله وينطقها لا تكون آلهة فكيف يجوز أن يكون من يستحيل عليه الكلام في قدمه إلها؟ تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً . وإذا لم يجز أن يكون الله سبحانه في قدمه بمرتبة دون مرتبة الأصنام التي لا تنطق، فقد وجب أن يكون الله لم يزل متكلماً .
And know - may Allah have mercy on you - that the saying of the Jahmiyya that the speech of Allah is created entails for them that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, would have always been like idols that do not speak or talk, if He had always been non-speaking. Because Allah, Mighty and Majestic, informs about Abraham, peace be upon him, that he said to his people when they said to him: 'Did you do this to our gods, O Abraham?' He said: 'Rather, this their chief did it, so ask them if they can speak.' [Anbiya 21:62-63] So he argued against them that idols, if they do not speak and talk, are not gods, and that the deity cannot be non-speaking and non-talking. So since idols, for which it is not impossible that Allah give them life and make them speak, are not gods, then how can it be permissible that He for whom speech is impossible in His pre-eternity be a god? Exalted is Allah above that, a great exaltation. And if it is not permissible that Allah, glorified, be in His pre-eternity at a rank below the rank of idols that do not speak, then it is necessary that Allah has never ceased to be speaking.
Source pages p. 76

Commentary
This is a distinct section (fasl) that uses a powerful analogy from the story of Abraham. The Jahmiyya claim that God was not speaking in pre-eternity (qidam) would mean He was like mute idols. But Abraham proved idols cannot be gods because they do not speak. Even idols could potentially be given speech by God, yet they are not gods. So how can the One for whom speech is impossible (i.e., if God were silent eternally) be God? The conclusion is that God must be eternally speaking (lam yazal mutakalliman). This argument elevates the attribute of speech as essential to divinity.

Key Terms
جهمية
أصنام
ناطقة
متكلم
قدم
إله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God's silence in pre-eternity does not diminish His divinity.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari shows that speech is a necessary attribute of God; without it, He would be inferior to mute idols, which cannot be gods.

Checkpoint
What does al-Ash'ari conclude from Abraham's argument about the idols?
A) Idols can sometimes be gods if they speak.
B) God must be eternally speaking because silence would make Him inferior to idols.
C) God can create speech in idols.
D) Abraham was wrong about idols.

Key Takeaway
God must be eternally speaking; silence would contradict His divinity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that God had always been silent, how would that change the way you pray or listen for His guidance? Does your certainty that He speaks now affect how you approach the Qur'an as His living speech?
?
The Jahmiyya thought they were exalting God by denying that His speech is eternal, but al-Ash'ari shows they reduced Him to the level of mute idols. Where have you tried to 'protect' God's transcendence by limiting His attributes, perhaps downplaying His anger or His mercy because they seem too human? Will you repent of that today?
?
Abraham's argument hinged on the simple fact that idols cannot speak. How often do you treat the Qur'an as if it were a silent book—read without expectation that the Eternal Speaker is addressing you right now? What will you change today to hear His speech as living and present?

God Speaks After Creation: The Hadith of 'To Whom Belongs the Kingdom'
Al-Ash'ari cites a hadith proving God speaks after creation, refuting those who deny it.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses the hadith of 'To whom belongs the kingdom' to argue that God speaks after creation, while refuting the Jahmiyya claim that temporal speech implies createdness.

Core Idea
God's speech in time is not created; it is an eternal attribute manifested.

Al-Ash'ari presents another proof from transmitted tradition—a hadith about the Day of Resurrection. In this hadith, God speaks after all creatures have died, asking and answering Himself. This shows that God speaks in time, yet al-Ash'ari insists this speech is not created. We must understand how he maintains the distinction between temporal manifestation and eternal attribute.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation.
Read the Arabic text carefully, noting the hadith quotation and the footnote. Then read the literal translation to understand the meaning.
The footnote provides the chain of narration; it is part of the source text.
2
Understand the hadith context.
The hadith describes events after the first trumpet blast: all creatures die, then God announces 'I am the Compeller' and asks 'To whom belongs the kingdom?' This shows God speaking after creation.
Al-Ash'ari uses this to argue that God speaks in time.
3
Grasp the theological implication.
Al-Ash'ari refutes the idea that temporal speech implies createdness. Reflect on how he distinguishes the eternal attribute from its temporal manifestation.
This is a key difference between Ash'ari and Jahmiyya.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد قال الله تعالى مخبراً عن نفسه أنه يقول : ( لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ ) وجاءت الرواية (1) أنه يقول هذا القول فلا يَرُدُّ عليه أحد شيئاً، فيقول: (1) هو جزء من حديث الصور الطويل الذي رواه عبد بن حميد والطبراني، وأبو يعلى والطبراني في المطولات. والبيهقي في ( البعث ) من حديث أبي هريرة ، أن جميع الأحياء إذا ماتوا بعد النفخة الأولى ولم يبق إلا الله عز وجل قال الله سبحانه وتعالى: أنا الجبار، أنا الجبار، ثلاثاً. ثم يهتف بصوته: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ ) ثلاث مرات -
And Allah, the Exalted, has said, informing about Himself that He says: 'To whom belongs the kingdom today?' And the narration came (1) that He says this saying and no one responds to Him with anything, so He says: (1) It is part of the long hadith of the Trumpet narrated by Abd ibn Humaid, al-Tabarani, Abu Ya'la, and al-Tabarani in al-Mutawwalat, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Ba'th from the hadith of Abu Hurairah, that all living beings, when they die after the first blast and nothing remains but Allah, Mighty and Majestic, Allah, Glorified and Exalted, says: 'I am the Compeller, I am the Compeller' three times. Then He calls out with His voice: 'To whom belongs the kingdom today?' three times -
Source pages p. 76

Commentary
This is another proof (daleel aakhar) based on a narrated hadith about the Day of Resurrection. The hadith describes that after all creatures die, God will ask 'To whom belongs the kingdom today?' and then answer Himself. This shows that God speaks after creation—but this does not mean His speech is created. The speech here is an act of God that occurs in time, yet it is not created as an accident in a substrate. The Jahmiyya might use such hadith to argue for speech being temporal, but al-Ash'ari uses it to affirm that God speaks, and the speech is His attribute, not a created thing.

Key Terms
رواية
حديث الصور
يهتف بصوته
لمن الملك اليوم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God speaks in time, His speech must be created.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari distinguishes between the eternal attribute of speech and its temporal manifestation; the act of speaking in time does not make speech created.

Checkpoint
What does the hadith about 'To whom belongs the kingdom today?' demonstrate according to al-Ash'ari?
A) God does not speak.
B) God speaks after creation, but His speech is not created.
C) The Day of Judgment will never happen.
D) Angels will respond to God.

Key Takeaway
The hadith of 'To whom belongs the kingdom' proves that God speaks after creation, and this speech is not created because it is the temporal manifestation of His eternal attribute of speech.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that God asks 'To whom belongs the kingdom today?' and no one answers, do you truly live today as if the kingdom belongs to Him alone? Where in your life do you still act as if you own something independent of His sovereignty?
?
If God speaks in time—calling out with His voice—what does that mean for how you listen to His words in the Qur'an? Do you hear it as a living, present address to you today, or as a distant historical record?
?
The hadith shows that after all creatures die, God alone remains and speaks. Are you preparing for that moment when every voice falls silent and only His voice is heard? What needs to change in your heart so that you are ready to respond to Him now?

Refuting the Jahmiyya: God is Eternally Speaking
Analogical argument proving God's eternal speech from Ibrahim's idol argument.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the analogical argument from Ibrahim's rebuttal that proves God is eternally speaking and refutes the Jahmiyya claim that His speech is created.

Core Idea
If God were not eternally speaking, He would be lower than mute idols.

The Jahmiyya claimed that God's speech is created. Imam al-Ash'ari responds with a powerful analogy drawn from the Qur'anic story of Ibrahim (AS). By showing that mute idols cannot be gods, he demonstrates that God must be eternally speaking, otherwise He would be at a rank below them. This lesson unpacks that argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing, then read the translation. Notice the structure: a conditional statement followed by a Qur'anic proof and a conclusion.
Pay attention to the logical flow: if... then... because...
2
Identify the premise from Ibrahim's argument
Ibrahim argued that idols that do not speak cannot be gods. This establishes a principle: a deity must be capable of speech.
The Qur'anic verse is central to the analogy.
3
Apply the premise to the Jahmiyya view
The Jahmiyya say God's speech is created, implying He was not always speaking. The author shows this would make God like mute idols, which is impossible.
This step reveals the absurd consequence.
4
Draw the conclusion
Since God cannot be inferior to idols, He must be eternally speaking. This refutes the Jahmiyya claim.
The argument is a proof by necessity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فصل :
واعلموا - رحمكم الله - أن قول الجهمية إن كلام الله مخلوق، يلزمهم به أن يكون الله عز وجل لم يزل كالأصنام التي لا تنطق ولا تتكلم لو كان لم يزل غير متكلم ; لأن الله عز وجل يخبر عن إبراهيم عليه الصلاة والسلام أنه قال لقومه لما قالوا له : أَنتَ فَعَلْتَ هَذَا بِنَا لِهَتِنَا يَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ * قَالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُ كَبِيرُهُمْ هَذَا فَتَتَلُوهُمْ إن كَانُوا يَنطِقُونَ ﴾ [الأنبياء: ٦٢-٦٣] فاحتج عليهم بأن الأصنام إذا لم تكن ناطقة متكلمة لم تكن آلهة، وأن الإله لا يكون غير ناطق ولا متكلم، فلما كانت الأصنام التي لا يستحيل أن يحييها الله وينطقها لا تكون آلهة فكيف يجوز أن يكون من يستحيل عليه الكلام في قدمه إلها؟ تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً .
وإذا لم يجز أن يكون الله سبحانه في قدمه بمرتبة دون مرتبة الأصنام التي لا تنطق، فقد وجب أن يكون الله لم يزل متكلماً .
Chapter: And know — may God have mercy on you — that the statement of the Jahmiyya that the speech of God is created entails for them that God, mighty and majestic, would have been like the idols that do not speak and do not utter, if He were not eternally speaking. For God informs about Ibrahim, peace be upon him, that he said to his people when they said to him: 'You have done this to our gods, O Ibrahim?' He said: 'Rather, this their chief did it, so ask them if they can speak' — so he argued against them that if the idols are not speaking, they are not gods, and that a deity cannot be non-speaking and non-uttering. Since idols which are not impossible for God to revive and make speak are not gods, how can it be permissible that one for whom speech is impossible in His pre-eternity be a god? Exalted is God above that, a great exaltation. And since it is not permissible that God, glorified, in His pre-eternity be at a rank below the rank of idols that do not speak, then it is necessary that God is eternally speaking.
Source pages p. 76

Commentary
This passage is a proof by analogy (qiyas) against the Jahmiyya who claim God's speech is created. The author begins by stating that their view leads to a necessary consequence: if God were not eternally speaking, He would be like idols that cannot speak. He then cites the Qur'anic story of Ibrahim, who argued that idols that cannot speak cannot be gods. The underlying principle is that a true deity must be capable of speech. Since idols, which could potentially be made to speak by God, are not gods, how can one for whom speech is impossible in eternity be considered God? This would place God below idols, which is impossible. Therefore, God must be eternally speaking.

Key Terms
الجهمية
كلام الله مخلوق
الأصنام
الإله
الناطق
المتكلم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Jahmiyya think that created speech does not diminish God's divinity.
Correction: This analogy shows that if God were not eternally speaking, He would be at a rank below mute idols, which is impossible for the Creator. Therefore, God must be eternally speaking.

Checkpoint
According to the author, what does the Jahmiyya view logically imply about God?
A) God is greater than idols because He speaks.
B) God would be like mute idols if He were not eternally speaking.
C) God created speech in Himself.
D) God's speech is eternal but created.

Key Takeaway
God must be eternally speaking; otherwise, He would be at a rank below mute idols, which is impossible for the Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed God was not speaking, would your worship be empty like idol worship? How does this knowledge challenge you to treat the Qur'an as His living speech?
?
When you read the Qur'an, do you sense that you are hearing the voice of the Eternal Speaker, or have you become dull to its divine origin? What would it take for you to listen as if God is speaking directly to you right now?
?
The Jahmiyya reduced God's speech to a creation; are there areas in your life where you treat God's commands as temporary or negotiable? Commit today to obey one command you have been ignoring, because it is from the One who cannot be silent.

Proof from God's Speech on the Day of Judgment
Proof that God speaks independently of creation using the Day of Judgment verse.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Qur'anic account of the Day of Judgment demonstrates that God's speech is uncreated and independent of creation.

Core Idea
God speaks even when no creation exists, proving His speech is uncreated.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a theological proof from the Qur'an itself. He cites the verse where God asks 'Whose is the kingdom today?' on the Day of Judgment, when all creatures have perished. This lesson examines how this scenario establishes that God's speech is not a part of creation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the translation to capture the overall argument.
Notice the scriptural citation at the start.
2
Understand the scenario
The proof hinges on the Day of Judgment when all creation perishes—no humans, angels, jinn, or any living thing. God then asks 'Whose is the kingdom today?'.
This moment of absolute emptiness is key.
3
Grasp the conclusion
Since God speaks when nothing created exists, His speech cannot be part of creation. Therefore, God's speech is external to creation and uncreated.
This refutes the view that God's speech is created.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۹۷ دلیل آخر :
وقد قال الله تعالى مخبراً عن نفسه أنه يقول : ( لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ ) وجاءت الرواية أنه يقول هذا القول فلا يَرُدُّ عليه أحد شيئاً، فيقول: هو لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ [غافر : ١٦] ، فإذا كان عزّ وجلّ قائلاً مع فناء الأشياء إذ لا إنسان ولا ملك ولا حي ولا جان ولا شجر ولا مدر، فقد صح أن كلام الله عز وجل خارج عن الخلق ; لأنه يوجد ولا شيء من المخلوقات موجود .
97. Another proof: And God the Exalted has informed about Himself that He says: 'Whose is the kingdom today?' And the narration has come that He says this statement and no one answers Him anything, so He says: 'It is for Allah, the One, the Overpowering.' [Ghafir: 16]. And if He, exalted and majestic, is speaking while things have perished, when there is no human, no angel, no living thing, no jinn, no tree, no clay, then it has been established that the speech of God the Exalted is external to creation, because it exists while nothing of the created things exists.
Source pages p. 76

Commentary
This is a proof from the Qur'an and tradition that God's speech exists without creation. The author cites the verse where God asks 'Whose is the kingdom today?' on the Day of Judgment when all creatures have perished. Since God speaks at a time when no creation exists, His speech cannot be a part of creation or dependent on creation. Therefore, God's speech is eternal and not created.

Key Takeaway
God's speech is independent of creation because it occurs even when no creation exists.

?
This proof shows God's speech is independent of creation. What would change in your heart if you truly believed every verse was uttered by God Himself, without any creation mediating it?
?
On the Day of Judgment, God will ask 'Whose is the kingdom today?' and no one will answer. Are you living today as if you already know the answer is Allah, or are you acting as if you own your life? If you were to hear that question right now, what would your life's answer show?

Proof from God Speaking to Moses
Proof that God's speech cannot be created in another from the verse on Moses.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the verse of Moses (AS) demonstrates that God's speech is an eternal attribute not created in something else.

Core Idea
God's speech is direct and cannot be a created accident in another.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a concise proof from the Quranic verse where Allah speaks directly to Moses. He defines the key term 'takleem' and then draws an analogy with God's knowledge to refute the Jahmiyya claim that His speech is created in something else. This lesson unpacks that argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the verse, the definition, and the logical conclusion.
2
Understand the definition of 'takleem'
The author defines 'takleem' as direct face-to-face speech. This establishes that God's speech is direct, not mediated.
This refutes the idea that the speech was created in a tree or angel.
3
Grasp the analogy with knowledge
Just as God's knowledge cannot be created in something else, His speech cannot be. This is a logical refutation of the Jahmiyya position.
The analogy highlights that both are eternal attributes of God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۹۸- دليل آخر :
وقد قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَكَلَّمَ اللهُ مُوسَى تَكْلِيمًا ﴾ [النساء : ١٦٤] والتكليم هو المشافهة بالكلام، ولا يجوز أن يكون كلام المتكلم حالاً في غيره مخلوقاً في شيء سواه، كما لا يجوز ذلك في العلم .
98. Another proof: And God the Exalted said: 'And Allah spoke to Moses directly.' [An-Nisa: 164] And 'takleem' is speaking face-to-face. And it is not permissible for the speech of the speaker to be an accident in another, created in something else, just as it is not permissible in knowledge.
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This is a proof from the verse where God speaks directly to Moses. The author defines 'takleem' as direct speech from speaker to listener. He then argues that it is impossible for God's speech to be a created attribute residing in something else, just as God's knowledge cannot be created in something else. This refutes the Jahmiyya claim that God's speech is created in a body or object.

Key Terms
التكليم
المشافهة
كلام المتكلم
حال
مخلوق
العلم

Correction: The verse shows direct speech from Allah to Moses, and the analogy with knowledge proves that speech, like knowledge, is an eternal attribute of Allah, not a created accident in another.

Checkpoint

A) Because the verse says 'Moses heard the speech.'
B) Because speech is an attribute that cannot be transferred, like knowledge.

D) Because it is a metaphor.

Key Takeaway
The verse of Moses proves that God's speech is an eternal attribute directly from Him, not a created accident in another.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

What would it take for you to receive it as such?
?

Proof from Surah Al-Ikhlas: God's Names Cannot Be Created
Imam al-Ash'ari's proof from Surah Al-Ikhlas that the Qur'an is uncreated.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the argument that if the Qur'an were created, then God's names and attributes would also be created, which is impossible.

Core Idea
If the Qur'an is created, God's names become created—an impossibility for the Eternal.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a concise but powerful proof from Surah Al-Ikhlas. The Qur'an contains the names of Allah, such as 'Allah' and 'Al-Samad'. If the Qur'an were a created thing, those names would be created as well. But God's names are inseparable from His essence; if they were created, then His oneness, knowledge, and power would also be created. This leads to an absurdity, so the Qur'an must be uncreated. Let's examine the logic.

Guided Path
1
Read the Quranic verse
Read the verse from Surah Al-Ikhlas which is the foundation of the argument. It affirms God's uniqueness and eternity.
The verse is a key premise.
2
Understand the question
The author asks how the Qur'an can be created if it contains God's names. Reflect on the implications: the names are part of the Qur'an.
3
Trace the logical consequence
If the names are created, then God's attributes (oneness, knowledge, power) would also be created, which is impossible. Recognize the reductio ad absurdum.
This is the core of the proof.
4
Conclude the proof
The impossibility proves that the Qur'an cannot be created. See how this ties into the broader debate about the Qur'an's nature.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۹۹ دلیل آخر :
وقال الله تعالى: (قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ * اللهُ الصَّمَدُ * لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ * وَلَمْ يَكُن لَهُ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌ ﴾ [سورة الإخلاص)، فكيف يكون القرآن مخلوقاً وأسماء الله في القرآن؟ هذا يوجب أن تكون أسماء الله مخلوقة، ولو كانت أسماؤه مخلوقة لكانت وحدانيته مخلوقة، وكذلك علمه وقدرته، تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً .
99. Another proof: And God the Exalted said: 'Say, He is Allah, [who is] One, Allah, the Eternal Refuge. He neither begets nor is born, nor is there to Him any equivalent.' [Surah Al-Ikhlas]. So how can the Qur'an be created while the names of God are in the Qur'an? This necessitates that the names of God are created. And if His names were created, then His Oneness would be created, and likewise His knowledge and power. Exalted is God above that, a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This is a proof from the Qur'an itself. The author argues that the Qur'an contains the names of God (e.g., Allah, Al-Samad). If the Qur'an were created, then those names would be created. But if God's names are created, that implies that His attributes (Oneness, Knowledge, Power) are also created, which is impossible because God's attributes are eternal. Therefore, the Qur'an cannot be created.

Key Terms
القرآن
مخلوق
أسماء الله
وحدانيته
علمه
قدرته

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The words of the Qur'an are created, but the meaning is uncreated.
Correction: The author argues that the names of God in the Qur'an are not separate from His essence. If the words are created, then the names (and thus attributes) are created, which is impossible. Therefore, the entire Qur'an—both words and meaning—must be uncreated.

Checkpoint
What does the author claim would follow if the Qur'an were created?
A) Only the letters are created, not the meaning.
B) God's names would be created, leading to created attributes.
C) The Qur'an would be eternal.
D) God would have a beginning.

Key Takeaway
The Qur'an contains God's names; if it were created, those names would be created, but God's names and attributes are eternal, so the Qur'an is uncreated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Qur'an, do you truly believe that you are engaging with the eternal speech of Allah, not a created thing? How does this belief affect the care and reverence you give to every word?

?
The argument shows that if the Qur'an were created, even God's oneness would be created. Does your heart tremble at the thought that any diminishment of the Qur'an's status would diminish your understanding of who Allah is? What steps will you take to ensure you treat the Qur'an as the eternal word it is?

Proof from 'Tabarak' That God's Name Is Uncreated
Proof from Qur'anic usage of 'Tabarak' for God's name.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Qur'anic phrase 'Tabarak' (Blessed) is used exclusively for God and thus proves that God's name is uncreated.

Core Idea
The word 'Tabarak' is never used for creation, so God's name must be uncreated.

In this passage, Imam al-Ash'ari presents a concise yet powerful proof from the Qur'an. He cites the verse 'Blessed is the name of your Lord' and observes that the term 'Tabarak' is never applied to created things. This linguistic observation establishes that God's name—and by extension the Qur'an—cannot be created. Let's examine the proof carefully.

Guided Path
1
Read the proof passage
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Notice the structure: citation of verse, then observation, then conclusion (though incomplete).
The passage ends abruptly due to OCR truncation.
2
Identify the key observation
The author notes that the word 'Tabarak' is not said to any created thing. This is the core of the argument. Contemplate why this observation is significant.
Consider: why does the author focus on the word 'Tabarak' and not just the verse?
3
Understand the conclusion
Although the text cuts off, the intended conclusion is that since 'Tabarak' is exclusive to God, God's name cannot be a created entity. Ask yourself: how does this connect to the larger debate about the Qur'an being created?
This proof is one of several supporting the uncreated nature of the Qur'an.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۱۰۰ - دليل آخر :
وقد قال الله تعالى: نَبَرَكَ اسْمُ رَبِّكَ ﴾ [الرحمن : ۷۸] ولا يقال لمخلوق تبارك فدل
100. Another proof: And God the Exalted said: 'Blessed is the name of your Lord' [Ar-Rahman: 78]. And it is not said to a created thing 'Tabarak' (blessed). So this indicates...
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This is a proof from the Qur'anic phrase 'Tabarak ismu rabbika' (Blessed is the name of your Lord). The author observes that the word 'Tabarak' is never used for any created thing; it is exclusively for God. Since the name of God is described as 'Tabarak', and 'Tabarak' is not said to creation, the name of God cannot be created. The text ends abruptly with 'so this indicates' but the conclusion is clear: God's name is uncreated, and therefore the Qur'an (which contains God's names) is uncreated.

Key Terms
تبارك
اسم ربك
مخلوق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think the word 'Tabarak' can be applied to created things, like saying 'a blessed tree' or 'a blessed person'.
Correction: In Islamic theological usage, 'Tabarak' is an exclusive attribute of God. The Qur'an only uses it for God, and it is not said to creation. This linguistic fact underpins the argument.

Checkpoint
Why does the author use the verse 'Tabarak ismu rabbika' as a proof?
A) It shows God's name is blessed.
B) Because 'Tabarak' is never said to a created thing, so God's name cannot be created.
C) It proves God's name is eternal.
D) It indicates the Qur'an is blessed.

Key Takeaway
The exclusive use of 'Tabarak' for God proves that His name is uncreated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
The exclusive use of 'Tabarak' for God shows His unique majesty. Has there ever been a moment when you felt the weight of God's majesty while reciting His names? If not, what is preventing that feeling, and what step can you take this week to cultivate it?

Proof that God's Speech Remains After Creation
Imam Ashari proves God's speech is uncreated through its post-creation persistence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Imam Ashari argues that God's speech must be uncreated, based on its persistence after the annihilation of all created beings.

Core Idea
God's speech endures beyond all creatures, so it cannot be created.

Imam Ashari presents a concise but powerful proof that God's speech is not a created thing. By referencing the Day of Resurrection, when all creation perishes yet God still speaks, he establishes that divine speech exists independently of the created order. This lesson unpacks that argument and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its literal English translation. Notice how the verse of the Qur'an is used as the foundation of the argument.
The verse is from Surah Ghafir, verse 16.
2
Identify the premise and conclusion
Ashari's argument has a clear logical structure: a premise (God speaks when no creatures exist) leads to a conclusion (His speech is outside of creation). Work through this reasoning step by step.
The key phrase is 'outside of creation' (خارج عن الخلق).
3
Understand the significance of 'fana al-ashya'
The term 'fana' (annihilation) refers to the end of all contingent beings. Grasp why the absolute absence of creatures is crucial to the argument.
If any creature existed, one could argue speech is attached to it.
4
Reflect on the theological implication
Consider what this means for the attribute of speech: it is eternal, not a temporal event. This distinguishes the Ash'ari position from the Mu'tazila who claimed speech is created.
This proof is a cornerstone of the Ash'ari creed.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
هو لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ [غافر : ١٦] ، فإذا كان عزّ وجلّ قائلاً مع فناء الأشياء إذ لا إنسان ولا ملك ولا حي ولا جان ولا شجر ولا مدر، فقد صح أن كلام الله عز وجل خارج عن الخلق ؛ لأنه يوجد ولا شيء من المخلوقات موجود .
He is Allah, the One, the Subduer [Ghafir 40:16]. So if He, mighty and majestic, is speaking while things perish, when there is no human, no angel, no living being, no jinn, no tree, no stone, then it is established that the speech of Allah, mighty and majestic, is outside of creation; because it exists while nothing of the created things exists.
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This passage presents a proof that God's speech is uncreated. It begins by citing the Qur'anic verse (Ghafir 40:16) which describes the Day of Resurrection when Allah asks 'To whom belongs the kingdom today?' and answers Himself. Ashari then argues that on that day, all creatures have perished—no human, angel, jinn, tree, or stone remains. Yet God is still speaking. Since His speech continues even when no created being exists, it cannot itself be a created thing. Therefore, the speech of Allah is outside of creation (uncreated).

Key Terms
كلام الله
خارج عن الخلق
فناء الأشياء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God's speech is a transient event that begins and ends within time.
Correction: This proof shows that God's speech persists even after all temporal things cease, so it cannot be temporal. It is eternal and uncreated.

Checkpoint
What conclusion does Imam Ashari draw from the fact that God speaks on the Day of Resurrection when no creatures exist?
A) God's speech is created at that moment.
B) God's speech is eternal and uncreated.
C) God's speech is a new creation each time.
D) God does not actually speak.

Key Takeaway
God's speech is uncreated because it persists even when all creation is annihilated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's speech endures beyond all creation, including your own fleeting life, what does that demand of your relationship with the Quran today? Are you living as if His words are eternal or temporary?
?
On the Day when every creature you love and every thing you own is gone, God's speech remains. What would that Day reveal about what you truly treasured? What must you anchor yourself to now?
?
If nothing created can contain or limit God's words, then what excuse do you have for treating His commands as optional, as if they will pass away? What one change can you make today to honor His eternal speech?

Proof from Moses' Direct Speech
Imam Ashari argues that God's direct speech to Moses proves His speech is uncreated and not indwelling in creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the verse about Moses' direct speech with Allah demonstrates that Allah's speech is uncreated and not an accident in a created being.

Core Idea
God's direct speech to Moses proves His speech is uncreated and belongs to Him alone.

Imam Ashari presents a concise yet powerful argument from the Qur'an. He cites the verse where Allah speaks directly to Moses and explains the term 'taklim'. The argument hinges on the impossibility of Allah's speech being a created accident inhering in something else, just as His knowledge cannot inhere in creation. This lesson unpacks that reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and translation
Start by reading the Arabic verse and its literal translation. Notice the word 'taklim' (direct speech).
The verse is from Surah an-Nisa 4:164.
2
Understand the definition of taklim
Imam Ashari defines 'taklim' as 'mushafahah' (face-to-face address). This implies direct communication without intermediaries.
This definition is crucial for the argument.
3
Follow the logical argument
If Allah's speech were created and existed in another being (like a bush or angel), then the speech would belong to that creature, not to Allah. This is impossible for Allah.
The argument uses a reductio ad absurdum.
4
Consider the analogy with knowledge
Just as Allah's knowledge cannot inhere in a created thing, His speech cannot either. Both are attributes of Allah Himself.
This analogy reinforces the point.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد قال الله تعالى: ﴿ وَكَلَّمَ اللهُ مُوسَى تَكْلِيمًا ﴾ [النساء : ١٦٤] والتكليم هو المشافهة بالكلام، ولا يجوز أن يكون كلام المتكلم حالاً في غيره مخلوقاً في شيء سواه، كما لا يجوز ذلك في العلم .
And Allah the Exalted said: 'And Allah spoke to Moses with [direct] speech' [an-Nisa 4:164]. And 'taklim' (direct speech) is addressing with speech, and it is not permissible that the speech of the speaker inheres in another, being created in something else, just as that is not permissible in the case of knowledge.
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This passage is another proof that the Qur'an is uncreated. Ashari cites the verse in which Allah says He spoke directly to Moses. The word 'taklim' means face-to-face address. Ashari argues that if God's speech were created and existed as an accident in another being (like a tree or an angel), then God would not be the actual speaker; rather, the speech would belong to the creature. This is impossible for Allah, just as it is impossible for His knowledge to inhere in a created thing. Therefore, God's speech is not created.

Key Terms
تكليم
مشافهة
كلام المتكلم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some claim that God's speech is created and exists in a created subject (e.g., the bush or an angel).
Correction: The verse attributes speech directly to Allah, and the definition of 'taklim' as direct address shows that the speech belongs to Allah, not to any creature. If it were created in another, the speech would be that creature's, not Allah's.

Checkpoint
What does the verse 'And Allah spoke to Moses with direct speech' imply about the nature of God's speech?
A) It was created in the bush.
B) It is uncreated and directly from God.
C) It is an attribute of Jibreel.
D) It is a temporary sound.

Key Takeaway
Allah's direct speech to Moses proves that His speech is uncreated and belongs to Him alone, not to any created being.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
