?
Your words have the power to build or destroy. If Allah's speech is pure and eternal, what does it say about your own speech? Are your words a reflection of His attribute of speech, or are they careless and harmful?
?
The verse says Allah spoke to Moses directly. Do you believe that Allah still speaks to you today through His Book? What would change in your life if you listened to every verse as if it were spoken directly to you, right now?

Proof from the Names of Allah in the Qur'an
Imam Ashari argues that if the Qur'an were created, God's names in it would also be created, leading to absurdities.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Imam Ashari's argument that the Qur'an cannot be created because it contains Allah's names, which are inseparable from His essence.

Core Idea
If the Qur'an were created, Allah's names would be created, but that is impossible.

Imam Ashari presents a concise proof from the Qur'an itself. He quotes Surah al-Ikhlas, which affirms Allah's oneness and uniqueness. He then argues that if the Qur'an were a created thing, then the names of Allah that appear in it would also be created. Since Allah's names are not created—they are part of His eternal attributes—the Qur'an must be uncreated. This lesson walks through that logical chain.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and the question
Begin by reading the verse from Surah al-Ikhlas and the rhetorical question that follows. Notice how the verse establishes Allah's attributes.
The verse is a declaration of tawhid.
2
Identify the logical implication
Ashari says: if the Qur'an were created, then the names of Allah within it would also be created. Understand that this is a direct consequence.
3
Trace the chain of absurdity
If names are created, then Allah's oneness (al-Ahad) would be created, and then His knowledge and power. This chain shows the impossibility.
The attributes are inseparable from the essence.
4
Recognize the conclusion
The chain leads to an absurdity (Allah's attributes being created), so the premise must be false: the Qur'an is not created.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقال الله تعالى: (قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ * اللهُ الصَّمَدُ * لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ * وَلَمْ يَكُن لَهُ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌ ﴾ [سورة الإخلاص)، فكيف يكون القرآن مخلوقاً وأسماء الله في القرآن؟ هذا يوجب أن تكون أسماء الله مخلوقة، ولو كانت أسماؤه مخلوقة لكانت وحدانيته مخلوقة، وكذلك علمه وقدرته، تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً .
And Allah the Exalted said: 'Say: He is Allah, [the] One. Allah, the Self-Sufficient Master. He begets not, nor was He begotten. And there is none co-equal unto Him' [Surah al-Ikhlas]. So how could the Qur'an be created while the names of Allah are in the Qur'an? This would necessitate that the names of Allah are created. And if His names were created, then His oneness would be created, and likewise His knowledge and His power. Exalted is Allah above that, a tremendous exaltation.
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
In this proof, Ashari uses the fact that the Qur'an contains the beautiful names of Allah. He cites Surah al-Ikhlas, which affirms Allah's oneness, self-sufficiency, and uniqueness. Ashari asks: if the Qur'an were created, then the names of Allah that appear in it would also be created. But that is impossible because Allah's names are inseparable from His essence. If His names were created, then His oneness (which is expressed by the name 'al-Ahad') would be created, and likewise His knowledge and power would be created. This would imply that Allah's attributes are created, which is absurd. Therefore, the Qur'an cannot be created.

Key Terms
أسماء الله
وحدانيته
علمه
قدرته

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think only the meanings of the names are eternal, not the words; so the recited Qur'an could be created.
Correction: Ashari argues that the recited Qur'an contains the very names of Allah. If the words are created, then the names (as words) would be created, but the names are inseparable from the attributes they denote. Thus, the Qur'an cannot be created.

Checkpoint
What does Imam Ashari conclude from the presence of Allah's names in the Qur'an?
A) The Qur'an is created but the names are eternal.
B) The Qur'an is uncreated because God's names cannot be created.
C) The names are created because they are in a created book.
D) Only the meaning of the Qur'an is uncreated.

Key Takeaway
The presence of Allah's names in the Qur'an proves that the Qur'an is uncreated because His names are eternal attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the name 'Allah' or 'al-Ahad' in prayer, do you truly grasp that this name is not a created word but a reflection of His eternal essence? How does that realization change the way you approach your salah today?
?
If the names of Allah in the Qur'an were merely created words, your own act of recitation would be clinging to something temporary. Does the eternity of His names give you deeper confidence that your worship is connected to the Eternal? Will you let that truth transform your recitation from habit to awe?
?
The argument shows that if the names were created, then oneness itself would be created. Has there ever been a moment when you doubted the absolute oneness of Allah? How does this proof strengthen your reliance on the Qur'an as the uncreated, ultimate truth that grounds your tawhid?

Proof from Tabarak and the Face of the Lord
Imam Ashari uses the blessedness of God's name and the eternity of His Face to prove His names are uncreated.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses two Qur'anic verses to argue that the names of Allah are uncreated.

Core Idea
The Qur'an calls Allah's name 'blessed' – a word never used for creation.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents two simple yet powerful arguments from the Qur'an. He points out that the word 'tabaraka' is only used for the eternal, not for created things. He then connects the eternity of Allah's Face to the eternity of His names. Both verses lead to the same conclusion: the names of Allah are uncreated.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic verses and translation
Read the two Qur'anic verses carefully in Arabic and then the literal translation. Notice the key terms 'tabaraka' and 'wajhu rabbika'.
These verses are from Surah ar-Rahman.
2
Understand the 'tabaraka' argument
The word 'tabaraka' is a term of blessing used exclusively for Allah. Since it is never said of a created thing, the name of Allah described as 'tabaraka' must be uncreated.
This is a linguistic and theological argument.
3
See the parallel argument from the Face
Just as Allah's Face is eternal and cannot be created, so too His names, which are mentioned in the Qur'an, cannot be created. The two proofs reinforce each other.
Both arguments rely on the eternity of divine attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد قال الله تعالى: ﴿ تَبَارَكَ اسْمُ رَبِّكَ ﴾ [الرحمن : ۷۸] ولا يقال لمخلوق تبارك فدل هذا على أن أسماء الله غير مخلوقة، وقال: ﴿وَيَبْقَى وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ﴾ [الرحمن : ۲۷] فكما لا يجوز أن يكون وجه ربنا مخلوقاً، فكذلك لا يجوز أن تكون أسماؤه مخلوقة .
And Allah the Exalted said: 'Blessed is the name of your Lord' [ar-Rahman 55:78]. And it is not said of a created thing 'tabaraka' (blessed). So this indicates that the names of Allah are uncreated. And He said: 'And there will remain the Face of your Lord' [ar-Rahman 55:27]. So just as it is not permissible that the Face of our Lord be created, likewise it is not permissible that His names be created.
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This passage presents two scriptural arguments. First, the verse 'Tabaraka ismu rabbika' (Blessed is the name of your Lord). The word 'tabaraka' (blessed) is a term of exaltation that applies only to the eternal and uncreated. It is never used for created things. Since Allah's name is described as 'tabaraka', it must be uncreated. Second, the verse 'Wa yabqa wajhu rabbika' (And there will remain the Face of your Lord). The Face of Allah is an attribute that endures forever; it is not subject to annihilation. Just as it is impossible for Allah's Face to be created (since it is eternal), so too it is impossible for His names to be created. Since the Qur'an contains these names, it too is uncreated.

Key Terms
تبارك
وجه ربك
أسماء الله غير مخلوقة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some people think that the word 'tabaraka' can be applied to created things like the Qur'an itself.
Correction: Imam al-Ash'ari clarifies that 'tabaraka' is only used for the uncreated, so the name of Allah (and by extension the Qur'an) is uncreated.

Checkpoint

A) Because it is written in the Qur'an.
B) Because the verse says it is 'tabaraka', which is never said of a creature.
C) Because it is a part of Allah's essence.
D) Because the Face of the Lord is not created.

Key Takeaway
The blessedness of Allah's name and the eternity of His Face both demonstrate that His names are uncreated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
You have just learned that 'tabārak' is never said of created things. When you look at your own achievements, do you secretly feel they are 'blessed' in the same way? What needs to change in your heart to reserve that word for Allah alone?

Proof from God's Testimony of His Own Oneness
Ashari argues from the verse to prove the eternity of God's speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Ashari uses the verse of testimony to prove that God's speech (the Quran) is uncreated and eternal.

Core Idea
God's testimony to His oneness must be eternal, so His speech is uncreated.

Imam Ashari presents a logical argument based on the Qur'anic verse where Allah bears witness to His own oneness. He contends that this testimony cannot be a temporal event—it must have occurred before creation, otherwise the eternal truth of tawhid would not have been attested. This leads to the conclusion that God's speech, which is His testimony, is eternal and not created.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and its context
Begin by reading the verse (Al 'Imran 3:18) where Allah declares His own testimony. Understand that this verse is the foundation of Ashari's argument.
The verse includes angels and people of knowledge, but Ashari focuses on Allah's testimony.
2
Grasp the starting premise
Ashari asserts that God must have heard this testimony from Himself, not from a creature. If He heard it from a creature, it wouldn't be His own testimony.
3
Consider the dilemma
The testimony either occurred before creation or after it. Examine both possibilities logically.
This is a classic 'either-or' argument.
4
Analyze the 'after creation' option
If the testimony came after creation, then before creation there was no testimony. That would imply that tawhid (God's oneness) was not testified to before creation, which would make tawhid not established eternally—an absurdity.
Ashari argues that what cannot be testified to is impossible.
5
Conclude: testimony is eternal
Therefore, the testimony must have been before creation, making it eternal. Since God's speech is His testimony, His speech is also uncreated.
This refutes the view that the Quran is created.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد قال الله عز وجل : ( شَهِدَ اللَّهُ أَنَّهُ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَالْمَلَيكَةُ وَأُولُوا الْعِلْمِ قَابِمَا بِالْقِسْطِ [آل عمران :۱۸] ولا بد أن يكون شهد بهذه الشهادة، وسمعها من نفسه؛ لأنه إن كان سمعها من مخلوق فليست شهادة له، وإذا كانت شهادة لـه وقـد (۱) شهد بها فلا يخلو أن يكون شهد بها قبل كون المخلوقات، أو بعد كون المخلوقات. فإن كان شهد بها بعد كون المخلوقات، فلم تكن شهادة لنفسه بالألوهية قبل الخلق ، وكيف يكون ذلك كذلك؟ وهذا يوجب أن التوحيد لم يكن يشهد به شاهد قبل الخلق ولو استحالت الشهادة بالوحدانية قبل كون الخلق لاستحال إثبات التوحيد ووجوده، وأن يكون واحداً قبل الخلق؛ لأن ما تستحيل الشهادة عليه مستحيل، وإن كانت شهادته لنفسه بالتوحيد قبل الخلق [مستحيلة] فقد بطل أن يكون كلام الله عز وجل مخلوقاً لأن كلامه شهادته .
And Allah, mighty and majestic, said: 'Allah bears witness that there is no deity except Him, and [so do] the angels and those of knowledge, maintaining [creation] with justice' [Al 'Imran 3:18]. And it is necessary that He bore witness with this testimony, and heard it from Himself; because if He had heard it from a created being, it would not be a testimony for Him. And if it is a testimony for Him, and He has testified with it, then it is either that He testified with it before the existence of created things, or after the existence of created things. If He testified with it after the existence of created things, then there was no testimony for His divinity before creation. How could that be so? And this would necessitate that tawhid (oneness) had not been testified to by any witness before creation. And if the testimony to oneness before the creation of creatures were impossible, then the establishment and existence of tawhid and His being One before creation would be impossible; because that to which testimony is impossible is impossible. And if His testimony for Himself to oneness before creation [is impossible], then it is invalidated that the speech of Allah, mighty and majestic, be created, because His speech is His testimony.
Source pages p. 77

Commentary
This passage is a detailed logical argument based on the verse 'Allah bears witness that there is no god but Him' (Al 'Imran 3:18). Ashari argues that God's testimony to His own oneness must be an eternal act. First, He must have heard this testimony from Himself, not from a creature (otherwise it wouldn't be His testimony). Then, the testimony must either have occurred before creation or after it. If it occurred after creation, then before creation there was no testimony to God's oneness. But that would mean that tawhid (the truth that God is One) was not testified to before creation. However, tawhid is an eternal truth; if testimony to it is impossible before creation, then tawhid itself would not have been established before creation—an absurdity. Therefore, the testimony must have been before creation, meaning God's speech (which is His testimony) is eternal and uncreated.

Key Terms
شهادة
التوحيد
قبل الخلق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's testimony could be a temporal event occurring after creation.
Correction: If the testimony were after creation, then before creation there was no testimony, which would make the eternal truth of tawhid impossible. Hence it must be eternal.

Checkpoint
Why does Ashari argue that God's testimony to His oneness must have been before creation?
A) Because the verse says so explicitly.
B) Otherwise tawhid would not have been testified to before creation, making it possible that God was not One eternally.
C) Because angels testified before creation.
D) Because creation needs a witness.

Key Takeaway
God's speech is uncreated because it is His eternal testimony to His own oneness.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's testimony to His own oneness is eternal, what does that mean for your own testimony of faith? Is your declaration of "Lā ilāha illā Allāh" merely a repetition of words, or does it echo an eternal truth that God Himself declares? How will you ensure your shahada is not just a temporal utterance but a reflection of that eternal reality?
?
Consider the possibility that you might have subconsciously believed God's speech is created—like a book or a sound that began in time. How does the logical impossibility of a created testimony to God's oneness shake your understanding of who God is? Will you let this argument transform your awe of His eternal perfection?
?
If the truth of tawhid required an eternal witness, and that witness is God Himself, then your knowledge of God is founded on His own self-testimony. Does your confidence in tawhid rest on this solid rock, or on fragile human arguments? What would change in your life if you truly internalized that God Himself stands as the eternal witness to His own oneness?

Proof from Glorifying the Name of the Lord
Imam Ashari argues from the command to glorify Allah's name that the name is uncreated, and thus the Qur'an is uncreated.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Qur'anic command to glorify Allah's name serves as a proof that the name (and thus the Qur'an) is uncreated, refuting the Jahmiyyah claim.

Core Idea
Glorifying Allah's name is worship; only the Uncreated can be worshipped.

Imam Ashari presents a concise but powerful argument from the Qur'an itself. The Jahmiyyah claimed the Qur'an was created, yet Allah commands us to glorify His name. Since glorifying a creature is impermissible, the name must be uncreated, and therefore the Qur'an—which contains Allah's names—is also uncreated.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice how Imam Ashari frames the argument as a refutation of the Jahmiyyah.
The passage ends abruptly; the argument is still complete.
2
Identify the scriptural references
Find the two Qur'anic references: 'Glorify the name of your Lord, the Most High' (al-A'la 87:1-2) and the mention of 'jadd' (majesty) from another verse.
The second reference is incomplete in the text.
3
Understand the logical chain
Trace the reasoning: Allah commands glorification of His name → the name is the object of worship → worshipping a creature is shirk → therefore the name must be uncreated → since the Qur'an contains Allah's names, it too must be uncreated.
4
Reflect on the refutation of Jahmiyyah
Consider how this argument undermines the Jahmiyyah position. If the name of Allah is uncreated, how can the Qur'an, which is His speech and contains His names, be created?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومما يدل على بطلان قول الجهمية، وأن القرآن كلام الله غير مخلوق : أن أسماء الله من القرآن وقد قال عز وجل: سَبِّحِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ الْأَعْلَى الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى ) [الأعلى : ١-٢] ولا يجوز أن يكون اسم ربك الأعلى الذي خلق فسوى مخلوقاً، كما لا يجوز أن يكون جد ربنا مخلوقاً، قال الله تعالى في سورة
And among what indicates the falsehood of the statement of the Jahmiyyah, and that the Qur'an is the speech of Allah, uncreated: that the names of Allah are from the Qur'an, and He, mighty and majestic, said: 'Glorify the name of your Lord, the Most High, Who created and proportioned' [al-A'la 87:1-2]. And it is not permissible that the name of your Lord, the Most High, Who created and proportioned, be created, just as it is not permissible that the majesty (jadd) of our Lord be created. Allah the Exalted said in the surah...
Source pages p. 78

Commentary
This passage is a final proof against the Jahmiyyah who claimed the Qur'an is created. Ashari points out that the Qur'an contains the names of Allah, and Allah commands us to glorify His name in the verse 'Glorify the name of your Lord, the Most High'. The name of Allah is the object of our glorification; it is something eternal and divine. It cannot be created, because we are commanded to glorify it as an act of worship directed to Allah Himself. If the name were created, we would be glorifying a creature, which is shirk. Likewise, Allah's 'jadd' (majesty) is uncreated. Since the Qur'an contains these names, the Qur'an itself must be uncreated. The proof is concluded with a reference to another verse, but the text cuts off.

Key Terms
تسبيح اسم الرب
جد ربنا
بطلان قول الجهمية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The names of Allah are just human labels with no inherent sanctity; they can be considered created.
Correction: The Qur'anic command to glorify Allah's name shows that the name itself is an object of worship and divine, not a mere label. Glorification is only due to the Uncreated, so the name must be uncreated.

Checkpoint
According to this proof, why is the name of the Lord not created?
A) Because it is written in the Qur'an.
B) Because Allah commands us to glorify it, and glorifying a creature is impermissible.
C) Because it is an Arabic word.
D) Because it is mentioned in a Meccan surah.

Key Takeaway
The command to glorify Allah's name proves it is uncreated, and therefore the Qur'an, which contains His names, is uncreated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Qur'an, do you truly believe you are uttering the uncreated speech of Allah? How does that change the way you recite? Will you commit to reciting with greater reverence, knowing you are glorifying His uncreated name?
?
If the name of Allah is uncreated and worthy of worship, what about your own speech about Allah? Do you speak of Him carelessly? How can you ensure your words honor His uncreated name?
?
The Jahmiyyah denied this truth. Is there any area in your heart that doubts the uncreatedness of the Qur'an? What step will you take to strengthen your conviction?

Proof from Allah's Testimony
Al-Ash'ari argues that Allah's testimony of tawhid must be eternal, hence His speech is uncreated.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses the Quranic verse of Allah's testimony to prove that His speech is uncreated.

Core Idea
Allah's own testimony of tawhid must be eternal, so His speech is uncreated.

In this passage, Imam al-Ash'ari presents a logical proof from the Qur'an. He argues that Allah's testimony to His own oneness cannot be a created thing, because then it would not be His testimony. Let's trace the argument carefully.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and passage
Read the Arabic passage once along with the translation. Notice how al-Ash'ari starts with the Quranic verse before building his argument.
The verse is from Surah Al Imran, ayah 18.
2
Identify the key claim
Identify al-Ash'ari's claim that Allah's testimony must be heard from Himself, not from a created being. Why would it not be a testimony for Him if heard from a creature?
This premise is crucial for the argument.
3
Follow the conditional reasoning
Trace the dichotomy: either the testimony occurred before creation or after. Follow the reductio for the 'after' option, leading to absurdity.
The absurdity is that tawhid would have no witness before creation, contradicting its eternal nature.
4
Understand the conclusion
Understand how the impossibility of the 'after' option forces the conclusion that Allah's speech (His testimony) is uncreated.
The conclusion directly refutes the claim that the Qur'an is created.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد قال الله عز وجل : ( شَهِدَ اللَّهُ أَنَّهُ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ وَأُولُوا الْعِلْمِ قَائِمًا بِالْقِسْطِ [آل عمران :۱۸] ولا بد أن يكون شهد بهذه الشهادة، وسمعها من نفسه؛ لأنه إن كان سمعها من مخلوق فليست شهادة له، وإذا كانت شهادة له وقد شهد بها فلا يخلو أن يكون شهد بها قبل كون المخلوقات، أو بعد كون المخلوقات. فإن كان شهد بها بعد كون المخلوقات، فلم تكن شهادة لنفسه بالألوهية قبل الخلق ، وكيف يكون ذلك كذلك؟ وهذا يوجب أن التوحيد لم يكن يشهد به شاهد قبل الخلق ولو استحالت الشهادة بالوحدانية قبل كون الخلق لاستحال إثبات التوحيد ووجوده، وأن يكون واحداً قبل الخلق؛ لأن ما تستحيل الشهادة عليه مستحيل، وإن كانت شهادته لنفسه بالتوحيد قبل الخلق [مستحيلة] فقد بطل أن يكون كلام الله عز وجل مخلوقاً لأن كلامه شهادته .
And Allah Almighty said: 'Allah bears witness that there is no deity except Him, and [so do] the angels and those of knowledge, maintaining justice.' It is necessary that He testified with this testimony and heard it from Himself; because if He heard it from a created being, it would not be a testimony for Him. And if it is a testimony for Him and He testified by it, then it is either that He testified before the existence of created things or after their existence. If He testified after the existence of created things, then there was no testimony for His divinity before creation. How can that be so? This necessitates that tawhid had no witness testifying to it before creation. And if testifying to oneness before creation were impossible, then establishing tawhid and its existence and that He is one before creation would be impossible; because what is impossible to testify to is impossible. And if His testimony for Himself of tawhid before creation is impossible, then it becomes false that the speech of Allah Almighty is created, because His speech is His testimony.
Source pages p. 78

Commentary
This passage presents a proof from the Quranic verse (Al Imran 18) that Allah's speech is uncreated. Al-Ash'ari argues that Allah's testimony of tawhid must be from Himself and eternal. If it were created in a created being, then Allah would not have personally testified before creation, leading to the absurdity that tawhid had no witness before creation. Since that is impossible, Allah's speech (which is His testimony) must be uncreated.

Key Terms
شهادة
توحيد
كلام الله
مخلوق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that 'Allah bears witness' means He created the testimony in a created being.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari argues that if He heard it from a created being, it would not be His own testimony; it must be from Himself and eternal.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, if Allah's testimony were created after creation, what would follow?
A) Tawhid would still have an eternal witness
B) There would be no testimony to tawhid before creation
C) It would prove creation is eternal
D) Prophethood would be invalid

Key Takeaway
Allah's speech is uncreated because His testimony of tawhid is eternal and cannot be a created event.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Quran, do you treat it as the eternal speech of Allah, or as a mere book? How does your attitude change if you truly believe it is uncreated?
?
Do you find yourself questioning the eternity of Allah's attributes? How does this proof challenge your prior assumptions?
?
Reflect on the weight of Allah bearing witness to His own oneness. How does this impact your worship of Him alone?

Proof from the Uncreatedness of Allah's Names
Al-Ash'ari argues that since Allah's names are uncreated, His speech (the Qur'an) is also uncreated.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand al-Ash'ari's proof that Allah's names are uncreated, and how this leads to the conclusion that the Qur'an (His speech) is also uncreated.

Core Idea
If Allah's names are uncreated, His speech must also be uncreated.

Al-Ash'ari presents a concise argument from the Qur'an itself. He points out that Allah's names are part of the Qur'an, and since the Qur'an commands glorifying Allah's name, that name cannot be created—otherwise we would be glorifying a creature. Similarly, Allah's majesty ('jadd') is described as exalted, not created. This analogy extends to His speech, establishing its uncreatedness.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation carefully to grasp the flow of the argument.
Notice the verses cited: Surah al-A'la 1-2 and Surah al-Jinn 3.
2
Identify the key analogy
Al-Ash'ari draws an analogy between the uncreatedness of Allah's names, His majesty, and His greatness.
He uses the verse about jadd to reinforce the point.
3
Understand the logical connection
If Allah's names are uncreated, then His speech, which includes those names, is also uncreated.
4
Reflect on the implication
This refutes the Jahmiyya claim that the Qur'an is created.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومما يدل على بطلان قول الجهمية، وأن القرآن كلام الله غير مخلوق : أن أسماء الله من القرآن وقد قال عز وجل: سَبِّحِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ الْأَعْلَى الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى [الأعلى : ١-٢] ولا يجوز أن يكون اسم ربك الأعلى الذي خلق فسوى مخلوقاً، كما لا يجوز أن يكون جد ربنا مخلوقاً، قال الله تعالى في سورة الجن : ﴿وَأَنَّهُ تَعَلَى جَدُّ رَبَّنَا ﴾ [الجن:٣] وكما لا يجوز أن تكون عظمته مخلوقة، كذلك لا يجوز أن يكون كلامه مخلوقاً .
And among what indicates the falsehood of the Jahmiyya's claim and that the Qur'an is the uncreated speech of Allah is that Allah's names are from the Qur'an. And He Almighty said: 'Glorify the name of your Lord, the Most High, Who created and proportioned.' It is not permissible that the name of your Lord, the Most High, Who created and proportioned, be created, just as it is not permissible that the majesty (jadd) of our Lord be created. Allah Almighty said in Surah al-Jinn: 'And that the majesty of our Lord is exalted.' And just as it is not permissible that His greatness be created, likewise it is not permissible that His speech be created.
Source pages p. 78

Commentary
This passage provides another proof from the Quran itself. Al-Ash'ari argues that Allah's names are part of the Qur'an, and the Qur'an commands glorifying the name of the Lord. If that name were created, then one would be glorifying a created thing, which is absurd. Similarly, Allah's 'jadd' (majesty) is described as exalted, not created. By analogy, His speech (the Qur'an) cannot be created.

Key Terms
أسماء الله
كلام الله
غير مخلوق
جد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Allah's names are created attributes.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari argues that since Allah's names are part of the Qur'an and the Qur'an commands glorifying them, they cannot be created; similarly, His majesty and speech are uncreated.

Checkpoint
What does al-Ash'ari compare the uncreatedness of Allah's names to?
A) The creation of the heavens
B) The majesty (jadd) of Allah
C) The speech of prophets
D) The angels

Key Takeaway
Allah's names are uncreated, and by analogy His speech (the Qur'an) is also uncreated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
The Jahmiyya reduced the Qur'an to a created thing. Where in your heart might you be reducing Allah's speech to a mere book, treating it casually or failing to submit to its authority? What will you do to change that starting today?

Proof from 42:51: Allah's Speech Cannot Be Created
Imam Ash'ari argues from 42:51 that created speech would erase prophetic distinction.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Imam Ash'ari's logical argument from Surah ash-Shura that Allah's speech cannot be created, because created speech would nullify the distinct modes of revelation and absurdly diminish the rank of prophets.

Core Idea
If speech were created in a tree, Moses would be outranked by every Jew who hears a prophet.

Imam Ash'ari presents a powerful scriptural and rational argument against the Jahmiyya claim that Allah's speech is created. He quotes verse 42:51, which outlines three exclusive ways Allah speaks to humans. If speech were always created in some object, these distinctions would collapse, and absurd consequences follow—especially lowering Moses' rank below ordinary believers. This passage shows how the Ash'ari school defends the uncreated, eternal divine attribute of speech.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and its translation
Begin by reading the Arabic verse (42:51) and its literal translation. Note the three distinct modes of divine communication: revelation, from behind a veil, or through an angel.
2
Understand the conditional argument
Imam Ash'ari then poses a conditional: if Allah's speech only exists as created in something created (like a tree), then the specification of these three modes would be meaningless, because all creatures could hear that created speech from that thing.
This is the core logical move.
3
Trace the reductio ad absurdum about Moses
The argument then focuses on Moses: if he heard Allah's speech created in a tree, then anyone hearing speech from a prophet would be of higher rank, because they hear directly from a prophet while Moses heard from a tree.
This leads to the absurdity that a Jew hearing a prophet would outrank Moses.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
Conclude that since such absurdities are impossible, the premise that Allah's speech is created must be false. The verse therefore implies that Allah's speech is an uncreated attribute.
This is a classic kalam argument using scriptural evidence and reason.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد قال الله عز وجل: ﴿وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً أَوْ مِن وَرَاءِ حِجَابٍ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولًا فَيُوحِيَ بِإِذْنِهِ مَا يَشَاءُ﴾ فلو كان كلام الله لا يوجد إلا مخلوقاً في شيء مخلوق، لم يكن لاشتراط هذه الوجوه معنى؛ لأن الكلام قد سمعه جميع الخلق ووجدوه - بزعم الجهمية – مخلوقاً في غير الله عز وجل وهذا يوجب إسقاط مرتبة النبيين صلوات الله وسلامه عليهم. ويجب عليهم إذا زعموا أن كلام الله لموسى خلقه في شجرة، أن يكون من سمع كلام الله عز وجل من ملك أو نبي أتى به من عند الله، أفضل مرتبة من سماع الكلام من موسى؛ لأنهم سمعوه من نبي ولم يسمعه موسى من الله عز وجل، وإنما سمعه من شجرة، وأن يزعموا أن اليهودي إذا سمع كلام الله من النبي عليه الصلاة والسلام أفضل مرتبة في هذا المعنى من موسى بن عمران صلى الله عليه وسلم؛ لأن اليهودي سمعه من نبي من أنبياء الله، وموسى سمعه مخلوقاً في شجرة، ولو كان مخلوقاً في شجرة لم يكن مكلماً لموسى من وراء حجاب؛ لأن من حضر الشجرة من الجن والإنس قد سمعوا الكلام من ذلك المكان، وكان سبيل موسى وغيره في ذلك سواء في أنه ليس كلام الله له من وراء حجاب.
And Allah Almighty said: 'And it is not for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a veil or by sending a messenger to reveal by His permission what He wills.' So if the speech of Allah does not exist except as created in a created thing, then there would be no meaning to stipulating these modes; because the speech would have been heard by all creatures and found—according to the claim of the Jahmiyya—created in something other than Allah Almighty, and this necessitates nullifying the rank of the prophets, may Allah's blessings and peace be upon them. And it is necessary upon them, when they claim that Allah's speech to Moses He created in a tree, that whoever hears the speech of Allah Almighty from an angel or a prophet who brought it from Allah is of higher rank than hearing the speech from Moses; because they heard it from a prophet and Moses did not hear it from Allah Almighty, but rather heard it from a tree, and that they claim that the Jew when he hears the speech of Allah from the prophet, peace be upon him, is of higher rank in this sense than Moses ibn 'Imran, peace be upon him; because the Jew heard it from a prophet of Allah's prophets, and Moses heard it as created in a tree, and if it were created in a tree, then he would not have been spoken to by Allah from behind a veil; because whoever was present at the tree from jinn and humans would have heard the speech from that place, and the way of Moses and others would be the same in that it is not Allah's speech to him from behind a veil.
Source pages p. 79

Commentary
This passage presents a proof from the Quran (42:51) that Allah's speech cannot be created. The argument: if Allah's speech were always created in some created thing (like a tree), then the distinction between direct revelation, behind a veil, or via an angel would be meaningless, because everyone could hear that created speech from that thing. Moreover, if Moses heard speech created in a tree, then anyone hearing speech from a prophet would be of higher rank than Moses, as they hear from a human prophet while Moses heard from a tree. This leads to absurdities: a Jew hearing from a prophet would be superior to Moses, and Moses would not have been spoken to from behind a veil (since the tree was public).

Key Terms
كلام الله
مخلوق
جهمية
وراء حجاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the verse directly states that Allah's speech is uncreated, but it is an argument by implication and reason.
Correction: The verse describes modes of communication; the argument against createdness is a rational deduction from the absurd consequences of the opposing view.

Checkpoint
What is the main argument Imam Ash'ari makes from verse 42:51 against the createdness of Allah's speech?
A) The verse proves that Allah speaks through angels only.
B) If speech were created in something, then the modes of revelation would be meaningless and the rank of prophets would be diminished.
C) The verse shows that Moses heard speech from a tree.
D) The verse proves that Allah's speech is eternal.

Key Takeaway
Allah's speech cannot be created because the modes of revelation would lose their meaning and the rank of prophets would be absurdly diminished, as shown by the reductio on Moses.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Quran, do you truly feel that Allah is speaking directly to you, or do you treat it as ordinary words? How does this lesson's argument challenge your daily reverence for the Quran?
?
The reductio shows that if Moses heard created speech, his rank would be diminished. Does your heart recognize the uniqueness of prophetic direct communication? How does that recognition shape your love for the prophets and your urgency to follow their message?
?
If you were to accept that Allah's speech is created, you would have to accept that a Jew hearing the Quran from the Prophet outranks Moses. Does that thought unsettle you? What does your emotional response reveal about your belief in the uncreatedness of the Quran?

6 min
The Foreleg Argument: Exposing Inconsistency
Imam Ash'ari refutes the Jahmiyya using the consistency argument from the foreleg.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses the foreleg hadith to expose the logical inconsistency in the Jahmiyya's claim that Allah's speech is created.

Core Idea
Consistency forces the Jahmiyya to either accept absurdity or abandon their position.

Imam Ash'ari presents a masterful refutation by consistency. The Jahmiyya claim that Allah's speech to Moses was created in a tree. Yet they accept the hadith of a speaking foreleg. Ash'ari shows that their own logic leads to a blasphemous conclusion, forcing them to choose between inconsistency and absurdity. This lesson walks through the reasoning step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to get the flow of the argument.
Notice that the passage is a continuous reductio ad absurdum.
2
Identify the two examples
The Jahmiyya use the tree as an example of created speech. The foreleg hadith is another example they accept. Identify both.
The foreleg is from the hadith of the poisoned sheep.
3
Understand the logical consequence
If the foreleg's speech is created by Allah, then it must be Allah's speech. That would mean Allah said 'Do not eat me'—an absurdity.
The argument forces a choice: either accept the absurdity or reject the premise.
4
See the forced choice
If they deny that the foreleg's speech is Allah's, they must also deny that the tree's speech is Allah's, contradicting their original claim.
Thus their position is self-defeating.
5
Reflect on the method
Reflect on how this refutation uses the opponent's own accepted premises to destroy their argument.
This is a common method in kalam: ilzam (forcing a contradiction).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم يقال لهم: إذا زعمتم أن معنى أن الله عز وجل كلم موسى أنه خلق كلاماً كلمه به في الشجرة، وقد خلق الله عندكم في الذراع كلاماً؛ لأن الذراع قالت للرسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم: لا تأكلني فإني مسمومة. فلزمكم أن ذلك الكلام الذي سمعه النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم كلام الله عز وجل، فإن استحال أن يكون الله تكلم بذلك الكلام المخلوق فما أنكرتم من أنه مستحيل أن يخلق الله عز وجل كلامه في شجرة؛ لأن كلام المخلوق لا يكون كلاماً لله، فإن كان كلام الله، وكان معنى أن الله تكلم عندكم أنه خلق الكلام فيلزمكم أن يكون الله متكلماً بالكلام الذي خلقه في الذراع. فإن أجابوا إلى ذلك قيل لهم: فالله عز وجل على قولكم هو القائل: لا تأكلني فإني مسمومة تعالى الله عن قولكم وافترائكم عليه علواً كبيراً. وإن قالوا: لا يجوز أن يكون كلام الله مخلوقاً في ذراع. قيل لهم: وكذلك لا يجوز أن يكون كلام الله مخلوقاً في شجرة.
Then it is said to them: When you claim that the meaning of 'Allah spoke to Moses' is that He created speech with which He spoke to him in the tree, and according to you Allah created speech in the foreleg; because the foreleg said to the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him: 'Do not eat me, for I am poisoned.' So it is necessary upon you that that speech which the Prophet heard is the speech of Allah Almighty. If it is impossible that Allah spoke with that created speech, then why do you deny that it is impossible that Allah created His speech in a tree? Because the speech of a created being cannot be the speech of Allah. If it is the speech of Allah, and the meaning of 'Allah spoke' according to you is that He created the speech, then it follows that Allah is a speaker with the speech He created in the foreleg. If they agree to that, it is said to them: Then Allah Almighty, according to your statement, is the one who said: 'Do not eat me, for I am poisoned.' Exalted is Allah above your statement and your slander against Him, a great exaltation. And if they say: It is not permissible for Allah's speech to be created in a foreleg, it is said to them: Likewise, it is not permissible for Allah's speech to be created in a tree.
Source pages p. 80

Commentary
This is a refutation by consistency. The Jahmiyya claim Allah's speech to Moses was created in a tree. But they also accept the hadith that a foreleg (of a poisoned sheep) spoke to the Prophet ﷺ, warning him. If they say Allah created speech in that foreleg, then that speech must also be Allah's speech. But then Allah would be saying 'Do not eat me' according to them, which is absurd and blasphemous. If they deny that the foreleg's speech is Allah's, then by the same logic they must deny that the tree's speech is Allah's. Thus their position is inconsistent.

Key Terms
ذراع
كلام مخلوق
سم
حديث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think Imam Ash'ari is rejecting the foreleg hadith or denying that the foreleg spoke.
Correction: Ash'ari accepts the hadith; he uses it to expose the inconsistency in the Jahmiyya's position. The argument does not question the authenticity of the hadith.

Checkpoint
What is the point of the foreleg (ذراع) argument in refuting the Jahmiyya?
A) To prove that animals can speak.
B) To show that the hadith contradicts the Qur'an.
C) To demonstrate that if they accept the foreleg speech as created by Allah, then they must accept that Allah said 'Do not eat me', which is absurd.
D) To prove that Allah does not speak at all.

Key Takeaway
The foreleg argument exposes a fatal inconsistency in the Jahmiyya's doctrine of created speech by showing that their own logic leads either to blasphemy or self-contradiction.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever held a belief that, when pressed to its logical conclusions, would force you to admit something absurd or impious? What did you do when you realized that inconsistency?
?
This lesson shows how dangerous it is to accept premises without thinking through their implications. In your own understanding of Allah, which of His attributes are you most likely to take for granted without examining whether they are consistent with His majesty?
?
Would you rather cling to a comfortable but logically contradictory belief about Allah, or accept a difficult truth that makes you uncomfortable? What does your answer reveal about your desire for truth over comfort?

The Wolf's Speech and the Absurdity of Jahmiyya's View
Imam Ash'ari poses a rhetorical question and a reductio ad absurdum using the wolf's speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses the example of the speaking wolf to expose a logical flaw in the Jahmiyya's doctrine of created speech.

Core Idea
If the wolf's speech is Allah's, then the wolf surpasses Moses – an absurdity.

Imam Ash'ari continues his refutation of the Jahmiyya by presenting a specific example from the Sunnah: the wolf that spoke by Allah's power. He uses this to show the unacceptable consequences of their claim that Allah's speech is created. This passage is a masterful reductio ad absurdum, forcing the opponent into an impossible position.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and the translation
Read the Arabic and English carefully. Notice the structure: a question, then a reductio, then a supporting hadith.
2
Identify the two parts of the argument
The first part draws a comparison between the wolf and Moses. The second part cites the actual hadith of the wolf.
The hadith is authenticated.
3
Understand the reductio
If the created speech in the wolf is Allah's speech, then the wolf heard Allah directly – making it better than Moses who heard from a tree. This is absurd, so the premise must be wrong.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
The Jahmiyya cannot accept that the wolf is better than Moses, so they must reject the idea that created speech is Allah's speech.
This supports the Ash'ari view that Allah's speech is an eternal attribute.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم يسألون عن الكلام الذي أنطق الله تعالى به الذئب لما أخبر عن نبوة النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم، فيقال لهم: إذا كان الله عز وجل يتكلم بكلام يخلقه في الذئب، فقد كان ينبغي أن يكون الذئب أفضل من موسى عليه السلام، لأنه سمع كلام الله منه، وموسى إنما سمع من الشجرة. ويقال لهم: أليس أن الله تبارك وتعالى أنطق الذئب حتى قال: من لها يوم السبع، يوم لا راعي لها غيري؟ قال: آمنت به أنا وأبو بكر وعمر.
Then they are asked about the speech by which Allah enabled the wolf to speak when it informed about the prophethood of the Prophet ﷺ. So it is said to them: If Allah Almighty speaks with speech that He creates in the wolf, then the wolf should be better than Moses, peace be upon him, because he heard the speech of Allah from it, while Moses only heard from the tree. And it is said to them: Did not Allah, Blessed and Exalted, enable the wolf to speak until it said: 'Who will protect them on the day of the wild beast, the day when there is no shepherd for them but me?' He said: 'I believe in it, I and Abu Bakr and Umar.'
Source pages p. 80

Commentary
This passage poses a further question to the Jahmiyya. If Allah creates speech in the wolf (as in the hadith where the wolf spoke about the Day of Judgment and the Prophet testified to it), then according to their own principle, the speech created in the wolf must be Allah's speech. But then the wolf, hearing Allah's speech directly, would be superior to Moses who heard from a tree. This is absurd, showing that their understanding of how Allah speaks is flawed.

Key Terms
ذئب
أنطق
كلام الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Jahmiyya thought that the speech Allah creates in something (like the wolf) is actually Allah's eternal speech.
Correction: Imam Ash'ari shows that if that were true, the wolf would be superior to Moses, which is impossible. Therefore, created speech is not Allah's speech; Allah's speech is an eternal attribute.

Checkpoint
What does the example of the wolf aim to show against the Jahmiyya?
A) That animals can be prophets.
B) That if Allah creates speech in the wolf, then the wolf would be superior to Moses, which is absurd.
C) That the wolf's speech is not real.
D) That Moses heard speech from the wolf.

Key Takeaway
Imam Ash'ari uses the wolf's speech to show that if created speech were Allah's speech, it would lead to the absurd conclusion that the wolf is superior to Moses.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever unknowingly treated the Qur'an as a created book by not feeling its eternal weight in your heart? How does this argument affect your reverence when you recite it?
?
If you had to choose: would you rather hear Allah's speech from a wolf or from a tree? Why does the distinction matter for your understanding of prophethood and divine attributes?
?
This argument exposes the absurdity of conflating created and uncreated. In your own life, where might you be confusing temporal creations (people, status, wealth) with Allah's eternal attributes, leading to misplaced priorities?

Refutation of Created Speech via the Speaking Arm
Imam Ashari refutes the Mu'tazilite claim that God's speech is created in something else by analogy with the speaking arm.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses the analogy of the speaking arm to refute the Mu'tazilite doctrine of created speech, exposing its logical inconsistency.

Core Idea
The Mu'tazilite view leads to the absurd conclusion that God said 'Do not eat me.'

This lesson presents one of Imam Ashari's sharpest refutations. He confronts the Mu'tazilites with a dilemma: if they claim God's speech is created in a tree, then by the same logic, the speech from the arm of a sheep (a hadith) would also be God's speech, leading to a blatantly absurd statement. The argument hinges on consistency.

Guided Path
1
Understand the Mu'tazilite claim
The Mu'tazilites say that when Allah spoke to Musa, He created speech in the tree. Recognize that this is their starting position.
They hold that speech is an accident and cannot be an attribute of God.
2
Recognize the parallel case
Imam Ashari brings up the hadith where a foreleg of a sheep speaks to the Prophet, warning it is poisoned. The Mu'tazilites accept this as a miracle, but they must also consider the speech created in that arm.
3
See the dilemma
If they consider the arm's speech as created, then by their own logic it must be God's speech, making God say 'Do not eat me' – an absurdity. If they deny it's God's speech, then they contradict their own principle about the tree. Either way, their position fails.
This shows their doctrine is internally inconsistent.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم يقال لهم : إذا زعمتم أن معنى أن الله عز وجل كلّم موسى أنه خلق كلاماً كلمه به في الشجرة، وقد خلق الله عندكم في الذراع كلاماً؛ لأن الذراع قالت: لا تَأْكُلْني فَإِنِّي مَسمُومَة . فلزمكم أن ذلك الكلام الذي سمعه النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم كلام الله عز وجل، فإن استحال أن يكون الله تكلم بذلك الكلام المخلوق فما أنكرتم من أنه مستحيل أن يخلق الله عز وجل كلامه في شجرة؛ لأن كلام المخلوق لا يكون كلاماً لله، فإن كان كلام الله، وكان معنى أن الله تكلم عندكم أنه خلق الكلام، فيلزمكم أن يكون الله متكلماً بالكلام الذي خلقه في الذراع. فإن أجابوا إلى ذلك قيل لهم : فالله عز وجل على قولكم هو القائل: لا تأكلني فإني مسمومة تعالى الله عن قولكم وافترائكم عليه علواً كبيراً . وإن قالوا: لا يجوز أن يكون كلام الله مخلوقاً في ذراع. قيل لهم : وكذلك لا يجوز أن يكون كلام الله مخلوقاً في شجرة .
Then it is said to them: If you claim that the meaning of Allah, Exalted and Majestic, speaking to Musa is that He created speech with which He spoke to him in the tree, and you hold that Allah created speech in the arm (of the sheep), because the arm said: 'Do not eat me, for I am poisoned.' Then it necessarily follows that that speech which the Prophet (peace be upon him) heard is the speech of Allah, Exalted and Majestic. If it is impossible that Allah spoke by that created speech, then why do you deny that it is impossible that Allah, Exalted and Majestic, creates His speech in a tree? Because the speech of a created being cannot be the speech of Allah. If it were the speech of Allah, and according to you the meaning of Allah speaking is that He created speech, then it necessarily follows that Allah is the speaker of the speech He created in the arm. If they answer affirmatively, it is said to them: Then Allah, Exalted and Majestic, according to your statement, is the one who says: 'Do not eat me, for I am poisoned'—exalted is Allah above your statement and your slander against Him, with great exaltation. And if they say: It is not permissible that the speech of Allah be created in an arm, it is said to them: Likewise, it is not permissible that the speech of Allah be created in a tree.
Source pages p. 80

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of the Mu'tazilite doctrine that God's speech is created in something else. The author presents a dilemma to the opponents. They claim that God created speech in the tree for Musa. However, they also accept a hadith in which the foreleg of a sheep (الذراع) spoke to the Prophet, warning him not to eat it because it was poisoned. If the speech from the arm is created, then according to their own principle, it would be the speech of God, which is absurd because God would then be saying 'Do not eat me.' If they deny that the arm's speech is God's speech, then they contradict themselves regarding the tree. Thus, the argument exposes the inconsistency: if the arm's speech cannot be God's speech, neither can the tree's speech. The author concludes by affirming that God is far above such attributions.

Key Terms
كلام الله
كلام مخلوق
ذراع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the argument is simply that the arm's speech is impossible or that the miracle is being denied.
Correction: The point is the logical contradiction: the Mu'tazilites cannot consistently hold that speech in a tree is God's speech while denying that speech in an arm is God's speech, because the same principle applies.

Checkpoint
According to Imam Ashari, why does the Mu'tazilite position on God's speech lead to absurdity?
A) Because it requires God to be a created being
B) Because it forces them to accept that God spoke through the arm of a sheep, which is absurd
C) Because it denies the miracle of the tree
D) Because it contradicts the Quran

Key Takeaway
The Mu'tazilite doctrine of created speech is self-refuting because it leads to an absurd consequence when applied consistently.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you reflect on the Mu'tazilite attempt to protect God's transcendence by denying His speech an eternal attribute, can you see that same well-meaning but misguided reasoning in your own efforts to 'protect' Allah's majesty? In what area of your life are you prone to rationalize an interpretation that, on deeper scrutiny, leads to an absurd consequence about Allah? What will you do to correct it today?
?
The argument shows that a small inconsistency in doctrine can lead to attributing something unworthy to Allah. Where in your own beliefs about Allah—perhaps in your understanding of His attributes or actions—do you have a hidden inconsistency that you haven't confronted? What would it cost you to resolve it, and are you willing to pay that price?
?
The arm's speech, if taken as created, would make Allah say 'Do not eat me.' This is obviously false, yet the Mu'tazilites persisted. Is there any belief you hold about Allah that, if followed to its logical conclusion, would lead to something you know deep down is beneath His majesty? Will you examine it now and seek the truth regardless of the discomfort?

The Wolf's Speech: A Proof That God's Speech Is Uncreated
Imam Ashari uses the miraculous wolf speech to refute created speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses the miraculous speech of the wolf to argue that God's speech is not created in a creature.

Core Idea
If a miracle speaks, its speech must be divine, not created.

Imam Ashari challenges the Mu'tazilite claim that God's speech is created in something else. He brings a powerful example: the wolf that spoke to testify to Muhammad's prophethood. This miraculous speech, if attributed to a created wolf, would lead to absurd consequences. Let's see how he forces the Mu'tazilites into a corner.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation carefully. Notice the question-and-answer style.
The passage is a rhetorical question to the Mu'tazilites.
2
Identify the Mu'tazilite premise
The Mu'tazilites believe God creates speech in another being. Understand this assumption.
This is the key premise Ashari uses against them.
3
Apply the premise to the wolf
If God creates speech in another, then the wolf's speech must be God's speech because it is miraculous.
4
Consider the reductio ad absurdum
If the wolf is the speaker, then the tree at Sinai could also be the speaker, leading to the blasphemy that a created tree said 'I am Allah.'
This shows the absurdity of the Mu'tazilite position.
5
Conclude the argument
Thus the Mu'tazilites must admit the wolf's speech is God's own speech, which contradicts their view that all speech is created.
The miraculous nature proves it is divine.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم يسألون عن الكلام الذي أنطق الله تعالى به الذئب لما أخبر عن نبوة النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم، فيقال لهم : إذا كان الله عز وجل يتكلم بكلام يخلقه في غيره، فما أنكرتم أن يكون الكلام الذي سمعه من الذئب كلاماً لله، ويكون إعجازه يدل على أنه كلام الله تعالى، وفي هذا ما يجب عليهم أن الذئب لم يتكلم به وأنه كلام الله عز وجل؛ لأن كون الكلام من الذئب معجز، كما أن كونه من الشجرة معجز، فإن كان الذئب متكلماً بذلك الكلام المنقول، فما أنكرتم أن الشجرة متكلمة بالكلام إن كان خُلِقَ في شجرة، وأن يكون المخلوق قال: ﴿ يَمُوسَى إِنّى أَنَا اللهُ ﴾ تعالى عن ذلك علواً كبيراً.
Then they are asked about the speech which Allah, Exalted, caused the wolf to utter when it informed of the prophethood of the Prophet (peace be upon him). It is said to them: If Allah, Exalted and Majestic, speaks by speech which He creates in something other than Himself, then why do you deny that the speech which was heard from the wolf is the speech of Allah, and that its miraculousness indicates that it is the speech of Allah, Exalted? And in this is what necessitates for them that the wolf did not speak it, and that it is the speech of Allah, Exalted and Majestic, because the occurrence of speech from the wolf is miraculous, just as its occurrence from the tree is miraculous. If the wolf is the speaker of that transmitted speech, then why do you deny that the tree is the speaker of the speech if it was created in a tree, and that the created being said: 'O Musa, indeed I am Allah'? Exalted is He above that, with great exaltation.
Source pages p. 80

Commentary
This passage presents a further argument against the Mu'tazilites by referring to the hadith in which a wolf spoke to a shepherd, testifying to the prophethood of Muhammad. The author argues: if God creates speech in something else, then the speech from the wolf must be God's speech because it is miraculous. The miraculous nature (إعجاز) demands that it be divine speech, not the wolf's own speech. If the wolf were the speaker, then by analogy, why not also accept that the tree was the speaker when God spoke to Musa? That would lead to the absurdity that a created being (a tree) claimed divinity by saying 'I am Allah.' Thus, the Mu'tazilites are forced to deny that the wolf spoke, which contradicts their principle that the speech is created in the animal.

Key Terms
ذئب
إعجاز
معجز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think Imam Ashari is disputing the authenticity of the wolf story. Actually, he uses it as a valid premise to highlight logical inconsistency.
Correction: The argument does not depend on whether the story is authentic; it is a hypothetical that exposes the flaw in the Mu'tazilite principle.

Checkpoint
According to Imam Ashari's argument, what does the miraculous speech of the wolf indicate?
A) That wolves can speak like humans
B) That the speech was created in the wolf
C) That the speech must be God's speech
D) That the wolf is a prophet

Key Takeaway
The miraculous speech of the wolf proves it cannot be created speech; it must be the uncreated speech of Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The wolf spoke a truth about prophethood. When you hear the Quran, do you hear it as the very speech of Allah, or as just another story? What in your life needs to change so that every verse trembles your heart?
?
The Mu'tazilites thought they were defending God's transcendence by saying His speech is created. Are there areas in your worship where you try to 'protect' Allah with your own reasoning, only to fall into error? Will you submit to the revealed truth even when it challenges your logic?
?
If the wolf's speech could not be created speech, then the Quran is directly from Allah, not a created thing. When you recite a verse, do you feel the weight that you are uttering the uncreated? Commit today to recite at least one verse with the awe that it is Allah speaking to you.

Livingness Requirement for Speech Creation Refuted
Imam Ashari refutes the objection that speech can only be created in a living being.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Imam Ashari's refutation of the Mu'tazilite objection that speech can only be created in a living being, and how he uses logical consistency and a Quranic verse to undermine their position.

Core Idea
God's speech is not limited by the livingness of its locus.

Imam Ashari now turns to a potential objection from the Mu'tazilites: that speech can only be created in a living being, so how could the Quran be created in the tree? He counters with a logical inversion, showing the inconsistency of their own position. He also invokes the Quranic testimony that the heavens and earth 'spoke' to Allah, further undermining the objection.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation together. Notice how the objection is presented and then refuted.
Pay attention to the logical structure: 'if... then...'.
2
Identify the opponent's objection
The Mu'tazilites argue that speech can only originate in a living being, so the tree cannot be the speaker of the Quran.
3
Understand Ashari's counter-argument
Ashari turns the objection around: if speech is created in a non-living thing (tree), then the creator of that speech (Allah) is living, so there is no contradiction. If they insist on livingness, then they must explain why Allah cannot create speech in a non-living thing.
This is a reductio ad absurdum.
4
Examine the Quranic evidence
Ashari cites Quran 41:11 where the heavens and earth 'speak' to Allah, showing that inanimate objects can be described as speaking by Allah's power.
This verse is used to support the permissibility of speech being attributed to non-living things.
5
Reflect on the logical implication
Consider how this argument defends the orthodox view that the Quran is created in a specific location (the tree) while affirming that Allah is the sole Creator and Speaker.
This refutes the Mu'tazilite claim that speech requires a living subject.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم يقال لهم : إذا كان كلام الله عز وجل مخلوقاً في غيره عندكم، فما يؤمنكم أن يكون كل كلام تسمعونه مخلوقاً في شيء، وهو حق أن يكون كلام الله عز وجل؟ فإن قالوا: لا تكون الشجرة متكلمة ؛ لأن المتكلم لا يكون إلا حياً . قيل لهم ولا يجوز خَلْقُ الكلام في شجرة؛ لأن من خَلَقَ الكلام فيه لا يكون إلا حياً، فإن جاز أن يُخلَقَ الكلام فيما ليس بحي، فلم لا يجوز أن يتكلم من ليس بحي؟ ويقال لهم : لم لا قلتم إنه يقول من ليس بحي، لأنه عز وجل أخبر أن السماوات والأرض: ﴿ قَالَتَا أَتَيْنَا طَائِعِينَ ﴾ [فصلت: ١١].
Then it is said to them: If the speech of Allah, Exalted and Majestic, is created in something other than Himself according to you, then what prevents you from thinking that every speech you hear is created in something, and that it is truly the speech of Allah, Exalted and Majestic? If they say: The tree cannot be a speaker, because a speaker is only living. It is said to them: It is also not permissible to create speech in a tree, because the one who creates speech in it is only living. If it is permissible that speech be created in that which is not living, then why is it not permissible that one who is not living speaks? And it is said to them: Why do you not say that one who is not living speaks, because He, Exalted and Majestic, informed that the heavens and the earth: 'They said, "We come willingly."'
Source pages p. 81

Commentary
This passage is a response to a possible objection from the Mu'tazilites. They might argue that the tree cannot be the speaker because speech requires a living agent. The author turns the objection around: if speech can be created in something non-living (like a tree), then the creator of that speech (God) is living. So there is no problem. But if they insist that only a living being can be the locus of speech, then why do they allow God to create speech in a tree? The author also cites the Quranic verse where the heavens and earth speak, indicating that inanimate objects can be used by God to 'speak' in a metaphorical sense. This undercuts the Mu'tazilite claim that speech cannot originate from non-living things.

Key Terms
حي
خلق الكلام
السماوات والأرض

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the argument is about physical speech by inanimate objects, but it is about the logical consistency of the opponents' position.
Correction: The argument is a logical reductio against the Mu'tazilite condition that speech requires a living locus. It does not claim that trees literally speak, but that if speech can be created in a non-living thing, then the non-living thing can be said to 'speak' metaphorically by God's power.

Checkpoint
How does Imam Ashari counter the objection that a speaker must be living?
A) He denies that God is living
B) He cites a Quranic verse where heavens and earth speak
C) He argues that trees are alive
D) He says that speech does not require a speaker

Key Takeaway
Imam Ashari refutes the livingness requirement by showing that if speech can be created in a non-living thing, then the living one is the Creator, and the non-living can be said to 'speak' by God's action, as evidenced by the Quran.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that the heavens and earth 'said, We come willingly', do you truly accept that God can make inanimate objects speak, or does your mind resist because it feels impossible? What does that resistance reveal about your reliance on your own reasoning over divine revelation? Will you commit to affirming what Allah has said, even when it defies your logical expectations?
?
The Mu'tazilites argued that speech can only come from a living being, and they used that to deny that the Quran is God's eternal speech. Have you ever placed a condition on God's power based on your own limited understanding of how things work? What condition would you repent of today?
?
If God can create speech in a tree and still call it His speech, then your own ability to speak is ultimately a gift from Him. Do you use your speech to honor Him as the true source of all speech, or do you act as if your words are your own? What will you say differently tomorrow to acknowledge His sovereignty over your tongue?

The Curse on Iblis Proves God's Speech is Uncreated
Imam Ashari uses the eternal curse on Iblis to argue that God's speech cannot be created.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses the Qur'anic curse on Iblis to argue that God's speech must be eternal and not created.

Core Idea
The eternal curse on Iblis proves God's speech cannot be created.

In this passage, Imam Ashari presents a concise logical argument against the Mu'tazilite claim that God's speech is created. He cites the Qur'anic verse where Allah curses Iblis until the Day of Judgment, and then draws a necessary implication: if the speech were created, it would be perishable, but the curse endures, so it cannot be created. This argument underscores the distinction between God's eternal attributes and temporary created things.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Begin by reading the Arabic text and its literal translation. Note the question-and-answer format and the logical structure.
The argument is cut off at the end, but the main premises are clear.
2
Identify the key premise
Find the verse about the curse and note its duration: 'until the Day of Judgment'. Then observe the conditional statement: if speech is created, it is perishable.
The word 'fānīyāt' means perishable things.
3
Understand the logical implication
Combine the two premises: the curse is permanent, but created things are perishable. Therefore, the curse (and by extension God's speech) cannot be created. Try to complete the missing conclusion in your own words.
This reasoning shows the necessity of God's speech being uncreated.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم يقال لهم : أليس قد قال الله عز وجل لإبليس : ﴿ وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَى إِلَى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ ﴾ [ص: ٧٨] فلا بد من نعم، ويقال لهم : فإذا كان كلام الله مخلوقاً، وكانت المخلوقات فانيات؛ فيلزمكم إذا أفنى الله عز وجل الأشياء أن تكون
Then it is said to them: Did not Allah, Exalted and Majestic, say to Iblis: 'And indeed, upon you is My curse until the Day of Judgment'? They must answer yes. And it is said to them: If the speech of Allah is created, and created things are perishable, then it necessarily follows for you that when Allah, Exalted and Majestic, annihilates all things, that
Source pages p. 81

Commentary
This passage introduces a further argument against the createdness of God's speech. The author asks the Mu'tazilites to affirm that God cursed Iblis until the Day of Judgment. Then he argues: if God's speech (the curse) is created, then it is subject to annihilation like all other created things. But the curse is explicitly stated to last until the Day of Judgment, implying its permanence. Therefore, it cannot be created. The argument is cut off, but the conclusion is that the curse must be eternal (uncreated).

Key Terms

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think the argument is about the literal pronunciation or physical sound of the curse, not the divine attribute of speech.
Correction: The argument focuses on the nature of God's speech as an eternal attribute, not on its temporal expression. The curse is a form of God's speech, and its permanence indicates that God's speech itself is eternal and uncreated.

Checkpoint
What does the curse on Iblis imply about God's speech according to Imam Ashari?
A) That it is created like other things
B) That it is non-existent
C) That it must be eternal and uncreated
D) That it is only metaphorical

Key Takeaway
The curse on Iblis, being perpetual until the Day of Judgment, demonstrates that God's speech is not subject to annihilation and thus is uncreated and eternal.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
The Mu'tazilites thought God's speech was created and therefore temporary. Are there aspects of Allah's commands in your life that you treat as temporary or negotiable? Which ones?
?
Iblis's curse is everlasting. How does that reality shape your view of disobeying Allah? Does the permanence of divine justice make you fear sin more?

Refuting the Mu'tazilite Objection on Speech Locus
Al-Ash'ari refutes the Mu'tazilite objection that only a living being can be the locus of God's speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that God can create speech in non-living things, and that the Quran itself attributes speech to non-living beings, refuting the Mu'tazilite claim.

Core Idea
God's speech is not limited to living beings; He creates it wherever He wills.

Al-Ash'ari presents a crisp logical refutation of the Mu'tazilite claim that only a living being can be the locus of God's speech. He first turns their own assumptions against them, then seals the argument with a Quranic example. This passage is a masterclass in theological argumentation, showing how scripture and reason work together.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the back-and-forth structure: Al-Ash'ari poses a question, presents the Mu'tazilite objection, then refutes it.
The argument is dialectical, typical of kalam.
2
Identify the Mu'tazilite position
What exactly do the Mu'tazilites claim about where speech must be created? Look for their objection about the tree.
They claim only a living being can be the subject of speech.
3
Follow Al-Ash'ari's refutation
Al-Ash'ari uses a reductio ad absurdum: if speech is created in a non-living thing, then that thing can be described as speaking. He then cites the Quranic verse about the heavens and earth speaking.
4
Reflect on the broader principle
This argument establishes that God's speech is not dependent on a living locus. It can be created in anything, and the Quranic description overrides human assumptions.
This is a key point in the doctrine of the uncreatedness of the Quran.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم يقال لهم : إذا كان كلام الله عز وجل مخلوقاً في غيره عندكم، فما يؤمنكم أن يكون كل كلام تسمعونه مخلوقاً في شيء، وهو حق أن يكون كلام الله عز وجل؟ فإن قالوا: لا تكون الشجرة متكلمة ؛ لأن المتكلم لا يكون إلا حياً . قيل لهم ولا يجوز خَلْقُ الكلام في شجرة؛ لأن من خَلَقَ الكلام فيه لا يكون إلا حياً، فإن جاز أن يُخلَقَ الكلام فيما ليس بحي، فلم لا يجوز أن يتكلم من ليس بحي؟ ويقال لهم : لم لا قلتم إنه يقول من ليس بحي، لأنه عز وجل أخبر أن السماوات والأرض: ( قَالَتَا أَنينا طايعينَ ) [فصلت : ١١] .
Then it is said to them: If the speech of Allah Almighty is created in other than Him according to you, what prevents you from thinking that every speech you hear is created in something, and it is true that it is the speech of Allah Almighty? If they say: The tree cannot be speaking because the speaker is only alive. It is said to them: And it is not permissible to create speech in a tree because the one who creates speech in it is only alive. If it is permissible that speech be created in what is not alive, then why is it not permissible for what is not alive to speak? And it is said to them: Why do you not say that what is not alive speaks, because He Almighty informed that the heavens and the earth (both said: 'We come willingly') [Fussilat:11].
Source pages p. 81

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of the Mu'tazilite claim that God's speech must be created in a living subject. Al-Ash'ari uses a logical argument: If the Mu'tazilites allow that God's speech can be created in a non-living thing (like the wolf or tree, as claimed earlier), then any speech we hear could potentially be God's speech. When they object that a tree cannot be a speaker because only a living being can speak, Al-Ash'ari turns their own logic against them: if speech is created in a non-living thing by God, then the act of creation is performed by a living being (God), but the locus itself does not need to be alive. The analogy with the wolf (mentioned earlier) shows inconsistency. He then supports this with Quranic evidence: the heavens and the earth spoke, proving that non-living things can be described as speaking when God creates speech in them.

Key Terms
كلام الله
مخلوق
الشجرة
المتكلم
حي
إعجاز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only a living being can be the locus of speech, so God's speech must be created in a living being.
Correction: The Quran attributes speech to non-living things (e.g., heavens and earth), and God can create speech in anything without the locus needing to be alive.

Checkpoint
What is the Mu'tazilite objection that Al-Ash'ari addresses in this passage?
A) That only a living being can be a speaker.
B) That the Quran was revealed in Arabic.
C) That the wolf spoke literally.
D) That God's speech is eternal.

Key Takeaway
God's speech is not bound to a living locus; He creates it in anything, as evidenced by the Quran's attribution of speech to the heavens and earth.
