Consistency in Divine Attributes: Speech and Power
Al-Ash'ari exposes inconsistency in opponents' reasoning about divine attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses logical consistency to refute those who affirm speech but deny knowledge of Allah.

Core Idea
Consistency demands that if speech is specific, so is power.

Al-Ash'ari presents a sharp logical argument against opponents who accept that Allah has speech but reject that He has knowledge. He shows that the very reasoning they use would force them to accept that Allah is power, which they do not. This exposes the inconsistency in their position.

Guided Path
1
Read the argument
Read the Arabic passage and its literal translation to grasp the logical flow. Notice the structure: opponent's claim, analogy, reference to Qadariyyah, conclusion.
2
Identify the opponent's premise
The opponent affirms speech (kalam) but denies knowledge (ilm) because speech is more specific (akhass) than knowledge.
What is the reasoning behind this? They think a more specific attribute implies a more general one? Actually they affirm the specific without the general.
3
Al-Ash'ari's counter
Al-Ash'ari says: by the same logic, since knowledge is more general than power (qudra), you should affirm power as a specific attribute. He notes that the Qadariyyah actually hold that Allah cannot create disbelief, which means power is more specific.
This is a reductio: if your reasoning leads to a conclusion you reject, your reasoning is flawed.
4
Understand the reference to Qadariyyah
The Qadariyyah sect denied Allah's power over human actions like disbelief. They held that power is more specific than knowledge because it does not cover all things. Al-Ash'ari uses their own position to show inconsistency.
This strengthens the argument by showing the opponent already implicitly accepts the logic they reject.
5
Conclude the inconsistency
Al-Ash'ari concludes that the opponent must either accept power as an attribute of Allah or abandon their reasoning about speech and knowledge. Their position is self-contradictory.
This is a classic kalam method: use the opponent's own premises to force a contradiction.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم يقال لهم: إذا أوجبتم أن لله كلاماً وليس له علم؛ لأن الكلام أخص من العلم والعلم أعم منه، فقولوا: إن الله قدرة؛ لأن العلم أعندكم من القدرة؛ لأن من مذاهب القدرية أنهم لا يقولون إن الله لا يقدر أن يخلق الكفر، فقد أثبتوا القدرة أخص من العلم، فينبغي لهم أن يقولوا على اعتلالهم إن الله قدرة.
Then it is said to them: If you affirm that Allah has speech but He does not have knowledge, because speech is more specific than knowledge and knowledge is more general than it, then say that Allah has power, because knowledge according to you is more general than power; because from the doctrines of the Qadariyyah is that they do not say that Allah cannot create disbelief, so they have affirmed power as more specific than knowledge. So it is incumbent upon them, according to their reasoning, to say that Allah is power.
Source pages p. 116

Commentary
This passage is a refutation argument against those who claim Allah has speech (kalam) but deny He has knowledge (ilm). Al-Ash'ari points out an inconsistency: if they argue that speech is more specific (akhass) than knowledge, and knowledge is more general (a'amm), then by the same logic, they should also say Allah is power (qudra) because knowledge is more general than power. He notes that the Qadariyyah (a sect) actually affirm that Allah cannot create disbelief, implying that power is more specific than knowledge. Therefore, their own reasoning forces them to attribute power to Allah just as they attribute speech. The argument is a reductio ad absurdum: if they deny knowledge based on specificity, they must also deny power, but they don't—so their position is inconsistent.

Key Terms
كلام
علم
قدرة
أخص
أعم
القدرية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: This argument means that speech and power are identical attributes.
Correction: It does not equate them; it only shows that the opponent's reasoning about specificity is inconsistent.

Checkpoint
What does al-Ash'ari argue the Qadariyyah must say according to their own reasoning about speech and knowledge?
A) Allah is power
B) Allah is not powerful
C) Knowledge is more specific than speech
D) Speech is more general than power

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's argument shows that denying knowledge while affirming speech is logically untenable because it would force one to also affirm power, which the opponents reject.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You believe Allah is all-knowing and all-powerful, but do you sometimes act as if His knowledge is limited to what you understand? In what area of your life must you submit to His full knowledge and power?
?
The Qadariyyah limited Allah's power to avoid attributing evil to Him. What is one belief or attitude you hold that unconsciously diminishes Allah's absolute power over your life? What will you do today to correct it?
?
Al-Ash'ari exposes inconsistency in reasoning about divine attributes. When you examine your own beliefs, are there inconsistencies you have been comfortable ignoring? Will you this week confront one such inconsistency with the demand of divine revelation?

Consistency Between Descriptions of Allah: Speech and Knowledge
Al-Ash'ari questions the inconsistency of denying knowledge while affirming speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses the relationship between general and specific attributes to argue for the consistency of Allah's descriptions.

Core Idea
Specificity does not negate generality in divine attributes.

Al-Ash'ari continues his logical argument by examining the attribute of knowledge (ʿilm) in relation to speech (kalām). He points out that 'knowing' is a more general description than 'speaking,' so the specificity of speech should not be used to deny that Allah is knowing. This lesson unpacks that argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the literal translation. Let the rhetorical flow of al-Ash'ari's question sink in.
Pay attention to the terms 'aʿamm' (more general) and 'akhaṣṣ' (more specific).
2
Identify the general and specific attributes
Note that 'ʿālim' (knowing) is described as more general (aʿamm) than 'mutakallim' (speaking). The specific attribute is speech.
3
Reflect on the logical principle
Ask yourself: Why does al-Ash'ari argue that the specificity of speech does not negate the generality of knowledge? Consider the principle that a specific attribute does not exclude a more general one.
This principle is key to understanding the consistency of divine attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم يقال: أليس الله عالماً، والوصف له بأنه عالم أعم من الوصف له بأنه متكلم مكلَّم؟ ثم لم يجب؛ لأن الكلام أخص من أن يكون الله تعالى متكلماً غير عالم؟ فلم لا قلتم إن الكلام - وإن كان أخص من العلم - لا ينفي أن يكون الله عالماً، كما لم ينف بخصوص الكلام أن يكون الله عالماً.
Then it is said: Is not Allah knowing, and the description of Him as knowing more general than the description of Him as speaking and addressed? Then why is it not necessary? Because speech is more specific than that Allah, exalted, be speaking without being knowing? So why did you not say that speech—even if it is more specific than knowledge—does not negate that Allah is knowing, just as the specificity of speech did not negate that Allah is knowing?
Source pages p. 116

Commentary
This passage continues the argument by focusing on the attribute 'Alim' (knowing). Al-Ash'ari asks: Isn't the description 'knowing' more general than the description 'speaking'? If so, then the fact that speech is specific should not negate that Allah is knowing. Yet the opponents deny knowledge while affirming speech. Al-Ash'ari points out the contradiction: they accept that Allah is described as knowing (which is more general), so why do they use the specificity of speech to deny knowledge? The argument shows that specificity does not automatically negate the more general attribute.

Key Terms
عالم
متكلم
مكلَّم
أعم
أخص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that if an attribute is specific, it can exclude a more general attribute.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari shows that specificity does not negate generality; Allah can be both knowing and speaking without contradiction.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, what does the specificity of speech not negate?
A) That Allah is powerful
B) That Allah is knowing
C) That speech is eternal
D) That Allah is speaking

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari establishes that the specificity of speech does not negate the more general attribute of knowledge, preserving coherence in divine descriptions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever limited your understanding of Allah by assuming that one attribute contradicts another, even though He is perfect and beyond such limitations? How can you train yourself to accept all that He has revealed about Himself without trying to reconcile apparent inconsistencies by human logic? Make a conscious effort today to affirm all of Allah's attributes as described in the Quran and Sunnah, even when your limited mind struggles to see how they fit together.
?
In your daily life, do you sometimes reject a general truth because of a specific experience? How does the principle that 'specificity does not negate generality' challenge you to see Allah's attributes as consistent and whole? Commit to studying the attributes of Allah with the conviction that they are all true and harmonious, and ask Allah to increase your certainty.

Proving Divine Attributes Through Revelation
Al-Ash'ari demonstrates that if they prove knowledge by verses, they must also prove power by similar verses.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses scriptural and rational arguments to establish that Allah possesses both knowledge and power.

Core Idea
If revelation proves one attribute, it proves others too.

In this passage, al-Ash'ari engages with opponents who accept that Allah is 'Alim (knowing) based on a verse. He shows that consistency requires them to also affirm Allah's knowledge and power from other verses. He then addresses a second line of reasoning from the design of the world, demonstrating that the same logic yields both attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let al-Ash'ari's argument unfold naturally.
Note that there are two distinct moves in the argument.
2
Identify the two lines of argument
First, al-Ash'ari uses scriptural proof: opponents accept verse 42:12 for knowledge, so he cites verses 4:166 and 35:11 for knowledge and 41:15 for power. Second, he uses the argument from design: the world shows wise crafts, which require a knower and a powerful agent.
The second argument shows that reason alone also demands both attributes.
3
Understand the consistency requirement
Ask yourself: Why does al-Ash'ari insist that if the opponent proves knowledge from scripture, they must also prove power from scripture? The answer lies in the principle that the same method should be applied consistently.
This is a dialectical move to force the opponent to accept all attributes that scripture teaches.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: من أين علمتم أن الله عالم؟ فإن قالوا: بقوله تعالى: ﴿إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ﴾ [الشورى: 12]. قيل لهم: ولذلك فقولوا: إن الله علم بقوله: ﴿أَنزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ﴾ [النساء: 166] وبقوله: ﴿وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَى وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِ﴾ [فاطر: 11] وكذلك قولوا: إن له قوة لقوله: ﴿أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللَّهَ الَّذِى خَلَقَهُمْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً﴾ [فصلت: 15]. فإن قالوا: قلنا: إن الله عالم؛ لأنه صنع العالم على ما فيه من آثار الحكمة واتساق التدبير. قيل لهم: فلم لا قلتم إن الله علم بما ظهر في العالم من حكمه وآثار تدبيره؟ لأن الصنائع الحكمية لا تظهر إلا من ذي علم، كما لا تظهر إلا من عالم، وكذلك لا تظهر إلا من ذي قوة كما لا تظهر إلا من قادر.
And it is said to them: From where do you know that Allah is knowing? If they say: By His statement, the Most High: 'Indeed, He is of all things Knowing' (Quran 42:12). It is said to them: Therefore say that Allah has knowledge by His statement: 'He revealed it with His knowledge' (Quran 4:166) and by His statement: 'And no female conceives nor gives birth except with His knowledge' (Quran 35:11). And likewise say that He has power by His statement: 'Have they not seen that Allah, who created them, is greater than them in strength' (Quran 41:15). If they say: We say that Allah is knowing because He made the world with what is in it of traces of wisdom and coherence of governance. It is said to them: Then why do you not say that Allah has knowledge by what has appeared in the world of His wisdom and traces of His governance? For wise crafts do not appear except from one with knowledge, just as they do not appear except from a knower, and likewise they do not appear except from one with power, just as they do not appear except from a powerful one.
Source pages p. 116

Commentary
This passage is a dialectical argument where al-Ash'ari addresses a possible proof from the opponents. They claim to know Allah is 'Alim' through a verse (42:12). Al-Ash'ari then points out that other verses also indicate knowledge and power (4:166, 35:11, 41:15). If they accept scriptural proof for 'Alim,' they must also accept it for 'knowledge' and 'power.' Then al-Ash'ari considers an alternative: maybe they prove Allah's knowledge from the wisdom evident in creation. Al-Ash'ari counters that the same reasoning shows Allah has knowledge and power, because wise effects require a knower and a powerful agent. Thus, the opponent's own method forces them to affirm both attributes.

Key Terms
عالم
علم
قوة
حكمة
تدبير
صنائع حكمية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think al-Ash'ari is proving God's existence from design (teleological argument).
Correction: Actually, al-Ash'ari is not proving existence; he is refuting those who deny attributes while using design to prove knowledge. He shows that design proves both knowledge and power.

Checkpoint
What does al-Ash'ari say about wise crafts?
A) They appear only from a knower and a powerful one
B) They appear only from a powerful one but not a knower
C) They appear only from a knower but not a powerful one
D) They appear from anyone

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari shows that both scripture and reason consistently require Allah to have both knowledge and power.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you look at the order and wisdom in the world, do you honestly stop at acknowledging a Designer, or do you let that recognition lead you to affirm all His attributes, even the ones that demand obedience and accountability from you? What attribute do you most resist accepting fully in your life?
?
Al-Ash'ari shows that the same reasoning that proves Allah's knowledge also proves His power. Is there an attribute of Allah you are comfortable acknowledging but another you shy away from because it challenges your autonomy? How will you respond to that inconsistency today?
?
The opponents in the passage used selective proof: they accepted what suited their argument and rejected the rest. Where in your own life do you pick and choose which of Allah's attributes to honor, and how will you repent from that selectivity starting now?

Names Imply Attributes
Al-Ash'ari rebuts the denial of attributes by appealing to Quranic mention of names.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why accepting Allah's names from the Quran necessarily implies accepting the attributes they signify, and how this argument exposes inconsistency in the opponents' position.

Core Idea
If His names are in the Book, so are His attributes.

This passage presents a concise scriptural argument from Imam al-Ash'ari. He challenges those who accept the divine names mentioned in the Quran but deny the corresponding attributes. The argument is simple but decisive—if the names are undeniable because they are in the Book, then the attributes (the meanings of those names) are equally undeniable.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the literal translation. Notice the dialogue format: a question, an objection, and a rebuttal.
The argument is compressed; let the author's own rhythm come through.
2
Identify the two groups
One group denies Allah's knowledge (and other attributes). The other group (the same? or a hypothetical?) accepts the names because they are in the Quran. Al-Ash'ari addresses the inconsistency.
The 'they' refers to those who deny attributes but accept names.
3
Understand the logical move
If the names are accepted solely because they are mentioned in the Quran, then the same reasoning applies to attributes like knowledge and power, which are also mentioned. The opponent's acceptance of names commits them to accepting attributes.
This is a scriptural argument, not purely rational.
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider that names and attributes are closely linked: a name (e.g., 'Alim) points to an attribute (knowledge). Denying the attribute would undermine the meaning of the name.
The passage does not say names are the same as attributes, but that they imply them.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: إذا نفيتم علم الله فلم لا نفيتم أسماءه؟ فإن قالوا: كيف ننفي أسماءه وقد ذكرها في كتابه؟ قيل لهم: فلا تنفوا العلم والقوة؛ لأنه تبارك وتعالى ذكر ذلك في كتابه العزيز.
And it is said to them: If you deny Allah's knowledge, why do you not deny His names? If they say: How can we deny His names when He has mentioned them in His Book? It is said to them: Then do not deny knowledge and power, because He, blessed and exalted, mentioned that in His Mighty Book.
Source pages p. 117

Commentary
This is a brief but powerful argument from scripture. Al-Ash'ari points out that the opponents accept the divine names (like 'Alim, Qadir') because they are in the Quran. But they deny the corresponding attributes (knowledge, power). If the names are undeniable because they are in the Book, then the attributes (which are the meanings of those names) are also in the Book and thus undeniable. The argument shows the inconsistency of accepting the name but rejecting the attribute it signifies.

Key Terms
أسماء
علم
قوة
كتاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that names and attributes are identical, so if you accept the name you don't need to posit a separate attribute.
Correction: This passage shows that names and attributes are distinct yet inseparable. The name 'Alim' signifies the attribute of knowledge, but the name is not the same as the attribute. Accepting the name without the attribute is inconsistent.

Checkpoint
Why does al-Ash'ari say the opponents should not deny knowledge and power?
A) Because they are the same as names
B) Because they are mentioned in the Book just like names
C) Because knowledge is more general
D) Because the Prophet taught them

Key Takeaway
Accepting Allah's names from the Quran logically requires accepting the attributes they signify, because both are equally present in divine revelation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever accept some parts of the Quran (like the beautiful names of Allah) but resist others (like His attributes of power and knowledge) because they demand more from you? Will you commit to accepting every mention of Allah in His Book, even those that confront your comfort?
?
If you truly believed that Allah's knowledge and power are as real as His mercy and love, how would your dua change tonight? Would you plead with more urgency, knowing He hears and is able?
?
The opponents in this passage were inconsistent—they accepted names but denied attributes. Is there an attribute of Allah that you struggle to fully affirm? What would it take for you to surrender to the full reality of His revelation?

Allah's Teaching Proves His Knowledge
Al-Ash'ari argues that Allah's teaching of the Prophet proves His knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses the fact that Allah taught the Prophet to prove that Allah possesses knowledge.

Core Idea
Teaching always implies knowledge; Allah taught the Prophet, so He must know.

In this passage, Al-Ash'ari presents a concise logical argument drawn from the Quranic reality that Allah taught the Prophet the laws and rulings. He argues that it is impossible for someone to teach what they do not know, so Allah must have knowledge. The passage concludes with a declaration of Allah's transcendence above the Jahmiyya denial of attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice the logical flow: premise, principle, conclusion, and refutation.
The argument is simple but foundational.
2
Identify the key premise
The argument begins with the premise that Allah taught the Prophet the laws and rulings. This is an accepted fact for Muslims.
What does teaching require?
3
Trace the logical conclusion
Since teaching requires knowledge, Allah must have known what He taught. Therefore, the Jahmiyya who deny divine knowledge are refuted.
Al-Ash'ari ends by exalting Allah above their false claim.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: قد علم الله تعالى نبيه صلى الله عليه وسلم الشرائع والأحكام، والحلال والحرام، ولا يجوز أن يُعَلِّمَهُ ما لا يعلمه، فكذلك لا يجوز أن يُعَلِّم الله نبيه ما لا عِلْمَ الله به تعالى الله عن قول الجهمية علواً كبيراً.
And it is said to them: Allah, the Most High, taught His Prophet, peace be upon him, the laws and rulings, the permissible and the forbidden, and it is not permissible that He teaches him what He does not know. So likewise, it is not permissible that Allah teaches His Prophet what Allah has no knowledge of. Exalted is Allah above the saying of the Jahmiyyah, a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 117

Commentary
This passage is a simple but decisive argument based on the fact that Allah taught the Prophet (ﷺ) religious laws. Teaching necessarily implies that the teacher has knowledge of what is taught. It is impossible for someone to teach what they do not know. Therefore, Allah must have knowledge. Al-Ash'ari ends by declaring Allah exalted above the false claims of the Jahmiyyah (who denied divine attributes).

Key Terms
تعليم
علم
نبيه
شرائع
أحكام

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Jahmiyya claim that Allah does not have knowledge (or attributes).
Correction: Al-Ash'ari argues that because Allah taught the Prophet, He must have knowledge, so the Jahmiyya claim is false.

Checkpoint
Why does al-Ash'ari argue that Allah must have knowledge?
A) Because the Prophet taught laws
B) Because Allah taught the Prophet laws
C) Because the Jahmiyyah said so
D) Because the Quran says 'He knows'

Key Takeaway
Allah's teaching of the Prophet proves that He has knowledge, because teaching requires knowledge.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you learn something about your faith, do you truly believe that it comes from the All-Knowing Creator who personally taught His Prophet? How does this conviction affect your commitment to act on that knowledge?
?
If teaching requires knowledge, what does the quality of your own teaching—whether in parenting, friendship, or work—reveal about your own heart? Are you teaching others things you haven't fully learned or lived yourself?

Divine Attributes

Emotional Attributes Necessitate Real Meanings
Al-Ash'ari argues that cursing, anger, and pleasure imply real attributes in Allah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses analogical reasoning to argue that emotional actions of Allah (cursing, anger, pleasure) imply real attributes, just as knowledge implies an attribute of knowledge.

Core Idea
If Allah curses or is angry, these actions require real attributes, not just metaphors.

This passage from Al-Ash'ari's Al-Ibanah presents a key argument against those who deny that Allah has real attributes. He uses a simple analogical method: if an action we attribute to Allah has a corresponding meaning in human actions, then it must be a real attribute in Allah. We'll see how he builds this argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Identify the initial premise
Read the first sentence: Al-Ash'ari asks if the curse of Allah and the curse of the Prophet both have meaning. Notice that the opponents agree.
2
Trace the extension to knowledge
Next, he extends the same logic to knowledge: if Allah teaches the Prophet, both have knowledge. See how this establishes a pattern.
This shows that the principle is general.
3
Apply the principle to other attributes
Finally, he applies the principle to anger, pleasure, life, hearing, and sight. Each requires its corresponding attribute.
This completes the argument for real attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: أليس إذا لعن الله الكافرين فلعنه لهم معنى، ولعن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم لهم معنى؟ فإن قالوا: نعم. فيقال لهم: فما أنكرتم من أن الله تعالى إذا علم نبيه شيئاً فكان للنبي علم، والله سبحانه علم، وإذا كنا متى أثبتناه غاضباً على الكافرين فلا بد من إثبات غضب، وكذلك إذا أثبتناه راضياً عن المؤمنين فلا بد من إثبات رضى، وكذلك إذا أثبتناه حياً سميعاً بصيراً فلا بد من إثبات حياة وسمع وبصر.
And it is said to them: Does it not happen that if Allah curses the disbelievers, then His curse upon them has a meaning, and the curse of the Prophet, peace be upon him, upon them has a meaning? If they say: Yes. Then it is said to them: So what prevents you from [affirming] that if Allah teaches the Prophet something, then the Prophet has knowledge and Allah, subhanahu, has knowledge? And whenever we affirm that He is angry with the disbelievers, it is necessary to affirm anger, and likewise if we affirm that He is pleased with the believers, it is necessary to affirm pleasure, and likewise if we affirm that He is living, hearing, seeing, it is necessary to affirm life, hearing, and sight.
Source pages p. 117

Commentary
This passage uses an analogical argument. Al-Ash'ari gets the opponents to agree that Allah's curse on disbelievers has a real meaning, just as the Prophet's curse has a meaning. He then extends this to knowledge: if Allah teaches the Prophet, then both Prophet and Allah have knowledge. He then broadens the principle: whenever we affirm a descriptive attribute of Allah (e.g., angry, pleased, living, hearing, seeing), it necessarily implies the corresponding attribute (anger, pleasure, life, hearing, sight). This establishes that attributes are real and distinct, not just metaphorical.

Key Terms
لعن
غضب
رضى
حياة
سمع
بصر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some deny that attributes like anger and pleasure are real in Allah, claiming they are mere metaphors for His actions.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari shows that if we say Allah curses or is angry, we must affirm the corresponding attribute (curse, anger) as a real meaning, just as with knowledge.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, what does affirming that Allah is angry with disbelievers require?
A) Denial of anger
B) Affirmation of anger as an attribute
C) Only metaphorical interpretation
D) Affirmation that anger is a creation

Key Takeaway
If you attribute an action to Allah, you must affirm the corresponding attribute as real.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that Allah's anger is real, or have you reduced it to a metaphor for punishment? Examine your heart: when you read verses about His anger, do you feel a genuine fear that changes how you live, or do you explain it away? Commit today to take His anger as a real attribute that demands your repentance.
?
The passage uses the Prophet's curse as an analogy. Do you treat Allah's actions as concrete realities in your daily life? For example, when you hear that Allah hears and sees, do you act as if you are constantly aware of that? This week, pick one attribute (like hearing) and actively remind yourself of its reality in every situation.
?
If Allah's attributes are real, then your relationship with Him must be real. Are you living as if His pleasure or anger truly matters? What would change in your worship if you internalized that Allah's attributes are not abstract concepts but real descriptions of your Lord? Write down one specific change you will make starting today.

Divine Attributes

4 min
Divine Names Are Derived from Attributes: Proof of Their Reality
Al-Ash'ari argues that Allah's names are derived from attributes, necessitating their reality

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari argues that the derivation of divine names from attributes necessitates the reality of those attributes.

Core Idea
Divine names are not mere titles; they are derived from real attributes.

Al-Ash'ari opens his argument with a linguistic observation: names like 'Alim (Knowing) clearly come from 'ilm (knowledge). He then presents a logical dichotomy: divine names are either derived to convey a meaning or are mere appellations. Since they cannot be mere appellations, they must be derived, and that derivation proves the reality of the attributes. This lesson walks through that reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice how al-Ash'ari uses examples of names like 'Alim, Qadir, etc.
2
Identify the examples of derived names
The passage lists several divine names that are clearly derived from attributes: 'Alim from 'ilm, Qadir from qudra, etc. Make a note of each pair.
This establishes a pattern.
3
Understand the dichotomy: derived vs. appellation
Al-Ash'ari states that names must be either derived to convey a meaning (ifada ma'na) or mere appellations (talqib). Consider why appellations are not suitable for Allah.
The consensus of Muslims is cited.
4
Follow the conclusion: attributes are real
Since the names are not appellations but derived, the attributes from which they are derived must be real. For example, because Allah is called 'Alim, He must have knowledge.
This is the key logical step.
5
Reflect on the analogy with 'exists'
Al-Ash'ari draws an analogy: just as the term 'exists' conveys the meaning of existence, so the term 'Alim conveys knowledge. This reinforces the argument.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: وجدنا اسم عالم اشتق من علم، واسم قادر اشتق من قدرة، وكذلك اسم حي اشتق من حياة، واسم سميع اشتق من سمع، واسم بصير اشتق من بصر، ولا تخلو أسماء الله عز وجل من أن تكون مشتقة، إما لإفادة معنى أو على طريق التلقيب، فلا يجوز أن يسمى الله عز وجل على طريق التلقيب باسم ليس فيه إفادة معنى، وليس مشتقاً من صفة. فإذا قلنا: إن الله عز وجل عالم قادر فليس تلقيباً، كقولنا: زيد وعمرو، وعلى هذا إجماع المسلمين. وإذا لم يكن ذلك تلقيباً، وكان مشتقاً من علم، فقد وجب إثبات العلم، وإن كان ذلك لإفادة معنى فلا يختلف ما هو (كذا) لإفادة معنى. وجب. ووجب إذا كان معنى العالم منا أن له علماً أن يكون: كل عالم فهو ذو علم، كما إذا كان قولي: موجود مفيداً معنى الإثبات، كان الباري تعالى واجباً إثباته؛ لأنه سبحانه وتعالى موجود.
And it is said to them: We find that the name 'Alim (knowing) is derived from 'ilm (knowledge), and the name Qadir (powerful) is derived from qudra (power), and likewise the name Hayy (living) is derived from haya (life), and the name Sami' (hearing) is derived from sam' (hearing), and the name Basir (seeing) is derived from basar (sight). And the names of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, are not free from being derived, either to convey a meaning or by way of appellation (talqib). So it is not permissible to name Allah, Mighty and Majestic, by way of appellation with a name that does not convey a meaning and is not derived from an attribute. For if we say that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, is 'Alim and Qadir, it is not an appellation like our saying Zayd and 'Amr; and upon this is the consensus of the Muslims. And if it is not an appellation, and it is derived from 'ilm, then the affirmation of 'ilm is necessarily required; and if that is to convey a meaning, then what is (kadha) for conveying a meaning does not differ. It is necessary. And it is necessary that if the meaning of 'alim among us is that he has knowledge, then every 'alim is possessor of knowledge, just as if my saying 'exists' (mawjud) is beneficial for the meaning of affirmation, then the Creator, Exalted, is necessarily affirmed because He, glorified and exalted, exists.
Source pages p. 118

Commentary
This passage presents an argument for the reality of divine attributes from the nature of divine names. Al-Ash'ari begins by observing that common divine names like 'Alim (Knower), Qadir (Powerful), Hayy (Living), Sami' (Hearer), and Basir (Seer) are all derived from corresponding abstract nouns: 'ilm (knowledge), qudra (power), haya (life), sam' (hearing), basar (sight). He then states that the names of Allah must be either derived to convey a meaning (ifada ma'na) or used as mere appellations (talqib) without conveying meaning. It is not permissible to call Allah by a name that is a mere appellation without conveying meaning and not derived from an attribute. For example, when we say 'Allah is 'Alim and Qadir,' it is not like calling Zayd or 'Amr—mere labels. There is consensus of Muslims on this. Since these names are not mere labels but are derived from attributes, the attribute of 'ilm (knowledge) must be affirmed (wajaba ithbat al-'ilm). Similarly, the meaning of 'alim in created beings is that he has knowledge, so every 'alim is a possessor of knowledge. Just as the term 'exists' (mawjud) conveys the meaning of affirmation, and thus the Creator must be affirmed because He is existent, so too the names derived from attributes require the reality of those attributes.

Key Terms
علم
قدرة
حياة
سمع
بصر
تلقيب
مشتق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think divine names are mere titles without implying real attributes.
Correction: This passage clarifies that divine names are derived from attributes, proving the attributes are real.

Checkpoint
What is al-Ash'ari's main argument in this passage?
A) Divine names are arbitrary labels for Allah.
B) Divine names are derived from attributes, proving the attributes are real.
C) Divine names are only used in the Quran.
D) Divine names cannot be known.

Key Takeaway
The derivation of divine names from attributes proves that Allah's attributes are real and not mere labels.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you call upon Allah as Al-‘Alim (the All-Knowing), do you truly live as if He sees every thought you harbor and every deed you conceal? If you did, what sin would you abandon today?
?
The passage proves that Allah’s names are not empty labels—they demand that you affirm His attributes in your heart. Which of His attributes do you deny by your actions? Name one specific area of your life where you act as if He is not Al-Qadir over that situation, and commit to changing that today.
?
Al-Ash’ari argues that just as ‘exists’ necessitates existence, ‘Alim necessitates knowledge. Do you treat Allah’s knowledge as real enough to judge your secret intentions? If so, why do you still harbor doubts about His power or wisdom? Repent right now for any moment you belittled His attributes.

Quranic Proofs for Allah's Universal Knowledge
Al-Ash'ari cites Quranic verses to prove Allah's universal knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses Quranic verses to argue that Allah has knowledge of all things, including specific events like pregnancies and revelations.

Core Idea
Allah's knowledge is proven by both general and specific Quranic verses.

In this passage from Al-Ibanah, Imam Al-Ash'ari confronts those who deny that Allah has knowledge of particular events. He challenges them with a simple question and then marshals Quranic evidence to show that denying such knowledge is inconsistent with scripture. Let's examine his argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Understand the Challenge
Al-Ash'ari asks the opponents a direct question about Allah's knowledge of specific things. Notice the phrasing: 'Do you say that Allah has knowledge of things prior to them...'
The opponents are the Mu'tazila, Jahmiyya, and Haruriyya.
2
Identify the Quranic Verses
He cites specific verses (4:166, 35:11, 11:14) and then general verses (2:29, 6:59). See how each verse is used to support his argument.
The verses cover both specific events and universal knowledge.
3
Analyze the Logical Argument
He argues that if the general verses necessitate that Allah is 'Alim (knowing), then the specific verses also prove He has knowledge. Why would opponents deny this?
4
Reflect on the Refutation
The passage shows that the opponents are inconsistent: they accept some verses but reject others. This inconsistency undermines their denial.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال للمعتزلة والجهمية والحرورية أتقولون إن الله علماً بالأشياء سابقاً فيها، ولوضع كل حامل، وحمل كل أنثى، وبإنزال كل ما أنزل؟ فإن قالوا: نعم، فقد أثبتوا العلم ووافقوا. وإن قالوا لا قيل لهم هذا جحد منكم؛ لقول الله عز وجل: ﴿أَنزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ﴾ [النساء: ١٦٦] ولقوله: ﴿وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَى وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِ﴾ [فاطر: ١١] ولقوله: ﴿فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّمَا أُنزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللهِ﴾ [هود: ١٤]. وإذا كان قول الله عز وجل: ﴿بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ﴾ [البقرة: ٢٩] ﴿وَمَا تَسْقُطُ مِن وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُهَا﴾ [الأنعام: ٥٩] يوجب أنه عليم يعلم الأشياء كذلك فما أنكرتم أن تكون هذه الآيات توجب أن الله علماً بالأشياء، سبحانه وبحمده.
And it is said to the Mu'tazila, Jahmiyya, and Haruriyya: Do you say that Allah has knowledge of things prior to them, and of the placement of every pregnant female, and of the conception of every female, and of the sending down of everything that is sent down? If they say: Yes, then they have affirmed knowledge and agreed. And if they say: No, it is said to them: This is denial on your part, due to the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: 'He sent it down with His knowledge' (4:166), and due to His saying: 'And no female conceives nor gives birth except with His knowledge' (35:11), and due to His saying: 'Then if they do not respond to you, know that it was sent down with the knowledge of Allah' (11:14). And if the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: 'He is Knower of all things' (2:29) and 'Not a leaf falls but He knows it' (6:59) necessitates that He is 'Alim (knowing), knowing things, then why do you deny that these verses necessitate that Allah has knowledge of things—glory and praise be to Him?
Source pages p. 118

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of those who deny that Allah has knowledge (the Mu'tazila, Jahmiyya, and Haruriyya). Al-Ash'ari poses a question: Do you affirm that Allah has prior knowledge of things, of pregnancies, and of revelations? If they affirm, they agree with the correct position. If they deny, they are refuted by several Quranic verses: (4:166) 'He sent it down with His knowledge'; (35:11) 'no female conceives nor gives birth except with His knowledge'; (11:14) 'know that it was sent down with the knowledge of Allah'. Furthermore, general verses like (2:29) 'He is Knower of all things' and (6:59) 'Not a leaf falls but He knows it' necessitate that Allah is 'Alim and knows all things. Thus, if they accept these verses as proof of Allah's knowledge in general, they cannot deny that the specific verses also prove that Allah has knowledge of things. The argument is that the Quran consistently affirms Allah's knowledge, so denying it is contradictory to scripture.

Key Terms
علم
عليم
القرآن

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Allah only has general knowledge, not specific knowledge of details.
Correction: The Quranic verses prove Allah's knowledge encompasses all things, specific and general.

Checkpoint
What is the purpose of citing Quranic verses in this passage?
A) To prove that Allah's knowledge is limited.
B) To show that the opponents already affirm some verses but deny others inconsistently.
C) To argue that knowledge is not an attribute of Allah.
D) To refute the existence of Allah.

Key Takeaway
The Quran consistently affirms Allah's knowledge of all things, making denial of His specific knowledge a contradiction of scripture.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that Allah knows every single thought, desire, and sin you try to hide? If you are honest, does that belief change how you act when you are alone? What would it take for that belief to penetrate your heart, not just your mind?
?
When you read the verse 'Not a leaf falls but He knows it', do you feel a sense of awe and accountability, or do you gloss over it? If you lived every moment aware that Allah's knowledge surrounds you, what would you do differently starting right now?
?
The Mu'tazila denied Allah's knowledge of particulars because it seemed too intimate—an attempt to protect God's transcendence. Is there any area of your life where you act as if Allah does not see, as if He is distant and unaware? Let this lesson strip away that illusion: He knows. Will you live in light of that truth today?

Allah's Knowledge of Distinguishing Friends and Enemies
Al-Ash'ari argues that Allah must know the distinction between believers and disbelievers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand al-Ash'ari's argument that Allah must have knowledge of the distinction between believers and disbelievers, and why denying that leads to an impossible consequence.

Core Idea
If creatures can distinguish friends from enemies, Allah must know that distinction.

This passage presents another polemical argument from al-Ash'ari against his opponents. He forces them to admit that Allah has knowledge of who is a believer and who is a disbeliever, and shows that denying this leads to the absurd conclusion that creatures are superior to the Creator in knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once and then the English translation. Notice the rhetorical questions and the logical flow.
2
Identify the logical structure
Trace the argument: al-Ash'ari asks about Allah's knowledge and will, then uses the opponent's concession to show that if they affirm will, they must also affirm knowledge.
The key move is the analogy: just as they admit Allah has will for faith, He must have knowledge of the distinction.
3
Analyze the reductio ad absurdum
Reflect on the final sentence: if creatures have knowledge that Allah lacks, they would be superior to Him. Why is that impossible?
This is a standard theological argument: any imperfection in Allah is inconceivable.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: أتقولون أن الله عز وجل علم بالتفرقة بين أوليائه وأعدائه؟ وهل هو مريد لذلك؟ وهل له إرادة للإيمان إذا أراد الإيمان؟ فإن قالوا: نعم، فقد وافقوا. وإن قالوا: إذا أراد الإيمان فله إرادة. قيل لهم: وكذلك إذا فرق بين أوليائه وأعدائه فلا بد من أن يكون له علم بذلك، وكيف يجوز أن يكون للخلق علم بذلك، وليس للخالق عز وجل علم بذلك؟! وهذا يوجب أن للخلق مزية في العلم وفضيلة على الخالق، تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً.
And it is said to them: Do you say that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, has knowledge of distinguishing between His friends and His enemies? And does He will that? And does He have a will for faith when He wills faith? If they say: Yes, then they have agreed. And if they say: When He wills faith, He has a will. It is said to them: Likewise, when He distinguishes between His friends and His enemies, there must be for Him knowledge of that. And how can it be permissible that creatures have knowledge of that, and the Creator, Mighty and Majestic, does not have knowledge of that?! And this necessitates that creatures have superiority in knowledge and excellence over the Creator—exalted is Allah above that, greatly exalted.
Source pages p. 119

Commentary
This passage is another objection-and-answer, this time focusing on Allah's knowledge of distinguishing between friends and enemies. Al-Ash'ari asks the opponents: Do you affirm that Allah has knowledge to distinguish between His friends (believers) and enemies (disbelievers)? And does He will that? Do you affirm that He has a will for faith when He wills faith? If they affirm these, they agree with the orthodox position. If they say that He has a will when He wills faith, then similarly, when He distinguishes, He must have knowledge of that distinction. Al-Ash'ari then uses a reductio: it is impossible that creatures (like the Prophet or believers) could have such knowledge (knowing who is a friend or enemy) while the Creator does not. If that were the case, creatures would have a superiority (maziyya) in knowledge over the Creator, which is an impossible and blasphemous conclusion. Thus, Allah must have knowledge of such distinctions.

Key Terms
علم
إرادة
تفرقة
أولياء
أعداء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some claim that Allah's knowledge is general and does not extend to individual distinctions among people.
Correction: This passage affirms that Allah must have specific knowledge of who is a believer and who is a disbeliever, otherwise creatures would be superior to Him.

Checkpoint
What conclusion does al-Ash'ari draw if creatures have knowledge of distinction but Allah does not?
A) Creatures are equal to Allah in knowledge.
B) Creatures would be superior to Allah.
C) Allah still knows everything.
D) The argument is invalid.

Key Takeaway
Allah must have specific knowledge of who are His friends and enemies; denying this implies an absurd superiority of creatures over the Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

This passage exposes such denial as resulting in creatures being superior to the Creator. Can you identify any area of your life where you act as if you know better than Allah?
?
Al-Ash'ari forces you to confront an absurdity: claiming that a creature knows something the Creator does not. After reading this, is there any part of your heart that still holds back from submitting to Allah's complete knowledge? What step will you take today to align your belief with the truth that Allah alone knows the distinction, and to live accordingly?

Divine Attributes
beginner
4 min
Creatures cannot be higher than the Creator in knowledge
Al-Ash'ari uses a reductio to show that denying Allah's knowledge implies creatures are superior

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses a reductio ad absurdum to prove that Allah must have knowledge.

Core Idea
If denying Allah's knowledge makes creatures superior, the denial is absurd.

This lesson examines a concise but powerful argument from Imam al-Ash'ari. He uses a logical technique called reductio ad absurdum: assume the opposite and show it leads to an unacceptable conclusion. Here, he shows that denying Allah's knowledge would make creatures higher than the Creator, which is impossible.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once to get its rhythm. Then read the translation. Note that this is a single sentence containing a complete argument.
The Arabic is compact; the translation unpacks it slightly.
2
Identify the premise
Notice the premise: among creatures, one who has knowledge is more worthy of high rank than one who lacks knowledge. This is presented as self-evident.
3
Apply the premise to the Creator
The argument applies this same standard to Allah. If the opponents claim Allah has no knowledge, then by the same principle, creatures who have knowledge would be higher in rank than Allah.
This is the reductio: the conclusion is absurd.
4
Understand the conclusion
Since the conclusion is blasphemous (creatures superior to the Creator), the original claim (Allah has no knowledge) must be false. Therefore Allah has knowledge.
The argument does not equate divine and human knowledge in essence, only in logical principle.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: إذا كان من له علم من الخلق أولى بالمنزلة الرفيعة ممن لا علم له فإذا زعمتم أن الله عز وجل لا علم له لزمكم أن الخلق أعلى مرتبة من الخالق تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً.
And it is said to them: If one who has knowledge among creatures is more worthy of high rank than one who has no knowledge, then if you claim that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, has no knowledge, it follows necessarily that creatures are higher in rank than the Creator—exalted is Allah above that, greatly exalted.
Source pages p. 119

Commentary
This passage is a concise reductio ad absurdum argument. Al-Ash'ari premises that among creatures, one who possesses knowledge is more deserving of a high rank (al-manzila al-rafi'a) than one who does not have knowledge. This is an intuitively obvious point. Then he applies it to the Creator: If the opponents claim that Allah has no knowledge, then by the same principle, creatures who do have knowledge would be higher in rank than the Creator. This is an absurd and blasphemous conclusion, so the premise must be false: Allah must have knowledge. The argument is simple but powerful, relying on the self-evident superiority of knowledge.

Key Terms
علم
منزلة رفيعة
خلق
خالق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think this argument equates divine and human knowledge in a crude way, implying Allah's knowledge is like a creature's.
Correction: The argument uses a logical analogy that is commonly accepted; it does not claim that divine and human knowledge are identical in essence, only that the principle of knowledge conferring superiority applies analogically.

Checkpoint
What follows from claiming Allah has no knowledge according to this passage?
A) Allah is still greater than creatures.
B) Creatures become equal to Allah.
C) Creatures would be higher in rank than Allah.
D) Knowledge is irrelevant to rank.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's reductio proves that Allah must have knowledge, otherwise creatures would be superior to Him—an impossibility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you act as if you know better than Allah's commands, what does that imply about your view of His knowledge? How will you realign your decision-making this week to reflect that He knows all?
?
If you truly believe Allah knows all, why do you still worry about outcomes? What step can you take today to trust His knowledge over your anxiety?
?
Do you ever find yourself thinking your own knowledge or opinion is superior to what Allah has revealed? What is one area where you need to submit your intellect to His, and what concrete action will you take?

Divine Attributes
beginner
4 min
Quranic Proof of God's Knowledge
Al-Ash'ari cites Quranic verses to prove God knows all things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses Quranic verses to argue that God possesses knowledge as an attribute distinct from His essence.

Core Idea
God's knowledge is proven by the Qur'an itself; every leaf that falls testifies to His perfect knowledge.

In this passage, Imam Al-Ash'ari turns to the Qur'an to establish that God has the attribute of knowledge. He selects verses that explicitly mention 'knowledge' as an act or possession of God, and then argues by analogy: if verses that say God 'knows all things' prove He is 'Knowing,' then these verses prove He actually possesses knowledge. This is a foundational demonstration of the scripture-based method of the Ash'ari school.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic passage slowly. Then read the English translation. Pay attention to the Quranic citations embedded in the text.
The Arabic uses a concise style; the translator has added verse numbers for clarity.
2
Identify the three explicit verses
Find the three verses that Al-Ash'ari quotes at the beginning. Each mentions 'knowledge' (ilm) directly. Note how they relate different things (revelation, conception, the Qur'an) to God's knowledge.
These verses form the first part of the proof.
3
Understand the analogical argument
Read the second part of the passage. Al-Ash'ari argues: if verses saying 'God knows all things' prove He is Knowing, then the earlier verses also prove He has knowledge. See how he connects the two sets of verses.
This is a qiyas (analogical) argument from scripture.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أَنزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ ﴾ [النساء: ١٦٦] ولقوله: ﴿وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَى وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِ ﴾ [فاطر: ١١] ولقوله: ﴿فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّمَا أُنزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللَّهِ ﴾ [هود: ١٤]. وإذا كان قول الله عز وجل: ﴿بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ ﴾ [البقرة: ٢٩] ﴿وَمَا تَسْقُطُ مِن وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُهَا ﴾ [الأنعام: ٥٩] يوجب أنه عليم يعلم الأشياء كذلك فما أنكرتم أن تكون هذه الآيات توجب أن الله علماً بالأشياء، سبحانه وبحمده.
He sent it down with His knowledge [Quran 4:166]; and His saying: 'And no female conceives nor gives birth except with His knowledge' [Quran 35:11]; and His saying: 'But if they do not respond to you, then know that it was sent down with the knowledge of Allah' [Quran 11:14]. And if the saying of Allah Almighty: 'Over all things is Knowing' [Quran 2:29] and 'Not a leaf falls but that He knows it' [Quran 6:59] necessitates that He is Knowing, knowing all things — likewise, why do you deny that these verses necessitate that Allah has knowledge of things? Glory be to Him and His praise.
Source pages p. 119

Commentary
This passage is a proof from the Qur'an that God possesses knowledge as an attribute. Al-Ash'ari first cites three verses that explicitly mention God's knowledge: the first (4:166) says He sent down the revelation with His knowledge, the second (35:11) says conception and birth occur only with His knowledge, and the third (11:14) says the Qur'an was sent down with God's knowledge. He then argues: if verses like 'Over all things is Knowing' (2:29) and 'Not a leaf falls but that He knows it' (6:59) prove that God is Knowing (alīm) and knows all things, then similarly these cited verses prove that God has (possession of) knowledge (ilm) of things. The conclusion is that God has an attribute of knowledge, not just that He knows in some sense. The phrase 'subhanahu wa bihamdihi' ends the argument with praise.

Key Terms
علم
عليم
بكل شيء عليم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's knowledge is identical with His essence, so He does not have a separate attribute of knowledge.
Correction: This passage does not directly argue for a separate attribute; it simply proves that God has knowledge from the Qur'an. The Ash'ari position is that knowledge is an attribute distinct from essence, but this verse-based argument only establishes the existence of knowledge in God.

Checkpoint
What do the verses cited by Al-Ash'ari prove about God?
A) That God is powerful
B) That God has knowledge of things
C) That God is eternal
D) That God is unseen

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari proves that God possesses knowledge by citing Quranic verses that explicitly attribute knowledge to God, and then arguing by analogy from verses that describe God as 'Knowing' to conclude that God has actual knowledge.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You know that God's knowledge encompasses every leaf that falls—yet do you live as if your hidden thoughts and actions are truly before Him? What is one thing you will change today to acknowledge His awareness?
?
The Qur'an was sent down with God's knowledge; do you receive each verse with the awe of knowing it comes from His infinite wisdom? How will you approach your next reading differently?
?
If God knows your struggles before you even voice them, why do you still carry the burden of pretense? What would it mean for you to live in complete transparency before the All-Knowing?

The Necessity of Divine Knowledge for Distinguishing Friends and Enemies
Al-Ash'ari argues for divine knowledge via distinction.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Al-Ash'ari's argument that God must possess knowledge in order to distinguish between His friends and enemies, and why denying this would imply creatures' superiority over the Creator.

Core Idea
Distinguishing friends from enemies requires knowledge; denying it to God is absurd.

In this passage, Imam Al-Ash'ari engages those who accept that God wills but deny He knows. He uses a logical series of questions to show that if God distinguishes between groups, He must have knowledge of them. Furthermore, if creatures can know this distinction while God cannot, it would imply creatures are superior—an impossibility. This lesson unpacks the argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation once to get the flow of questions and answers.
2
Identify the starting assumption
Notice that Al-Ash'ari begins by asking about God's knowledge and will of the distinction. This sets up the opponent's possible answers.
The opponent admits God wills faith.
3
Follow the logical consequence
If God distinguishes between friends and enemies, He must have knowledge of that distinction. Otherwise creatures would surpass God.
This is the core argument.
4
Reflect on the theological implication
Consider why Al-Ash'ari considers it impossible for creatures to be superior to God in knowledge.
This affirms divine perfection.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : أتقولون أن الله عز وجل علم بالتفرقة بين أوليائه وأعدائه؟ وهل هو مريد لذلك؟ وهل له إرادة للإيمان إذا أراد الإيمان؟ فإن قالوا: نعم، فقد وافقوا . وإن قالوا : إذا أراد الإيمان فله إرادة . قيل لهم : وكذلك إذا فرق بين أوليائه وأعدائه فلا بد من أن يكون له علم بذلك، وكيف يجوز أن يكون للخلق علم بذلك، وليس للخالق عز وجل علم بذلك؟! وهذا يوجب أن للخلق مزية في العلم وفضيلة على الخالق، تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً .
And it is said to them: Do you say that Allah Almighty has knowledge of the distinction between His friends and His enemies? And does He intend that? And does He have will for faith when He wills faith? If they say: Yes, then they have agreed. And if they say: When He wills faith, He has will. It is said to them: Likewise, if He distinguishes between His friends and His enemies, He must have knowledge of that. And how is it permissible that creatures have knowledge of that, while the Creator Almighty does not have knowledge of that?! And this necessitates that creatures have superiority in knowledge and excellence over the Creator—far exalted is Allah above that, a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 119

Commentary
This passage is a dialectical argument (jawaab) addressed to those who deny that God has knowledge. Al-Ash'ari poses a series of questions: Do you affirm that God knows the distinction between His friends and enemies? Does He will that? Does He have a will for faith when He wills faith? The opponents will affirm these (since they accept God wills and distinguishes). Then the key point is made: if God distinguishes between parties, He must have knowledge of that distinction. Moreover, if creatures can know such a distinction while the Creator does not, then creatures would have superiority over God—which is impossible. The argument is based on the principle that the attribute of knowledge is necessary for any act of distinction; denying it to God would imply a defect.

Key Terms
علم
إرادة
أولياء
أعداء
تفرقة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Knowledge of distinction is not required for God because He simply decrees.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari argues that distinguishing between friends and enemies inherently requires knowledge; otherwise the distinction would be arbitrary and creatures would surpass God.

Checkpoint
What does Al-Ash'ari argue would be the consequence if God had no knowledge of the distinction between friends and enemies?
A) It would be impossible for Him to have will
B) Creatures would have superiority over the Creator
C) Distinction would not happen
D) God would be ignorant of His own actions

Key Takeaway
God must possess knowledge because He distinguishes between His friends and enemies; denying that implies creatures' superiority.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that God knows you personally—your specific struggles, intentions, and hidden deeds? If you fully embraced that reality, what habit or action would you change starting today?
?
The argument warns against thinking creatures could have superiority over God. Have you ever, even subtly, trusted your own reasoning above God’s revelation? What would it mean to humbly submit your intellect to His knowledge?
?
God distinguishes His friends from His enemies. Are you living today as one of His friends or as one who is indifferent? What one concrete step can you take before the end of this week to confirm your friendship with Allah?

Knowledge Elevates Rank: A Proof for Divine Knowledge
Al-Ash'ari argues that knowledge elevates rank, so God must have knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses a logical analogy to prove that knowledge is a necessary attribute of Allah.

Core Idea
Knowledge elevates rank; denying knowledge to God would place creatures above Him.

This short but powerful argument from Imam Al-Ash'ari uses a simple analogy from human experience to demonstrate the necessity of divine knowledge. The logic is straightforward: if knowledge brings higher rank among creatures, then God—who is the Most High—must possess knowledge, or else creatures would be greater than their Creator. This lesson unpacks the reasoning and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage carefully
Read the Arabic text once to get the rhythm. Then read the translation. Notice the structure: a conditional statement leading to an absurd conclusion.
The passage is one sentence, but it contains a full argument.
2
Identify the analogy
Al-Ash'ari starts with a premise about creatures: the one with knowledge is more deserving of high rank. Understand why this premise is accepted by both sides.
This premise is common sense—knowledge is a perfection.
3
Apply the analogy to God
The argument applies the same logic to God. If God lacked knowledge, then creatures (who have knowledge) would be higher in rank. This is absurd, so God must have knowledge.
This is a reductio ad absurdum: assuming the denial leads to an impossible consequence.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
The passage ends with 'far exalted is Allah above that'—a declaration of God's transcendence. Reflect on how this argument affirms divine perfection.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : إذا كان من له علم من الخلق أولى بالمنزلة الرفيعة ممن لا علم له فإذا زعمتم أن الله عز وجل لا علم له لزمكم أن الخلق أعلى مرتبة من الخالق تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً .
And it is said to them: If one of the creatures who has knowledge is more deserving of the high station than one who has no knowledge, then if you claim that Allah Almighty has no knowledge, it necessarily follows that creatures are of a higher rank than the Creator—far exalted is Allah above that, a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 119

Commentary
This is a concise reductio ad absurdum. Al-Ash'ari begins with a premise that his opponents accept: among creatures, the one who possesses knowledge is more deserving of a high station than the one without knowledge. Applying that to God: if they claim God has no knowledge, then by the same logic, creatures (who do have knowledge) would be of a higher rank than God. This is blasphemous, so the premise that God lacks knowledge is false. The argument assumes that the principle applies to God as well—knowledge is a perfection, and its absence is imperfection.

Key Terms
علم
منزلة رفيعة
مرتبة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God's rank is so incomparable that human logic cannot be applied to Him.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari's argument shows that while God is transcendent, basic logical principles—like the connection between knowledge and perfection—still apply. Denying knowledge to God leads to an absurd outcome that contradicts His majesty.

Checkpoint
What does Al-Ash'ari conclude if one claims God has no knowledge?
A) It is possible that God still has high rank
B) Creatures would be lower than God
C) Creatures would be higher in rank than God
D) The argument is invalid because God is not like creatures

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's simple analogy proves that knowledge is an attribute of perfection necessary for Allah, as its absence would contradict His supreme rank.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that Allah’s knowledge is absolute and beyond your own? Reflect on a recent decision where you prioritized your own judgment over what you know of His commands. What does that reveal about how you rank His knowledge compared to yours?
?
If knowledge elevates rank even among creatures, what does your personal investment in seeking Islamic knowledge say about the rank you aspire to with Allah? Are you satisfied with a lower rank, or are you actively striving to elevate it through learning His religion?

Denying Knowledge Implies Deficiency
Al-Ash'ari argues that denying knowledge to God would attribute ignorance and deficiency to Him.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Al-Ash'ari's logical argument that denying knowledge to God necessarily implies attributing ignorance and deficiency to Him, which is impossible.

Core Idea
Denying God's knowledge logically implies attributing imperfection to Him.

This passage presents a logical proof from impossibility. Al-Ash'ari uses a comparison to creatures to show that denying knowledge to God would contradict His perfection. The argument is straightforward: among creatures, lack of knowledge means ignorance and deficiency; therefore, if God lacked knowledge, He would have those defects, which is impossible.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its literal English translation. Notice the flow of the argument: it begins with a conditional statement about creatures.
2
Understand the analogical reasoning
Al-Ash'ari draws an analogy: in creatures, lacking knowledge entails ignorance and deficiency. This premise is taken as obvious.
The analogy is not a direct comparison but a logical implication.
3
Identify the consequence for God
If the same were applied to God, denying His knowledge would mean He is subject to ignorance and deficiency. But God is beyond such defects, so the denial is false.
This is a reductio ad absurdum: the opponent's position leads to an impossibility.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
The argument forces the opponent to affirm God's knowledge to avoid attributing deficiency. It shows that knowledge is a necessary attribute of God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : إذا كان من لا علم له من الخلق يلحقه الجهل والنقصان، فما أنكرتم من أنه لا بد من إثبات علم الله؟ وإلا ألحقتم به النقصان جل وعز عن قولكم وعلا، ألا ترون أن من لا يعلم من الخلق يلحقه الجهل والنقصان، ومن قال ذلك في الله عز وجل وصف الله سبحانه بما لا يليق به، فكذلك إذا كان من قيل له من الخلق لا علم له لحقه الجهل والنقصان، وجب أن لا يُنْفَى ذلك عن الله عز وجل لأنه لا يلحقه جهل ولا نقصان .
And it is said to them: If one of the creatures who has no knowledge is overtaken by ignorance and deficiency, then why do you deny the necessity of affirming God's knowledge? Otherwise you would attribute deficiency to Him—He is glorified and exalted above your statement, and He is high. Do you not see that among creatures, one who does not know is overtaken by ignorance and deficiency, and whoever says that regarding Allah Almighty has described Allah, glorified be He, with what does not befit Him. So likewise, if it is said of a creature that he has no knowledge, he is overtaken by ignorance and deficiency; it is necessary that this (negation of knowledge) not be denied of Allah Almighty because He is not overtaken by ignorance or deficiency.
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage is another logical argument. The premise: among creatures, lacking knowledge entails ignorance and deficiency (naqsān). Therefore, if one denies knowledge to God, one would be attributing ignorance and deficiency to Him—which is impossible. Al-Ash'ari reinforces that describing God in such a way is unbefitting. The conclusion affirms that knowledge must be ascribed to God because He is free from all deficiency.

Key Terms
جهل
نقصان
إثبات علم الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might argue that the comparison between creatures and God is invalid because God is not like creatures.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari's argument is not a direct comparison but a logical implication: if the denial of knowledge leads to deficiency in one case (creatures), it must also lead to deficiency in the other (God), unless there is a relevant difference. But the attribute of 'knowledge' is the same, and the consequence (deficiency) is contradictory to God's nature, so the denial is impossible.

Checkpoint
What does Al-Ash'ari say is the consequence of denying knowledge to God?
A) It would imply God has no power
B) It would attribute ignorance and deficiency to God
C) It would mean creatures are ignorant
D) It would be a valid denial because God is beyond attributes

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari shows that denying God's knowledge is self-contradictory because it logically implies attributing deficiency to Him, which is impossible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you deny that God knows every detail of your life, what does that imply about your view of God's perfection? How does this lesson confront your own tendency to think God is unaware of your struggles? What will you change today to live in constant awareness that God knows all?
?
When you sin secretly, do you ever act as if God does not see? Reflect on how the denial of God's knowledge in your actions actually attributes deficiency to God. What is one concrete way you will remind yourself of God's all-encompassing knowledge starting today?
?
Al-Ash'ari's argument shows that denying knowledge to God is self-contradictory. Where in your life have you contradicted your belief in God's perfection by acting as if He is ignorant of your reality? What step will you take to align your actions with your belief?

Order in the World Requires a Knowing Creator
Al-Ash'ari argues that the ordered structure of the world proves its Maker has knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Al-Ash'ari's teleological argument that the ordered structure of the world requires a Creator who possesses knowledge, power, and life.

Core Idea
Order in the world proves its Maker has knowledge, power, and life.

Al-Ash'ari presents a powerful argument from design. He asks a simple question: can orderly, wise crafts be produced by someone without knowledge? The answer leads to affirming that God must possess not only power but also knowledge and life. This lesson unpacks that argument and shows why it refutes those who deny divine attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Notice the rhythm of the argument.
The passage is a single continuous argument.
2
Identify the key question
Al-Ash'ari asks: 'Is it permissible that wise crafts be arranged by one who is not knowing?' This is the starting point.
The phrase 'wise crafts' (al-ṣanāʾiʿ al-ḥikmiyya) refers to the orderly design in the world.
3
Follow the logical implication
The opponents concede that such crafts require an agent who is knowing, powerful, and living. Al-Ash'ari then uses this concession to prove that the world's Maker must have these attributes.
Notice the parallel structure: the same reasoning applies to all attributes.
4
Extend the argument
Al-Ash'ari warns that the same arguments about knowledge also apply to power, life, hearing, and sight. This shows the argument's scope.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : هل يجوز أن ينسق الصنائع الحكمية من ليس بعالم؟ فإن قالوا : ذلك محال ولا يجوز في وجود الصنائع التي تجري على ترتيب ونظام إلا من عالم قادر حي . قيل لهم : وكذلك لا يجوز وجود الصنائع الحكمية التي تجري على ترتيب ونظام إلا من ذي علم وقدرة وحياة، فإن جاز ظهورها لا من ذي علم فما أنكرتم من جواز ظهورها لا من عالم قادر حي . وكل مسألة سألناهم عنها في العلم فهي داخلة عليهم في القدرة والحياة والسمع والبصر.
And it is said to them: Is it permissible that wise crafts be arranged by one who is not knowing? If they say: That is impossible; it is not permissible for crafts that proceed upon order and system to exist except from one who is knowing, powerful, and living. It is said to them: Likewise, it is not permissible for the wise crafts that proceed upon order and system to exist except from one possessing knowledge, power, and life. If they permit their appearance from one without knowledge, then why do you deny the permissibility of their appearance from one who is not knowing, powerful, and living? And every question we asked them about knowledge applies to power, life, hearing, and sight.
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage is an argument from design (teleological). Al-Ash'ari challenges the opponents: can a non-knowing agent produce orderly, wise crafts (al-ṣanāʾiʿ al-ḥikmiyya) that exhibit arrangement and system? The opponents will agree that this is impossible; such crafts require an agent who is knowing (ʿālim), powerful (qādir), and living (ḥayy). Al-Ash'ari then extends the point: if they admit that these crafts require a knowing agent, then they cannot deny that God possesses knowledge. He also warns that the same arguments about knowledge apply equally to power, life, hearing, and sight—so the reasoning is transferable.

Key Terms
الصنائع الحكمية
ترتيب
نظام
عالم
قادر
حي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think the argument only proves power, not knowledge.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari explicitly states that order requires both knowledge and power. He asks about 'wise crafts' (ḥikmiyya) and says they cannot come from one without knowledge.

Checkpoint
According to Al-Ash'ari, what kind of agent is needed to produce orderly, wise crafts?
A) One who is merely powerful
B) One who is knowing, powerful, and living
C) One who is eternal
D) One who is all-wise but not necessarily knowing

Key Takeaway
The observable order in the world necessitates a Creator who is knowing, powerful, and living.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you consider the intricate order in your own body—the beating of your heart, the coordination of your limbs—do you truly believe that this is the work of a Knowing, Powerful, and Living Creator, or do you sometimes take it for granted as mere biology? What would change in your daily gratitude if you saw each heartbeat as a direct sign of His knowledge?
?
The argument says that no orderly thing can come from a non-knowing source. Reflect on the areas of your life where you act as if things happen by chance—your career, your health, your relationships. Are you living as if God is ignorant of the order He placed in your life? How can you consciously attribute the good order in your life to His knowledge today?
?
Al-Ash'ari challenges his opponents admitting that order requires a knowing agent, then uses that against their denial. Have you ever admitted a truth about God with your lips but then lived in a way that contradicts it? What would it mean for you personally to let the order in creation press you into a deeper acknowledgment of God's attributes right now?
