Proof for God's Knowledge from Creaturely Ignorance
Al-Ash'ari argues that God must have knowledge because ignorance is a deficiency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the imperfection of ignorance in creatures proves that God must possess knowledge.

Core Idea
If ignorance is a defect in creatures, it cannot be in God.

This passage presents a powerful argument from analogy used by Imam al-Ash'ari to affirm God's knowledge. It starts from a simple observation about creatures and derives a necessary attribute of the Creator. The argument relies on the principle that what is a deficiency in creation cannot be attributed to Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic text and its translation. Notice how the argument is structured as a response to an opponent.
The passage uses a conditional sentence.
2
Identify the premise
Find the statement about creatures who lack knowledge. What is said about them?
The premise is that ignorance brings deficiency.
3
Follow the application to God
See how the author applies this premise to God. He argues that denying God's knowledge would imply deficiency in Him.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
What is the final conclusion? How does the author avoid attributing deficiency to God?
God must have knowledge to avoid deficiency.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : إذا كان من لا علم له من الخلق يلحقه الجهل والنقصان، فما أنكرتم من أنه لا بد من إثبات علم الله؟ وإلا ألحقتم به النقصان جل وعز عن قولكم وعلا، ألا ترون أن من لا يعلم من الخلق يلحقه الجهل والنقصان، ومن قال ذلك في الله عز وجل وصف الله سبحانه بما لا يليق به، فكذلك إذا كان من قيل له من الخلق لا علم له لحقه الجهل والنقصان، وجب أن لا يُنْفَى ذلك عن الله عز وجل لأنه لا يلحقه جهل ولا نقصان .
And it is said to them: If one among creation who has no knowledge is afflicted with ignorance and deficiency, then what prevents you from affirming that it is necessary to affirm God's knowledge? Otherwise you would attribute deficiency to Him—exalted and high above your statement. Do you not see that one among creation who does not know is afflicted with ignorance and deficiency, and whoever says that concerning God Almighty describes God with what is not befitting Him? Similarly, if it is said of one among creation that he has no knowledge, he is afflicted with ignorance and deficiency, so it is necessary that this not be negated of God Almighty, because He is not afflicted with ignorance or deficiency.
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage is a response (jawaab) using an argument from analogy (qiyaas) to prove that God must have knowledge. The author first states a premise: any creature lacking knowledge is thereby incomplete and deficient. He then applies this to God: if one denies that God has knowledge, one would be attributing deficiency to God, which is impossible. Therefore, God must have knowledge. The argument is based on the principle that what constitutes imperfection in creatures cannot be affirmed of God, and conversely, what is a perfection in creatures must be affirmed of God in a manner befitting Him. The author drives the point home by repeating the conditional: if a creature without knowledge is deficient, then the same reasoning compels affirming knowledge for God to avoid any hint of deficiency in the Divine.

Key Terms
جهل (ignorance)
نقصان (deficiency)
إثبات علم الله (affirmation of God's knowledge)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the argument implies God is like creatures, making the analogy literal.
Correction: The argument is not about likeness but about the universal concept of perfection and deficiency. What is a defect in creatures must also be a defect in God if attributed, so to avoid imperfection, God must have knowledge.

Checkpoint
What is the core premise used to prove that God must have knowledge?
A) God's knowledge is like human knowledge.
B) Ignorance is a deficiency in creatures, so it cannot apply to God.
C) The Quran explicitly says God is knowledgeable.
D) The Mu'tazila deny God's knowledge.

Key Takeaway
God's knowledge is proven by the principle that what is a deficiency in creatures cannot be attributed to the Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If ignorance is a deficiency in creatures, how does recognizing your own ignorance move you to seek knowledge of Allah? What area of your faith have you been neglecting to learn about, and will you commit to studying it this week?
?
The lesson proves that God cannot be ignorant. Does your daily life reflect a deep conviction that Allah knows everything you do and think? When was the last time you acted as if He did not know, and what will you change about that?
?
The argument hinges on the principle that deficiency is impossible for God. In what ways have you described or imagined Allah with human-like deficiencies, and how can you purify your belief to be worthy of His majesty?

Proof of God's Attributes from Orderly Creation
Response arguing orderly acts necessitate a knowing, powerful, living agent.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the observable order and system in creation logically requires that the Creator possesses knowledge, power, and life.

Core Idea
Order in creation demands a knowing, powerful, and living Creator.

This passage presents a rational argument from design. Imam al-Ash'ari challenges his opponents to consider the well-ordered works of the universe. He argues that such order cannot arise from an ignorant agent; it must come from a being who is knowing, powerful, and living. This lesson will unpack that argument and show how it establishes multiple divine attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice the dialectical style: a question, an admission, and a refutation.
The argument is built on a common premise shared with the opponents.
2
Identify the key premise
The author asks: can well-ordered works come from an ignorant agent? The opponents admit it's impossible. This premise is the foundation of the proof.
3
See the application to other attributes
If order requires knowledge, it also requires power and life—since only a living, powerful being can act with order. The author extends this to hearing and sight as well.
This refutes those who accept only one attribute.
4
Reflect on the dialectical structure
The author uses the opponent's own admission against them. If they allow order without knowledge, they cannot deny order without power or life.
This is a classic reductio ad absurdum.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : هل يجوز أن ينسق الصنائع الحكمية من ليس بعالم؟ فإن قالوا : ذلك محال ولا يجوز في وجود الصنائع التي تجري على ترتيب ونظام إلا من عالم قادر حي . قيل لهم : وكذلك لا يجوز وجود الصنائع الحكمية التي تجري على ترتيب ونظام إلا من ذي علم وقدرة وحياة، فإن جاز ظهورها لا من ذي علم فما أنكرتم من جواز ظهورها لا من عالم قادر حي . وكل مسألة سألناهم عنها في العلم فهي داخلة عليهم في القدرة والحياة والسمع والبصر
And it is said to them: Is it permissible that well-ordered skilled works be produced by one who is not knowledgeable? If they say: That is impossible, and it is not permissible in the existence of works that proceed upon order and system except from a knowing, powerful, living [agent]. It is said to them: Likewise, it is not permissible for the existence of well-ordered skilled works that proceed upon order and system to be from [anyone] other than one possessing knowledge, power, and life. For if it is permissible for them to appear from one without knowledge, then what prevents you from allowing their appearance from one who is not knowing, powerful, [and] living? And every issue we have asked them about concerning knowledge applies to them regarding power, life, hearing, and sight.
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage is another response (jawaab) employing a rational argument based on the observed order in the world. The author first poses a question: can ordered, skillful works come from an ignorant agent? The Mu'tazila (the implied opponents) would agree that it is impossible—such works can only come from a being who is knowing (alim), powerful (qadir), and living (hayy). The author then applies this universally: all well-ordered, systematic works must originate from a possessor of these attributes. He then turns the argument back on the opponent: if one allows such works to appear without a knowing agent, then there is no basis to deny that they could appear without any attributes at all. Finally, the author states that the same reasoning used for knowledge applies equally to the other attributes of power, life, hearing, and sight—each is necessary for the production of the order we observe.

Key Terms
صنائع حكمية (well-ordered works)
ترتيب ونظام (order and system)
عالم قادر حي (knowing, powerful, living)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the argument only proves God's knowledge, not power or life.
Correction: The author explicitly extends the argument to power, life, hearing, and sight, showing that all are necessary for the production of orderly works.

Checkpoint
What is the key observation that proves God must have knowledge, power, and life?
A) The Quran describes God as knowing.
B) The order and system in creation cannot come from an ignorant agent.
C) Humans are created with knowledge.
D) The Mu'tazila deny these attributes.

Key Takeaway
The orderly universe proves that its Creator is knowing, powerful, and living.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you look at the intricate order of your own heartbeat or the cycles of nature, do you sincerely attribute that order to a knowing, powerful, living Creator, or does your heart sometimes lean toward attributing it to blind chance? What would change today if you truly internalized that every orderly detail is from the Living, Knowing, Powerful One?
?
The argument forces the opponent to accept all attributes consistently. In your own life, do you acknowledge God's knowledge, power, and life in every blessing and trial, or do you selectively attribute some things to your own effort or luck? Take a moment to repent for the times you have overlooked His attributes.
?
If you truly believed that the same God who ordered the universe is personally ordering the events of your day, how would you face any chaos or confusion coming your way? Will you pause right now and thank Him for His perfect knowledge and power over your situation?

Refutation of Mu'tazila on Sami' and Basir
Refutation of Mu'tazila claim that Sami' and Basir mean Alim.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari refutation of the Mu'tazilite claim that God's attributes 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' are merely synonyms for 'Knowing', and how the argument reveals the necessity of affirming distinct divine attributes.

Core Idea
If hearing and seeing were just knowing, then everything known would be heard—an absurdity that refutes the Mu'tazila.

In this passage, Imam al-Ash'ari directly confronts the Mu'tazilite attempt to reduce God's attributes of Hearing and Seeing to mere knowledge. Using both scriptural evidence and logical consistency, he shows that their interpretation leads to contradictions. As you read, pay attention to how he compares their position to that of the Christians and uses a dialectical argument to expose the flaw.

Guided Path
1
Identify the Mu'tazilite claim
Read the opening sentence to understand the position being refuted: the Mu'tazila claim that 'Sami' and 'Basir' mean 'Alim'.
2
Follow the scriptural challenge
Notice how the author cites verses where God explicitly says 'I hear and I see' and asks the Mu'tazila if they interpret these as knowledge. This sets up the first inconsistency.
3
Analyze the dialectical argument
Examine the series of analogical questions: if Sami' and Basir mean Alim, then Qadir should also mean Alim, etc. See how the author forces the Mu'tazila into a reductio ad absurdum.
4
Observe the final rebuttal
Read the last part where the Mu'tazila object, and the author turns their logic against them: if hearing were knowledge, everything known would be heard—an absurdity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وزعمت المعتزلة أن قول الله عز وجل: سميع بصير ) [ الحج : 11] إن معناه عليم . قيل لهم : فإذا قال الله عز وجل : ( إِنَّني مَعَكُمَا أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَى ) [طه: ٤٦] وقال : قَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجَدِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِهَا ﴾ [المجادلة:1] فهـل معـنـى ذلـك عندكم علم؟ فإن قالوا: نعم . قيل لهم: فقد وجب عليكم أن تقولوا معنى قوله : ( أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَى ) ؛ أعلم، وأعلم إذ كان معنى ذلك العلم . ونفت المعتزلة صفات رب العالمين وزعمت أن معنى سميع بصير، أي بمعنى عليم، كما زعمت النصارى أن سمع الله هو بصره وهو رؤيته، كلامه، وهو علمه، وهو ابنه عز الله وجل وتعالى عن ذلك علواً كبيراً . فيقال للمعتزلة : إذا زعمتم أن معنى سميع وبصير معنى عالم، فهلا زعمتم أن معنى قادر معنى عالم ، فإذا زعمتم أن معنى سميع وبصير معنى قادر، فهلا زعمتم أن معنى قادر معنى عالم، وإذا زعمتم أن معنى حي معنى قادر فلم لا زعمتم أن معنى قادر معنى عالم؟ فإن قالوا: هذا يوجب أن يكون كل معلوم مقدوراً . قيل لهم : ولو كان معنى سميع بصير معنى عالم لكان كل معلوم مسموعاً، وإذا لم يجز ذلك بطل قولكم .
And the Mu'tazila claimed that God Almighty's saying 'Hearing, Seeing' (Sami', Basir) [al-Hajj 22:11] means 'Knowing' (Alim). It is said to them: When God Almighty said, 'Indeed, I am with you both; I hear and I see' [Ta-Ha 20:46], and said, 'Indeed, God has heard the speech of the one who argues with you concerning her husband' [al-Mujadilah 58:1], does that mean, according to you, 'knowledge'? If they say: Yes. It is said to them: Then it becomes necessary for you to say that the meaning of His statement 'I hear and I see' is 'I know' and 'I know', since the meaning of that is knowledge. And the Mu'tazila denied the attributes of the Lord of the worlds and claimed that the meaning of 'Sami', Basir' is in the sense of 'Alim', just as the Christians claimed that God's hearing is His sight, His seeing, His speech, and His knowledge, and His son—exalted is God and high above that, a great exaltation. So it is said to the Mu'tazila: If you claim that the meaning of 'Sami'' and 'Basir' is the meaning of 'Alim', then why do you not claim that the meaning of 'Qadir' (Powerful) is the meaning of 'Alim'? And if you claim that the meaning of 'Sami'' and 'Basir' is the meaning of 'Qadir', then why do you not claim that the meaning of 'Qadir' is the meaning of 'Alim'? And if you claim that the meaning of 'Hayy' (Living) is the meaning of 'Qadir', then why do you not claim that the meaning of 'Qadir' is the meaning of 'Alim'? If they say: This would require that everything known be an object of power (maqdur). It is said to them: And if the meaning of 'Sami', Basir' were 'Alim', then everything known would be something heard. And since that is not permissible, your statement is invalidated.
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage is a refutation (radd) of the Mu'tazilite claim that the divine attributes 'Hearing' (Sami') and 'Seeing' (Basir) are merely synonyms for 'Knowing' (Alim). The author begins by citing the Mu'tazilite position. Then he brings scriptural evidence: verses where God explicitly says 'I hear and I see' and 'God has heard', asking whether the Mu'tazila interpret these as referring only to knowledge. If they say yes, then they are forced to say that 'hear' and 'see' mean 'know', which is a distortion of the plain meaning. The author then draws a parallel: the Mu'tazila's denial of distinct attributes resembles the Christian claim that God's hearing is His sight, etc., which leads to internal collapse of distinctions (and even the claim that His Son is His knowledge—a clear Christian heresy). The author then uses a dialectical argument (qiyaas al-musawaat) to show the inconsistency: if Sami' and Basir both mean Alim, then by the same logic, Qadir (Powerful) should also mean Alim, and Hayy (Living) should mean Qadir, leading to all attributes collapsing into one. When the Mu'tazila object that this would make everything known an object of power (thus blurring distinctions), the author counters by turning their own logic against them: if Sami' and Basir meant Alim, then everything known would be heard, which is absurd. Therefore, their position is false. The passage effectively defends the classical Ash'ari position that God's attributes are real and distinct, not merely names for one essence.

Key Terms
سميع بصير (Hearing, Seeing)
عليم (Knowing)
قادر (Powerful)
حي (Living)
صفات (attributes)
معتزلة (Mu'tazila)
نصارى (Christians)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's attributes 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' are just ways of saying He knows all.
Correction: This leads to absurdity because it blurs all attributes, contradicts scripture, and implies that everything known is heard, which is false.

Checkpoint
What consequence does al-Ash'ari draw from the Mu'tazila claim that 'Sami' and 'Basir' mean 'Alim'?
A) It leads to understanding God's essence.
B) It implies that everything known is heard, which is false.
C) It reduces the number of attributes.
D) It agrees with the Quran.

Key Takeaway
The Mu'tazilite attempt to reduce distinct divine attributes to mere knowledge is self-defeating because it leads to contradictions and distorts the plain meaning of the Qur'an.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite 'Sami' Allahu liman hamidah', do you truly believe that Allah hears your specific words, or do you reduce it to mere knowledge? How would your prayer change if you were certain that His hearing is a real, distinct attribute that engages with your voice? This week, before you say 'Rabbana lakal hamd', pause and consciously affirm that Allah hears you now—and then speak accordingly.
?
The Mu'tazila denied distinct attributes to protect God's unity, but you might unconsciously do something similar by feeling uncomfortable with affirming attributes that sound anthropomorphic. Examine your heart: do you ever shy away from believing that Allah sees your private actions or hears your silent thoughts because it feels too 'human'? If so, repent and affirm His attributes as the salaf did—without tashbih (likening) but with full reality. Write down one area of your life where you need to act more consciously as if Allah sees and hears you.

Ash'ari's Dialectical Challenge: Do 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' Mean Knowledge?
Ash'ari queries the Mu'tazila on the meaning of hearing and seeing.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses a logical question to expose the Mu'tazila's reduction of divine attributes to knowledge.

Core Idea
Ash'ari forces the Mu'tazila to admit their own logical consequence.

Imam Ash'ari engages the Mu'tazila on the meaning of divine attributes. He quotes Quranic verses where Allah says 'I hear and I see' and 'Allah has heard.' Then he asks a simple question: do they understand these as meaning knowledge? This small question leads to a significant theological commitment.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice how the passage is structured as a question and answer exchange.
The passage is short but dense. Pay attention to the logical flow.
2
Identify Ash'ari's question
What does Ash'ari ask the Mu'tazila? Look at the opening: 'When Allah says... does this meaning with you (i.e., do you understand it as) knowledge?'
3
Understand the consequence
After the Mu'tazila answer 'Yes', Ash'ari draws a conclusion. What does he say they must then hold? Note the repetition of 'I know'.
This reveals the Mu'tazila's commitment to interpreting hearing and seeing as knowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قيل لهم : فإذا قال الله عز وجل : ( إِنَّني مَعَكُمَا أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَى ) [طه: ٤٦] وقال : قَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجَدِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِهَا ﴾ [المجادلة:1] فهـل معـنـى ذلـك عندكم علم؟ فإن قالوا: نعم . قيل لهم: فقد وجب عليكم أن تقولوا معنى قوله : ( أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَى ) ؛ أعلم، وأعلم إذ كان معنى ذلك العلم.
It was said to them: When Allah Almighty says: 'Indeed, I am with you; I hear and I see' [Ta-Ha 46] and says: 'Indeed, Allah has heard the statement of the one who argues with you concerning her husband' [Al-Mujadilah 1], does this meaning with you (i.e., do you understand it as) knowledge? If they say: Yes. It is said to them: Then you must necessarily say that the meaning of His saying 'I hear and I see' is 'I know', and I know, since the meaning of that is knowledge.
Source pages p. 121

Commentary
This passage is a brief dialectical exchange. Ash'ari poses a question to his Mu'tazila opponents: when Allah says in the Quran 'I hear and I see' and 'Allah has heard', do they interpret this as meaning knowledge? The opponents answer affirmatively. Ash'ari then draws the logical consequence: if they admit that, then they must hold that the meaning of 'I hear and I see' is 'I know'. The repetition of 'I know' emphasizes that the attribute of hearing and seeing is reduced to knowledge. This sets the stage for a broader refutation of the Mu'tazila's denial of distinct attributes.

Key Terms
سمع
بصر
علم
معنى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Mu'tazila might think they can admit that hearing means knowledge without any further consequence.
Correction: Ash'ari shows that their admission logically commits them to saying that 'I hear and I see' literally means 'I know', which reduces the distinct attribute of hearing to knowledge.

Checkpoint
According to Ash'ari, what does the Mu'tazila's admission about the meaning of 'hearing' and 'seeing' in the Quran commit them to?
A) That hearing and seeing are distinct attributes.
B) That the meaning of 'I hear and I see' is 'I know'.
C) That God's attributes are all identical.
D) That God does not have hearing and seeing.

Key Takeaway
Ash'ari demonstrates that the Mu'tazila's interpretation of hearing and seeing as knowledge leads to the conclusion that the Quranic words literally mean 'I know'.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that Allah hears and sees everything, do you truly believe He hears your whispered sins and sees your hidden motives? How does that reality change what you do when no one is watching?
?
If someone asked you to prove that Allah's hearing and seeing are real attributes and not just metaphors for His knowledge, would you be able to defend the literal words of the Quran? What is at stake for your faith if you cannot?
?
Do you ever find yourself reducing Allah's attributes to abstract concepts to make them easier to understand? Has this lesson exposed any tendency in your heart to explain away the awe of His hearing and seeing? What will you do to restore that awe today?

6 min
Refutation of Mu'tazila Reduction of Hearing and Seeing to Knowledge
Ash'ari refutes the Mu'tazila claim that hearing and seeing mean knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari refutes the Mu'tazila claim that God's attributes of hearing and seeing are merely synonyms for His knowledge, using logical reductio arguments.

Core Idea
Reducing divine attributes to knowledge leads to absurd consequences across all attributes.

Imam al-Ash'ari here confronts the Mu'tazila position that God's attributes of hearing and seeing are nothing but His knowledge. He draws a parallel to Christian doctrines that conflate God's attributes and then uses a series of hypothetical questions to expose the inconsistency of the Mu'tazila claim. The passage ends by turning the Mu'tazila's own objection against them.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to understand the Mu'tazila claim and Ash'ari's comparison to Christians.
Notice the rhetorical style of Ash'ari.
2
Follow the reductio arguments
Ash'ari asks: if 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' mean 'Knowing', then why not also reduce 'Powerful' and 'Living' to 'Knowing'? This shows the arbitrariness of the Mu'tazila reduction.
3
Examine the objection and counter
The Mu'tazila object that reducing 'Powerful' to 'Knowing' would make everything known capable. Ash'ari turns the tables: by their logic, reducing 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' to 'Knowing' would make everything known heard.
This demonstrates the absurdity.
4
Conclude the lesson
Reflect on how Ash'ari defends the distinct reality of divine attributes against reductionist approaches.
This lesson reinforces the importance of affirming attributes as real and distinct.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونفت المعتزلة صفات رب العالمين وزعمت أن معنى سميع بصير، أي بمعنى عليم، كما زعمت النصارى أن سمع الله هو بصره وهو رؤيته، كلامه، وهو علمه، وهو ابنه عز الله وجل وتعالى عن ذلك علواً كبيراً . فيقال للمعتزلة : إذا زعمتم أن معنى سميع وبصير معنى عالم، فهلا زعمتم أن معنى قادر معنى عالم ، فإذا زعمتم أن معنى سميع وبصير معنى قادر، فهلا زعمتم أن معنى قادر معنى عالم، وإذا زعمتم أن معنى حي معنى قادر فلم لا زعمتم أن معنى قادر معنى عالم؟ فإن قالوا: هذا يوجب أن يكون كل معلوم مقدوراً . قيل لهم : ولو كان معنى سميع بصير معنى عالم لكان كل معلوم مسموعاً، وإذا لم يجز ذلك بطل قولكم .
And the Mu'tazila negated the attributes of the Lord of the worlds and claimed that the meaning of 'Hearing, Seeing' is 'Knowing', just as the Christians claimed that God's hearing is His sight and His vision, His speech, and is His knowledge, and is His son—exalted is Allah above that greatly. So it is said to the Mu'tazila: If you claim that the meaning of 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' is the meaning of 'Knowing', then why don't you claim that the meaning of 'Powerful' is the meaning of 'Knowing'? And if you claim that the meaning of 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' is the meaning of 'Powerful', then why don't you claim that the meaning of 'Powerful' is the meaning of 'Knowing'? And if you claim that the meaning of 'Living' is the meaning of 'Powerful', then why don't you claim that the meaning of 'Powerful' is the meaning of 'Knowing'? If they say: This would necessitate that everything known is capable. It is said to them: And if the meaning of 'Hearing, Seeing' were the meaning of 'Knowing', then everything known would be heard. Since that is not permissible, your statement is invalid.
Source pages p. 121

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of the Mu'tazila position. Ash'ari begins by stating that the Mu'tazila deny the attributes of God and claim that the terms 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' are just synonyms for 'Knowing'. He compares this to the Christian doctrine that identifies God's attributes with each other and even with His son. Then Ash'ari uses a series of hypothetical questions: if you reduce 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' to 'Knowing', why don't you also reduce 'Powerful' to 'Knowing'? And if you claim that 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' mean 'Powerful' (which is another possibility), then why not reduce 'Powerful' to 'Knowing'? And if you reduce 'Living' to 'Powerful', why not reduce 'Powerful' to 'Knowing'? The point is that their method of reduction is arbitrary. The Mu'tazila object that if they reduced 'Powerful' to 'Knowing', then every known thing would be capable (which is false). Ash'ari turns this against them: if 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' mean 'Knowing', then everything known would be heard, which is also false. Therefore their position is invalid.

Key Terms
سميع بصير
عليم
صفات
معتزلة
نصارى
قادر
حي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Mu'tazila think that 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' are simply synonyms for 'Knowing' and do not imply distinct attributes.
Correction: Ash'ari shows that such reduction leads to absurd consequences, such as everything known being heard, and applies to other attributes like 'Powerful' and 'Living'.

Checkpoint
What objection does Ash'ari raise against the Mu'tazila's claim that 'Hearing' and 'Seeing' mean 'Knowing'?
A) If that were true, then everything known would be heard.
B) If that were true, then God would not be powerful.
C) If that were true, then God would have a son.
D) If that were true, then the Christians would be correct.

Key Takeaway
Divine attributes are real and distinct; reducing them to a single attribute leads to logical absurdities.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever find yourself mentally reducing Allah's attributes to just 'He knows everything,' forgetting that He actually hears your whispers and sees your tears? How would your worship change if you fully believed His hearing is distinct from His knowledge?
?
When you read this passage, you see a logical inconsistency in reducing attributes. But have you yourself ever acted as if Allah's mercy is just 'knowing you need help' rather than a real, active mercy that intervenes? What step will you take today to call upon Him by His specific attributes—asking Him to hear you, see you, and be powerful for you?

intermediate
5 min
The Necessity of an Eternal Will from Eternal Knowledge
Ash'ari establishes the first part of his argument: eternal knowledge implies eternal will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Ash'ari deduces the eternity of God's will from His eternal knowledge.

Core Idea
Eternal knowledge demands an eternal will.

Ash'ari begins by establishing a common ground with the Mu'tazila: they agree that God is eternally knowing. He then argues that eternal knowledge necessarily implies eternal will—whatever God knows will occur, He must will it to occur. This short passage lays the cornerstone for the necessity of God's will being eternal and uncreated.

Guided Path
1
Recognize the initial premise
Notice that Ash'ari starts by getting the Mu'tazila to affirm that God is eternally knowing. This is the starting point of the argument.
This premise is accepted by both parties.
2
Follow the logical entailment
Ash'ari then argues: if God knows something will happen at a specific time, He must will it to happen at that time. Similarly, if He knows it will not happen, He must will it not to happen. Thus, His will is eternal just like His knowledge.
The key is the correlation between knowledge and will.
3
Consider the objection
The Mu'tazila object that God's will is created. After reading this part, prepare to see how Ash'ari refutes this in the next lesson.
This objection sets up the next argument.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الباب العاشر الكلام في الإرادة والرد على المعتزلة في ذلك (١) ١٨٤ - مسألة يقال لهم : ألستم تزعمون أن الله عز وجل لم يزل عالماً؟ فمن قولهم : نعم، قيل لهم : فلم لا قلتم : إن من لم يزل عالماً في وقت من الأوقات، فلم يزل مريداً أن يكون في ذلك الوقت، وما لم يزل عالماً أنه لا يكون فلم يزل مريداً أن لا يكون، وأنه لم يزل مريداً أن يكون ما علم كما علم؟ فإن قالوا: لا نقول إن الله لم يزل مريداً؛ لأن الله مريد بإرادة مخلوقة . يقال لهم : ولم زعمتم أن الله عز وجل مريد بإرادة مخلوقة؟ ومـا الفصل بينكم وبين الجهمية في زعمهم أن الله عالم بعلم مخلوق، وإذا لم يجز أن يكون علم الله مخلوقاً، فما أنكرتم أن لا تكون إرادة الله مخلوقة؟ فإن قالوا: لا يجوز أن يكون علم الله محدثاً؛ لأن ذلك يقتضي أن يكون حدث بعلم آخر، كذلك لا إلى غاية قيل لهم ما أنكرتم أن لا تكون إرادة الله محدثة مخلوقة؛ لأن ذلك يقتضي أن تكون حدثت عن إرادة أخرى، ثم كذلك لا إلى غاية .
Chapter Ten: Speech about the Will and Refutation of the Mu'tazila concerning that. Issue 184: It is said to them: Do you not claim that Allah Almighty is eternally knowing? From their statement: Yes. It is said to them: Then why do you not say that whoever is eternally knowing at a time, then he is eternally willing that (thing) to be at that time, and what he is eternally knowing that it will not be, he is eternally willing that it not be, and that he is eternally willing that what he knows be as he knows? If they say: We do not say that Allah is eternally willing, because Allah is willing by a created will. It is said to them: And why do you claim that Allah Almighty is willing by a created will? And what is the distinction between you and the Jahmiyya in their claim that Allah knows by a created knowledge? And if it is not permissible for Allah's knowledge to be created, then why do you deny that Allah's will is created? If they say: It is not permissible for Allah's knowledge to be originated, because that would require that it originate by another knowledge, and so on ad infinitum. It is said to them: Why do you deny that Allah's will is originated and created? Because that would require that it originate from another will, and so on ad infinitum.
Source pages p. 122

Commentary
This passage is a proof for the eternity of God's will. Ash'ari begins by getting the Mu'tazila to acknowledge that God is eternally knowing. Then he argues: if God is eternally knowing that something will occur at a certain time, then He must be eternally willing that it occur at that time. Similarly, if He knows it will not occur, He must be eternally willing that it not occur. So His will should be eternal like His knowledge. The Mu'tazila object that God's will is created. Ash'ari responds by comparing them to the Jahmiyya, who claimed that God's knowledge is created. Since the Mu'tazila reject created knowledge (because knowledge cannot be created), they should also reject created will. The Mu'tazila then argue that knowledge cannot be created because that would lead to an infinite regress (every created knowledge would need another knowledge to create it). Ash'ari turns this against them: the same applies to will; if will is created, it would require another will to create it, leading to infinite regress. Therefore, will must be eternal and uncreated.

Key Terms
إرادة
علم
مريد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God's will is temporal and changes like human will.
Correction: Ash'ari argues that God's will must be eternal because His knowledge is eternal and will corresponds to knowledge.

Checkpoint
What premise do the Mu'tazila accept that Ash'ari uses as the starting point for his argument?
A) God is eternally willing.
B) God is eternally knowing.
C) God's will is created.
D) God's knowledge is created.

Key Takeaway
God's eternal knowledge necessarily implies an eternal will that corresponds to it.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's will is eternal and perfect, why do you sometimes will what He does not will? What does that reveal about the state of your heart and your submission to Him?
?
Do you truly believe that every trial and blessing in your life is exactly what God willed for you from eternity? How would living with that conviction change your reactions to hardship and gratitude?
?
When you choose to sin, you are willing something that God does not will. How does the eternity of God's will confront you with your own rebellion, and what will you do about it today?

6 min
Refuting the Createdness of Divine Will
Ash'ari refutes the Mu'tazila claim of a created will using analogy and infinite regress.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Ash'ari's refutation of the Mu'tazila claim that God's will is created, using the analogy with knowledge and the infinite regress argument.

Core Idea
If knowledge cannot be created, then will cannot be created either.

In the previous lesson, Ash'ari presented the positive argument that eternal knowledge implies eternal will. The Mu'tazila countered by claiming that God's will is created. In this lesson, Ash'ari refutes that claim by comparing it to the Jahmiyya's claim that God's knowledge is created—a position the Mu'tazila themselves reject. He then uses the infinite regress argument to show that both knowledge and will must be uncreated.

Guided Path
1
Understand the analogical argument
Ash'ari asks: why do you claim will is created? He compares you to the Jahmiyya who say knowledge is created. Since you reject created knowledge, you should reject created will.
This is an argument by analogy.
2
Examine the infinite regress argument
If knowledge were created, it would need another knowledge to create it, leading to an infinite chain. The same applies to will. Therefore, will cannot be created.
This is a reductio ad absurdum.
3
Reflect on the conclusion
Both arguments together prove that God's will is eternal and uncreated, just like His knowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الباب العاشر الكلام في الإرادة والرد على المعتزلة في ذلك (١) ١٨٤ - مسألة يقال لهم : ألستم تزعمون أن الله عز وجل لم يزل عالماً؟ فمن قولهم : نعم، قيل لهم : فلم لا قلتم : إن من لم يزل عالماً في وقت من الأوقات، فلم يزل مريداً أن يكون في ذلك الوقت، وما لم يزل عالماً أنه لا يكون فلم يزل مريداً أن لا يكون، وأنه لم يزل مريداً أن يكون ما علم كما علم؟ فإن قالوا: لا نقول إن الله لم يزل مريداً؛ لأن الله مريد بإرادة مخلوقة . يقال لهم : ولم زعمتم أن الله عز وجل مريد بإرادة مخلوقة؟ ومـا الفصل بينكم وبين الجهمية في زعمهم أن الله عالم بعلم مخلوق، وإذا لم يجز أن يكون علم الله مخلوقاً، فما أنكرتم أن لا تكون إرادة الله مخلوقة؟ فإن قالوا: لا يجوز أن يكون علم الله محدثاً؛ لأن ذلك يقتضي أن يكون حدث بعلم آخر، كذلك لا إلى غاية قيل لهم ما أنكرتم أن لا تكون إرادة الله محدثة مخلوقة؛ لأن ذلك يقتضي أن تكون حدثت عن إرادة أخرى، ثم كذلك لا إلى غاية .

It is said to them: Then why do you not say that whoever is eternally knowing at a time, then he is eternally willing that (thing) to be at that time, and what he is eternally knowing that it will not be, he is eternally willing that it not be, and that he is eternally willing that what he knows be as he knows? If they say: It is not permissible for Allah's knowledge to be originated, because that would require that it originate by another knowledge, and so on ad infinitum. It is said to them: Why do you deny that Allah's will is originated and created? Because that would require that it originate from another will, and so on ad infinitum.

Then he argues: if God is eternally knowing that something will occur at a certain time, then He must be eternally willing that it occur at that time. Similarly, if He knows it will not occur, He must be eternally willing that it not occur. So His will should be eternal like His knowledge. The Mu'tazila object that God's will is created. The Mu'tazila then argue that knowledge cannot be created because that would lead to an infinite regress (every created knowledge would need another knowledge to create it). Ash'ari turns this against them: the same applies to will; if will is created, it would require another will to create it, leading to infinite regress. Therefore, will must be eternal and uncreated.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
When you reflect on the infinite regress argument—that every created thing requires a creator—do you feel a deepened sense of awe at the necessity of an eternal Creator? How does this impact your reliance upon Allah in moments of uncertainty?
?
If you were among the Mu'tazila, what would it take for you to admit your inconsistency? Are you willing to accept truth even when it contradicts your prior assumptions, and what is preventing you from doing so today?

Eternal Knowledge Necessitates Eternal Will
Imam Ash'ari presents the first part of his argument, linking eternal knowledge to eternal will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses the Mu'tazila's own acceptance of eternal knowledge to prove that God's will must also be eternal.

Core Idea
If God eternally knows, He must eternally will what He knows.

Imam Ash'ari opens his rebuttal of the Mu'tazila on the attribute of will by drawing out a logical consequence of their own position. Since they affirm that God has always been knowing, he presses them to admit that this knowledge must be accompanied by an eternal will. This short passage sets up the core of the argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening question and response
Notice the Imam's tactic of taking the Mu'tazila's own premise: they affirm God's eternal knowledge.
2
Identify the logical implication
See how eternal knowledge of an event requires eternal will for that event. The Imam presents this as a necessary connection.
The argument relies on a conceptual link between knowledge and will.
3
Consider the Mu'tazila objection
The Mu'tazila deny the implication, claiming that God's will is created, not eternal.
This objection sets up the subsequent refutation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الباب العاشر
الكلام في الإرادة والرد على المعتزلة في ذلك (۱)
١٨٤ - مسألة
يقال لهم : ألستم تزعمون أن الله عز وجل لم يزل عالماً؟ فمن قولهم : نعم، قيل لهم : فلم لا قلتم : إن من لم يزل عالماً في وقت من الأوقات، فلم يزل مريداً أن يكون في ذلك الوقت، وما لم يزل عالماً أنه لا يكون فلم يزل مريداً أن لا يكون، وأنه لم يزل مريداً أن يكون ما علم كما علم؟ فإن قالوا: لا نقول إن الله لم يزل مريداً؛ لأن الله مريد بإرادة مخلوقة . يقال لهم : ولم زعمتم أن الله عز وجل مريد بإرادة مخلوقة؟ ومـا الفصل بينكم وبين الجهمية في زعمهم أن الله عالم بعلم مخلوق، وإذا لم يجز أن يكون علم الله مخلوقاً، فما أنكرتم أن لا تكون إرادة الله مخلوقة؟ فإن قالوا: لا يجوز أن يكون علم الله محدثاً؛ لأن ذلك يقتضي أن يكون حدث بعلم آخر، كذلك لا إلى غاية قيل لهم ما أنكرتم أن لا تكون إرادة الله محدثة مخلوقة؛ لأن ذلك يقتضي أن تكون حدثت عن إرادة أخرى، ثم كذلك لا إلى غاية .
فإن قالوا: لا يجوز أن يكون علم الله محدثاً؛ لأن من لم يكن عالماً ثم علم لحقه النقصان .
قيل لهم ولا يجوز أن تكون إرادة الله محدثة مخلوقة؛ لأن من لم يكن مريداً ثم أراد لحقه النقصان، وكما لا يجوز أن تكون إرادته تعالى محدثة مخلوقة، كذلك لا يجوز أن يكون كلامه محدثاً مخلوقاً .
The Tenth Chapter: Discussion on the Will and Refutation of the Mu'tazila Concerning That.
184 - Issue
It is said to them: Do you not claim that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, has always been knowing? They say: Yes. It is said to them: Then why do you not say that whoever has always been knowing at a time, then he has always been willing that it be at that time, and whatever he has always known will not be, then he has always been willing that it not be, and that he has always been willing that what he knew be just as he knew? If they say: We do not say that Allah has always been willing; because Allah wills with a created will. It is said to them: Why do you claim that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, wills with a created will? And what is the distinction between you and the Jahmiyya in their claim that Allah knows with a created knowledge? If it is not permissible for Allah's knowledge to be created, then why do you deny that Allah's will is created? If they say: It is not permissible for Allah's knowledge to be originated, because that would require that it occur by another knowledge, and so on ad infinitum. It is said to them: What prevents you from saying that Allah's will is originated and created? Because that would require that it occur by another will, and so on ad infinitum.
If they say: It is not permissible for Allah's knowledge to be originated, because whoever was not knowledgeable and then knew would be affected by deficiency.
It is said to them: It is not permissible for Allah's will to be originated and created, because whoever was not willing and then willed would be affected by deficiency. And just as it is not permissible for His will, Exalted, to be originated and created, so also it is not permissible for His speech to be originated and created.
Source pages p. 122

Commentary
This passage is a polemical argument against the Mu'tazila regarding the attribute of will (irada). The Imam begins by establishing a premise the Mu'tazila accept: that God (عز وجل) has always been knowing (لم يزل عالماً). He then uses this to challenge their denial of an eternal will. If God eternally knows that something will occur at a specific time, then He must have willed it from eternity. Similarly, if He knows something will not occur, He must have eternally willed its non-occurrence. The Mu'tazila respond that God wills with a created will (إرادة مخلوقة). The Imam counters by likening them to the Jahmiyya, who claimed a created knowledge. Drawing an analogy: if a created knowledge is unacceptable for God, a created will should also be unacceptable. He then refutes their objection that a created knowledge would lead to an infinite regress (لا إلى غاية) by pointing out that the same regress applies to a created will. When the Mu'tazila further argue that an originated knowledge implies deficiency (النقصان)—since acquiring knowledge after ignorance is a change for the worse—the Imam applies the same reasoning: an originated will would also imply deficiency. Finally, he extends the argument to speech (كلام), arguing that if the will cannot be created, neither can the speech of God. The entire argument is a reductio ad absurdum, forcing the Mu'tazila to consistently apply their own principles.

Key Terms
إرادة
علم
مخلوقة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the Mu'tazila in this passage, how does God will?
A) With an eternal will
B) With a created will
C) He does not will
D) With a temporal will

Key Takeaway
Eternal knowledge logically entails eternal will; the Mu'tazila's claim of a created will is inconsistent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's eternal knowledge determines everything that happens, and His will is inseparable from that knowledge, then every event in your life—every success, every trial—was willed by Him before time began. How does this truth change the way you face your current struggles? Will you submit to His will rather than resist it?
?
The Mu'tazila tried to separate God's will from His knowledge to protect His transcendence, but ended up with an inconsistency. Where in your own life have you created a contradiction by trying to reconcile your understanding of God with your desires? Will you humbly accept His attributes as He describes them, even if they challenge your logic?
?
You affirm that God knows all—your past, present, future. But do you live as if you truly believe His will for you is good? What specific area of your life are you still trying to control, as if you know better than His eternal decree? Will you surrender that area to Him today?

Refuting the Created Will: Analogy and Extension to Speech
Imam Ash'ari refutes the Mu'tazila by analogy, infinite regress, deficiency, and extends to speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses analogy, infinite regress, and deficiency arguments to refute the Mu'tazila's claim of a created will, and extends the same reasoning to God's speech.

Core Idea
If a created will is absurd, then a created speech is equally absurd.

In the second part of the argument, Imam Ash'ari systematically dismantles the Mu'tazila's position. He draws an analogy to the Jahmiyya's error, points out the infinite regress that would result from a created attribute, and argues that a created will would imply deficiency in God. He then extends the same reasoning to God's speech, showing that the Mu'tazila's view leads to unacceptable conclusions.

Guided Path
1
Identify the analogy to Jahmiyya
Notice how the Imam compares the Mu'tazila's claim to the Jahmiyya's claim of a created knowledge, which is rejected.
This shows inconsistency in the Mu'tazila's position.
2
Trace the infinite regress argument
Understand why a created attribute leads to an endless chain of attributes, which is impossible.
The same logic applies to both knowledge and will.
3
Examine the deficiency argument
Acquiring an attribute after lacking it implies imperfection in God.
This is applied to both knowledge and will.
4
Note the extension to speech
Realize that if will cannot be created, speech cannot be either.
This broadens the argument to another attribute.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

١٨٤ - مسألة

It is said to them: Do you not claim that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, has always been knowing? They say: Yes. It is said to them: Then why do you not say that whoever has always been knowing at a time, then he has always been willing that it be at that time, and whatever he has always known will not be, then he has always been willing that it not be, and that he has always been willing that what he knew be just as he knew? If they say: We do not say that Allah has always been willing; because Allah wills with a created will. It is said to them: Why do you claim that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, wills with a created will? And what is the distinction between you and the Jahmiyya in their claim that Allah knows with a created knowledge? If it is not permissible for Allah's knowledge to be created, then why do you deny that Allah's will is created? If they say: It is not permissible for Allah's knowledge to be originated, because that would require that it occur by another knowledge, and so on ad infinitum. It is said to them: What prevents you from saying that Allah's will is originated and created? Because that would require that it occur by another will, and so on ad infinitum.
If they say: It is not permissible for Allah's knowledge to be originated, because whoever was not knowledgeable and then knew would be affected by deficiency.
It is said to them: It is not permissible for Allah's will to be originated and created, because whoever was not willing and then willed would be affected by deficiency. And just as it is not permissible for His will, Exalted, to be originated and created, so also it is not permissible for His speech to be originated and created.

Checkpoint
What does the Imam say would happen if God's will were originated?

Key Takeaway
The attributes of will and speech must be eternal, as attributing temporality to them leads to absurdities and implies imperfection in God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that God's will is eternal and unchanging, how would that change the way you view His decree in your life—especially the hardships you face? Willingly commit today to trust that God's eternal will is perfectly wise and not subject to regret.
?
You just learned that attributing temporality to God's attributes implies deficiency. Search your own heart: do you ever speak about God as if He might 'change His mind' or become 'surprised' by your actions? What does that say about your understanding of His perfection? Repent of any mental image that diminishes His majesty.
?
Imam Ash'ari argued that if speech is created, then God would have been silent then spoke—implying imperfection. How does this truth compel you to approach the Qur'an, the uncreated speech of Allah? Will you commit to reciting it with the reverence it deserves as an eternal attribute of your Lord?

The Effectiveness of God's Will: A Proof from al-Ibanah
Imam Ash'ari refutes Mu'tazila by reductio ad absurdum.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses a logical reductio to argue that God's will is always effective, refuting the Mu'tazila position.

Core Idea
If God's will were not effective, most of it would never happen.

This passage presents a concise but powerful argument from Imam Ash'ari against the Mu'tazila, who claimed that God does not will evil deeds. The Imam shows that this claim leads to a contradiction with the observed reality that disbelief is more prevalent than faith, and with the consensus that God's will is always carried out. Let's unpack the logic step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its literal translation once to get the overall flow of the argument. Notice the concise, logical style.
The argument is structured as a conditional: if the Mu'tazila claim is true, then absurd consequences follow.
2
Identify the Mu'tazila premise
The author explicitly states the Mu'tazila claim: that disbelief and disobedience were within God's authority but He does not will them, and He willed all people to believe but they did not.
This premise is the starting point of the reductio.
3
Follow the logical deduction
The Imam deduces that if the premise is true, then most of what God willed (universal faith) did not happen, and most of what He did not will (disbelief) did happen. This is because disbelief is more common than faith.
The conclusion contradicts the fundamental Islamic consensus that God's will is always effective.
4
Understand the appeal to consensus
The final sentence invokes the unanimous agreement of Muslims (ijma'): 'what Allah wills to be, is, and what He does not will, is not.' This consensus is the standard by which the Mu'tazila position is judged false.
5
Reflect on the theological implication
This argument defends the Sunni view that God's will is absolute and covers all things, including evil actions. It does not deny human responsibility but affirms God's sovereignty.
The argument is not about free will but about the effectiveness of God's will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
دليل آخر :
ويقال لهم : إذا زعمتم أنه قد كان في سلطان الله عز وجل الكفر والعصيان، وهو لا يريده وأراد أن يؤمن الخلق أجمعون، فلم يؤمنوا. فقد وجب على قولكم : إن أكثر ما شاء الله أن يكون لم يكن، وأكثر ما شاء الله أن لا يكون كان؛ لأن الكفر الذي كان وهو لا يشاؤه عندكم أكثر من الإيمان الذي كان وهو يشاؤه، وأكثر ما شاء الله أن يكون لم يكن ، وهذا جحد لما أجمع عليه المسلمون من أن ما شاء الله أن يكون ،كان، وما لا يشاء لا يكون .
Another proof:
And it is said to them: When you claim that disbelief and disobedience were within the authority of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, while He does not will them, and He willed that all mankind believe, but they did not believe. Then according to your statement it necessarily follows that most of what Allah willed to be was not, and most of what Allah willed not to be was; because disbelief which occurred—and He does not will it according to you—is more than faith which occurred—and He wills it—and most of what Allah willed to be was not. And this is a denial of what Muslims have unanimously agreed upon: that what Allah wills to be, is, and what He does not will, is not.
Source pages p. 123

Commentary
This passage is a proof (دليل) against the Mu'tazila concerning the effectiveness of God's will. The Imam presents a reductio ad absurdum based on the Mu'tazila claim that God does not will disbelief and disobedience, but He willed that all people believe, yet not all believed. Since disbelief is more prevalent than faith, it follows that most of what God wills (faith) does not happen, and most of what He does not will (disbelief) does happen. This contradicts the Muslim consensus (إجماع) that what God wills occurs and what He does not will does not occur. The argument highlights the logical inconsistency in the Mu'tazila position: if God's will is not universally effective, then He is not omnipotent in will, which is a form of jahd (denial).

Key Terms
سُلطان
الكفر
العصيان
الإيمان
مشيئة
إجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God does not will disbelief and disobedience, yet they occur.
Correction: This leads to contradiction: most of what God wills (faith) does not happen, and most of what He does not will (disbelief) does happen, which contradicts the consensus that what God wills must be.

Checkpoint
What is the consequence of the Mu'tazila claim according to this proof?
A) Most of what God wills happens
B) Most of what God wills does not happen
C) Disbelief is less than faith
D) God wills both good and evil

Key Takeaway
The Mu'tazila position leads to the absurdity that most of God's will is ineffective, contradicting the consensus that God's will is always effective.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that everything that happens—including your own sins and doubts—is under God's will? If so, how does that reshape your humility before Him? If not, what part of your life are you still trying to keep out of His sovereign plan?
?
When you see evil and disbelief in the world, does your heart remain anchored in the certainty that God's will is always effective, or do you quietly adopt the Mu'tazila assumption that God is merely permissive? Examine your reaction to suffering: do you find yourself questioning God's control?
?
The consensus of the Muslims is that 'what God wills is, and what He does not will is not.' Does your daily reliance on God reflect this total surrender, or do you live as if some outcomes depend solely on you? Commit today to repeating 'ما شاء الله كان وما لم يشأ لم يكن' in moments of both success and failure.

The Argument Comparing Iblis's Will to God's Will
Imam Ash'ari argues that the Mu'tazila effectively make Iblis's will more effective than God's.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses a reductio ad absurdum argument against the Mu'tazila by showing that their position implies Iblis's will is more effective than God's.

Core Idea
If most of what occurs is disbelief, then Iblis's will is more effective than God's.

Imam Ash'ari continues his refutation of the Mu'tazila by drawing out a shocking consequence of their view on divine will. He argues that if God does not will disbelief, yet disbelief predominates, then Iblis's will is actually more effective than God's. This passage is a powerful example of theological reasoning that exposes the logical implications of a flawed premise.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Carefully read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Let the flow of the argument sink in before analyzing.
Notice the opening phrase 'حجة أخرى' indicates this is another argument in a series.
2
Identify the Mu'tazila premise
The argument derives from the Mu'tazila statement that disbelief is more than faith, and that God does not will disbelief. Recognize this as the starting point.
The Mu'tazila hold that disbelief occurs without God's will.
3
Trace the logical derivation
Follow how the Imam derives that most of what occurs is willed by Iblis, making his will more effective. Pay attention to the terms 'أنفذ' (more effective) and 'مشيئة' (will).
The reasoning is: since most events are disbelief, and Iblis wills disbelief, most events align with Iblis's will.
4
Recognize the theological consequence
The Imam points out that this gives Iblis a rank in will that belongs only to God, which is blasphemous. Understand why this is a reductio ad absurdum.
The conclusion is that the Mu'tazila position leads to exalting Iblis above God.
5
Reflect on the argument's force
Consider why this argument is effective against the Mu'tazila. Think about how it uses their own premises to reach an unacceptable conclusion.
This is a classic theological argument form: showing that a view leads to absurdity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
حجة أخرى :
ويقال لهم: يستفاد من قولكم إن كثيراً مما شاء إبليس أن يكون كان؛ لأن الكفر أكثر من الإيمان، وأكثر ما كان هـو شـاءه ؛ فقد جعلتم مشيئة إبليس أنفذ غيره ؛ لأن أكثر ما شاءه كان وأكثر ما كان فقد شاءه، وفي هذا إيجاب أنكم قد جعلتم لإبليس مرتبة في المشيئة ليست لرب العالمين، تعالى الله عز وجل عن قول الظالمين علواً كبيراً .
من مشيئة رب العالمين جل ثناؤه وتقدست أسماؤه ولا إله
Another argument:
And it is said to them: It is derived from your statement that much of what Iblis willed to be came to be; because disbelief is more than faith, and most of what occurred, he (Iblis) willed it. So you have made the will of Iblis more effective than other than him, because most of what he willed came to be and most of what occurred he willed. And in this there is an implication that you have assigned to Iblis a rank in will that is not for the Lord of the worlds. Exalted is Allah, Mighty and Majestic, above the sayings of the wrongdoers, a great exaltation.
From the will of the Lord of the worlds, majestic is His praise and sanctified are His names, and there is no god...
Source pages p. 123

Commentary
This passage is another argument (حجة) against the Mu'tazila. The Imam draws a further consequence from their position: they claim that disbelief occurs despite God not willing it, and that faith is less than disbelief. Since disbelief is the object of Iblis's will (as he desires disobedience), most of what occurs is what Iblis wills. Thus, Iblis's will is more effective (أنفذ) than God's will, because most actual events align with Iblis's desire rather than God's. The Imam points out the blasphemous implication: the Mu'tazila have given Iblis a station in will that is not for the Lord of the worlds. The passage ends with a declaration of God's transcendence above such statements. The final line 'من مشيئة رب العالمين...' may be a separate exclamation or part of the argument, emphasizing God's absolute will.

Key Terms
إبليس
مشيئة
أنفذ
الكفر
الإيمان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think this argument is about Iblis having power to create evil.
Correction: It is about the logical consequence of the Mu'tazila position on will: if most events are not willed by God, then they are willed by Iblis, making his will more effective than God's.

Checkpoint
What do the Mu'tazila effectively grant to Iblis according to this argument?
A) Power to create evil
B) A rank in will that is not for God
C) The ability to know the unseen
D) Lordship over part of creation

Key Takeaway
The Mu'tazila position on divine will logically leads to the blasphemous conclusion that Iblis's will is more effective than God's.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever found yourself complaining about something in your life that you thought was 'unfair' from God? In that moment, were you not effectively questioning God's will and elevating your own desire above His? How can this lesson move you to surrender every complaint to His absolute wisdom?
?
The Mu'tazila's error led them to exalt Iblis's will. What unconscious ways might you be exalting your own will—through worry, resentment, or insisting on your own plans? Will you today, in a specific area of your life, say: 'I will what You will, even if it hurts'?
?
This argument forces you to choose: either God wills everything that happens, including evil, or something else is more effective. You cannot escape this logic. Have you truly accepted that God's will encompasses even the sin and suffering in your life? Or do you secretly harbor a belief that some things happen outside His control? Confess that to Him now and ask for the grace to submit fully.

The Parallel Argument for God's Attributes
Imam Ashari refutes the claim that God's knowledge is created by drawing a parallel to will and speech.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses a parallel argument to refute the claim that God's knowledge is created, by appealing to consistency with will and speech.

Core Idea
Consistency among divine attributes forces the denial of their creation.

This passage presents a clever reductio ad absurdum. The opponents argue that God's knowledge cannot be created because that would imply imperfection. Imam Ashari turns their own logic against them, showing that the same reasoning applies to God's will and speech. Since they affirm the uncreatedness of speech (the Qur'an), they must also accept that knowledge and will are uncreated.

Guided Path
1
Read the opponent's objection
Read the first sentence where the opponent denies that God's knowledge can be originated. Notice the reasoning: change implies imperfection.
The key term here is 'naqṣān' (imperfection).
2
Examine the rebuttal
Read the rebuttal starting with 'qīla lahum'. The author applies the same logic to God's will.
The word 'ka-mā' (just as) signals the parallel.
3
Reflect on the conclusion
Notice the final statement: the same prohibition applies to speech. The argument forces the opponent to be consistent.
This is a classic example of argument by analogy in kalām.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإن قالوا: لا يجوز أن يكون علم الله محدثاً؛ لأن من لم يكن عالماً ثم علم لحقه النقصان .
قيل لهم ولا يجوز أن تكون إرادة الله محدثة مخلوقة؛ لأن من لم يكن مريداً ثم أراد لحقه النقصان، وكما لا يجوز أن تكون إرادته تعالى محدثة مخلوقة، كذلك لا يجوز أن يكون كلامه محدثاً مخلوقاً.
If they say: It is not permissible for God's knowledge to be originated (muḥdath), because one who was not knowledgeable then became knowledgeable is affected by imperfection (naqṣān). It is said to them: Nor is it permissible for God's will to be originated and created, because one who was not willing then willed is affected by imperfection. And just as it is not permissible for His exalted will to be originated and created, likewise it is not permissible for His speech to be originated and created.
Source pages p. 123

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer refutation. The opponents claim that God's knowledge cannot be originated (created in time) because that would imply change and imperfection. The author (Imam Ashari) turns the argument back on them: if that reasoning applies to knowledge, it applies equally to will and speech. By their own logic, they must either accept that all three attributes are uncreated or none. Since they affirm God's speech is eternal (as the Qur'an is uncreated), they must also affirm the same for knowledge and will. The argument is a reductio ad absurdum forcing consistency.

Key Terms
علم الله
محدث
إرادة الله
كلام الله
نقصان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that this argument is about God's attributes being similar to human attributes in their nature.
Correction: The argument is not about similarity of nature but about the logical implications of denying creation in one attribute while affirming it in another. It is a consistency argument.

Checkpoint
What is the main purpose of the parallel argument in this passage?
A) To prove that God's knowledge is created.
B) To force consistency in denying creation of attributes.
C) To deny that God has will.
D) To affirm that God's speech is created.

Key Takeaway
Imam Ashari uses a simple but powerful parallel argument to defend the uncreatedness of all divine attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The opponents applied a standard to knowledge that they did not apply to speech. In your own life, where do you hold inconsistent standards—judging others by a rule you would not apply to yourself? What will you do this week to align your judgments?
?
If God's attributes are perfectly consistent and uncreated, does your trust in the Qur'an reflect that eternal consistency? Or do you sometimes treat His speech as if it were a temporal product? What change in your attitude toward the Qur'an does this truth demand?
?
Consider your own knowledge: you are not born knowing everything; you learn over time. Does that imperfection remind you of your contingency and need for God? How can you turn the very fact of your acquired knowledge into an act of worship today?

4 min
Proof That Denying God's Will Contradicts Consensus
Imam Ashari refutes opponents using logical consequence and consensus.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses the prevalence of disbelief to refute the claim that God does not will all events.

Core Idea
If God did not will disbelief, then most of His will is unrealized—an absurdity against consensus.

This passage presents a powerful argument against those who assert that disbelief occurs without God's will. Imam Ashari examines the observable world where disbelief is more abundant than faith. If God willed universal faith but did not will the prevalent disbelief, then the majority of what happens contradicts His will—a position that directly opposes the unanimous agreement of Muslims that God's will is always effective.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text carefully, noting the structure: conditional premise, logical consequence, and appeal to consensus.
Pay attention to the key terms 'مشيئة' and 'إجماع'.
2
Understand the opponents' claim
The opponents claim that God does not will disbelief and disobedience, yet these occur in His dominion. They also claim that God willed everyone to believe, but not all believed.
3
Follow the reductio ad absurdum
Imam Ashari shows that if the opponents are correct, then most of what God wills (universal faith) did not happen, and most of what He does not will (disbelief) did happen—a direct contradiction to the consensus that God's will is always realized.
4
Reflect on the role of consensus
The argument concludes by stating that this view denies the unanimous agreement (ijmāʿ) of Muslims that whatever God wills occurs and whatever He does not will does not occur.
Consensus is a foundational source of Islamic theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : إذا زعمتم أنه قد كان في سلطان الله عز وجل الكفر والعصيان، وهو لا يريده وأراد أن يؤمن الخلق أجمعون، فلم يؤمنوا. فقد وجب على قولكم : إن أكثر ما شاء الله أن يكون لم يكن، وأكثر ما شاء الله أن لا يكون كان؛ لأن الكفر الذي كان وهو لا يشاؤه عندكم أكثر من الإيمان الذي كان وهو يشاؤه، وأكثر ما شاء الله أن يكون لم يكن ، وهذا جحد لما أجمع عليه المسلمون من أن ما شاء الله أن يكون ،كان، وما لا يشاء لا يكون .
And it is said to them: When you claim that disbelief and disobedience were present in the dominion of God Almighty while He does not will it, and He willed that all creatures believe, but they did not believe. So according to your statement, it becomes necessary that most of what God willed to be did not happen, and most of what God willed not to be did happen; because the disbelief that occurred while He does not will it — in your view — is more than the faith that occurred while He wills it. And most of what God willed to be did not happen. And this is a denial of what Muslims have unanimously agreed upon: that what God wills to be happens, and what He does not will does not happen.
Source pages p. 123

Commentary
This passage is a proof (dālīl) against the claim that God does not will all events, specifically disbelief. The argument proceeds by considering the state of the world: there is more disbelief than faith. If God does not will disbelief, then most of what occurs is against His will. But if He willed universal faith, His will would be mostly frustrated. This contradicts the consensus (ijmāʿ) of Muslims that whatever God wills happens, and whatever He does not will does not happen. Thus the opponents' view is refuted.

Key Terms
سلطان الله
مشيئة
إيمان
كفر
إجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: This argument denies human free will and suggests fatalism.
Correction: The argument does not discuss human free will; it only affirms God's absolute sovereignty and the effectiveness of His will over all events.

Checkpoint
What does the author claim is the consequence of the opponents' view regarding God's will?
A) God's will is always fulfilled.
B) Most of what God wills does not happen.
C) God's will is identical to human will.
D) God does not have a will.

Key Takeaway
Denying that God wills all events leads to the absurdity that most of His will is unrealized, contradicting Muslim consensus.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believe that nothing happens outside of God's will, how does that change the way you react when you see disbelief in the world? Do you submit to His decree or question His wisdom?
?
When you commit a sin, do you acknowledge that it occurred by God's will while still taking responsibility for your choice? How does this passage push you to reconcile divine sovereignty with personal accountability in your own heart?
?
The passage argues that denying God's will over all events leads to an absurdity. Have you ever harbored a thought that limited God's control to only 'good' things? What does that reveal about the depth of your trust in His perfect wisdom?

4 min
The Reductio: Iblis' Will vs. God's Will
Imam Ashari proves that opponents' view grants Iblis a higher rank in will than God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses a reductio ad absurdum to show that denying God's universal will leads to the blasphemous conclusion that Iblis' will is more effective than God's.

Core Idea
Denying God's universal will logically elevates Iblis above God.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a sharp logical argument against those who claim that God does not will disbelief. He shows that this view leads to an absurd and blasphemous consequence. Let's examine the argument closely.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Get a feel for the argument's flow.
Notice the compact style: the argument is a single sentence in Arabic.
2
Identify the opponents' premise
The opponents claim that God does not will disbelief. This is the starting point of the reductio.
This premise is implied, not explicitly stated in the passage.
3
Observe the factual claim
The passage states that disbelief is more abundant than faith. This is the factual observation.
This observation is taken as given for the sake of argument.
4
Follow the logical deduction
Since most of what exists is disbelief, and Iblis wills disbelief, it follows that most of what exists is what Iblis willed.
This makes Iblis' will 'more effective' (anfadh) than God's, on their view.
5
Recognize the conclusion and doxology
The conclusion: opponents have granted Iblis a rank in will not belonging to God. The passage ends with exalting God above such claims.
This is a reductio ad absurdum – the conclusion is absurd, so the premise must be false.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: يستفاد من قولكم إن كثيراً مما شاء إبليس أن يكون كان؛ لأن الكفر أكثر من الإيمان، وأكثر ما كان هـو شـاءه ؛ فقد جعلتم مشيئة إبليس أنفذ غيره ؛ لأن أكثر ما شاءه كان وأكثر ما كان فقد شاءه، وفي هذا إيجاب أنكم قد جعلتم لإبليس مرتبة في المشيئة ليست لرب العالمين، تعالى الله عز وجل عن قول الظالمين علواً كبيراً .
And it is said to them: From your statement it can be inferred that much of what Iblis willed to be happened, because disbelief is more than faith, and most of what exists is what he willed. So you have made the will of Iblis more effective (anfadh) than the will of others, because most of what he willed happened, and most of what happened he willed. In this there is an affirmation that you have granted Iblis a rank in will that does not belong to the Lord of the worlds. Exalted is God Almighty above what the wrongdoers say, greatly exalted.
Source pages p. 123

Commentary
This is another proof (ḥujja) that works by comparison. The opponents claim that God does not will disbelief. However, disbelief is abundant. Iblis desires disbelief. Therefore, on their view, Iblis' will is more often realized than God's will. This is absurd and blasphemous, as it elevates Iblis above God in efficacy. The argument is a reductio ad absurdum highlighting the logical consequence of denying God's universal will.

Key Terms
مشيئة إبليس
أنفذ
مرتبة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The argument proves that Iblis actually has a more effective will than God.
Correction: The argument is a reductio ad absurdum, showing the logical consequence of the opponents' premise, not an assertion of Iblis' actual power.

Checkpoint
What does the author conclude about the opponents' view regarding Iblis?
A) Iblis is equal to God in will.
B) Iblis has a higher rank in will than God.
C) Iblis has no will.
D) Iblis' will is ineffective.

Key Takeaway
Denying God's universal will leads to the absurd conclusion that Iblis' will is more effective than God's, highlighting the necessity of affirming God's absolute sovereignty.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever, even in a moment of despair, felt that evil has more sway in this world than God's plan? How does this argument confront that feeling and call you to reaffirm His absolute will?
?
If you truly believe that everything occurs by God's will, including your own failures and sins, how must that change the way you approach repentance and trust in His mercy?
?
The opponents' premise led to elevating Iblis. Are there any beliefs you hold that, upon reflection, attribute more power to creation than to the Creator? What would it cost you to surrender that belief completely to God?
