The Proof of True Power
Imam Ashari argues that the one whose will is always realized is more worthy of power.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses a logical dichotomy to prove that Allah alone truly possesses the attribute of power.

Core Idea
True power means your will always happens—and only Allah satisfies that.

Imam Ashari presents a compelling argument that forces his opponents to admit that the one whose will is always fulfilled is the true possessor of power. By asking a simple question, he exposes the absurdity of denying Allah's universal will. This passage is a masterclass in theological reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Imam Ashari uses a rhetorical question that sets up a logical trap.
2
Identify the dichotomy
In the first sentence, two options are presented: the one whose will always succeeds versus the one whose will often fails. Understand why these are the only possibilities.
This is the foundation of the argument.
3
Follow the opponent's possible replies
Imam Ashari anticipates two replies: an obstinate one and a seemingly reasonable one. See how he refutes each with a reductio ad absurdum.
Pay attention to the analogy about knowledge.
4
Understand the Iblis consequence
If the opponent accepts the initial correct premise, they must also accept that Iblis would be more powerful than Allah according to their own worldview. This forces consistency.
This is a brilliant use of internal logic.
5
Conclude about Allah's power
The argument concludes that Allah's will is always realized, making Him the only true possessor of the attribute of power.
This refutes those who deny Allah's control over all events.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : أيما أولى بصفة الاقتدار من إذا شاء أن يكون الشيء كان لا محالة، وإذا لم يرده لم يكن، أو من يريده أن يكون فلا يكون، ويكون مالا يريد؟ فإن قالوا من لا يكون أكثر ما يريده أولى بصفة الاقتدار كابروا. وقيل لهم : إن جاز لكم ما قلتموه جاز لقائل أن يقول: من يكون مالا يعلمه أولى بالعلم ممن لا يكون إلا ما يعلمه .
وإن رجعوا عن هذه المكابرة، وزعموا أن من إذا أراد أمراً كان، وإذا لم يرده لا يكون أولى بصفة الاقتدار لزمهم على مذاهبهم أن يكون إبليس لعنه الله ـ أولى بالاقتدار من الله عز وجل؛ لأن أكثر ما أراده كان، وأكثر ما كان قد أراده .
--
وقيل لهم : إذا كان من إذا أراد أمراً كان وإذا لم يرده لم يكن أولى بصفة الاقتدار، فيلزمكم أن يكون الله عز وجل إذا أراد أمراً كان، وإذا لم يرده لم يكن ؛ لأنه أولى بصفة الاقتدار
And it is said to them: Which of the two is more worthy of the attribute of power (al-iqtidār): one who, when he wills something to be, it is without fail, and when he does not will it, it is not; or one who wills it to be but it is not, and that which he does not will occurs? If they say: One for whom most of what he wills does not happen is more worthy of the attribute of power, they would be obstinate (kābirū). And it is said to them: If what you say is permissible for you, then it is permissible for someone to say: One who has knowledge of what he does not know is more worthy of knowledge than one who only knows what he knows. And if they retract from this obstinacy and claim that the one who, when he wills something, it is, and when he does not will it, it is not, is more worthy of the attribute of power, then according to their doctrines it would be necessary that Iblis — may God curse him — is more worthy of power than God Almighty, because most of what he willed happened, and most of what happened he willed. And it is said to them: If the one who, when he wills something, it is, and when he does not will it, it is not, is more worthy of the attribute of power, then it follows that God Almighty, when He wills something, it is, and when He does not will it, it is not; because He is more worthy of the attribute of power.
Source pages p. 123

Commentary
This is a complex proof (ḥujja) involving a question about who is more worthy of the attribute of power (iqtidār). The author poses a dichotomy: the true possessor of power is the one whose will is always realized, not the one whose will is often frustrated. The opponents might stubbornly claim the opposite, but that would lead to absurdities (e.g., someone who knows lies is more knowledgeable). If they accept the obvious, then Iblis would be more powerful than God according to their own premises, because on their view Iblis' will is more often realized. The only consistent conclusion is that God's will is always effective. The argument forces the opponents to admit that God's will encompasses all events.

Key Terms
صفة الاقتدار
مشيئة
إبليس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think this argument gives Iblis actual power over Allah.
Correction: The argument uses Iblis as a hypothetical reductio to show inconsistency; it does not claim Iblis has power.

Checkpoint
According to the proof, who is truly more worthy of the attribute of power?
A) The one whose will often fails.
B) The one whose will always succeeds.
C) Iblis.
D) Humans.

Key Takeaway
Only Allah's will is always realized, proving He alone possesses true power.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When your carefully made plans fail, do you feel a pang of resentment against Allah's decree? How can you train your heart to say 'Allah's will be done' with genuine submission, knowing His will is always realized?
?
Think of a time you credited your own ability for a success. If you truly believed that only Allah's will is always effective, how would your attribution change? What would you do differently tomorrow to acknowledge His power in your daily accomplishments?
?
If you lived every moment with the conviction that nothing happens except by Allah's will—that your will has no independent power—what fears would dissolve? What specific worry can you entrust to Him right now?

The Parallel Between God's Knowledge and Will
Imam Ashari parallels knowledge and will to argue that God's will must encompass all things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses an argument from consistency to prove that God's will encompasses all things, parallel to His knowledge.

Core Idea
God's will must encompass all, just as His knowledge does.

Imam Ash'ari now turns to the attribute of will. Having established that God's knowledge is all-encompassing, he asks his opponents: if that makes God worthy of divinity, then the same reasoning must apply to His will. This short but powerful argument forces the opponent to accept God's universal will or abandon their own position.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Let the argument's flow come through.
Notice how the author asks a comparative question first.
2
Identify the parallel
The author draws a parallel between knowledge and will. He asks: if all-encompassing knowledge makes one worthy of divinity, then all-encompassing will should do the same.
3
Understand the logical implication
If the opponent accepts the premise for knowledge, they must accept it for will. To reject it would be inconsistent.
This is a classic consistency argument.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
The conclusion is that God wills everything that occurs. Nothing happens outside His will.
This affirms God's absolute sovereignty.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : أيما أولى بالإلهية والسلطان من لا يكون إلا ما يعلمه ولا يغيب عن علمه شيء، ولا يجوز ذلك عليه؟ أو من يكون مالا يعلمه، ويعزب عن علمه أكثر الأشياء؟
فإن قالوا: من لا يكون إلا ما يعلمه ولا يعزب عن علمه شيء أولى بصفة الإلهية .
قيل لهم : فكذلك من لا يريد كون شيء إلا ما كان ولا يكون إلا ما يريده، ولا يعزب عن إرادته شيء، أولى بصفة الإلهية، كما قلتم ذلك في العلم، وإن قالوا ذلك تركوا قولهم، ورجعوا عنه، وأثبتوا الله عز وجل مريداً لكل كائن، وأوجبوا أنه لا يكون إلا ما يريد أن يكون .
And it is said to them: Which of the two is more worthy of divinity (al-ilāhiyya) and sovereignty (al-sulṭān): one who only brings into existence what he knows, and nothing escapes his knowledge, and that is not possible concerning him; or one who brings into existence what he does not know, and most things are absent from his knowledge? If they say: The one who only brings into existence what he knows and nothing is absent from his knowledge is more worthy of the attribute of divinity. It is said to them: Likewise, the one who does not will anything to be except what is, and only brings into existence what he wills, and nothing escapes his will, is more worthy of the attribute of divinity, just as you said that concerning knowledge. And if they say that, they would abandon their statement, retract from it, and affirm that God Almighty wills everything that occurs, and they would affirm that nothing occurs except what He wills to be.
Source pages p. 124

Commentary
This final passage (mas'ala) is a comparative argument between knowledge and will. The opponents already accept that God's knowledge encompasses all things — He knows all that occurs and nothing escapes His knowledge. The author asks them: is the one whose knowledge is all-encompassing more worthy of divinity? They must say yes. Then he draws the parallel: similarly, God's will must also encompass all things. If they admit that, they would have to abandon their claim that God does not will evil and affirm that God wills everything that exists. The argument is a powerful appeal to consistency: the same reasoning they accept for knowledge applies to will.

Key Terms
الإلهية
السلطان
العلم
الإرادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think this argument claims God's will is identical to His knowledge.
Correction: The argument does not assert identity; it uses an analogy. Knowledge and will are distinct attributes, but the same principle of comprehensiveness applies to both.

Checkpoint
What parallel does the author draw in this passage?
A) God's knowledge and human knowledge.
B) God's will and human will.
C) God's knowledge and God's will.
D) God's power and God's knowledge.

Key Takeaway
God's will, like His knowledge, must encompass all things—nothing occurs outside His will.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
If God's will encompasses all, including your failures and sins, how should that change your view of repentance and reliance on Him?
?
In a moment of deep confusion about your destiny, can you surrender your 'why' to the certainty that nothing occurs except what God wills? What would that surrender cost you today?

The Flawed Definition of Power: a Reductio Argument
Refutes two definitions of divine power using reductio arguments.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why defining divine power by the proportion of willed events that occur leads to absurd consequences, and how this refutation upholds the perfect efficacy of God's will.

Core Idea
Defining power by success rate makes Satan seem more powerful than God.

This passage from Imam al-Ash'ari's al-Ibana refutes a misguided notion about divine power. Some claimed that power is measured by how often one's will is fulfilled. The author shows that this definition leads to two absurdities: it would make ignorance a sign of knowledge, and it would elevate Satan over God. Let's examine the argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Identify the opponent's claim
Read the passage and find the initial claim: the one who brings about most of what He wills is more deserving of power. Notice how the author immediately calls this 'stubbornness'.
This sets up the first definition to be refuted.
2
Follow the knowledge analogy
The author draws a parallel: if power is about causing many willed events, then knowledge could be defined by causing many unknown events. This is clearly absurd, showing the definition is flawed.
This is the first reductio.
3
Examine the Iblis argument
If the opponent retreats to a definition requiring perfect correspondence between will and occurrence, then Iblis would be considered more powerful because most of what he willed happened. This absurdity proves the definition wrong.
This is the second and stronger reductio.
4
Understand the correct conclusion
The passage ends by affirming that God alone has perfect power: whatever He wills happens, and nothing happens against His will.
This is the orthodox position the author defends.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لا محالة، وإذا لم يرده لم يكن، أو من يريده أن يكون فلا يكون، ويكون ما لا يريد؟ فإن قالوا من لا يكون أكثر ما يريده أولى بصفة الاقتدار كابروا. وقيل لهم : إن جاز لكم ما قلتموه جاز لقائل أن يقول: من يكون ما لا يعلمه أولى بالعلم ممن لا يكون إلا ما يعلمه . وإن رجعوا عن هذه المكابرة، وزعموا أن من إذا أراد أمراً كان، وإذا لم يرده لا يكون أولى بصفة الاقتدار لزمهم على مذاهبهم أن يكون إبليس لعنه الله ـ أولى بالاقتدار من الله عز وجل؛ لأن أكثر ما أراده كان، وأكثر ما كان قد أراده . -- وقيل لهم : إذا كان من إذا أراد أمراً كان وإذا لم يرده لم يكن أولى بصفة الاقتدار، فيلزمكم أن يكون الله عز وجل إذا أراد أمراً كان، وإذا لم يرده لم يكن ؛ لأنه أولى بصفة الاقتدار
Undoubtedly, and if He does not will it, it is not, or [the one] who wills something to be and it is not, and what He does not will comes to be? If they say: 'The one who brings about most of what He wills is more deserving of the attribute of power,' they are being stubborn. And it is said to them: If what you say is permissible, then it is permissible for someone to say: 'The one who brings about what He does not know is more deserving of knowledge than the one who brings about only what He knows.' And if they retreat from this stubbornness and claim that the one who, when He wills a thing it is, and when He does not will it it is not, is more deserving of the attribute of power, then it follows from their doctrines that Iblis (may God curse him) is more deserving of power than God Almighty, because most of what he willed came to be, and most of what came to be he had willed. -- And it is said to them: If the one who, when He wills a thing it is, and when He does not will it it is not, is more deserving of the attribute of power, then it follows for you that God Almighty, when He wills a thing it is, and when He does not will it it is not, because He is more deserving of the attribute of power.
Source pages p. 124

Commentary
This passage is a refutation against those who define divine power (iqtidar) based on the proportion of what one wills that actually comes to be. The argument proceeds in stages. First, the author presents their claim: the one who causes to happen most of what He wills is more deserving of the attribute of power. The author immediately calls this stubbornness. Then he offers an analogy with knowledge: if their criterion were valid, then one who creates things he does not know would be more knowledgeable than one who creates only what he knows — which is absurd. Then the author considers if they retreat from this and adopt a different definition: that the one who, when He wills something it happens, and when He does not will it does not happen, is more powerful. The author shows that this definition would actually make Iblis (Satan) more powerful than God, because most of what Iblis willed occurred (according to their own view of events). Finally, he concludes by affirming that God is the one who truly possesses this attribute: whatever He wills happens, and whatever He does not will does not happen, because He is the most deserving of power. Thus, the passage establishes that God's will is perfectly effective and that no event occurs outside His will.

Key Terms
الإقتدار
الإرادة
العلم
إبليس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Divine power is measured by the proportion of willed events that occur.
Correction: True power requires that all willed events occur and no unwilled events occur; this is perfectly true for God alone.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why does the definition of power as 'most of what one wills happens' lead to absurdity?
A) Because it would make Iblis more powerful than God.
B) Because it conflicts with the Quran.
C) Because it implies God is ignorant.
D) Because it is a matter of faith.

Key Takeaway
The passage demonstrates that God's power is perfect: whatever He wills must occur, and nothing occurs without His will.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's power means that whatever He wills happens and nothing happens without His will, then what does that mean for the things that happen to you that you consider bad or unfair? Are you able to trust that even those events are within His perfect will, or do you struggle with accepting that they were willed by Him?
?
In this lesson, the author shows that any definition of power that makes it relative leads to absurdities. How often do you compare God's power to your own or to others, rather than accepting that His power is absolute and beyond comparison? What would change in your daily life if you truly believed that God's power is unmatched and that His will is always done?
?
The passage uses the example of Iblis to show that even evil beings have their wills realized under God's overarching power. Reflect on a time when you felt that evil was winning. How does this lesson's affirmation that nothing occurs without God's will challenge your understanding of that event? How does it call you to respond to evil in the world?

Comparison of Knowledge and Will in Deserving Divinity
Compares knowledge and will to prove that God's will encompasses all things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the same reasoning that establishes God's all-encompassing knowledge also establishes His all-encompassing will.

Core Idea
If God knows all, He must also will all.

In this passage, Imam al-Ash'ari uses a simple analogy to argue for the universality of God's will. He first asks which kind of being is more deserving of divinity: one who creates only what He knows, or one who creates what He does not know? The answer is obvious, and then he applies the same logic to will. This teaches us that if we accept that God's knowledge is comprehensive, we must also accept that His will is comprehensive.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the literal translation. Let the author's argument unfold.
2
Identify the two options presented
Notice the contrast: a being who only creates what He knows versus one who creates what He does not know. Which is more deserving of divinity?
The answer is the former.
3
Understand the analogy
The same logic is applied to will: if the one with comprehensive knowledge is more deserving, then the one with comprehensive will is also more deserving.
This shows consistency in reasoning.
4
Reflect on the implication
If God's will is universal, then everything that happens is by His will. No event occurs outside His will.
This includes both good and evil events.
5
Consider the misconception
Some might think that evil cannot be willed by God, but this passage shows that universal will follows from universal knowledge.
The passage does not discuss evil explicitly, but the logic applies.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۱۸۸ - مسألة : ويقال لهم : أيما أولى بالإلهية والسلطان من لا يكون إلا ما يعلمه ولا يغيب عن علمه شيء، ولا يجوز ذلك عليه؟ أو من يكون ما لا يعلمه، ويعزب عن علمه أكثر الأشياء؟ فإن قالوا: من لا يكون إلا ما يعلمه ولا يعزب عن علمه شيء أولى بصفة الإلهية . قيل لهم : فكذلك من لا يريد كون شيء إلا ما كان ولا يكون إلا ما يريده، ولا يعزب عن إرادته شيء، أولى بصفة الإلهية، كما قلتم ذلك في العلم، وإن قالوا ذلك تركوا قولهم، ورجعوا عنه، وأثبتوا الله عز وجل مريداً لكل كائن، وأوجبوا أنه لا يكون إلا ما يريد أن يكون .
188 - Issue: And it is said to them: Which of the two is more deserving of divinity and sovereignty: the one who brings into existence only what He knows, and nothing is absent from His knowledge, and that is not possible for Him? Or the one who brings into existence what He does not know, and most things are absent from His knowledge? If they say: The one who brings into existence only what He knows, and nothing is absent from His knowledge, is more deserving of the attribute of divinity. Then it is said to them: Likewise, the one who does not will the existence of anything except what comes to be, and nothing is absent from His will, is more deserving of the attribute of divinity, just as you said that in regard to knowledge. And if they say that, they abandon their statement and retract it, and they affirm that God Almighty is the willer of every existing thing, and they necessitate that nothing comes to be except what He wills to be.
Source pages p. 124

Commentary
This passage is a question (mas'ala) that uses an analogy to argue for the comprehensive nature of God's will. The author poses a comparative question: which is more deserving of divinity and sovereignty — a being who only creates what He knows (with nothing absent from His knowledge) or a being who creates things He does not know? The expected answer is the former. The author then applies the same logic to will: just as the one who only creates what He knows is superior, so the one who only wills what comes to be (with nothing absent from His will) is superior. If the opponents concede this, they must abandon their previous claim that God does not will everything that happens, and instead affirm that God wills every existing thing and that nothing occurs except by His will. The passage thus demonstrates that the same principle accepted for knowledge must also be applied to will.

Key Terms
الإلهية
السلطان
العلم
الإرادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's will is not universal because evil occurs.
Correction: This passage shows that if God's knowledge is universal (including evil), then His will must also be universal. The logic of deserving divinity demands that the being who knows all also wills all.

Checkpoint

B) To argue that just as God's knowledge encompasses all, so His will encompasses all.
C) To prove that God is the only creator.
D) To refute the claim that God knows evil.

Key Takeaway
God's will, like His knowledge, encompasses everything that occurs.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

Nothing Occurs Against God's Will
Imam al-Ash'ari refutes the claim that something occurs in God's dominion against His will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the logical argument that denying God's overarching will for all events leads to attributing weakness and poverty to God.

Core Idea
Denying God's will over any event implies weakness in His sovereignty.

In this passage, Imam al-Ash'ari presents a concise reductio ad absurdum against those who claim that something can happen in God's dominion without His will. The argument forces the opponent to admit that such a view ultimately describes God with attributes of weakness and poverty, which is impossible for the Almighty. We will follow the logical steps and see why this is a powerful refutation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once in Arabic, then read the English translation. Try to follow the flow of the argument without overthinking.
The passage uses a series of conditional statements leading to an absurd conclusion.
2
Identify the first conditional
The argument starts with: 'If you say that there exists in His dominion that which He does not will, then there existed in His dominion that which He dislikes.' Notice how the claim is extended step by step.
The opponent must agree to this consequence.
3
Follow the reductio
Next, the argument pushes further: if God dislikes something, why not also accept that He refuses its existence? This leads to the conclusion that sins occur whether God wills or refuses, implying His will is ineffective.
This is the absurdity: God's will would be irrelevant.
4
Understand the final implication
The final step: an ineffective will is a sign of weakness and poverty. Since God is absolutely perfect and powerful, such a view is impossible. The passage ends by exalting God above that.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : إذا قلتم إنه يكون في سلطانه تعالى ما لا يريد، فقد كان إذاً في سلطانه ما كرهه، فلا بد من نعم . فيقال لهم : فإذا كان في سلطانه ما يكرهه، فما أنكرتم أن يكون في سلطانه ما يأبى كونه، فإن أجابوا إلى ذلك قيل لهم : فقد كانت المعاصي شاء الله أم أبى، وهذه صفة الضعف والفقر، تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً .
And it is said to them: If you say that there exists in His dominion, exalted is He, that which He does not will, then there existed in His dominion that which He dislikes. So they must answer yes. Then it is said to them: If there exists in His dominion that which He dislikes, then what prevents you from admitting that there exists in His dominion that which He refuses to be? If they concede to that, it is said to them: Then sins exist whether God wills or refuses, and this is an attribute of weakness and poverty. Exalted is God above that, a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 125

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of those who claim that something happens in God's dominion against His will. The argument proceeds as follows: (1) The opponent is asked: If you say that something occurs in God's dominion that He does not will, then it follows that something occurs that He dislikes. They must answer yes. (2) Then it is said: If something occurs that He dislikes, why not also accept that something occurs that He absolutely refuses to be? If they concede, then it means sins occur whether God wills or refuses. (3) This would attribute to God the attribute of weakness and poverty (since His will is not effective). The author then declares God exalted above that. The key point is that denying God's overarching will leads to absurd consequences about God's sovereignty and perfection.

Key Terms
سلطان
إرادة
كراهة
ضعف
فقر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God dislikes sins but they happen without His will, preserving human free will.
Correction: This passage shows that such a view leads to attributing weakness to God, because if something happens against His will, His will is not effective, implying imperfection.

Checkpoint
According to Imam al-Ash'ari, what does the claim that something occurs in God's dominion against His will ultimately imply?
A) That God's will is all-encompassing
B) That God has attributes of weakness and poverty
C) That humans have absolute free will
D) That God is just

Key Takeaway
Denying that all events occur by God's will leads to the absurd conclusion that God is weak, which is impossible for the Almighty.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When something happens in your life that you deeply dislike, do you truly believe it occurs by God's will? If not, what does that imply about your view of His sovereignty? Examine your heart: are you willing to accept that even that disliked event is from Him?
?
Consider a sin you committed. Do you believe God willed that sin to occur? If you deny His will over it, how does that align with this passage's warning about attributing weakness to God? What change must you make in your understanding of God's will?
?
If you were to live as if every detail, including your failures and trials, occurs by God's will, how would your reaction to them change? What is holding you back from that conviction? Commit to one specific response you will adopt when faced with an unwanted event.

God's Anger at Sin Does Not Imply Compulsion
Imam al-Ash'ari argues that if sins made God angry, they must be against His will, but that would imply coercion.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God's anger and displeasure at human actions do not imply that those actions occur against His will, and how that would mistakenly attribute weakness to God.

Core Idea
If sin were against God's will, the servant would compel Him — impossible.

This lesson tackles a subtle objection: if God becomes angry when we sin, doesn't that mean He does not will the sin? Imam al-Ash'ari shows that such reasoning leads to an absurd conclusion — that the servant forces God's hand. Understanding this helps us maintain both God's sovereignty and His moral perfection.

Guided Path
1
Read the opponent's question
Notice how the passage begins by forcing the opponent to admit that some actions of servants anger God. Read the first sentence carefully.
The opponent is compelled to answer 'yes' to the first question.
2
Follow the logical consequence
The second sentence draws the implication: if those actions are not willed by God, then the servant has overpowered God. Identify why this is an impossible attribute for God.
The term for 'compel' (أكرهوه) shares a root with 'coercion' (قهر).
3
Reflect on the conclusion
Imam al-Ash'ari ends by exalting God above such weakness. Consider why the only consistent position is that all actions, even sins, occur by God's will.
This vindicates the Ash'ari position on divine decree (qadar).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : أليس مما فعل العباد ما يُسخطه تعالى وما يغضب عليهم إذا فعلوه، فقد أغضبوه وأسخطوه؟ فلا بد من نعم . يقال لهم : فلو فعل العباد ما لا يريد وما يكرهه لكانوا أكرهوه، وهذه صفة القهر، تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً .
And it is said to them: Is there not among the actions of the servants that which displeases the Exalted and angers them when they do it, so they have angered Him and displeased Him? So they must answer yes. It is said to them: If the servants did that which He does not will and which He dislikes, then they would have compelled Him, and this is an attribute of being overcome. Exalted is God above that, a great exaltation.
Source pages p. 125

Commentary
This passage is another refutation. The opponent is asked: Do not the servants sometimes do things that anger and displease God? They must answer yes. Then the author argues: If the servants did that which God does not will and dislikes, then they would have compelled God (i.e., forced His will). That would mean God is subject to coercion (القهر), which is an attribute of creaturely weakness. God is exalted above that. Thus, the only consistent position is that everything occurs by God's will, even sins.

Key Terms
سخط
غضب
كره
قهر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God's anger at sin means He does not will the sin to occur — i.e., it happens against His will.
Correction: God's anger is a moral evaluation, not a lack of power. If sin happened against His will, the servant would have overpowered God, which is impossible.

Checkpoint
What does the argument claim would be the consequence if servants did what God does not will and dislikes?
A) They would be punished justly
B) They would have compelled God (أكرهوه)
C) They would have obeyed God
D) They would be forgiven

Key Takeaway
God's anger at sin does not mean sin occurs against His will; otherwise, the servant would have compelled God, which is impossible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

Proof from the Quran: God Does as He Wills
A reductio proof using Quranic verse and logical necessity

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses the Quranic verse 'Doer of what He wills' to prove that everything in God's dominion occurs by His will, and why any alternative implies divine imperfection.

Core Idea
If anything happened against God's will, God would be forgetful or weak—impossible. So everything is by His will.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a powerful argument combining scripture and reason. He begins by citing the Quranic verse 'Doer of what He wills' (Hud 107, al-Buruj 16). Then he shows that denying this verse leads to absurd conclusions about God's attributes. This lesson unpacks his reasoning and its implications for understanding divine will and human actions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the translation to grasp the overall flow.
Notice the rhetorical structure: a series of questions and answers.
2
Identify the key Quranic proof
Find the verse 'فَعَّالٌ لِمَا يُرِيدُ' and note how it is used as an indisputable starting point.
The opponent must affirm this verse.
3
Trace the reductio ad absurdum
Follow the argument: if God did something He does not will, it would imply He was heedless or weak. Both are impossible for God.
The same logic is then applied to the actions of servants.
4
Apply to all beings in God's dominion
Understand that the argument extends to everything that occurs in God's kingdom, proving universal divine will.
This counters the idea that some events escape God's will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : أليس قد قال الله تعالى: (فَعَّالٌ لِمَا يُرِيدُ) [هود : ١٠٧ والبروج : ١٦]؟ فلا بد من نعم . فيقال لهم : فمن زعم أن الله تعالى فعل ما لا يريد، وأراد أن يكون من فعله ما لا يكون لزمه أن يكون قد وقع ذلك وهو ساه غافل عنه، أو أن الضعف والتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده لحقه، فلا بد من نعم . فيقال لهم : فكذلك من زعم أنه يكون في سلطان الله عز وجل مالا يريده من عبيده لزمه أحد أمرين : إما أن يزعم أن ذلك كان عن سهو وغفلة، أو أن يزعم أن الضعف والتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده لحقه
And it is said to them: Has not God the Exalted said: 'Doer of what He wills' (Hud 107 and Al-Buruj 16)? So they must answer yes. Then it is said to them: Whoever claims that God the Exalted did what He does not will, and willed that something occur from His action that does not occur, it follows that that occurred while He was heedless and forgetful of it, or that weakness and deficiency in reaching what He willed befell Him. So they must answer yes. Then it is said to them: Likewise, whoever claims that there exists in the dominion of God the Mighty and Majestic that which He does not will from His servants, one of two things follows upon him: either he claims that that occurred due to heedlessness and forgetfulness, or he claims that weakness and deficiency in reaching what He wills befell Him.
Source pages p. 125

Commentary
This passage provides a scriptural proof and a rational argument. First, the opponent is asked about the verse 'Doer of what He wills.' They must affirm it. Then the author argues: If someone claims that God does what He does not will, or wills something that does not happen, then that would imply that when something happens against His will, He is either forgetful or weak. Both are impossible for God. The same applies to actions of servants: if something occurs in God's dominion that He does not will, the only explanations are God's forgetfulness or weakness, which are impossible. Therefore, everything that happens must be by God's will.

Key Terms
فعال لما يريد
إرادة
سهو
غفلة
ضعف
تقصير

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Quranic verse 'Doer of what He wills' only applies to God's own direct actions, not to the actions of His creatures.
Correction: The author extends the verse's implication to everything in God's dominion, showing that the same logic applies to all events.

Checkpoint

A) God was ignorant or weak
B) God was heedless and forgetful, or weak and deficient
C) God was absent or sleeping
D) God was unjust or cruel

Key Takeaway
Everything that occurs in God's dominion is by His will; otherwise, God would be imperfect.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Recall a recent difficulty or disappointment in your life. Did you truly believe that it was by God's will, or did you feel resentment? How can the verse 'فَعَّالٌ لِمَا يُرِيدُ' transform your response to hardship? Commit to reciting this verse the next time you face something you dislike.
?
The proof shows that denying God's will in any event implies attributing forgetfulness or weakness to Him. Is there an area of your life where you act as if God is not in control? Repent and affirm that everything—good and bad—is from His will. Write down one specific situation where you need to surrender to His decree.
?
If you truly believe that God does as He wills, how should this affect your fear of the future? Are you trusting in His will or relying on your own plans? Ask yourself: what would it mean to live each day with the confidence that everything is under His perfect will?

The Analogy Between God's Knowledge and Will
Imam al-Ash'ari argues by analogy that denial of divine will implies divine deficiency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the argument that denying God's will for any action is as impossible as denying His knowledge, because both would attribute defects to God.

Core Idea
God's will must encompass all actions, just as His knowledge does.

In this passage, Imam al-Ash'ari employs a powerful analogy. He shows that the Mu'tazilite denial of God's will for all actions leads to the same absurdity as denying His knowledge—both imply imperfection in God. Through a series of rhetorical questions, he forces the opponent to concede that attributing heedlessness or weakness to God is impossible.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Note the rhetorical style of alternating questions and required answers.
The argument proceeds in parallel steps.
2
Identify the two analogies
First, the analogy between claiming God does something without knowledge (ignorance) and claiming He does something without will (heedlessness). Second, the extension to actions of creatures: if servants act without God's knowledge, that implies ignorance; similarly if they act without His will, it implies heedlessness.
3
Understand the conclusion
The author concludes that there is no difference between God's own actions and those of others—both require His will to avoid attributing deficiency to God.
This reinforces the Ash'ari position on divine will encompassing all things.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : أليس من زعم أن الله عز وجل فعل مالا يعلمـه قـد نـسـب الله سبحانه إلى ما لا يليق به من الجهل؟ فلا بد من نعم. فيقال لهم : فكذلك يلزم من زعم أن الله فعل ما لا يريده لزمه أن ينسب الله سبحانه إلى السهو والتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده، فإذا قالوا: نعم قيل لهم : وكذلك يلزم من زعم أن العباد يفعلون مالا يعلم الله ، نسب الله تعالى إلى الجهل، فلا بد من نعم . فيقال لهم : فكذلك إذا كان في كون فعل فعله الله وهو لا يريده إيجاب أو ضعف وتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده، فكذلك إذا كان من غيره مالا يريده وجب إثبات سهو وغفلة أو ضعف وتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريد، لا فرق في ذلك ما كان منه وما كان من غيره .
And it is said to them: Is it not the case that whoever claims that God the Mighty and Majestic did something that He did not know has attributed to God, glorified is He, that which is unfitting for Him of ignorance? So they must answer yes. Then it is said to them: Likewise, it follows from whoever claims that God did something that He does not will that he attributes to God, glorified is He, heedlessness and deficiency in reaching what He wills. So if they say yes, it is said to them: And likewise, it follows from whoever claims that the servants do something that God does not know has attributed to God, glorified is He, ignorance. So they must answer yes. Then it is said to them: So likewise, if in the occurrence of an action that God Himself does and He does not will it, it necessitates heedlessness or weakness and deficiency in reaching what He wills, then likewise if from other than Him there occurs something He does not will, it becomes necessary to affirm heedlessness and forgetfulness or weakness and deficiency in reaching what He wills. There is no difference in that between what is from Him and what is from other than Him.
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage uses an analogy to strengthen the argument. The opponent is asked: If someone claims God does something He does not know, that attributes ignorance to God. They agree it is invalid. Similarly, claiming God does something He does not will attributes heedlessness and deficiency to God. Then the author extends the argument: If someone claims that the servants do something God does not know, that also attributes ignorance to God, which is impossible. So by analogy, if an action from the servants occurs that God does not will, it would also imply heedlessness or weakness in God. The point is that just as God's knowledge encompasses all actions, so does His will. There is no difference between God's own actions and those of creatures in this respect: if any action occurs without God's will, it entails a defect in God.

Key Terms
علم
جهل
إرادة
سهو
غفلة
تقصير

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's will does not extend to sinful actions of creatures.
Correction: The author demonstrates that just as God's knowledge must encompass all actions (including sinful ones) to avoid ignorance, so His will must encompass all actions to avoid heedlessness. There is no difference between God's own actions and those of creatures in this respect.

Checkpoint
In this passage, what is the parallel drawn between denying God's knowledge and denying God's will?
A) Both lead to attributing ignorance to God
B) Both lead to attributing ignorance or heedlessness to God
C) Both are permissible within reason
D) Both are required for human free will

Key Takeaway
The argument shows that denying God's will for any action is as impossible as denying His knowledge, as both would imply imperfection in God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are there areas of your life—your mistakes, your failures, even your sins—where you live as if God's will does not apply, as if they are outside His control? How does this lesson challenge you to see those areas differently?

If you truly believed that every single event, good or evil, occurs only by God's will, how would that change the way you respond to hardship? Would you still despair or rebel, knowing it is from Him?
?
The author argues that denying God's will for any action implies deficiency in God. How does this truth confront your own tendency to question God's wisdom when things don't go your way? What would it look like to trust His will completely?

Reductio on God's Dominion
Imam al-Ash'ari uses a reductio argument: if something occurs against God's will, then something could occur without His knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses a reductio ad absurdum to defend the perfection of God's will and knowledge against those who claim something occurs against His will.

Core Idea
If something can be known but not willed, then something could be unknown without deficiency — a self-defeating contradiction.

This passage presents a concise logical argument. Imam al-Ash'ari challenges those who claim something can occur in God's dominion against His will. He shows that the same logic would force them to admit something unknown to God, which they deny, thus refuting their position.

Guided Path
1
Read the argument
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation carefully. Notice the conditional structure: 'if... then...'.
The argument is a single, tightly packed sentence.
2
Identify the opponent's claim
The opponent claims that something in God's dominion can occur against His will, while God knows it and is not weak.
This is the starting point of the reductio.
3
Follow the reductio logic
The author argues: if that is allowed, then by parallel logic you must also allow something in God's dominion that He does not know, without deficiency. The opponent would reject that.
The parallel is based on the attributes of will and knowledge.
4
Understand the conclusion
Since the opponent rejects the second case, the first case is also invalid. The original claim is self-defeating.
The argument is a classic reductio ad absurdum.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : إذا كان في سلطان الله مالا يريده وهو يعلمه، ولا يلحقه الضعف والتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده فما أنكرتم أن يكون في سلطانه مالا يعلمه ولا يلحقه النقصان فإن لم يجز هذا لم يجز ما قلتموه .
And it is said to them: If there is in the dominion of God that which He does not will while He knows it, and weakness and deficiency in reaching what He wills do not befall Him, then what prevents you from [admitting] that there is in His dominion that which He does not know and deficiency does not befall Him? For if this is not permissible, then what you have said is not permissible.
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage is a succinct reductio ad absurdum. The opponent concedes that something might exist in God's dominion that He does not will, but God knows it and is not weak. The author argues: If that is possible, then why not also admit that something exists in His dominion that He does not know, and no deficiency befalls Him? The opponent would reject that because God's knowledge must encompass everything. But if they reject it, then their original claim (that something occurs against His will) is also invalid because the logic is parallel. Thus, the opponent's position is self-defeating.

Key Terms
سلطان
إرادة
علم
نقصان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Something can be known but not willed by God, so it's acceptable for something to occur against His will.
Correction: If you allow that, then by analogy you must allow something unknown to God, which is absurd. Therefore, the original claim is also absurd.

Checkpoint
What is the logical conclusion of this reductio argument?
A) If something can be known but not willed, then something can be unknown but not willed
B) If something can be known but not willed, then something can be unknown without deficiency
C) If the opponent admits something unknown can exist, then they are consistent
D) The opponent's position is impossible because it leads to allowing unknown events

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Ash'ari's reductio proves that claiming something occurs against God's will is logically incoherent, as it would force the absurd conclusion that something could be unknown to God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a situation that seems unjust or painful, do you secretly believe that God either doesn't know about it or didn't will it? How does this reductio challenge that hidden thought? Will you now submit to His will even when it's hard?
?
Is there an area of your life where you act as if your choices are outside God's will — as if He hasn't decreed them? How does the logical impossibility of something occurring against His will confront your independence? What will you surrender to Him today?
?
If you truly internalize that nothing happens without God's will, how does that change your response to the next disappointment or loss? Are you willing to accept it as from Him, or will you still let resentment linger? What step will you take to cultivate trust in His decree?

God's Will Proven Through Creation
Imam al-Ash'ari provides a proof that God wills all things by referencing His creation of all things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the foundational proof that God wills all that exists, based on the fact that He creates all things, including disbelief and sins.

Core Idea
God's creation of everything proves His will for everything.

Imam al-Ash'ari begins by addressing a critical question: why do we say God wills all things? The answer is rooted in the clear evidence that God creates everything, even those things we might find difficult to attribute to Him, such as disbelief and sins. This passage sets the stage for a deeper discussion on divine will and creation.

Guided Path
1
Read the question and answer
Read the Arabic passage and its translation carefully. Notice the question-and-answer format: the opponent asks for the proof, and the author begins his response.
The passage is incomplete due to a page break, but the core argument is present.
2
Understand the logical link
Identify the proof: 'the evidence that God created disbelief and sins.' Ask yourself: why does creation imply will? In Islamic theology, creation is an act of the Creator, and every act of creation is an expression of His will.
3
Reflect on the significance
Consider that the proof explicitly includes sins. This shows that God's will encompasses all things, even those that seem contrary to His commands. Reflect on how this connects to the broader doctrine of qadar (divine decree).
The full argument will be completed in later passages.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إن قال قائل : لم قلتم : إن الله مريد لكل كائن أن يكون ، ولكل مالا يكون أن لا يكون؟ قيل له : الدليل على ذلك : أن الحجة قد وضحت، أن الله عز وجل خلق الكفر والمعاصي - وسنبين ذلك بعد هذا الموضع من كتابنا - وإذا
If someone says: Why do you say that God wills every existent to be, and every non-existent not to be? It is said to him: The proof for that is that the evidence has become clear that God the Mighty and Majestic created disbelief and sins - and we will explain that after this place in our book - and if
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage presents a question and the beginning of an answer. The questioner asks: Why do you assert that God wills every existing thing to exist, and every non-existing thing not to exist? The answer begins: The proof is that it has been clearly established that God created disbelief and sins. The implication is that since God created them, He must have willed them (otherwise creation would be without will). The author then says he will explain this later in the book. The text cuts off with 'and if' (وإذا), likely starting a conditional clause. The lesson is incomplete due to the page break, but the core idea is presented: the proof that God wills all things is that He creates everything, including disbelief and sins.

Key Terms

خلق
كفر
معاصي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that God creating sins does not necessarily mean He wills them.
Correction: The text affirms that creation itself is proof of will. Since God creates sins, He must will them, even if He dislikes them in terms of moral approval.

Key Takeaway
God's creation of all things, including sins, proves that He wills them.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

The Rational Necessity of Allah's Will
Imam al-Ash'ari argues by analogy that denying divine will implies deficiency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational necessity that Allah's will encompasses all creation, as argued by Imam al-Ash'ari through a reductio ad absurdum.

Core Idea
Denying Allah's will over any action implies attributing deficiency to Him.

Imam al-Ash'ari uses a powerful logical tool: assuming the opponent's position and showing it leads to absurd consequences. In this passage, he demonstrates that if one claims Allah does something He does not will, that logically forces one to attribute negligence or weakness to Allah—something no believer would accept. By extending the same logic to human actions, he solidifies the necessity of divine will over all events.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the literal translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the repeated structure: 'fayqalu lahum' and 'fala budda min na'am'.
2
Identify the three concessive steps
The argument proceeds by extracting three concessions from the opponent: ignorance (first), negligence (second), and ignorance again (third). Find each one and see how they are linked.
Each concession is prefaced with 'alaysa...' or 'kadhalika yalzam...'.
3
Understand the logical analogy
The core move is: if you accept that attributing ignorance to Allah is wrong, then you must also accept that attributing negligence is wrong. The logic is the same for both divine and human actions.
The final sentence states the conclusion: 'la farqa fi dhalika...'.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
Ask yourself: why does the argument work? Because it forces a contradiction: the opponent denies divine will but cannot accept the resulting deficiency. This shows that divine will must encompass all events.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : أليس من زعم أن الله عز وجل فعل ما لا يعلمه قد نسب الله سبحانه إلى ما لا يليق به من الجهل؟ فلا بد من نعم. فيقال لهم : فكذلك يلزم من زعم أن الله فعل ما لا يريده لزمه أن ينسب الله سبحانه إلى السهو والتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده، فإذا قالوا: نعم قيل لهم : وكذلك يلزم من زعم أن العباد يفعلون ما لا يعلم الله نسب الله تعالى إلى الجهل، فلا بد من نعم. ويقال لهم : فكذلك إذا كان في كون فعل فعله الله وهو لا يريده إيجاب أو ضعف وتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده، فكذلك إذا كان من غيره ما لا يريده وجب إثبات سهو وغفلة أو ضعف وتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريد، لا فرق في ذلك بين ما كان منه وما كان من غيره.
And it is said to them: Is it not that whoever claims that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, does what He does not know has attributed to Allah, Exalted, what is not befitting of Him of ignorance? So they must answer yes. Then it is said to them: Likewise, it follows that whoever claims that Allah does what He does not will necessarily attributes to Allah negligence and failure to attain what He wills. And if they say yes, it is said to them: And likewise it follows that whoever claims that the servants do what Allah does not know has attributed to Allah ignorance. So they must answer yes. And it is said to them: Thus, if there is in the existence of an act that Allah does while He does not will it, it necessitates weakness and failure to attain what He wills. So likewise, if there is from other than Him what He does not will, it becomes necessary to affirm forgetfulness and heedlessness, or weakness and failure to attain what He wills. There is no difference in that between what is from Him and what is from other than Him.
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage is a proof by reductio ad absurdum. It begins by asking the opponent whether claiming that Allah does something He does not know leads to attributing ignorance to Him. The opponent must agree that this is impermissible. Then the Ash'ari analogizes: similarly, claiming that Allah does something He does not will leads to attributing negligence and failure to achieve His will. Again, the opponent must agree. The argument then extends to human actions: claiming that servants do what Allah does not know also attributes ignorance. The conclusion is that just as it is impossible for something to occur in Allah's knowledge without His knowledge, it is also impossible for something to occur in His dominion without His will, because any such occurrence would imply deficiency (forgetfulness, heedlessness, weakness) in Allah. The argument uses the opponent's own concession to force consistency.

Key Terms
السهو
التقصير
الإرادة
الجهل
الغفلة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think this argument anthropomorphizes Allah by attributing human-like negligence.
Correction: It is an argument from negation: we deny such attributes absolutely, not affirm them. The reasoning shows that denying divine will leads to an impossible consequence, so we affirm the opposite.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what does claiming that Allah does something He does not will imply?
A) That Allah is ignorant
B) That Allah is negligent and fails to achieve His will
C) That Allah is weak
D) That Allah is forgetful

Key Takeaway
Allah's will is necessarily all-encompassing; any exception would imply impermissible deficiency.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that every single event in your life, including your own choices, is under Allah's will? If so, why do you sometimes feel regret or anger about past events? What does that reveal about your heart's acceptance of His decree, and how will you train yourself to submit more fully starting today?
?
When you sin, do you attribute it to your own will overriding Allah's will? How does this lesson correct that, and what change in your attitude towards repentance and reliance on Allah does this require from you right now?
?
Examine your everyday speech for phrases like 'if only I had...' or 'why did this happen?'. Do these imply that something occurred without Allah's will? What specific steps will you take to align your tongue and heart with the truth that nothing escapes His will?

Inconsistency in Denying Divine Knowledge while Affirming Divine Will
Argument that if lack of will does not imply weakness, then lack of knowledge also should not.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Ash'ari argument exposes an inconsistency in the opponents' reasoning regarding divine will and knowledge.

Core Idea
If lack of will doesn't imply weakness, lack of knowledge shouldn't imply deficiency.

This passage presents a sharp logical rebuttal. The opponents had claimed that something can exist in Allah's dominion that He does not will, yet without weakness. The Ash'ari turns this against them: why then deny that something can exist that He does not know, without implying deficiency? If they reject that, they must also reject the original claim. This reveals an inconsistency in their position.

Guided Path
1
Read the argument
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the structure: conditional statement leading to a rhetorical question.
2
Identify the opponents' claim
The opponents accept that God does not will everything but denies He is weak. What is the basis for this?
They consider will as independent of knowledge.
3
Spot the inconsistency
If lack of will does not imply weakness, why would lack of knowledge imply deficiency? The opponents should apply the same logic.
The argument uses analogy.
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider why the opponents must reject their own position if they reject the conclusion. This is a classic reductio ad absurdum.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : إذا كان في سلطان الله مالا يريده وهو يعلمه، ولا يلحقه الضعف والتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده فما أنكرتم أن يكون في سلطانه مالا يعلمه ولا يلحقه النقصان فإن لم يجز هذا لم يجز ما قلتموه .
And it is said to them: If there is in the dominion of Allah what He does not will while He knows it, and weakness and failure to attain what He wills do not befall Him, then why do you deny that there is in His dominion what He does not know and deficiency does not befall Him? For if this is not permissible, then what you have said is not permissible.
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage presents an argument against the opponents who claim that something can exist in Allah's dominion that He does not will, yet that does not imply weakness. The Ash'ari uses this point to turn the argument around: if that is acceptable, then why do they deny that something can exist in His dominion that He does not know, which also does not imply deficiency? If they reject the possibility of unknowing, then by the same logic they should reject the possibility of unwilled. The argument exposes an inconsistency in the opponent's position.

Key Terms
سلطان
الضعف
التقصير
النقصان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the argument is about limiting God's power, but it is about logical consistency between divine attributes.
Correction: The argument does not claim that God actually lacks knowledge; it shows that the opponents cannot consistently allow unwilled things while denying unknown things.

Checkpoint
What inconsistency does the argument point out in the opponent's position?
A) They accept that God can be weak but not ignorant
B) They accept that God can will things unknowingly
C) They accept that lack of will does not imply weakness, but reject that lack of knowledge does not imply deficiency
D) They accept that God knows everything but not that He wills everything

Key Takeaway
The Ash'ari argument shows that the opponents' position on divine will and knowledge is logically inconsistent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever held a belief about Allah where you accepted a limitation in one attribute but rejected a parallel limitation in another without real justification? What does this argument reveal about the consistency of your own understanding of Allah's attributes?
?
The opponents allowed that Allah does not will some things, but not that He does not know some things. In your own life, when you face events you did not will (like trials), do you still trust that Allah knows them perfectly? Or do you sometimes feel He is unaware? How does this passage challenge those feelings?
?
This argument forces you to examine hidden contradictions in your theology. If you were to apply the same logical standard to your own beliefs today, where might you find an inconsistency that you need to resolve before Allah?

Proof that Allah Wills Every Existing Thing
Objection and two proofs for divine will encompassing all things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah's creation of everything implies His will for everything, and how the impossibility of deficiency in Allah necessitates that nothing occurs against His will.

Core Idea
Whatever Allah creates, He wills; nothing occurs in His dominion without His will.

This passage addresses a critical objection to divine will: how can we say Allah wills everything? Imām al-Ashʿarī provides two proofs grounded in Allah's attributes of creation and perfection. The first links creation to will, the second argues from the impossibility of deficiency. Together they establish that Allah's will encompasses all that occurs, including sins and unbelief.

Guided Path
1
Understand the objection
Read the opening question where the objector challenges the claim that Allah wills every existent to exist and every non-existent to not exist. Recognize the nature of the challenge.
The objector is questioning why we assert such a universal will.
2
Examine the first proof
The first answer argues that since Allah created unbelief and sins (which He will later explain), and He cannot create what He does not will, therefore He wills them. Notice the logical link between creation and will.
This proof is straightforward: creation implies will.
3
Examine the second proof
The second answer uses an analogy from knowledge to will: just as it is impossible for something in Allah's dominion to occur without His knowledge (as that would imply deficiency), so too it is impossible without His will. Otherwise, it would imply forgetfulness or weakness.
This proof highlights the perfection of Allah's attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إن قال قائل : لم قلتم : إن الله مريد لكل كائن أن يكون ، ولكل مالا يكون أن لا يكون؟ قيل له : الدليل على ذلك : أن الحجة قد وضحت، أن الله عز وجل خلق الكفر والمعاصي - وسنبين ذلك بعد هذا الموضع من كتابنا - وإذا وجب أن الله سبحانه خالق لذلك، فقد وجب أنه مريد له؛ لأنه لا يجوز أن يخلق مالا يريده . وجواب آخر : أنه لا يجوز أن يكون في سلطان الله عز وجل من إكساب العباد مالا يريده، كما لا يجوز أن يكون من فعله المجمع على أنه فعل مالا يريده؛ لأنه لو وقع من فعله مالا يعلمه لكان في ذلك إثبات النقصان وكذلك القول لو وقع من عباده مالا يعلمه، فكذلك لا يجوز أن يقع من عباده مالا يريده ؛ لأن ذلك يوجب أن يقع عن سهو وغفلة، أو عن ضعف وتقصير عن بلوغ ما يريده كما يجب ذلك لو وقع من فعله المجمع على أنه فعله مالا يريده وأيضاً فلو كانت المعاصي - وهو لا يشاء أن تكون المعاصي - كائنة شاء الله أم أبى، وهذه صفة الضعف . تعالى الله عن ذلك علواً كبيراً . وقد أوضحنا أن الله لم يزل مريداً على الحقيقة التي علمه عليها، فإذا كان الكفر مما يكون وقد علم ذلك فقد أراد أن يكون .
If someone says: Why do you say that Allah is willing for every existent to exist, and for every non-existent to not exist? It is said to him: The proof of that is that the argument has been clarified that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, created unbelief and sins - and we will explain that after this place in our book - and if it is established that Allah is the Creator of that, then it is established that He is willing for it, because it is not permissible that He creates what He does not will. And another answer: It is not permissible that there be in the dominion of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, from the acquisition of the servants what He does not will, just as it is not permissible that there be from His act that which is agreed to be an act He does not will; because if there occurred from His act what He does not know, in that would be an affirmation of deficiency. And likewise the statement if there occurred from His servants what He does not know. So likewise it is not permissible that there occur from His servants what He does not will, because that necessitates that it occur due to forgetfulness and heedlessness, or due to weakness and failure to attain what He wills, just as that would be necessary if there occurred from His act that which is agreed to be His act and He does not will it. And also, if sins - while He does not wish that sins be - were to occur whether Allah wills or refuses, that is an attribute of weakness. Exalted is Allah above that, with a great exaltation. And we have clarified that Allah has always been willing in the true sense which is known about Him. So if unbelief is something that occurs, and He knows that, then He willed that it occur.
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage contains an objection and two answers. The objector asks: why do you assert that Allah wills every existent to exist and every non-existent to not exist? The first answer: the proof is that Allah created unbelief and sins, and it is impossible for Him to create what He does not will. Therefore, since He created them, He must have willed them. The second answer uses an analogy: just as it is impossible for something in Allah's dominion to occur without His knowledge (as that would imply deficiency), so too it is impossible for anything to occur without His will, because such an occurrence would imply forgetfulness, heedlessness, weakness, or failure. The passage further argues that if sins occurred against Allah's will, that would be a sign of weakness, which is impossible for Allah. The conclusion reaffirms that Allah's will is in accordance with His knowledge: since He knew unbelief would occur, He willed it to occur.

Key Terms
الإرادة
الخلق
المعاصي
الكفر
السهو
الغفلة
الضعف
التقصير

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That this passage implies Allah wills evil in a moral sense.
Correction: The passage argues for ontological necessity: whatever occurs is willed by Allah, regardless of moral evaluation. It does not say Allah loves or approves of evil, only that His will encompasses all events as part of His sovereignty.

Checkpoint
According to the first answer, what is the proof that Allah wills every existing thing?
A) Allah's mercy
B) The creation of unbelief and sins
C) The logical consistency of knowledge and will
D) The inability of servants to act

Key Takeaway
Allah wills everything that exists because He creates it and because anything occurring without His will would imply imperfection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you commit a sin, do you truly believe that Allah willed it to occur? If yes, does that make you feel more accountable or less? How should this belief affect your repentance?
?
The objector in this passage implicitly thinks that some things happen without Allah's will. Is there any area of your life where you think, 'This happened despite Allah's will'? How does this lesson challenge that thought?
?
If all events—including your most painful trials—are willed by Allah, can you genuinely say, 'I am content with His decree'? What would change if you lived that conviction daily?

The Logical Consistency of Divine Knowledge and Will
Logical proof that God must will what He knows.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational argument that God must will whatever He knows will occur, and why opposing this leads to contradiction.

Core Idea
God's will cannot contradict His knowledge—whatever He knows will happen, He wills.

This lesson presents a concise logical proof from Imam al-Ash'ari that God's will aligns with His knowledge. The argument starts from the premise that God knows all future events, including unbelief. If someone claims God does not will unbelief, then they are saying God wills the opposite of what He knows—an impossibility. Through this reasoning, we see that divine attributes are perfectly consistent.

Guided Path
1
Read the argument carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the conditional structure: 'if...then...' and the conclusion.
The argument is dense; take your time.
2
Identify the premises
The argument assumes God knows unbelief will occur. Then it presents two options: either God wills it or not.
The opponents claim God does not will it.
3
Understand the reductio
If God does not will unbelief, then He wills the opposite of what He knows—a contradiction. Therefore, the only consistent position is that God wills what He knows.
This is a proof by contradiction.
4
Reflect on divine consistency
Consider why this argument is important: it upholds the coherence of God's attributes and rejects any suggestion of conflict between His knowledge and will.
This relates to the doctrine of divine perfection.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : إذا كان الله عز وجل علم أن الكفر يكون، وأراد أن لا يكون، فقد أراد أن يكون ما علم على خلاف ما علم، وإذا لم يجز ذلك، فقد أراد أن يكون ما لم يعلم كما علم
And it is said to them: If Allah knew that unbelief would occur, and He willed that it not occur, then He willed that what He knew be contrary to what He knew. And if that is not permissible, then He willed that what He did not know be as He knew.
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This is a concise logical proof. It assumes that Allah knows that unbelief will occur. If the opponents claim that Allah does not will unbelief, then they are claiming that Allah willed that what He knows (i.e., that unbelief will occur) be contrary to His knowledge. That is a contradiction, because willing something to happen while knowing it will not happen is absurd. Alternatively, if they reject that contradiction, then they must admit that Allah willed what He knew (i.e., unbelief). The argument forces the conclusion that divine will must align with divine knowledge.

Key Terms
العلم
الإرادة
الكفر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God can know something will happen but not will it.
Correction: The argument shows that would lead to contradiction; God's will must align with His knowledge.

Checkpoint
What does the argument conclude about the relationship between God's knowledge and will?
A) They are independent
B) God's will can oppose His knowledge
C) God must will what He knows will occur
D) God's knowledge is limited by His will

Key Takeaway
God's will is perfectly consistent with His knowledge; He cannot know something will happen and also will that it not happen.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of a sin you committed after knowing it was wrong. Did you ever imagine that Allah knew you would sin but wished it wouldn't happen? How does this lesson challenge that assumption and push you to take full responsibility for your actions?
?
If Allah's will is perfectly aligned with His knowledge, then every trial you face was willed by Him. What specific difficulty in your life have you been resisting, blaming on fate or chance? How can you now surrender to His decree, trusting that His will is consistent with His perfect knowledge?
?
The argument shows that it is impossible for Allah to know an event and not will it. Does this truth unsettle you or bring you peace? What does it reveal about your own trust in Allah's wisdom—do you truly believe that whatever He knows and wills is ultimately for your good?

Does Willing Folly Make One a Fool?
Al-Ash'ari refutes the claim that willing folly makes one a fool using the story of Abel.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses the Quranic story of Abel to refute the claim that willing folly makes one a fool, thereby defending God's attribute of will.

Core Idea
Willing folly does not make the willer a fool—not even a prophet.

This passage presents a direct objection to the Ash'ari position on God's will. The opponents argue that willing folly is inherently foolish. Al-Ash'ari responds with a striking proof from the Qur'an: Abel willed his own death, an act of folly, yet he is praised, not called a fool. This shows that the connection between willing folly and being a fool is not necessary.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then read the English translation. Notice the objection and the response.
The passage is concise; pay attention to the logical flow.
2
Identify the opponent's claim
Find the statement attributed to the opponents: 'Because the one who wills folly is a fool.' Understand why this challenges divine attributes.
3
Analyze al-Ash'ari's Quranic proof
Al-Ash'ari cites the story of Abel (Qur'an 5:28-29). Note how Abel wills his own death (a folly) but is not considered a fool. This undermines the opponent's claim.
4
Reflect on the theological conclusion
Consider the implication: if willing folly does not make a righteous person a fool, then God, who wills human folly, is not thereby attributed with folly.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإن قالوا: لأن مريد السَّفَه سفيه . قيل لهم : ولم قلتم ذلك؟ أو ليس قد أخبر الله تعالى عن ابن آدم أنـه قال لأخيه : ﴿ لَئِن بَسَطتَ إِلَيَّ يَدَكَ لِتَقْتُلَنِي مَا أَنَا بِبَاسِطِ يَدِى إِلَيْكَ لَأَقْتُلَكَ إِنِّي أَخَافُ اللهَ رَبَّ الْعَالَمِينَ * إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَن تَبُوءَ بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ فَتَكُونَ مِنْ أَصْحَابِ النَّارِ ﴾ [المائدة: ٢٨-٢٩] فأراد أن لا يقتل أخاه لئلا يعذب وأن يقتله أخوه حتى يبوء بإثم قتـله لـه وسائر آثامه التي كانت عليه، فيكون من أصحاب النار، فأراد قتل أخيه الذي هو سفه ، ولم يكن بذلك سفيها ، فلم زعمتم أن الله سبحانه إذا أراد سفه العباد وجب أن ينسب ذلك إليه؟
For they said: Because the one who wills folly is a fool. It is said to them: Why did you say that? Has not God informed us about the son of Adam that he said to his brother: 'If you stretch your hand to kill me, I will not stretch my hand to kill you, indeed I fear God, Lord of the worlds. Indeed I want you to bear my sin and your sin and be among the companions of the Fire.' So he willed not to kill his brother so that he would not be punished, and he willed that his brother kill him so that he would bear the sin of killing him and his other sins that were upon him, and thus be among the companions of the Fire. So he willed the killing of his brother, which is folly, and he was not thereby a fool. So why do you claim that when God wills the folly of His servants, it necessarily follows that He is attributed with folly?
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This passage presents an objection and al-Ash'ari's answer using the story of Abel and Cain. The opponents argue that anyone who wills folly (sufh) is a fool (safih). Al-Ash'ari cites the Quranic verses where Abel says he wills not to kill his brother but wills that his brother kill him, which is a form of folly (willing a sinful act). Yet Abel is not considered a fool. Therefore, willing folly does not necessarily make the willer a fool. So God, who wills folly in His servants, is not thereby a fool.

Key Terms
سفه
سفيه
إرادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Willing evil necessarily makes the willer evil or foolish.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari shows using Abel that one can will folly without being a fool, so the attribute of will in God does not imply imperfection.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari's argument, who willed folly but was not a fool?
A) Cain
B) Abel
C) Pharaoh
D) Satan

Key Takeaway
Willing folly does not make one a fool, as shown by Abel; thus God's willing of human folly does not mar His perfection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Abel was willing to endure being killed rather than commit murder. What 'follies' are you willing to face for the sake of Allah? Are you actively avoiding situations where your reputation or comfort might be sacrificed?
?
When you see someone committing a foolish or sinful act, do you immediately label them as foolish or evil? How does Abel's example challenge you to separate the act from the person's essential character?
?
Al-Ash'ari uses this argument to show that God's will for evil in the world does not diminish His perfection. Do you struggle with the reality of evil in your life? Does this lesson change how you view God's will in your hardships? If so, how will you respond the next time you face a trial?
