Proof from Prophet Yusuf's Willing Imprisonment
Al-Ash'ari uses Yusuf's willing of a sin to show that willing folly does not make one a fool.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses the example of Prophet Yusuf to argue that willing a sinful act does not make the willer a fool, and how this applies to divine attributes.

Core Idea
Willing a sin does not inherently make one a fool.

Al-Ash'ari presents a concise argument from the story of Prophet Yusuf. Yusuf preferred prison over adultery, and his imprisonment was a sin committed by his brothers. Yet Yusuf willed that imprisonment and was not considered a fool. This example is used to address a theological objection about God's will and folly.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice how Al-Ash'ari cites the Qur'anic verse and then draws a logical conclusion.
The verse is from Surah Yusuf, verse 33.
2
Identify the core analogy
Identify the analogy: Yusuf willed a sinful act (his imprisonment) but was not a fool. Therefore, when God wills folly from His servants, He is not a fool either.
3
Understand the theological objection being answered
The objection likely was: if God wills sinful acts from people, then God must be foolish. Al-Ash'ari refutes this by showing a human parallel where willing a sin does not make one foolish.
4
Connect to divine attributes
Reflect on how this argument preserves God's perfection: willing folly in others does not entail folly in the willer, especially when the act is willed for a wise purpose.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : قد قال يوسف عليه السلام: ﴿ رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ ﴾ [يوسف:٣٣] وكان سجنهم إياه معصية، فأراد المعصية التي هي سجنهم إياه دون فعل ما يدعونه إليه، ولم يكن بذلك سفيهاً، فما أنكرتم من أن لا يجب إذا أراد الباري سبحانه سفه العباد - بأن يكون قبيحاً منهم خلافاً للطاعة ـ أن يكون سفيهاً .
And it is said to them: Yusuf, peace be upon him, said: 'My Lord, prison is more beloved to me than what they call me to.' And their imprisoning him was a disobedience. So he willed the disobedience which was their imprisoning him, rather than doing what they called him to, and he was not thereby a fool. So what prevents you from [acknowledging] that when the Exalted Creator wills the folly of His servants — in that it is vile from them contrary to obedience — He is not necessarily a fool?
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
This is another proof. Prophet Yusuf preferred prison over sin, and his imprisonment was a sinful act from his brothers. He willed that sin (the imprisonment) but was not a fool. Therefore, when God wills folly from His servants (i.e., their sinful acts), He is not a fool either.

Key Terms
سفه
سفيه
معصية
إرادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Willing a sinful act necessarily makes the willer sinful or foolish.
Correction: The story of Yusuf shows that one can will a sin (the imprisonment) without being foolish, and thus God's willing of folly in servants does not entail foolishness in God.

Checkpoint
What did Yusuf will that was a disobedience?
A) To commit zina
B) To kill his brother
C) To be imprisoned
D) To worship idols

Key Takeaway
Willing a sin for a good reason does not make one a fool, and this principle applies to God as well.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you are faced with a difficult choice between two imperfect options, do you trust that God's wisdom is greater than your own? How does Yusuf's example of choosing a lesser evil (prison) to avoid a greater one (sin) challenge your tendency to doubt God's plan when you suffer?
?
Have you ever judged someone harshly for committing a sin, assuming they were foolish? After learning that a prophet like Yusuf willed a sin (imprisonment) without being foolish, how must you reconsider your attitude toward those who struggle with sin?

Analogy of Seeing Sin vs. Willing Sin
Al-Ash'ari argues by analogy that God's will for folly does not make Him foolish.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses an analogy from human experience to defend God's attribute of will without implying imperfection.

Core Idea
God's willing folly does not make Him a fool, just as His seeing sin does not.

This passage from al-Ash'ari's al-Ibanah tackles a common objection: if God wills evil, doesn't that make Him evil? Al-Ash'ari counters with a striking analogy, showing that actions attributed to God in creation do not carry the same moral implications as they do for humans. Let's examine his reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the parallel structure: 'the one who sees... is a fool' and 'the one who wills... is a fool'.
The argument is concise and relies on two pairs of examples.
2
Identify the two comparisons
Al-Ash'ari draws a parallel between seeing sin and willing sin. In both cases, the human action is considered foolish, but God does the same without being foolish.
The first comparison is about seeing, the second about willing.
3
Trace the logical flow
The argument runs: (1) A human who sees sin is called a fool. (2) God sees sin but is not called a fool. (3) Therefore, the same logic applies to willing: a human who wills folly is a fool, but God wills folly and is not a fool.
The conclusion is implied: God's will for folly does not make Him foolish.
4
Reflect on the theological point
This argument defends God's attribute of will against the objection that willing evil would imply evil in God. The analogy shows that God's actions are not judged by human standards.
It preserves divine transcendence while acknowledging God's creative will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : أليس من يرى منـا جـرم المسلمين كان سفيها، والله سبحانه يراهم ولا يُنسب إلى السفه؟ فلا بد من نعم . فيقال لهم : فيما أنكرتم أن من أراد السفه منا كان سفيها، والله سبحانه يريد سفه السفهاء ولا ينسب إلى الله تعالى سفه، تعالى الله عن ذلك
And it is said to them: Is it not that among us, the one who sees the crime of Muslims is a fool, while God sees them and is not attributed with folly? So they must say yes. Then it is said to them: What prevents you from [acknowledging] that among us, the one who wills folly is a fool, while God wills the folly of the foolish and is not attributed with folly? Exalted is God above that.
Source pages p. 128

Commentary
Al-Ash'ari uses an analogy: if a person sees a crime and does nothing, they are considered a fool. Yet God sees all crimes and is not called a fool. Similarly, if a person wills folly, they become a fool. But God wills folly (i.e., creates the acts of folly in His servants) and is not a fool. The analogy shows that God's willing folly does not imply foolishness in Him.

Key Terms
سفه
سفيه
يرى
يريد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that if God wills evil, He must be evil or foolish.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari shows by analogy that God's will for folly does not make Him a fool, just as His seeing sin does not make Him a fool. Divine attributes are not measured by human standards.

Checkpoint
What does al-Ash'ari compare God's willing of folly to?
A) God's seeing of sin
B) God's creating of sin
C) God's commanding of sin
D) God's forbidding of sin

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari defends God's will by analogy: if seeing sin does not make God a fool, then willing sin also does not make Him a fool.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you see evil and suffering in the world, do you ever find yourself silently accusing God of being unfair or less than good? How does Al-Ash'ari's analogy challenge you to trust that God's will for evil does not taint His perfection?

?
Have you ever looked down on someone else's sin, thinking they are foolish, while forgetting that God willed their action? How does this lesson call you to humility and reliance on God's wisdom rather than your own judgment?

God's Transcendence Above Law
Al-Ash'ari argues God is not under law, so His willing folly does not make Him a fool.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Al-Ash'ari's argument that human concepts of foolishness do not apply to God because God is not under any higher authority or law.

Core Idea
God is above all law—so human categories of folly do not apply to Him.

Al-Ash'ari addresses a key objection: if God wills what is evil, does that make Him a fool? He responds by distinguishing human folly from divine action. Human folly arises from violating a superior's command, but God has no superior. This passage shows how divine transcendence protects God from such human judgments.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic passage once silently, then aloud. Let the rhythm of the argument sink in before analyzing it.
2
Understand human folly
Notice how Al-Ash'ari defines human folly: a person is a fool because they willed folly, were forbidden, and are under a higher authority. Identify each condition.
Human folly is relational—it depends on a lawgiver.
3
Contrast with God's status
Now see the contrast: God is not under any law, no one sets limits or commands for Him. Therefore, the conditions for folly do not apply.
4
Draw the conclusion
The conclusion follows directly: when God wills something that appears vile, it does not make Him a fool. Understand why this argument is logically sound.
This protects divine transcendence from anthropomorphic judgments.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: السفيه منا إنما كان سفيها كما أراد السفه؛ لأنه نهي عن ذلك، ولأنه تحت شريعة من هو فوقه، ومن يحد له الحدود، ويرسم لـه الرسوم، فلما أتى ما نهي عنه كان سفيهاً، ورب العالمين جل ثناؤه وتقدست أسماؤه ليس تحت شريعة ولا فوقه من يحد له الحدود، ويرسم له الرسوم ولا فوقه مبيح ولا حاظر ولا آمر ولا زاجر ، فلم يجب إذا أراد أن يكون قبيحاً أن ينسب إلى السفه سبحانه وتعالى
And it is said to them: The fool among us is only a fool because he willed folly, because he was forbidden from that, and because he is under the law of one above him who sets limits and prescribes rules for him. So when he did what he was forbidden, he became a fool. But the Lord of the worlds, may His praise be exalted and His names sanctified, is not under a law, nor above Him is one who sets limits or prescribes rules, nor above Him is a permitter or forbidder or commander or restrainer. So it is not necessary, when He wills something to be vile, that He be attributed with folly, glory be to Him and exalted.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This proof distinguishes human folly from divine will. A human is a fool because he violates a law given by a superior. God has no superior, no lawgiver, no one to set limits or commands. Therefore, when God wills something that is vile (qabih) in human terms, it does not make Him a fool, since the concept of 'fool' applies only to those who transgress a higher authority. God is above all authority.

Key Terms
سفيه
شريعة
حدود
رسوم
قبيح

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that if God wills something evil, then He must be foolish, just as a human would be.
Correction: This is false because human folly is defined by violating a higher law. God has no higher authority, so the category does not apply.

Checkpoint
Why is a human considered a fool for willing folly?
A) Because foolishness is inherently evil
B) Because he violates a law from a superior
C) Because he harms himself
D) Because he is irrational

Key Takeaway
God's transcendence means human moral categories do not apply to His actions; He is not a fool for willing what seems vile to us.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever secretly thought that God's decree for your life was foolish or unfair? What does this passage reveal about the arrogance of that thought? Will you repent and acknowledge that He is above all law, including your sense of fairness?
?
When you face a trial that seems meaningless, do you trust that God's will is not subject to your categories of good and evil? What concrete step can you take today to submit to His decree without demanding an explanation?
?
If God is not a fool for willing what is vile, then what right do you have to question His wisdom when He allows evil in the world? How will you remind yourself of this truth when you are tempted to accuse Him?

Analogy of the Master and Slaves: God's Will and Human Foolishness
Al-Ash'ari presents two analogies to show God is not a fool.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses the analogy of a master allowing zina to argue that God's permission of sin does not imply foolishness.

Core Idea
God's permission of evil does not make Him foolish, unlike humans.

Al-Ash'ari presents a powerful analogy to counter the Mu'tazilite claim that if God wills evil, He must be evil or foolish. He compares God to a human master who allows his slaves to commit adultery while able to stop them—such a master is a fool. But God, who also allows sin while being able to prevent it, is not a fool. This sharp contrast shows that God's attributes are beyond human moral categories.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Notice the parallel structure between the human master and God.
The passage has two similar sentences.
2
Identify the two comparisons
Find where Al-Ash'ari compares a human master allowing zina to God allowing zina, and then compares a human who wills folly to God who wills folly. Note the conclusions in each case.
The first comparison is about actions (allowing zina), the second about will (willing folly).
3
Reflect on the purpose of the analogy
Ask yourself: Why does Al-Ash'ari use this analogy? What point is he making about God? The commentary will help you see that he is rejecting the idea that God's actions can be judged by human standards of foolishness.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم: أليس من خلى بين عبيده وبين إمائـه منـا يـزنـي بعـضـهـم ببعض، وهو يقدر على التفريق بينهم يكون سفيهاً، ورب العالمين عز وجل قد خلى بين عبيده وإمائه يزني بعضهم ببعض، وهو يقدر على التفريق بينهم وليس سفيهاً، وكذلك من أراد السفه منا كان سفيهاً، ورب العالمين جل وعز يريد السفه، وليس سفيهاً .
And it is said to them: Is it not that among us, one who lets his male and female slaves commit zina with each other while being able to separate them is a fool? Yet the Lord of the worlds, mighty and majestic, has let His male and female slaves commit zina with each other while being able to separate them, and He is not a fool. Likewise, among us, the one who wills folly is a fool, while the Lord of the worlds wills folly and is not a fool.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
Al-Ash'ari presents another analogy. If a human master has the power to prevent his slaves from committing adultery but does not, he is considered a fool. God, however, has power to prevent all sin but allows it, yet He is not called a fool. Similarly, if a human wills folly, he becomes a fool, but God wills folly and is not a fool. The analogy reinforces that God's actions are not subject to human judgments of foolishness.

Key Terms
سفه
سفيه
خلى
يفرق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's allowing sin implies that He is foolish or unjust, just as a human who allows sin would be.
Correction: The passage argues that God's permission of sin does not make Him foolish because His wisdom and sovereignty are incomparable to human limitations.

Checkpoint

A) Stealing
B) Adultery (zina)
C) Murder
D) Lying

Key Takeaway
God's permission of sin does not reflect foolishness, as His wisdom is incomparable to human standards.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever judged God's actions by human standards, feeling that He should have prevented a certain evil? How does this lesson challenge you to submit to His unmatched wisdom instead of measuring Him with your own measure? This week, when you encounter something you don't understand, will you pause and remind yourself that God's ways are not yours?
?
The human master who allows sin is called a fool, but God who allows far greater sin is not. Why does your heart still sometimes resent God for permitting trials in your life? Are you willing to trust His perfect wisdom, even when His permissions cause you pain?
?
If God's will for folly does not make Him foolish, then what does it say about your own will when you desire something contrary to His command? Will you examine a desire you have right now that might be folly in His sight, and repent of it?

God's Willing of Folly Without Becoming a Fool
Al-Ash'ari argues by analogy that God wills folly without being a fool.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses an analogy from human willing to argue that God can will folly without being foolish, emphasizing divine transcendence.

Core Idea
God wills what He wills without being defined by it.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a brief but powerful argument to defend God's transcendence. He draws a parallel between human and divine willing, pointing out a crucial distinction. This passage shows how theological reasoning uses clear analogies to protect God from human attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Let the argument's structure emerge before you analyze it.
Notice the parallel structure: human case → divine case.
2
Identify the analogy's two sides
Find the human side: a human who wills obedience is called obedient, and one who wills folly is called a fool. Then the divine side: God wills obedience but is not called obedient.
3
Draw the conclusion
Logically, if God is not called obedient despite willing obedience, then He is not called a fool despite willing folly. Understand why this protects God's transcendence.
The key is that God's willing does not make Him acquire the human quality.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم من أراد طاعة الله منا كان مطيعاً، كما أن من أراد السفه كان سفيهاً، ورب العالمين عز وجل يريد الطاعة وليس مطيعاً، فكذلك يريد السفه، وليس سفيهاً .
And it is said to them: Among us, the one who wills obedience to God is obedient, just as the one who wills folly is a fool. Yet the Lord of the worlds, mighty and majestic, wills obedience and is not obedient. So likewise, He wills folly and is not a fool.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This short proof uses a parallel: if a human wills obedience, he is called obedient; if he wills folly, he is called a fool. But God wills obedience from His servants, yet He is not called obedient (since obedience is a human quality of submitting to a higher command, and God has no higher). Similarly, God wills folly from His servants, but He is not called a fool. The attribute is not transferred from the action to the agent in God's case.

Key Terms
طاعة
مطيع
سفه
سفيه
إرادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God wills folly (creates foolish actions), then He must be foolish.
Correction: God's willing of an action does not make Him acquire the human quality associated with that action. Just as He wills obedience without being obedient, He wills folly without being foolish.

Checkpoint
According to this proof, what does God will but is not Himself?
A) Power, but not powerful
B) Obedience, but not obedient
C) Knowledge, but not knowing
D) Life, but not living

Key Takeaway
God's attributes are not limited by human categories; He wills all that occurs without acquiring the human labels for those actions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you commit a sin that God wills, do you feel shame and repentance, or do you rationalize it by saying 'God willed it, so it's okay'? This lesson teaches that God is not defined by the actions He wills, but you are. Which response shows true faith?
?
Think of a time you blamed God for evil or suffering in the world. Did you secretly believe that His will made Him less good? The source reminds that God wills all things without being tainted by them. Can you surrender your judgment and trust His perfect wisdom?
?
If God wills both obedience and folly, but is only pleased with obedience, how does that impact your daily choices? Will you strive to obey, knowing that your obedience is His will and that He is untainted by the disobedience He also wills? Let this drive you to worship with awe.

Quranic Proof that God Wills All That Occurs
Al-Ash'ari proves God wills fighting from Quranic conditional.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses Quranic verses to prove that God wills even sinful actions like fighting.

Core Idea
God's will is universal: what occurs, He wills.

In this lesson, we examine a concise Quranic proof from Imam Al-Ash'ari. The author cites two key verses to show that if God had not willed fighting, it would not occur; since it does occur, it is by His will. This argument defends the doctrine of divine decree against those who claim God does not will evil.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage carefully
Read the Arabic text once, then the translation. Notice how Al-Ash'ari quotes the Quran and then explains the implication.
The passage includes two verses; observe the logical connection.
2
Identify the logical structure
The passage uses a conditional argument: if God had willed no fighting, it would not happen. The occurrence of fighting proves His will. Then it reinforces with a second verse.
3
Reflect on the doctrinal implication
Consider why this proof matters: if God does not will evil, how can evil occur? Al-Ash'ari affirms that God wills all events, including sinful acts.
This connects to the broader debate on divine decree and human responsibility.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : قال الله عز وجل : ﴿ وَلَوْ شَاءَ اللهُ مَا اقْتَتَلُوا ﴾ فأخبر أنه لو شاء أن لا يقتتلوا ما اقتتلوا، قال: ﴿ وَلَكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ ﴾ [البقرة: ٢٥٣] من القتال، فإذا وقع القتال فقد شاءه، كما أنه قال: ﴿ وَلَوْ رُدُّوا لَعَادُوا لِمَا نُهُوا عَنْهُ ﴾ [الأنعام : ٢٨]
And it is said to them: God, mighty and majestic, said: 'And if God had willed, they would not have fought.' So He informed that if He willed that they not fight, they would not fight. He said: 'But God does what He wills' regarding the fighting. So when fighting occurs, He has willed it. Just as He said: 'And if they were returned, they would return to what they were forbidden.'
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This final proof uses direct Quranic verses to establish that God wills the evil acts of His creatures. The verse 'If God had willed, they would not have fought' implies that since fighting does occur, God must have willed it. Another verse states 'God does what He wills,' confirming that all events are by His will. The passage also quotes 'If they were returned, they would return to what they were forbidden,' indicating persistent sin by God's will. Thus, God wills folly (sinful acts) but is not a fool.

Key Terms
شاء
يريد
قتال
نهي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God wills something evil, He must be evil or foolish.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari affirms that God wills evil acts like fighting, but He is not evil; His wisdom and justice are beyond human comprehension.

Checkpoint
What does the verse 'if God had willed, they would not have fought' indicate?
A) God does not will fighting
B) Fighting occurs contrary to God's will
C) God wills the fighting that occurs
D) Fighting is beyond God's control

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari proves that God wills all events, including sinful acts, using Quranic conditional logic.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you reflect on a sin you have committed repeatedly, do you truly believe that God willed that sin to happen? How does that belief affect your attitude toward repentance? Will you today ask God for forgiveness with the certainty that He willed it and yet forgives?
?
Consider a recent trial or conflict you witnessed. Can you recite the verse 'ولو شاء الله ما اقتتلوا' and affirm that God willed that conflict? What does that trust in God's will cost you emotionally? Will you submit to His wisdom even when you don't understand?
?
The verse 'ولو ردوا لعادوا لما نهوا عنه' exposes the human tendency to return to sin. What is that one sin you know you would return to if given the chance? How does knowing that God wills even that drive you to rely on Him more desperately? Will you take one concrete step today to break that cycle?

Divine Attributes

4 min
Comparison of Willing Obedience and Willing Folly
Al-Ash'ari argues by analogy: if willing obedience does not make God obedient, then willing folly does not make Him foolish.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses an analogy to defend divine transcendence by distinguishing between God's will and creaturely attributes.

Core Idea
God's willing something does not make Him possess that attribute.

Al-Ash'ari presents a simple but powerful argument. He points out that in human experience, willing an action makes us described by that action, but this does not apply to God. This lesson explores how he uses this parallel to rebut the charge that God's will for folly would make Him foolish.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the parallel structure: 'as... so...'.
2
Understand the human analogy
Identify the two human examples: willing obedience leads to being obedient; willing folly leads to being a fool. This is the baseline comparison.
3
Apply the analogy to God
Now see how Al-Ash'ari applies the same logic to God: He wills obedience but is not obedient; He wills folly but is not a fool. Ask yourself why the analogy holds.
The key is that God's will is for others, not for Himself.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم من أراد طاعة الله منا كان مطيعاً، كما أن من أراد السفه كان سفيهاً، ورب العالمين عز وجل يريد الطاعة وليس مطيعاً، فكذلك يريد السفه، وليس سفيهاً .
And it is said to them: Whoever among us intends obedience to God becomes obedient, just as whoever intends folly becomes a fool. Yet the Lord of the worlds, mighty and majestic, intends obedience and is not obedient. Likewise, He intends folly and is not a fool.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This is a concise argument (hujja) using parallelism. In humans, willing an action (obedience or folly) leads to being described by that action. But God wills that humans obey Him, yet He is not described as 'obedient' because obedience is a creaturely action. Similarly, when He wills foolishness (i.e., that a creature commit folly), He is not described as foolish because folly is a creaturely attribute. Thus the attribution of a state to the one who wills it does not transfer to God when He wills it for others. This preserves divine transcendence.

Key Terms
الطاعة (al-ta'a)
السفه (al-safah)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God wills folly, He must be foolish.
Correction: God's willing folly for others does not make Him foolish, just as His willing obedience does not make Him obedient.

Checkpoint
Why is God not called obedient despite willing obedience from humans?
A) Because obedience is impossible for God
B) Because obedience is a creaturely act, not a divine attribute
C) Because God only wills disobedience
D) Because God is above all attributes

Key Takeaway
God's will for creaturely actions does not entail that He possesses those attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God’s willing obedience does not make Him obedient, what does that reveal about your own acts of worship? Are you ever tempted to think your obedience obligates God or makes Him 'owe' you something? How must you adjust your heart to pure servitude?
?
When you see foolishness and evil in the world, have you ever secretly questioned God’s wisdom? This lesson says God wills folly but is not foolish. Can you trust His wisdom even when you don’t understand? Identify one current event that tests your trust and commit to repeating this argument to yourself.
?
This lesson draws a sharp line between Creator and creature. In what subtle ways do you still blur that line—for example, expecting God to act like a disappointed human parent or to 'get angry' like you do? Write down one specific habit of thought you will abandon today to honor His transcendence.

6 min
Refutation using verse on divine will and guidance
Al-Ash'ari refutes opponents using the verse about filling Hell.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses Qur'an 32:13 to refute the claim that Allah could have compelled all people to guidance, and why coerced faith is not true guidance.

Core Idea
Coerced faith is no faith at all.

Al-Ash'ari addresses a key verse that opponents use to argue that if Allah willed, He could have forced everyone to believe. He shows that this interpretation misunderstands divine will and human responsibility. Through logical questioning and the example of Pharaoh, he demonstrates that true guidance cannot be forced.

Guided Path
1
Read the verse and the opponents' interpretation
Read the Arabic passage and translation. Note how the opponents reinterpret the verse to mean coercion.
The verse is from Surat al-Sajdah (32:13).
2
Analyze the first rebuttal: consistency of divine action
Follow Al-Ash'ari's argument: if coercion leads to guidance, then causing disbelief would lead to disbelief, which undermines the opponents' claim that only a disbeliever does disbelief.
3
Examine the second rebuttal: the example of Pharaoh
See how Pharaoh's coerced faith at drowning was not accepted, showing that coercion does not benefit.
Pharaoh's statement at drowning is mentioned in Qur'an 10:90-91.
4
Understand the conclusion
Realize that the verse does not support coercion; Allah's decree is based on wisdom, and coerced guidance does not remove punishment.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : قال الله عز وجل: ﴿ وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَا نَيْنَا كُلَّ نَفْسٍ هُدَنهَا وَلَكِن حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ مِي لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ ﴾ [السجدة : ۱۳] ، وإذا حق القول بذلك فما شاء أن تؤتي كل نفس هداها ؛ لأنه إذا لم يؤتها هداها لمـا حق القول بتعذيب الكافرين، وإذا لم يرد ذلك فقد شاء ضلالتها •
فإن قالوا: معنى ذلك لو شئنا لأجبرناهم على الهدى واضطررناهم إليه
قيل لهم : فإذا أجبرهم على الهدى واضطرهم إليه أيكونون مهتدين؟ فإن قالوا: نعم .
قيل لهم : فإذا كان إذا فعل الهدى كانوا مهتدين، فما أنكرتم لو فعل كفر الكافرين لكانوا كافرين، وهذا هدم لقولهم ؛ لأنهم زعموا أنه لا يفعل الكفر إلا كافر .
ويقال لهم أيضاً على أي وجه يؤتيهم الهدى لـو آتاهم إياه، وشاء ذلك لهم؟
فإن قالوا على الإلجاء .
قيل لهم : وإذا ألجأهم إلى ذلك هل ينفعهم ما يفعلونه على طريق الإلجاء؟ فإن قالوا: لا
قيل لهم : فإذا أخبر أنه لو شاء لآتاهم الهدى لولا ما حق منه من القول أنه يملأ جهنم، وإذا كان لو ألجأهم لم يكن نافعاً لهم ولا مزيلاً للعـذاب عنهم، كما لم ينفع فرعون قوله الذي قاله عند الغرق والإلجاء، فلا معنى لقولكم، لأنه لولا ما حق من القول لأوتيت كل نفس هداها، وإتيان الهدى على الوجه الذي قلتموه لا يزيل العذاب .
And it is said to them: Allah Almighty said: 'And if We had willed, We would have given every soul its guidance, but the word from Me has become true: I will surely fill Hell with jinn and people all together.' [Sajdah 32:13]. And when the word is true about that, He did not will to give every soul its guidance; because if He did not give it its guidance, the word about punishing the disbelievers became true; and if He did not will that, then He willed their misguidance. So if they say: the meaning of that is: if We had willed, We would have compelled them to guidance and forced them to it. It is said to them: if He compelled them to guidance and forced them to it, would they be guided? If they say: yes. It is said to them: if when He does the guidance they would be guided, what prevents you from saying that if He did the disbelief of the disbelievers they would be disbelievers? And this destroys their statement, because they claimed that only a disbeliever does disbelief. And it is also said to them: in what way would He give them guidance if He gave it to them and willed that for them? If they say: by coercion. It is said to them: and if He coerced them to that, would what they do by way of coercion benefit them? If they say: no. It is said to them: when He informed that if He willed He would give them guidance, were it not for the word that became true from Him that He will fill Hell, and if He coerced them it would not be beneficial for them nor remove the punishment from them, just as the statement of Pharaoh that he said at the time of drowning and coercion did not benefit him. So your statement has no meaning, because were it not for the word that became true, every soul would have been given its guidance; and giving guidance in the way you mentioned does not remove punishment.
Source pages p. 130

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer argument. Al-Ash'ari cites Qur'an 32:13, where Allah says that if He willed, He would give every soul guidance, but His decree to fill Hell prevents that. The opponents interpret this as meaning that Allah could have coerced people to guidance. Al-Ash'ari replies by asking whether such coercion would actually make them guided; if they say yes, then consistency would require that if Allah caused disbelief in disbelievers, they would be disbelievers—undermining the opponents' claim that only a disbeliever commits disbelief. Then Al-Ash'ari questions the mode of giving guidance: if by coercion, would it benefit them? The opponents admit it would not, using Pharaoh as an example whose coerced faith at drowning was not accepted. Therefore, the verse's meaning is not about coercion, but about Allah's will and decree. The argument demonstrates that divine will and knowledge determine outcomes, not human coercion.

Key Terms
الهدى
الإلجاء
حق القول
تعذيب الكافرين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that Allah's statement 'if We willed' implies that He could have compelled everyone to guidance, and that such coercion would be effective.
Correction: The passage shows that coerced faith is not true guidance and does not remove punishment, as demonstrated by the example of Pharaoh.

Checkpoint
What does Al-Ash'ari use the example of Pharaoh to demonstrate?
A) That Pharaoh was a true believer at drowning.
B) That coerced faith is not accepted by Allah.
C) That Allah willed Pharaoh's guidance.
D) That the verse applies only to Pharaoh.

Key Takeaway
Allah's will does not negate human responsibility; coerced faith is not true guidance and does not avert punishment.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever gone through the motions of worship out of habit or fear, without genuine submission? How does this passage expose the emptiness of such faith before Allah?
?
If you have been guided, do you recognize that it is solely by Allah's will, not your own merit? How does this truth humble you and change your attitude toward others?
?
Do you ever judge others' faith based on their outward actions, forgetting that true guidance is from Allah? How can you practically remind yourself that coerced or superficial faith is not what Allah accepts?

Divine Will and the Withholding of Provision
Al-Ash'ari constructs an analogy from two verses to argue for divine will and knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses two Qur'anic verses to argue that Allah's withholding of provision and worldly ornament is based on His knowledge of human tendencies, and that this parallels His decision not to create disbelievers.

Core Idea
Allah withholds because He knows what we would become.

Al-Ash'ari presents a subtle argument from divine action. He cites two verses that show Allah restrains Himself from granting certain worldly gifts because He knows the disastrous spiritual consequences. This lesson unpacks the logic: if Allah's actions were arbitrary, He would not bother to withhold. But He does, precisely because His will is guided by His omniscience.

Guided Path
1
Read the two verses carefully
Read the two Qur'anic verses cited by Al-Ash'ari. Note what each says about Allah's action and the hypothetical condition attached. Let the verses speak before you interpret.
Verse 42:27 talks about provision; 43:33 about silver ceilings.
2
Trace the analogy
Al-Ash'ari connects the two examples to the question of creating disbelievers. Identify how the logic of withholding in the verses is parallel to Allah's decision regarding creation.
3
Consider the underlying principle
Why does this argument refute those who deny that Allah's will determines creation? The key is Allah's knowledge of consequences. Reflect on how this establishes divine will and omniscience.
This addresses the misconception that withholding is arbitrary.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : قال الله عز وجل : ( وَلَوْ بَسَط اللهُ الرِّزْقَ لِعِبَادِهِ، لَبَغَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ ) [الشورى: ۲۷] وقال تعالى: ﴿ وَلَوْلَا أَن يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً لَجَعَلْنَا لِمَن يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمَنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقفا من فِضَّةٍ ﴾ [الزخرف:۳۳] مخبراً أنه لولا أن يكون الناس مجتمعين على الكفر لبسط للكافرين الرزق، وجعل لبيوتهم سقفاً من فضة، لكنه لم يبسط لهم الرزق، ولم يجعل للكافرين سقفاً من فضة، فمـا أنكرتم من أنه تعالى لو لم يرد أن يكون الكافرون ما خلقه، علمه بأنه إذا خلقهم كانوا كافرين، كما أنه لو أراد أن يكون الناس على الكفر مجتمعين لجعل للكافرين سقفاً من فضة ومعارج عليها يظهرون ؛ لكنه لم يجعل للكافرين سقفاً سقفاً من فضة ومعارج عليها يظهرون، لئلا يكونوا الناس جميعاً على الكفر متطابقين، إذا كان في علمه أنه لو فعل ذلك لكانوا جميعاً على الكفر متطابقين
And it is said to them: Allah Almighty said: 'And if Allah had extended provision for His servants, they would have transgressed in the land.' [Shura 42:27] and He said: 'And if not that mankind would become one nation, We would have made for those who disbelieve in the Most Merciful, for their houses, ceilings of silver.' [Zukhruf 43:33] informing that were it not that people would unite upon disbelief, He would have extended provision to the disbelievers and made for their houses ceilings of silver, but He did not extend provision to them, and did not make for the disbelievers ceilings of silver. So what prevents you from saying that He, exalted, if He did not will that the disbelievers exist, He would have created them, [with] His knowledge that if He created them they would be disbelievers, just as if He wished that people be united upon disbelief, He would have made for the disbelievers ceilings of silver and stairways upon which they ascend; but He did not make for the disbelievers ceilings of silver and stairways upon which they ascend, lest all people might become united upon disbelief, since it is in His knowledge that if He did that they would all be united upon disbelief.
Source pages p. 131

Commentary
This passage is an argument from divine action based on Qur'anic verses. Al-Ash'ari cites two verses: one stating that if Allah expanded provision, people would transgress; another stating that if not for the fear that humanity would become one disbelieving nation, Allah would have given disbelievers silver ceilings and stairways. The point is that Allah withholds these worldly gifts because of His knowledge that if He granted them, humans would become completely united in disbelief. Al-Ash'ari analogizes: similarly, if Allah did not will disbelievers to exist, He would not create them, because He knows they would become disbelievers. The argument shows that Allah's actions are based on His prior knowledge and will, and that He prevents certain outcomes because of what He knows would result. This refutes opponents who deny that Allah's will determines creation and disbelief.

Key Terms
بسط الرزق
أمة واحدة
سقف من فضة
علمه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Allah's withholding of provision is arbitrary or due to meanness.
Correction: Allah withholds based on His perfect knowledge that granting would lead to transgression or universal disbelief.

Checkpoint
According to Al-Ash'ari, why does Allah not give disbelievers silver ceilings?
A) Because they do not deserve them.
B) Because it would cause all people to unite in disbelief.
C) Because silver is forbidden.
D) Because the verse is not literal.

Key Takeaway
Allah's actions, including withholding provision, are determined by His knowledge of what would happen if He acted otherwise.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of a blessing you have asked Allah for but He has not given you. Could it be that His withholding is based on His knowledge that you would misuse it? What does that reveal about your own heart's readiness for that blessing? Will you trust His knowledge over your desire?
?
When you see others who have wealth and comfort yet are far from Allah, do you feel envy or do you remember that Allah withholds from some precisely because He knows what would become of them? How does this passage change the way you view those who have less—and those who have more?
?
If Allah had expanded your provision in a way that led you to transgression, would you have been grateful or would you have forgotten Him? Look at what you have now. Is it enough to make you turn to Him in gratitude, or does it make you want more? What does your current attitude say about whether you deserve to have your provision expanded?

Answer to the Objection on the Creation of Falsehood
Al-Ash'ari contextualizes the verse to refute an objection.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari refutes the claim that the verse 'not created in vain' implies Allah does not create falsehood, by contextualizing it as referring to resurrection and recompense.

Core Idea
The verse's purpose is to affirm judgment, not deny Allah's creation of evil.

In this passage, Al-Ash'ari tackles an objection raised by those who argue that a Quranic verse proves Allah did not create falsehood. He explains that the verse actually refutes the polytheists' denial of the afterlife and affirms that creation has a purpose: recompense. By reading the verse in its full context, the objection dissolves.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection and verse
Read the Arabic passage carefully. The objectors cite Surah Sad 27: 'And We did not create the heaven and the earth and what is between them in vain.' They claim this proves Allah did not create falsehood.
Note the key term 'باطل' (falsehood) in the objection.
2
Identify Al-Ash'ari's answer
Al-Ash'ari says the verse was revealed to deny the polytheists who denied resurrection. The meaning is that creation was not without purpose; rather, there will be reward and punishment.
He reinterprets 'in vain' as 'without purpose', not 'without falsehood'.
3
See the supporting verse
Al-Ash'ari quotes Sad 28: 'Or should We make those who believe and do righteous deeds like the corrupters...' to show that believers and disbelievers will not be treated equally after resurrection.
The verse confirms that judgment is the purpose of creation.
4
Understand the refutation
The objection fails because the verse does not deny creation of falsehood; it affirms that creation has a purpose, which includes distinguishing between good and evil through recompense.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإن سألوا عن قول الله عز وجل: ﴿وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاء وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بينهما بطلا ) [ ص: ۲۷] فقالوا: هذه الآية تدل على أن الله عز وجل لم يخلق الباطل. والجواب عن ذلك : أن الله عز وجل أراد تكذيب المشركين الذين :قالوا لا حشر ولا نشور ولا إعادة، فقال تعالى: ما خلقت ذلك، وأنا لا أثيب من أطاعني ولا أعاقب من عصاني، كما ظن الكافرون أنه لا حشر ولا نشور ولا ثواب ولا عقاب ألا تراه قال: ﴿ذَلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النار ) [ص: ۲۷] . وبين ذلك بقوله : أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ ) [ ص: ۲۸] أي: لا نسوي بينهم في أن نفنيهم أجمعين ونعيدهم فيكون سبيلهم سبيلاً واحداً .
And if they ask about the saying of Allah Mighty and Majestic: 'And We did not create the heaven and the earth and what is between them in vain' (Sad 27). They said: This verse indicates that Allah Mighty and Majestic did not create falsehood. And the answer to that: That Allah Mighty and Majestic intended to deny the polytheists who said there is no gathering, no resurrection, no return. So He Exalted said: I did not create that, and I will not reward those who obey Me nor punish those who disobey Me, as the disbelievers thought that there is no gathering, no resurrection, no reward and no punishment. Do you not see He said: 'That is the assumption of those who disbelieve, so woe to those who disbelieve from the Fire' (Sad 27). And He clarified that by His saying: 'Or should We make those who believe and do righteous deeds like the corrupters in the earth, or should We make the righteous like the wicked?' (Sad 28). That is: We do not make them equal in that We annihilate them all together and return them, so their way becomes one way.
Source pages p. 136

Commentary
This passage is another objection-and-answer (مسألة). The objectors claim that the verse 'We did not create the heaven and the earth in vain' (Sad 27) proves that Allah did not create falsehood (باطل) — by which they mean disbelief, evil, etc. Therefore, disbelief is not created by Allah. Al-Ash'ari answers by explaining the context. The verse was revealed to refute the polytheists who denied resurrection and judgment. The meaning is that Allah did not create the heavens and earth without purpose; rather, He will recompense believers and disbelievers differently. The verse continues (Sad 28) asking rhetorically whether believers and evil-doers will be made equal. Al-Ash'ari explains that this means they will not be treated identically after resurrection. Thus the verse is not about the non-creation of evil, but about the wisdom of creation and the necessity of judgment. Therefore the objection is invalid.

Key Terms
باطل
حشر
نشور
ثواب
عقاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The verse 'not created in vain' proves that Allah did not create falsehood (evil, disbelief).
Correction: The verse actually means that creation has a purpose, which includes resurrection and judgment, not that falsehood is uncreated.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, what false belief did the polytheists hold that the verse in Sad refutes?
A) That Allah created evil
B) That there is no resurrection or recompense
C) That the heavens and earth were created for play
D) That Allah does not create anything

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari demonstrates that a verse must be understood in its full context, not isolated, to correctly interpret its meaning regarding divine attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read a verse like 'not created in vain,' do you ever use it to avoid taking responsibility for your actions, assuming your evil deeds are not part of Allah's plan? How does Al-Ash'ari's contextual reading challenge you to instead see that every action will be judged?
?
Think of a time you misinterpreted a verse to fit your own desire. Will you now commit to always seeking the full context before drawing conclusions about Allah's attributes, even when it is uncomfortable?
?
The polytheists denied judgment to justify their lifestyle. What lies do you tell yourself to avoid accountability, and how does this lesson expose them? Will you repent and change today?

Reconciling the Verses on Good and Evil
Resolves apparent contradiction between two Quranic verses on source of good and evil.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Quran itself resolves the apparent contradiction between attributing all things to Allah and attributing evil to human actions.

Core Idea
The verse blaming the self for evil is a quotation, not a divine decree.

Sometimes two Quranic verses seem to say opposite things. One says everything is from Allah, another says evil is from yourself. This lesson shows how Imam al-Ash'ari resolves this by uncovering the context: the second verse is actually quoting the disbelievers' claim, not making a statement of fact. Through careful reading of the surrounding verses, the unity of divine sovereignty is preserved.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection and answer
Read the Arabic passage once to grasp the flow: an objection is raised using 4:79, then an answer is provided by quoting 4:78 and explaining the context.
Notice that the author uses the Quran itself to resolve the apparent contradiction.
2
Identify the key Quranic verses
The author cites two verses: 4:78 ('All is from Allah') and 4:79 ('Good from Allah, evil from yourself'). See how he shows that 4:79 is actually a quotation of what the disbelievers say.
The key insight is that the Quran omits the phrase 'in their saying' because the preceding context makes it clear.
3
Understand the principle of Quranic consistency
The author argues that the Quran cannot contradict itself. Therefore, both verses must be harmonized. Reflect on why this principle is essential for sound theology.
This shows the importance of tafsir in resolving apparent conflicts.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٢١٦ - مسألة : وإن سألوا عن قول الله عز وجل: مَا أَصَابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ وَمَا أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ ) [النساء : ٧٩] . والجواب عن ذلك: أن الله عز وجل قال: ﴿وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ ) يعني الخصب والخير، ﴿ يَقُولُوا هَذِهِ مِنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ ) يعني الجدب والقحط والمصائب، ﴿ يَقُولُواْ هَذِهِ مِنْ عِندِكَ ) أي لشؤمك، قال الله تعالى: يا محمد ( قُلْ كُلٌّ مِنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ فَمَا لِهَؤُلَاءِ الْقَوْمِ لَا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثًا ) [النساء :۷۸] في قولهم : مَا أَصَابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ وَمَا أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ ) فحذف قولهم؛ لأن ما تقدم من الكلام يدل عليه؛ لأن القرآن لا يتناقض، ولا يجوز أن يقول في آية إن الكل من عند الله، ثم يقول في الآية الأخرى التي تليها إن الكل ليس من عند الله، على أن ما أصاب الناس هو غير ما أصابوه وهذا يبين بطلان تعلقهم بهذه الآية، ويوجب عليهم الحجة
216 - Issue: If they ask about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: 'Whatever good befalls you is from Allah, and whatever evil befalls you is from yourself' [An-Nisa 4:79]. And the answer to that: That Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'And if good befalls them' meaning abundance and goodness, 'they say, "This is from Allah," and if evil befalls them' meaning drought, famine, and calamities, 'they say, "This is from you"' meaning from your ill omen. Allah said: 'O Muhammad, say: "All is from Allah." What is wrong with these people that they hardly understand any statement?' [An-Nisa 4:78] regarding their saying: 'Whatever good befalls you is from Allah, and whatever evil befalls you is from yourself.' So He omitted their statement because the preceding speech indicates it; because the Quran does not contradict itself, and it is not permissible that it says in one verse that all is from Allah, then says in the other verse that follows it that all is not from Allah. Moreover, what afflicts people is different from what they themselves do? And this demonstrates the invalidity of their clinging to this verse, and it makes the argument binding upon them.
Source pages p. 137

Commentary
This passage presents an objection-and-answer regarding the apparent contradiction between two verses: one attributing evil to the self and the other stating all is from God. The author resolves this by explaining that the verse in question (4:79) is actually a quotation of the disbelievers' words, not a direct statement from God. He shows that the preceding context in 4:78 already establishes that all is from God, and the Quran does not contradict itself. Therefore, those who claim that evil is not from God are refuted, and the argument is binding upon them.

Key Terms
حسنة (good)
سيئة (evil)
خصب (abundance)
قحط (drought)
شؤم (ill omen)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that Quran 4:79 proves that evil is entirely from human actions and not from Allah, contradicting other verses that say all is from Allah.
Correction: The author shows that 4:79 is a quotation of what the disbelievers say, not a direct divine statement. The context in 4:78 makes this clear, so there is no contradiction.

Checkpoint
What is the main reason the author gives for understanding that the verse 'ما أصابك من حسنة فمن الله وما أصابك من سيئة فمن نفسك' does not contradict 'قل كل من عند الله'?
A) The Quran contains contradictions
B) The verse about evil from the self is a quote of the disbelievers' words
C) The verse about evil refers only to minor sins
D) The verse was abrogated

Key Takeaway
The verse attributing evil to the self is a quotation of the disbelievers' words, harmonizing with Allah's statement that all is from Him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When a trial enters your life, is your first inward reaction to blame someone—your circumstances, another person, even yourself—rather than acknowledging it from Allah? What would it cost you to truly say, 'All is from Allah' in that moment?
?
The disbelievers attributed the Prophet's presence as an ill omen for their calamities. Have you ever secretly resented a believer because of a hardship you associated with them? How can this lesson purge that hidden shirk from your heart?
?
If you genuinely believed that every single evil—every loss, pain, and disappointment—is from Allah’s decree, what would change about your response to the next calamity? Are you willing to thank Him for it before you see any good in it?

God Can Command the Impossible: The Prostration Argument
Imam al-Ash'ari uses the Day of Judgment prostration to argue that God can command what is impossible.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses the Day of Judgment prostration to argue that God can command what is impossible, refuting the Qadariyya.

Core Idea
God's command does not require that He grant ability.

This passage addresses a classic theological question: If God commands something, must He also give the ability to obey? Imam al-Ash'ari presents a proof from the Qur'an and hadith that on the Day of Judgment, hypocrites will be commanded to prostrate but will be unable. This demonstrates that God's command is not contingent on human ability.

Guided Path
1
Read the source text carefully
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Note the verse (al-Qalam 42) and the hadith about the hypocrites. Let the Imam's own words set the stage.
Pay close attention to the phrase 'فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ' (they will not be able).
2
Unpack the argument
The Imam argues: God commands prostration, but the hypocrites are unable. Therefore, God can command what is impossible. This refutes the Qadariyya who say ability must accompany command.
Consider: Does this mean God commands the impossible in this life? Or only in the Hereafter?
3
Reflect on the theological implication
Think about what this means for God's power and human responsibility. If command does not imply ability, then human ability is not a constraint on God's commands.
This is a key point in the Ash'ari view of divine omnipotence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۲۱۹- جواب : ويقال لهم : أليس قد قال الله عز وجل: (يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَن سَاقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ) [القلم : ٤٢] أليس قد أمرهم الله عز وجل بالسجود في الآخرة؟ وجاء في الخبر : ( أن المنافقين يجعل في أصلابهم كالصفائح فلا يستطيعون السجود)، وفي هذا تثبيت لما نقوله من أنه لا يجب لهم على الله عز وجل إذا أمرهم أن يقدرهم، وهو بطلان قول القدرية .
Answer: And it is said to them: Did not Allah, Blessed and Exalted, say: 'The day when the shin shall be uncovered and they shall be called to prostrate, but they will not be able to' (al-Qalam: 42). Did He not command them to prostrate in the Hereafter? And it came in the report: 'The hypocrites will have something like iron sheets placed in their backs, so they will not be able to prostrate.' In this is confirmation of what we say: that there is no obligation upon Allah, if He commands them, to enable them. And this invalidates the saying of the Qadariyya.
Source pages p. 138

Commentary
This passage is a response to the objection that God's command implies ability. The Imam cites a verse and a hadith showing that on the Day of Judgment, God will command hypocrites to prostrate, but they will be physically unable. This proves that God can command what is impossible (or at least that ability is not a necessary concomitant of command). The conclusion is that the Qadariyya (who affirm free will and that God only commands what one can do) are refuted.

Key Terms
يكشف عن ساق
السجود
القدرية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the verse is purely metaphorical; the Imam uses it literally to make a theological point about God's power.
Correction: Whether literal or metaphorical, the argument stands: God's command does not necessitate enabling. The verse and hadith are taken as factual in this context.

Checkpoint
What does the hadith say about the hypocrites' prostration?
A) They will have sheets of iron in their backs.
B) They will be given strength.
C) They will be forgiven.
D) They will be turned into animals.

Key Takeaway
God's command does not impose an obligation on Him to grant ability; this refutes the Qadariyya.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly submit to the reality that God may command you something you cannot do, yet hold you accountable? How does this challenge your sense of fairness before Him?
?
When you face a command of God that seems impossible, do you rely on your own ability or on His mercy? What does this verse teach you about your dependence on Him?
?
If God does not owe you the ability to obey, what does that say about your need for His grace in every act of worship? Are you crying out to Him for that grace?

God's Command to Abu Lahab: Divine Foreknowledge and Ability
Imam Ash'ari refutes Mu'tazilite objection using Abu Lahab example.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari uses the case of Abu Lahab to argue that God can command what He knows will not happen, refuting the Mu'tazilite objection that divine commands require human ability to comply.

Core Idea
God's command does not depend on human ability to comply.

Imam Ash'ari responds to the Mu'tazilite objection that God only commands what humans can do. He cites the case of Abu Lahab, whom Allah commanded to believe despite revealing that he would not. This lesson examines that argument and its implications for divine command and human free will.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to grasp the argument's flow. Notice the Quranic citation at the start.
The passage is a jawāb (answer) to an unstated objection.
2
Identify the unstated objection
The passage presupposes a Mu'tazilite objection: that God cannot command what He knows will not be done. Try to reframe the objection in your own words before seeing the author's response.
This objection is rooted in Mu'tazilite theology about divine justice and human capacity.
3
Analyze the logical steps
Break down the argument into steps: the Quranic verse, the command, the divine knowledge, and the incompatibility of faith with certain knowledge of non-occurrence. Map how each step leads to the conclusion.
The argument is compact—each step builds on the previous.
4
Consider the broader implication
Reflect on what this argument means for the relationship between divine omniscience, command, and human ability. How does this differ from the Mu'tazilite view?
This demonstrates that divine commands are not contingent on human ability to comply.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۲۲۱- جواب : ويقال لهم: أليس قد قال الله تبارك وتعالى: (تَبَّتْ يَدَا أَبِي لَهَبٍ وَتَبَّ * مَا أَغْنَى عَنْهُ مَالُهُ وَمَا كَسَبَ * سَيَصْلَى نَارًا ذَاتَ هَبِ﴾ [المسد : ١-٣] وأمره مع ذلك بالإيمان، فأوجب عليه أن يعلم أنه لا يؤمن، وأن الله صادق في إخباره عنـه أنـه لا يؤمن، وأمره مع ذلك أن يؤمن ولا يجتمع الإيمان والعلم بأنه لا يكون ولا يقدر القادر على أن يؤمن، وأن يعلم أنه لا يؤمن، وإذا كان هذا هكذا، فقد أمر الله سبحانه أبا لهب بما لا يقدر عليه؛ لأنه أمره أن يؤمن، وأنه يعلم أنه لا يؤمن .
221 - Answer: And it is said to them: Has not Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said: 'Perish the hands of Abu Lahab and perish! His wealth and what he earned availed him not. He will burn in a fire of flame' [Al-Masad 1-3] and He commanded him to believe despite that, so He necessitated that He knows that he will not believe, and that Allah is truthful in His report about him that he will not believe, and He commanded him to believe despite that, and faith cannot coexist with knowledge that it will not occur, nor can the one who is able to believe while knowing that he will not believe. If this is the case, then Allah, Glorified, has commanded Abu Lahab to do what he is incapable of; because He commanded him to believe while He knows that he will not believe.
Source pages p. 139

Commentary
This passage is a response (jawāb) to an unstated objection, likely from the Mu'tazila, who argue that God cannot command what He knows will not be done because that would be commanding the impossible. The author cites the case of Abu Lahab: Allah revealed surah Al-Masad, which states that Abu Lahab will perish and enter Hellfire, yet despite that, Allah commanded him to believe. The argument proceeds: since Allah's knowledge is certain and He reported that Abu Lahab will not believe, it follows that Allah knew he would not believe. Faith (īmān) cannot coexist with the certain knowledge that it will not occur. Therefore, Allah commanded Abu Lahab to believe while knowing he would not. The conclusion is that this is a case where God commanded something that He knew would not happen. The author's point is that this does not detract from God's justice or power; rather it highlights that divine commands are not contingent on human ability in the Mu'tazilite sense. This refutes the Mu'tazilite claim that God only commands what the human is capable of doing in a way that is possible.

Key Terms
أبو لهب
الإيمان
علم الله
أمر
قدرة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God cannot command what He knows will not happen because that would be commanding the impossible.
Correction: This passage shows that God can and does command what He knows will not happen, as in the case of Abu Lahab. The command does not depend on human ability; divine omniscience does not limit divine command.

Checkpoint
What is the example of Abu Lahab used to demonstrate?
A) That God does not command disbelief.
B) That God can command something He knows will not happen.
C) That Abu Lahab was capable of believing.
D) That faith and knowledge are the same.

Key Takeaway
God's command does not require that the commanded action be possible for the human; divine omniscience does not limit divine command.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have read that Allah commanded Abu Lahab to believe despite knowing he never would. Are there commands of Allah in your own life that you find difficult or impossible to follow? How does this passage challenge your assumption that your ability to obey determines the validity of Allah's command? Will you continue to excuse yourself, or will you submit to the command regardless of your feelings?
?
When you hear the story of Abu Lahab, do you feel a sense of relief that you are not doomed like him? Or do you feel an urgent need to examine your own response to Allah's commands? What command of Allah are you currently resisting because you think you cannot do it, and what would it look like to obey anyway?
?
Allah's command to Abu Lahab was a test and a proof. What command of Allah is testing you right now? Instead of focusing on your own perceived inability, how can you trust that the command itself is good and that Allah will enable you if He wills? What step can you take today to act on that command, even if you feel incapable?

The Qadariyya Dilemma on God's Command
Al-Ash'ari presents a dialectical argument against the Qadariyya using a dilemma.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses a logical dilemma to refute the Qadariyya position on God's command and predestination.

Core Idea
God's knowledge is infallible; no one can escape what He knows.

Al-Ash'ari presents a sharp dilemma to those who deny predestination. He asks a simple question about God's command to those He knows will not believe. The opponent's answer leads either to agreement with the Ash'ari or to an absurd claim that humans can escape divine knowledge. This short passage cuts to the heart of the debate on qadar.

Guided Path
1
Read the dilemma
Read the Arabic passage and translation. Notice the structure: a question, two possible answers, and their consequences.
2
Identify the two options
The opponent can say 'No' (agree) or 'Yes' (claim ability). Understand each implication.
3
Reflect on the theological point
The argument shows that God's knowledge is not contingent on human action. It defends divine omniscience against the Qadariyya.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٢٢٢- مسألة ويقال لهم : أليس أمر الله عز وجل بالإيمان من علم أنه لا يؤمن؟ فمن قولهم: نعم، يقال لهم : فأنتم قادرون على الإيمان، ويتأتى لكم وإن قالوا: لا، وافقوا. وإن قالوا: نعم، زعموا أن العباد يقدرون على الخروج من علم الله تعالى الله عز وجل عن ذلك علواً كبيراً .
222 - Issue: And it is said to them: Is not the command of Allah Almighty to faith from one whom He knows will not believe? So from their statement: Yes, it is said to them: Then you are able to believe, and it is possible for you. And if they say: No, they agree. And if they say: Yes, they claim that the servants are able to exit from the knowledge of Allah, exalted is Allah Almighty above that greatly.
Source pages p. 140

Commentary
This passage presents a dialectical argument against those who deny predestination. The questioner asks: Did God command faith to someone He knows will not believe? If the opponent says 'No', they agree with the Ash'ari position that God's command does not contradict His knowledge. If they say 'Yes', then the questioner forces them to admit that humans have the ability to believe even if God knows they will not, which implies that humans can escape God's knowledge. The Ash'ari concludes that this is a great exaltation above Allah, i.e., it is impossible. The argument uses a dilemma to show the inconsistency of the opponent.

Key Terms
قدرية
علم الله
الإيمان
القدر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The argument denies human free will entirely.
Correction: The argument does not deny free will; it affirms that God's knowledge is infallible and cannot be escaped.

Checkpoint
What is the consequence if the opponent says 'Yes' to the question about God commanding faith to those He knows will not believe?
A) They agree with the Ash'ari position.
B) They claim that servants can exit from God's knowledge.
C) They deny human ability.
D) They affirm God's justice.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari uses a dilemma to show that the Qadariyya cannot consistently affirm both God's command and His omniscience.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This dilemma forces you to choose: either you admit that God's command to the one He knows will not believe is genuine, meaning you must accept that God's knowledge is infallible and you cannot escape it. Or you deny that God commands such people, but then you contradict your own belief that He commands all. Which horn of the dilemma do you find yourself in? And what does your choice reveal about your heart's submission to God's knowledge?
?
Al-Ash'ari says that if you affirm that God commands those He knows will not believe, then you claim to be able to escape God's knowledge. Have you ever acted as though you could hide from God's knowledge, knowing that He sees and knows all? How does this verse make you feel about your own attempts to act contrary to what God knows about you?
?
The Qadariyya wanted to protect human responsibility, but Al-Ash'ari shows they end up denying God's attribute of knowledge. Are you sometimes more concerned with defending your own perceived freedom than with affirming God's perfect knowledge? What would it cost you to submit fully to the fact that God knows everything, including your future choices?

The Seal on Hearts and Divine Guidance
Al-Ash'ari questions the Mu'tazila about the seal on hearts and divine guidance.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari uses Quranic verses to argue that God's sealing of hearts contradicts the Mu'tazili claim that God guides all.

Core Idea
God's seal on hearts is real and incompatible with universal guidance.

Al-Ash'ari begins by citing two clear Quranic verses that describe God sealing hearts and making them tight for those He wills to misguide. He then challenges the Mu'tazila with a logical dilemma: if they claim God guides those sealed hearts, they contradict scripture; if they deny it, they admit the seal is genuine. This lesson explores this argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the verses
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the two Quranic verses cited: 2:7 and 6:125. Also note the reference to locks from 47:24.
The verses are given with surah and verse numbers.
2
Understand the first question and its implication
Al-Ash'ari asks: Do you claim that those with sealed hearts were guided? If they answer yes, they contradict the verse because a sealed heart cannot be guided. If no, they concede the seal is real.
This is a classic dilemma used in kalam.
3
Analyze the second question about opened and sealed hearts
He then asks how a breast can be both opened to faith and tight/constricted/sealed. These are incompatible, showing the verses are consistent and the Mu'tazili view is inconsistent.
The locks verse reinforces the idea of obstruction.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٢٢٦- مسألة في الختم : يقال لهم : أليس قد قال الله عز وجل : خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَى قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَى سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلَى أَبْصَرِهِمْ غِشَوَةٌ [البقرة : ۷] وقال عز وجل: فَمَن يُرِدِ اللهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ وَمَن يُرِدْ أَن يُضِلَهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقًا حَرَجًا [الأنعام: ١٢٥] فخبرونا عن الذين ختم الله على قلوبهم وعلى سمعهم ، أتزعمون أنه هداهم وشرح للإسلام صدورهم وأضلهم؟ فإن قالوا: نعم، تناقض قولهم . وقيل لهم : كيف تكون الصدور مشروحة للإيمان وهي ضيقة حرجة مختوم عليها، وكيف يجمع القفل الذي قال الله عز وجل : أَمْ عَلَى قُلُوبٍ أَقْفَالُهَا [محمد : ٢٤]
226 - Issue on the Seal: It is said to them: Has not Allah Almighty said: 'Allah has set a seal upon their hearts and upon their hearing, and over their vision is a veil' [Quran 2:7] and said Almighty: 'And whoever Allah wills to guide, He opens his breast to Islam, and whoever He wills to misguide, He makes his breast tight and constricted' [Quran 6:125]. So inform us about those whom Allah has sealed upon their hearts and upon their hearing, do you claim that He guided them and opened their breasts to Islam and misguided them? So if they say: Yes, then their statement is contradictory. And it is said to them: How can the breasts be opened to faith while they are tight, constricted, and sealed upon? And how can the lock which Allah Almighty mentioned: 'Or are there locks upon their hearts?' [Quran 47:24] be combined?
Source pages p. 141

Commentary
This passage is an argument from the Qur'an against the Mu'tazila who claim that God does not misguide or seal hearts in a way that prevents faith. Al-Ash'ari cites two verses: one explicitly says God seals hearts, hearing, and vision; the other says God makes the breast tight for those He wills to misguide. He then asks the opponent: Do you claim that people with sealed hearts are guided? If they say yes, that is a contradiction because a sealed heart cannot be guided. If they say no, then they concede that God's seal is real and that some are not guided. He further asks how a breast can be both opened to faith and tight/constricted/sealed—these are incompatible. The second verse about locks reinforces the point.

Key Terms
الختم
القلوب
الهدى
الضلال
شرح الصدر
الأقفال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think al-Ash'ari denies human responsibility entirely.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari affirms divine sovereignty over guidance and misguidance, but this does not negate human responsibility; rather it establishes that guidance is by God's will, and the seal is a consequence of their own rejection.

Checkpoint
According to the Quranic verses cited, what happens to the hearts of those whom Allah wills to misguide?
A) They are opened to Islam.
B) They are made tight and constricted.
C) They are filled with faith.
D) They are sealed with the seal of the prophets.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari demonstrates that the Quranic verses on the seal and guidance are logically consistent with divine sovereignty, and any claim of universal guidance contradicts them.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that your own salvation depends entirely on Allah's will, not on your own efforts? How does this verse on the seal challenge your reliance on your own deeds?
?
If Allah has sealed the hearts of some, what urgency does that place on your da'wa? How can you preach to someone whose heart may be sealed?
?
Have you ever considered that you could be among those with sealed hearts? What then is your only hope for guidance? Reflect on your dependence on Allah's mercy.
