ونؤمن بعذاب القبر ، وبالحوض .
وأن الميزان حق ، والصراط حق ، والبعث بعد الموت حق ، وأن الله عز وجل يوقف العباد في الموقف ويحاسب المؤمنين .
وأن الإيمان قول وعمل يزيد وينقص
ونسلم بالروايات الصحيحة عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم التي رواها الثقات عدل عن عدل حتى ينتهي إلى رسول الله .
وندين بحب السلف الذين اختارهم الله عز وجل لصحبة نبيه عليه السلام ونثني عليهم بما أثنى الله به عليهم ونتولاهم أجمعين ونقول : إن الإمام الفاضل بعد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم أبو بكر الصديق رضوان الله عليه .
وأن الله أعز به الدين وأظهره على المرتدين ، وقدمه المسلمون بالإمامة كما قدمه رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم للصلاة ، وسموه جميعهم خليفة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم
ثم عمر بن الخطاب رضي الله عنه .
ثم عثمان بن عفان رضي الله عنه وإن الذين قتلوه قتلوه ظلماً وعدواناً .
ثم علي بن أبي طالب رضي الله عنه .
فهؤلاء الأئمة بعد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم وخلافتهم خلافة النبوة ونشهد بالجنة للعشرة الذين شهد لهم رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم بها ، ونتولی سائر أصحاب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ونكف عما شجر بينهم .
وندين الله بأن الأئمة الأربعة خلفاء راشدون مهديون فضلاء لا يوازيهم في الفضل وغيرهم .
ونصدق جميع الروايات التي يثبتها أهل النقل من النزول إلى السماء الدنيا، وأن الرب عز وجل يقول هل من سائل ، هل من مستغفر ، وبسائر ما نقلوه وأثبتوه خلافاً لما قال أهل الزيغ والتضليل ، ونعول فيما اختلفنا فيه على کتاب ربنا وسنة نبينا وإجماع المسلمين وما كان في معناه : ولا نبتدع في دين الله ما لم يأذن لنا ، ولا نقول على الله ما لا نعلم
ونقول : إن الله عز وجل يجيء يوم القيامة كما قال : ﴿ وَجَاءَ رَبُّكَ وَالْمَلَكُ صَفَا صَفا ) .
وأن الله عز وجل يقرب من عباده كيف شاء كما قال : ﴿ وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ جبل الوريد ) وكما قال : ( ثُمَّ دَنَا فَنَدَكَ * فَكَانَ قَابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنَى )
ومن ديننا أن نصلي الجمعة والأعياد وسائر الصلوات والجماعات خلف کل بر وغيره . كما روي أن عبد الله بن عمر عنهما كان يصلي خلف الحجاج .
And we profess faith in Allah, Mighty and Majestic, that He turns hearts between two fingers of the fingers of Allah, Mighty and Majestic. And that He, Mighty and Majestic, places the heavens and the earth on a finger, and the earths on a finger, as the narration came from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
And we profess that we do not assign anyone of the people of tawhid and those adhering to faith to Paradise or Hell, except for one whom the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, attested to Paradise.
And we hope for Paradise for the sinners and fear for them that they may be punished in the Fire.
•
And we say: Indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will bring out a people from the Fire after they have been charred, by the intercession of the Messenger, in affirmation of what the narrations from the Messenger, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, have brought.
And we believe in the punishment of the grave, and the Basin.
And that the Scale is true, and the Bridge is true, and the resurrection after death is true, and that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will stop the servants at the standing and will reckon the believers.
And that faith is speech and action, it increases and decreases.
And we submit to the authentic narrations from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, which the trustworthy have narrated, trustworthy from trustworthy, until it reaches the Messenger of Allah.
And we hold as religion the love of the Salaf whom Allah, Mighty and Majestic, chose for the companionship of His Prophet, peace be upon him, and we praise them with what Allah praised them with, and we befriend them all, and we say: Indeed the virtuous Imam after the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, is Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the pleasure of Allah be upon him.
And that Allah strengthened the religion through him and manifested him over the apostates, and the Muslims put him forward for leadership as the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, put him forward for prayer, and they all named him the Caliph of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
Then 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him.
Then 'Uthman ibn 'Affan, may Allah be pleased with him, and indeed those who killed him killed him wrongfully and aggressively.
Then 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him.
These are the imams after the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and their caliphate is the caliphate of prophethood. And we testify to Paradise for the ten whom the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, testified to Paradise. And we befriend the rest of the Companions of the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and we refrain from what occurred among them.
And we hold as religion that the four imams are rightly-guided caliphs, guided, virtuous, none equals them in virtue, and others.
And we affirm all the narrations that the people of transmission have established, such as the descent to the lowest heaven, and that the Lord, Mighty and Majestic, says: "Is there anyone asking? Is there anyone seeking forgiveness?" and all that they have transmitted and established, contrary to what the people of deviation and misguidance said. And we rely, in what we differ about, on the Book of our Lord, the Sunnah of our Prophet, the consensus of the Muslims, and what is in its meaning. And we do not introduce innovations in the religion of Allah that which He has not permitted us, nor do we say about Allah what we do not know.
And we say: Indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will come on the Day of Resurrection as He said: "And your Lord comes, and the angels, rank upon rank."
And that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, draws near to His servants as He wills, as He said: "And We are nearer to him than the jugular vein," and as He said: "Then He drew near and came down, and was at a distance of two bow-lengths or nearer."
And it is part of our religion that we pray the Friday prayer, the Eid prayers, and all prayers and congregations behind every pious and impious [ruler], as it was narrated that 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar, may Allah be pleased with both of them, used to pray behind al-Hajjaj.
Source pages p. 24

Commentary
This passage is a continuous creedal statement by Imam al-Ash'ari enumerating the core beliefs of Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah. It follows the method of the Salaf in affirming what is reported without modality or figurative interpretation.

The passage begins by affirming God's absolute control: He turns hearts between two of His fingers, and He places the heavens and earths on a finger, as reported in hadith. This is an affirmation of God's attributes of action and His power, without speculating on how.

Next, al-Ash'ari addresses faith and judgement of believers: we do not definitively assign anyone to Paradise or Hell except those explicitly attested by the Prophet. For sinful believers, we maintain hope of Paradise but fear punishment, balancing hope and fear.

He then affirms intercession: God will bring out from the Fire those who have been charred (i.e., severely punished) through the intercession of the Prophet, in line with authentic hadiths.

Eschatological beliefs are listed: punishment of the grave, the Basin (of the Prophet on the Day of Judgment), the Scale (for weighing deeds), the Bridge (over Hell), resurrection, and the standing before God for reckoning of the believers.

Regarding faith itself, al-Ash'ari defines it as 'speech and action,' and it increases and decreases. This is the Sunni position, opposing the Murji'ah who say faith is only belief and does not increase or decrease.

He then emphasizes acceptance of authentic hadiths transmitted through trustworthy chains, affirming the importance of the Sunni method of hadith criticism.

The passage then turns to the Companions and the Caliphs. We must love the Salaf (the early Muslims, especially the Companions) and praise them as God praised them. The best after the Prophet is Abu Bakr, followed by 'Umar, then 'Uthman (noting his murder was unjust), then 'Ali. These four are the imams and their caliphate is the 'caliphate of prophethood.' He also testifies to Paradise for the ten Companions given glad tidings (the 'ashara mubashshara), refrains from commenting on disputes among Companions, and affirms the superiority of these four imams over all others.

Al-Ash'ari then affirms the accepted narrations about God's descent to the lowest heaven and His asking 'Is there anyone asking?' etc. He rejects the interpretations of those who deny these (the 'people of deviation and misguidance') and states that in matters of disagreement, the source of authority is the Quran, Sunnah, and Ijma'. He warns against innovation and speaking about God without knowledge.

Finally, he affirms that God will come on the Day of Resurrection in a manner befitting Him, and that He draws near to His servants as He wills, citing Quranic verses. The passage concludes with the practical ruling that we pray behind any ruler, whether pious or impious, as 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar did behind al-Hajjaj, a notoriously unjust governor. This demonstrates the Sunni principle of obedience and unity in congregational worship.

Key Terms
الشفاعة
عذاب القبر
الحوض
الميزان
الصراط
البعث
الإيمان قول وعمل
يزيد وينقص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some say faith is only belief in the heart and does not increase or decrease (Murji'ah).
Correction: Al-Ash'ari explicitly says faith is speech and action, and it increases and decreases.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what is the definition of faith?
A) Only belief in the heart
B) Speech and action, it increases and decreases
C) Only good deeds
D) Confession of the tongue without action

Key Takeaway
Faith is both speech and action, and it can increase and decrease; God's mercy includes intercession for sinful believers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You claim to hope for Paradise for sinners, but do you truly fear the punishment you deserve? Which sin have you normalized that you should instead be terrified of facing on the Day your deeds are weighed?
?
Your faith increases and decreases. Be honest: when did it decrease this month? What concrete action will you take tonight to reverse that decline, starting now?
?
You believe in intercession, but that mercy is not cheap. If today were your judgment, what would the Scale show? Are you willing to change one habit this week to tip it toward mercy?

Faith and Disbelief
intermediate
5 min
Love of the Salaf and the Rightly-Guided Caliphs
Al-Ash'ari states beliefs regarding the Companions, the four caliphs, and the ten promised Paradise.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni position on loving the Companions, the priority and superiority of the four caliphs, and the practice of refraining from discussing their disputes.

Core Idea
The best after the Prophet are Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali.

Al-Ash'ari emphasizes the obligation to love the Salaf, especially the Companions, and details the order of the four caliphs. He also affirms the caliphate of prophethood for the first four and the Paradise of the ten promised Companions.

Guided Path
1
Read and distinguish the components
The passage covers: love of Salaf, praise of Companions, succession of four caliphs, testimony of Paradise for the ten, and refraining from disputes.
2
Learn the order of the caliphs
Memorize the sequence: Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Uthman, then Ali. Note that Uthman's murder is condemned as unjust.
3
Understand the principle of refraining from disputes
We befriend all Companions and do not delve into their conflicts. Their caliphate is that of prophethood.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وندين الله عز وجل بأنه يقلب القلوب بين أصبعين من أصابع الله عز وجل. وأنه عز وجل يضع السموات والأرض على إصبع والأرضين على إصبع كما جاءت الراوية عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم
وندين بأن لا ننزل أحداً من أهل التوحيد والمتمسكين بالإيمان جنة ولا ناراً إلا من شهد له رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم بالجنة
ونرجو الجنة للمذنبين ونخاف عليهم أن يكونوا بالنار معذبين
•
ونقول : إن الله عز وجل يخرج قوماً من النار بعد أن امتحشوا بشفاعة الرسول ولا تصديقاً لما جاءت به الروايات عن الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم .
ونؤمن بعذاب القبر ، وبالحوض .
وأن الميزان حق ، والصراط حق ، والبعث بعد الموت حق ، وأن الله عز وجل يوقف العباد في الموقف ويحاسب المؤمنين .
وأن الإيمان قول وعمل يزيد وينقص
ونسلم بالروايات الصحيحة عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم التي رواها الثقات عدل عن عدل حتى ينتهي إلى رسول الله .
وندين بحب السلف الذين اختارهم الله عز وجل لصحبة نبيه عليه السلام ونثني عليهم بما أثنى الله به عليهم ونتولاهم أجمعين ونقول : إن الإمام الفاضل بعد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم أبو بكر الصديق رضوان الله عليه .
وأن الله أعز به الدين وأظهره على المرتدين ، وقدمه المسلمون بالإمامة كما قدمه رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم للصلاة ، وسموه جميعهم خليفة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم
ثم عمر بن الخطاب رضي الله عنه .
ثم عثمان بن عفان رضي الله عنه وإن الذين قتلوه قتلوه ظلماً وعدواناً .
ثم علي بن أبي طالب رضي الله عنه .
فهؤلاء الأئمة بعد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم وخلافتهم خلافة النبوة ونشهد بالجنة للعشرة الذين شهد لهم رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم بها ، ونتولی سائر أصحاب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ونكف عما شجر بينهم .
وندين الله بأن الأئمة الأربعة خلفاء راشدون مهديون فضلاء لا يوازيهم في الفضل وغيرهم .
ونصدق جميع الروايات التي يثبتها أهل النقل من النزول إلى السماء الدنيا، وأن الرب عز وجل يقول هل من سائل ، هل من مستغفر ، وبسائر ما نقلوه وأثبتوه خلافاً لما قال أهل الزيغ والتضليل ، ونعول فيما اختلفنا فيه على کتاب ربنا وسنة نبينا وإجماع المسلمين وما كان في معناه : ولا نبتدع في دين الله ما لم يأذن لنا ، ولا نقول على الله ما لا نعلم
ونقول : إن الله عز وجل يجيء يوم القيامة كما قال : ﴿ وَجَاءَ رَبُّكَ وَالْمَلَكُ صَفَا صَفا ) .
وأن الله عز وجل يقرب من عباده كيف شاء كما قال : ﴿ وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ جبل الوريد ) وكما قال : ( ثُمَّ دَنَا فَنَدَكَ * فَكَانَ قَابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنَى )
ومن ديننا أن نصلي الجمعة والأعياد وسائر الصلوات والجماعات خلف کل بر وغيره . كما روي أن عبد الله بن عمر عنهما كان يصلي خلف الحجاج .
And we profess faith in Allah, Mighty and Majestic, that He turns hearts between two fingers of the fingers of Allah, Mighty and Majestic. And that He, Mighty and Majestic, places the heavens and the earth on a finger, and the earths on a finger, as the narration came from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
And we profess that we do not assign anyone of the people of tawhid and those adhering to faith to Paradise or Hell, except for one whom the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, attested to Paradise.
And we hope for Paradise for the sinners and fear for them that they may be punished in the Fire.

And we believe in the punishment of the grave, and the Basin.
And that the Scale is true, and the Bridge is true, and the resurrection after death is true, and that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will stop the servants at the standing and will reckon the believers.

And that Allah strengthened the religion through him and manifested him over the apostates, and the Muslims put him forward for leadership as the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, put him forward for prayer, and they all named him the Caliph of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
Then 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him.
Then 'Uthman ibn 'Affan, may Allah be pleased with him, and indeed those who killed him killed him wrongfully and aggressively.
Then 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him.
These are the imams after the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and their caliphate is the caliphate of prophethood. And we testify to Paradise for the ten whom the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, testified to Paradise. And we befriend the rest of the Companions of the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and we refrain from what occurred among them.
And we hold as religion that the four imams are rightly-guided caliphs, guided, virtuous, none equals them in virtue, and others.
And we affirm all the narrations that the people of transmission have established, such as the descent to the lowest heaven, and that the Lord, Mighty and Majestic, says: "Is there anyone asking? Is there anyone seeking forgiveness?" and all that they have transmitted and established, contrary to what the people of deviation and misguidance said. And we rely, in what we differ about, on the Book of our Lord, the Sunnah of our Prophet, the consensus of the Muslims, and what is in its meaning. And we do not introduce innovations in the religion of Allah that which He has not permitted us, nor do we say about Allah what we do not know.
And we say: Indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will come on the Day of Resurrection as He said: "And your Lord comes, and the angels, rank upon rank."
And that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, draws near to His servants as He wills, as He said: "And We are nearer to him than the jugular vein," and as He said: "Then He drew near and came down, and was at a distance of two bow-lengths or nearer."

Commentary

Eschatological beliefs are listed: punishment of the grave, the Basin (of the Prophet on the Day of Judgment), the Scale (for weighing deeds), the Bridge (over Hell), resurrection, and the standing before God for reckoning of the believers.

He warns against innovation and speaking about God without knowledge.

The passage concludes with the practical ruling that we pray behind any ruler, whether pious or impious, as 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar did behind al-Hajjaj, a notoriously unjust governor. This demonstrates the Sunni principle of obedience and unity in congregational worship.

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some sects (like Khawarij or extreme Shia) revile or curse some Companions.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari commands love and praise for all Companions, and to refrain from their disputes.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, who is the first caliph after the Prophet?
A) Umar ibn al-Khattab
B) Abu Bakr al-Siddiq
C) Ali ibn Abi Talib
D) Uthman ibn Affan

Key Takeaway
Ahl al-Sunnah loves the Companions, affirms the order of the four caliphs, and refrains from their disputes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you genuinely love the Companions and the Salaf as a religious duty, or do you hold any resentment or doubt about their actions? What step will you take this week to learn more about their virtues as praised by Allah?
?
When you hear about disputes among the Companions, do you feel inclined to judge or take sides? According to this lesson, what must you do, and how can you discipline your heart to refrain from such discussions?
?
The passage says the four caliphs are guided and virtuous, none equals them. Does your view of later Muslim leaders ever lead you to diminish the status of these four? How will you actively honor them in your daily thoughts and words?

Faith and Disbelief
intermediate
5 min
Affirmation of Divine Descension, Coming, and Nearness
Al-Ash'ari affirms divine descent, coming, and nearness, and states the Sunni methodology for resolving differences.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Ahl al-Sunnah affirms God's descension to the lowest heaven, His coming on the Day of Resurrection, and His nearness to His servants as reported, without figurative interpretation.

Core Idea
God descends, comes, and draws near, but we do not ask how.

Al-Ash'ari returns to divine attributes, affirming the narrations about God's descent to the lowest heaven, His coming on the Day of Resurrection, and His nearness to His servants. He rejects the interpretations of those who deny these, and emphasizes reliance on Quran, Sunnah, and Ijma.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and note the attitude
Al-Ash'ari says 'we affirm' and 'we say' about these narrations. He contrasts with 'people of deviation and misguidance' who reject them.
2
Identify the three specific events
Find: 1) Descent to lowest heaven, 2) Coming on Day of Resurrection, 3) Nearness to servants. Each is supported by Quranic verses.
3
Understand the methodology
Al-Ash'ari states the sources of authority: Quran, Sunnah, Ijma, and warns against innovation and speaking without knowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وندين الله عز وجل بأنه يقلب القلوب بين أصبعين من أصابع الله عز وجل. وأنه عز وجل يضع السموات والأرض على إصبع والأرضين على إصبع كما جاءت الراوية عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم
وندين بأن لا ننزل أحداً من أهل التوحيد والمتمسكين بالإيمان جنة ولا ناراً إلا من شهد له رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم بالجنة
ونرجو الجنة للمذنبين ونخاف عليهم أن يكونوا بالنار معذبين
•
ونقول : إن الله عز وجل يخرج قوماً من النار بعد أن امتحشوا بشفاعة الرسول ولا تصديقاً لما جاءت به الروايات عن الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم .
ونؤمن بعذاب القبر ، وبالحوض .
وأن الميزان حق ، والصراط حق ، والبعث بعد الموت حق ، وأن الله عز وجل يوقف العباد في الموقف ويحاسب المؤمنين .
وأن الإيمان قول وعمل يزيد وينقص
ونسلم بالروايات الصحيحة عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم التي رواها الثقات عدل عن عدل حتى ينتهي إلى رسول الله .
وندين بحب السلف الذين اختارهم الله عز وجل لصحبة نبيه عليه السلام ونثني عليهم بما أثنى الله به عليهم ونتولاهم أجمعين ونقول : إن الإمام الفاضل بعد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم أبو بكر الصديق رضوان الله عليه .
وأن الله أعز به الدين وأظهره على المرتدين ، وقدمه المسلمون بالإمامة كما قدمه رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم للصلاة ، وسموه جميعهم خليفة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم
ثم عمر بن الخطاب رضي الله عنه .
ثم عثمان بن عفان رضي الله عنه وإن الذين قتلوه قتلوه ظلماً وعدواناً .
ثم علي بن أبي طالب رضي الله عنه .
فهؤلاء الأئمة بعد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم وخلافتهم خلافة النبوة ونشهد بالجنة للعشرة الذين شهد لهم رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم بها ، ونتولی سائر أصحاب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ونكف عما شجر بينهم .
وندين الله بأن الأئمة الأربعة خلفاء راشدون مهديون فضلاء لا يوازيهم في الفضل وغيرهم .
ونصدق جميع الروايات التي يثبتها أهل النقل من النزول إلى السماء الدنيا، وأن الرب عز وجل يقول هل من سائل ، هل من مستغفر ، وبسائر ما نقلوه وأثبتوه خلافاً لما قال أهل الزيغ والتضليل ، ونعول فيما اختلفنا فيه على کتاب ربنا وسنة نبينا وإجماع المسلمين وما كان في معناه : ولا نبتدع في دين الله ما لم يأذن لنا ، ولا نقول على الله ما لا نعلم
ونقول : إن الله عز وجل يجيء يوم القيامة كما قال : ﴿ وَجَاءَ رَبُّكَ وَالْمَلَكُ صَفَا صَفا ) .
وأن الله عز وجل يقرب من عباده كيف شاء كما قال : ﴿ وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ جبل الوريد ) وكما قال : ( ثُمَّ دَنَا فَنَدَكَ * فَكَانَ قَابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنَى )
ومن ديننا أن نصلي الجمعة والأعياد وسائر الصلوات والجماعات خلف کل بر وغيره . كما روي أن عبد الله بن عمر عنهما كان يصلي خلف الحجاج .
And we profess faith in Allah, Mighty and Majestic, that He turns hearts between two fingers of the fingers of Allah, Mighty and Majestic. And that He, Mighty and Majestic, places the heavens and the earth on a finger, and the earths on a finger, as the narration came from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
And we profess that we do not assign anyone of the people of tawhid and those adhering to faith to Paradise or Hell, except for one whom the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, attested to Paradise.
And we hope for Paradise for the sinners and fear for them that they may be punished in the Fire.
•
And we say: Indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will bring out a people from the Fire after they have been charred, by the intercession of the Messenger, in affirmation of what the narrations from the Messenger, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, have brought.
And we believe in the punishment of the grave, and the Basin.
And that the Scale is true, and the Bridge is true, and the resurrection after death is true, and that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will stop the servants at the standing and will reckon the believers.
And that faith is speech and action, it increases and decreases.
And we submit to the authentic narrations from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, which the trustworthy have narrated, trustworthy from trustworthy, until it reaches the Messenger of Allah.
And we hold as religion the love of the Salaf whom Allah, Mighty and Majestic, chose for the companionship of His Prophet, peace be upon him, and we praise them with what Allah praised them with, and we befriend them all, and we say: Indeed the virtuous Imam after the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, is Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the pleasure of Allah be upon him.
And that Allah strengthened the religion through him and manifested him over the apostates, and the Muslims put him forward for leadership as the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, put him forward for prayer, and they all named him the Caliph of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
Then 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him.
Then 'Uthman ibn 'Affan, may Allah be pleased with him, and indeed those who killed him killed him wrongfully and aggressively.
Then 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him.
These are the imams after the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and their caliphate is the caliphate of prophethood. And we testify to Paradise for the ten whom the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, testified to Paradise. And we befriend the rest of the Companions of the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, and we refrain from what occurred among them.
And we hold as religion that the four imams are rightly-guided caliphs, guided, virtuous, none equals them in virtue, and others.
And we affirm all the narrations that the people of transmission have established, such as the descent to the lowest heaven, and that the Lord, Mighty and Majestic, says: "Is there anyone asking? Is there anyone seeking forgiveness?" and all that they have transmitted and established, contrary to what the people of deviation and misguidance said. And we rely, in what we differ about, on the Book of our Lord, the Sunnah of our Prophet, the consensus of the Muslims, and what is in its meaning. And we do not introduce innovations in the religion of Allah that which He has not permitted us, nor do we say about Allah what we do not know.
And we say: Indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will come on the Day of Resurrection as He said: "And your Lord comes, and the angels, rank upon rank."
And that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, draws near to His servants as He wills, as He said: "And We are nearer to him than the jugular vein," and as He said: "Then He drew near and came down, and was at a distance of two bow-lengths or nearer."
And it is part of our religion that we pray the Friday prayer, the Eid prayers, and all prayers and congregations behind every pious and impious [ruler], as it was narrated that 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar, may Allah be pleased with both of them, used to pray behind al-Hajjaj.
Source pages p. 24

Commentary
This passage is a continuous creedal statement by Imam al-Ash'ari enumerating the core beliefs of Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah. It follows the method of the Salaf in affirming what is reported without modality or figurative interpretation.

The passage begins by affirming God's absolute control: He turns hearts between two of His fingers, and He places the heavens and earths on a finger, as reported in hadith. This is an affirmation of God's attributes of action and His power, without speculating on how.

Next, al-Ash'ari addresses faith and judgement of believers: we do not definitively assign anyone to Paradise or Hell except those explicitly attested by the Prophet. For sinful believers, we maintain hope of Paradise but fear punishment, balancing hope and fear.

He then affirms intercession: God will bring out from the Fire those who have been charred (i.e., severely punished) through the intercession of the Prophet, in line with authentic hadiths.

Eschatological beliefs are listed: punishment of the grave, the Basin (of the Prophet on the Day of Judgment), the Scale (for weighing deeds), the Bridge (over Hell), resurrection, and the standing before God for reckoning of the believers.

Regarding faith itself, al-Ash'ari defines it as 'speech and action,' and it increases and decreases. This is the Sunni position, opposing the Murji'ah who say faith is only belief and does not increase or decrease.

He then emphasizes acceptance of authentic hadiths transmitted through trustworthy chains, affirming the importance of the Sunni method of hadith criticism.

The passage then turns to the Companions and the Caliphs. We must love the Salaf (the early Muslims, especially the Companions) and praise them as God praised them. The best after the Prophet is Abu Bakr, followed by 'Umar, then 'Uthman (noting his murder was unjust), then 'Ali. These four are the imams and their caliphate is the 'caliphate of prophethood.' He also testifies to Paradise for the ten Companions given glad tidings (the 'ashara mubashshara), refrains from commenting on disputes among Companions, and affirms the superiority of these four imams over all others.

Al-Ash'ari then affirms the accepted narrations about God's descent to the lowest heaven and His asking 'Is there anyone asking?' etc. He rejects the interpretations of those who deny these (the 'people of deviation and misguidance') and states that in matters of disagreement, the source of authority is the Quran, Sunnah, and Ijma'. He warns against innovation and speaking about God without knowledge.

Finally, he affirms that God will come on the Day of Resurrection in a manner befitting Him, and that He draws near to His servants as He wills, citing Quranic verses. The passage concludes with the practical ruling that we pray behind any ruler, whether pious or impious, as 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar did behind al-Hajjaj, a notoriously unjust governor. This demonstrates the Sunni principle of obedience and unity in congregational worship.

Key Terms
النزول إلى السماء الدنيا
المجيء
القرب
الإجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some deny or interpret God's descent and coming as metaphorical, claiming it implies movement or location.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari affirms these as real actions of God without limiting Him to space or time, following the Salaf.

Checkpoint
What does Al-Ash'ari say about those who deny the narrations of descent and God's saying?
A) They are people of deviation and misguidance
B) They are excused
C) They are correct
D) They are sinners but still Muslims

Key Takeaway
Ahl al-Sunnah affirms God's descent, coming, and nearness as reported, relying on Quran, Sunnah, and Ijma.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that God descends and asks 'Is there anyone seeking forgiveness?', do you feel personally addressed? How does this affect your nightly prayers and your hope for forgiveness?
?
The passage warns against speaking about God without knowledge. Are there areas of your belief or practice where you have adopted opinions without verifying them against the Quran and Sunnah? What will you do to correct that?
?
If God is nearer to you than your jugular vein, why do you often feel distant from Him? What barriers have you built, and what will you remove today to feel His nearness?

Faith and Disbelief
beginner
3 min
Praying Behind All Rulers: The Sunni Practice of Unity
Al-Ash'ari states the Sunni practice of praying behind rulers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Ahl al-Sunnah prays behind both pious and impious rulers as a matter of unity and obedience, following the example of Ibn Umar.

Core Idea
We pray behind every ruler, regardless of their piety.

Al-Ash'ari concludes with a practical ruling: it is part of our religion to pray Friday and Eid prayers behind any ruler, even an unjust one. He cites the practice of Abdullah ibn Umar praying behind al-Hajjaj, a notoriously unjust governor.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the ruling
The passage states: 'It is part of our religion to pray Friday, Eid, and all congregational prayers behind every pious and impious [ruler].'
2
Understand the reasoning
Praying behind rulers maintains community cohesion and is a form of obedience to legitimate authority, even if the ruler is sinful.
3
Learn from the example of Ibn Umar
Abdullah ibn Umar prayed behind al-Hajjaj, who was unjust, showing the principle in practice.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وندين الله عز وجل بأنه يقلب القلوب بين أصبعين من أصابع الله عز وجل. وأنه عز وجل يضع السموات والأرض على إصبع والأرضين على إصبع كما جاءت الراوية عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم
وندين بأن لا ننزل أحداً من أهل التوحيد والمتمسكين بالإيمان جنة ولا ناراً إلا من شهد له رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم بالجنة
ونرجو الجنة للمذنبين ونخاف عليهم أن يكونوا بالنار معذبين
•
ونقول : إن الله عز وجل يخرج قوماً من النار بعد أن امتحشوا بشفاعة الرسول ولا تصديقاً لما جاءت به الروايات عن الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم .
ونؤمن بعذاب القبر ، وبالحوض .
وأن الميزان حق ، والصراط حق ، والبعث بعد الموت حق ، وأن الله عز وجل يوقف العباد في الموقف ويحاسب المؤمنين .
وأن الإيمان قول وعمل يزيد وينقص
ونسلم بالروايات الصحيحة عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم التي رواها الثقات عدل عن عدل حتى ينتهي إلى رسول الله .
وندين بحب السلف الذين اختارهم الله عز وجل لصحبة نبيه عليه السلام ونثني عليهم بما أثنى الله به عليهم ونتولاهم أجمعين ونقول : إن الإمام الفاضل بعد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم أبو بكر الصديق رضوان الله عليه .
وأن الله أعز به الدين وأظهره على المرتدين ، وقدمه المسلمون بالإمامة كما قدمه رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم للصلاة ، وسموه جميعهم خليفة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم
ثم عمر بن الخطاب رضي الله عنه .
ثم عثمان بن عفان رضي الله عنه وإن الذين قتلوه قتلوه ظلماً وعدواناً .
ثم علي بن أبي طالب رضي الله عنه .
فهؤلاء الأئمة بعد رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم وخلافتهم خلافة النبوة ونشهد بالجنة للعشرة الذين شهد لهم رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم بها ، ونتولی سائر أصحاب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ونكف عما شجر بينهم .
وندين الله بأن الأئمة الأربعة خلفاء راشدون مهديون فضلاء لا يوازيهم في الفضل وغيرهم .
ونصدق جميع الروايات التي يثبتها أهل النقل من النزول إلى السماء الدنيا، وأن الرب عز وجل يقول هل من سائل ، هل من مستغفر ، وبسائر ما نقلوه وأثبتوه خلافاً لما قال أهل الزيغ والتضليل ، ونعول فيما اختلفنا فيه على کتاب ربنا وسنة نبينا وإجماع المسلمين وما كان في معناه : ولا نبتدع في دين الله ما لم يأذن لنا ، ولا نقول على الله ما لا نعلم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that one should only pray behind a just and pious ruler.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari says we pray behind both pious and impious, as the ruler's personal sins do not invalidate the prayer.

Checkpoint
Who is cited as an example for praying behind an unjust ruler?
A) Abu Bakr
B) Abdullah ibn Umar
C) Al-Ash'ari himself
D) Umar ibn al-Khattab

Key Takeaway
Ahl al-Sunnah prays behind all rulers to preserve unity, following the example of Ibn Umar.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever avoided a congregational prayer because you disapproved of the imam's character? How does the example of Ibn Umar challenge your sense of spiritual superiority?
?
If you were in a community where the ruler is known for injustice, would your heart lean towards boycott or unity? What does your instinct reveal about your attachment to the unity of the Ummah?
?
The lesson teaches that unity in worship takes precedence over personal judgment. In what relationships or situations in your life are you letting criticism of others prevent you from joining in collective good? Will you commit to praying behind them, literally or figuratively, for the sake of Allah?

Faith and Disbelief

They Cause Despair of God's Mercy
Accusation that they cause despair and deny forgiveness for major sins.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the author of al-Ibanah condemns the Qadariyyah for causing despair and denying God's mercy to sinners.

Core Idea
Claiming eternal hell for every major sinner denies God's mercy.

This lesson addresses a key charge against the Qadariyyah: that they cause people to despair of God's mercy by insisting that all major sinners will remain in Hell forever. The author argues this contradicts the Quranic principle that God forgives whom He wills.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the literal translation to understand the plain meaning.
Notice the strong language: 'made despair' and 'judged'.
2
Identify the accusation and the Quranic proof
Find the two actions attributed to the Qadariyyah and the Quranic verse cited as a refutation.
The verse is from Surah al-Nisa' (4:48).
3
Reflect on the theological implication
Ask yourself: why does the author consider this a serious error? How does it relate to God's mercy and justice?
This connects to the broader debate on the fate of sinners.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقنطوا الناس من رحمة الله وآيسوهم من روحه، وحكموا على العصاة بالنار والخلود فيها، خلافاً لقول الله عز وجل: ﴿وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَلِكَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ﴾ [النساء: ٤٨].
And they made people despair of the mercy of God and caused them to lose hope in His spirit, and they judged the sinners to be in the Fire and to abide therein forever, contrary to the saying of God Almighty: 'And He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills' [Surah al-Nisa':48].
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This passage condemns the Qadariyyah for causing people to despair of God's mercy by asserting that sinners will abide eternally in Hell, which contradicts the Quranic verse that states God forgives lesser sins for whom He wills.

Key Terms
القنوط
الخلود في النار
المغفرة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: All major sinners will remain in Hell forever regardless of God's will.
Correction: The Quran shows that God can forgive any sin except shirk (if one dies without repentance) for whom He wills, so not all sinners are eternally damned.

Checkpoint
What do the Qadariyyah claim about sinners according to this passage?
A) They will be forgiven
B) They will abide forever in Hell
C) They will be punished briefly
D) They are not sinful

Key Takeaway
The Qadariyyah's denial of forgiveness for major sinners contradicts God's mercy as stated in the Quran.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt that your sins are too great for God to forgive? How does this passage challenge that feeling? What one step can you take today to truly trust in His mercy?
?
The Qadariyyah made people despair of God's mercy. Are there ways you subconsciously despair of God's mercy towards yourself or others? What would change if you truly believed God forgives what is less than shirk for whom He wills? Name one person you need to extend hope to.

beginner

Refuting Despair of Allah's Mercy
Al-Ash'ari accuses the sect of making people despair of Allah's mercy.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari refutes the sect's claim of eternal punishment for all sinners using Qur'an 4:48.

Core Idea
Qur'an 4:48 preserves hope in Allah's mercy for all except shirk.

Al-Ash'ari presents his fourteenth accusation against the heretics: they cause people to despair of Allah's mercy by insisting that every sinner will be eternally punished. This contradicts a key Qur'anic verse that limits unforgivable sin to shirk alone. Understanding this refutation shows how Islamic theology maintains balance between justice and mercy.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then its translation. Notice the accusation and the verse cited.
The passage is short but dense.
2
Identify the sect's claim
What exactly is the sect asserting? They make people despair of mercy and judge all disobedient to be forever in Hell.
3
Examine the Qur'anic refutation
Find the verse (4:48) and see how it refutes the claim. The verse states that Allah forgives all except shirk.
The phrase 'what is less than that' covers all sins besides shirk.
4
Reflect on the theological implication
Why is this important? The sect closes the door of mercy. The Qur'an keeps it open. This balances divine justice and mercy.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقنطوا الناس من رحمة الله وآيسوهم من روحه، وحكموا على العصاة بالنار والخلود فيها، خلافاً لقول الله عز وجل: {وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَلِكَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ} [النساء: 48].
And they made people despair of the mercy of Allah and caused them to lose hope of His spirit, and they judged the disobedient to be in the Fire and to remain therein forever, contrary to the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: {And He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills} [an-Nisa: 48].
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This is the fourteenth accusation: the heretics cause people to despair of Allah's mercy by asserting that all sinners will be eternally punished in Hellfire. Al-Ash'ari refutes this by citing Qur'an 4:48, which states that Allah forgives all sins except shirk (associating partners with Him) and forgives lesser sins for whom He wills. The heretics' view contradicts this verse because it denies the possibility of forgiveness for grave sinners who die without repentance, effectively closing the door of mercy.

Key Terms
قنطوا
آيسوهم
الخلود

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: All Muslims who commit major sins will be eternally punished in Hell regardless of repentance or Allah's mercy.
Correction: Qur'an 4:48 explicitly states that Allah forgives all sins except shirk for whom He wills, meaning grave sinners may be forgiven if they did not commit shirk.

Checkpoint
What verse does al-Ash'ari use to refute the claim of eternal punishment for all sinners?
A) {Say: 'I do not possess...'}
B) {And He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills}
C) {The Most Gracious, He taught the Qur'an}
D) {Indeed, Allah is with the patient}

Key Takeaway
This passage shows that the Qur'an explicitly limits unforgivable sin to shirk, thereby preserving hope in Allah's mercy for other sins.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt that a sin you committed is too great or too repeated for Allah to forgive? How does this verse challenge that feeling, and will you now repent sincerely?
?
When you hear about the severe punishment for sin, does it sometimes make you feel hopeless about your own state? How can you hold onto the balance of fear and hope taught in this passage?
?
Think of a major sin you struggle with. Do you truly believe that Allah can forgive it if you turn to Him? If not, what is holding you back from trusting His mercy revealed in Qur'an 4:48?

Not Excommunicating Sinners: The Sunni Position on Takfir
Al-Ash'ari states the Sunni rule against takfir of sinners, with an exception for istihlal, and distinguishes Islam from iman.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni stance on declaring Muslims disbelievers for major sins, including the exception for istihlal and the distinction between Islam and faith.

Core Idea
We do not excommunicate sinners, but rejecting divine law is disbelief.

Al-Ash'ari presents the Sunni position on a controversial issue: whether a Muslim who commits a major sin loses his faith. He refutes the Kharijite view that any major sin causes disbelief. He also clarifies that while sins do not expel one from Islam, considering sin permissible does. Finally, he distinguishes between outward Islam and inward faith.

Guided Path
1
Understand the Kharijite position
Read the passage and note the Kharijites' view that major sins cause disbelief. This is the backdrop for the Sunni position.
The Kharijites are a sect that excommunicated sinners.
2
State the Sunni general rule
The passage declares 'we do not excommunicate anyone of the people of the Qibla for a sin.' This is the core principle.
3
Study the exception for istihlal
If someone commits a major sin while considering it permissible (mustahill), then he becomes a disbeliever. Understand the condition of istihlal.
Istihlal means believing the sin is lawful.
4
Learn the Islam/Iman distinction
The passage ends by saying Islam is broader than iman. Reflect on how this relates to the issue of takfir.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وندين بأن لا نُكَفِّر أحداً من أهل القبلة بذنب يرتكبه كالزنا والسرقة وشرب الخمر، كما دانت بذلك الخوارج، وزعموا أنهم بذلك كافرون. ونقول: إن من عمل كبيرة من هذه الكبائر مثل الزنا والسرقة وما أشبهها مستحلاً لها غير معتقد لتحريمها كان كافراً. ونقول: إن الإسلام أوسع من الإيمان، وليس كل إسلام إيمان.
And we hold as religion that we do not excommunicate anyone of the people of the Qibla for a sin he commits, such as adultery, theft, and drinking wine, as the Kharijites held, and they claimed that thereby they are disbelievers. And we say: Whoever commits a major sin from among these major sins, such as adultery, theft, and what resembles them, considering it permissible, not believing in its prohibition, he is a disbeliever. And we say: Indeed Islam is broader than faith, and not every Islam is faith.
Source pages p. 48

Commentary
This passage addresses the issue of takfir (excommunication). The author distinguishes the Sunni position from the Kharijites, who declare Muslims guilty of major sins as disbelievers. Al-Ash'ari states that we do not excommunicate a Muslim for major sins like adultery, theft, or drinking alcohol. However, he adds an exception: if a person commits such a sin while considering it permissible (mustahill) and not believing it is forbidden, then such a person is a disbeliever because he rejects divine law. Finally, he makes a distinction between Islam (outward submission) and faith (iman), stating that Islam is more inclusive than iman; not every person who outwardly practices Islam possesses true faith.

Key Terms
تكفير
أهل القبلة
كبائر
إسلام
إيمان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that committing any major sin makes a person a disbeliever.
Correction: According to this passage, committing a major sin does not automatically make one a disbeliever. Only if the person considers the sin permissible (istihlal) does he become a disbeliever.

Checkpoint
When does a person who commits a major sin become a disbeliever according to this passage?
A) Immediately upon committing the sin
B) Only if he commits theft
C) If he considers it permissible and does not believe it is forbidden
D) If he repeats the sin

Key Takeaway
Sins do not remove one from Islam unless one considers them permissible, and Islam is broader than faith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever looked down on a fellow Muslim because of a sin they committed, perhaps thinking they are less of a believer? How does this passage challenge the judgment in your heart, and what will you do to change your attitude starting today?
?
The passage says that only considering a sin permissible leads to disbelief. Reflect: are there any sins in your life that you have begun to rationalize or make excuses for, as if they are 'not that bad'? Will you repent from that mindset and reaffirm the prohibition in your heart?
?
You may say you are Muslim, but do you have true iman? This verse separates the two. What evidence is there in your daily life that your Islam is accompanied by iman? If it is not, what step will you take today to nurture the faith that saves?

Not Excommunicating Sinners and the Exception of Deeming Sin Lawful
Al-Ash'ari refutes the Khawarij by not excommunicating sinners, except when they deem the sin lawful.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the position of Ahl al-Sunna regarding major sins: that sin alone does not remove one from faith, but deeming a sin lawful does.

Core Idea
Sin does not cause excommunication; considering sin permissible does.

Al-Ash'ari clarifies the pivotal difference between committing a major sin and believing it is lawful. The Khawarij declared all major sinners disbelievers; the Sunni position is that faith remains unless the sinner denies the prohibition.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice the contrast with the Khawarij.
The passage begins with 'نُكَفِّر' (we do not excommunicate).
2
Identify the two categories
The passage first gives a general rule: no excommunication for sin. Then it gives an exception: if the sin is deemed lawful, it is excommunication.
The exception is introduced by 'ونقول'.
3
Contrast with the Khawarij
Understand that the Khawarij made the first category (sin alone) into excommunication. Al-Ash'ari refutes them.
4
Reflect on the implication
Ask yourself: Why does this distinction matter? It protects against extremism while maintaining that denying divine law is kufr.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

And we say: Islam is broader than Iman, and not every Islam is Iman.
Source pages p. 48

Commentary

The Distinction Between Islam and Iman
Al-Ash'ari distinguishes between Islam and Iman, stating Islam is broader.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Islam and Iman are not identical; Islam is a broader category that includes outward submission, while Iman requires inner conviction.

Core Idea
Islam is outward submission; Iman is inward faith.

In this concise statement, al-Ash'ari establishes a crucial theological distinction: Islam encompasses all who outwardly submit, but Iman is a deeper, more specific state. This distinction protects against both Khawarij extremism and Murji'ah laxity.

Guided Path
1
Read the short passage
Read the single sentence in Arabic and English. Notice the comparative 'أوسع' (broader).
The sentence is: 'ونقول : إن الإسلام أوسع من الإيمان، وليس كل إسلام إيمان.'
2
Understand the difference in scope
Islam is broader: it includes everyone who submits outwardly (e.g., confesses the shahada). Iman is narrower: it requires inner conviction.
Thus, not every Muslim has Iman in the fullest sense.
3
Consider the implications
Think why this distinction matters. It avoids excommunicating those with weak faith while acknowledging that true faith is more than external acts.

Not Judging Eternal Fate Without Explicit Promise
Al-Ash'ari forbids definitively judging anyone's eternal fate except those explicitly promised.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sunni theology suspends definitive judgment on the eternal fate of monotheists, except for those explicitly promised Paradise by the Prophet.

Core Idea
Only explicit divine testimony can settle a person’s eternal destiny.

This passage from Al-Ash'ari's al-Ibānah addresses a critical question: Can we, as believers, definitively say who is in Paradise or Hell? The answer is no—except for those whom the Prophet himself specifically promised Paradise. For all others, we balance hope in Allah's mercy with fear of His justice.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage in Arabic and English
First, read the Arabic sentence aloud or silently. Then read the literal translation. Notice the key terms 'ahl al-tawḥīd', 'rajā’', and 'khawf'.
The passage is concise but carries a weighty theological principle.
2
Identify the forbidden action
Al-Ash'ari says we do not 'assign' (nunazzil) anyone to Paradise or Hell. This means we cannot make a definitive judgment about a person's final abode unless we have explicit scriptural evidence.
This avoids both overconfidence and despair.
3
Understand the exception
The only exception is someone for whom the Messenger of Allah testified (shahida) that they are in Paradise. These are the ten promised paradise, etc.
This exception is based on clear hadith, not personal opinion.
4
Grasp the dual attitude toward sinners
For sinful monotheists, we hold both hope (rajā’) for Paradise and fear (khawf) of Hellfire. This balanced position avoids despair of Allah's mercy and presumption of security.
This reflects the Sunni middle path between Kharijites and Murji'ites.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٤٥ - وندين بأن لا ننزل أحداً من أهل التوحيد والمتمسكين بالإيمان جنةً ولا ناراً إلا من شهد له رسول الله بالجنة، ونرجو الجنة للمذنبين، ونخاف عليهم أن يكونوا بالنار معذبين.
45 - And we commit that we do not assign anyone of the people of tawhid and those holding fast to iman either to Paradise or to the Fire, except one for whom the Messenger of Allah testified that he is in Paradise; and we hope for Paradise for the sinners, and we fear for them that they may be punished in the Fire.
Source pages p. 48

Commentary
This passage establishes the Sunni position on judging the final destiny of believers who commit sins. Al-Ash'ari states that we cannot definitively say a particular monotheist is in Paradise or Hell, unless the Prophet explicitly gave that glad tiding. For other sinners, we maintain a balanced attitude: we hope for Allah's mercy and thus hope for Paradise, but we also fear His justice and fear Hell for them. This avoids both despair and presumption.

Key Terms
أهل التوحيد
الجنة
النار
الرجاء
الخوف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that all Muslims are guaranteed Paradise regardless of their sins.
Correction: This passage teaches that we cannot definitively judge anyone's final abode except those explicitly promised by the Prophet. For other believers, we hope for mercy but fear punishment.

Checkpoint
For whom can we definitively say they are in Paradise according to this passage?
A) All monotheists
B) The Prophet's companions
C) Only those whom the Prophet explicitly testified for
D) All who die as Muslims

Key Takeaway
We cannot definitively judge any monotheist's eternal fate unless the Prophet explicitly promised them Paradise; for others, we balance hope in mercy with fear of punishment.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

The Sunni Definition of Iman: Speech, Action, and Fluctuation
Al-Ash'ari defines Iman as speech and action, increasing and decreasing.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the orthodox Sunni definition of Iman as consisting of both speech and action, and that it can increase and decrease based on obedience and sin.

Core Idea
Iman is not static; it grows with good deeds and weakens with sins.

In this concise passage, Imam al-Ash'ari lays down the foundational Sunni understanding of faith. He emphasizes that Iman is not merely an inner conviction but includes both verbal confession and righteous deeds, and that it can increase or decrease. This counters the view of the Murji'ah who consider Iman as only belief and unchanging.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic text and translation
Read the passage in Arabic first to get a sense of the author's style, then read the translation aloud. Notice the clarity of the wording.
The word 'يزيد وينقص' (increases and decreases) is key.
2
Identify the three components of Iman
The passage states Iman is قول (speech) and عمل (action), and that it يزيد وينقص (increases and decreases). These are the three essential points.
Speech refers to the verbal shahada; action includes deeds of the heart and limbs.
3
Reflect on the evidence from authentic narrations
Al-Ash'ari grounds this definition in authentic hadith transmitted by reliable narrators. Consider why he emphasizes the chain of transmission.
This shows that the definition is not based on mere opinion but on prophetic tradition.
4
Contrast with the view of the Murji'ah
Understand that the Murji'ah hold Iman is only belief, does not include actions, and does not increase or decrease. This passage directly refutes that.
The commentary explains the refutation.
5
Summarize in your own words
Write a one-sentence definition of Iman based on this passage, including all three elements.
This will help solidify the concept.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٤٨ - وأن الإيمان قول وعمل، يزيد وينقص، ونسلم الروايات الصحيحة في ذلك عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم التي رواها الثقات عدل عن عدل حتى تنتهي إلى رسول الله.
48 - And that Iman is speech and action, it increases and decreases; and we submit to the authentic narrations about that from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, which reliable narrators transmitted, trustworthy from trustworthy, until they reach the Messenger of Allah.
Source pages p. 48

Commentary
This passage gives the Sunni definition of Iman: it consists of both qawl (speech, i.e., utterance of the shahada) and 'amal (actions of the heart and limbs). Moreover, Iman can increase through obedience and decrease through sin. Al-Ash'ari adds that this is accepted based on sound hadiths transmitted through a chain of trustworthy narrators. This refutes the Murji'ah who claim Iman is only belief and does not increase or decrease.

Key Terms
الإيمان
قول
عمل
يزيد
ينقص
ثقات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe Iman is only inner belief and does not include actions, nor does it increase or decrease.
Correction: This passage clearly states that Iman includes speech and action, and that it increases and decreases, thus refuting the Murji'ah position.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, Iman does which of the following?
A) It is fixed and never changes
B) It increases and decreases
C) It consists only of speech
D) It consists only of action

Key Takeaway
Iman is both speech and action, and it can increase and decrease based on one's obedience and sin, as affirmed by authentic prophetic traditions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If your Iman truly increases with obedience and decreases with sin, what did your actions today do to your Iman? Be honest — did you add to it or subtract from it? What one change will you make starting tomorrow to ensure it grows?
?
The passage says Iman is both speech and action. Is there any area of your life where your speech (what you say you believe) and your actions are inconsistent? Will you commit to aligning them, starting with a small step tonight?
?
When you commit a sin, do you feel the decrease in your Iman, or have you become numb to it? Ask yourself: will you let this lesson remind you to seek forgiveness immediately, or will you let another sin pass without turning back to Allah?

Loving the Salaf: A Core Tenet of Ahl al-Sunna
Al-Ash'ari affirms love for the Salaf whom Allah chose for the Prophet's companionship.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why loving the Salaf (the early generations, especially the Companions) is a fundamental belief of Ahl al-Sunna, based on Allah's choice of them for the Prophet's companionship.

Core Idea
Love for the Salaf is based on Divine selection, not personal preference.

In this brief but weighty statement, Imam al-Ash'ari closes a section by affirming a core Sunni belief: love for the Salaf. He grounds this love not in human sentiment but in Allah's own choice of these individuals for the honor of accompanying His Prophet. This episode explains why such love is a matter of faith for Ahl al-Sunna.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Start by reading the original Arabic sentence and its literal translation. Notice the concise formulation: 'we commit to love' (ندين بحب) – it is a matter of religious commitment.
The verb يدين implies religious practice, not mere emotion.
2
Understand the basis of love
The passage states that Allah chose the Salaf for the Prophet's companionship. Reflect on why this makes love for them a theological duty, not an option.
Divine choice elevates the Companions to a special status.
3
Connect to Sunni theological identity
Consider how this stance distinguishes Ahl al-Sunna from groups that criticize or show hostility to the Companions. Loving the Salaf is a marker of orthodoxy.
This is a polemical point against the Rawafid and others.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٤٩ - وندين بحب السلف الذين اختارهم الله تعالى لصحبة نبيه.
49 - And we commit to love of the Salaf whom Allah Almighty chose for the companionship of His Prophet.
Source pages p. 48

Commentary
This short passage closes the section by asserting the duty of loving the Salaf (the early generations of Muslims, particularly the Companions). Al-Ash'ari notes that Allah Himself selected them for the honor of accompanying the Prophet. This is a fundamental stance of Ahl al-Sunna, opposing any sect that shows hostility to the Companions.

Key Terms
السلف
صحبة
نبيه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
Why are the Salaf deserving of love according to this passage?
A) Because they were wealthy
B) Because Allah chose them for the Prophet's Companionship
C) Because they ruled the Islamic state
D) Because they were numerous

Key Takeaway
Loving the Salaf is a religious obligation because Allah Himself chose them for the honor of accompanying His Prophet.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If loving the Salaf is a religious commitment because Allah chose them, then what does that require from you when you hear someone belittle or criticize a Companion? Will you actively defend their honor or remain silent?
?
Do you truly love the Companions as an act of worship, or have you taken their status for granted? What specific step will you take this week to learn about one Companion's life and increase your love for them?
?
The Salaf were chosen by Allah—does your heart feel any trace of resentment or envy toward them? Ask Allah to purify your heart and to make you among those who sincerely love those whom He loves.

Refraining from Judging Individuals to Paradise or Hell
Doctrine of not assigning paradise or hell except by prophetic testimony

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ashari doctrine of withholding judgment on the eternal fate of individual Muslims except where explicitly testified by the Prophet, and the balanced attitude of hope and fear for sinners.

Core Idea
We do not assign paradise or hell except by prophetic testimony.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a foundational principle of Ahl al-Sunnah: we refrain from passing final judgment on any believer's eternal destiny. This passage clarifies that only those explicitly promised paradise by the Prophet are to be judged as such. For all other sinners, we maintain a balance of hope and fear, avoiding both despair and presumption.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage and its literal translation. Notice the key terms 'أهل التوحيد' and 'المذنبين'.
2
Identify the core doctrine
The passage states that we do not assign paradise or hell to anyone except those testified by the Prophet. This is a principle of restraint in theological judgment.
3
Understand the balance of hope and fear
Notice the dual attitude: we hope for paradise for sinners and fear punishment for them. This avoids both despair and presumption.
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider how this doctrine affects how we view other Muslims. It encourages humility and refraining from declaring anyone's eternal fate without divine testimony.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وندين بأن لا ننزل أحداً من أهل التوحيد والمتمسكين بالإيمان جنةً ولا ناراً إلا من شهد له رسول الله بالجنة، ونرجو الجنة للمذنبين، ونخاف عليهم أن يكونوا بالنار معذبين
And we hold by creed that we do not assign anyone from the people of tawhid and those adhering to faith to paradise nor hell, except for one for whom the Messenger of Allah testified to paradise, and we hope for paradise for the sinners, and we fear for them that they may be punished in hell.
Source pages p. 49

Commentary
This passage states a key Ashari doctrine: we do not pass final judgment on the eternal destiny of any individual Muslim, whether righteous or sinful, except those explicitly promised paradise by the Prophet. It then balances hope for the sinners with fear of punishment, reflecting the Ahl al-Sunnah position of avoiding both despair and presumption.

Key Terms
أهل التوحيد
المذنبين
شهد له رسول الله بالجنة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think we cannot have any hope for sinners' salvation because we refrain from judging them.
Correction: The passage explicitly states 'we hope for paradise for the sinners,' showing that hope and fear coexist.

Checkpoint
What is the stance of Ahl al-Sunnah regarding judging a believer's final abode according to this passage?
A) They judge based on apparent deeds.
B) They do not judge except by prophetic testimony.
C) They declare all sinners will go to hell.
D) They guarantee paradise for all believers.

Key Takeaway
Ahl al-Sunnah refrain from judging any individual's final destiny except by explicit prophetic testimony, maintaining a balanced attitude of hope for paradise for sinners and fear of punishment.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
Can you name one person in your life whom you have already 'written off' spiritually? What would it take for you to entrust their final end to Allah alone, as this passage commands?

Faith: Speech and Action, Increase and Decrease
Defines faith as speech and action, with increase and decrease, and accepts authentic hadith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the classical Sunni position that faith comprises both speech and action, can increase and decrease, and is rooted in authentic prophetic traditions.

Core Idea
Faith is both belief and practice, and it can grow or diminish.

This passage from Imam al-Ash'ari's al-Ibanah presents a concise definition of faith that would become a hallmark of Sunni orthodoxy. It affirms that faith is not merely inner conviction but includes outward action, and that it is subject to change. The authority for this view is traced through reliable chains of transmission.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the key elements: faith defined as speech and action, its variability, and the appeal to authentic hadith.
The Arabic is concise; each phrase carries weight.
2
Identify the three components of faith
The passage mentions that faith is 'speech and action' and that it 'increases and decreases'. Recognize that these are two distinct affirmations: the composition of faith and its variability.
This counters views that faith is only belief or only confession.
3
Understand the evidential basis
The passage grounds its claims in authentic hadith transmitted by trustworthy narrators. This shows that the definition is not merely rational but based on revelation.
The phrase 'thiqat 'adl 'an 'adl' emphasizes the reliability of the chain.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأن الإيمان قول وعمل، يزيد وينقص، ونسلم الروايات الصحيحة في ذلك عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم التي رواها الثقات عدل عن عدل حتى تنتهي إلى رسول الله
And that faith is speech and action, it increases and decreases, and we submit to the authentic narrations concerning that from the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, which have been transmitted by trustworthy narrators, reliable from reliable, until they reach the Messenger of Allah.
Source pages p. 49

Commentary
This passage defines faith (iman) as comprising both speech (qawl) and action (amal), and affirms its variability—increase and decrease. It also endorses accepting the authentic hadith on this topic through reliable chains (thiqat 'adl 'an 'adl), grounding this belief in revealed sources.

Key Terms
الإيمان
قول
عمل
يزيد وينقص
الثقات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some sects (e.g., Murji'ah) say faith does not increase or decrease.
Correction: This passage affirms that faith does increase and decrease, aligning with Sunni orthodoxy.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, faith is:
A) Only speech.
B) Only action.
C) Speech and action, increasing and decreasing.
D) Speech, action, and intention, increasing but not decreasing.

Key Takeaway
Faith, in Sunni Islam, is a combination of inner speech (conviction) and outer action, and it can vary in strength based on obedience and sin.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If your faith truly increases with obedience and decreases with sin, examine your last 24 hours. Which actions have you performed that increased your faith, and which have decreased it? Be honest with yourself and repent for the latter right now.
?
Do you live as though your actions are part of your faith, or do you separate belief from practice? Identify one area where you are inconsistent—perhaps a duty you neglect or a sin you excuse. What will you change today to bring your action in line with your belief?
?
The Prophet's narrations on faith are transmitted through trustworthy chains. Have you truly submitted to what he taught about faith increasing and decreasing, or do you hold on to a comfortable notion that faith is static? Ask Allah to grant you certainty and the resolve to act.

Loving the Salaf with Allah's Praise
Declares love for the Salaf and praising them with Allah's praise.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Ahl al-Sunnah love the Salaf and praise them exactly as Allah praised them in the Quran, without exaggeration or innovation.

Core Idea
Praise the Salaf as Allah praised them — nothing more, nothing less.

This short but powerful sentence summarizes the stance of Ahl al-Sunnah towards the early generations: profound love, praise only from the Quran, and collective allegiance. Before reading, reflect on how easy it is to either over-praise or neglect their true status. This lesson shows the balanced path.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Notice the three verbs: 'adhere to loving', 'praise', and 'befriend'. They form a complete attitude.
The Arabic is a single sentence but carries three distinct commitments.
2
Identify the three actions
List the three actions: 1) Love the Salaf, 2) Praise them with what Allah praised them with, 3) Befriend all of them. Reflect on why each is necessary.
Praising 'with what Allah praised them' is a restriction — we do not add our own praises.
3
Consider the balancing role
Think about the extremes this avoids: excessive veneration that leads to innovation, or neglect that leads to disrespect. This formula keeps the community balanced.
This is a common point of confusion; the commentary clarifies.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وندين بحب السلف الذين اختارهم الله تعالى لصحبة نبيه، ونثني عليهم بما أثنى الله به عليهم ونتولاهم أجمعين
And we adhere to loving the Salaf whom Allah, the Exalted, chose for the companionship of His Prophet, and we praise them with what Allah praised them with, and we befriend them all.
Source pages p. 49

Commentary
This passage states the doctrine of loving the Salaf (the early generations, especially the Companions) and praising them only as Allah praised them in the Quran. It also affirms allegiance (tawalli) to all of them collectively.

Key Terms
السلف
صحبة
التولي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that this doctrine forbids any criticism of the Companions, even factual historical critique.
Correction: The passage only requires praising them with Allah's praise; it does not claim they were sinless or that discussing their human shortcomings is prohibited.

Checkpoint
How does this passage say we should praise the Salaf?
A) With excessive praise.
B) With what Allah praised them.
C) With new invented praises.
D) With silence.

Key Takeaway
The correct way to honor the Salaf is to love them and praise them only as Allah has praised them in the Quran.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever find yourself adding extra praise to a Companion beyond what Allah has said, perhaps out of personal admiration? How can you check your speech against this passage?
?
Is your love for the Salaf rooted in their being chosen by Allah, or in cultural or emotional attachments? What would it take for your heart to align fully with this teaching?
?
When you hear others speak about the Salaf in exaggerated or critical ways, are you willing to gently correct based on this principle? Will you commit to only praising with Allah's praise starting today?

Core Idea
We affirm paradise only for those whom the Prophet explicitly promised.

This lesson addresses a specific article of faith: testifying that the ten companions whom the Prophet directly promised paradise are indeed in paradise. This is not a general statement about all Muslims but a precise act of testimony based on clear textual evidence.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic sentence carefully. Then read the literal translation. Note the key phrase 'the ten' and 'testify'.
2
Identify the doctrine
The passage affirms a specific belief: that we testify to paradise for a specific group. Understand that this is limited to the ten explicitly named in hadith.
3
Reflect on the exclusivity
Consider why this doctrine is important: it shows that certainty about paradise is only from explicit divine or prophetic testimony, not from personal opinion.
4
Connect to Ahl al-Sunnah

Praying Behind Any Imam: Pious or Sinful
Al-Ash'ari affirms praying behind righteous and sinful imams.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that according to Al-Ash'ari, the validity of congregational prayer is not dependent on the imam's personal righteousness, as demonstrated by the practice of Abdullah ibn Umar.

Core Idea
Prayer behind a sinful imam remains valid.

Al-Ash'ari presents a clear article of faith regarding congregational prayer: it is to be performed behind any imam, regardless of his moral state. This teaching is rooted in the practice of a prominent companion, Abdullah ibn Umar, who prayed behind the controversial governor al-Hajjaj. This lesson examines this ruling and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic sentence from Al-Ash'ari's creed and its English translation. Notice the direct and declarative style.
The passage is a single sentence that encapsulates a key doctrinal position.
2
Identify the core claim
The passage asserts that it is part of 'our religion' to pray Friday, Eid, and all congregational prayers behind every person, whether pious (bar) or sinful (fajir).
The term 'fajir' covers anyone openly committing sins.
3
Recognize the evidence
Al-Ash'ari supports the claim by citing the practice of Abdullah ibn Umar, who prayed behind al-Hajjaj, a notorious Umayyad governor known for injustice.
This shows that the companion himself acted on this principle.
4
Understand the implication
The validity of the prayer is not contingent upon the imam's righteousness. This refutes those who might make the imam's moral state a condition for the soundness of the congregation.
This is a practical ruling that emphasizes unity and avoids division.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومن ديننا أن نصلي الجمعة والأعياد خلف كل بر وفاجر، كذلك وسائر الصلوات والجماعات كما روي عن عبد الله بن عمر رضي الله عنهما أنه كان يصلي خلف الحجاج
And from our religion is that we pray the Friday (prayer) and the two Eid prayers behind every pious and sinful person, likewise all other prayers and congregations, as narrated from Abdullah ibn Umar (may Allah be pleased with them both) that he used to pray behind al-Hajjaj.
Source pages p. 52

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal statement (article of faith) from Al-Ash'ari's creed. It declares that it is part of the Ash'ari belief to perform the Friday prayer and the two Eid prayers behind any imam, whether he is pious (bar) or sinful (fajir). This ruling is extended to all other prayers and congregational prayers. The author supports this position by citing the practice of the companion Abdullah ibn Umar, who prayed behind the notorious Umayyad governor al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, known for his tyranny. This demonstrates that the validity of the prayer is not contingent upon the moral state of the imam. The statement implicitly refutes those who would make the imam's righteousness a condition for the validity of collective worship.

Key Terms
صلاة الجمعة
صلاة العيد
إمامة
بر
فاجر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that praying behind a sinful imam invalidates the prayer or is impermissible.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari explicitly states that it is part of the creed to pray behind every pious and sinful person, and he substantiates this with the practice of Ibn Umar praying behind al-Hajjaj.

Checkpoint
What does Al-Ash'ari state about praying behind a sinful imam?
A) It is not permissible.
B) It is permissible, citing the practice of Ibn Umar.
C) It is only permissible in Friday prayer.
D) It is forbidden and invalidates the prayer.

Key Takeaway
The validity of congregational prayer does not depend on the imam's personal righteousness, in line with the practice of the Companions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever hesitated to pray behind an imam because you doubted his character? What does this lesson teach you about your own arrogance in judging the validity of your worship based on the imam's sinfulness? How will you change your attitude in the next congregational prayer?
?
If you knew that your imam committed a major sin, would you still pray behind him with full presence of heart? Or would you let your knowledge of his sin distract you? What does your reaction reveal about your understanding of sincerity in worship?
?
The companion Ibn Umar prayed behind al-Hajjaj, a tyrant. Are you willing to put aside your personal grievances and follow the congregation even if you disagree with the leader's lifestyle? How does your ego get in the way of unity in worship?
