Punishment of the Grave and the Appeal to Consensus
Al-Ash'ari accuses them of denying punishment of the grave and cites consensus.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why al-Ash'ari appeals to consensus to affirm the punishment of the grave, and how this relates to his broader argument against deviant sects.

Core Idea
Denying the punishment of the grave contradicts the unanimous agreement of the early Muslim community.

Al-Ash'ari continues listing the beliefs that the deviant sects have rejected. In this passage, he accuses them of denying the punishment of the grave, a doctrine firmly established by the consensus of the Companions and Successors. This lesson examines how al-Ash'ari uses communal agreement as a proof against their denial.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the literal translation. Notice the two key claims: the denial and the affirmation of consensus.
The passage is very short, so pay attention to every word.
2
Identify the two components
The passage has two parts: 1) the accusation that they denied punishment of the grave, and 2) the counter-proof that the early community agreed on it. This structure is typical of al-Ash'ari's method: state the error, then present the orthodox evidence.
3
Recognize the role of consensus
Al-Ash'ari appeals to 'ijma' (consensus) as a binding authority. For the classical Sunni tradition, consensus of the Companions and Successors is a definitive proof. The denial of an issue on which there is consensus is considered innovation.
Consensus is one of the primary sources of Islamic law and theology.
4
Reflect on the rhetorical strategy
By citing consensus, al-Ash'ari positions his opponents as outside the mainstream community. This is a powerful rhetorical move: it frames the debate as a matter of communal orthodoxy versus individual opinion.
This approach is consistent throughout the Ibanah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجحدوا عذاب القبر، وأن الكفار في قبورهم يعذبون، وقد أجمع على ذلك الصحابة والتابعون - رضي الله عنهم أجمعين -
And they denied the punishment of the grave, and that the disbelievers are punished in their graves, while the Companions and the Successors, may Allah be pleased with them all, have agreed upon that.
Source pages p. 38

Commentary
This is a third accusation. Al-Ash'ari asserts that the deviant sects reject the concept of ‘adhab al-qabr (punishment of the grave). He specifies that they deny that disbelievers are punished in their graves. Al-Ash'ari then appeals to consensus (ijma‘): the Companions and the Successors all agreed on this matter, making the rejection a violation of established communal agreement.

Key Terms
عذاب القبر
الكفار
الإجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
On what basis does al-Ash'ari affirm punishment of the grave?
A) Rational deduction
B) Consensus of the early generations
C) Personal opinion
D) Philosophical argument

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari affirms the punishment of the grave by appealing to the consensus of the early Muslim community, making its denial a departure from orthodox belief.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe in the punishment of the grave? How does that belief affect your actions today? If you are honest, have you been careless about matters the Companions and Successors unanimously affirmed?
?
When you hear 'consensus of the Companions and Successors', do you submit to their authority, or do you rationalize away clear teachings? Where in your life do you need to align your belief with theirs?
?
Imagine standing in your grave under the questioning of Munkar and Nakir. Are you preparing for that moment? What have you done today to ensure you are among those who will be saved from the punishment?

Refuting the Denial of Exit from Hell
Refutation of Qadariyyah denial of exiting Hell, citing hadith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Qadariyyah's position that Hell is permanent for all who enter, and how a prophetic hadith refutes it by showing that God will remove some people after purification.

Core Idea
The Fire can purify and some will be removed, contrary to Qadariyyah's denial.

The Qadariyyah held a harsh view on Hell: once in, never out. But the Prophet ﷺ taught that God's mercy can bring people out after purification. Imam al-Ash'ari cites this hadith to refute their claim. Let's see how a single prophetic report overturns a whole theological position.

Guided Path
1
Read the Qadariyyah's claim
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the direct quote of their position: 'whoever enters the Fire will not come out of it.' This is the view being refuted.
This is a stark claim about the permanence of Hell for all its inhabitants.
2
Identify the refutation
Imam al-Ash'ari immediately contrasts their claim with 'contrary to what is reported from the Messenger of God.' The hadith is the authority that overturns their position.
The refutation is based on transmitted revelation, not rational argument alone.
3
Understand the hadith's content
The hadith says God will bring out some people after they have been 'burned and become embers.' This shows that Hell is not necessarily eternal for all; some are purified and then released.
The word 'امتحشوا' implies severe burning, yet even they are saved.
4
Reflect on the theological implication
This passage refutes the Qadariyyah by affirming intercession and God's mercy. It also teaches that punishment can be a means of purification for some believers.
This connects to the broader Sunni belief in intercession and God's mercy.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وزعموا أن من دخل النار لا يخرج منها، خلافاً لما جاءت به الرواية عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم أن الله عز وجل يخرج من النار قوماً بعد أن امتحشوا فيها وصاروا حمماً.
And they claimed that whoever enters the Fire will not come out of it, contrary to what is reported from the Messenger of God, peace be upon him, that God Almighty will bring out from the Fire some people after they have been burned in it and become embers.
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This passage refutes the Qadariyyah's claim that Hell is eternal for all who enter it, citing a hadith that God will eventually remove some people from Hell after they are purified by fire.

Key Terms
الخروج من النار
الشفاعة
امتحشوا

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Qadariyyah believed that everyone who enters Hell remains there forever, with no possibility of exit or intercession.
Correction: The Prophet ﷺ taught that God will remove some people from Hell after they have been purified by fire, showing that exit is possible and intercession is valid.

Checkpoint
What does the hadith mentioned prove against the Qadariyyah's claim?
A) Everyone will enter Hell
B) Some people will be taken out of Hell
C) Hell is eternal for all
D) No one will leave Hell

Key Takeaway
The Qadariyyah's denial of any exit from Hell is refuted by the Prophet's hadith that God will remove some people after they have been purified by fire.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to be judged and found guilty of sins that deserve Hell, would this hadith give you any hope? In what way does that hope change how you view God's character today?
?
This hadith says God removes people after they have become 'embers'. Does the severity of the punishment before removal make you take your sins more seriously? How should it affect your repentance?
?
Do you sometimes assume that God's mercy is limited or that His punishment is purely retributive without any redemptive purpose? How does this passage challenge that assumption? What will you do the next time you feel despair of God's mercy?

The Prophetic Refutation of Eternal Hell for Believers
Al-Ash'ari refutes the claim of eternal Hell by citing a hadith about the exit of ember-like people.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses a prophetic hadith to refute the claim that no one ever leaves Hellfire, affirming the possibility of intercession and eventual exit for sinful believers.

Core Idea
Hellfire is not eternal for all; some believers will be brought out after purification.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents his fifteenth accusation against certain heretical groups who claim that anyone who enters Hellfire never leaves. He counters this with a well-known hadith of the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) that explicitly states Allah will bring people out after they have been completely burnt and turned into embers. This passage is crucial for understanding the Ash'ari position on the eventual salvation of sinful Muslims.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to grasp the basic claim and counter-evidence.
Notice the contrast between 'they claimed' and 'contrary to what came.'
2
Identify the heretical claim and its refutation
Find the statement of the heretical group and then the prophetic narration that contradicts it. Understand that Al-Ash'ari uses a text from revelation to refute a rationalist claim.
3
Reflect on the theological implication
Consider why this hadith is significant: it shows that even after severe punishment, divine mercy can prevail. This supports the Ash'ari view that intercession is effective for sinful believers.
This does not imply everyone leaves; only those with a grain of faith.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وزعموا أن من دخل النار لا يخرج منها، خلافا لما جاءت به الرواية عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم: "أن الله عز وجل يخرج من النار قوماً بعد أن امتحشوا فيها وصاروا حمماً"
And they claimed that whoever enters the Fire will not come out of it, contrary to what has come in the narration from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him: 'Indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will bring out people from the Fire after they have been burned up in it and become embers.'
Source pages p. 40

Commentary
This is the fifteenth accusation: the heretics assert that once a person enters Hellfire, they never leave. Al-Ash'ari counters this with a well-known hadith (narrated by Abu Huraira, among others) where the Prophet states that Allah will eventually bring out people from Hell after they have been completely burned and turned into embers. This supports the Ash'ari position that even those Muslims who entered Hell for their sins may eventually be interceded for and removed, contrary to the Mu'tazili and Khariji view of eternal punishment for grave sinners.

Key Terms
لا يخرج
امتحشوا
حمماً

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: No one ever leaves Hellfire; once in, always in.
Correction: The hadith explicitly states that Allah will bring out people from Hell after they have been purified by fire, showing that some do exit.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith cited, what condition are the people brought out of Hell in?
A) They are unharmed
B) They are turned to embers after being burned
C) They are still alive
D) They are pleading for mercy

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Ash'ari uses a prophetic hadith to prove that some people will eventually leave Hellfire after being completely burnt, affirming divine mercy and intercession.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that Allah's mercy can overcome your worst sins, or do you secretly fear that your sins are too great for even Allah to forgive? How does this hadith challenge that fear?
?
If you knew that your most shameful sins could lead you to Hellfire for purification, would that change how you view your disobedience today? What would you do differently?
?
How does knowing that Allah will eventually bring out some believers from Hell after they have been burnt affect your hope for those you love who have fallen into grave sin? Are you willing to intercede for them through your own faith?

Belief in Paradise, Hell, and Resurrection
Al-Ash'ari affirms four eschatological realities.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand four essential eschatological beliefs affirmed by Imam al-Ash'ari: the reality of Paradise, Hell, the coming of the Hour, and bodily resurrection.

Core Idea
Paradise, Hell, the Hour, and resurrection are undeniably real.

Imam al-Ash'ari begins a new section on the afterlife with a crisp affirmation of four core realities. These are not symbolic or metaphorical—they are literal truths that every Muslim must believe. This short passage anchors the Sunni creed on the final destination and the inevitable event that precedes it.

Guided Path
1
Read the creedal statement
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the decisive tone: 'true' (حق) and 'no doubt' (لا ريب فيها).
Al-Ash'ari uses the same word 'حق' for both Paradise and Hell, emphasizing their equal certainty.
2
Identify the four beliefs
List the four distinct beliefs: Paradise is true, Hell is true, the Hour is coming without doubt, and Allah will resurrect those in the graves.
These are not merely hopes but foundational doctrinal positions.
3
Understand the significance
Reflect on why Al-Ash'ari starts with these. They counter those who allegorize the afterlife or deny resurrection. The 'Hour' here means the Day of Judgment.
In Sunni kalam, these are matters of sam‘iyyat (scriptural hearing) that must be accepted literally.
4
Test your understanding
Answer the checkpoint question to ensure you grasp the negation: the Hour comes without doubt.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٢٦ - وأن الجنة حق والنار حق، وأن الساعة آتية لا ريب فيها، وأن الله يبعث من في القبور
26. And that Paradise is true, and Hell is true, and that the Hour is coming, no doubt in it, and that Allah will resurrect those in the graves.
Source pages p. 44

Commentary
This passage is a creedal affirmation of eschatological realities. Al-Ash'ari states four essential beliefs: (1) Paradise is a real place, (2) Hell is a real place, (3) the Day of Judgment (the Hour) will undoubtedly occur, and (4) Allah will resurrect the dead from their graves. This is the standard Sunni position, reaffirming the literal truth of the afterlife as described in the Quran and Sunnah, against those who might allegorize or deny these events.

Key Terms
الجنة
النار
الساعة
البعث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Paradise, Hell, and resurrection are metaphorical or symbolic.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari insists they are literal truths: 'حق' (true) and 'لا ريب فيها' (no doubt in it).

Checkpoint
According to this passage, which of the following is not affirmed?
A) Paradise is true
B) Hell is true
C) The Hour will come with doubt
D) Allah will resurrect the dead

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari affirms that Paradise, Hell, the Hour, and resurrection are real and certain, leaving no room for doubt or allegorical interpretation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that Paradise and Hell are real places, or do you sometimes act as if they are distant metaphors? What would change in your daily choices if you lived with absolute certainty that the Hour is coming without doubt?
?
When you think of your grave, do you expect Allah to resurrect you? How does that belief affect your preparation for that moment starting today?
?
This passage affirms no doubt in the Hour. Is there any part of your life where you live as if the Hour might not come? What concrete action can you take today to close that gap and live in its reality?

Seeing Allah in the Hereafter: The Beatific Vision
Al-Ash'ari affirms the beatific vision as an article of faith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni doctrine that Allah will be seen in the Hereafter with the physical eyes, as affirmed by Al-Ash'ari from hadith.

Core Idea
Believers will see Allah in the afterlife, as clearly as the full moon, without modality.

The question of whether Allah can be seen in the Hereafter was a major point of contention between Sunni theologians and the Mu'tazila. Al-Ash'ari here states the Sunni position clearly: belief in the beatific vision is a religious duty, grounded in authentic hadith. He uses the familiar image of the full moon to illustrate the clarity of this vision, while avoiding any suggestion of corporeality.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic text and its English translation carefully. Notice the key phrase "nadeen" (we hold as a religious duty) which indicates this is an article of faith, not a speculative opinion.
The passage is from Al-Ash'ari's Al-Ibanah, a foundational Sunni creedal work.
2
Understand the comparison to the full moon
Al-Ash'ari compares seeing Allah to seeing the full moon on a clear night. This emphasizes the vision's reality and clarity, not that Allah resembles the moon. It is a metaphorical illustration from a well-known hadith.
The hadith about seeing Allah like the full moon is narrated in Bukhari and Muslim.
3
Recognize the source of authority
The phrase "as has come in the narrations from the Messenger of Allah" shows that this belief is based on transmitted reports (samiyyat), not rational deduction. This distinguishes the Sunni approach from the Mu'tazila who relied on reason to deny vision.
4
Reflect on the refutation of opposing views
The Mu'tazila and other sects denied the possibility of seeing Allah, arguing it implies direction or corporeality. Al-Ash'ari's affirmation refutes them by grounding the vision in revelation while maintaining tanzih (transcendence).
The Sunni position affirms the vision 'without modality' (bila kayf).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٤٢ - وندين بأن الله تعالى يُرى في الآخرة (٥) بالأبصار، كما يُرى القمر ليلة البدر يراه المؤمنون كما جاءت الروايات عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم
42. And we hold as a religious duty that Allah, exalted is He, will be seen in the Hereafter with the eyes, just as the moon is seen on the night of the full moon – the believers will see Him, as has come in the narrations from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him.
Source pages p. 47

Commentary
This passage affirms the Sunni doctrine of ru'yat Allah (seeing Allah) in the Hereafter. Al-Ash'ari states that it is a religious obligation (nadeen) to believe that Allah will be seen in the afterlife with the physical eyes (bil-absar). He compares it to seeing the full moon, a well-known hadith. This refutes the Mu'tazila and other sects who denied the possibility of seeing Allah in the Hereafter, arguing that Allah is not in a place or direction. Al-Ash'ari bases this on authentic hadith reports from the Prophet. The phrase 'as has come in the narrations' indicates reliance on transmitted sources (samiyyat) for this belief.

Key Terms
رؤية الله
الآخرة
الأبصار
القمر ليلة البدر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Believing that affirming the beatific vision implies Allah is visible like a physical object or limited in space.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari affirms physical vision of Allah in the Hereafter, but the comparison to the moon is only to illustrate clarity, not to suggest corporeality. The Sunni doctrine is that Allah will be seen without modality (bila kayf).

Checkpoint
How does al-Ash'ari describe the manner in which believers will see Allah in the Hereafter?
A) With the heart, not the eyes
B) With the eyes, like seeing the full moon
C) Through intellectual understanding
D) It is impossible to see Allah

Key Takeaway
Believing in the beatific vision of Allah in the Hereafter is a religious duty, based on explicit hadith, and it does not compromise Allah's transcendence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly long to see Allah with your own eyes, or have you become content with knowing about Him intellectually? What would change in your life if you lived with the certainty that you will see Him as clearly as the full moon?
?
If you were to die tonight, would you have any doubt that you will see Allah? What is the state of your certainty (yaqeen) regarding this promise, and how does that affect your actions today?
?
The Prophet described the vision of Allah as the greatest joy of Paradise. Is seeing Allah your greatest desire? Or have you allowed worldly desires to eclipse that hope? Name one concrete thing you can do today to increase your longing for that vision.

Intercession of the Prophet for Sinners
Al-Ash'ari affirms intercession for punished sinners.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni doctrine of the Prophet's intercession for sinful Muslims who have been punished in Hell.

Core Idea
The Prophet's intercession rescues even those charred in Hell.

This lesson focuses on a key aspect of Sunni eschatology: the intercession of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ for sinful members of his community. Al-Ash'ari affirms that Allah will remove some people from Hell after they have been punished, through the Prophet's intercession, based on authentic traditions.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Start by reading the original Arabic and its English translation to grasp the core claim about intercession.
Notice the word 'imtahashu' (charred) – it indicates they experienced punishment.
2
Understand the term 'imtahashu'
This term shows that the people were burned in the Fire before being brought out. Intercession does not mean they escape punishment entirely.
3
Reflect on the basis in narrations
Al-Ash'ari grounds this belief in hadiths from the Prophet. Consider how this doctrine relies on transmitted reports (samiyyat).
This is a matter of samiyyat (revelational knowledge), not rational deduction.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٤٦ - ونقول: إن الله عز وجل يخرج من النار قوماً بعد أن امتحشوا بشفاعة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم، تصديقاً لما جاءت به الروايات عن رسول الله.
46 - And we say: Allah Almighty will bring out from the Fire a people after they have been charred (imtahashu) through the intercession of the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, confirming what has come in narrations from the Messenger of Allah.
Source pages p. 48

Commentary
This passage affirms the Sunni doctrine of intercession (shafa'ah) for sinful Muslims. Al-Ash'ari states that Allah will remove some individuals from Hell after they have been punished (burned) due to the Prophet's intercession. This is based on authentic hadiths. The word 'imtahashu' indicates they have been consumed by the fire but not annihilated, and by the Prophet's request, they are brought out.

Key Terms
الشفاعة
النار
امتحشوا

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think intercession means sinners are forgiven without any punishment.
Correction: The passage shows they are punished ('charred') first, then brought out by intercession.

Checkpoint
What happens to the people mentioned in this passage before they are brought out of the Fire?
A) They are not punished at all
B) They have been charred (imtahashu)
C) They immediately go to Paradise
D) They are forgiven without punishment

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's intercession is a real means by which Allah brings out sinners from Hell after they have been punished, confirming the narrations.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This passage teaches that intercession occurs only after punishment. Does knowing that you will face the consequences of your sins before any intercession change how you view your actions today? What specific sin do you need to take more seriously as a result?
?
Do you truly believe that the Prophet's intercession can reach even the most charred sinners, or do you subconsciously limit God's mercy? What does your heart say about your own worthiness of intercession?
?
Al-Ash'ari bases this belief on narrations, not rational proof. How does that challenge your reliance on your own reasoning in matters of faith? Are you willing to accept doctrines solely because the Prophet taught them, even if they seem difficult to comprehend?

Unseen Matters
beginner
4 min
Belief in Punishment of the Grave and Hereafter Signs
Al-Ash'ari enumerates seven eschatological beliefs as true.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Al-Ash'ari affirms several eschatological beliefs as literal realities, including punishment of the grave, the Basin, the Scale, the Bridge, resurrection, standing before Allah, and reckoning.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari's creed insists on the literal reality of the afterlife events.

The source passage is a concise enumeration of core Sunni beliefs about the afterlife. Al-Ash'ari lists seven eschatological realities without allegorization, affirming them as true. This lesson unpacks each of these beliefs as part of the Sunni creed.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the literal translation to understand the surface meaning.
Notice the concise style; each item is stated as a separate truth.
2
Identify each eschatological item
List out the seven items: punishment of the grave, the Basin, the Scale, the Bridge, resurrection, the standing place, and reckoning.
These are all part of the Sunni creed on the afterlife.
3
Reflect on the word 'haqq'
Each item is described as 'haqq' (true). Consider what this implies about their ontological reality.
Al-Ash'ari is asserting that these are not mere metaphors but actual events.
4
Contrast with allegorical interpretations
Think about why some groups allegorize these concepts. How does Al-Ash'ari's affirmation counter that?
The straightforward affirmation is a hallmark of Sunni orthodoxy.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٤٧ - ونؤمن بعذاب القبر ونقول: إنَّ الحوض والميزان حق ، والصراط حق والبعث بعد الموت حق وأن الله عز وجل يوقف العباد في الموقف، ويحاسب المؤمنين.
47 - And we believe in the punishment of the grave, and we say: Indeed the Basin (Hawd) and the Scale (Mizan) are true, and the Bridge (Sirat) is true, and the resurrection after death is true, and that Allah Almighty stops the servants at the standing place (Mawqif) and will reckon with the believers.
Source pages p. 48

Commentary
This passage enumerates several eschatological beliefs: punishment of the grave (a trial in the grave for the soul), the Basin (Hawd) of the Prophet where believers drink before entering Paradise, the Scale (Mizan) weighing deeds, the Bridge (Sirat) over Hell, the physical resurrection after death, and the standing before Allah for judgment. Al-Ash'ari affirms all as true and part of the Sunni creed.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT listed as true in this passage?
A) The Basin (Hawd)
B) The Scale (Mizan)
C) The Bridge (Sirat)
D) The vision of Allah

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's creed insists on the literal truth of punishment of the grave, Hawd, Mizan, Sirat, resurrection, standing, and reckoning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
The Bridge (Sirat) is sharp and real. If you were to die tonight, what would be your hope to cross it safely? What concrete deed can you add to your scale today?

Intercession and Removal from Hellfire
Affirms the Prophet's intercession and removal of some from hell after punishment

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari doctrine of intercession (shafa'a) and its specific timing: sinful believers are removed from hell after punishment, not before.

Core Idea
Intercession saves believers from hell only after they have been punished.

This brief passage from al-Ibana affirms a key Ash'ari belief about intercession. It states that the Prophet's intercession will remove some people from hell after they have suffered punishment, based on authentic hadith. This clarifies the scope and timing of intercession, correcting a common oversimplification.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Begin by reading the Arabic text aloud, then read the literal English translation. Notice the precise wording: 'after they have been burned'.
Pay attention to the word 'امتحشوا' (imtahashu) which implies severe burning.
2
Identify the three key terms
Locate the terms 'شفاعة' (intercession), 'امتحشوا' (they were burned), and 'الروايات' (narrations). Understand that the doctrine is rooted in transmitted hadith.
These terms are foundational for understanding the Ash'ari position on intercession.
3
Reflect on the timing of intercession
The passage explicitly states intercession happens after people have been burned. This contradicts the idea that intercession prevents all punishment. Consider why this distinction matters.
This reflects the Ash'ari view that intercession benefits those who have already entered hell and been punished.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونقول: إن الله عز وجل يخرج من النار قوماً بعد أن امتحشوا بشفاعة رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم، تصديقاً لما جاءت به الروايات عن رسول الله
And we say: Indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, will bring out from the Hellfire a people after they have been burned through the intercession of the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, in confirmation of what has come in the narrations from the Messenger of Allah.
Source pages p. 49

Commentary
This passage affirms the doctrine of intercession (shafa'ah) for sinful believers. It states that Allah will remove some from hell after they have been punished (imtahashu meaning charred). This is based on authentic hadith, which are the source of this belief.

Key Terms
شفاعة
امتحشوا
الروايات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Intercession grants immediate entry to Paradise without any punishment for sinful believers.
Correction: The passage states that intercession occurs 'after they have been burned', meaning punishment precedes removal.

Checkpoint
What does the passage say about those taken out of hell by intercession?
A) They never entered hell.
B) They were taken out before punishment.
C) They were taken out after being burned.
D) They were taken out immediately.

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's intercession removes some believers from hell after they have been punished, confirming authentic hadith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that your own sins might lead you to enter Hellfire and be burned before any intercession, would your daily choices and habits change? What would you do differently starting today?
?
How often do you rely on the Prophet's intercession as a safety net for your sins without considering the reality of punishment? What is one specific sin you will commit to abandoning because of this lesson?
?
This passage shows that intercession comes after punishment, not before. Does your repentance today reflect the urgency of avoiding Hell, or are you assuming you will be saved without consequence? What step will you take today to show you take this seriously?

Affirmation of the Dajjal's Emergence
Imam Ash'ari affirms the Dajjal's emergence as narrated in hadith

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Sunni creed affirms the emergence of the Dajjal as a literal eschatological figure based on prophetic narration, not rational speculation.

Core Idea
The Dajjal is affirmed by transmitted report, not reason.

Imam Ash'ari here states one of the key eschatological beliefs of Ahl al-Sunnah: the emergence of the Dajjal (Antichrist) before the Day of Judgment. This brief sentence encapsulates the Sunni methodology of relying on revealed texts for matters of the unseen. As you read, notice the authoritative language and the source of authority invoked.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic sentence once, noting the key terms: ونقر, خروج الدجال, الرواية. Then read the translation.
The verb 'nuqirru' (we affirm) is a collective creedal statement.
2
Identify the source of authority
The passage states 'as the narration came from the Messenger of Allah'. This shows that the basis for affirming the Dajjal is prophetic hadith, not rational argument.
This counters those who deny or reinterpret eschatological events.
3
Reflect on the theological implication
Why does Imam Ash'ari mention the source? Because in matters of the unseen (sam'iyyat), the Sunni position is to accept what has been authentically reported, without adding or subtracting.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونقر بخروج الدجال كما جاءت به الرواية عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم.
And we affirm the emergence of the Dajjal (Antichrist) as the narration came from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him).
Source pages p. 52

Commentary
This passage is a brief creedal statement about the Dajjal, a figure who will appear before the Day of Judgment. Imam Ash'ari says that the Sunni position is to affirm (nuqirru bi-) the emergence of the Dajjal as it has been narrated (ka-ma ja'at bihi al-riwaya) from the Prophet (peace be upon him). This includes the well-known hadiths describing his fitna and the coming of Jesus to kill him. The affirmation is based on transmitted reports (riwaya), not rational deduction. This counters those who deny such eschatological events or reinterpret them allegorically.

Key Terms
إقرار
خروج الدجال
رواية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some deny the Dajjal as a literal figure or reinterpret it allegorically.
Correction: This passage asserts that the Dajjal's emergence is a literal event to be affirmed exactly as narrated in authentic hadith.

Checkpoint
On what basis does Imam Ash'ari affirm the emergence of the Dajjal?
A) Rational proof.
B) Consensus of scholars.
C) Narration from the Prophet.
D) Personal opinion.

Key Takeaway
The Sunni position on the Dajjal is to affirm his emergence based on prophetic narration, without seeking rational explanations.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly accept the reality of the Dajjal as a literal figure, or do you find yourself leaning toward symbolic interpretations when you encounter such teachings? What would change in your daily vigilance if you took his coming as certain?
?
The Prophet (saw) gave detailed descriptions of the Dajjal's fitna. How much of those descriptions have you internalized, and what concrete steps have you taken to protect your faith from his deception?
?
If the Dajjal were to appear tomorrow, would your knowledge of the prophetic reports be sufficient to recognize and resist him? What are you doing today to ensure that your faith is built on the transmitted narrations rather than personal opinion?

Belief in the Emergence of Dajjal
Al-Ash'ari affirms belief in Dajjal's emergence based on narration.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Al-Ash'ari affirms the emergence of the Dajjal as a matter of faith based solely on prophetic narration.

Core Idea
Belief in Dajjal's coming is rooted in prophetic narration, not rational speculation.

This lesson presents a concise article of faith from Al-Ash'ari's 'Al-Ibanah'. He states clearly that the Ash'ari creed includes affirming the emergence of the Dajjal (Antichrist) exactly as reported in the authentic narrations from the Prophet. This establishes that eschatological beliefs are taken from revelation, not human reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the original Arabic sentence slowly. Notice the verb 'nuqirr' (we affirm) which indicates a creedal statement.
The Arabic is concise and definitive.
2
Understand the affirmation
Al-Ash'ari uses 'nuqirr' which implies both acknowledgment and acceptance. Understand that this is not just intellectual assent but a declaration of faith.
3
Identify the basis: narration
The phrase 'kama ja'at bihi al-riwayah' shows that the basis is the transmitted report from the Prophet. This highlights the primacy of scripture in matters of the unseen.
4
Consider the theological implication

This connects to the misconception that Dajjal is merely symbolic.

Source Text

This is a belief based on scriptural authority (hadith) and is part of the Sunni creed. The Dajjal's emergence is a major sign of the Hour, and believing in it is obligatory.

Punishment of the Grave and Questioning by Munkar and Nakir
Al-Ash'ari affirms belief in grave punishment and Munkar and Nakir.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Al-Ash'ari affirms belief in the punishment of the grave and the questioning by the angels Munkar and Nakir as part of Sunni eschatology.

Core Idea
The grave holds an intermediate state of reward or punishment before the Final Judgment.

In this brief but weighty sentence, Al-Ash'ari declares the Sunni position on an unseen reality: the life of the grave. He affirms that the deceased experience a foretaste of their ultimate destiny and are questioned by two noble angels. This passage is a foundational creedal statement on matters that lie beyond human reason.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation once without analysis. Let the author's own words settle.
Notice the phrase 'We believe' — it signals collective Sunni creed.
2
Identify the three components
Find and list the three distinct affirmations: punishment of the grave, the angels Munkar and Nakir, and their questioning of the dead.
3
Reflect on the significance
Consider why this belief matters: it affirms an immediate, unseen reality after death, based on revelation.
This distinguishes Sunni Islam from groups that deny the punishment of the grave.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونؤمن بعذاب القبر، ومنكر ونكير عليهما الصلاة والسلام، ومساءلتهما المدفونين في قبورهم
And we believe in the punishment of the grave, and (the angels) Munkar and Nakir (peace be upon them both), and their questioning of those buried in their graves.
Source pages p. 53

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal statement on the afterlife. Al-Ash'ari affirms belief in three related eschatological matters: (1) the punishment of the grave (adhab al-qabr), which is an intermediate state of punishment or bliss before the Resurrection; (2) the two angels Munkar and Nakir, who are sent to question the deceased; and (3) their questioning (mas'alah) of those buried in their graves. The author sends peace upon these angels, acknowledging their role. This belief is based on authentic hadith and is a fundamental part of Sunni eschatology, distinguishing it from groups that deny the punishment of the grave.

Key Terms
عذاب القبر
منكر ونكير
مساءلة
قبر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the soul is not questioned until the Day of Judgment, or that the grave is only a resting place.
Correction: The passage affirms an immediate questioning by Munkar and Nakir in the grave, based on revelation.

Checkpoint
According to Al-Ash'ari, who questions the deceased in their graves?
A) The angel of death only.
B) The angels Munkar and Nakir.
C) The Prophet Muhammad.
D) No one; the soul sleeps until Judgment.

Key Takeaway
Belief in the punishment of the grave and questioning by Munkar and Nakir is a core tenet of Sunni eschatology.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If Munkar and Nakir questioned you in your grave right now, what would you answer about your faith and deeds? Is your life today preparing you to give a confident answer?
?
Do you truly believe that your actions in this life determine your state in the grave? What would change starting today if you lived with that certainty every moment?
?
You have heard that the grave is either a garden from Paradise or a pit from Hellfire. How would your habits, worship, and treatment of others shift if you knew your grave could begin to take shape with your next deed?

Belief in the Punishment of the Grave and the Questioning by Munkar and Nakir
Imam Ashari affirms the reality of the grave punishment and the questioning, citing the full hadith of the Prophet.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic belief in the punishment of the grave and the questioning of the deceased by the angels Munkar and Nakir, as based on the detailed hadith of the Prophet.

Core Idea
The grave is a real station of reward or punishment, and every soul is questioned by the angels.

This lesson presents Imam al-Ash'ari's affirmation of the reality of the grave's punishment and the questioning by Munkar and Nakir. He provides the full hadith of al-Bara' ibn 'Azib as evidence, which gives a vivid, sensory description of what happens to the believer and the disbeliever at death and in the grave. The hadith is one of the most detailed and widely cited narrations on this topic.

Guided Path
1
Read the creedal statement
Start with Imam al-Ash'ari's concise affirmation: we believe in the punishment of the grave and the questioning by Munkar and Nakir. This sets the doctrinal foundation.
Notice that he associates peace upon the angels.
2
Examine the believer's death and questioning
Read the portion of the hadith describing the death of a believer: the ease of soul extraction, the ascent, the questioning, and the reward. Pay attention to the sensory details.
The believer answers with certainty and receives expansion of the grave.
3
Examine the disbeliever's death and questioning
Now read the parallel account for the disbeliever: the painful extraction, the foul smell, the closed gates, the failed questioning, and the punishment.
The disbeliever's grave constricts and a hideous figure appears.
4
Reflect on the lesson's purpose
Consider why Imam al-Ash'ari includes this lengthy hadith. It serves as direct evidence for the realities of the grave, countering those who might interpret them metaphorically.
The hadith is cited from multiple trusted sources.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٥٩ - ونؤمن بعذاب القبر، ومنكر ونكير عليهما الصلاة والسلام، ومساءلتهما المدفونين في قبورهم. روى أحمد في ( المسند ( ٢٨٧/٤ و ٢٩٥ - ٢٩٦ ، والحاكم في ( المستدرك »، وأبو داود رقم (٤٢٥٣) في السنة : باب في المسألة في القبر وعذاب القبر، والنسائي ١٠١/٤ في الجنائز باب عذاب القبر، و ٣٧/١ - ٤٠ من حديث البراء بن عازب رضي الله عنه قال: كُنَّا فِي جِنَازَةِ فِي بَقِيعِ الْغَرْقَدِ، فَأَتَانَا النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم، فَقَعَدَ وَقَعَدْنَا حَوْلَهُ، كَأَنَّ على رؤوسِنَا الطَّيْرَ، وَهُوَ يُلْحَدُ له، فَقَالَ: أعُوذُ بالله مِنْ عَذَابِ الْقَبْرِ، ثلاث مَرَّاتٍ، ثُمَّ قَالَ: "إِنَّ الْعَبْدَ الْمُؤْمِنَ إِذَا كَانَ فِي إِقْبَالِ مِنَ الآخرة وَانْقِطَاعِ مِنَ الدُّنْيَا، نَزَلَتْ إِليه الملائكة، كَأَنَّ على وُجُوهِهِم الشَّمْسُ كَفَنْ مِنْ أَكْفَانِ الْجَنَّةَ، وَحَنُوطٌ من حَنُوطِ الْجَنَّةِ، فَجَلَسُوا مِنْهُ مَدَّ الْبَصَر، يَجْلِسَ عِندَ رَأْسِهِ، فَيَقُولُ: يَا أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الطَّيِّبة، أخرجي إلى مَغْفِرَةِ الله ورضوانه، قَالَ: فَتَخْرُجُ تَسِيلُ كَمَا تَسِيلُ الْقَطْرَة مِنْ فَمِ السَّقَاءِ، فَيَأْخُذَهَا، فَإِذَا أَخَذَهَا لَمْ يَدَعُوهَا فِي يَدِهِ طَرْفَة عين، حتى يَأْخُذُوهَا فَيَجْعَلُوهَا في ذَلِكَ الْكَفَنَ وَذَلِكَ الْحَنُوطِ، وَيَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا رِيحٌ كَأَطْيَبِ نَفْحَةِ مِسْكٍ وُجِدَتْ على وَجْهِ الْأَرْضِ. قَالَ: فَيَصْعَدُونَ بِهَا، فلا يَمُرُونَ بِهَا - يعني على مَلَإٍ مِنَ الملائكة - إِلَّا قَالُوا: مَا هَذِهِ الرُّوحُ الطَّيِّبَةُ؟ فَيَقُولُونَ: فُلَانُ ابْنُ فُلَانِ بِأَحْسَنِ أَسْمَائِه التي كَانُوا يُسَمُّونَه بِهَا فِي الدُّنْيَا، حتى ينتهوا بها إلى السَّمَاءِ، فَيَسْتَفْتِحُونَ له، فَيُفْتَحُ له، فَيُشَيَّعُه مِن كُلِّ سَمَاءِ مُقَرَّبُوهَا، إلى السَّمَاءِ التي تليها، حتى يُنْتَهَى بِهَا إِلى السَّمَاءِ التي فيها الله، فَيَقُولُ الله عَزَّ وَجَلَ: اكْتُبُوا كِتَابَ عَبْدِي فِي عِلِّيِّينَ، وَأَعِيدُوهُ إلى الأَرْضِ، فَإِنِّي مِنْهَا خَلَقْتُهُمْ، وَفِيهَا أُعِيدُهُمْ، وَمِنْهَا أُخْرِجُهُمْ تَارَةً أُخْرَى. قَالَ: فَتُعَادُ رُوحُهُ في جَسَدِهِ، فَيَأْتِيهِ مَلَكَانِ فَيُجْلِسَانِهِ، فَيَقُولَانِ له: مَنْ رَبُّكَ؟ فَيَقُولُ: رَبِّي الله، فَيَقُولَانِ له: مَا دِينَكَ؟ فَيَقُولُ: دِينِي الإسلام، فَيَقُولَانِ له: مَا هَذَا الرَّجُلُ الذي بُعِثَ فِيكُم؟ فَيَقُولُ: هُوَ رَسُولُ الله، فَيَقُولَانِ له: مَا عِلْمُكَ؟ فَيَقُولُ: قَرَأْتُ كِتَابَ الله فَآمَنْتُ بِهِ وَصَدَّقْتُ، فَيُنَادِي مُنَادٍ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ: أَنْ صَدَقَ عَبْدِي، فَأَفْرِسُوهُ فِرَاشًا مِنَ الْجَنَّةَ، وَافْتَحُوا لَهُ بَابًا إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ، قَالَ: فَيَأْتِيهِ مِنْ رَوْحِهَا وَطِيبِهَا، وَيُفْسَحُ لَهُ في قَبْرِهِ مَدَّ بَصَرِهِ، قَالَ: وَيَأْتِيهِ رَجُلٌ حَسَنُ الْوَجْهِ، حَسَنُ الشِّيَابِ، طَيِّبُ الرِّيحِ، فَيَقُولُ: أَبْشِرْ بِالَّذِي يَسُرُّكَ، هَذَا يَوْمُكَ الذي كُنْتَ تُوعَدُ، فَيَقُولُ لَهُ: مَنْ أَنْتَ؟ فَوَجْهُكَ الْوَجْهُ الذي يَجِيءُ بِالْخَيْرِ، فَيَقُولُ: أَنَا عَمَلُكَ الصَّالِحُ، فَيَقُولُ: يَا رَبِّ، أَقِمِ السَّاعَةَ حتى أَرْجِعَ إلى أَهْلِي وَمَالِي. قَالَ: وَإِنَّ الْعَبْدَ الْكَافِرَ إِذَا كَانَ فِي انْقِطَاعٍ مِنَ الدُّنْيَا وَإِقْبَالٍ مِنَ الآخِرَة، نَزَلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَلَائِكَةٌ فَيَجْلِسُونَ مِنْهُ مَدَّ الْبَصَرِ، ثُمَّ يَجِيءُ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ حَتَّى يَجْلِسَ عِنْدَ رَأْسِهِ، فَيَقُولُ: أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الْخَبِيثَةُ اخرجي إِلى سَخَطٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَغَضَبٍ، قَالَ: فَتَفَرَّقُ فِي جَسَدِهِ، فَتَنْزَعُهَا كَمَا يُنْزَعُ الْفَوْدُ مِنَ الصُّوفِ الْمَبْلُولِ، فَيَأْخُذُهَا، فَإِذَا أَخَذَهَا لَمْ يَدَعُوهَا فِي يَدِهِ طَرْفَةَ عَيْنٍ، حَتَّى يَجْعَلُوهَا فِي تِلْكَ الْمُسُوحِ، وَيَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا كَأَنْتَنِ رِيحٍ خَبِيثَةٍ وُجِدَتْ على وَجْهِ الْأَرْضِ، فَيَصْعَدُونَ بِهَا، فَلَا يَمُرُونَ بِهَا على مَلَإٍ مِنَ الْمَلَائكَةِ إِلَّا قَالُوا: مَا هَذَا الرُّوحُ الْخَبِيثُ؟ فَيَقُولُونَ: فُلَانُ ابْنُ فُلَانِ بِأَقْبَحِ أَسْمَائِهِ التي كَانَ يُسَمَّى بِهَا فِي الدُّنْيَا، حَتَّى يُنْتَهَى بِهَا إِلَى السَّمَاءِ الدُّنْيَا، فَيُسْتَفْتَحُ لَهُ، فَلَا يُفْتَحُ لَهُ، ثُمَّ قَرَأَ رَسُولُ الله صلى الله عليه وسلم: وَلَا نُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوَابُ السَّمَاءِ وَلَا يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ حَتَّى يَلِجَ الْجَمَلُ فِي سَمِّ الْخِيَاطِ، فَيَقُولُ الله عَزَّ وَجَلَّ: اكْتُبُوا كِتَابَهُ فِي سِجِّينٍ، فِي الْأَرْضِ السُّفْلَى، فَتُطْرَحُ رُوحُهُ طَرْحًا، ثُمَّ قَرَأَ: وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَكَأَنَّمَا خَرَّ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ فَتَخْطَفُهُ الطَّيْرُ أَوْ تَهْوِي بِهِ الرِّيحُ فِي مَكَانٍ سَحِيقٍ، فَتُعَادُ رُوحُهُ فِي جَسَدِهِ، وَيَأْتِيهِ مَلَكَانِ فَيُجْلِسَانِهِ، فَيَقُولَانِ لَهُ: مَنْ رَبُّكَ؟ فَيَقُولُ: هَاهْ هَاهْ، لَا أَدْرِي، فَيَقُولَانِ لَهُ: مَا هَذَا الرَّجُلُ الذي بُعِثَ فِيكُمْ، فَيَقُولُ: هَاهْ هَاهْ، لَا أَدْرِي، فَيُنَادِي مُنَادٍ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ: أَنْ كَذَبَ، فَافْرِشُوهُ فِرَاشًا مِنَ النَّارِ، وَافْتَحُوا لَهُ بَابًا إِلَى النَّارِ، فَيَأْتِيهِ مِنْ حَرِّهَا وَسَمُومِهَا، وَيَضِيقُ عَلَيْهِ قَبْرُهُ، حَتَّى تَخْتَلِفَ فِيهِ أَضْلَاعُهُ، وَيَأْتِيهِ رَجُلٌ قَبِيحُ الْوَجْهِ، قَبِيحُ الشِّيَابِ، مُنْتَنُ الرِّيحِ، فَيَقُولُ: أَبْشِرْ بِالَّذِي يَسُوءُكَ، هَذَا يَوْمُكَ الذي كُنْتَ تُوعَدُ، فَيَقُولُ: مَنْ أَنْتَ؟ فَوَجْهُكَ الْوَجْهُ الذي يَجِيءُ بِالشَّرِّ، فَيَقُولُ: أَنَا عَمَلَكَ الْخَبِيثُ، فَيَقُولُ: رَبِّ لَا تُقِمِ السَّاعَةَ).
59 - And we believe in the punishment of the grave, and in Munkar and Nakir, peace be upon them, and in their questioning of those buried in their graves. Ahmad narrated in al-Musnad (4/287 and 295-296), and al-Hakim in al-Mustadrak, and Abu Dawud number (4253) in as-Sunnah: Chapter on the questioning in the grave and the punishment of the grave, and an-Nasa'i 4/101 in al-Jana'iz: Chapter on the punishment of the grave, and [he narrated] from the hadith of al-Bara' ibn 'Azib, may Allah be pleased with him, who said: We were in a funeral procession in Baqi' al-Gharqad, and the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, came to us; he sat and we sat around him as if birds were on our heads, and he was being buried. He said: 'I seek refuge with Allah from the punishment of the grave,' three times, then he said: 'Indeed the believing servant, when he is in a state of turning away from the world and approaching the hereafter, angels descend to him, as if their faces are the sun, with a shroud from the shrouds of Paradise and perfumed oil from the perfumed oil of Paradise. They sit from him as far as the eye can see. Then the Angel of Death comes until he sits at his head and says: O good soul, come out to the forgiveness of Allah and His pleasure. He said: So it comes out flowing like a drop from the mouth of a waterskin. He takes it, and when he takes it, they do not leave it in his hand for the blink of an eye until they take it and place it in that shroud and that perfumed oil, and there comes out from it a fragrance like the most pleasant scent of musk found on the face of the earth. He said: Then they ascend with it, and they do not pass by any group of angels except they say: What is this good soul? They say: So-and-so, son of so-and-so, with the best of his names by which they used to call him in the world, until they bring it to the heaven. They ask permission for it, and it is opened for it. The close ones of each heaven escort it to the next heaven until it reaches the heaven in which Allah is. Allah, Exalted and Mighty, says: Write the book of My servant in "Illiyin", and return him to the earth, for from it I created them, into it I will return them, and from it I will bring them out another time. He said: So his soul is returned to his body, and two angels come and sit him up and say to him: Who is your Lord? He says: My Lord is Allah. They say: What is your religion? He says: My religion is Islam. They say: What about this man who was sent among you? He says: He is the Messenger of Allah. They say: What is your knowledge? He says: I read the Book of Allah, so I believed in it and affirmed it. Then a caller calls from heaven: My servant has spoken the truth, so spread a bed for him from Paradise and open for him a gate to Paradise. He said: So there comes to him its fragrance and its sweetness, and his grave is expanded for him as far as his eye can see. He said: And a man with a beautiful face, beautiful clothes, and a good smell comes to him and says: Rejoice with what delights you; this is the day you were promised. He says to him: Who are you? Your face is the face that brings good. He says: I am your righteous deeds. He says: O Lord, establish the Hour so that I may return to my family and my wealth. He said: As for the disbelieving servant, when he is in a state of turning away from the world and approaching the hereafter, angels descend to him from heaven and sit from him as far as the eye can see. Then the Angel of Death comes until he sits at his head and says: O evil soul, come out to the wrath of Allah and anger. He said: So it disperses throughout his body, and he pulls it out just as a tuft is pulled from wet wool. He takes it, and when he takes it, they do not leave it in his hand for the blink of an eye until they put it in that sackcloth, and there comes out from it the most foul stench of a rotten smell found on the face of the earth. They ascend with it, and they do not pass by any group of angels except they say: What is this evil soul? They say: So-and-so, son of so-and-so, with the ugliest of his names by which he used to be called in the world, until he is taken to the lowest heaven. Permission is requested for it, but it is not opened for him. Then the Messenger of Allah, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, recited: "The gates of heaven shall not be opened for them, nor shall they enter Paradise until a camel goes through the eye of a needle" (Quran 7:40). Allah, Exalted and Mighty, says: Write his book in Sijjin, in the lowest earth. So his soul is thrown away. Then he recited: "And whoever associates partners with Allah, it is as if he fell from the sky, so birds snatch him, or the wind blows him to a far place" (Quran 22:31). So his soul is returned to his body, and two angels come and sit him up and say to him: Who is your Lord? He says: Hah, hah, I do not know. They say: What about this man who was sent among you? He says: Hah, hah, I do not know. Then a caller calls from heaven: He has lied, so spread for him a bed from the Fire and open for him a gate to the Fire. So there comes to him its heat and its scorching wind, and his grave constricts upon him until his ribs interlock. And a man with an ugly face, ugly clothes, and a foul smell comes to him and says: Rejoice with what will distress you; this is the day you were promised. He says: Who are you? Your face is the face that brings evil. He says: I am your evil deeds. He says: O Lord, do not establish the Hour.
Source pages p. 53

Commentary
This passage is a creedal statement followed by a detailed hadith proof. The author, Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, begins by affirming the belief in the punishment of the grave (عذاب القبر) and the two angels Munkar and Nakir (منكر ونكير) who question the deceased. He then provides the full hadith of the Prophet (peace be upon him) narrated by al-Bara' ibn 'Azib as evidence. The hadith gives a vivid, sensory description of the death and questioning of both the believer and the disbeliever.

 For the believer: at death, angels appear with shrouds and perfume from Paradise. The soul flows out easily, is taken up, and is praised by the angels. It is taken to the heavens, and Allah commands the soul to be recorded in 'Illiyin and returned to the earth. The soul is reunited with the body, and then two angels ask the three questions: 'Who is your Lord?' (مَنْ رَبُّكَ), 'What is your religion?' (مَا دِينَكَ), and 'What about the man sent among you?' (مَا هَذَا الرَّجُلُ). The believer answers correctly, and a caller confirms his truthfulness. The grave is expanded, a beautiful figure appears representing his righteous deeds, and he is shown his place in Paradise.

 For the disbeliever: at death, angels appear with sackcloth. The soul is extracted painfully, and it emits a foul smell. The angels curse it, and it is taken to the lowest heaven but the gates are not opened. The soul is thrown back, and the disbeliever is questioned. He answers that he does not know, and a caller announces his lie. The grave constricts, a hideous figure appears representing his evil deeds, and he is shown his place in Hell.

 The author uses this hadith to substantiate the articles of faith regarding the unseen realities of the grave. The narrative emphasizes the literal, physical nature of these events, countering allegorical interpretations.

Key Terms
عذاب القبر
منكر ونكير
مساءلة القبر
روح
قبر
الملائكة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the punishment of the grave is purely metaphorical or spiritual, not a real physical event.
Correction: This hadith and the creedal statement affirm that the punishment of the grave and the questioning are literal, physical realities experienced by the deceased.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith narrated by al-Bara' ibn 'Azib, what is the first question the two angels ask the deceased?
A) What is your religion?
B) Who is your Lord?
C) What about this man who was sent among you?
D) What did you do in the world?

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Ash'ari grounds the belief in the punishment of the grave and the questioning by Munkar and Nakir in a detailed, sensory hadith that describes the literal realities of the grave for both the believer and the disbeliever.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to be questioned in your grave right now, would you answer the three questions with certainty? What are you doing today to ensure you know the answers?
?
The believer in the hadith longs for the Hour to return to his family and wealth; what does your attachment to your family and possessions say about your readiness for the hereafter?
?
The disbeliever's evil deeds appear as an ugly, foul-smelling figure; what deeds are you accumulating that will shape the figure you meet in the grave?

Unseen Matters
beginner
4 min
Belief in the Mi'raj and Dreams
Imam Ashari affirms belief in the Mi'raj hadith and that dreams have interpretations.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Imam Ash'ari affirms both the literal reality of the Night Journey (Mi'raj) and the validity of dream interpretation as part of orthodox Sunni belief.

Core Idea
Dreams and miracles both participate in a greater truth: the unseen realm is real and interpretable.

This brief creedal statement from Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari comes from his work al-Ibanah 'an Usul al-Diyanah. It addresses two matters that belong to the sam'iyyat (matters known through revelation): the Night Journey of the Prophet (Mi'raj) and the interpretation of dreams. Though the text is short, it stakes out a clear position against those who might spiritualize the Mi'raj or dismiss dreams as meaningless.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic sentence once to get its rhythm. Then read the translation. Note that the creed is stated as a direct affirmation: 'we believe' and 'we affirm'.
Notice the three clauses linked by 'and'.
2
Identify the three affirmations
The passage contains three distinct claims: belief in the Mi'raj hadith, affirming many dreams, and that those dreams have interpretations. List them.
The Mi'raj is the Night Journey to Jerusalem and ascension to heaven.
3
Understand the connection
Ask why these two topics are mentioned together. Both involve the unseen realm (al-ghayb) and require acceptance of revelation. The Mi'raj is a historical miracle; dreams are personal experiences that can carry divine messages.
This shows that Ahl al-Sunnah accept supernatural events as real.
4
Reflect on the position's significance
Imam Ash'ari is subtly refuting those who deny the physical reality of the Mi'raj or who treat dreams as superstitious. By affirming both with moderation (he says 'much of' dreams, not all), he upholds the middle path.
This guards against both excessive literalism and total denial.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٦٠ - ونصدق بحديث المعراج، ونصحح كثيراً من الرؤيا في المنام، ونقول إن لذلك تفسيراً.
60 - And we believe in the hadith of the Mi'raj, and we affirm much of the vision in dreams, and we say that that has an interpretation.
Source pages p. 54

Commentary
This passage is a brief creedal statement. Imam Ash'ari declares that he and the Ahl al-Sunnah affirm the truth of the hadith of the Mi'raj (the Night Journey of the Prophet). This refers to the Prophet's physical travel from Makkah to Jerusalem and his ascension to the heavens, as narrated in authentic hadith. He then states that they also affirm that many visions seen in dreams (الرؤيا in المنام) are true and have interpretations. Dreams can be a means of revelation or good tidings, but they require interpretation. The speaker affirms both the literal reality of the Mi'raj and the validity of dream interpretation as part of Islamic belief.

Key Terms
المعراج
الرؤيا
المنام
تفسير

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some claim the Mi'raj was only a spiritual vision or that dreams are irrelevant to Islamic belief.
Correction: Imam Ash'ari asserts the physical reality of the Mi'raj and the interpretability of dreams, showing that both are part of the Sunni creed.

Checkpoint
What does Imam Ash'ari say about dreams in this passage?
A) They are false and misleading.
B) Many have a valid interpretation.
C) They should always be taken literally.
D) Only prophets have true dreams.

Key Takeaway
Belief in the Mi'raj and the interpretation of true dreams are affirmed by Imam Ash'ari as part of the orthodox Sunni creed.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you wake from a vivid dream, do you immediately dismiss it as random, or do you pause to consider that Allah may be speaking to you through a portion of prophethood? What would it mean for your life if you began to take your true dreams seriously and sought their interpretation?
?
The Prophet's Mi'raj was a physical journey to the heavens. Does your belief in this event translate into a living conviction that the unseen world is more real than the world you see? If so, how does that change your priorities today?
?
Imam Ash'ari affirms that dreams have interpretations. When you experience a disturbing dream, do you seek to understand its meaning or simply ignore it? How might your relationship with Allah deepen if you treated your dreams as potential messages requiring reflection?

Charity and Prayer Benefit the Deceased
Imam Ashari affirms that charity and prayer for deceased Muslims benefit them.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni belief that charity and prayer for deceased Muslims benefit them, and why this distinguishes Ahl al-Sunnah from other sects.

Core Idea
The dead can receive benefit from the living's good deeds.

In this brief but powerful statement, Imam Ash'ari affirms a core Sunni belief: that deceased Muslims can benefit from charity and prayers offered on their behalf by the living. This sets the Sunni position apart from sects that deny any such benefit. Let's examine the text and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation to grasp the direct wording. Notice the two parts: the action (charity and prayer) and the belief (Allah benefits).
The passage is a single sentence but packs a creedal claim.
2
Understand the key terms
Focus on 'الصدقة' (charity) and 'الدعاء' (supplication) as acts performed for the deceased. Recognize that 'موتى المسلمين' refers to deceased Muslims.
These terms are central to the practice.
3
Recognize the belief statement
Note the phrase 'ونؤمن بأن الله ينفعهم بذلك' – a declaration of belief that Allah benefits them. This is a matter of faith, not mere opinion.
The verb 'نؤمن' (we believe) emphasizes doctrinal certainty.
4
Reflect on theological significance
Consider why this belief matters: it affirms the interconnectedness of the Muslim community across life and death, and that deeds of the living can aid the deceased by Allah's will.
This counters the view that the dead are entirely cut off from the living's actions.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٦١ - ونرى الصدقة على موتى المسلمين والدعاء لهم، ونؤمن بأن الله ينفعهم بذلك.
61 - And we hold charity for the deceased Muslims and supplication for them, and we believe that Allah benefits them by that.
Source pages p. 54

Commentary
This passage is a creedal article concerning the efficacy of good deeds performed on behalf of the deceased. Imam Ash'ari states that the Ahl al-Sunnah practice charity (الصدقة) and supplication (الدعاء) for deceased Muslims, and they believe that Allah benefits the dead through these actions. This is based on evidence from the Quran and Sunnah that the dead can receive reward from the living's charitable acts and prayers. The author does not detail the mechanism but affirms it as a belief.

Key Terms
الصدقة
الدعاء
موتى المسلمين
الانتفاع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some sects deny that the dead can benefit from the deeds of the living.
Correction: This passage asserts the Sunni position that the dead can benefit, by Allah's will.

Checkpoint
What does this passage state about charity and prayer for the deceased?
A) They are forbidden.
B) They are neutral.
C) Allah benefits the deceased by them.
D) They only benefit the living.

Key Takeaway
Imam Ash'ari affirms that charity and supplication for deceased Muslims are beneficial, as Allah grants them benefit.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that your charity for your deceased loved ones reaches them? If so, how does that change the way you pray for them today?
?
If you were to die tomorrow, would you have left behind charity that continues to benefit you? What specific steps will you take now to ensure that?
?
When you pray for the deceased, do you focus more on the ritual or on the heartfelt conviction that Allah hears and grants benefit? How can you deepen that conviction starting today?

Unseen Matters
beginner
4 min
Belief in Mi'raj and Interpretation of Dreams
Imam Ashari affirms belief in the Mi'raj and interpretation of dreams.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that mainstream Sunni belief affirms the reality of the Prophet's Mi'raj and the validity of dreams, but insists that dreams require interpretation.

Core Idea
Belief in Mi'raj and dreams is affirmed, but dreams are not literal—they need interpretation.

Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, in his creedal work al-Ibanah, lists several articles of faith. Among them is the affirmation of the Prophet's night journey (Mi'raj) and the confirmation that many dreams are true, yet require interpretation. This short passage encapsulates a balanced approach to revealed and experiential knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the original Arabic
Read the Arabic sentence once. Notice the three verbs: نصدق, نصحح, نقول. Each introduces a distinct belief.
2
Translate and parse the meaning
Read the literal translation. Identify the three components: belief in Mi'raj, confirmation of many dreams, and the statement that they have interpretation.
3
Reflect on the role of interpretation
Why does the Imam say dreams require interpretation? This guards against taking every dream as literal truth, which could lead to error.
This connects to the common misunderstanding.
4
Connect to broader creed
Consider why these beliefs are listed in a creed. They affirm the supernatural (Mi'raj) and the validity of authentic dreams while maintaining rational interpretation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونصدق بحديث المعراج، ونصحح كثيراً من الرؤيا في المنام، ونقول إن لذلك تفسيراً.
And we believe in the hadith of the Mi'raj, and we confirm many of the dreams in sleep, and we say that there is an interpretation for that.
Source pages p. 54

Commentary
This passage is a creedal statement affirming the reality of the Prophet's night journey (Mi'raj) as a genuine event. It also validates the authenticity of many dreams experienced in sleep, but insists that dreams require interpretation—they are not taken literally without explanation. The Imām asserts these as part of Sunni belief.

Key Terms
المعراج
الرؤيا

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that dreams are taken as literal revelations without any further explanation.
Correction: The Imam clarifies that dreams require interpretation (تفسير), meaning they are not literal truths but must be understood symbolically or contextually.

Checkpoint
What does the Imām say about dreams in this passage?
A) All dreams are literal truths.
B) Many dreams are true but require interpretation.
C) Dreams have no significance.
D) Only prophetic dreams are valid.

Key Takeaway
The Imam affirms the Mi'raj and the truth of many dreams, but insists all dreams require interpretation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
You affirm the Mi'raj as a literal event, but do you give the same level of certainty to the dreams you claim are from Allah? How can you ensure you are not mixing personal desire with divine inspiration?

Charity and Prayer Benefit the Deceased
Affirming that charity and prayers benefit deceased Muslims.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ahl al-Sunnah belief that charitable acts and prayers on behalf of deceased Muslims bring them benefit from Allah.

Core Idea
Charity and prayer for the dead reach them and Allah grants benefit.

This lesson addresses a sensitive point of Islamic creed: whether the living can help the deceased after death. Imam al-Ash'ari states clearly that charity and supplication for dead Muslims are not only permissible but that Allah benefits them thereby. This counters those who deny any posthumous benefit from the actions of the living.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic sentence carefully. Note the verbs: 'نرى' (we consider) and 'نؤمن' (we believe) — both indicate a matter of creed.
The passage is short but packs a doctrinal claim.
2
Understand the two actions
Identify the two actions mentioned: charity (الصدقة) and supplication (الدعاء). Both are directed toward 'موتى المسلمين' (deceased Muslims).
3
Grasp the benefit (النفع)
The passage states that Allah benefits the dead by these actions. This benefit is not automatic but comes from Allah's response to the charity and prayers offered on their behalf.
This refutes the view that no deed of the living can affect the dead.
4
Reflect on the theological context
Consider why this was a point of contention. Some groups denied any interaction between the living and the dead. Imam al-Ash'ari affirms it as part of the creed of Ahl al-Sunnah.
This is part of the samiyyat (matters of hearing) category.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونرى الصدقة على موتى المسلمين والدعاء لهم، ونؤمن بأن الله ينفعهم بذلك.
And we consider charity for dead Muslims and supplication for them, and we believe that Allah benefits them by that.
Source pages p. 54

Commentary
This is a doctrinal point affirming that charitable acts and prayers offered on behalf of deceased Muslims can bring benefit to them. The Imām states it as a matter of belief, not merely a practice. This counters those who deny any efficacy of such deeds after death.

Key Terms
الصدقة
الدعاء
الموتى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The dead cannot receive any benefit from the actions of the living.
Correction: This passage affirms that charity and supplication on behalf of deceased Muslims bring them benefit by Allah's grace.

Checkpoint
What does the Imām believe about charity for the dead?
A) It is useless.
B) Allah benefits them by it.
C) Only for righteous dead.
D) It is forbidden.

Key Takeaway
Charity and prayers for deceased Muslims are valid means by which Allah benefits them.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever doubted whether your supplications for a deceased loved one actually reach them? This lesson affirms that Allah benefits them by your du'a. Will you commit to regularly making du'a for your departed family and friends, trusting that Allah delivers the benefit?
?
Many people neglect the dead after burial. Are you actively including the deceased believers in your charity and prayers? What is one concrete action you will take this week to give sadaqah on behalf of a deceased Muslim?
?
If you truly believe that your charity and supplication benefit the dead, does your life reflect that belief? Do you prioritize these acts, or are they an afterthought? This lesson calls you to action—what will you change starting today?

The Punishment of the Disbeliever’s Soul in the Grave
Narrative hadith on disbeliever's soul and grave punishment

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the sequence of events for the disbeliever’s soul after death, including its rejection from heaven, questioning in the grave, and the beginning of punishment.

Core Idea
The disbeliever’s soul faces rejection, interrogation, and torment immediately after death.

This lesson presents a hadith that describes the detailed fate of the disbeliever's soul from the moment of death. It shows how the soul is treated with contempt by the angels, denied entry to heaven, and returned to the body to face questioning and punishment in the grave. Understanding this narrative helps solidify belief in the unseen realities of the barzakh.

Guided Path
1
Read the complete hadith
Read the Arabic passage and its translation carefully. Notice the vivid imagery and the sequence of events.
The hadith includes multiple Quranic verses quoted by the Prophet.
2
Identify the key stages
List the main stages: soul extraction, ascent to heaven, rejection, writing in Sijjin, return to body, angelic questioning, punishment, and appearance of evil deeds.
3
Reflect on the Quranic verses
The Prophet cites verses from Surah Al-A'raf and Surah Al-Hajj. Consider how these verses reinforce the narrative.
These verses show the cosmic rejection of the disbeliever.
4
Ponder the personification of deeds
Notice that the disbeliever's evil deeds appear as a hideous man. This illustrates how one's actions take shape in the afterlife.
This concept appears in many hadiths about the grave.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
حتى يَجْعَلُوهَا فِي تِلْكَ الْمُسُوحِ، وَيَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا كَأَنتَنِ رِيحٍ خَبِيثَة وُجِدَتْ على وَجْهِ الْأَرْضِ، فَيَصْعَدُونَ بِهَا، فَلَا يَمُرُونَ بِهَا على مَلا مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ إِلا قَالُوا: مَا هَذَا الرُّوحُ الْخَبِيثُ؟ فَيَقُولُونَ: فلانُ ابْنُ فُلَانِ بِأَقْبَح أَسْمَائِهِ التي كانُ يُسَمُّى بِهَا فِي الدُّنْيَا، حتى يُنْتهَى بِهَا إِلَى السَّمَاءِ الدُّنْيَا، فَيُسْتَفْتَح له، فَلا يُفْتَح له، ثُمَّ قَرَأَ رَسُولُ الله صلى الله عليه وسلم وَلَا نُفَتَحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوَابُ السَّمَاءِ وَلَا يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ حَتَّى يَلِعَ الْجَمَلُ في سَةِ الْبَاطِ [الأعراف: ٤٠]، فَيَقُولُ الله عَزَّ وَجَلَّ: اكْتُبُوا كِتَابَه في سجين، في الأَرْضِ السُّفْلَى، فَتُطْرَحُ رُوحُه طَرْحاً، ثُمَّ قَرَأَ: (وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَكَأَنَّمَا خَرَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ فَتَخْطَفُهُ الطَّيْرُ أَوْ تَهْوِى بِهِ الرِّيحُ فِي مكان سَعِيقٍ) [الحج: ۳۱]، فَتُعَادُ رُوحُهُ في جَسَدِهِ، وَيَأْتِيهِ مَلَكَانِ فَيُجْلِسَانِهِ، فَيَقُولان له: مَنْ ربُّكَ؟ فَيَقُولُ: هَاه هاه، لا أَدْرِي، فَيَقُولان له: مَا هَذَا الرَّجُلُ الذي بُعِثَ فِيكُمْ، فَيَقُولُ: هَاه هاه، لا أَدْرِي، فَيُنَادِي مُنَادٍ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ: أَنْ كَذَبَ، فَافْرِشوه مِنَ النَّارِ، وَافْتَحُوا لَه بَابًا إِلَى النَّارِ، فَيَأْتِيه مِنْ حَرَّهَا وَسَمَومِهَا، وَيَضِيقُ عليه قبره، حتى تَختَلِفَ فيه أضلاعُهُ، وَيَأْتِيهِ رَجُلٌ قَبِيحُ الْوَجْه، قَبْرُه النياب، متن الريح، فَيَقُولُ: أَبْشِرْ بِالَّذِي يَسُوَءَكَ، هَذَا يَوْمُكَ الَّذِي كُنْتَ تُوعَدُ، فَيَقُولُ: مَنْ أَنَتَ؟ فَوَجْهَكَ الْوَجْه يَجِيءُ بِالشَّرِّ، فَيَقُولُ: أَنَا عَمَلَكَ الْخَبِيثُ، فَيَقُولُ: رَبِّ لا تُقِمِ السَّاعَة. وهو حديث صحيح.
Until they put it in those sacks, and there comes out from it the most foul stench found on the face of the earth, so they ascend with it, and they do not pass by a group of angels except they say: What is this foul soul? They say: So-and-so son of so-and-so with the ugliest of his names by which he was called in the world, until they reach with it the nearest heaven, and they seek opening for him, but it is not opened for him. Then the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) recited: 'The gates of heaven will not be opened for them, nor will they enter Paradise until a camel passes through the eye of a needle' [Al-A'raf 40]. Then Allah Almighty says: Write his book in Sijjin, in the lowest earth, so his soul is thrown away. Then he recited: 'And whoever associates partners with Allah, it is as if he had fallen from the sky and the birds snatched him, or the wind blew him to a far place' [Al-Hajj 31]. Then his soul is returned to his body, and two angels come and make him sit, and they say to him: Who is your Lord? He says: Hah, hah, I do not know. They say: What about this man who was sent among you? He says: Hah, hah, I do not know. Then a caller from heaven calls: He has lied, so spread for him from the Fire, and open for him a door to the Fire, so there comes to him its heat and scorching wind, and his grave constricts upon him until his ribs interlock, and there comes to him a man with an ugly face, (?), (?), he says: Receive glad tidings of what will distress you, this is your day which you were promised. He says: Who are you? Your face is the face that brings evil. He says: I am your evil deed. He says: O Lord, do not establish the Hour. And it is an authentic hadith.
Source pages p. 54

Commentary
This passage is a narrative describing the fate of the disbeliever's soul at death and the punishment in the grave. It begins with the extraction of the soul, which is stinking and rejected. Angels remark on its foulness. It is taken to the lowest heaven but refused entry. The Imam quotes Quranic verses to emphasize that the gates of heaven are closed to them. Then the soul is thrown down (to Sijjin). After being returned to the body, two angels question the disbeliever about his Lord and about the Prophet Muhammad. The disbeliever answers in confusion. Then a command is given to spread fire for him and open a door to hell. The grave becomes extremely tight. Then a personification of his evil deeds appears and gives him terrible tidings. The disbeliever, in despair, asks that the Hour not be established. The Imam concludes this is an authentic hadith.

Key Terms
الروح الخبيث
سجين
الملكان
فتنة القبر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the soul of a disbeliever goes straight to hellfire after death, with no intermediate state.
Correction: This hadith shows an intermediate state (barzakh) where the soul is rejected from heaven, returned to the body, and experiences punishment in the grave before the Resurrection.

Checkpoint
What happens when the disbeliever's soul is brought to the nearest heaven?
A) It is welcomed.
B) The gate is opened for it.
C) The gate is not opened for it.
D) It enters Paradise immediately.

Key Takeaway
The disbeliever's soul experiences rejection from heaven, interrogation, and torment in the grave as a foretaste of eternal punishment.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When the angels ask you 'Who is your Lord?' will you answer with certainty like a believer, or will you stammer like the disbeliever in this hadith? What are you doing today to ensure you know your Lord beyond a name?
?
The disbeliever is called by the ugliest of his names. If your deeds were personified, would you be greeted with terror or with joy? What name would your deeds earn you right now, and what change can you make before you face that moment?
?
The disbeliever begs not to be resurrected because his punishment is unbearable. Do you live as if you truly believe that the grave is the first stage of the hereafter, or do you act as if this world is all there is? Write down one specific action you will take today to prepare for the questioning.
