Belief in the Reality of Magic in the World
Affirming that magic exists and sorcerers exist in the world.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Sunni position affirms the real existence of magic and sorcerers in the world, contrary to those who deny it.

Core Idea
Magic is real and exists in the world, not mere illusion.

Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari states clearly that magic is a genuine phenomenon with real effects. This lesson presents his affirmation that both magic and sorcerers truly exist, establishing a core Sunni belief about the unseen.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage
Read the Arabic sentence and its translation. Notice the double affirmation: 'wa anna al-sihra kā'in mawjūd'.
The bracket around 'and sorcerers' indicates a textual variant.
2
Identify the belief being affirmed
The Imam is not just saying magic is possible; he says 'we believe' (nuṣaddiqu) that it is present in the world.
3
Counter the misconception
The affirmation directly opposes the view that magic is only illusion or imagination. Consider why this matters for aqeedah.
4
Connect to broader faith
Recognizing magic's reality affirms that supernatural events occur by Allah's permission, not by independent powers.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونصدق بأن في الدنيا سحراً [وسحرة]، وأن السحر كائن موجود في الدنيا.
And we believe that in the world there is magic and sorcerers, and that magic is existent and present in the world.
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This is a doctrinal point asserting the reality of magic as something that truly occurs in the world, and that there are people who practice it (sorcerers). The Imām emphasizes that magic is not just illusion but has actual existence. This counters those who deny the effects of magic altogether.

Key Terms

السحرة

Key Takeaway
The Sunni creed affirms that magic is a real, existing phenomenon in the world, performed by sorcerers, and not merely an illusion.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

Prayer Over All Muslims, Righteous and Sinner
Stating that we pray over all Muslims, regardless of their piety.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sunni Islam includes all Muslims, both righteous and sinner, in funeral prayer and inheritance.

Core Idea
Sunni mercy covers every Muslim in death prayer and inheritance.

Imam al-Ash'ari states a key difference from extremist groups: we pray over every Muslim who dies, no matter their sins. This simple phrase affirms the unity of the Muslim community and rejects excommunication based on sin. Let's see what it means.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic sentence and its translation. Notice the inclusive language: 'their righteous and their sinner'.
2
Understand the theological position
This statement rejects the Kharijite view that serious sinners are no longer Muslims. Ash'ari insists that funeral prayer and inheritance apply to all who pray toward the qibla.
The qibla is the direction of prayer, so 'people of the qibla' means Muslims.
3
Reflect on the implications
Ask yourself: If a Muslim commits a major sin, does he still have rights in the community? This passage says yes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وندين بالصلاة على من مات من أهل القبلة، برهم وفَاجِرهم، وتوارثهم.
And we adhere to prayer over whoever dies from the people of the qibla, their righteous and their sinner, and inheritance among them.
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This passage affirms the Sunni practice of performing the funeral prayer for every Muslim who dies, regardless of their level of righteousness or sinfulness. It also implies that inheritance rights apply among them, meaning that a Muslim inherits from another Muslim even if the deceased was a sinner. This distinguishes from the Kharijite position of excommunicating sinners.

Key Terms
الصلاة على الميت
أهل القبلة
توارث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that a Muslim who commits major sins should not receive funeral prayers or inherit from righteous Muslims.
Correction: Ash'ari teaches that all Muslims, even sinners, have the right to funeral prayer and inheritance, as long as they belong to the qibla.

Checkpoint
Over whom do we perform the funeral prayer according to this passage?
A) Only righteous Muslims.
B) Only sinners who repent.
C) All Muslims, righteous and sinner.
D) Only scholars and martyrs.

Key Takeaway
The funeral prayer and inheritance apply to all Muslims, regardless of their sinfulness, affirming the unity of the Muslim community.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is there any Muslim you have mentally excluded from your prayers or from being part of 'your' community because of their sins? What would the Prophet ﷺ do if he were standing next to you right now?
?
If you were to die tonight, what would you want others to believe about your state of faith? How does this lesson challenge you to treat those you consider 'sinners'?
?
Do you truly believe that your inheritance of love in this life and prayers in the afterlife belong to every Muslim, even those you disagree with? If not, what is blocking your heart?

Paradise and Hell Are Created; Death by Appointed Term
Affirming that Paradise and Hell are created, and that death and killing occur at the appointed time.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why classical Sunni creed affirms that Paradise and Hell are already created, and that both natural death and being killed occur by Allah's appointed term.

Core Idea
Allah has already created Paradise and Hell, and every death happens by His decree.

Imam al-Ash'ari here affirms two interrelated beliefs: that Paradise and Hell are not merely future creations but exist now, and that every death—whether natural or violent—occurs exactly at the time Allah has ordained. These points guard against erroneous views that undermine Allah's power and pre-knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation to grasp the two affirmations.
2
Identify the two creedal points
Notice the two clauses: one about Paradise/Hell, the other about death and killing.
3
Reflect on the significance
Understand that this refutes the idea that Paradise/Hell are not yet created, and that a killed person dies before his term.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونقر أن الجنة والنار مخلوقتان. وأن من مات وقتل فبأجله مات وقتل.
And we affirm that Paradise and Hell are created. And that whoever dies and is killed, by his appointed term he dies and is killed.
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This passage contains two related creedal points. First, Paradise and Hell are already created and exist now, not just future creations. Second, both natural death and being killed occur by the predestined appointed term (ajal). This refutes the view that a killed person died before his term, affirming that everything happens by divine decree.

Key Terms
الجنة والنار
مخلوقتان
الأجل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Paradise and Hell are not yet created; they will be created after judgment.
Correction: The Imam explicitly affirms they are created now.

Checkpoint
What does the phrase 'فبأجله مات وقتل' indicate?
A) The killed person dies before his term.
B) Both natural death and killing happen at the appointed term.
C) Only natural death has a term.
D) The killer determines the term.

Key Takeaway
Sunni creed affirms that Paradise and Hell are already created, and that every death occurs by Allah's precise decree.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that every moment of your life, including your death, is decreed by Allah? How does that change how you fear or worry about death?
?
If Paradise and Hell are already created, does that increase your urgency to seek the former and avoid the latter? What specific action will you take today?
?
When you hear news of someone being murdered, do you consider that Allah's decree was at work, or do you doubt? How can you train your heart to accept that while still seeking justice?

The Test of Polytheists' Children in the Afterlife
Stating the test of polytheists' children by fire.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Sunni stance on the fate of polytheists' children as a test by fire, as reported by Imam al-Ash'ari.

Core Idea
Children of polytheists face an afterlife test, not automatic punishment.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a concise but crucial doctrine regarding the children of polytheists who died before accountability. This lesson examines the narrated test by fire, clarifying that their fate is determined by obedience to Allah's command, not by their parents' beliefs. The passage anchors this view in a transmitted report.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Start by reading the original Arabic alongside its literal translation to grasp the precise wording of the doctrine.
Notice the key verb 'اقتحموها' (plunge into it) – it implies a command.
2
Understand the core claim
Identify that the passage states a specific event: Allah will kindle a fire and command the children to enter it. This is not a punishment but a test.
The commentary explains that obedience leads to safety, disobedience to punishment.
3
Connect to broader Aqeeda
Reflect on how this doctrine fits within Ash'ari theology: divine justice, testing in the afterlife, and mercy towards those who never received the message.
This passage challenges the notion that all unbelievers' children are automatically condemned.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقولنا في أطفال المشركين: ( إن الله تعالى يؤجج لهم في الآخرة ناراً، ثم يقول لهم: اقتحموها )، كما جاءت بذلك الرواية.
And our saying concerning the children of the polytheists: (Verily Allah Almighty will kindle for them a fire in the Hereafter, then He will say to them: Plunge into it), as the narration has come.
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This passage states the Sunni position on the fate of children who died before reaching accountability, specifically children of polytheist parents. The Imām reports a narration that in the Hereafter, Allah will create a fire and command these children to enter it, as a test. Those who obey will find it cool and safe, while the disobedient will be punished. This affirms that such children are subject to a test in the afterlife, not automatically condemned.

Key Terms
أطفال المشركين
النار
الاقتحام

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe all children of polytheists go directly to Hell without any test.
Correction: The Ash'ari position holds they are tested in the afterlife by Allah commanding them to enter a fire; those who obey are saved.

Checkpoint
What happens to the children of polytheists according to the narration cited?
A) They go directly to Paradise.
B) They go directly to Hell.
C) They are tested with a fire command.
D) They are not judged.

Key Takeaway
Children of polytheists are neither automatically saved nor damned; they undergo an afterlife test by fire.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

The Createdness of Paradise and Hellfire
A creedal affirmation that Paradise and Hell are created.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Paradise and Hell are created beings, not eternal, according to Sunni doctrine.

Core Idea
Paradise and Hell are created, not eternal.

This short but significant article from Imam al-Ash'ari's al-Ibanah clearly states a key point of Sunni belief: both Paradise and Hell are created entities. This counters any notion that they are pre-eternal. Let's examine the text and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic statement
Read the Arabic sentence once. Notice the dual feminine form 'makhluqatan' indicating both are created.
The statement is very concise, typical of creedal articles.
2
Understand the translation
Read the literal translation: 'And we affirm that Paradise and Hellfire are created.' Affirmation (iqrar) implies a firm belief.
3
Identify the theological point
Recognize that this affirms they have a beginning in time, contrary to those who claim they are eternal (qadim).
This is a refutation of certain theological positions.
4
Reflect on the significance
Consider why this matters: if Paradise and Hell are created, they are not co-eternal with Allah, emphasizing His sole eternity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

64 - And we affirm that Paradise and Hellfire are created.
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This is a concise affirmation that both Paradise and Hell are created beings (makhluqatan), meaning they are not eternal in the sense of being uncreated. This counters the view that they are pre-eternal.

The Test of Polytheists' Children in the Hereafter
Statement on the test of polytheists' children via fire.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Ash'ari doctrine that polytheists' children will face a test in the afterlife, based on a narrated tradition.

Core Idea
Polytheists' children will be tested with a fire in the Hereafter.

This short but significant article addresses a sensitive theological question: the fate of children who died before reaching moral responsibility. Imam al-Ash'ari presents the position that these children will be tested in the afterlife by being commanded to enter a fire. This teaching is rooted in a transmitted narration and reflects the Ash'ari commitment to relying on revealed texts over speculative reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once and then the English translation. Notice the concise style and the direct reference to a narrated tradition.
The passage is a single sentence but carries a complete doctrinal position.
2
Identify the key components
Identify the subject (children of polytheists), the action (Allah kindles a fire and commands them to enter it), and the source (a narration).
3
Reflect on the theological significance
Consider why this teaching matters: it addresses the justice and mercy of Allah towards those who did not receive the message in this world.
This test allows them to prove obedience and possibly attain paradise.
4
Understand the basis in narration
Notice that the author grounds the position in a transmitted report ('as the narration has come'). This underscores the method of relying on revelation rather than reason alone.
The exact narration is not quoted here, but the doctrine is presented as established.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٦٨ - وقولنا في أطفال المشركين: (إن الله تعالى يؤجج لهم في الآخرة ناراً، ثم يقول لهم: اقتحموها)، كما جاءت بذلك الرواية.
68 - And our saying concerning the children of polytheists: (Indeed Allah, exalted, will kindle for them a fire in the Hereafter, then He will say to them: Enter it), as the narration has come with that.
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This article presents the Ash'ari position on the children of polytheists (atfal al-mushrikin): they will be tested in the Hereafter. Allah will kindle a fire and command them to jump into it. Those who obey will find it cool and safe (as per other hadiths), while the disobedient will be punished. The author bases this on a reported narration.

Key Terms
أطفال المشركين
اقتحموها
الآخرة

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that all children of polytheists go to Hell without any chance of salvation.
Correction: The Ash'ari position teaches that these children will be tested in the Hereafter with a command to enter a fire, allowing them to prove their obedience.

C) They will be safe

Quranic Proofs for Seeing Allah
Imam Ashari cites four Quranic verses as proof for seeing Allah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses four Quranic verses to establish that believers will see Allah on the Day of Resurrection, based on the interpretation of early scholars.

Core Idea
The Quran itself confirms that the greatest reward is seeing Allah.

This lesson examines Imam Ashari's concise yet powerful Quranic argument for the beatific vision. He presents four key verses, each interpreted by the early scholars (ahl al-ta'wil) to point to the reality of seeing Allah. Understanding this proof is essential for appreciating the Sunni position on this major attribute.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice how Ashari moves from one verse to the next without much commentary.
2
Identify each verse and its interpretation
Find the four verses cited. For each, note what interpretation Ashari gives (either directly or by quoting 'ahl al-ta'wil').
The key term 'الزيادة' appears in the first verse.
3
Understand the logical structure
Notice how the last verse about veiling is used as a contrast: if disbelievers are veiled, believers must not be veiled, hence they see.
This is a crucial argument from absence of veiling to presence of seeing.
4
Reflect on the significance
Ask yourself why seeing Allah is considered the greatest reward. Consider how this connects to the broader doctrine of divine attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ودليل آخر : قال الله عز وجل : لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا الْحُسْنَى وَزِيَادَةٌ [ يونس : ٢٦]. قال أهل التأويل : النظر إلى الله عز وجل، ولم يُنْعِم الله عز وجل على أهل جنانه بأفضل من نظرهم إليه ورؤيتهم له. وقال عز وجل: (وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ ﴾ [ق:٣٥]. قيل: النظر إلى الله عز وجل. وقال : ( تَحِيَّتُهُم يوم يلقونَهُ سَلَمٌ ﴾ [الأحزاب: ٤٤] وإذا لقيه المؤمنون رأوه. وقال الله عز وجل : كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَذٍ مَحْجُوبُونَ ﴾ [المطففين: ١٥] فحجبهم عن رؤيته، ولا يحجب عنها المؤمنين .
Another proof: Allah Almighty said: 'For those who have done good is the best (reward) and more.' [Yunus:26]. The people of interpretation said: (the more) is looking at Allah Almighty, and Allah Almighty has not bestowed upon the people of His Paradise anything better than their looking at Him and seeing Him. And He Almighty said: 'And with Us is more.' [Qaf:35]. It is said: looking at Allah Almighty. And He said: 'Their greeting on the day they meet Him is "Peace."' [Al-Ahzab:44] And when the believers meet Him, they see Him. And Allah Almighty said: 'No! Indeed, from their Lord that Day they will be veiled.' [Al-Mutaffifin:15] So He veiled them from seeing Him, and He does not veil the believers from it.
Source pages p. 63

Commentary
This passage is a proof from the Quran for the visibility of Allah on the Day of Resurrection. The author presents several verses. First, the verse 'for those who have done good is the best and more' where 'more' (al-ziyada) is interpreted by the early scholars (ahl al-ta'wil) as looking at Allah. Then the verse 'and with Us is more' similarly interpreted. Then the verse 'their greeting on the day they meet Him is peace' implies that meeting involves seeing. Finally, the verse 'No! Indeed, from their Lord that Day they will be veiled' is used as evidence: if the disbelievers are veiled, then the believers are not veiled, so they see. The argument follows the principle that the Quran confirms itself: the specific mention of veiling for the disbelievers indicates that the believers are not veiled, and thus they see.

Key Terms
الحسنى
الزيادة
النظر إلى الله
الرؤية
المحجوبون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that 'زيادة' (ziyadah) refers to additional rewards in Paradise other than seeing Allah, such as palaces or rivers.
Correction: Imam Ashari quotes the early scholars (ahl al-ta'wil) who unanimously interpreted 'ziyadah' as looking at Allah, making it the greatest reward.

Checkpoint
According to Ashari, what does the 'زيادة' in the verse 'للذين أحسنوا الحسنى وزيادة' refer to?
A) Increased reward in Paradise
B) Looking at Allah Almighty
C) Forgiveness of sins
D) Eternal life

Key Takeaway
The Quran explicitly indicates that believers will see Allah through verses on 'more' reward, meeting, and veiling of disbelievers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the greatest reward is seeing Allah, does your heart genuinely yearn for that moment? What would change in your daily life if you truly believed that the vision of Allah is your ultimate goal?
?
The disbelievers are veiled from seeing Allah. Is there any 'veil' in your heart right now—like pride, disobedience, or attachment to this world—that blocks you from experiencing the reality of Allah? What specific step can you take today to remove that veil?
?
When you recite the verses about the 'increase' in reward, do you feel a deep longing for the sight of Allah, or are you more focused on the lesser pleasures of Paradise? Ask yourself: Am I truly living for the 'more' that is beyond everything else?

5 min
Answer to the objection of 'La tudrikuhu al-absar'
Imam Ashari answers the objection raised by the verse 'La tudrikuhu al-absar'

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand two interpretations of the verse 'La tudrikuhu al-absar' and how Imam Ashari defends the possibility of seeing Allah in the afterlife.

Core Idea
The verse does not deny all vision, but only vision in this world or by disbelievers.

This lesson addresses a common objection raised against the belief that believers will see Allah in the Hereafter. The verse 'Vision does not comprehend Him' (Al-An'am 103) seems to contradict other verses promising vision. Imam Ashari explains two ways to reconcile them, showing the Quran's internal consistency.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
Understand the question about the meaning of 'La tudrikuhu al-absar'.
2
Explore the first interpretation
It is possible that the verse refers to this world only; in the Hereafter believers will see Allah.
This is supported by the principle that the greatest pleasures belong to the best abode.
3
Explore the second interpretation
It is possible that the verse refers specifically to the eyes of disbelievers.
This is supported by another verse that says faces will look at their Lord.
4
See the supporting evidence
The Quran confirms itself: one verse says faces will look at their Lord, so the verse denying comprehension must refer to disbelievers.
5
Conclude
The verse does not negate vision for believers in the Hereafter.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
سؤال : فإن قال قائل : فما معنى قوله تعالى: (لَا تُدْرِكُهُ الأَبْصَرُ ) ؟ [الأنعام: ١٠٣] . قيل له : يحتمل أن يكون : لا تدركه في الدنيا وتدركه في الآخرة ؛ لأن رؤية الله تعالى أفضل اللذات وأفضل اللذات تكون في أفضل الدارين . ويحتمل أن يكون الله عز وجل أراد بقوله : ( لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصَرُ) يعني : لا تدركه أبصار الكافرين المكذبين، وذلك أن كتاب الله يُصَدِّقُ بعضه بعضاً، فلما قال في آية : إن الوجوه تنظر إليه يوم القيامة، وقال في آية أخرى : إن الأبصار لا تدركه علمنا أنه إنما أراد أبصار الكفار لا تدركه
Question: If someone says: What is the meaning of Allah's statement: 'Vision does not comprehend Him'? [Al-An'am:103]. He is told: It is possible that it means: they do not comprehend Him in this world, but they will comprehend Him in the Hereafter, because the vision of Allah the Most High is the greatest of pleasures, and the greatest of pleasures are in the best of the two abodes. And it is possible that Allah Almighty meant by His statement: 'Vision does not comprehend Him' meaning: the eyes of the disbelievers who deny do not comprehend Him, and that is because the Book of Allah confirms one part another. When He said in one verse: 'Faces will look at their Lord' and in another verse: 'Vision does not comprehend Him', we know that He only meant the eyes of the disbelievers do not comprehend Him.
Source pages p. 63

Commentary
This passage is an objection and answer concerning the verse 'لا تدركه الأبصار'. The objector asks for its meaning. The author offers two possible interpretations. First, it may refer to this world only; in the Hereafter believers will see Him. This is supported by the principle that the greatest pleasures belong to the best abode. Second, it may mean that only the eyes of the disbelievers do not comprehend Him. The author favors this second interpretation by appealing to the internal consistency of the Quran: another verse says 'Faces will look at their Lord' (Qiyama 23), so the verse about not comprehending cannot apply to believers. Therefore, it must refer to disbelievers. This resolves the apparent contradiction.

Key Terms
لا تدركه الأبصار
النظر
الرؤية
أبصار الكافرين
تصديق الكتاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think 'La tudrikuhu al-absar' absolutely denies any vision of Allah forever.
Correction: The passage shows that the verse either refers to this world only or specifically to disbelievers, so believers will see Allah in the Hereafter.

Checkpoint
Which interpretation of 'لا تدركه الأبصار' does Ashari support by appealing to the consistency of the Quran?
A) It refers to this world only
B) It refers to the eyes of disbelievers
C) It means Allah cannot be seen at all
D) It refers to the eyes of angels

Key Takeaway
The verse does not deny the vision of Allah in the afterlife; it either restricts it to this world or to disbelievers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly yearn to see your Lord? How does your daily life reflect that yearning, or does it reveal that you are more attached to this world?
?
If you were certain that on the Day of Judgment you would look at Allah's Face, what would you change about your priorities today? What is holding you back from that longing?
?
The verse promises that faces will look at their Lord. Does your face shine with hope of that moment? If not, why not? What would it take for you to live in anticipation of that vision?

Proof from Hadith for Seeing Allah Like the Moon
Imam Ashari provides hadith evidence for beatific vision and defends its authenticity

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari uses the hadith about seeing Allah like the moon as evidence for literal vision (ru'yat al-'iyan) and defends its authenticity through multiple chains.

Core Idea
The hadith of seeing Allah like the moon proves literal vision and is more widely transmitted than key legal hadiths.

Imam Ashari now turns to a prophetic hadith to support the belief that believers will see Allah with their eyes on the Day of Resurrection. This hadith, well-known among Muslims, compares the vision to seeing the full moon without any doubt. Ashari not only cites the text but also defends its reliability by comparing its transmission to that of several widely accepted legal hadiths.

Guided Path
1
Read the hadith and its translation
Read the Arabic hadith and its literal translation. Notice the key phrase 'ترون ربكم' and the comparison to the moon.
2
Understand the argument for literal sight
The commentary highlights that when vision is used absolutely and likened to direct sight, it means physical sight. Consider why this is important for the doctrine.
3
Examine the defense of authenticity
Ashari compares the number of narrators of this hadith to that of stoning, bequest, wiping over socks, etc. Ask yourself: why does he choose these specific comparisons? What does this tell you about the status of the hadith?
These comparisons suggest the hadith is even more authentic than well-known legal rulings.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
دليل آخر : ومما يدل على إثبات رؤية الله عزّ وجل بالأبصار، ما روته الجماعات من الجهات المختلفة عن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم أنه قال : « تَرَوْنَ رَبَّكُمْ كَمَا تَرَوْنَ القَمَرَ لَيْلَةَ البَدْرِ ، لا تَضَارُوْنَ فِي رُؤْيَتِهِ (۱). والرؤية إذا أطلقت إطلاقاً ومثلت برؤية العيان، لم يكن معناها إلا رؤية العيان . وروريت الرؤية عن رسول الله من طرق مختلفة عدة رواتها أكثر من عدة خبر الرجم، ومن عدة من روى أن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم قال : ( لا وَصِيَّةَ لِوارِث (۲) ، صلى الله عليه وسلم ومن عدة رواة المسح على الخفين ومن عدة رواة قول رسول الله : ( لا تُنكَحُ (٤٠)
Another proof: Among what indicates the affirmation of seeing Allah Almighty with the eyes is what the groups from various directions narrated from the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, that he said: 'You will see your Lord as you see the moon on the night of the full moon, you will not have any doubt about seeing Him (1).' And vision, when it is used absolutely and is likened to direct sight, its meaning is nothing but direct sight. And vision has been narrated from the Messenger of Allah through various channels; its narrators are more numerous than the narrators of the hadith of stoning, and more numerous than those who narrated that the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: 'No bequest to an heir (2),' and more numerous than the narrators of wiping over the khuffs, and more numerous than the narrators of the saying of the Messenger of Allah: 'Do not marry (40).'
Source pages p. 64

Commentary
This passage is a proof from the prophetic hadith. The author cites the famous hadith that believers will see Allah on the Day of Resurrection as clearly as seeing the moon. He emphasizes that the comparison to seeing the moon indicates that it is a literal physical sight ('ru'yat al-'iyan). He then defends the authenticity of this hadith by noting its many chains of transmission. The number of its narrators exceeds that of well-known hadiths such as the stoning hadith (hadd al-rajm), the hadith 'no bequest to an heir', the hadith on wiping over socks (mash 'ala al-khuffayn), and the hadith on prohibiting marriage (possibly 'do not marry a woman to her paternal aunt'). By citing these comparators, Ashari argues that the hadith of vision is even more widely transmitted and therefore authentic.

Key Terms
الرؤية
رؤية العيان
ترون ربكم
لا تضارون
خبر الرجم
لا وصية لوارث
المسح على الخفين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The hadith 'ترون ربكم' is a metaphorical vision, not literal sight.
Correction: Ashari argues that the comparison to seeing the moon indicates direct physical sight, and the phrase 'لا تضارون' (no doubt) confirms its certainty.

Checkpoint
According to Ashari, what does the phrase 'لا تضارون في رؤيته' in the hadith suggest?
A) That seeing Allah will be without doubt
B) That people will compete to see
C) That some will miss seeing
D) That the vision will be partial

Key Takeaway
The hadith of seeing Allah like the moon is a central proof for the literal vision of Allah on the Day of Resurrection, and its authenticity is supported by its widespread transmission.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You will see your Lord as clearly as the full moon. Are you living today as if that moment is the most certain reality, or do you let fleeting worldly sights dominate your attention?
?
If this hadith is more reliably transmitted than many rulings you follow without question, why does it not stir a deeper urgency in your heart to prepare for that vision?
?
The Prophet promised a sight without any doubt. What doubts about Allah’s existence or His promise still linger in your soul, and what will you do today to uproot them?

Two Hadith Proofs That Allah Is Above the Heaven
Imam Ash'ari presents two hadith proofs for Allah being above the heaven.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand two hadith proofs from Imam Ash'ari that affirm Allah's being above the heaven and the correct understanding of al-fawqiyya.

Core Idea
The Prophet affirmed a slave-girl's faith because she said Allah is in the heaven.

This lesson presents two hadith-based arguments from Imam Ash'ari's al-Ibanah. The first hadith speaks of standing before Allah on Judgment Day, while the second involves the Prophet's question to a slave-girl. Both point to Allah's being above the heaven, but without physical proximity.

Guided Path
1
Read the two hadith proofs
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the two distinct narrations: the first about the servant's questioning before Allah, and the second about the slave-girl.
The second hadith is more explicit about location.
2
Understand how each hadith indicates Allah's above-ness
Consider how the first hadith implies a direction (before Allah) and the second involves a direct question about location. Reflect on why the Prophet approved the slave-girl's answer.
3
Note the concluding clarification
Imam Ash'ari explicitly states that Allah's being above the heaven (fawqiyya) does not mean He is physically close to the Throne. This guards against anthropomorphism.
This is a crucial point to avoid misunderstanding.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
١٤٩ - دليل آخر : وروت العلماء عن النبي الله أنه قال : « إن العبد لا تزول قدماه من بين يدي الله عز وجل حتى يسأله عن عمله ». وروت العلماء أن رجلاً أتى النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم بأمة سوداء فقال: يا رسول الله ! إني أريد أن أعتقها في كفّارة، فهل يجوز عتقها؟ فقال لها النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم: «أين الله؟ قالت : في السماء ، قال : فمن أنا؟ قالت : أنت رسول الله ، فقال النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم : «أعتقها فإنها مؤمنة». وهذا يدل على أن الله عز وجل على عرشه فوق السماء، فوقية لا تزيده قرباً من العرش.
149 - Another proof: The scholars narrated from the Prophet, peace be upon him, that he said: 'Indeed the servant's two feet will not depart from before Allah, Mighty and Majestic, until He asks him about his deeds.' And the scholars narrated that a man came to the Prophet, peace be upon him, with a black slave-girl and said: 'O Messenger of Allah! I want to set her free as an expiation, so is it permissible to set her free?' The Prophet, peace be upon him, said to her: 'Where is Allah?' She said: 'In the heaven.' He said: 'Who am I?' She said: 'You are the Messenger of Allah.' So the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: 'Set her free, for she is a believer.' And this indicates that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, is upon His Throne above the heaven, a highness that does not increase His closeness to the Throne.
Source pages p. 103

Commentary
This passage is a proof section (dilil) in which Imam Ash'ari presents two hadith-based arguments for Allah's being above the heaven. First, he cites the hadith that on Judgment Day the servant will be questioned directly before Allah, implying that Allah is in a place where He can address the servant. Second, he narrates the well-known hadith of the black slave-girl, where the Prophet asks her 'Where is Allah?' and she answers 'In the heaven,' and the Prophet affirms her faith, showing that believing in Allah's being in the heaven is a sign of true iman. The author then explicitly concludes that Allah is on His Throne above the heaven, with a 'fawqiyya' (highness) that is not to be understood as physical closeness to the Throne, thus avoiding anthropomorphism.

Key Terms
دليل
فوقية
العرش
فوق السماء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that 'Allah is above the heaven' means He is physically located in a specific place or close to the Throne in a spatial sense.
Correction: Imam Ash'ari clarifies that fawqiyya is a highness that does not entail physical proximity or modality; it is a transcendence befitting Allah.

Checkpoint
According to the hadith of the slave-girl, what did she affirm about Allah?
A) That Allah is everywhere
B) That Allah is in the heaven
C) That Allah is on the earth
D) That Allah is in the hearts

Key Takeaway
Imam Ash'ari uses two hadiths to prove that Allah is above the heaven, emphasizing that this does not imply physical closeness.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you imagine Allah being 'above the heaven,' does your heart feel distant from His nearness? How does the lesson's clarification that His highness is not physical transform your relationship with Him? Will you now affirm this belief even when others mock it as primitive?
?
The slave-girl’s simple answer was enough for the Prophet to declare her faith. If you were asked 'Where is Allah?' right now, would your answer carry the same unhesitating certainty? What doubts or intellectual pride hold you back from such simple faith?
?
You have now seen that the Prophet used the question 'Where is Allah?' as a test of true iman. Do you actually live as if Allah is above the heaven—meaning He sees and hears you from there? If not, what will you change today to make your belief a lived reality?

Refuting Denial of Resurrection and Recompense
Refutes disbelievers' assumption of no afterlife

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari refutes the disbelievers' denial of resurrection and recompense using Quranic evidence.

Core Idea
Divine justice demands differentiation in the afterlife; believers and disbelievers cannot be equal.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a concise argument against those who deny the afterlife. He cites two Quranic verses from Surah Sad that expose the false assumption that there is no resurrection or reward. This lesson unpacks the logical and scriptural refutation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice the author's direct style and the Quranic quotations.
2
Identify the disbelievers' assumption
Find the phrase describing what the disbelievers thought. Understand the components: no gathering, no resurrection, no reward, no punishment.
This assumption is the target of refutation.
3
Analyze the Quranic response
Look at the two verses quoted. The first warns of woe for such assumption; the second asks a rhetorical question implying inequality of treatment.
The second verse is particularly important for the argument.
4
Reflect on divine justice
Consider the author's explanation: God does not make all equal by annihilating and resurrecting them to the same fate. Justice requires differentiation.
This connects the verses to the core idea.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
من أطاعني ولا أعاقب من عصاني، كما ظن الكافرون أنه لا حشر ولا نشور ولا ثواب ولا عقاب ألا تراه قال: ﴿ذَلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النار ) [ص: ۲۷] . وبين ذلك بقوله : أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ ) [ ص: ۲۸] أي: لا نسوي بينهم في أن نفنيهم أجمعين ونعيدهم فيكون سبيلهم سبيلاً واحداً .
Whoever obeys Me and I do not punish whoever disobeys Me, as the disbelievers thought that there is no gathering, no resurrection, no reward, and no punishment. Do you not see He said: 'That is the assumption of those who disbelieve, so woe to those who disbelieve from the Fire' [Sad 38:27]. And He clarified that with His saying: 'Or should We treat those who believe and do righteous deeds like the corrupters in the earth, or should We treat the righteous like the wicked?' [Sad 38:28] meaning: We do not make them equal in that We annihilate them all and resurrect them so that their way becomes one way.
Source pages p. 137

Commentary
This passage refutes the disbelievers' claim that God does not punish sinners or reward believers, implying no resurrection or accountability. The author cites Quran 38:27 and 38:28 to show that God explicitly states that the fate of believers and disbelievers is not the same. The verse in Sad warns that such an assumption leads to woe. The following verse asks rhetorically whether God would treat the believers and the corrupters equally, which would be unjust. The author explains that God does not make them equal by simply annihilating them all and resurrecting them to the same fate. So the argument establishes that the disbelievers' assumption is false and that divine justice requires differentiation in the afterlife.

Key Terms
حشر (gathering)
نشور (resurrection)
ثواب (reward)
عقاب (punishment)
ظن (assumption)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God's mercy negates punishment for disbelief, implying no afterlife accountability.
Correction: The passage shows via Quranic verses that divine justice demands retribution for disbelief and reward for faith, so resurrection and recompense are necessary.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what do the disbelievers incorrectly assume about God?
A) That God will reward them
B) That there is no resurrection or recompense
C) That God will punish all equally
D) That God will not create the afterlife

Key Takeaway
God's justice requires that believers and disbelievers receive different fates in the afterlife, refuting the denial of resurrection and recompense.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that every single thought, word, and action will be brought before God for judgment, what would you change about your life starting today?
?
The disbelievers assumed God would not punish. Is there any area of your life where you are presuming upon God's mercy to excuse disobedience? What will you do about it right now?
?
Imagine standing before God and hearing the question: 'Did you live as if your deeds mattered for the afterlife?' Will your answer match the reality of resurrection and recompense? What one concrete step can you take today to align your life with that truth?

The Quran as Guidance for Believers Only
Al-Ash'ari answers an objection that the Quran is guidance for all people.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari interprets the Qur'anic phrase 'guidance for the people' as specific to believers, using the principle of non-contradiction within the Qur'an.

Core Idea
The Qur'an's guidance is only for those who accept it, not for all people.

Al-Ash'ari now addresses an objection based on a well-known verse that describes the Qur'an as 'guidance for the people.' The objector argues that this includes disbelievers. Al-Ash'ari's response demonstrates a key principle of interpretation: general statements in the Qur'an are qualified by more specific ones, and the overall message remains consistent.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
Start by reading the objection that cites Surah al-Baqarah 2:185. Notice how the objector uses the phrase 'guidance for the people' to claim the Qur'an guides disbelievers as well.
The objection is a common one.
2
Analyze Al-Ash'ari's answer
Read Al-Ash'ari's reply. He argues the verse is specific (khas). Identify the two Qur'anic proofs he uses to support this: that the Qur'an is guidance for the pious (muttaqin) and that Allah does not guide disbelievers.
These proofs come from other verses.
3
Understand the principle
Reflect on the underlying principle: the Qur'an does not contradict itself. Therefore, a general expression must be interpreted in light of other verses that specify its meaning.
This principle is crucial for tafsir.
4
Apply the lesson
Consider how this principle applies to other verses. For example, find another verse that uses a general term but is qualified elsewhere.
Think of verses about mercy or punishment.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٢٤٦- مسألة يسألون عنها :
تقولون : أليس قد قال الله عز وجل: ﴿ شَهْرُ رَمَضَانَ الَّذِي أُنزِلَ فِيهِ الْقُرْءَانُ هُدًى لِلنَّاسِ وَبَيِّنَتِ ﴾ [البقرة : ١٨٥] فما أنكرتم أن يكون القرآن هدى للكافرين والمؤمنين .
قيل لهم : الآية خاصة؛ لأن الله عز وجل قد بين لنا أنه هُدَى للمتقين، وأخبرنا أنه لا يهدي الكافرين والقرآن لا يتناقض، فوجب أن يكون قوله: هدًى لِلنَّاسِ ) أراد المؤمنين دون الكافرين .
246 - A question they ask about: Do you not say that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'The month of Ramadan in which the Qur'an was sent down as guidance for the people and clear proofs.' So what prevents you from acknowledging that the Qur'an is guidance for the disbelievers and the believers? It was said to them: The verse is specific; because Allah has explained to us that it is guidance for the pious, and He informed us that He does not guide the disbelievers. And the Qur'an does not contradict. So it must be that His saying 'guidance for the people' meant the believers, not the disbelievers.
Source pages p. 152

Commentary
This passage presents an objection and its answer. The opponent cites Surah al-Baqarah 2:185 which calls the Qur'an 'guidance for mankind' (hudan lil-nas) and argues this includes disbelievers. Al-Ash'ari responds that the verse is specific (khas), not general. He supports this by referring to other verses that describe the Qur'an as guidance only for the pious (hudan lil-muttaqin) and that Allah does not guide disbelievers. Because the Qur'an cannot contradict itself, the general expression in 2:185 must be interpreted as referring to believers alone. This illustrates a key principle of tafsir: general statements in the Qur'an are qualified by more specific ones.

Key Terms
خاص
هدى
متقين
لا يهدي الكافرين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that 'هدى للناس' in 2:185 means the Qur'an guides everyone, including disbelievers, regardless of their acceptance.
Correction: The Qur'an is guidance only for those who are receptive (the pious). Allah does not guide those who persist in disbelief. General verses are qualified by specific ones.

Checkpoint
Why does al-Ash'ari say the verse 'هدى للناس' is specific?
A) Because it abrogated other verses.
B) Because other verses show the Quran is only guidance for the pious.
C) Because the Prophet explained it.
D) Because the verse uses the word 'شهر'.

Key Takeaway
The Qur'an's guidance is exclusively for those who accept it; general statements are always qualified by more specific verses in the divine text.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read that the Qur'an is 'guidance for the pious', do you examine whether you truly possess the piety (taqwa) that allows its guidance to transform you? What specific step will you take today to increase your taqwa so that you benefit from the Qur'an as guidance?
?
Al-Ash'ari resolved an apparent contradiction by submitting to the principle that the Qur'an does not contradict itself. Are there verses you have dismissed or struggled with because they seemed to conflict with your understanding? Will you commit to letting the Qur'an interpret itself, even if it challenges your assumptions?
?
The guidance of the Qur'an is not automatic; it only reaches those who actively respond. In what areas of your life have you been reading the Qur'an without allowing it to guide your actions? Write down one verse that you will obey starting today.

Resolving the Guidance of Thamud
Al-Ash'ari resolves an apparent Qur'anic contradiction regarding the guidance of Thamud.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how to resolve apparent contradictions in the Qur'an by interpreting general statements in light of specific ones, using the example of Thamud.

Core Idea
The Qur'an never contradicts itself; apparent clashes reveal intended meanings.

Imam al-Ash'ari addresses a common objection: how can Allah say He guided Thamud when they were disbelievers, while other verses say Allah does not guide disbelievers? The answer lies in understanding that the verse refers only to the believing faction of Thamud. This lesson shows the method of reconciling verses by context and internal consistency.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection and the verse
Read the objection citing Surah Fussilat 41:17, which states that Allah guided Thamud, and note the apparent conflict with verses like 'Allah does not guide the disbelievers.'
Focus on the wording: 'We guided them'.
2
Identify the apparent contradiction
Observe the apparent tension: the verse seems to claim Allah guided a group that was clearly disbelieving. Ask yourself how this can be consistent.
3
Examine Al-Ash'ari's first answer
Learn that 'Thamud' here refers to the believers among them, not the entire tribe. This is supported by the verse about salvation with Salih (Hud 11:66).
The principle: a general term can be specified by context.
4
Understand the principle of non-contradiction
Recognize that the Qur'an does not contradict itself; it confirms its parts. When an apparent contradiction arises, we must reconcile by specification or other interpretive tools.
This preserves the integrity of the entire revelation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٢٤٨ - مسألة :
وإن سأل سائل عن قول الله تعالى: ﴿ وَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَهَدَيْنَهُمْ فَاسْتَحَبُّوا الْعَمَى عَلَى الهدى ) [فصلت: ۱۷] فقال : أليس ثمود كانوا كافرين وقد أخبر الله أنـه هـداهم؟ والجواب في هذه الآية على وجهين :
أحدهما: أن ثمود على فريقين كافرين ومؤمنين، وهم الذين أخبر الله أنه أنجاهم مع صالح بقوله عز وجل : نَجَيْنَا صَالِحًا وَالَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةِ مِنَّا ﴾ [هود : ٦٦] فالذين عنى الله عز وجل من ثمود أنه هداهم هم المؤمنون دون الكافرين؛ لأن الله عز وجل قد بيّن لنا في القرآن أنه لا يهدى الكافرين، والقرآن لا يتناقض، بل يصدق بعضه بعضاً، فإذا أخبرنا في موضع أنه لا يهدي الكافرين، ثم أخبر في موضع آخر أنه هدى ثمود علمنا أنه إنما أراد المؤمنين من ثمود دون الكافرين
248 - A question: And if someone asks about Allah's saying: 'And as for Thamud, We guided them, but they preferred blindness over guidance.' So he says: Were not Thamud disbelievers, and Allah informed that He guided them? The answer regarding this verse has two aspects: One of them is that Thamud were of two factions: disbelievers and believers. They are those whom Allah informed that He saved with Salih, by His saying: 'We saved Salih and those who believed with him by a mercy from Us.' So those whom Allah intended from Thamud that He guided them are the believers, not the disbelievers; because Allah has explained to us in the Qur'an that He does not guide the disbelievers, and the Qur'an does not contradict, rather it confirms some parts with others. So when He informed us in one place that He does not guide the disbelievers, and then informed in another place that He guided Thamud, we know that He only meant the believers from Thamud, not the disbelievers.
Source pages p. 153

Commentary
This passage presents an objection and its answer. The opponent points to Surah Fussilat 41:17 where Allah says He guided Thamud, yet Thamud were disbelievers. This seems to contradict other verses that say Allah does not guide disbelievers. Al-Ash'ari gives two answers; only the first is provided here. The first answer is that 'Thamud' in the verse refers specifically to the believers among them, not to the entire tribe. This is supported by the fact that Allah saved the believers with Salih (Hud 11:66). The principle is that the Qur'an does not contradict itself; when a general statement appears alongside a specific restriction, the general must be interpreted in light of the specific. Thus, 'We guided Thamud' means He guided the believers of Thamud, which is consistent with the rule that Allah does not guide disbelievers.

Key Terms
ثمود
فريقين
لا يهدي الكافرين
لا تناقض

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Qur'an contains a contradiction because it says Allah guided Thamud but also says He does not guide disbelievers.
Correction: The verse about Thamud refers only to the believers among them, as supported by other verses. The Qur'an never contradicts itself; apparent conflicts are resolved by proper interpretation.

Checkpoint
What is the first interpretation of the verse about Thamud?
A) Thamud were all guided.
B) Thamud refers only to the believers among them.
C) The guidance was temporary.
D) The verse is abrogated.

Key Takeaway
Apparent contradictions in the Qur'an are resolved by understanding contextual and specific meanings; the Qur'an confirms itself.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When in your life have you preferred blindness—your own desires or stubbornness—over clear guidance from Allah's Book? What would it take today to abandon that blindness and embrace the guidance you know is true?
?
This passage shows that Allah's guidance is specific to those who believe. How does this confront your assumption that everyone has equal access to guidance while you remain comfortable in your current state of faith? Are you truly among the believers who are being guided, or are you settling for a label without acting on the guidance?
?
If the Qur'an can resolve an apparent contradiction so precisely, what does that demand of your trust in its coherence when you face verses that challenge your lifestyle? Which verse have you been dismissing because it seems to conflict with your desires, and when will you submit to it fully?

Intercession: For Sinners, Not the Sinless
Ash'ari argues that intercession is for sinners who commit major sins

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Ash'ari's argument that intercession is for sinners who commit major sins, not for believers already promised Paradise, and how this refutes the Mu'tazila position.

Core Idea
Intercession is a mercy for sinners, not a bonus for the righteous.

In this passage, Ash'ari takes up the doctrine of intercession, a cornerstone of Sunni belief. He begins by noting the consensus that the Prophet has intercession, then asks the critical question: for whom? The answer he defends will distinguish Ahl al-Sunna from the Mu'tazila.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and note the consensus
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Observe that Ash'ari starts by stating the Muslim consensus that the Prophet has intercession. This is the foundation of the argument.
Consensus (ijma) is a key source of authority in this argument.
2
Identify the two possible answers
Ash'ari presents two possible answers to the question 'for whom is intercession?': either for sinners who commit major sins, or for sincere believers already promised Paradise. Notice how he frames the opposition's expected response.
3
Understand the refutation of the second answer
Focus on Ash'ari's reasoning: if believers are already promised Paradise, intercession is redundant, and if they have merited it, delaying them would be unjust. Intercession to prevent injustice is absurd because God does no wrong.
This refutation relies on the attributes of God's promise and justice.
4
Consider the anticipated objection
Ash'ari anticipates that opponents might say intercession is for an increase in reward, not for entry. This sets up his next argument (not included in this passage). Reflect on how this objection might be answered.
The passage ends here, but the argument continues in the next chapter.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الباب الثالث عشر الكلام في الشفاعة والخروج من النار (۱)
٢٦٠ - مسألة : ويقال لهم : قد أجمع المسلمون أن لرسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم شفاعة، فلمن الشفاعة أهي للمذنبين المرتكبين الكبائر ، أم للمؤمنين المخلصين؟ فإن قالوا للمذنبين المرتكبين الكبائر وافقوا . وإن قالوا للمؤمنين المبشرين بالجنة الموعودين بها . قيل لهم : فإذا كانوا بالجنة موعودين وبها مبشرين، والله عز وجل لا يخلف وعده فما معنى الشفاعة لقوم لا يجوز عندكم أن لا يدخلهم الله جناته؟ وما معنى قولكم أنهم قد استحقوها على الله عز وجل واستوجبوها عليه سبحانه، وإذا كان الله عز وجل لا يظلم مثقال ذرة، كان تأخيرهم عن الجنة ظلماً، وإنما يشفع الشفعاء إلى الله عز وجل في أن لا يظلم على مذاهبكم تعالى الله عن افتراءكم عليه علواً كبيراً . فإن قالوا: يشفع النبي إلى الله عز وجل في أن يزيدهم من فضله لا في أن يدخلهم جناته
Chapter Thirteen: On intercession and exit from the Fire. (1) Question 260: And it is said to them: The Muslims have unanimously agreed that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وسلم) has intercession. Then for whom is the intercession? Is it for the sinful committers of major sins, or for the sincere believers? If they say: For the sinful committers of major sins, they have agreed. And if they say: For the believers who are given glad tidings of Paradise and promised it. It is said to them: Since they are promised Paradise and given glad tidings of it, and Allah Almighty does not break His promise, what is the meaning of intercession for a people who, according to you, it is not possible that Allah would not admit them into His gardens? And what is the meaning of your statement that they have merited it upon Allah Almighty and made it obligatory upon Him, the Exalted? And if Allah Almighty does not wrong anyone by an atom's weight, then delaying them from Paradise would be injustice. And the intercessors only intercede with Allah Almighty to not be unjust according to your schools. Exalted is Allah above your slander against Him, a great exaltation. Then if they say: The prophet intercedes with Allah Almighty to increase them from His bounty, not to admit them into His gardens.
Source pages p. 162

Commentary
This passage opens a new chapter on intercession (شفاعة) and exiting the Fire. Ash'ari begins by stating the consensus that the Prophet has intercession. He then poses a question: is it for sinners who commit major sins, or for the sincere believers who are already promised Paradise? If the opponents say it is for sinners, they agree with Ahl al-Sunna. If they say it is for the promised believers, Ash'ari refutes this. He argues that since these believers are already promised Paradise and God does not break His promise, intercession for them would be meaningless. Moreover, if they have merited Paradise, then delaying them would be injustice, and intercession would be to prevent injustice—which is absurd because God does not wrong anyone. Then Ash'ari anticipates a possible objection: the opponents might say that intercession is for an increase in reward, not for entry into Paradise. The passage ends at this point, leaving the response incomplete. This argument is directed against the Mu'tazila, who denied intercession for grave sinners.

Key Terms
الشفاعة
المذنبين
الكبائر
المؤمنين المخلصين
الجنة
الاستحقاق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Intercession is only for believers already promised Paradise, to increase their reward.
Correction: Ash'ari argues that intercession is primarily for sinners who commit major sins, not for those already promised Paradise, because intercession for the latter would be redundant and imply injustice.

Checkpoint
According to Ash'ari, if intercession were for believers already promised Paradise, what problem arises?
A) God would be breaking His promise
B) Intercession would be meaningless because they already deserve Paradise
C) The believers would not need intercession
D) Intercession would cause injustice to sinners

Key Takeaway
Intercession is a mercy for major sinners, not a redundant bonus for those already guaranteed Paradise.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin, do you genuinely seek Allah's mercy through the Prophet's intercession, or do you treat it as a cheap guarantee that allows you to keep sinning without consequence? What would change in your daily choices if you truly believed intercession is only for those who repent and strive?
?
This passage refutes the idea that intercession is for the already righteous. Does your heart secretly feel that you are 'good enough' to deserve Paradise without needing intercession? What does that reveal about your understanding of your own sinfulness and your reliance on Allah's mercy alone?

The Rational Meaning of Intercession
Ash'ari refutes limiting intercession to the righteous.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Ash'ari's argument that intercession must be for sinners, not the righteous, based on the rational definition of intercession and God's promise.

Core Idea
Intercession is for removing punishment, not for the already-promised.

Ash'ari presents a dialectical argument to clarify the true recipients of the Prophet's intercession. He challenges those who limit it to the righteous by exposing the logical consequences of their view. The argument forces us to reconsider what intercession actually means in a rational framework.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and translation. Notice the question-and-answer structure as Ash'ari engages an opponent.
The argument builds step by step.
2
Identify the two options
Ash'ari presents two possibilities: intercession for sinners or for the righteous. See how he handles each response.
3
Follow the objections
When the opponent says intercession is for the righteous, Ash'ari raises objections based on God's promise and justice. Understand each objection.
He then counters the claim of increase of reward.
4
Understand the rational definition
Ash'ari concludes by stating the only rational meaning of intercession: removing deserved punishment or granting un-promised favor.
This definition excludes the righteous.
5
Reflect on the conclusion
Ask yourself: Why does this argument prove that intercession must be for sinners? How does it refute the opposing view?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : قد أجمع المسلمون أن لرسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم شفاعة، فلمن الشفاعة أهي للمذنبين المرتكبين الكبائر ، أم للمؤمنين المخلصين؟
فإن قالوا للمذنبين المرتكبين الكبائر وافقوا .
وإن قالوا للمؤمنين المبشرين بالجنة الموعودين بها .
قيل لهم : فإذا كانوا بالجنة موعودين وبها مبشرين، والله عز وجل لا يخلف وعده فما معنى الشفاعة لقوم لا يجوز عندكم أن لا يدخلهم الله جناته؟ وما معنى قولكم أنهم قد استحقوها على الله عز وجل واستوجبوها عليه سبحانه، وإذا كان الله عز وجل لا يظلم مثقال ذرة، كان تأخيرهم عن الجنة ظلماً، وإنما يشفع الشفعاء إلى الله عز وجل في أن لا يظلم على مذاهبكم تعالى الله عن افتراءكم عليه علواً كبيراً .
فإن قالوا: يشفع النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم إلى الله عز وجل في أن يزيدهم من فضله لا في أن يدخلهم جناته .
قيل لهم : أوليس قد وعدهم الله عز وجل ذلك؟ فقال تعالى: فَيُوَفِيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِ (النساء : 173) والله عز وجل لا يخلف وعده، فإنما يشفع إلى الله عز وجل عندكم من أن يخلف وعده، وهذا جهل منكم وإنما الشفاعة المعقولة فيمن استحقه عقاباً أن يوضع عنه عقابه، أو في من لم يعده شيئاً أن يتفضل به عليه، فأما إذا كان الوعد بالتفضل سابقاً فلا وجه لهذا.
And it is said to them: The Muslims have agreed that the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) has intercession. So for whom is the intercession? Is it for the sinners who commit major sins, or for the sincere believers? If they say: For the sinners who commit major sins, they agree. And if they say: For the believers who are given glad tidings of Paradise and promised it, it is said to them: If they are promised Paradise and given glad tidings of it, and Allah (mighty and majestic) does not break His promise, then what is the meaning of intercession for a people whom you hold that it is not permissible that Allah should not admit them to His gardens? And what is the meaning of your saying that they have deserved it from Allah (mighty and majestic) and obligated it upon Him (glorified be He)? And if Allah (mighty and majestic) does not wrong (anyone) by the weight of a particle, then delaying them from Paradise would be wrong. Rather, the intercessors intercede with Allah (mighty and majestic) that He not wrong, according to your doctrines—exalted is Allah above your slander, a great exaltation. If they say: The Prophet (peace be upon him) intercedes with Allah (mighty and majestic) to increase them from His bounty, not to admit them to His gardens, it is said to them: Has not Allah (mighty and majestic) already promised them that? For He said, the Most High: "He will pay them in full their rewards and increase them from His bounty" (an-Nisa 4:173). And Allah (mighty and majestic) does not break His promise. So according to you, he intercedes with Allah (mighty and majestic) only lest He break His promise. This is ignorance on your part. Rather, the rational intercession is for one who deserves punishment, that his punishment be removed, or for one whom He has not promised anything, that He bestow favor upon him. As for when the promise of bounty precedes, there is no basis for this.
Source pages p. 162

Commentary
This passage is a dialectical argument (objection-and-answer) where Ash'ari refutes the view that intercession is only for the righteous. He begins by stating the consensus on the Prophet's intercession, then poses a question: is it for sinners or the righteous? He presents two possible answers from opponents. If they say it is for sinners, they agree with the correct view. If they say it is for the righteous who are already promised Paradise, Ash'ari raises several objections: (1) Intercession would be meaningless since they are already promised entry, and Allah does not break His promise. (2) Claiming they have deserved Paradise obligates Allah, but Allah is not unjust—delaying them would be injustice, and intercession to prevent injustice is absurd. (3) If they then claim intercession is for increase of reward, Ash'ari counters that Allah has already promised increase (Qur'an 4:173), so intercession would be to ensure Allah does not break His promise, which is ignorance. He concludes by defining the rational meaning of intercession: either removing deserved punishment or granting un-promised favor. Since the righteous are already promised both entry and increase, intercession cannot be for them in this sense, proving it must be for sinners.

Key Terms
شفاعة
مذنب
كبائر
وعده
ظلم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Intercession is only for increasing the reward of the righteous.
Correction: Ash'ari shows that this would make intercession meaningless or imply God breaks His promise. The rational meaning of intercession is to remove punishment or grant un-promised favor.

Checkpoint
According to Ash'ari, what is the rational meaning of intercession (الشفاعة المعقولة)?
A) To ask God to increase the reward of the righteous
B) To remove punishment from one who deserves it, or to grant favor to one who has not been promised anything
C) To ensure God does not break His promise
D) To intercede for non-Muslims

Key Takeaway
The rational meaning of intercession determines its recipients: it must be for sinners, not the righteous, because the righteous already have God's promise of Paradise and increase.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you rely on the intercession of the Prophet as a license to continue in sin without repentance? This lesson teaches that intercession is for those who deserve punishment—but do you truly deserve it without turning back to Allah? What sin must you abandon today so that you may be a candidate for that intercession?
?
When you hear the phrase 'the Prophet's intercession', does it make you feel safe about your sins, or does it drive you to seek forgiveness before it is too late? If you knew that intercession is only for those who repent and strive, would your attitude toward sin change starting now?
?
Are you more concerned about your own desires than about Allah's promise and justice? The lesson shows that intercession aligns with God's given promises, not with our wishful thinking. Will you today take a promise from Allah seriously enough to change your life, or will you continue hoping for salvation without action?

Answering the Objection on Intercession Only for the Pleasing
Ash'ari answers an objection about intercession and cites hadith on major sins.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ash'ari refutes the claim that intercession is only for sinless believers by interpreting the Qur'anic verse and citing prophetic traditions.

Core Idea
Intercession is for sinners whom Allah approves.

When opponents cite the Qur'anic verse 'they do not intercede except for whom He approves' to restrict intercession to the righteous, Ash'ari offers a counter-interpretation. He shows that the verse itself, combined with authentic hadith, supports intercession for sinful Muslims. This lesson unpacks his argument and the supporting evidence.

Guided Path
1
Understand the objection and verse
Read the objection: opponents use verse 21:28 to claim intercession only for the sinless. Note the Arabic phrase 'illa liman irtada'.
The verse is from Surah al-Anbiya.
2
Examine Ash'ari's answer
Ash'ari says the answer is that 'except for whom He approves' means those are the ones for whom intercession is made. He does not limit it to the sinless.
The answer directly uses the same phrase.
3
Consider the hadith evidence
Two hadith are cited: the Prophet's intercession is for people of major sins, and sinners come out of the Fire. This supports intercession for sinful believers.
These hadith are transmitted (ruwiya).
4
Reflect on the theological implication
Understand that Allah's approval (rida) is not limited to the sinless; believers with sins can still be among those with whom He is pleased, allowing intercession to benefit them.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإن سألوا عن قول الله عز وجل: وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَى (الأنبياء : 28) .
فالجواب عن ذلك : ( إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَى ) فهم يشفعون له.
وقد روي أن شفاعة النبي لأهل الكبائر.
وروي عن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم : أن المذنبين يخرجون من النار.
Then if they ask about the saying of Allah (mighty and majestic): "And they do not intercede except for whom He approves" (al-Anbiya 21:28). The answer to that is: "Except for whom He approves"—so they intercede for him. And it has been reported that the intercession of the Prophet is for the people of major sins. And it has been reported from the Prophet (peace be upon him) that the sinners come out of the Fire.
Source pages p. 163

Commentary
This passage is a brief objection-and-answer. The opponents cite the Qur'anic verse 'they do not intercede except for whom He approves' to argue that intercession is only for those who are already pleasing to Allah (i.e., the righteous). Ash'ari gives a straightforward answer: the phrase 'except for whom He approves' means that those people are the ones for whom intercession is made. In other words, the verse does not restrict intercession to the righteous; rather, it indicates that the interceded-for are those with whom Allah is pleased, and Allah is pleased with the believers even if they have sins. He then supports this with transmitted reports (riwayat): the Prophet's intercession is for the people of major sins (ahl al-kaba'ir), and sinners are brought out of the Fire. These hadiths directly establish that intercession benefits sinful Muslims, contrary to the opponents' claim.

Key Terms
شفاعة
الكبائر
ارْتَضَى
خروج من النار

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The verse 'except for whom He approves' means intercession is only for sinless believers, excluding sinners.
Correction: Ash'ari explains that the verse actually means the interceded-for are those with whom Allah is pleased, and Allah is pleased with believers even if they have major sins. The hadith show the Prophet intercedes for sinners and they exit the Fire.

Checkpoint
How does Ash'ari interpret the verse "وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَى"?
A) The prophets only intercede for those who have already earned Paradise by their good deeds
B) The verse means that those for whom intercession is made are those with whom Allah is pleased, which includes sinful believers
C) The verse denies intercession altogether
D) The verse refers only to the angels, not the prophets

Key Takeaway
Ash'ari defends intercession for sinful Muslims by reinterpreting 'except for whom He approves' as inclusive of believers with sins and by citing hadith that the Prophet intercedes for major sinners and they leave Hell.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you have committed major sins, do you truly believe that Allah's approval includes you? How will you balance fear of His justice with hope in His mercy today?
?
The Prophet intercedes for sinners—does this give you a license to keep sinning? What concrete change in your life will you make to show gratitude for this mercy?

Torment of the Grave: Consensus and Hadith
Al-Ash'ari refutes Mu'tazila denial with consensus and hadith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Al-Ash'ari uses consensus (ijma') and multiple hadiths to affirm the reality of the torment of the grave against the Mu'tazila denial.

Core Idea
Consensus of the Companions confirms the grave's torment.

Al-Ash'ari begins this chapter by naming the Mu'tazila as deniers of the grave's punishment. He then builds a two-pronged case: first, the early Muslim community's unanimous acceptance (consensus), and second, multiple authentic hadiths in which the Prophet himself sought refuge from this torment. The passage shows how transmitted evidence outweighs rational speculation in matters of the unseen.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening claim
Al-Ash'ari states the Mu'tazila denial and then invokes consensus because no Companion rejected the narrations.
Notice that he prays for protection before proceeding.
2
Examine the consensus argument
He argues that since no one among the Companions denied or rejected the hadiths about grave torment, it became an agreed-upon truth (ijma').
This is a key methodological move: silence of the Companions implies acceptance.
3
Review the first hadith
The hadith of Abu Hurayra where the Prophet commands seeking refuge from grave torture shows the Prophet's own teaching.
4
Review the second hadith
Umm Khalid heard the Prophet himself seeking refuge, providing a direct action-based proof.
This adds a different type of evidence: the Prophet's practice.
5
Consider the third hadith
The beginning of Anas's hadith hints at another narration; though incomplete here, it adds to the multiplicity of chains.
Multiple chains strengthen the reliability of the report.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الباب الخامس عشر
الكلام في عذاب القبر
(۱)
٢٦٣ - وأنكرت المعتزلة عذاب القبر أعاذنا الله منه. وقد روي عن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم من وجوه كثيرة، وروي عن أصحابه رضي الله عنهم عنهم أجمعين، أحد أنه أنكره ونفاه وجحده، فوجب أن يكون إجماعاً
منهم
وما روي عن من أصحاب النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم .
وروى أبو بكر بن أبي شيبة قال: حدثنا أبو معاوية، عن الأعمش، عن أبي صالح عن أبي هريرة رضي الله عنه قال: قال رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم:
تَعَوَّذُوا بالله مِنْ عَذَابِ القَبْرِ (۳).
وروى
أحمد بن إسحاق الحضرمي، قال حدثنا وهيب، قال: حدثنا موسى بن عقبة قال: حدثتني أم خالد بنت خالد بن سعيد بن العاص رضي الله عنها، أنها سمعت رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم يَتَعَوَّذُ مِنْ عَذَابِ القَبْرِ (۳)، أعاذنا الله
منه
وروى أنس بن مالك رضي الله عنه عن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم أنه قال: « لَوْلا أَنْ
Chapter Fifteen: Speech on the Torment of the Grave.
(1) 263 - And the Mu'tazila denied the torment of the grave — may Allah protect us from it. And it has been narrated from the Prophet (peace be upon him) from many chains, and narrated from his Companions (may Allah be pleased with them all), and no one is known who denied it, rejected it, or disavowed it, so it must be a consensus (ijma') from them. And what has been narrated from the Companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him).
And Abu Bakr ibn Abi Shayba reported: Abu Mu'awiya narrated to us, from al-A'mash, from Abu Salih, from Abu Hurayra (may Allah be pleased with him) who said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) said: 'Seek refuge with Allah from the torment of the grave.'
And Ahmad ibn Ishaq al-Hadrami reported: Wuhayb narrated to us, saying: Musa ibn Uqba narrated to us, saying: Umm Khalid bint Khalid ibn Sa'id ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with her) narrated to me that she heard the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) seek refuge from the torment of the grave — may Allah protect us from it.
And Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) narrated from the Prophet (peace be upon him) that he said: 'Were it not that...'
Source pages p. 166

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of the Mu'tazila who denied the punishment of the grave. Al-Ash'ari begins by stating that the Mu'tazila rejected this doctrine, and he prays for Allah's protection from it. He then establishes the evidence: the torment of the grave has been transmitted from the Prophet (peace be upon him) through many chains, and from the Companions collectively. Since no one among the early Muslims (the Salaf) is known to have denied, rejected, or disavowed it, it constitutes a consensus (ijma'). He then cites specific hadiths to support this. The first is from Abu Hurayra: the Prophet commanded seeking refuge from the torment of the grave. The second is from Umm Khalid who heard the Prophet himself seeking refuge from it. A third hadith from Anas ibn Malik is quoted partially—it begins 'Were it not that...' but the text cuts off; this likely refers to the famous hadith about the Prophet's prayer to be spared from grave torment. The overall structure is: (1) statement of the opponents' position, (2) argument from consensus: because no Companion rejected it, it must be a matter of agreed truth, (3) multiple corroborating narrations proving that the Prophet and his Companions believed in and taught the reality of grave punishment.

Key Terms
عذاب القبر (torment of the grave)
إجماع (consensus)
المعتزلة (Mu'tazila)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The torment of the grave is only metaphorical or spiritual.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari uses multiple authentic hadiths and consensus to show it is a real, physical punishment.

Checkpoint
What primary evidence does Al-Ash'ari use to prove the reality of the torment of the grave?
A) Rational argument alone
B) Consensus (ijma') and multiple hadiths
C) A single hadith from Anas ibn Malik
D) The opinion of the Mu'tazila

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari establishes the reality of grave torment through the consensus of the Companions and multiple prophetic hadiths.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live as if the torment of the grave is a real possibility for you tonight? What would change if you believed it with certainty?
?
The Prophet himself sought refuge from this torment. When was the last time you sincerely asked Allah to protect you from it, and what prevents you from making that du'a a daily habit?
?
If you were to face the grave in the next hour, what one deed or habit would you desperately wish you had corrected? Why are you still delaying that correction?
