Miracles of the Righteous (Karamat)
Affirming the possibility of saintly miracles (karamat).

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Allah may grant miraculous signs to righteous individuals (karamat) as a distinct category from prophetic miracles.

Core Idea
Righteous saints can receive divine signs, affirming God's power to honor whom He wills.

In this brief but important passage, Imam al-Ash'ari affirms a core Sunni doctrine: that Allah may grant extraordinary signs (karamat) to righteous individuals, not just prophets. This establishes a distinction between prophetic miracles (mu'jizat) and saintly miracles (karamat), both of which are possible by Allah's power.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Note the key phrase 'يخصهم الله بآيات' (Allah singles them out with signs).
2
Identify the claim
The passage says 'يجوز' (it is permissible) for Allah to grant signs to the righteous. Why is the word 'يجوز' significant?
It indicates possibility, not obligation.
3
Contrast with prophetic miracles
Understand that karamat are distinct from mu'jizat. Prophetic miracles prove prophethood; saintly miracles honor righteousness.
Both are from Allah, but differ in purpose and context.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ونقول: إن الصالحين يجوز أن يخصهم الله عز وجل بآيات ويظهرها عليهم.
And we say: That the righteous, it is permissible that Allah Almighty singles them out with signs and manifests them upon them.
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This passage affirms the Sunni doctrine of karamat (miracles of the saints). It states that Allah may grant special supernatural signs to righteous individuals as a honoring miracle. This distinguishes between prophetic miracles (mu'jizat) and saintly miracles (karamat). The Imām uses the word 'āyāt' meaning signs or wonders.

Key Terms
الصالحين
آيات
الكرامات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only prophets can perform miracles.
Correction: While prophetic miracles (mu'jizat) are for proving prophethood, saints may also be granted karamat as honoring signs by Allah's permission.

Checkpoint
What does the Imām say about righteous individuals?
A) They cannot have any miracles.
B) Allah may show signs through them.
C) Only prophets have signs.
D) They are equal to prophets.

Key Takeaway
Allah may grant extraordinary signs to righteous individuals (karamat), distinct from prophetic miracles.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever witnessed or heard of something extraordinary that you attributed to a righteous person? Did it strengthen your faith or make you doubt? How does this lesson's emphasis on 'permissibility' shape your attitude toward such events?
?
If Allah were to honor you with a small sign of His pleasure, would you become more humble or more self-impressed? What does your honest answer reveal about your heart?
?
Do you secretly wish for a miracle to confirm your own righteousness? What does that desire say about your trust in Allah's hidden blessings and your dependence on visible validation?

Prophethood
intermediate

The Objection About Dhihar and Its Answer
Al-Ash'ari answers the objection about dhihar and refutes the Mu'tazila by analogy.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari defends prophetic knowledge by distinguishing between rulings that are not yet required and eternal truths, using the case of dhihar.

Core Idea
Prophets need not know what is not yet required; but eternal truths must be known.

Al-Ash'ari has argued that prophets know all necessary truths about Allah, including the impossibility of seeing Him. An objector raises a point: if the Prophet Muhammad did not know the ruling of dhihar before revelation, maybe Moses could also be ignorant of the impossibility of vision. This passage shows how al-Ash'ari carefully distinguishes between rulings that are not yet legislated and eternal truths about Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
Read the first part where the objector asks about dhihar. Notice the comparison drawn between the Prophet's ignorance of dhihar and Moses' supposed ignorance of vision.
2
Understand al-Ash'ari's answer
Al-Ash'ari says the Prophet did not know dhihar's ruling because it had not yet been obligated. Once obligated, he was informed. So his ignorance was natural.
3
See the refutation of the Mu'tazila
Al-Ash'ari turns the objection back: the Mu'tazila claim vision is always impossible, so Moses should have known. If he didn't, they'd be claiming superior knowledge.
4
Reflect on the distinction
Consider the key difference: a new ruling vs. an eternal truth. This distinction is crucial for defending prophetic infallibility.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٧٥ فإن قال قائل : ألستم تعلمون حكم الله في الظهار اليوم، ولم يكن نبي الله يعلم ذلك قبل أن ينزل؟ قيل له : لم يكن يعلم نبي الله ذلك قبل أن يلزم الله العباد حكم الظهار، فلما لزمهم الحكم به أعلم نبيه قبلهم ، ثم أعلم نبي الله عباد الله ذلك، ولم يأت عليه وقته لزمه حكمه، فلم يعلمه عليه الصلاة والسلام . وأنتم زعمتم أن موسى عليه الصلاة والسلام كان قد لزمه أن يعلم حكم الرؤية، وأنها مستحيلة عليه، وإذا لم يعلم ذلك وقت أن لزمه علمه، وعلمتموه أنتم الآن، لزمكم بجهلكم ، أنكم بما لزمكم العلم به الآن أعلم من موسى عليه الصلاة والسلام بما لزمه العلم به وهذا خروج عن دين المسلمين .
75. Then if someone says: Do you not know the ruling of Allah regarding dhihar today, while the Prophet of Allah did not know it before it was revealed? It is said to him: The Prophet of Allah did not know that before Allah obligated the ruling of dhihar upon the servants. When He obligated them with that ruling, He informed His prophet before them, then the Prophet of Allah informed the servants of Allah. And it did not come upon him at its time so that its ruling would be binding upon him, so he (peace be upon him) did not know it. But you claim that Moses (peace be upon him) was obligated to know the ruling of vision, and that it is impossible for Him; and if he did not know that at the time when knowledge was required of him, while you know it now, then it follows from your ignorance that you are more knowledgeable than Moses (peace be upon him) regarding what was required of him to know, and this is a departure from the religion of the Muslims.
Source pages p. 61

Commentary
This passage handles an objection raised against the previous proof. The objector says: 'You (the Ash'aris) know today the ruling of dhihar (a pre-Islamic form of divorce that was later legislated), but the Prophet Muhammad did not know it before it was revealed. Similarly, perhaps Moses did not know the ruling regarding the vision of Allah at the time of his request.' Al-Ash'ari answers by distinguishing the two cases. The ruling of dhihar was not yet legislated at the time of the Prophet, so he was not required to know it. Once it was revealed, he learned it. In contrast, the Mu'tazila claim that the vision of Allah was always impossible, and thus Moses, as a prophet, was obligated to know this at the time of his request. If he did not know it and they do, then they are claiming to be more knowledgeable than Moses, which is unacceptable. The key difference is that for dhihar, the ruling did not yet exist, so ignorance was natural; but for vision, the Mu'tazila assert it was always impossible, so ignorance would be a deficiency. Therefore, their objection fails.

Key Terms
ظهار (dhihar)
علم (knowledge)
حكم (ruling)
موسى (Moses)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that if the Prophet was ignorant of a future ruling, then prophets can be ignorant of eternal truths.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari shows that the ruling didn't exist yet, so ignorance was natural. Eternal truths, however, must be known by prophets.

Checkpoint
What is the key difference between the Prophet's ignorance of dhihar and Moses' supposed ignorance of the impossibility of vision?
A) The ruling of dhihar did not exist yet; vision's impossibility is eternal.
B) Moses was not a prophet at that time.
C) The Prophet was informed after revelation; Moses was not.
D) The Mu'tazila do not believe in the impossibility of vision.

Key Takeaway
Prophets must know eternal truths about Allah, but not necessarily future rulings that have not yet been revealed.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you sometimes assume that your current understanding of Allah is complete, forgetting that even prophets sought more knowledge? What step will you take today to seek a truth about Allah you have not yet grasped?
?
Al-Ash'ari's argument warns against labeling yourself more knowledgeable than a prophet. In what area of your faith have you subtly believed your reasoning surpasses what revelation teaches? How will you submit to the Qur'an and Sunnah on that point starting now?

Prophethood
intermediate
5 min
Answering the Objection: Why Did Allah Condemn the Request to See Him?
Imam Ashari refutes the objection that Allah's condemnation implies vision is impossible

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam Ashari refutes the objection that Allah's condemnation of the Israelites' request to see Him proves that vision of Allah is impossible.

Core Idea
Allah's condemnation was for disbelief, not because vision is impossible.

A common objection against the possibility of seeing Allah is that Allah Himself condemned those who asked to see Him. But is that condemnation based on the impossibility of the request, or on the attitude behind it? Imam Ashari explains that the Israelites asked to see Allah as a condition for believing in Moses, out of denial. Thus, the verse is about their disbelief, not about the nature of vision.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection and the quoted verses
Begin by reading the objection presented in the passage: Allah considered the request to see Him as 'great' (استكبر). Notice the verses from An-Nisa and Al-Baqarah that are cited. Ask yourself: why would Allah condemn a request if the thing requested is possible?
2
Examine Ashari's explanation
Ashari explains that the Israelites asked to see Allah 'by way of denying the prophethood of Moses' and 'leaving faith in him until they see Allah.' Their request was a condition for belief, not a sincere inquiry. Therefore, Allah's condemnation was for their attitude, not the request itself.
The key phrase: 'على طريق الإنكار لنبوة موسى' (by way of denying the prophethood of Moses).
3
Understand the analogy with the People of the Scripture
Ashari draws an analogy: just as the People of the Scripture asked for a book to descend from the sky to test the Prophet, and Allah condemned them not because it was impossible but because of their refusal to believe, similarly the Israelites' request was condemned due to their disbelief.
This analogy shows that the impossibility of the thing requested is not the reason for condemnation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مسألة والجواب عنها : فإن قال قائل : قد استكبر الله تعالى سؤال السائلين له أن يُرى بالأبصار، فقال: ﴿ يَسْتَلْكَ أَهْلُ الْكِتَابِ أَن تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتَبًا مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا مُوسَى أَكْبَرَ مِن ذَلِكَ فَقَالُوا اَرِنَا اللَّهَ جَهْرَةً ) [النساء : ١٥٣] . فيقال لهم : إن بني إسرائيل سألوا رؤية الله عز وجل على طريق الإنكار لنبوة موسى عليه الصلاة والسلام، وترك الإيمان به حتى يروا الله ؛ لأنهم :قالوا : لَن تُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى نَرَى الله جَهْرَةً ) [البقرة : 00] . فلما سألوه الرؤية عن طريق ترك الإيمان بموسى عليه السلام حتى يرىهم الله نفسه؛ استعظم الله سؤالهم من غير أن تكون الرؤية مستحيلة عليه، كما استعظم سؤال أهل الكتاب أن ينزل عليهم كتاباً من السماء مــن غير أن يكون ذلك مستحيلاً، ولكن لأنهم أبوا أن يؤمنوا بنبي الله [صلى الله عليه وسلم] حتى ينزل عليهم كتاباً من السماء .
Problem and its answer: If someone says: Allah considered great the request of those who asked to be seen by eyes, for He said: 'The People of the Scripture ask you to bring down to them a book from the sky. But they had asked of Moses greater than that and said, "Show us Allah outright."' [An-Nisa:153]. So it is said to them: The Children of Israel asked to see Allah Almighty by way of denying the prophethood of Moses, peace be upon him, and leaving faith in him until they see Allah, because they said: 'We will never believe you until we see Allah outright.' [Al-Baqarah:55]. So when they asked for the vision by way of leaving faith in Moses until Allah shows Himself to them, Allah considered their request great, not because vision is impossible for Him, just as He considered great the request of the People of the Scripture to bring down a book from the sky, not because that is impossible, but because they refused to believe in the Prophet of Allah [peace be upon him] until a book is brought down to them from the sky.
Source pages p. 63

Commentary
This passage addresses an objection: if seeing Allah is possible, why did Allah condemn those who asked to see Him? The author explains that the Children of Israel asked to see Allah as a condition for believing in Moses, i.e., out of disbelief and rejection. Therefore, Allah's condemnation was for their obstinate attitude, not because vision is inherently impossible. He draws an analogy with the People of the Scripture who asked for a book to descend from the sky: that request is not impossible, but Allah condemned it because it was made out of rejection of the Prophet. Thus, the verse does not prove that vision of Allah is impossible.

Key Terms
الاستكبار
الرؤية
بني إسرائيل
الإنكار
الإيمان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that Allah's anger at the request to see Him proves that He cannot be seen.
Correction: Allah's condemnation was for the Israelites' attitude of disbelief, not because the act of seeing Him is impossible. The same applies to the request for a book from the sky.

Checkpoint
Why did Allah consider the request of the Children of Israel to see Him as 'great' (استكبر)?
A) Because seeing Allah is impossible
B) Because they asked out of disbelief and refusal to believe Moses without seeing
C) Because they asked in an improper manner
D) Because they were not sincere

Key Takeaway
Allah's condemnation of the Israelites' request was due to their disbelief, not the impossibility of seeing Him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you ask Allah for something, do you check your heart: is your request a humble plea of faith or a defiant demand? The Israelites' request was condemned because of their attitude. What does this reveal about your own requests to Allah this week?
?
The People of the Scripture asked for a book as a condition to believe. Are there conditions you place on your submission to Allah—like 'I will obey when I see a certain outcome'? How can you purify your faith from such conditions?
?
Allah's condemnation is always just. If you feel He has 'refused' a request of yours, could it be that He is correcting your heart's posture, not denying the request itself? Will you examine your attitude before asking again?

Proof from the Modes of Revelation
Al-Ash'ari argues that if Allah's speech were created, the distinct modes of revelation would be meaningless.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah's speech cannot be created, as shown by the distinct modes of revelation and the absurd consequences of the Jahmiyya view.

Core Idea
If Allah's speech were created, the special revelation to prophets would be meaningless.

Al-Ash'ari draws on a key Quranic verse to expose a fatal flaw in the Jahmiyya claim that Allah's speech is created. He shows that if speech were merely a created thing, the three distinct modes of revelation would collapse, and the unique status of prophets—especially Moses—would be lost. This argument forces us to affirm that Allah's speech is an eternal attribute, not a created entity.

Guided Path
1
Read the Quranic verse
Read the verse (Ash-Shura 51) carefully to understand the three distinct modes of revelation Allah mentions.
These modes are: direct inspiration, from behind a veil, or through an angel.
2
Follow the logical implication
Consider the consequence if Allah's speech were created in a thing. Then the modes would not be distinct, making the verse's specification meaningless.
3
Consider the absurd consequences
Reflect on the specific absurdities for Moses and the rank of prophets. This shows the Jahmiyya position leads to unacceptable outcomes.
If speech were in a tree, anyone could hear it, and Moses' rank would be degraded.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد قال الله عز وجل: ﴿وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْيا أَوْ مِن وَرَاي حِجَابٍ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولًا فَيُوحِيَ بِإِذْنِهِ مَا يَشَاءُ ﴾ [الشورى: ٥١] فلو كان كلام الله لا يوجد إلا مخلوقاً في شيء مخلوق. لم يكن لاشتراط هذه الوجوه معنى ؛ لأن الكلام قد سمعه جميع الخلق ووجدوه - بزعم الجهمية – مخلوقاً في غير الله عز وجل وهذا يوجب إسقاط مرتبة النبيين صلوات الله وسلامه عليهم . ويجب عليهم إذا زعموا أن كلام الله لموسى خلقه في شجرة، أن يكون من سمع كلام الله عز وجل من مَلَك أو نبي أتى به من عند الله، أفضل مرتبة من سماع الكلام من موسى ؛ لأنهم سمعوه من نبي ولم يسمعه موسى الله عز وجل، وإنما سمعه من شجرة، وأن يزعموا أن اليهودي إذا سمع كلام الله من النبي عليه الصلاة والسلام أفضل مرتبة في هذا المعنى من موسى بن عمران صلى الله عليه وسلم ؛ لأن اليهودي سمعه من نبي من أنبياء الله ، وموسى سمعه مخلوقاً في شجرة، ولو كان مخلوقاً في شجرة لم يكن مُكَلَّماً لموسى من وراء حجاب ؛ لأن مَن حضر الشجرة من الجن والإنس قــد سمعوا الكلام من ذلك المكان، وكان سبيل موسى وغيره في ذلك سواء في أنه ليس كلام الله له من وراء حجاب .
And Allah Almighty said: 'It is not for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation, or from behind a veil, or send a messenger to reveal by His permission what He wills.' So if Allah's speech existed only as created in a created thing, then the specification of these modes would have no meaning; because all creatures would have heard the speech and found it – according to the claim of the Jahmiyya – created in something other than Allah Almighty, and this necessitates the nullification of the rank of the prophets, peace and blessings be upon them. And it would be necessary for them, if they claim that Allah's speech to Moses was created in a tree, that whoever heard Allah's speech from an angel or a prophet who brought it from Allah would be of a higher rank than hearing the speech from Moses; because they heard it from a prophet, while Moses did not hear Allah Almighty's speech, rather he heard it from a tree. And they would claim that a Jew, if he hears Allah's speech from the prophet (peace and blessings be upon him), has a higher rank in this sense than Moses son of Imran (peace be upon him); because the Jew heard it from a prophet of Allah, while Moses heard it created in a tree. And if it were created in a tree, then Moses would not have been spoken to from behind a veil; because whoever was present at the tree from the jinn and humans would have heard the speech from that place, and the condition of Moses and others would be the same in that it is not Allah's speech to him from behind a veil.
Source pages p. 79

Commentary
This passage uses the Quranic verse (Ash-Shura 51) to refute the Jahmiyya. Allah distinguishes three modes of revelation: direct inspiration, from behind a veil, or through an angel. If Allah's speech were created in a created thing (like a tree), then the speech would be accessible to all, nullifying the special status of prophets. Moreover, if Moses heard Allah's speech created in a tree, then anyone present at the tree would also hear it, making Moses' experience ordinary. This would also absurdly mean that a Jew hearing the Quran from the Prophet has a higher rank than Moses, because the Jew hears it from a prophet while Moses heard it from a tree. Thus, the Jahmiyya's view leads to unacceptable consequences.

Key Terms
تكليم
وحي
وراء حجاب
موسى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Jahmiyya believe that Allah's speech is created in a thing, like a tree.
Correction: If speech were created, the distinct modes of revelation would lose meaning, and prophethood would be degraded, as Al-Ash'ari demonstrates.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, if Allah's speech to Moses were created in a tree, what would be the consequence?
A) Moses would be the highest prophet
B) The tree would become a prophet
C) Anyone present could hear it
D) The Jew would be lower than Moses

Key Takeaway
Allah's speech is an uncreated attribute, as proven by the irreducibly distinct modes of revelation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite or hear the Quran, do you truly believe you are hearing Allah's uncreated speech, or do you treat it as ordinary words? What would change in your heart if you fully internalized this truth?
?
If the Jahmiyya view were correct, the Prophet Muhammad’s reception of the Quran would be no different from a tree. How does the belief in uncreated speech affect your love and reverence for the Prophet?
?
The argument shows that minimizing Allah’s speech leads to diminishing the rank of prophets. Are there areas in your life where you unknowingly reduce the Quran to mere human advice? What concrete step can you take today to honor it as divine?

God's Command to Abu Lahab: An Argument Against the Qadariyya
Imam al-Ash'ari argues that God commanded Abu Lahab to believe, knowing he would not, showing God's command can be impossible.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses the case of Abu Lahab to argue that God can command what is impossible for a person.

Core Idea
God's foreknowledge doesn't limit His command; He can command the impossible.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a powerful rebuttal to the Qadariyya who claim God only commands what is possible. Using the Quranic revelation about Abu Lahab, he shows that God commanded him to believe even though He knew he never would. This seemingly contradictory situation forces us to recognize that divine command is not contingent on human capability.

Guided Path
1
Read the Passage
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it, then read the English translation. Let the author's argument unfold naturally.
2
Identify the Components
Note the Quranic verse quoted, God's foreknowledge that Abu Lahab would not believe, the command to believe, and the logical conclusion that the command was impossible for him.
3
Understand the Refutation
Realize that this argument refutes the Qadariyya position that God only commands what is possible, because here a command is given despite foreknowledge of impossibility.
4
Reflect on the Implications
Consider the broader theological principle: God's sovereignty includes the authority to command even what is humanly impossible, emphasizing His absolute power and wisdom.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۲۲۱- جواب : ويقال لهم: أليس قد قال الله تبارك وتعالى: (تَبَّتْ يَدَا أَبِي لَهَبٍ وَتَبَّ * مَا أَغْنَى عَنْهُ مَالُهُ وَمَا كَسَبَ * سَيَصْلَى نَارًا ذَاتَ هَبَ) [المسد : ١-٣] وأمره مع ذلك بالإيمان، فأوجب عليه أن يعلم أنه لا يؤمن، وأن الله صادق في إخباره عنه أنه لا يؤمن، وأمره مع ذلك أن يؤمن ولا يجتمع الإيمان والعلم بأنه لا يكون ولا يقدر القادر على أن يؤمن، وأن يعلم أنه لا يؤمن، وإذا كان هذا هكذا، فقد أمر الله سبحانه أبا لهب بما لا يقدر عليه؛ لأنه أمره أن يؤمن، وأنه يعلم أنه لا يؤمن .
Answer: And it is said to them: Did not Allah, Blessed and Exalted, say: 'Perish the two hands of Abu Lahab, and perish! His wealth has not availed him, nor what he earned. He will burn in a fire of flame' (al-Masad: 1-3). And He commanded him, along with that, to believe. So He made it necessary upon him that he knows that he will not believe, and that Allah is truthful in His report about him that he will not believe. And He commanded him, along with that, to believe. And faith cannot coexist with the knowledge that it will not be, nor is the one who has the power able to believe while knowing that he will not believe. If this is so, then Allah, glorified be He, has commanded Abu Lahab with something he was not able to do, because He commanded him to believe while he knows that he will not believe.
Source pages p. 139

Commentary
This passage is a final argument against the Qadariyya who claim that God only commands what is possible. The Imam uses the case of Abu Lahab: God revealed that Abu Lahab would go to Hell, thus He knew he would not believe. Yet God still commanded him to believe. This creates a situation where Abu Lahab is commanded to do something that he cannot do because his disbelief is foreknown and decreed. Thus, God can command the impossible. This supports the Ash'ari doctrine that divine command does not entail capability in the commanded person.

Key Terms
أبو لهب
الإيمان
العلم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's command to Abu Lahab was insincere or not a real command.
Correction: Imam al-Ash'ari argues that the command was genuine and valid, but it was impossible for Abu Lahab to obey because of God's foreknowledge and decree. This shows that God's commands are not contingent on human ability.

Checkpoint
Why does Imam al-Ash'ari say that God commanded Abu Lahab with something he could not do?
A) Because God knew he would not believe.
B) Because God made him unable to hear.
C) Because he was a polytheist.
D) Because he was rich.

Key Takeaway
God's command to Abu Lahab demonstrates that divine command can be impossible for the commanded person, affirming God's absolute sovereignty over His creation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a command from Allah that feels impossible for you, do you retreat into despair or do you still submit? How does Abu Lahab's example reveal that your inability does not invalidate the command?
?
If Allah knows you will fail at something He commands, does that make your effort meaningless? What does this lesson teach you about the value of obedience even when you feel incapable?
?
Consider an area of your life where you resist Allah's command because you think it's too hard. How does the story of Abu Lahab challenge that excuse? Will you now take one step toward that command, trusting that the obligation remains regardless of your weakness?

The Strengthening of Prophets and the Predestination Dilemma
Imam al-Ash'ari uses a dilemma based on two prophetic examples to argue for predestination.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses the examples of Prophet Muhammad and Prophet Joseph to argue that God does not grant the same strengthening to disbelievers, thereby supporting the doctrine of predestination (qadar).

Core Idea
God's strengthening of prophets proves that disbelief persists only because He wills it.

Imam al-Ash'ari presents a powerful argument grounded in two Quranic verses. By examining the special strengthening (tathbit) given to Prophet Muhammad and the proof (burhan) given to Prophet Joseph, he constructs a dilemma that forces his opponents to either accept predestination or face a logical contradiction. This passage is a masterclass in using scriptural evidence to defend a theological position.

Guided Path
1
Read the Quranic verses
Read the two verses from Surah al-Isra and Surah Yusuf carefully. Notice how both verses describe a divine intervention that prevented the prophets from sinning.
The first verse speaks of 'strengthening' (tathbit), the second of 'proof' (burhan).
2
Understand the dilemma
The author poses a question: Does God also give this strengthening to disbelievers? Trace the two possible answers and their consequences.
3
Analyze the first branch
If opponents say God does NOT give such strengthening to disbelievers, they admit that God's will determines disbelief (qadar).
4
Analyze the second branch
If opponents say God DOES give it, then disbelievers should not persist in disbelief. Since they do persist, the premise is false.
5
Reflect on the conclusion
The only consistent position is that God does not grant such strengthening to disbelievers, confirming that disbelief occurs by God's will.
This is the author's defense of predestination.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويقال لهم : قال الله عز وجل لنبيه : ﴿ وَلَوْلَا أَن ثَبَّتْنَاكَ لَقَدْ كِدتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئًا قَلِيلًا ﴾ [الإسراء : ٧٤] ، وقال تعالى يخبر عن يوسف صلى الله عليه وسلم ﴿ وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ، وَهَمَّ بِهَا لَوْلَا أَن رَأَى بُرْهَانَ رَبِّهِ ﴾ [يوسف: ٢٤] فحدثونا عن ذلك التثبيت والبرهان هل فعله الله عز وجل بالكافرين، أو ما هو مثله؟ فإن قالوا لا تركوا القول بالقدر . وإن قالوا: نعم. قيل لهم : فإذا كان لم يركن إليهم من أجل التثبيت فيجب لو كان فعل ذلك بالكافرين أن لا يثبتوا عن الكفر، وإذا لم يكونوا عن الكفر مفترقين فقد بطل أن يكون فعل بهم مثل ما فعله بالنبي صلى الله عليه وسلم من التثبيت الذي لما فعله به لم يركن إلى الكافرين .
And it is said to them: God Almighty said to His Prophet: 'And if We had not strengthened you, you would have almost inclined to them a little' [Al-Isra: 74]. And He Almighty informed about Yusuf (peace be upon him): 'And she certainly desired him, and he would have desired her if he had not seen the proof of his Lord' [Yusuf: 24]. So inform us about that strengthening (tathbit) and proof (burhan): did God Almighty do it to the disbelievers, or something like it? If they say no, they have abandoned the doctrine of predestination (qadar). And if they say yes, it is said to them: Since he did not incline to them because of the strengthening, then it would be necessary that if He had done that to the disbelievers, they would not persist (or desist?) from disbelief. And if they were not separating from disbelief, then it is false that He did to them the like of what He did to the Prophet (peace be upon him) of strengthening, which when He did it to him, he did not incline to the disbelievers.
Source pages p. 142

Commentary
This passage is a proof by analogy using two prophets. The author points to divine strengthening (tathbit) given to Prophet Muhammad and the proof (burhan) given to Prophet Joseph, which prevented them from sin. He then poses a dilemma to opponents: either God also gives this strengthening to disbelievers (but then they would not persist in disbelief, contrary to fact) or He does not (which would admit that God's will determines disbelief, i.e., qadar). The conclusion is that God does not grant such strengthening to disbelievers, confirming predestination.

Key Terms
تثبيت
برهان
قدر
الركون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The strengthening of prophets is a reward for their personal merit; it is earned.
Correction: The strengthening is purely divine grace, not based on merit. The argument shows that God could grant it to disbelievers but chooses not to, emphasizing His will.

Checkpoint
What dilemma does the author present to opponents regarding God's strengthening of disbelievers?
A) Either disbelievers are strengthened and still disbelieve, which is contradictory.
B) Either disbelievers are strengthened or not; if not, it proves qadar.
C) Either God strengthens all or none; neither supports free will.
D) Either the prophets were strengthened or they were not.

Key Takeaway
God's strengthening of prophets proves that disbelief persists only because He wills it, affirming the doctrine of predestination.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you look at your own persistent sins and weaknesses, do you truly believe that you would abandon them instantly if God strengthened you, or do you secretly think it's your own effort? What does your answer reveal about your conviction in divine decree?
?
If God withheld His strengthening from you in a moment of temptation, whose fault is your fall? Can you honestly say you rely on His grace, or do you still trust your own resolve?
?
The prophets were granted strengthening that made them steadfast against great pressure. How often have you prayed for such strengthening, and how often have you blamed your own inadequacy for your failures? What would change if you started seeing every sin as a proof that God did not grant you that grace?

The Prophet's Warning Is Universal, Guidance Is Specific
Al-Ash'ari resolves an objection regarding the scope of prophetic warning.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Ash'ari reconciles verses that appear to limit the Prophet's warning with the universal call of Islam, distinguishing between the act of warning and the effect of guidance.

Core Idea
The Prophet warned everyone, but only the pious benefit from guidance.

In this passage, al-Ash'ari addresses a key objection: if the Quran says the Prophet only warns those who follow the Reminder, how did he warn the disbelievers? The answer lies in distinguishing between the delivery of warning and its benefit. Let's follow his reasoning as he marshals Quranic evidence to show that warning is universal while guidance is specific.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
Read the opening objection carefully. The questioner cites two verses that appear to restrict the Prophet's warning to those who follow the Reminder and those who fear the Hour.
Notice the apparent contradiction: the Prophet warned everyone, yet these verses say otherwise.
2
Identify the reinterpretation
Al-Ash'ari responds by reinterpreting these verses: they describe the benefit of the warning, not its scope. Only those who follow and fear actually benefit.
Key term: 'yantaFi' (benefit) – the warning is delivered to all, but only some benefit.
3
Examine the evidence for universal warning
Al-Ash'ari then cites three other verses (al-Baqarah 2:6, ash-Shu'ara 26:214, Fussilat 41:13) that explicitly show the Prophet warned disbelievers. This proves the warning was universal.
Universal warning does not guarantee universal acceptance.
4
Understand the conclusion on guidance
Finally, al-Ash'ari distinguishes guidance: the Quran is guidance for the pious but blindness for disbelievers, and Allah does not guide disbelievers. Thus, guidance is specific to believers.
Guidance is an effect, not the act of warning itself.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٢٤٧- سؤال :
فإن قال قائل : أليس قد قال الله عز وجل: ﴿ إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الذِّكْرَ ) [يس: ١١] وقال تعالى: ﴿إِنَّمَا أَنتَ مُنذِرُ مَن يَخْشَاهَا ﴾ [النازعات: ٤٥] وقد أنذر النبي من اتبع الذكر ومن لم يتبع ومن خشي ومن لم يخش ؟
قيل له : نعم. فإن قالوا فما أنكرتم أن يكون قوله تعالى: (هُدًى [؟]) أراد به هدى لهم ولغيرهم. قيل لهم إن معنى قول الله عز وجل : ( إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الذِكْرَ ﴾ [يس:۱۱] إنما أراد به ينتفع بإنذارك من اتبع الذكر، وقوله تعالى: (إِنَّمَا أَنتَ مُنذِرُ مَن يَخْشَاهَا ﴾ [النازعات : ٤٥] أراد أن الإنذار ينتفع بـه من يخشى الساعة، ويخاف العقوبة فيها، وإن الله عز وجل قد أخبر في موضع آخر من القرآن أنه أنذر الكافرين، فقال: ﴿إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَ أَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ ﴾ [البقرة: ٦] وهذا هو خبر عن الكافرين، وقال تعالى : ( وَأَنذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الأقربين ) [الشعراء: ٢١٤] وقال تعالى: (أَنذَرْتُكُمْ صَاعِقَةً مِّثْلَ صَاعِقَةِ عَادٍ وَثَمُودَ ﴾ [فصلت: ۱۳] وهذا خطاب للكافرين فلما أخبر الله عز وجل في آيات من القرآن أنه أنذر الكافرين، كما أخبر الله في آيات من القرآن أنه أنذر من يخشاها وأنذر من اتبع الذكر، وجب بالقرآن أن الله قد أنذر المؤمنين والكافرين فلما أخبرنا الله أنه هدى للمتقين وعمى على الكافرين وأخبرنا أنه لا يهدي الكافرين، وجب أن يكون القرآن هدى للمؤمنين دون الكافرين .
247 - A question: If someone says: Did not Allah say: 'You only warn those who follow the Reminder' and 'You are only a warner of those who fear it'? And the Prophet warned those who follow the Reminder and those who do not, and those who fear and those who do not. It was said to him: Yes. Then they said: So what prevents you from acknowledging that His saying 'guidance [unknown]' meant guidance for them and others? It was said to them: The meaning of Allah's saying 'You only warn those who follow the Reminder' is that only those who follow the Reminder benefit from your warning. And His saying 'You are only a warner of those who fear it' means that the warning benefits those who fear the Hour and dread the punishment therein. And Allah has informed elsewhere in the Qur'an that He warned the disbelievers, saying: 'Indeed those who disbelieve, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not believe.' And this is news about the disbelievers. And He said: 'And warn your nearest kindred.' And He said: 'I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of 'Ad and Thamud.' And this is an address to the disbelievers. So when Allah informed in verses of the Qur'an that He warned the disbelievers, just as He informed in verses that He warned those who fear it and those who follow the Reminder, it is established by the Qur'an that Allah warned the believers and the disbelievers. But when Allah informed us that it is guidance for the pious and blindness upon the disbelievers, and informed us that He does not guide the disbelievers, it must be that the Qur'an is guidance for the believers, not the disbelievers.
Source pages p. 152

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer. The opponent cites verses that seem to restrict the Prophet's warning to 'those who follow the Reminder' and 'those who fear it'. Yet the Prophet warned everyone. Al-Ash'ari explains that these verses refer to the benefit of the warning: only those who follow and fear actually benefit (yantafi'), not that the warning is limited to them. He then shows from multiple verses (al-Baqarah 2:6, ash-Shu'ara 26:214, Fussilat 41:13) that Allah explicitly states He warned the disbelievers. Therefore, the warning was universal. However, the Qur'an itself is described as 'guidance for the pious' and 'blindness for the disbelievers', and Allah states He does not guide disbelievers. Consequently, the Qur'an is guidance only for believers, even though the warning reached all. This reconciles the apparent contradiction by distinguishing between the act of warning (which is universal) and the effect of guidance (which is specific).

Key Terms
إنذار
انتفاع
هدى
عمى
يهدي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The verses 'إنما تنذر من اتبع الذكر' mean the Prophet only warned those who follow the Reminder and fear the Hour, implying his mission was not universal.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari clarifies that these verses describe the benefit of the warning (only the fearful benefit) not the scope of the warning (which is universal).

Checkpoint
How does al-Ash'ari explain the verses that limit the Prophet's warning to those who follow and fear?
A) They mean the warning is only for them.
B) They mean only those benefit from the warning.
C) They are abrogated.
D) They apply only to the Prophet's time.

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's warning reached all, but only the believers benefit from its guidance.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The Prophet's warning has reached you. Are you truly among those who follow the Reminder, or have you only heard it without letting it change your heart? What specific step will you take today to ensure you are benefiting from the warning?
?
The Quran is guidance for the pious, not for those who turn away. Where is your heart right now—are you approaching the Quran as one seeking guidance, or as one who merely reads? What would it cost you to become truly pious?
?
If you know that the warning is universal but guidance is only for those who fear the Hour, does the reality of the Hour grip your soul? If not, what is stopping you from living in its shadow right now?

Wiping over Leather Socks is Sunnah in Travel and Residence
Imam Ash'ari affirms wiping over khuffs in all circumstances

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that Imam Ash'ari asserts wiping over khuffs is a Sunnah practiced both in residence and travel, refuting those who deny it.

Core Idea
Wiping over leather socks is a confirmed Sunnah in all situations.

This lesson presents a concise but significant ruling from Imam Ash'ari's Ibana. In a single sentence, he establishes the Sunni position on wiping over khuffs during ablution. The statement is a direct refutation of those who reject this practice, affirming its legitimacy in both settled and traveling contexts.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the original Arabic phrase and its literal translation to grasp the direct meaning.
Notice the two conditions explicitly mentioned: residence and travel.
2
Understand the term 'Sunnah'
In this context, 'Sunnah' refers to a recommended practice established by the Prophet, not obligatory but strongly encouraged.
3
Recognize the contrast
The phrase 'contrary to those who denied' highlights that this was disputed by some groups, such as the Khawarij.
Reflect on why some might deny it and how the Sunni position is grounded in hadith.
4
Consider the implication
This ruling emphasizes that the dispensation of wiping over khuffs applies universally, not limited to travel.
It counteracts the misconception that it is only allowed when traveling.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأن المسح على الخفين سنة في الحضر والسفر، خلافاً لقول من أنكر ذلك.
And that wiping over the two leather socks (khuffs) is a Sunnah in residence and travel, contrary to the statement of those who denied that.
Source pages p. 52

Commentary
This passage is a concise ruling on the practice of wiping over leather socks (al-masḥ 'alā al-khuffayn) during ablution. Imam Ash'ari declares that this is a confirmed Sunnah, applicable both when one is resident (ḥaḍar) and when traveling (safar). He contrasts this with the position of those who reject this practice. Historically, the Khawarij and some other sects denied the validity of wiping over khuffs, insisting on washing the feet. The Sunni position, based on widespread hadith and practice of the Companions, affirms it as a legitimate dispensation.

سفر
إنكار

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Wiping over leather socks is only permissible or recommended during travel, not when residing at home.
Correction: Imam Ash'ari explicitly states it is Sunnah in both residence and travel (سنة في الحضر والسفر), indicating it is a general practice.

Checkpoint

A) Only during travel.
B) Only during residence.
C) Both in travel and residence.
D) Only in cases of necessity.

Key Takeaway
Wiping over leather socks is a confirmed Sunnah in both residence and travel, based on prophetic practice.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
How many other Sunnahs do you minimize, thinking they are only for special circumstances? Identify one Sunnah you have been disregarding and commit to practicing it this week.

The Linguistic Reality of 'Basar'
Imam al-Ash'ari refutes the restriction of 'basar' to the eye using linguistic evidence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses linguistic evidence (ahl al-lugha) to argue that 'basar' applies to both the eye and the heart, countering those who restrict it to physical sight.

Core Idea
Language itself shows that 'basar' includes both eye and heart vision.

This short passage presents a critical argument about the term 'basar'. Some claimed it refers only to physical eye sight. Imam al-Ash'ari refutes this by appealing to the actual usage of the Arabic language, showing that 'basar' is used for both the eye and the inner vision of the heart. This linguistic principle has theological implications for how we understand seeing Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic mas'ala and its translation. Notice the structure: a conditional statement (if someone says...), followed by a refutation using language.
Pay attention to the rhetorical flow.
2
Identify the linguistic argument
The author cites 'ahl al-lugha' (the people of language) as authority. Find how he uses their usage to argue for the dual meaning of 'basar'.
The phrase 'سمى أهل اللغة القلب بصراً' is key.
3
Consider the logical implication
The argument uses a reductio ad absurdum: if one can arbitrarily restrict 'basar' to the eye, another could restrict it to the heart. Since neither is permissible, the word must encompass both.
This is a classic argumentative move.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مسألة :
إذا قال قائل منهم : إن البصر في الحقيقة هو بصر العين لا بصر القلب. قيل له : ولم زعمت هذا؟ وقد سمى أهل اللغة القلب بصراً، كما سموا بصر العين بصراً؟ وإن جاز لك ما قلته جاز لغيركم أن يزعم أن البصر في الحقيقة هو بصر القلب دون العين وإذا لم يجز هذا فقد وجب أن البصر بصر العين وبصر القلب معناه؟
A question: If one of them says: The vision in reality is the vision of the eye, not the vision of the heart. It is said to him: Why do you claim this? While the people of language have called the heart "vision" just as they called the eye's vision "vision"? And if what you have said is permissible, then it is permissible for others to claim that vision in reality is the vision of the heart without the eye. And if this is not permissible, then it is necessary that vision is the vision of the eye and the vision of the heart in meaning.
Source pages p. 70

Commentary
This is a separate question (mas'ala) about the definition of 'basar'. The opponent restricts the term to the physical eye. The author refutes by appealing to linguistic usage (ahl al-lugha) who use 'basar' for both the eye and the heart (e.g., in the Quran 'basar' is used for insight). The argument shows that if one can arbitrarily define 'basar' as only the eye, another could define it as only the heart. Since neither is exclusive, the term must encompass both in its meaning. This protects the possibility of seeing Allah with the heart (inner vision) and also with the eyes (in the Hereafter).

Key Terms
بصر
حقيقة
أهل اللغة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The term 'basar' in Arabic refers only to physical eyesight.
Correction: The people of language use 'basar' for both the eye and the heart (inner vision), so it cannot be restricted to physical sight alone.

Checkpoint
What is the author's point about the term 'basar'?
A) It refers only to the eye.
B) It refers only to the heart.
C) It refers to both eye and heart in language.
D) It has no clear meaning.

Key Takeaway
The linguistic evidence of 'ahl al-lugha' shows that 'basar' encompasses both physical and inner vision, preventing arbitrary restriction to either.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of Allah seeing you, do you only imagine a physical eye watching your actions? How does the realization that Allah's 'basar' encompasses inner vision change your awareness that He sees your intentions and inner states? Will you now live more consciously that Allah perceives your heart's movements, not just your outward deeds?
?
This lesson shows that the people of language used 'basar' for both eye and heart. Are you willing to let go of your own limited definitions of Allah's attributes and accept the full linguistic and theological scope? What would it mean for your faith to truly believe that Allah's vision includes both physical and spiritual dimensions?
?
If you have ever doubted that Allah can be seen with the heart, how does this linguistic evidence challenge that doubt? Will you now train your heart to seek Allah's vision through inner purification, knowing that the term 'basar' itself permits it?

The Linguistic Scope of Basar (Sight)
Imam Ashari refutes restricting basar to physical sight via linguistic usage and reductio.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why 'basar' in classical Arabic applies to both physical sight and spiritual insight, based on linguistic evidence and logical consistency.

Core Idea
Basar in Arabic includes both eye and heart.

This passage presents a dialectical argument from Imam Ashari against those who restrict basar to physical sight. Before reading, note that the argument hinges on the authority of linguistic usage, not rational speculation. The opponent's claim is met with a linguistic counterexample and a reductio ad absurdum.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation once to get a sense of the argument's flow.
Pay attention to the question-and-response structure.
2
Identify the opponent's claim
Find the statement attributed to the opponent: that basar in reality is only the sight of the eye.
This is the starting point of the refutation.
3
Notice the linguistic evidence
Imam Ashari cites that the people of language have used basar for both the eye and the heart.
This is a direct appeal to established usage.
4
Understand the reductio argument
If the opponent's arbitrary restriction were allowed, someone else could restrict basar to the heart alone, leading to contradiction. Therefore, the only consistent position is to accept both meanings.
This shows the logical flaw in the opponent's position.
5
Apply the lesson
Reflect on how this argument supports the possibility of seeing Allah with the heart (spiritual vision) while affirming the literal meaning for the eyes.
This connects to broader theological discussions about ru'yat Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
٨٧- مسألة : إذا قال قائل منهم : إن البصر في الحقيقة هو بصر العين لا بصر القلب. قيل له : ولم زعمت هذا؟ وقد سمى أهل اللغة القلب بصراً، كما بصر سموا بصر العين بصراً؟ وإن جاز لك ما قلته جاز لغيركم أن يزعم أن البصر في الحقيقة هو بصر القلب دون العين وإذا لم يجز هذا فقد وجب أن البصر بصر العين وبصر القلب معناه؟
87- Question: If someone among them says: 'Indeed, sight (al-basar) in reality is the sight of the eye, not the sight of the heart,' it is said to him: 'And why do you claim this? While the people of language have called the heart 'basar,' just as they have called the sight of the eye 'basar'? And if what you said is permissible for you, it becomes permissible for others to claim that sight in reality is the sight of the heart without the eye. And if this is not permissible, then it is necessitated that the meaning of sight is the sight of the eye and the sight of the heart?'
Source pages p. 70

Commentary
This passage is a question (مسألة) where Imam Ashari refutes an opponent who insists that 'basar' (sight) only refers to the physical eye. The argument is linguistic: the Arabs (people of language) have used 'basar' for both the eye and the heart (mental insight). The opponent's restriction is arbitrary. If they can arbitrarily restrict 'basar' to the eye, someone else could arbitrarily restrict it to the heart. Since that leads to contradiction, the correct understanding is that 'basar' encompasses both meanings. This supports the possibility of seeing Allah with the heart (spiritual vision) while also affirming the literal meaning for the eyes.

Key Terms
بصر
عين
قلب
لغة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think 'basar' only means physical sight.
Correction: Classical Arabic includes mental/spiritual vision as a valid meaning of basar.

Checkpoint

A) By quoting a verse from the Quran.
B) By citing that the people of language use it for both.
C) By logical proof that the opponent is inconsistent.
D) By referring to consensus of scholars.

Key Takeaway
Basar in Arabic encompasses both physical sight and spiritual insight, as established by linguistic usage and logical consistency.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
If 'basar' includes both eye and heart, are you neglecting the sight of your heart? What spiritual blindness do you need to address today?
?

Rational Judgments
intermediate

Question About Obligating Disbelievers to Hear and Believe
Imam al-Ash'ari presents an objection and answers with Quranic verses showing obligation without ability.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Ash'ari uses Quranic verses to demonstrate that God can obligate even those who are incapable, contrary to the claim that ability is a prerequisite for obligation.

Core Idea
God's obligation does not depend on human ability.

This lesson examines a theological objection: if God obligates disbelievers to hear and believe, they must have had the ability to do so. Imam al-Ash'ari counters with Quranic verses indicating they were unable, showing that obligation and ability are distinct. Prepare to see how scripture resolves a key debate in kalam.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
The passage opens with a question posed to opponents. Notice the assumption that God's obligation implies ability. Read the Arabic and translation.
2
Examine the counter-evidence
Imam al-Ash'ari quotes two verses (Hud 11:20 and al-Kahf 18:101) that state the disbelievers were unable to hear. Reflect on how these verses contradict the assumption.
3
Understand the conclusion
The final line states that God obligated them to listen despite their inability. This establishes that ability is not a condition for obligation. Consider the theological implications.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
۲۱۸ - مسألة في التكليف : ويقال لهم : أليس قد كلف الله عز وجل الكافرين أن يستمعوا الحق ويقبلوه ويؤمنوا بالله؟ فلا بد من نعم . فيقال لهم : فقد قال الله عز وجل : ( مَا كَانُوا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ السَّمْعَ ) [هود :٢٠] وقال: ﴿ وَكَانُوا لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَمعا ﴾ [الكهف: ١٠١] وقد كلفهم استماع الحق .
Issue concerning obligation: And it is said to them: Did not Allah, Mighty and Majestic, obligate the disbelievers to listen to the truth, accept it, and believe in Allah? There is no escape from 'yes'. Then it is said to them: Indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, has said: 'They were not able to hear' (Hud: 20) and said: 'And they were not able to hear' (al-Kahf: 101), and He obligated them to listen to the truth.
Source pages p. 138

Commentary
This passage is an objection raised by the Ash'ari against his opponents (likely the Qadariyya or Mu'tazila). The opponents claim that God's obligation on disbelievers to believe implies that they have the ability to believe. The Ash'ari counters by citing verses that state the disbelievers were unable to hear, implying that God obligated them to something they were incapable of. This supports the Ash'ari position that God can obligate what is impossible (taklif ma la yutaq), or at least that ability is not a prerequisite for obligation.

Key Terms
تكليف
يستطيعون السمع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Many assume that God only obligates what a person is capable of doing.
Correction: Imam al-Ash'ari shows that God can obligate even those who are unable, as seen in the Quran.

Checkpoint
What verses does Imam al-Ash'ari quote to show that disbelievers could not hear?
A) Surah al-Baqara and Surah Al Imran.
B) Surah Hud verse 20 and Surah al-Kahf verse 101.
C) Surah al-Masad and Surah al-Fatiha.
D) Surah al-An'am and Surah al-A'raf.

Key Takeaway
God can obligate actions even when the individual lacks the ability to perform them, as demonstrated by the Quranic verses about disbelievers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever felt that a command of Allah was beyond your ability? How does this passage reshape your understanding of that struggle? Will you now plead inability as an excuse, or cry out to Him for strength?
?
If God can obligate what is beyond your capacity, does that make you fear Him more or trust Him more? Why? Write down one area where you have been using 'I can't' as a shield, and commit to seeking His help instead.
?
Think of a sin you continue to commit because you feel you are unable to stop. Does this passage remove your excuse? How does it call you to respond right now, not with resignation but with repentance and reliance on Allah?

Rational Judgments

The Refutation of Provision Entailing Ownership
Al-Ash'ari refutes the entailment of ownership from provision.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Al-Ash'ari argues that God providing something does not necessarily mean He owns it, and how the examples of infants and animals demonstrate this.

Core Idea
Provision does not require ownership; God gives without giving legal title.

In this passage, Al-Ash'ari tackles a subtle objection: if God provides haram (forbidden things), wouldn't that imply He owns them? He counters with everyday examples—infants feeding from milk and animals grazing. The logic is simple yet profound: God can provide without transferring ownership. Let's examine the argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then read the English translation. Notice the back-and-forth dialectical style: the opponent states a claim, then Al-Ash'ari responds with a counterexample.
This passage is a concise theological argument.
2
Identify the opponent's claim
The opponent says: 'If God provided the haram, He would own the haram.' Understand why this is problematic: if God owned haram, that might imply imperfection or contradiction with His attributes.
3
Examine Al-Ash'ari's counterexamples
Al-Ash'ari asks about an infant nourished by mother's milk and a beast grazing grass. God provides these, but the infant and beast do not own them. This shows provision does not entail ownership.
The examples are from everyday life, making the argument accessible.
4
Grasp the logical conclusion
The conclusion: 'God provides something without giving ownership of it.' So the opponent's objection fails because it conflates provision with ownership.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مسألة أخرى في الأرزاق : ويقال لهم: لم أبيتم أن يرزق الله الحرام؟ فإن قالوا: لأنه لو رزق الحرام لملك الحرام. يقال لهم : خبرونا عن الطفل الذي يتغذى من لبن أمه، وعن البهيمة التي ترعى الحشيش، من يرزقهما ذلك؟ فإن قالوا : الله قيل لهم : فمن ملكهما؟ وهل للبهيمة ملك؟ فإن قالوا: لا قيل لهم : فلم زعمتم أنه لو رزق الحرام لملك الحرام، وقد يرزق الله لشيء ولا يُملكه إياه .
Another question about provisions: And it is said to them: Why do you refuse that God provides the haram? So if they say: Because if He provided the haram, He would own the haram. It is said to them: Inform us about the infant who is nourished from his mother's milk, and about the beast that grazes the grass, who provides them that? So if they say: God. It is said to them: Then who owns them? And does the beast have ownership? So if they say: No. It is said to them: So why did you claim that if He provided the haram He would own the haram, while God provides something without giving ownership of it?
Source pages p. 145

Commentary
This passage addresses the objection that God providing haram would mean He owns the haram. Al-Ash'ari responds by giving examples of infants and animals: God provides them with milk and grass, but they do not own those things. Thus, provision does not entail ownership. Therefore, the objection fails.

Key Terms
رزق (rizq)
حرام (haram)
ملك (milk: ownership)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If God provides something, He must own it.
Correction: Al-Ash'ari shows that God can provide without giving ownership, as with infants and animals.

Checkpoint
What example does Al-Ash'ari use to show that provision does not imply ownership?
A) A king giving gifts
B) An infant drinking mother's milk
C) A merchant selling goods
D) A farmer planting crops

Key Takeaway
Provision does not entail ownership; God provides without transferring legal title.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever thought that because you 'own' something you are entitled to it? How does this lesson challenge your sense of entitlement towards what you receive?
?
Consider a blessing in your life that you do not legally own—like your mother's love or the air you breathe. How should recognizing that God provides without owning change your gratitude and humility?

Module 17 · Lesson 1

Causality and Created Things
beginner
5 min
Benefit Without Beneficiary: A Logical Proof
Al-Ash'ari argues by reductio that God cannot benefit or guide without effect.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Al-Ash'ari's reductio argument that divine benefit and guidance necessarily require a receptive subject.

Core Idea
Allowing benefit without benefit leads to self-contradictory possibilities about God's power.

Al-Ash'ari presents a concise logical argument to clarify the nature of God's benefit and guidance. He uses reductio ad absurdum and a fortiori reasoning to show that benefits and guidance can only truly occur when the recipient is capable of receiving them. This passage is a masterclass in theological consistency.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage aloud
Read the Arabic text carefully, paying attention to the logical connectors (فإن, فما, ولو, فكذلك). This helps you grasp the flow of argument.
Notice the use of conditional statements.
2
Identify the two arguments
The passage contains two connected arguments: first about benefit and harm, then about guidance and power. Mark where one ends and the other begins.
The first ends at 'منتفعاً', the second continues from 'ولو جاز'.
3
Understand the reductio
The first argument starts from a premise the opponent accepts—God benefits some who are not benefited—and shows it leads to the absurdity that God could also harm without harm, which the opponent denies.
This is a classic reductio ad absurdum.
4
Follow the a fortiori argument
The second argument uses analogy: if benefit without benefit is allowed, then by stronger reason guidance without guidance and power without power would be allowed. Since the latter is impossible, the former is impossible.
This is an argument from the greater to the lesser.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أن ينفعهم فلا ينتفعون، وأنه يصلحهم فلا يصلحون، وإذا جاز أن ينفع من لا ينتفع بنفعه فما أنكرتم من أن يضر من لا تلحقه المضرة، فإن كان لا يضر إلا من يلحقه الضرر ، فكذلك لا ينفع إلا منتفعاً، ولو جاز أن ينفع من ليس منتفعاً، ويهدي من ليس مهتدياً جاز أن يقدر من ليس مقتدراً، وإذا استحال ذلك استحال أن ينفع من ليس منتفعاً، ويهدي من ليس مهتدياً .
That He benefits them but they are not benefited, and that He reforms them but they are not reformed. And if it is permissible that He benefits one who does not benefit by His benefit, then what prevents you from acknowledging that He harms one who is not harmed? For if He does not harm except one who is harmed, then likewise He does not benefit except a beneficiary. And if it were permissible that He benefits one who is not a beneficiary, and guides one who is not guided, then it would be permissible that He empowers one who is not empowered. And when that is impossible, then it is impossible that He benefits one who is not a beneficiary and guides one who is not guided.
Source pages p. 152

Commentary
This passage is a logical argument (burhan) by reductio ad absurdum. It begins by stating a premise that the opponent grants: God benefits some but they do not actually benefit (as in disbelievers). Al-Ash'ari argues that if one allows that God can benefit someone who derives no benefit, then consistency demands that one also allow that God can harm someone who is not harmed. But the opponent does not accept that God harms without harm reaching the person. Therefore, the original premise is false. The second part is an a fortiori argument: if it is possible for God to benefit a non-beneficiary and guide a non-guided, then by analogy it must be possible for God to empower a non-empowered person. But that is absurd (God cannot make an incapable person capable without changing his nature). Since the latter is impossible, the former is also impossible. The conclusion is that God's benefiting and guiding necessarily require the recipient to be receptive.

Key Terms
ينفع
يضر
يهدي
مقتدر
جاز
استحال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the argument limits God's power.
Correction: The argument defines the nature of benefit and guidance as effects that require a subject fit to receive them, not that God is unable to do something.

Checkpoint
According to the argument, what is the logical consequence if one allows that God can benefit someone who does not actually benefit?
A) God can also harm someone who is not harmed.
B) God cannot benefit anyone.
C) God is not all-powerful.
D) The Quran is not guidance.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari demonstrates that divine benefit and guidance necessarily imply a receptive subject, otherwise contradictory possibilities arise.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever assumed that God's guidance is automatic, regardless of your own readiness? What does this argument reveal about your responsibility to actively prepare your heart to receive His guidance? Will you commit to daily reflection on your receptivity to divine truth?
?
When you read that God 'benefits them but they are not benefited,' do you see yourself in that description? In what areas of your life are you receiving God's blessings but failing to truly benefit from them? What specific change will you make today to become a true beneficiary of His mercy?
?
This argument shows that God's actions are not random but fitting to the subject. How does this challenge any secret belief you might hold that God is arbitrary or unfair? Will you now trust that every trial He sends is suited to your state, and respond with patience and gratitude?
